As methods are executed repeatedly, the **JIT compiler** detects "hot" methods and compiles them to **native machine code** specific to the host CPU, storing them in a **code cache** for blazingly fast subsequent calls. **Comparison with C++:** | Aspect | Java | C++ | |:---|:---|:---| | Compilation output | Bytecode (`.class`) — portable | Native executable (`.exe`/ELF) — non-portable | | Stages | Two (source→bytecode→native) | One (source→native directly) | | Recompilation per platform? | No (JIT handles it) | Yes (build per OS/CPU) | | Runtime optimization? | Yes (JIT inlining, escape analysis) | None at runtime (compiler done) | | Distribution artifact | `.jar` containing `.class` files | Platform-specific binary | **Block Diagram (textual representation):** ``` .java ──javac──► .class ──JVM──► Interpreter ──JIT──► Native Code (source) (bytecode) (byte-by-byte) (CPU-specific) ``` > **[Mark Distribution:** 1 mark each for Stage 1, Stage 2, JIT explanation; 2 marks for diagram; 2 marks for C++ comparison table.**]** --- #### Model Solution — Q.A (b) [7 marks] The `javac` compiler is itself a multi-phase pipeline. We illustrate each phase with the sample statement: ```java int salary = 10000 * 12; ``` **1. Lexical Analysis (Scanning):** The character stream is broken into tokens: `[int] [salary] [=] [10000] [*] [12] [;]`. Whitespace and comments are discarded. **2. Syntax Analysis (Parsing):** Tokens are arranged into a **parse tree** following Java's grammar (a context-free grammar in BNF). A *syntax error* such as `int salary = 10000 * 12` (missing semicolon) is caught here. **3. Semantic Analysis:** - Type of `salary` is inferred as `int`. - The expression `10000 * 12` is *type-checked* (`int * int` is valid). - The literal `10000` is verified to fit within `int` range. - The result is a **typed, decorated AST**. **4. Annotation Processing (Optional):** Custom annotations such as `@Override` or Lombok's `@Getter` are processed. This stage can *generate additional source code* and feed it back into phase 1 (creating a loop). **5. Bytecode Generation:** The final AST is serialized to **JVM bytecode**, a sequence of 1-byte opcodes: | Offset | Opcode | Operand | Meaning | |:---:|:---|:---|:---| | 0 | `bipush` | 10000 | push 10000 onto operand stack | | 2 | `sipush` | 12 | push 12 onto operand stack | | 5 | `imul` | — | multiply top two stack values | | 6 | `istore_1` | — | store result into local var 1 (`salary`) | **Purpose of the Constant Pool:** The **constant pool** is a per-class table of symbolic references embedded in every `.class` file. It stores: - Class and interface names (e.g., `java/lang/System`). - Field and method names (e.g., `out`, `println`). - String literals (e.g., `"Hello, KTU!"`). - Numeric constants. The bytecode instructions refer to constant-pool entries by **index** (e.g., `ldc #13`), enabling **dynamic linking** — the JVM resolves these symbolic references to actual memory addresses only at runtime. This is what makes the same `.class` file work across JVM implementations. > **[Mark Distribution:** 1 mark per phase × 5 = 5 marks; 2 marks for constant pool explanation.**]** --- ### ❖ Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) **`[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Adapted for 2024 Scheme]`** **(a)** What is **JIT compilation**? Explain the **tiered compilation model** used by the HotSpot JVM. Why does Java often "start slow, then become faster"? **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare the **JDK, JRE, and JVM**. With a neat diagram, explain how a Java program moves from source code to execution. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Q.B (a) [7 marks] **JIT (Just-In-Time) Compilation** is a runtime optimization technique employed by modern JVMs. Instead of interpreting bytecode forever, the JVM identifies "hot" methods — those executed many times — and compiles them **on the fly** to native machine code tailored to the **actual CPU** running the program. **Tiered Compilation in HotSpot:** HotSpot begins with the **interpreter** (fast startup, slow steady-state) and progressively escalates as it gathers profiling data: | Tier | Compiler | Trigger | Output | Goal | |:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---| | 0 | Interpreter | First call. | Interpreted execution. | Quick startup. | | 1 | C1 (Client) | After a few invocations. | Lightly optimized native code. | Fast warm-up. | | 2 | C1 + Profiling | More invocations. | Code with type feedback counters. | Profile-guided. | | 3 | C2 (Server) | Method is "very hot". | Highly optimized native code. | Peak steady-state performance. | | 4 | C2 (post-opt) | After deoptimization recovery. | Re-optimized code. | Stability. | Each tier has its own *code cache*; the JVM may even deoptimize compiled code back to interpreted mode if a hot assumption (e.g., "this reference is never null") is violated. **Why "Start Slow, Then Faster":** - **Startup phase:** The interpreter executes bytecode one instruction at a time — slow but instant. - **C1 phase:** Hot methods are compiled with simple, safe optimizations — already faster. - **C2 phase:** Aggressive optimizations (inlining, escape analysis, loop transformations) produce near-C++ performance. - **Cumulative effect:** As more methods are compiled and profile data matures, the program **accelerates** the longer it runs. This is the **"warm-up"** phenomenon. > **[Mark Distribution:** 1 mark for JIT definition, 3 marks for tiered table, 1 mark for deoptimization, 2 marks for "start slow / faster" reasoning.**]** --- #### Model Solution — Q.B (b) [7 marks] **Comparison of JDK, JRE, JVM:** | Component | Full Form | Purpose | Key Contents | |:---|:---|:---|:---| | **JVM** | Java Virtual Machine | Executes bytecode. | Class loader, verifier, interpreter, JIT, GC. | | **JRE** | Java Runtime Environment | Provides everything needed to *run* Java programs. | JVM + core libraries (`rt.jar` / `java.base` module). | | **JDK** | Java Development Kit | Provides everything needed to *develop and run*. | JRE + `javac` + `javadoc` + `jar` + debugger. | > The relationship is **strictly nested**: > $$\text{JDK} \supset \text{JRE} \supset \text{JVM}$$ > A *user* who only wants to *run* a Java program needs only the **JRE**. A *developer* who wants to *write* Java programs needs the full **JDK** (which already includes the JRE). **Diagram — Source Code to Execution:** ``` ┌─────────────┐ javac ┌──────────────┐ │ Hello.java │ ──────────────► │ Hello.class │ │ (source) │ (build time) │ (bytecode) │ └─────────────┘ └──────────────┘ │ │ java Hello │ (run time) ▼ ┌──────────────────────┐ │ JVM in memory │ │ ┌──────────────────┐ │ │ │ 1. Class Loader │ │ ── loads Hello.class │ │ 2. Verifier │ │ ── checks safety │ │ 3. Interpreter │ │ ── runs bytecode │ │ 4. JIT Compiler │ │ ── hot methods → native │ └──────────────────┘ │ └──────────────────────┘ │ ▼ ┌──────────────────┐ │ Output on │ │ Console │ │ "Hello, KTU!" │ └──────────────────┘ ``` **Step-by-step lifecycle:** 1. **Write** the source: `Hello.java`. 2. **Compile** with `javac Hello.java` → produces `Hello.class`. 3. **Launch** with `java Hello` → JVM starts. 4. **Load** the class via the class loader subsystem. 5. **Verify** the bytecode (type safety, stack consistency, no illegal jumps). 6. **Initialize** static fields and run any `static {}` blocks. 7. **Interpret** the `main` method's bytecode, with **JIT** compiling hot loops. 8. **Print** the result and exit cleanly. > **[Mark Distribution:** 3 marks for comparison table, 2 marks for diagram, 2 marks for lifecycle description.**]** --- ## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Use this section as a 5-minute revision sheet before the exam.** - 🔹 **Java is a hybrid language** — it is *compiled* (to bytecode) **and** *interpreted+JIT compiled* (to native). It is **not** purely compiled, **not** purely interpreted. - 🔹 **`javac`** = source → bytecode (build time, offline, JDK tool). - 🔹 **JIT** = bytecode → native (run time, online, inside JVM). - 🔹 **JDK ⊃ JRE ⊃ JVM** — strict nesting. JDK is the largest; JRE is for users; JVM is the engine. - 🔹 **Magic number** of a `.class` file = **`0xCAFEBABE`**. Memorize as *"CAFE BABE"*. - 🔹 Bytecode opcodes are each **1 byte** wide. The JVM is a **stack-based** machine (not register-based). - 🔹 Five phases of `javac`: **Lexical → Syntax → Semantic → Annotation → Bytecode** (mnemonic: *"L-S-S-A-B"*). - 🔹 Constant pool stores all symbolic references; resolved to direct addresses at runtime. - 🔹 HotSpot uses **tiered compilation**: Interpreter → C1 → C1+Profile → C2. - 🔹 **Bytecode ≠ machine code.** A CPU cannot run `.class` files; only a JVM can. - 🔹 Useful tools: `javac` (compile), `java` (run), `javap` (disassemble), `jar` (archive), `javadoc` (docs), `jaotc` (AOT). - 🔹 **Java is platform-independent at the bytecode level; platform-dependent only at the JIT level** — a classic 2-mark "short note" answer. - 🔹 Two common confusions to avoid: 1. `java` command ≠ compiler. It *launches* the JVM. The compiler is `javac`. 2. `.class` files ≠ executables on their own. They are inputs to the JVM. > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Power Phrase (use verbatim in exams):** > *"The Java compiler (`javac`) translates `.java` source files into platform-independent JVM bytecode (`.class` files), which are then loaded, verified, interpreted, and Just-In-Time compiled by the JVM into native machine code at runtime — thereby combining the portability of interpretation with the performance of native compilation."*

topics.title: Java Virtual Machine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT633 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Java Virtual Machine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Java Virtual Machine (JVM)** is the cornerstone of the Java platform — an abstract, stack-based computing machine that provides the runtime environment in which Java *bytecode* (intermediate representation generated by the `javac` compiler) can be executed. Formally, the JVM is specified by the **Java Virtual Machine Specification (JVMS)** maintained by Oracle, and it forms the computational engine of the **Java Runtime Environment (JRE)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECIT633 – Module 1):**
> The JVM is a *specification*, not a piece of software. Vendors (Oracle JDK, OpenJDK, Azul Zulu, Eclipse Temurin, GraalVM) provide *implementations* of this specification for different hardware/OS combinations. This is the architectural reason behind Java's **"Write Once, Run Anywhere" (WORA)** paradigm.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Universal Translator" Analogy**
> Imagine you are a diplomat (your program) who speaks only **English (Java source code)**. You arrive in a foreign country (Windows, Linux, macOS, embedded ARM device). You do **not** know the local language. The **JVM acts as a universal, on-the-fly interpreter** that lives inside the country, takes your English statement, and translates it into the local dialect.
>
> - Your source `.java` file is compiled **once** into a `.class` file (bytecode) — analogous to writing a **letter in a universal script (Esperanto)**.
> - Every operating system has its **own JVM** that understands this universal script and converts it into native machine instructions for that specific hardware.
>
> The native OS never sees your original Java code — it only sees the **JVM** doing the work.

### 1.3 Position of JVM in the Java Ecosystem

| Layer | Component | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Top | Java Source (`.java`) | Human-readable code written by developer |
| Middle | Java Compiler (`javac`) | Converts source → bytecode (`.class`) |
| **Core Engine** | **JVM** | **Loads, verifies, and executes bytecode** |
| Bottom | Host Operating System / Hardware | Provides native resources |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (Frequently asked in KTU):**
> - **JVM** = Runtime engine only
> - **JRE** = JVM + Core Libraries (rt.jar / jrt-fs)
> - **JDK** = JRE + Development Tools (`javac`, `javadoc`, `jdb`)
>
> Therefore: **JDK ⊃ JRE ⊃ JVM**

### 1.4 Physical / Standard Constants

- **Bytecode instruction size:** exactly **1 byte** (opcodes range 0x00 – 0xFF = 256 possible opcodes)
- **JVM data type widths:** `byte` = **8 bits**, `short` = **16 bits**, `int` = **32 bits**, `long` = **64 bits**, `float` = **32 bits (IEEE 754)**, `double` = **64 bits (IEEE 754)**, `reference` = **32 bits (typically)**
- **Object header (HotSpot, 64-bit with compressed oops):** **12 bytes** (mark word 8B + class pointer 4B)
- **Default thread stack size:** **512 KB** (HotSpot) / **1 MB** (Linux default)
- **Default heap sizes:** `-Xms` (initial) and `-Xmx` (max) configurable
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Three Independent Subsystems of the JVM

The JVM is architecturally divided into three major subsystems, each with a clearly demarcated responsibility:

#### **A. ClassLoader Subsystem (The Delivery Truck)**
Responsible for **loading, linking, and initializing** `.class` files into the JVM's memory. It uses a *delegation model* (parent-first) to ensure security and uniqueness.

- **Loading:** Reads the `.class` file and creates a binary representation in method area.
- **Linking:** Three sub-phases —
  1. **Verify** — bytecode integrity check (no stack overflow, valid opcodes, type safety).
  2. **Prepare** — allocates memory for class-level `static` variables and assigns them **default values** (e.g., `int → 0`, `boolean → false`).
  3. **Resolve** — symbolic references in the constant pool are replaced with direct references.
- **Initialization:** Executes the `<clinit>` method — assigns *actual* user-defined values to static variables, runs `static` blocks in source order.

#### **B. Runtime Data Areas (The Warehouse)**
Memory regions allocated when the JVM starts. These are the **physical memory zones** used during execution.

#### **C. Execution Engine (The Worker)**
Reads bytecode, interprets it, and (if profitable) compiles hot code paths to native machine code using **Just-In-Time (JIT)** compilation. Manages the **Garbage Collector (GC)** for automatic heap reclamation.

### 2.2 The ClassLoader Delegation Model (Why?)

To prevent malicious code from replacing trusted core Java classes, the JVM uses a hierarchical chain-of-command:

1. **Bootstrap ClassLoader** (C/C++ implemented, loads `rt.jar`) — *root*
2. **Platform ClassLoader** (Java 9+; loads `java.sql`, `javax.*` extensions)
3. **Application ClassLoader** (loads classes from the classpath)
4. **Custom ClassLoaders** (developer-defined; e.g., Tomcat's `WebAppClassLoader`)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Delegation Rule (Parent-First):** When a class loader receives a load request, it **first delegates to its parent**. Only if the parent fails (throws `ClassNotFoundException`) does the child attempt to load the class itself. This is the "**Parent Delegation Model**" — a high-yield KTU question.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Bytecode size per instruction | $\text{size} = 1 \text{ byte}$ | OpCodes: $0 \le n \le 255$ |
| Object header size (64-bit HotSpot, compressed oops) | $\text{Header} = 8 (\text{mark word}) + 4 (\text{klass pointer})$ | **= 12 bytes** |
| Object header (no compressed oops) | $\text{Header} = 8 + 8$ | **= 16 bytes** |
| Array header overhead | $\text{arrayHeader} = 16 \text{ bytes}$ | 12B header + 4B length |
| Heap memory constraint | $X_{ms} \le X_{mx}$ | Initial heap ≤ Max heap |
| Stack slot size | $\text{slot} = 4 \text{ bytes}$ | Holds `int`, `float`, `reference` |
| Long/Double on stack | occupy $\vert 2 \text{ slots} \vert$ | Locals: `llocals` and `llocals+1` |
| Just-In-Time compilation threshold | $N_{invocations} \approx 10\,000$ | Server Compiler (`C2`) flag |
| Heap region size (G1 GC) | $2^{n} \text{ MB}, \; 1 \le n \le 32$ | Power-of-two regions |
| Method area = PermGen (pre-Java 8) / Metaspace (Java 8+) | $\text{Metaspace} = \text{native memory}$ | Default unlimited |

> **Engineering Utility:** The JVM's memory model underpins *every* Java application — from Android phones (ART — Android Runtime, a JVM variant) to high-frequency trading systems (Azul Zing / OpenJ9) and big-data clusters (Hadoop/Spark JVMs).

### 2.4 Why "Stack-Based" and Not "Register-Based"?

A *stack-based* machine pushes operands onto an operand stack, applies an operator, and pushes the result. A *register-based* machine (like the actual CPU) operates directly on named registers.

> Java's bytecode is **stack-based** for **portability** — the same bytecode works on x86, ARM, RISC-V, and PowerPC without modification, because the JVM (not the hardware) manages the stack simulation. Modern JIT compilers (C1, C2, Graal) then convert this stack-based IR into optimized *register-based* native code at runtime.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Memory Footprint of a Java Object

Let us derive the heap memory consumed by a single Java object instance — a high-value calculation in performance engineering.

**Problem Setup:**
Consider a class:
```java
class Employee {
    private long id;        // 8 bytes
    private int  age;        // 4 bytes
    private boolean active; // 1 byte
}
```

**Step 1 — Compute instance data size (raw):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DataSize}_{\text{raw}} &= \text{sizeof(long)} + \text{sizeof(int)} + \text{sizeof(boolean)} \\
&= 8 + 4 + 1 \\
&= 13 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply JVM field alignment (8-byte rule in 64-bit HotSpot):**

Fields are reordered by the JVM for compactness: `long` first, then `int`, then `boolean`. The total is padded up to the nearest multiple of 8.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DataSize}_{\text{aligned}} &= \lceil 13 / 8 \rceil \times 8 \\
&= 2 \times 8 \\
&= 16 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Add object header (12 bytes with compressed oops):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Size} &= \text{Header} + \text{DataSize}_{\text{aligned}} \\
&= 12 + 16 \\
&= 28 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify alignment of final size:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Aligned Total} &= \lceil 28 / 8 \rceil \times 8 \\
&= 4 \times 8 \\
&= \mathbf{32 \text{ bytes per Employee object}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Use `jol` (Java Object Layout) tool from OpenJDK to verify. The output of `GraphLayout.parseInstance(obj).totalSize()` will yield `32` — matching our derivation.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Class Loading Phases vs. Actions

| Phase | Action Performed | Memory Allocated? | Errors Raised |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Loading** | Read `.class` binary; create `Class<?>` object in method area | Yes (Method Area) | `ClassFormatError`, `ClassNotFoundException` |
| **Verifying** | Bytecode type-safety, stack consistency, control-flow validity | No | `VerifyError`, `LinkageError` |
| **Preparing** | Allocate memory for `static` fields; assign **default** values (0/null/false) | Yes (Method Area) | `OutOfMemoryError` |
| **Resolving** | Convert symbolic refs → direct refs (e.g., `java/lang/Object` → memory pointer) | No (refs only) | `NoSuchFieldError`, `IllegalAccessError` |
| **Initializing** | Execute `<clinit>`: assign user values, run `static {}` blocks in source order | No (writes existing memory) | `ExceptionInInitializerError` |

### 3.3 The `main()` Method — Bytecode-Level Derivation

Consider:
```java
public class Hello {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int x = 5;
        int y = x + 3;
        System.out.println(y);
    }
}
```

**Step 1 — Compilation produces `Hello.class` with these bytecodes for `main`:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Offset} &\quad \text{Opcode} \quad \text{Operands} \quad \text{Description} \\
\hline
0: \; &\; \text{iconst\_5}     \quad \; —           \quad \text{Push int constant 5 onto stack} \\
1: \; &\; \text{istore\_1}     \quad \; —           \quad \text{Pop → local var 1 (x)} \\
2: \; &\; \text{iload\_1}      \quad \; —           \quad \text{Push x} \\
3: \; &\; \text{iconst\_3}     \quad \; —           \quad \text{Push 3} \\
4: \; &\; \text{iadd}         \quad \; —           \quad \text{Pop 2, push sum} \\
5: \; &\; \text{istore\_2}     \quad \; —           \quad \text{Pop → local var 2 (y)} \\
6: \; &\; \text{getstatic}    \quad \; \#2 \quad      \text{Push System.out} \\
9: \; &\; \text{iload\_2}      \quad \; —           \quad \text{Push y} \\
10: \; &\; \text{invokevirtual} \quad \; \#3 \quad   \text{Call println(int)} \\
13: \; &\; \text{return}      \quad \; —           \quad \text{void return}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Each opcode = 1 byte → the entire method's bytecode is 14 bytes long (plus operand bytes for `getstatic` and `invokevirtual`).**

### 3.4 Derivation: Garbage Collection Eligibility

An object is eligible for GC when **no live thread** has a reference (strong, soft, weak, or phantom — depending on the reference type) pointing to it. The mathematically precise definition uses **reachability graphs**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{GC Roots} = \{ & \text{local variables of all JVM stack frames}, \\
                      & \text{active threads}, \\
                      & \text{static fields of loaded classes}, \\
                      & \text{JNI native references} \} \\[4pt]
\text{Reachable}(o) &\iff \exists \text{ a path from any } g \in \text{GC Roots to } o \\
\text{Eligible}_{GC}(o) &\iff \neg \text{Reachable}(o)
\end{aligned}
$$

> If a student defines GC eligibility as "no reference points to it," they lose **2 marks** in KTU valuation — the correct answer must invoke **GC Roots and reachability**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level JVM Architecture (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classFile[".class file (Bytecode)"]
    clsSub["ClassLoader Subsystem"]
    rtData["Runtime Data Areas"]
    execEng["Execution Engine"]
    jni["JNI + Native Method Libraries"]

    loading["Loading"]
    linking["Linking Verify Prepare Resolve"]
    init["Initialization clinit"]

    methodArea["Method Area"]
    heap["Heap"]
    jvmStack["JVM Stacks"]
    pcReg["PC Registers"]
    nms["Native Method Stack"]

    interp["Interpreter"]
    jit["JIT Compiler C1 C2 Graal"]
    gc["Garbage Collector G1 ZGC Shenandoah"]

    classFile --> clsSub
    clsSub --> loading
    clsSub --> linking
    clsSub --> init
    loading --> rtData
    linking --> rtData
    init --> rtData

    rtData --> methodArea
    rtData --> heap
    rtData --> jvmStack
    rtData --> pcReg
    rtData --> nms

    methodArea --> execEng
    heap --> execEng
    jvmStack --> execEng
    pcReg --> execEng

    execEng --> interp
    execEng --> jit
    execEng --> gc

    execEng -.uses.-> jni
    jni -.calls.-> methodArea
```

### 4.2 ClassLoader Delegation Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    bootCL["Bootstrap ClassLoader C C++ Loads rt.jar java.base module"]
    platCL["Platform ClassLoader Loads java.sql javax crypto extensions"]
    appCL["Application ClassLoader Loads classpath classes"]
    custCL["Custom ClassLoaders Tomcat WebAppPlugin OSGi Bundles"]

    bootCL --- platCL
    platCL --- appCL
    appCL --- custCL

    req["Class Load Request for com.app.MyClass"] --> appCL
    appCL -->|"delegate up"| platCL
    platCL -->|"delegate up"| bootCL
    bootCL -->|"Not found return"| platCL
    platCL -->|"Not found return"| appCL
    appCL -->|"Try own load Found instantiate"| success["MyClass Loaded Successfully"]
```

### 4.3 JVM Memory Layout — Thread-Shared vs. Thread-Private Regions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Shared["Thread-Shared Regions created at JVM startup"]
        ma["Method Area Class metadata Constant pool Static fields Method bytecode"]
        hp["Heap Young Gen Eden Survivor 0 Survivor 1 Old Gen Tenured"]
    end

    subgraph Private["Thread-Private Regions one per thread"]
        pc["PC Register address of current instruction"]
        jstack["JVM Stack Frames Local variables Operand stack"]
        nms["Native Method Stack for C C++ calls"]
    end

    style Shared fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style Private fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
```

### 4.4 Bytecode Execution Flow Through the JVM

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Src as Java Source .java
    participant Comp as javac Compiler
    participant BC as Bytecode .class
    participant CL as ClassLoader
    participant MA as Method Area
    participant EE as Execution Engine
    participant HW as Native Hardware

    Src->>Comp: Human-written code
    Comp->>BC: Compile to bytecode
    BC->>CL: Class loader reads file
    CL->>CL: Verify bytecode integrity
    CL->>MA: Store class metadata
    Note over EE: Thread starts running main
    EE->>MA: Fetch opcode at PC register
    EE->>EE: Interpret or JIT compile
    EE->>HW: Emit native CPU instructions
    HW-->>EE: Return result via operand stack
    EE-->>CL: Garbage collector reclaims unused objects
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📘 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper PECIT633]**
*Explain the three subsystems of the Java Virtual Machine.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The JVM is composed of three major subsystems:
1. **ClassLoader Subsystem** (1 mark) — performs Loading, Linking (Verify, Prepare, Resolve), and Initialization of `.class` files using the Parent Delegation Model.
2. **Runtime Data Areas** (1 mark) — memory regions including Method Area, Heap, JVM Stack, PC Register, and Native Method Stack; the first two are thread-shared, the rest are thread-private.
3. **Execution Engine** (1 mark) — executes bytecode using the Interpreter and JIT Compiler, and includes the Garbage Collector for automatic memory management.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Exam]**
*Differentiate between JDK, JRE, and JVM.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Component | Contents | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **JVM** | Runtime engine only | Executes bytecode |
| **JRE** | JVM + Core libraries (`java.lang`, `java.util`, `java.io`) | Provides runtime environment |
| **JDK** | JRE + Development tools (`javac`, `javadoc`, `jdb`, `jar`) | Used for developing Java programs |

Therefore, **JDK ⊃ JRE ⊃ JVM** (1 mark each for content and relationship).

---

### 📗 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

---

**Question A. [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, PECIT633 Module 1]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **architecture of the Java Virtual Machine**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The JVM architecture consists of three subsystems:

**1. ClassLoader Subsystem** (2 marks)
- **Loading:** The class loader reads the `.class` file from disk and stores its binary representation in the Method Area. Three built-in class loaders exist: Bootstrap (loads `rt.jar`), Platform (loads extension classes), and Application (loads classpath classes).
- **Linking:** Combines the in-memory class with the JVM's runtime state through Verify, Prepare, and Resolve.
- **Initialization:** Executes the `<clinit>` method which assigns real values to static fields and runs `static` blocks in textual order.

**2. Runtime Data Areas** (2 marks)
- **Method Area (shared):** Stores class-level metadata, constant pool, static variables, and method bytecode.
- **Heap (shared):** Stores all object instances and arrays; subject to Garbage Collection.
- **JVM Stack (per thread):** Each method call creates a stack frame containing local variables, operand stack, and frame data.
- **PC Register (per thread):** Holds the address of the current executing JVM instruction.
- **Native Method Stack (per thread):** Supports native (C/C++) method calls.

**3. Execution Engine** (2 marks)
- **Interpreter:** Reads and executes bytecode one instruction at a time — fast startup, slow steady-state.
- **JIT Compiler (C1, C2, Graal):** Compiles *hot* methods to native machine code for performance; the compiled code is cached in the Code Cache.
- **Garbage Collector:** Periodically reclaims memory from unreachable objects. Modern algorithms include G1, ZGC, and Shenandoah.
- **JNI:** Bridges Java with native libraries (`.dll`/`.so`).

**Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to Section 4.1 flowchart.

---

**(b)** Explain the **Parent Delegation Model** used by ClassLoaders. Why is it necessary? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Mechanism (4 marks):**
When the JVM receives a request to load a class (say, `com.app.MyClass`), the request is sent to the **Application ClassLoader**. This loader does **not** attempt to load the class immediately. Instead, it **delegates the request upward** to its parent — the **Platform ClassLoader**. The Platform ClassLoader, in turn, delegates to the **Bootstrap ClassLoader** (the root, implemented in C/C++).

The Bootstrap ClassLoader searches its trusted path (e.g., `java.base` module). If it finds the class, it loads it. If not, control returns to the Platform ClassLoader, which tries its own paths. If it also fails, the request returns to the Application ClassLoader, which finally attempts to load the class from the application classpath.

**Necessity (3 marks):**
1. **Security:** Prevents malicious code from substituting a fake `java.lang.String` class into the JVM.
2. **Uniqueness:** Ensures that the same class is loaded only once (saves memory).
3. **Consistency:** Guarantees that core Java API classes are the *same versions* for all applications, preventing type-incompatibility errors.

---

**OR**

---

**Question B. [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2024, PECIT633 Module 1]**

**(a)** Differentiate between **JVM Stack** and **Heap** memory. How are local variables and objects stored in each? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | JVM Stack | Heap |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Per-thread (thread-private) | Shared across all threads |
| **Memory lifecycle** | Created at thread start, destroyed at thread death | Exists from JVM start to JVM shutdown |
| **Content** | Stack frames, local variables, partial results, method calls | Objects, instance variables, arrays |
| **Access speed** | Very fast (LIFO, contiguous) | Slower (GC-managed) |
| **Thread safety** | Inherently thread-safe | Requires synchronization |
| **Size** | Small (~512 KB – 1 MB per thread) | Large (configurable via `-Xmx`) |
| **Error** | `StackOverflowError` | `OutOfMemoryError` |

**Storage of local variables (2 marks):** Primitive local variables (`int`, `double`, etc.) and **references** to objects are stored directly in stack frames. Each frame has a `locals[]` array; the slot width is 4 bytes (8 bytes for `long`/`double` spanning 2 slots).

**Storage of objects (2 marks):** The **object itself** (its instance fields, header) is always allocated in the **Heap**. The reference to it is stored on the stack (as a local variable) or in another object (as a field). This is why the statement `Employee e = new Employee();` allocates the `Employee` object in the heap but stores the reference `e` in the stack frame of the current method.

---

**(b)** Explain the **lifecycle of a class** inside the JVM — Loading, Linking, and Initialization — with an example. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Consider:
```java
class Database {
    static String url = "jdbc:mysql://localhost/db"; // prepare assigns null, init assigns this
    static { System.out.println("DB initialized"); }
}
public class App {
    public static void main(String[] a) { Database d = new Database(); }
}
```

**1. Loading (2 marks):**
The JVM receives a request to use `Database` (triggered by `new Database()`). The **Application ClassLoader** delegates to its parents (Platform → Bootstrap). Bootstrap returns "not found" (since `Database` is application-defined). Control returns to the Application ClassLoader, which reads `Database.class` from the classpath, parses its constant pool, and creates a `java.lang.Class<Database>` object in the Method Area.

**2. Linking (3 marks):**
- **Verify:** Checks bytecode for type-safety, valid jumps, and stack consistency. Catches tampering with the `.class` file.
- **Prepare:** Allocates memory for the static field `url` in the Method Area and assigns it the **default value** `null` (since `String` is a reference type).
- **Resolve:** The constant pool entry for `java.lang.Object` is replaced with a direct memory pointer to the `Object` class.

**3. Initialization (2 marks):**
The `<clinit>` method is executed: the field `url` is set to `"jdbc:mysql://localhost/db"` and the `static` block prints `"DB initialized"`. The class is now fully **initialized** and ready for use. The object `new Database()` is then allocated in the heap.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Prepare vs. Initialize** — students often claim static variables are set to user values during *Prepare*. The correct answer: Prepare assigns *default* values (0/null/false); *Initialization* assigns user values. (Lose 2 marks if swapped.)
> 2. **Saying "garbage collection removes objects"** — the precise wording is *"objects become eligible for GC when unreachable from any GC Root"*. (Lose 1 mark.)
> 3. **Forgetting thread-shared vs. thread-private** distinction in Runtime Data Areas. (Lose 2 marks.)
> 4. **Omitting the diagram** in 7-mark architecture questions. A well-labelled diagram earns **at least 1 mark** in KTU valuation.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The **JVM is a specification**, not software — vendors implement it.
- ✅ **WORA** (Write Once, Run Anywhere) is enabled because bytecode is platform-independent.
- ✅ **Three subsystems**: ClassLoader, Runtime Data Areas, Execution Engine.
- ✅ **Parent Delegation Model** is the security backbone of Java's class loading.
- ✅ **Loading → Linking (Verify, Prepare, Resolve) → Initialization** — the three-phase class lifecycle.
- ✅ **Method Area & Heap** are thread-shared; **Stack, PC Register, Native Method Stack** are thread-private.
- ✅ **Bytecode = 1 byte per instruction**; stack-based architecture.
- ✅ **Object header = 12 bytes** (64-bit HotSpot with compressed oops).
- ✅ **JIT compiler** converts hot bytecode to native code for performance.
- ✅ **Garbage collection** uses *reachability from GC Roots* — not simply "no references".
- ✅ **JDK ⊃ JRE ⊃ JVM** — memorize this hierarchy.
- ✅ **Default values in Prepare phase**, real values in **Initialization phase** — a favorite KTU trap.
- ✅ **StackOverflowError** = stack; **OutOfMemoryError** = heap; **ClassFormatError** = class loading.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Primitive Data Types and Wrapper Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECIT633 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Primitive Data Types and Wrapper Types # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What are Primitive Data Types? **Primitive Data Types** are the most fundamental, built-in data types in Java that are **not objects** and store raw, simple values directly in memory (typically on the stack). They are predefined by the Java language specification and serve as the atomic building blocks for data manipulation. > [!NOTE] > **Formal KTU 2024 Definition** > *"Primitive data types in Java are the basic data types that hold pure, simple values and have no methods or properties. Java defines eight such types: `byte`, `short`, `int`, `long`, `float`, `double`, `char`, and `boolean`."* ### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy: "The Post Office Boxes" Imagine a **post office with 8 different sizes of lockers**: - The **tiny locker (byte)** holds a single small parcel. - The **giant locker (double)** holds huge industrial shipments. - Each locker is **pre-built** by the post office (built into the language) and you just put your item in. - You **cannot** add extra features to a locker (no methods), but you can always **upgrade** a parcel to a bigger locker (type promotion/widening). In Java, primitives are those **pre-built lockers** — fast, efficient, and minimal. --- ## 1.2 What are Wrapper Types? **Wrapper Types** (or **Wrapper Classes**) are **object counterparts** of each primitive data type. They "wrap" the primitive value inside an object so that the value can be used wherever objects are required (e.g., Collections, Generics, Serialization). The eight wrapper classes in `java.lang` are: `Byte`, `Short`, `Integer`, `Long`, `Float`, `Double`, `Character`, `Boolean`. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 1)** > All wrapper classes are **immutable** (their value cannot be changed after creation) and belong to the `java.lang` package — they are **auto-imported**; no `import` statement is needed. ### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy: "The Gift Box" Take that small parcel (a `byte` value like `25`). If you put it inside a **decorative gift box** (the `Byte` object), you now have: - The parcel is still inside (value `25`). - The box has a **ribbon** and a **gift tag** (methods like `parseByte()`, `intValue()`). - The box can be **mailed to fancy services** like Collections or Generics that only accept "boxes", not loose parcels. A **Wrapper Class = Primitive value + Object features (methods) + Ability to interact with Java's object framework**. --- ## 1.3 Why Do We Need Wrappers When Primitives Exist? | Scenario | Why Primitive Fails | Why Wrapper Works | |----------|--------------------|--------------------| | `ArrayList` | Generics **only accept objects** | `ArrayList` works | | Nullability | Primitives **cannot be `null`** | `Integer x = null;` is valid | | Serialization | Objects serialize naturally | Wrappers support Java's serialization framework | | Thread Synchronization | Cannot lock on a primitive | Can synchronize on a wrapper object | | Reflection | Primitives are opaque | Wrapper classes expose fields via reflection | > [!TIP] > **KTU Board Tip:** In exams, when asked *"Why wrapper classes?"* always mention **Collections, Generics, and nullability** as the three core reasons. Examiners reward these three keywords. --- ## 1.4 The Java Type System at a Glance $$\text{Java Types} = \begin{cases} \text{Primitive Types (8)} \\ \text{Reference Types} \begin{cases} \text{Class Types (Wrapper Classes)} \\ \text{Array Types} \\ \text{Interface Types} \end{cases} \end{cases}$$ This is critical for KTU: **everything in Java is either a primitive or a reference** — there is no middle ground. The wrapper classes bridge these two worlds. # 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Eight Primitive Data Types — Detailed Breakdown Java specifies **exactly 8 primitive types**, each with a fixed size (in bits/bytes), a default value, and a defined range. These are part of the **Java Language Specification (JLS)** and are **platform-independent** (the size is the same on Windows, Linux, Mac — a key Java design decision). ### 2.1.1 Integer Types (Signed, 2's Complement) > [!NOTE] > **All integer types in Java are SIGNED.** There is no `unsigned` keyword in standard Java. They use **2's complement representation** to store negative numbers. **Range Formula for Signed Integers:** $$\text{Range} = [-2^{n-1},\ 2^{n-1} - 1]$$ where $n$ = number of bits. **Total Number of Distinct Values:** $$N_{\text{values}} = 2^n$$ ### 2.1.2 Floating-Point Types (IEEE 754 Standard) `float` and `double` follow the **IEEE 754** standard for floating-point arithmetic. - **`float`**: 32-bit, single-precision, suffix `f` or `F` (e.g., `3.14f`) - **`double`**: 64-bit, double-precision, default for decimal literals (e.g., `3.14`) $$\text{Precision} = \begin{cases} \text{float} \Rightarrow \sim 7 \text{ significant decimal digits} \\ \text{double} \Rightarrow \sim 15 \text{ significant decimal digits} \end{cases}$$ ### 2.1.3 Character Type - **`char`**: 16-bit, **unsigned**, uses **UTF-16** encoding. - Range: `\u0000` (0) to `\uFFFF` (65,535). ### 2.1.4 Boolean Type - **`boolean`**: Size is **JVM-dependent** (typically 1 bit conceptually, but 1 byte in practice in the JVM stack). - Only two valid values: `true` and `false`. --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorize this table — it appears in nearly every KTU Module 1 exam.** | Primitive Type | Wrapper Class | Size (bits/bytes) | Default Value | Min Value | Max Value | Category | |:--------------:|:-------------:|:-----------------:|:-------------:|:---------:|:---------:|:--------:| | `byte` | `Byte` | 8 / 1 | `0` | $-2^7 = -128$ | $2^7 - 1 = 127$ | Integer | | `short` | `Short` | 16 / 2 | `0` | $-2^{15} = -32{,}768$ | $2^{15} - 1 = 32{,}767$ | Integer | | `int` | `Integer` | 32 / 4 | `0` | $-2^{31} \approx -2.15 \times 10^9$ | $2^{31} - 1 \approx 2.15 \times 10^9$ | Integer | | `long` | `Long` | 64 / 8 | `0L` | $-2^{63}$ | $2^{63} - 1$ | Integer | | `float` | `Float` | 32 / 4 | `0.0f` | $\approx -3.4 \times 10^{38}$ | $\approx 3.4 \times 10^{38}$ | Floating | | `double` | `Double` | 64 / 8 | `0.0d` | $\approx -1.8 \times 10^{308}$ | $\approx 1.8 \times 10^{308}$ | Floating | | `char` | `Character` | 16 / 2 | `'\u0000'` | `'\u0000'` (0) | `'\uFFFF'` (65,535) | Character | | `boolean` | `Boolean` | 1* | `false` | N/A | N/A | Boolean | \* JVM-dependent, not strictly defined. --- ## 2.3 Wrapper Class Hierarchy All numeric wrapper classes extend the abstract class `java.lang.Number`. The `Character` and `Boolean` classes extend `Object` directly. $$\text{java.lang.Object} \begin{cases} \text{java.lang.Number} \begin{cases} \text{Byte} \\ \text{Short} \\ \text{Integer} \\ \text{Long} \\ \text{Float} \\ \text{Double} \end{cases} \\ \text{Character} \\ \text{Boolean} \end{cases}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters:** Because `Integer`, `Long`, `Float`, etc., all extend `Number`, you can call methods like `intValue()`, `doubleValue()`, `floatValue()` polymorphically. This is heavily tested in KTU viva and Part A questions. --- ## 2.4 Boxing, Unboxing, and Autoboxing These are the **three mechanisms** to convert between primitives and wrappers. ### 2.4.1 Manual Boxing (Pre-Java 5) **Definition:** Explicit conversion of a primitive to its wrapper object using `valueOf()` or constructor. ```java Integer obj = Integer.valueOf(50); // Recommended (uses cache) Integer obj2 = new Integer(50); // Deprecated since Java 9 ``` ### 2.4.2 Manual Unboxing **Definition:** Explicit conversion of a wrapper object back to its primitive using `xxxValue()` methods. ```java Integer obj = Integer.valueOf(50); int n = obj.intValue(); // Returns the primitive int ``` ### 2.4.3 Autoboxing (Java 5+) **Definition:** **Automatic** conversion performed by the compiler from primitive → wrapper. ```java Integer obj = 50; // Compiler auto-inserts Integer.valueOf(50) ``` ### 2.4.4 Auto-Unboxing (Java 5+) **Definition:** **Automatic** conversion performed by the compiler from wrapper → primitive. ```java Integer obj = 50; int n = obj; // Compiler auto-inserts obj.intValue() ``` > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Autoboxing happens **at compile time**, not runtime. The compiler inserts the corresponding `valueOf()` or `xxxValue()` call. Writing `Integer obj = 50;` is **syntactic sugar** — the bytecode is identical to `Integer obj = Integer.valueOf(50);`. --- ## 2.5 The Integer Cache — A Classic KTU Trap The `Integer` class (and `Byte`, `Short`, `Long`, `Character`) **caches** values in the range **$-128$ to $+127$**. - For values within this range, `valueOf(n)` returns the **same cached object**. - For values outside this range, a **new object** is created. > [!IMPORTANT] > **This is the most-asked wrapper concept in KTU Module 1 exams.** Memorize it. **Implication for `==` comparison:** ```java Integer a = 127; Integer b = 127; System.out.println(a == b); // true (same cached object) Integer c = 128; Integer d = 128; System.out.println(c == d); // false (different objects!) ``` Always use **`.equals()`** for wrapper comparison. # 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Why is the Range of `byte` Exactly [-128, 127]? > [!NOTE] > **Why this derivation matters:** Examiners love asking *"Why is the maximum value of byte 127 and not 128?"* Understanding the 2's complement mechanism gives you full marks. ### Step 1: Start with the Size A `byte` is **8 bits** in Java. $$n = 8 \text{ bits}$$ ### Step 2: Total Number of Distinct Bit Patterns For $n$ bits, the total number of unique binary patterns is: $$N_{\text{values}} = 2^n = 2^8 = 256$$ ### Step 3: Apply 2's Complement Split In 2's complement representation, the range is **not symmetric around zero**. The split is: - **Negative numbers:** occupy half of the patterns (including zero in the positive half). $$N_{\text{negative}} = 2^{n-1} = 2^7 = 128 \text{ values} \quad \Rightarrow \quad [-128, -1]$$ - **Non-negative numbers:** occupy the other half (including zero). $$N_{\text{non-negative}} = 2^{n-1} = 128 \text{ values} \quad \Rightarrow \quad [0, 127]$$ ### Step 4: Formulate the General Range For a signed integer type of $n$ bits: $$\text{Min} = -2^{n-1}$$ $$\text{Max} = +2^{n-1} - 1$$ ### Step 5: Apply to `byte` ($n = 8$) $$\text{Min} = -2^{8-1} = -2^7 = -128$$ $$\text{Max} = 2^{8-1} - 1 = 2^7 - 1 = 128 - 1 = 127$$ ### Step 6: Verify with the Bit Pattern `10000000` In 2's complement, the bit pattern `10000000` represents the **most negative number**, which is $-128$ (not $-0$, because $-0$ is the same as $0$ in 2's complement). This extra slot is the reason the negative side has one more value than the positive side. $$\text{Range of byte} = [-128,\ +127] \quad \blacksquare$$ --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 1: Type Promotion in Expressions **Question:** Evaluate the result of the following Java expression and identify the data type of the result. ```java byte a = 10; byte b = 20; byte c = a + b; System.out.println(c); ``` ### Step-by-Step Model Solution: **Step 1:** According to JLS §5.6.2, the binary numeric promotion rules state that when two values of type `byte` (or `short` or `char`) are combined with a binary arithmetic operator, **both are first promoted to `int`**. $$\text{byte } a = 10 \xrightarrow{\text{promotion}} \text{int } a' = 10$$ $$\text{byte } b = 20 \xrightarrow{\text{promotion}} \text{int } b' = 20$$ **Step 2:** The addition is performed at the `int` level. $$a' + b' = 10 + 20 = 30 \quad (\text{type: } \texttt{int})$$ **Step 3:** Attempting to assign this `int` result to a `byte` variable `c` requires an **explicit cast** (narrowing conversion). Without it: ```java byte c = a + b; // ❌ COMPILE ERROR: possible lossy conversion from int to byte ``` **Step 4:** Correct code: ```java byte c = (byte)(a + b); // ✅ Result: 30 ``` > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Students write `byte c = a + b;` and lose **2 marks** for compile error. Always mention **type promotion** and **explicit cast** in the answer. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 2: Autoboxing + Cache Pitfall **Question:** What is the output of the following Java snippet? Justify. ```java Integer x = 200; Integer y = 200; System.out.println(x == y); System.out.println(x.equals(y)); ``` ### Step-by-Step Model Solution: **Step 1:** Autoboxing converts `200` to `Integer.valueOf(200)`. $$\texttt{Integer x = Integer.valueOf(200);}$$ **Step 2:** Check the Integer cache range: $[-128, +127]$. $$200 \notin [-128,\ +127]$$ **Step 3:** Since `200` is **outside** the cache range, `valueOf(200)` creates a **new Integer object** on the heap each time. $$\text{x and y are two DIFFERENT objects in memory.}$$ **Step 4:** The `==` operator compares **references** (memory addresses) for objects. $$x \neq y \quad \Rightarrow \quad x == y \Rightarrow \texttt{false}$$ **Step 5:** The `.equals()` method compares **values** inside the wrappers. $$x.\texttt{equals}(y) \Rightarrow \texttt{true}$$ ### Final Output: ``` false true ``` --- ## 3.4 Worked Example 3: Autoboxing Performance Trap **Question:** Identify the performance issue in the following code. ```java Long sum = 0L; // Wrapper, not primitive for (long i = 0; i < Integer.MAX_VALUE; i++) { sum = sum + i; // Auto-unboxing + addition + autoboxing } ``` ### Step-by-Step Model Solution: **Step 1:** `sum + i` triggers **auto-unboxing** of `sum` (calls `sum.longValue()`), then primitive addition, then **autoboxing** of the result (calls `Long.valueOf(...)`). **Step 2:** This pattern creates **~2.1 billion** wrapper objects during the loop. **Step 3:** The fix is to use a primitive: ```java long sum = 0L; for (long i = 0; i < Integer.MAX_VALUE; i++) { sum = sum + i; } ``` > [!TIP] > **KTU Tip:** Mention this in viva to score bonus marks. Use primitives in tight loops; reserve wrappers for API boundaries (Collections, Generics). --- ## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Feature | Primitive Types | Wrapper Types | |:--------|:----------------|:--------------| | Stored in | Stack (typically) | Heap (as objects) | | Memory overhead | Minimal (1–8 bytes) | High (object header + data) | | Can be `null`? | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | | Has methods? | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (e.g., `parseInt`, `intValue`) | | Used in Collections? | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | | Default value | `0`, `0.0`, `false`, `'\u0000'` | `null` | | Comparison operator | `==` compares values | `==` compares references (use `.equals()`) | | Immutable? | N/A | ✅ Yes | | Performance | ⚡ Fast | 🐢 Slower (autoboxing overhead) | | Thread-safety | N/A | Immutable → thread-safe | # 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Class Hierarchy of Wrapper Classes ```mermaid graph TD A["java.lang.Object"] --> B["java.lang.Number (abstract)"] A --> C["java.lang.Boolean"] A --> D["java.lang.Character"] B --> E["java.lang.Byte"] B --> F["java.lang.Short"] B --> G["java.lang.Integer"] B --> H["java.lang.Long"] B --> I["java.lang.Float"] B --> J["java.lang.Double"] ``` > **Reading Guide:** `Number` is the abstract parent of all 6 numeric wrappers. `Boolean` and `Character` extend `Object` directly. This is the **only** inheritance tree you must memorize for Module 1. --- ## 4.2 Autoboxing & Auto-Unboxing Flow ```mermaid graph LR A["Primitive int (stack)"] -- "Autoboxing (compile-time)
valueOf(50)" --> B["Integer Object (heap)"] B -- "Auto-Unboxing (compile-time)
intValue()" --> A C["Primitive double"] -- "Autoboxing" --> D["Double Object"] D -- "Auto-Unboxing" --> C ``` > **Reading Guide:** The compiler inserts `valueOf()` and `intValue()` calls automatically. The double-headed arrows show that conversion is **bidirectional**. --- ## 4.3 Integer Cache Mechanism ```mermaid graph TD Start["Integer.valueOf(n)"] --> Check{"n in range -128 to 127?"} Check -- "YES" --> Cache["Return cached Integer object
(same reference)"] Check -- "NO" --> New["Create new Integer object on heap
(different reference)"] Cache --> Out1["x == y → true"] New --> Out2["x == y → false"] ``` > **Reading Guide:** Always use `.equals()` to compare wrapper values. The cache applies to `Integer`, `Byte`, `Short`, `Long`, and `Character` (range $-128$ to $+127$). `Float` and `Double` **do not** cache. --- ## 4.4 Primitive vs Wrapper: Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid graph TB subgraph Primitive["PRIMITIVE WORLD (Lightweight)"] P1["byte / short / int / long"] P2["float / double"] P3["char"] P4["boolean"] end subgraph Wrapper["WRAPPER WORLD (Object-Oriented)"] W1["Byte, Short, Integer, Long"] W2["Float, Double"] W3["Character"] W4["Boolean"] end subgraph Bridge["BRIDGE MECHANISMS"] B1["Autoboxing
(primitive → wrapper)"] B2["Auto-Unboxing
(wrapper → primitive)"] B3["valueOf() / xxxValue()"] end subgraph UseCases["OBJECT-ONLY FEATURES"] U1["Collections
List Integer, Set Double"] U2["Generics
T extends Number"] U3["Serialization"] U4["Nullability"] end Primitive -- "promote" --> Bridge Bridge -- "wrap" --> Wrapper Wrapper -- "enable" --> UseCases ``` > **Reading Guide:** Primitives live in the fast lane (stack). When Java APIs demand objects, the **Bridge Mechanisms** promote them to Wrappers, which then unlock **Object-Only Features** like Collections and Generics. # 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📝 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **List any four differences between primitive data types and wrapper classes in Java.** **Model Answer (4 key points — pick any 4 for 3 marks):** | # | Primitive Types | Wrapper Classes | |---|-----------------|-----------------| | 1 | Store raw values directly | Store values as objects | | 2 | Cannot be `null` | Can be `null` | | 3 | Cannot be used in Collections/Generics | Can be used in Collections/Generics | | 4 | No methods associated | Have utility methods (e.g., `parseInt`, `intValue`) | | 5 | Stored on the stack (typically) | Stored on the heap | --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **What is autoboxing? Give one example.** **Model Answer:** Autoboxing is the **automatic conversion of a primitive type into its corresponding wrapper object** performed by the Java compiler at compile time (introduced in Java 5). **Example:** ```java Integer obj = 50; // Autoboxing: int → Integer Double d = 3.14; // Autoboxing: double → Double Boolean flag = true; // Autoboxing: boolean → Boolean ``` The compiler automatically inserts `Integer.valueOf(50)`, `Double.valueOf(3.14)`, etc. --- ## 📝 PART B: Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice — 14 Marks) > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Module-level choice. Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each is split into two 7-mark sub-parts. --- ### **❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model] #### **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the eight primitive data types in Java with their size, range, and default values. Why does Java specify fixed sizes for these types? **Model Solution:** Java defines **eight primitive types**. They are platform-independent — the size is the same on every JVM (a deliberate design choice for portability, embodied in the slogan *"Write Once, Run Anywhere"*). **The Eight Primitive Types:** **1. Integer Types (signed, 2's complement):** - `byte` — **8 bits**, range $[-128, 127]$, default `0`. - `short` — **16 bits**, range $[-32{,}768, 32{,}767]$, default `0`. - `int` — **32 bits**, range $[-2^{31}, 2^{31}-1]$, default `0`. *Most commonly used integer type.* - `long` — **64 bits**, range $[-2^{63}, 2^{63}-1]$, default `0L`. *Used for large values (e.g., timestamps).* **2. Floating-Point Types (IEEE 754):** - `float` — **32 bits**, single-precision, default `0.0f`. ~7 significant digits. - `double` — **64 bits**, double-precision, default `0.0d`. ~15 significant digits. *Default for decimal literals.* **3. Character Type:** - `char` — **16 bits**, **unsigned**, UTF-16 encoded, range `['\u0000', '\uFFFF']`, default `'\u0000'`. **4. Boolean Type:** - `boolean` — represents `true` or `false`, default `false`. Size is **JVM-dependent** (conceptually 1 bit). **Why Fixed Sizes?** Java's designers wanted primitives to behave **predictably across platforms**. Unlike C/C++ (where `int` may be 16 or 32 bits depending on the machine), Java guarantees `int` is always 32 bits. This eliminates a major source of cross-platform bugs and is foundational to Java's **portability**. #### **(b) [7 Marks]** With a suitable Java program, demonstrate the concept of boxing, unboxing, autoboxing, and auto-unboxing. Explain what happens internally. **Model Solution:** **Java Program:** ```java public class WrapperDemo { public static void main(String[] args) { // 1. Manual Boxing (pre-Java 5 style) Integer obj1 = Integer.valueOf(100); // 2. Manual Unboxing int a = obj1.intValue(); // 3. Autoboxing (Java 5+) Integer obj2 = 200; // compiler inserts Integer.valueOf(200) // 4. Auto-Unboxing int b = obj2; // compiler inserts obj2.intValue() // 5. In expressions Integer sum = obj1 + obj2; // unbox both, add, autobox result int result = obj1 + 50; // unbox obj1, add primitive, no boxing System.out.println("obj1 = " + obj1); System.out.println("obj2 = " + obj2); System.out.println("sum = " + sum); System.out.println("result = " + result); } } ``` **Internal Explanation:** | Code Written | Compiler Inserts | Mechanism | |--------------|------------------|-----------| | `Integer obj2 = 200;` | `Integer obj2 = Integer.valueOf(200);` | Autoboxing | | `int b = obj2;` | `int b = obj2.intValue();` | Auto-Unboxing | | `Integer sum = obj1 + obj2;` | `Integer sum = Integer.valueOf(obj1.intValue() + obj2.intValue());` | Unbox + Add + Autobox | Autoboxing and auto-unboxing are **syntactic sugar** introduced in Java 5 to reduce boilerplate. They make the code cleaner but can introduce **performance overhead** in tight loops and **NullPointerException** risks if a wrapper is `null` during unboxing. --- ### **❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks)** [Alternative Choice] #### **(a) [7 Marks]** What are wrapper classes in Java? Why are they needed even though Java has primitive types? Explain the `Integer` cache with an example. **Model Solution:** **Wrapper Classes:** Wrapper classes are object representations of Java's eight primitive types, defined in the `java.lang` package. They "wrap" a primitive value inside an object, providing methods and enabling object-oriented features. The eight wrapper classes are: `Byte`, `Short`, `Integer`, `Long`, `Float`, `Double`, `Character`, `Boolean`. **Why Are They Needed?** 1. **Collections & Generics:** Java's Collections Framework (`ArrayList`, `HashMap`, etc.) and Generics (``) can **only work with objects**, not primitives. So `ArrayList` works but `ArrayList` does not. 2. **Nullability:** Primitives cannot be `null`, but wrappers can. This is useful for representing "no value" (e.g., a missing database field). 3. **Utility Methods:** Wrappers provide static methods like `Integer.parseInt("123")`, `Double.parseDouble("3.14")`, `Character.isDigit('5')`, etc. 4. **Serialization & Reflection:** Object-based frameworks (JavaBeans, JSON libraries) require object types. **The Integer Cache:** For performance, `Integer.valueOf(int n)` uses an **internal cache** for values in the range **$-128$ to $+127$**. Values within this range return the **same cached object**; values outside it create a **new object**. **Example:** ```java Integer a = 100, b = 100; Integer c = 200, d = 200; System.out.println(a == b); // true (same cached object) System.out.println(c == d); // false (different objects) System.out.println(c.equals(d)); // true (same value) ``` **Lesson:** Always use **`.equals()`** to compare wrapper objects, never `==`. #### **(b) [7 Marks]** Write a Java program to perform the following: (i) Convert a `String` to a primitive `int` using a wrapper method. (ii) Convert a primitive `int` to a `String` using a wrapper method. (iii) Demonstrate what happens if the `String` cannot be parsed. **Model Solution:** ```java public class ConversionDemo { public static void main(String[] args) { // (i) String -> primitive int String s1 = "1234"; int n1 = Integer.parseInt(s1); System.out.println("Parsed int: " + n1); // (ii) primitive int -> String int n2 = 5678; String s2 = Integer.toString(n2); System.out.println("Converted String: " + s2); // Alternative: using valueOf + concatenation String s3 = String.valueOf(n2); System.out.println("Using valueOf: " + s3); // (iii) Invalid parse demonstration try { String bad = "12AB"; int x = Integer.parseInt(bad); System.out.println(x); } catch (NumberFormatException e) { System.out.println("Error: " + e.getMessage()); } } } ``` **Expected Output:** ``` Parsed int: 1234 Converted String: 5678 Using valueOf: 5678 Error: For input string: "12AB" ``` **Explanation:** - `Integer.parseInt(String)` parses a decimal `String` and returns a primitive `int`. Throws `NumberFormatException` (a `RuntimeException`) if the string is not a valid integer. - `Integer.toString(int)` returns the `String` representation of the integer. - `String.valueOf(int)` is a flexible alternative that handles both primitives and objects. --- > [!WARNING] > **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > > **Common ways students LOSE marks in this topic:** > > 1. **Forgetting the `L` suffix on long literals:** Writing `long x = 1234567890123;` causes a compile error. Must write `1234567890123L`. > 2. **Confusing `==` with `.equals()` for wrappers:** `Integer a = 200; Integer b = 200; a == b` is `false` — students write `true` and lose **2 marks**. > 3. **Not knowing the cache range:** Always state **$-128$ to $+127$** explicitly. > 4. **Mixing up autoboxing with type casting:** Autoboxing ≠ casting. `(int) 3.14` is casting; `Integer x = (int) 3.14;` is **invalid** (cannot cast double to Integer directly). > 5. **Forgetting the default values:** Local primitive variables have **no default value** (uninitialized local `int` is a compile error). Default values apply only to **instance/static** variables. > 6. **Saying `char` is 8 bits:** It's **16 bits** (UTF-16) — a common trap. --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final 60-second revision — pin this in your memory before the exam.** ✅ **Java has exactly 8 primitive types:** `byte, short, int, long, float, double, char, boolean`. ✅ **All integer types are signed** (use 2's complement). Range formula: $[-2^{n-1},\ 2^{n-1}-1]$. ✅ **`char` is 16-bit UNSIGNED** UTF-16 — the only unsigned primitive in Java. ✅ **`boolean` size is JVM-dependent** — not strictly defined by the spec. ✅ **All 8 wrapper classes are in `java.lang`** (auto-imported, no `import` needed). ✅ **6 numeric wrappers extend `java.lang.Number`**; `Boolean` and `Character` extend `Object` directly. ✅ **Wrapper classes are IMMUTABLE** — values cannot be changed after creation. ✅ **Autoboxing = primitive → wrapper (compile-time sugar)**; Auto-unboxing = wrapper → primitive. ✅ **Integer cache range: $[-128, +127]$** — applies to `Byte, Short, Integer, Long, Character`. ✅ **Always use `.equals()` to compare wrappers** — `==` compares references and is unreliable outside the cache. ✅ **Wrappers CAN be `null`; primitives CANNOT** — auto-unboxing a `null` wrapper throws `NullPointerException`. ✅ **Wrappers are needed for Collections, Generics, nullability, and serialization** — three magic words for viva. ✅ **Primitives are faster** — use them in tight loops; reserve wrappers for API boundaries. ✅ **Default values (instance/static):** `0` (integer), `0.0` (float/double), `false` (boolean), `'\u0000'` (char). Local variables have **no default**. ✅ **Integer literal suffixes:** `L` for long, `f/F` for float, `d/D` for double (default). ✅ **Type promotion rule:** `byte`, `short`, `char` are promoted to `int` in binary arithmetic — requires explicit cast on assignment back.

topics.title: Casting and Autoboxing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECIT633 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Casting and Autoboxing # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Type Casting? **Type Casting** in Java is the mechanism of converting a value of one data type into another data type. It allows the programmer to explicitly or implicitly change the type of a variable, expression, or object reference to make it compatible with the operation being performed. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECIT633 Module 1):** > "Casting is the process of converting a value from one data type to another. It is of two types — *Widening* (implicit, safe) and *Narrowing* (explicit, potentially lossy). For objects, casting works along the inheritance hierarchy (upcasting and downcasting)." In Java, casting is broadly classified into: | Category | Sub-Type | Direction | Syntax | |----------|----------|-----------|--------| | **Primitive Casting** | Widening (Implicit) | small → large | automatic | | **Primitive Casting** | Narrowing (Explicit) | large → small | `(type)` | | **Reference Casting** | Upcasting (Implicit) | child → parent | automatic | | **Reference Casting** | Downcasting (Explicit) | parent → child | `(type)` | ## 1.2 What is Autoboxing? **Autoboxing** is the automatic conversion that the Java compiler makes between a **primitive type** and its corresponding **wrapper class object**. The reverse process — converting a wrapper object back to a primitive — is called **Unboxing**. Both features were introduced in **Java 5 (JDK 1.5)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The 8 Wrapper Classes (MUST memorize for KTU):** > `Byte`, `Short`, `Integer`, `Long`, `Float`, `Double`, `Boolean`, `Character` — they map directly to the 8 primitive types. ## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy **Casting Analogy — The Container Problem:** Imagine you have a small cup holding **250 ml** of water and a large jug that holds **1 litre**. Pouring the cup's water *into* the jug is effortless and safe — nothing is lost. This is **Widening (Upcasting)**. But pouring a full jug *back* into the cup is risky — water will overflow! You must deliberately tell the system *"I know what I'm doing, force the water into the cup."* This is **Narrowing (Downcasting)**. **Autoboxing Analogy — The Gift-Wrapping Service:** A primitive like `int x = 10;` is a *raw, unwrapped gift*. The wrapper class `Integer` is a *gift box* with the value safely enclosed inside. **Autoboxing** is like an automatic gift-wrapping counter at a shop — you hand over the raw `int` and the system hands you back a wrapped `Integer` box. **Unboxing** is the reverse: the counter unwraps the box and hands you back the bare primitive. > [!TIP] > **Why does Java need both?** Many Java APIs (especially the **Collections Framework** — `ArrayList`, `HashMap`, etc.) only store *objects*, not primitives. Autoboxing bridges this gap seamlessly so the programmer doesn't have to write manual conversion code. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Primitive Type Casting — Operational Rules Java follows a strict **type-promotion hierarchy**. Conversions follow the rule of "small fits into large" for widening, and require explicit operator-based truncation for narrowing. ### 2.1.1 Widening (Implicit / Automatic) Casting Performed automatically by the compiler when: - **Source type is smaller** than the **destination type**. - **No data loss** can occur. - The order is: `byte → short → int → long → float → double` - A `char` can be widened to `int` (gets the Unicode value), `long`, `float`, `double`. ```java int a = 100; double d = a; // Valid: int -> double (automatic) System.out.println(d); // Output: 100.0 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why `int → float` is widening despite different memory?** Because the *range* of values representable in `float` is larger than `int` (e.g., `float` can store up to ~3.4 × 10³⁸), even though it sacrifices some precision for very large numbers. ### 2.1.2 Narrowing (Explicit) Casting Required when: - **Source type is larger** than the **destination type**. - **Data loss / truncation** can occur. - Syntax: `(targetType) value` ```java double pi = 3.14159; int approx = (int) pi; // Explicit: double -> int System.out.println(approx); // Output: 3 (decimal part TRUNCATED, not rounded) ``` > [!WARNING] > **Truncation vs Rounding:** Narrowing **truncates** the fractional part. `(int) 3.9` gives `3`, not `4`. There is no automatic rounding in Java casting. ## 2.2 Reference (Object) Casting Works **only along an inheritance hierarchy**. Two siblings or unrelated classes *cannot* be cast to each other. ### 2.2.1 Upcasting (Subclass → Superclass) - **Always safe**, performed implicitly. - The object retains its actual runtime type (polymorphism). - A parent reference can only call methods declared in the parent class. ```java class Animal { void sound() { System.out.println("..."); } } class Dog extends Animal { void bark() { System.out.println("Woof"); } } Dog d = new Dog(); Animal a = d; // Implicit upcast — safe a.sound(); // OK — defined in Animal // a.bark(); // COMPILE ERROR — bark() not visible via Animal reference ``` ### 2.2.2 Downcasting (Superclass → Subclass) - **Must be explicit**: `Dog d = (Dog) a;` - **Risky** — if the object is *not* actually a `Dog`, a `ClassCastException` is thrown at **runtime**. ```java Animal a = new Dog(); // Upcast first Dog d = (Dog) a; // Explicit downcast — OK because actual object IS a Dog d.bark(); // Now OK Animal c = new Animal(); Dog x = (Dog) c; // Compiles fine, BUT throws ClassCastException at runtime ``` ### 2.2.3 The `instanceof` Operator Used to **safely check** the actual type of an object *before* downcasting to prevent `ClassCastException`. ```java if (a instanceof Dog) { Dog d = (Dog) a; // Safe downcast d.bark(); } ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pattern Matching for `instanceof` (Java 16+):** > ```java > if (a instanceof Dog d) { // auto-cast to d > d.bark(); > } > ``` ## 2.3 Autoboxing & Unboxing — Deep Mechanics ### 2.3.1 What the Compiler Does When you write: ```java Integer obj = 50; // Autoboxing int x = obj; // Unboxing ``` The Java compiler **translates** this behind the scenes to: ```java Integer obj = Integer.valueOf(50); // Autoboxing int x = obj.intValue(); // Unboxing ``` ### 2.3.2 The Integer Cache (CRITICAL for KTU!) The `Integer.valueOf()` method uses an **internal cache** for values in the range **−128 to 127**. Values in this range return the *same* cached object, but values outside this range create **new objects** on the heap. ```java Integer a = 100, b = 100; System.out.println(a == b); // true (same cached object) Integer c = 200, d = 200; System.out.println(c == d); // false (different objects!) System.out.println(c.equals(d)); // true (correct value comparison) ``` > [!WARNING] > **KTU Frequently Asked Pitfall:** Always use `.equals()` to compare wrapper objects, **never `==`**. The `==` operator compares *object references* (memory addresses), not values. ### 2.3.3 Where Autoboxing is Used 1. **Collections** — `ArrayList list = new ArrayList<>(); list.add(5);` (the `5` is autoboxed). 2. **Method arguments** — passing a primitive where a wrapper is expected. 3. **Generic type parameters** — generics cannot accept primitives; autoboxing handles this. 4. **Auto-unboxing in expressions** — `Integer sum = 10; sum = sum + 5;` (unbox → add → re-autobox). ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Rule / Concept | Formula / Syntax | Notes | |----------------|------------------|-------| | Widening implicit cast | `large = small;` | No data loss; auto | | Narrowing explicit cast | `small = (smallType) large;` | Truncates decimal | | Upcasting (object) | `Parent p = new Child();` | Always safe | | Downcasting (object) | `Child c = (Child) p;` | Runtime check needed | | Safe downcast check | `if (p instanceof Child c) { ... }` | Prevents `ClassCastException` | | Autoboxing | `Integer i = 5;` ⇔ `Integer i = Integer.valueOf(5);` | Java 5+ | | Unboxing | `int x = i;` ⇔ `int x = i.intValue();` | Java 5+ | | Integer cache range | $[-128, 127]$ | Cached → same object | | Wrapper equality | `obj1.equals(obj2)` | **Never use `==`** | | Wrapper classes | `Byte, Short, Integer, Long, Float, Double, Boolean, Character` | 8 total | > [!TIP] > **Real-World Utility:** Autoboxing is the silent engine behind every modern Java collection operation. When you write `Map wordCount = new HashMap<>(); wordCount.put("apple", 1);` — the `1` is autoboxed into an `Integer` object, and increments like `wordCount.put("apple", wordCount.get("apple") + 1);` involve unbox-add-rebox. This pattern powers search engines, word-frequency analyzers, and big-data counters. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough — Primitive Casting Boundary Cases Java enforces the promotion hierarchy strictly. Let us derive the **byte → short → int → long → float → double** chain using the IEEE-754 and JLS conversion rules. ### 3.1.1 Promotion Order Derivation $$ \begin{aligned} \text{byte (8-bit, signed, 2's complement)} &\rightarrow \text{short (16-bit)} \\ &\rightarrow \text{int (32-bit)} \\ &\rightarrow \text{long (64-bit)} \\ &\rightarrow \text{float (32-bit IEEE 754)} \\ &\rightarrow \text{double (64-bit IEEE 754)} \end{aligned} $$ Each arrow is a **widening conversion** — the destination can represent *every* value of the source. ### 3.1.2 Example Walkthrough — Code-to-Memory Trace Consider the following code fragment: ```java byte b = 50; short s = b; // Step 1: byte -> short (widening) int i = s; // Step 2: short -> int (widening) long L = i; // Step 3: int -> long (widening) float f = L; // Step 4: long -> float (widening, precision may differ) double d = f; // Step 5: float -> double (widening) ``` **Step-by-step reasoning:** - **Step 1:** `b = 50` fits in 8 bits. The compiler sign-extends the bit pattern to 16 bits, producing the same numerical value `50`. No operator is needed. - **Step 2:** Sign-extend `50` to 32 bits. Still `50`. - **Step 3:** Sign-extend `50` to 64 bits. Still `50`. - **Step 4:** Convert the `long` value `50` to `float` `50.0f`. This is a *value-preserving* conversion because the range of `long` $\subset$ range of `float`, but for very large longs precision can be lost. - **Step 5:** Sign-extend the `float` representation to `double`. Still `50.0`. ### 3.1.3 Reverse: Narrowing Trace Now, the reverse direction requires explicit casts and may lose information. ```java double d = 1234.9876; float f = (float) d; // Step 1 long L = (long) f; // Step 2 int i = (int) L; // Step 3 short s = (short) i; // Step 4 byte b = (byte) s; // Step 5 ``` **Step-by-step reasoning:** - **Step 1:** `(float) 1234.9876` → `1234.9877f` (rounded to nearest `float` representable value). - **Step 2:** `(long) 1234.9877f` → `1234` (decimal part **truncated**, *not* rounded). - **Step 3:** `(int) 1234` → `1234` (no loss). - **Step 4:** `(short) 1234` → `1234` (fits in 16-bit signed range `[−32768, 32767]`). - **Step 5:** `(byte) 1234` → `(byte) 1234 mod 256` → Java applies 2's-complement truncation. $1234 \mod 256 = 210$ (since $1234 - 4 \times 256 = 1234 - 1024 = 210$). Final `b = 210`. This last step demonstrates **silent data loss** — a classic KTU trap! ### 3.1.4 Mathematical Verification of `byte` Truncation $$ \begin{aligned} b_{\text{result}} &= (1234) \bmod 2^8 \\ &= 1234 - \left\lfloor \frac{1234}{256} \right\rfloor \times 256 \\ &= 1234 - (4)(256) \\ &= 1234 - 1024 \\ &= 210 \end{aligned} $$ Since $0 \le 210 \le 127$ is false, we must reinterpret $210$ as a signed 8-bit value: $$ 210_{10} = 11010010_2 \quad \xrightarrow{\text{2's complement interpret}} \quad -46 $$ So if the destination were `int`, you'd see `-46`. Since destination is `byte`, the bit pattern is stored as `210` (unsigned view) or equivalently `-46` (signed view). ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Autoboxing & Unboxing Evaluation ### 3.2.1 Expression Resolution Table | Java Source Code | Compiler-Translated Bytecode | Operation | |------------------|------------------------------|-----------| | `Integer i = 10;` | `Integer i = Integer.valueOf(10);` | Autoboxing | | `int x = i;` | `int x = i.intValue();` | Unboxing | | `Integer sum = 10; sum = sum + 5;` | `sum = Integer.valueOf(sum.intValue() + 5);` | Unbox → Add → Autobox | | `list.add(100);` (where list is `ArrayList`) | `list.add(Integer.valueOf(100));` | Autoboxing | | `int n = list.get(0);` | `int n = list.get(0).intValue();` | Unboxing | ### 3.2.2 The `==` vs `.equals()` Decision Matrix | Expression | Operand Range | Result | Why | |------------|---------------|--------|-----| | `Integer a = 50; Integer b = 50; a == b` | $[-128, 127]$ | `true` | Cached object reused | | `Integer a = 200; Integer b = 200; a == b` | outside cache | `false` | Two different heap objects | | `Integer a = 200; Integer b = 200; a.equals(b)` | any range | `true` | Compares value `200 == 200` | | `Integer a = 200; int b = 200; a == b` | any range | `true` | Auto-unbox `a` to `int`, then primitive compare | | `Double a = 5.0; Double b = 5.0; a == b` | any range | `false` | **No cache for `Double`/`Float`/`Long`** | > [!WARNING] > **Critical KTU Rule:** Only `Integer`, `Short`, `Byte`, `Character`, and `Long` (within sub-range) use the cache. `Float` and `Double` *never* cache — `==` always returns `false` for distinct object references. ### 3.2.3 Performance Cost Derivation Each autoboxing/unboxing triggers heap allocation, GC pressure, and method calls. A loop that does $N$ iterations with autoboxing incurs $O(N)$ object creations. Compare: ```java // Slow — autoboxing in tight loop Long sum = 0L; for (long i = 0; i < 1_000_000; i++) sum += i; // Each += : unbox sum, add, autobox result // Fast — pure primitives long sum = 0L; for (long i = 0; i < 1_000_000; i++) sum += i; // No object creation ``` For $N = 10^6$ iterations, the first version creates ~$10^6$ `Long` objects — a massive GC burden. The KTU-recommended best practice: **declare loop accumulators and counters as primitives** whenever possible. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Java Type Casting Hierarchy ```mermaid graph TD nodeA["byte
8-bit"] --> nodeB["short
16-bit"] nodeB --> nodeC["int
32-bit"] nodeC --> nodeD["long
64-bit"] nodeD --> nodeE["float
32-bit IEEE 754"] nodeE --> nodeF["double
64-bit IEEE 754"] nodeG["char
16-bit Unicode"] --> nodeC nodeW["Widening Path
Automatic - No Data Loss"]:::widening nodeN["Narrowing Path
Explicit - May Lose Data"]:::narrowing nodeF -.-> nodeC nodeF -.-> nodeB nodeC -.-> nodeA classDef widening fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#1d8348,color:#000 classDef narrowing fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#922b21,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Reference Casting in Inheritance ```mermaid graph TD nodeA["Object"] --> nodeB["Animal"] nodeB --> nodeC["Dog"] nodeB --> nodeD["Cat"] nodeU["Upcast - Implicit - Always Safe
Dog d = new Dog
Animal a = d"]:::up nodeV["Downcast - Explicit - Runtime Check
Dog d2 = (Dog) a
requires instanceof test"]:::down nodeW["ClassCastException Risk
Cat c = (Cat) a
if a is actually a Dog"]:::risk nodeC -.- nodeU nodeU -.- nodeV nodeV -.- nodeW classDef up fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f618d,color:#000 classDef down fill:#fcf3cf,stroke:#9a7d0a,color:#000 classDef risk fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#922b21,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Autoboxing & Unboxing Engine Flow ```mermaid graph TD nodeStart["Source Code
Integer i = 42
int x = i"] --> nodeCompile["Java Compiler"] nodeCompile --> nodeBox["Integer i = Integer.valueOf(42)
Autoboxing"] nodeCompile --> nodeUnbox["int x = i.intValue()
Unboxing"] nodeBox --> nodeCache{"Value in
cache range
-128 to 127"} nodeCache -- "Yes" --> nodeCacheHit["Return cached
Integer object"] nodeCache -- "No" --> nodeNewObj["new Integer object
allocated on heap"] nodeUnbox --> nodeUse["Use primitive
in arithmetic / I/O"] classDef primary fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f618d,color:#000 classDef cache fill:#fcf3cf,stroke:#9a7d0a,color:#000 classDef heap fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#922b21,color:#000 classDef use fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#1d8348,color:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — The `==` vs `.equals()` Decision Block ```mermaid graph TD nodeStart["Comparing Two Wrappers
e.g. Integer a, Integer b"] --> nodeQ1{"Type of
operands?"} nodeQ1 -- "Both primitives" --> nodePrim["Use =="] nodeQ1 -- "Both wrapper objects" --> nodeQ2{"Using = operator?"} nodeQ1 -- "Mixed: wrapper + primitive" --> nodeMixed["Auto-unbox wrapper
then use =="] nodeQ2 -- "Yes, using ==" --> nodeQ3{"Value in Integer
cache range?"} nodeQ2 -- "No, using .equals" --> nodeEq["Safe value comparison
ALWAYS USE THIS"] nodeQ3 -- "Yes" --> nodeCacheEq["Returns true
same object"] nodeQ3 -- "No" --> nodeCacheNeq["Returns false
