different objects"] classDef safe fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#1d8348,color:#000 classDef unsafe fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#922b21,color:#000 classDef neutral fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f618d,color:#000 ``` ## 4.5 Memory Layout — Autoboxing Object Creation ```mermaid graph LR nodeStack["Stack Frame
int x = 100
primitive value: 100"] --> nodeHeap["Heap Memory
Integer obj = autoboxed"] nodeHeap --> nodeObj1["Integer obj1 = 100
Points to CACHE"] nodeHeap --> nodeObj2["Integer obj2 = 100
Same CACHE entry"] nodeObj1 --> nodeCache["IntegerCache.value[-128 to 127]
Shared Singleton Array"] nodeObj2 --> nodeCache nodeStack2["Stack Frame
Integer y = 200"] --> nodeHeap2["Heap Memory
New Integer object"] nodeHeap2 --> nodeObj3["Integer y = 200
Points to NEW object"] nodeObj3 --> nodeCache2["Outside cache range
new Integer(200) allocated"] nodeStack3["Stack Frame
Integer z = 200"] --> nodeObj4["Integer z = 200
Points to DIFFERENT NEW object"] nodeObj4 --> nodeCache2 classDef stack fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f618d,color:#000 classDef heap fill:#fcf3cf,stroke:#9a7d0a,color:#000 classDef cache fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#1d8348,color:#000 ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **Differentiate between widening and narrowing type casting in Java with examples.** > **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Widening Casting | Narrowing Casting | |---------|------------------|-------------------| | **Direction** | Smaller type → Larger type | Larger type → Smaller type | | **Syntax** | Automatic (no operator) | Explicit cast: `(type)` | | **Data loss** | No data loss | Possible data loss / truncation | | **Compiler action** | Implicit | Requires programmer intervention | | **Example** | `int a = 10; double d = a;` | `double d = 10.5; int a = (int) d; // a = 10` | | **Use case** | Safe type promotion | Forcing fit into a smaller container | Widening conversion follows the order `byte → short → int → long → float → double`. Narrowing is the reverse and requires an explicit cast, otherwise the compiler raises *incompatible types* error. *(1 mark per row × 3 rows = 3 marks)* --- ### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **What is autoboxing in Java? Explain with an example involving `Integer` and `int`.** > **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** Autoboxing is the automatic conversion of a primitive type value into an instance of its corresponding wrapper class, performed by the Java compiler (introduced in Java 5). It eliminates the need for manual conversion using methods like `Integer.valueOf()`. **Code Example (1.5 marks):** ```java public class AutoBoxDemo { public static void main(String[] args) { int primitive = 50; // primitive int Integer wrapper = primitive; // AUToboxing: int -> Integer Integer explicit = Integer.valueOf(50); // manual way (pre-Java 5) int unboxed = wrapper; // UNboxing: Integer -> int System.out.println("Wrapper: " + wrapper); System.out.println("Unboxed: " + unboxed); } } ``` The compiler translates `Integer wrapper = primitive;` to `Integer wrapper = Integer.valueOf(primitive);` and `int unboxed = wrapper;` to `int unboxed = wrapper.intValue();`. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]` **a)** Explain the concept of **type casting** in Java. Differentiate between **primitive type casting** and **reference type casting** with suitable code examples. *(7 marks)* **b)** What is **autoboxing** and **unboxing**? Explain the **Integer cache** concept. Write a Java program to demonstrate that comparing wrapper objects using `==` is unreliable outside the cache range. *(7 marks)* --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] **Definition (1 mark):** Type casting is converting a value of one data type into another. Java supports casting for both **primitives** (int, double, etc.) and **references** (objects). **Primitive Type Casting (3 marks):** - **Widening (Implicit):** `byte → short → int → long → float → double`. Compiler does it automatically. ```java int a = 100; double d = a; // automatic widening, d = 100.0 ``` - **Narrowing (Explicit):** Requires `(type)` operator. May truncate data. ```java double pi = 3.14159; int x = (int) pi; // x = 3 (fractional part truncated) ``` **Reference Type Casting (3 marks):** - **Upcasting (Child → Parent):** Implicit and safe. ```java class Vehicle { void run() { System.out.println("Running"); } } class Car extends Vehicle { void drive() { System.out.println("Driving"); } } Car c = new Car(); Vehicle v = c; // implicit upcast v.run(); // OK // v.drive(); // Compile error - not visible via Vehicle reference ``` - **Downcasting (Parent → Child):** Explicit, risky. ```java Vehicle v = new Car(); Car c = (Car) v; // explicit downcast, safe because actual object is Car c.drive(); // OK ``` - **Safe Downcast with `instanceof`:** ```java if (v instanceof Car) { Car c = (Car) v; // safe c.drive(); } ``` If the actual object is not a `Car`, the downcast throws `ClassCastException` at runtime. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **Autoboxing and Unboxing (2 marks):** Autoboxing is the automatic conversion of a primitive to its corresponding wrapper object by the compiler. Unboxing is the reverse. Both were introduced in **Java 5**. ```java Integer obj = 50; // autoboxing: int -> Integer int val = obj; // unboxing: Integer -> int ``` Compiler translates: ```java Integer obj = Integer.valueOf(50); int val = obj.intValue(); ``` **Integer Cache Concept (2 marks):** The `Integer.valueOf(int)` method uses an **internal static cache** for values in the range **−128 to 127**. When called with a value in this range, it returns a *pre-existing cached* object instead of creating a new one. Values outside this range create a **new object** on the heap each time. This is an optimization to save memory for the most commonly used small integers. **Demonstration Program (3 marks):** ```java public class CacheDemo { public static void main(String[] args) { // Case 1: Values within cache range [-128, 127] Integer a = 100; Integer b = 100; System.out.println("Within cache, a == b : " + (a == b)); // true // Case 2: Values outside cache range Integer c = 200; Integer d = 200; System.out.println("Outside cache, c == d: " + (c == d)); // false // Correct way to compare wrapper values System.out.println("c.equals(d) : " + c.equals(d)); // true // Mixing wrapper with primitive auto-unboxes Integer e = 200; int f = 200; System.out.println("Mixed, e == f : " + (e == f)); // true } } ``` **Output:** ``` Within cache, a == b : true Outside cache, c == d: false c.equals(d) : true Mixed, e == f : true ``` **Conclusion:** Always use `.equals()` to compare wrapper objects. The `==` operator only works reliably when: 1. Both operands are primitives (or one is autounboxed to primitive). 2. Both are `Integer` references pointing to values in the $[-128, 127]$ range. --- ### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Java type promotion hierarchy** for primitives. Write a program to demonstrate the difference between **implicit and explicit casting** for `double` to `int` conversion. *(7 marks)* **b)** Explain **upcasting and downcasting** with respect to object references. What is `ClassCastException`? Show how the `instanceof` operator prevents it. *(7 marks)* --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] **Java Type Promotion Hierarchy (3 marks):** ``` byte → short → int → long → float → double ↑ char ``` The arrows denote **safe widening conversions**. Java implicitly widens from left to right. Reverse arrows require **explicit cast operators**. **Demonstration Program (4 marks):** ```java public class CastDemo { public static void main(String[] args) { // -------- IMPLICIT (Widening) -------- int i = 100; long l = i; // int -> long, automatic float f = l; // long -> float, automatic double d = f; // float -> double, automatic System.out.println("Implicit cast result: " + d); // 100.0 // -------- EXPLICIT (Narrowing) -------- double pi = 3.14159; int truncated = (int) pi; // double -> int, explicit System.out.println("Truncated value: " + truncated); // 3 (not 4!) // -------- Potential Data Loss Example -------- double big = 1234567.89; int loss = (int) big; System.out.println("After narrowing, big double -> int: " + loss); // 1234567 // -------- Narrowing past boundary -------- int large = 130; byte small = (byte) large; // 130 mod 256 = -126 (signed view) System.out.println("Byte overflow: " + small); // -126 } } ``` **Key Observations:** - Implicit casting never loses information. - Explicit casting **truncates** decimals (no rounding). - Converting an `int` larger than the destination range causes **silent overflow** (modular wraparound). --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **Upcasting (2 marks):** Converting a subclass reference to a superclass reference. Always safe and implicit. The object retains its *actual* runtime type. ```java class Employee { void work() { System.out.println("Working"); } } class Manager extends Employee { void lead() { System.out.println("Leading"); } } Manager m = new Manager(); Employee e = m; // implicit upcast e.work(); // OK (inherited method) // e.lead(); // COMPILE ERROR — lead() not in Employee ``` **Downcasting (2 marks):** Converting a superclass reference back to a subclass reference. Must be explicit. Dangerous if the object is not actually of the target type. ```java Employee e = new Manager(); // safe upcast Manager m = (Manager) e; // explicit downcast — safe because actual type is Manager m.lead(); // OK now ``` **`ClassCastException` (1.5 marks):** Thrown at **runtime** when a downcast is attempted on an object that is *not* an instance of the target class. Example: ```java Employee e = new Employee(); // actual type is Employee Manager m = (Manager) e; // COMPILE OK, RUNTIME FAILS // throws: java.lang.ClassCastException: class Employee cannot be cast to class Manager ``` **Using `instanceof` for Safe Downcast (1.5 marks):** ```java public class InstanceofDemo { public static void main(String[] args) { Employee[] arr = { new Manager(), new Employee(), new Manager() }; for (Employee e : arr) { if (e instanceof Manager) { // type check first Manager m = (Manager) e; // safe downcast m.lead(); } else { System.out.println("Not a Manager"); } } } } ``` The `instanceof` operator returns `true` only if the object on its left side is an instance of the class on its right. This prevents `ClassCastException` by ensuring the cast is valid before performing it. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **🚨 Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > > 1. **Using `==` for wrapper comparison** — Most common 2-mark loss. `==` compares *references*, not values. Always write `obj1.equals(obj2)` for value comparison. > 2. **Forgetting the cache range** — Many students write `Integer a = 200; Integer b = 200; a == b;` and answer `true`. The correct answer is `false` because 200 is outside $[-128, 127]$. > 3. **Confusing upcast with downcast direction** — Upcast = child → parent (always safe). Downcast = parent → child (risky). Drawing arrows in the wrong direction loses 1–2 marks. > 4. **Saying "narrowing rounds off"** — Java **truncates** (chops off) decimals; it does not round. `(int) 9.9` is `9`, not `10`. > 5. **Missing `instanceof` in downcast code** — Code that does `(Dog) animal;` without an `instanceof` check is marked down for unsafe practice. > 6. **Forgetting the `Java 5` introduction date** for autoboxing/unboxing — this is a 1-mark "definition completion" point that students miss. > 7. **Confusing `int` and `Integer` size** — `int` is 32-bit primitive (stack), `Integer` is an object on the heap (object header + value). This distinction is important in performance-related questions. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ **Casting** = converting one type to another; **two flavors** — primitive and reference. - ✅ **Widening (Implicit)**: smaller → larger, automatic, no data loss. Order: `byte → short → int → long → float → double`, also `char → int`. - ✅ **Narrowing (Explicit)**: larger → smaller, requires `(type)` cast operator, **truncates** decimals, can overflow. - ✅ **Upcasting** (subclass → superclass): always safe, implicit. Object's actual runtime type is preserved. - ✅ **Downcasting** (superclass → subclass): explicit, risky. Throws `ClassCastException` if actual type is incompatible. - ✅ **`instanceof` operator** is the gatekeeper for safe downcasting. Pattern matching form: `if (obj instanceof Type t)`. - ✅ **Autoboxing** = automatic primitive → wrapper. **Unboxing** = automatic wrapper → primitive. Both added in **Java 5**. - ✅ Compiler translates: `Integer i = 5;` → `Integer i = Integer.valueOf(5);` and `int x = i;` → `int x = i.intValue();`. - ✅ **8 wrapper classes**: `Byte, Short, Integer, Long, Float, Double, Boolean, Character` (uppercase class names). - ✅ **Integer cache range**: $[-128, 127]$ — values inside return same cached object; values outside create new objects. - ✅ Only `Integer`, `Short`, `Byte`, `Character`, `Long` cache values. `Float` and `Double` **never cache**. - ✅ Always use `.equals()` to compare wrapper objects. `==` compares references. - ✅ Mixing wrapper and primitive with `==` auto-unboxes the wrapper — safe in that case. - ✅ Autoboxing in tight loops causes GC pressure — declare accumulators as primitives. - ✅ Generics require wrapper types; autoboxing makes `List list = new ArrayList<>(); list.add(10);` work seamlessly. - ✅ Common KTU formula: `byte` result of large `int` cast = `value mod 256`, then interpreted as signed 2's complement. ---

topics.title: Arrays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECIT633 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Arrays # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Array (KTU Definition):** An array is a **linear, homogeneous, contiguous data structure** that stores a fixed-size sequential collection of elements of the **same data type**, accessed using an integer index. In the context of **Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)**, arrays serve as the foundational building block for storing primitive data and, more importantly, for storing **objects** of user-defined classes, thereby enabling the principle of *composition* (objects containing other objects). ### Key Properties of an Array | Property | Description | |---|---| | **Homogeneous** | All elements belong to the same data type (e.g., all `int`, all `Student` objects) | | **Fixed Size** | Size is determined at the time of declaration and is **immutable** in static arrays | | **Contiguous Memory** | Elements are stored in **adjacent memory locations**, enabling O(1) random access | | **Zero-Indexed** | In C++/Java, the first element is at index **0**, last at **n−1** | | **Random Access** | Any element can be fetched in **constant time** using its index | | **Linear Structure** | Elements are arranged in a single logical sequence | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Locker Room" > [!TIP] > **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a **row of identical lockers** in a school corridor. Each locker: > - Holds **one item** (a book, a bag, a file), > - All lockers are **the same size** (homogeneous), > - Lockers are placed **side by side without gaps** (contiguous), > - Each locker has a **unique number** painted on it (index), > - The **total number of lockers is fixed** when the row is built (fixed size). > > To access any locker, you only need to know its **number** — you don't have to start from locker 1 and walk through every locker. This is exactly how an array works in memory! ## 1.3 Why Arrays in OOP? In OOP, arrays become even more powerful because they can hold **objects** (instances of classes), not just primitives. This enables: - **Composition:** A `Department` class can have an array of `Employee` objects. - **Polymorphism:** An array of `Shape*` pointers can store `Circle`, `Rectangle`, and `Triangle` objects. - **Data Management:** Managing large collections of structured data efficiently. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Module 1 of *Object Oriented Programming* lays the conceptual foundation. Arrays are introduced as the **first data structure** that bridges primitive data handling with **object-based storage**, preparing students for advanced topics like *Vectors*, *Lists*, and *Stacks* in later modules. ## 1.4 Classification of Arrays Arrays can be classified into three major categories relevant to OOP: 1. **One-Dimensional Arrays (1D):** A simple linear list. Example: `int marks[50];` 2. **Multi-Dimensional Arrays (2D, 3D):** Arrays of arrays. Example: `int matrix[3][3];` 3. **Arrays of Objects:** Arrays where each element is an instance of a user-defined class. Example: `Student classList[60];` # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How Arrays Work ### Step 1: Declaration The compiler reserves a **block of contiguous memory** based on: - **Data type size** (e.g., `int` = 4 bytes) - **Number of elements** specified $$\text{Total Memory} = \text{Number of Elements} \times \text{Size of Each Element (in bytes)}$$ ### Step 2: Indexing Each element is assigned an **index** starting from **0**. For an array of size `n`: - **Valid indices:** 0, 1, 2, ..., n−1 - **Invalid indices:** < 0 or ≥ n (causes *out-of-bounds* access) ### Step 3: Access Given an index `i`, the CPU computes the memory address using **pointer arithmetic**: $$\text{Address of } A[i] = \text{Base Address} + (i \times \text{Element Size})$$ ### Step 4: Traversal A `for` loop is used to visit each element sequentially, typically from index `0` to `n-1`. ## 2.2 Declaration & Initialization Patterns (C++ Context) ### Static Array Declaration ```cpp int scores[5]; // Uninitialized — contains garbage values int ages[5] = {21, 22, 23, 24, 25}; // Fully initialized int temps[] = {30, 32, 28}; // Size inferred = 3 int vals[10] = {0}; // All 10 elements set to 0 ``` ### Multi-Dimensional Array ```cpp int matrix[3][4]; // 3 rows, 4 columns int cube[2][3][4]; // 3D array ``` ### Array of Objects ```cpp class Student { int rollNo; string name; public: void setData(int r, string n) { rollNo = r; name = n; } }; Student batch[60]; // Array of 60 Student objects batch[0].setData(101, "Anand"); // Accessing object methods ``` > [!NOTE] > **OOP-Specific Insight:** When an array of objects is created, the **default constructor** of the class is invoked for **each element**. If the class lacks a default constructor, you must either provide one or use *dynamic allocation* (e.g., `new`). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Address Calculation > [!IMPORTANT] > The following formulas are **high-yield** for KTU university exams. Memorize the **formula structure** (not just the equation) and you can solve any variant. ### Formula Table | # | Array Type | Memory Order | Address of Element | Variables | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 1D Array `A[n]` | Linear | $A + i \times s$ | $i$ = index, $s$ = element size | | 2 | 2D Array `A[m][n]` | Row-Major | $A + (i \times n + j) \times s$ | $i$ = row, $j$ = column | | 3 | 2D Array `A[m][n]` | Column-Major | $A + (j \times m + i) \times s$ | $i$ = row, $j$ = column | | 4 | 3D Array `A[p][q][r]` | Row-Major | $A + ((i \times q + j) \times r + k) \times s$ | $i,j,k$ = indices | | 5 | Memory Size (1D) | — | $N \times s$ bytes | $N$ = count, $s$ = size | | 6 | Memory Size (2D) | — | $m \times n \times s$ bytes | $m,n$ = dimensions | **Where:** - $A$ = Base address of array - $s$ = Size of each element in **bytes** - $i, j, k$ = Index values (starting from **0**) - $m, n, p, q, r$ = Dimension sizes ### C/C++ Specific Sizes (for exams) | Data Type | Size (bytes) | |---|---| | `char` | **1** | | `int` | **4** | | `float` | **4** | | `double` | **8** | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications - **Image Processing:** A 2D array stores pixel intensity values. - **Database Systems:** Records are stored in array-like structures for fast lookup. - **Game Development:** Game boards (chess, tic-tac-toe) use 2D arrays. - **Signal Processing:** Audio samples are stored in 1D arrays for FFT analysis. - **Scientific Computing:** Matrices for linear algebra operations use 2D arrays. ## 2.5 Advantages & Limitations > [!TIP] > **Exam Tip:** KTU questions often ask *"List the advantages and disadvantages of arrays."* Use this ready table. | Advantages | Limitations | |---|---| | **O(1) Random Access** by index | **Fixed Size** — cannot grow/shrink dynamically (in static arrays) | | **Cache-friendly** due to contiguous memory | **Homogeneous** — all elements must be same type | | Simple to declare and use | **Insertion/Deletion** in middle is **O(n)** — costly | | Easy to traverse using loops | Wastes memory if not fully utilized | | Foundation for other DS (stacks, queues) | No built-in bounds checking in C++ (UB if violated) | # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Address Calculation for 1D Array ### Problem Setup Given: - Base address $B = 1000$ - Array `A[10]` of `int` (size = **4 bytes**) - Find address of `A[4]` ### Derivation $$\text{Address of } A[i] = B + (i \times s)$$ **Step 1:** Identify the given values. $$B = 1000, \quad i = 4, \quad s = 4 \text{ bytes}$$ **Step 2:** Substitute into the formula. $$\text{Address of } A[4] = 1000 + (4 \times 4)$$ **Step 3:** Perform the multiplication. $$4 \times 4 = 16$$ **Step 4:** Add to the base address. $$1000 + 16 = 1016$$ ### Final Answer $$\boxed{\text{Address of } A[4] = 1016}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Address Calculation for 2D Array (Row-Major) ### Problem Setup Given: - Base address $B = 2000$ - Array `A[5][4]` of `int` (size = **4 bytes**) - Find address of `A[2][3]` - Storage: **Row-Major Order** (used by C/C++) ### Derivation $$\text{Address of } A[i][j] = B + ((i \times n) + j) \times s$$ Where $n$ = number of **columns** = 4. **Step 1:** Identify the given values. $$B = 2000, \quad i = 2, \quad j = 3, \quad n = 4, \quad s = 4$$ **Step 2:** Compute $i \times n$ (number of complete rows to skip). $$i \times n = 2 \times 4 = 8$$ This means we skip **8 complete elements** (2 full rows of 4 elements each). **Step 3:** Add $j$ to get the total offset in elements. $$(i \times n) + j = 8 + 3 = 11$$ **Step 4:** Multiply by element size $s$ to convert to bytes. $$11 \times 4 = 44 \text{ bytes}$$ **Step 5:** Add to base address. $$2000 + 44 = 2044$$ ### Final Answer $$\boxed{\text{Address of } A[2][3] = 2044}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Address Calculation for 2D Array (Column-Major) ### Problem Setup Given: - Base address $B = 2000$ - Array `A[5][4]` of `int` (size = **4 bytes**) - Find address of `A[2][3]` - Storage: **Column-Major Order** (used by Fortran, MATLAB) ### Derivation $$\text{Address of } A[i][j] = B + ((j \times m) + i) \times s$$ Where $m$ = number of **rows** = 5. **Step 1:** Identify the given values. $$B = 2000, \quad i = 2, \quad j = 3, \quad m = 5, \quad s = 4$$ **Step 2:** Compute $j \times m$ (number of complete columns to skip). $$j \times m = 3 \times 5 = 15$$ This means we skip **15 complete elements** (3 full columns of 5 elements each). **Step 3:** Add $i$ to get the total offset in elements. $$(j \times m) + i = 15 + 2 = 17$$ **Step 4:** Multiply by element size $s$. $$17 \times 4 = 68 \text{ bytes}$$ **Step 5:** Add to base address. $$2000 + 68 = 2068$$ ### Final Answer $$\boxed{\text{Address of } A[2][3] = 2068}$$ --- ## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix | Aspect | Row-Major Order | Column-Major Order | |---|---|---| | **Languages** | C, C++, Java, Python (NumPy) | Fortran, MATLAB, R | | **Formula** | $B + (i \times n + j) \times s$ | $B + (j \times m + i) \times s$ | | **Skip unit** | Complete **rows** (n elements) | Complete **columns** (m elements) | | **Traversal speed** | Row-by-row is **fastest** | Column-by-column is **fastest** | | **Cache locality** | Best for row-wise access | Best for column-wise access | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Pitfall:** A very common mistake is using $m$ (rows) instead of $n$ (columns) in the row-major formula. **Always verify:** in row-major, you skip *rows*, each row has $n$ elements. ## 3.5 Worked Example: Array of Objects Memory ### Problem A class `Employee` has two `int` data members (`id`, `salary`). Create an array of 5 `Employee` objects. Calculate the total memory. ### Solution **Step 1:** Size of one `Employee` object. $$\text{Size} = 2 \times \text{sizeof(int)} = 2 \times 4 = 8 \text{ bytes}$$ (Assuming no padding; with padding, it may be 8 or more depending on alignment.) **Step 2:** Size of array of 5 objects. $$5 \times 8 = 40 \text{ bytes}$$ ### Final Answer $$\boxed{\text{Total Memory} = 40 \text{ bytes}}$$ > [!NOTE] > In practice, compilers may add **padding bytes** to align data members, making the actual size slightly larger. For KTU exams, students are expected to use the **logical/ideal size** unless padding is explicitly mentioned. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Visualizing a 1D Array in Memory ```mermaid graph LR A0["Index 0
Value 10
Addr 1000"] A1["Index 1
Value 20
Addr 1004"] A2["Index 2
Value 30
Addr 1008"] A3["Index 3
Value 40
Addr 1012"] A4["Index 4
Value 50
Addr 1016"] A0 --> A1 A1 --> A2 A2 --> A3 A3 --> A4 ``` > **Interpretation:** Each box is **4 bytes** apart (int size). The base address is **1000**, and the array occupies addresses **1000 to 1016** (17 bytes total inclusive = 20 bytes exclusive). ## 4.2 2D Array (Row-Major) Memory Layout ```mermaid graph TB subgraph matrix["A 3x4 Matrix in Row-Major Order"] R0["Row 0: A0,0 | A0,1 | A0,2 | A0,3"] R1["Row 1: A1,0 | A1,1 | A1,2 | A1,3"] R2["Row 2: A2,0 | A2,1 | A2,2 | A2,3"] end R0 --> R1 R1 --> R2 MEM["Memory: A0,0 A0,1 A0,2 A0,3 A1,0 A1,1 A1,2 A1,3 A2,0 A2,1 A2,2 A2,3"] ``` > **Interpretation:** In row-major order, the entire first row is stored first, then the second row, and so on. The linear memory is a single sequence obtained by reading rows left-to-right, top-to-bottom. ## 4.3 Array of Objects — Block Diagram ```mermaid graph TB ARR["Array: Employee team 5"] E0["team 0
id 101
salary 50000"] E1["team 1
id 102
salary 55000"] E2["team 2
id 103
salary 60000"] E3["team 3
id 104
salary 65000"] E4["team 4
id 105
salary 70000"] ARR --> E0 ARR --> E1 ARR --> E2 ARR --> E3 ARR --> E4 ``` > **Interpretation:** Each `team[i]` is a full `Employee` object. The array name `team` acts as a **pointer to the first object**. Accessing `team[2].salary` returns `60000`. ## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Array Operations Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD A["User Input Index i"] --> B{"Is i valid?
0 less than i less than n"} B -- Yes --> C["Compute Address
Base plus i times Size"] B -- No --> D["Error: Out of Bounds"] C --> E["Fetch Element from RAM"] E --> F["Return Value to User"] D --> G["Program Terminates or Exception"] ``` > **Interpretation:** This flow shows the internal CPU/memory operations when a programmer writes `arr[i]`. First, a bounds check (if any) is done, then the address is computed using pointer arithmetic, and finally the value is retrieved from RAM. ## 4.5 Comparison: Static vs Dynamic Arrays ```mermaid graph LR subgraph staticArr["Static Array int arr 10"] S1["Size: Fixed at compile time"] S2["Memory: Stack"] S3["Deallocation: Automatic"] end subgraph dynamicArr["Dynamic Array int arr new int 10"] D1["Size: Decided at runtime"] D2["Memory: Heap"] D3["Deallocation: Manual delete arr"] end ``` # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define an array. List any four characteristics of arrays.** **Model Answer:** An array is a **linear, homogeneous, contiguous data structure** that stores a fixed-size collection of elements of the same data type, accessed using integer indices. **Four characteristics:** 1. **Homogeneous:** All elements must be of the same data type. 2. **Fixed Size:** The size is determined at declaration and cannot be changed. 3. **Contiguous Memory Allocation:** Elements are stored in adjacent memory locations. 4. **Random Access:** Any element can be accessed directly in **O(1) time** using its index. 5. **Zero-Indexed:** In C++/Java, indexing starts from 0. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between one-dimensional and two-dimensional arrays with suitable examples.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | One-Dimensional Array | Two-Dimensional Array | |---|---|---| | **Structure** | Linear list of elements | Table/grid with rows and columns | | **Declaration** | `int arr[10];` | `int mat[3][3];` | | **Indexing** | Single index `arr[i]` | Two indices `mat[i][j]` | | **Memory** | Single contiguous block | Stored in row-major or column-major order | | **Visualization** | A single row | A matrix/table | | **Example Use** | Storing marks of 50 students | Storing a 3×3 chess board state | --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students frequently confuse `m` (rows) and `n` (columns) in the address calculation formula. Always double-check: in **row-major**, you multiply the row index by the **number of columns (n)**, not rows. Also, remember that **indexing starts from 0**, so the first element has **zero offset**, not 1. Marks are deducted heavily for off-by-one errors. --- ### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (14 Marks) **(a) Explain the concept of arrays in C++ with a suitable example program that demonstrates the use of an array of objects. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** An array of objects is a collection of instances of a class stored in a contiguous memory block, accessed via an index. **Example Program:** ```cpp #include #include using namespace std; class Student { int rollNo; string name; float marks; public: // Default constructor (required for array creation) Student() { rollNo = 0; name = "Unknown"; marks = 0.0; } void setData(int r, string n, float m) { rollNo = r; name = n; marks = m; } void display() { cout << "Roll: " << rollNo << " | Name: " << name << " | Marks: " << marks << endl; } }; int main() { Student batch[3]; // Array of 3 Student objects batch[0].setData(101, "Anand", 89.5); batch[1].setData(102, "Bhavna", 92.0); batch[2].setData(103, "Chitra", 85.5); cout << "Student Details:" << endl; for(int i = 0; i < 3; i++) { batch[i].display(); } return 0; } ``` **Explanation of Key Concepts:** 1. **Default Constructor:** Must be defined explicitly, because creating `Student batch[3]` invokes the default constructor three times. 2. **Member Access:** The dot operator (`.`) is used to access object members through array elements, e.g., `batch[0].setData(...)`. 3. **Loop Traversal:** A `for` loop iterates through all objects, demonstrating **O(n) traversal**. --- **(b) An array `A[10][20]` is stored in memory in row-major order. If the base address is 5000 and each element occupies 4 bytes, find the address of `A[5][8]`. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given Data:** - Array dimensions: $m = 10$ rows, $n = 20$ columns - Base address: $B = 5000$ - Element size: $s = 4$ bytes - Required element: `A[5][8]` → $i = 5$, $j = 8$ - Storage order: **Row-Major** **Formula (Row-Major):** $$\text{Address of } A[i][j] = B + ((i \times n) + j) \times s$$ **Step 1:** Substitute the values. $$\text{Address} = 5000 + ((5 \times 20) + 8) \times 4$$ **Step 2:** Compute $5 \times 20$. $$5 \times 20 = 100$$ **Step 3:** Add the column index $j = 8$. $$100 + 8 = 108$$ This means we skip **108 complete elements** before reaching `A[5][8]`. **Step 4:** Multiply by element size. $$108 \times 4 = 432 \text{ bytes}$$ **Step 5:** Add to base address. $$5000 + 432 = 5432$$ ### Final Answer $$\boxed{\text{Address of } A[5][8] = 5432}$$ --- ### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (14 Marks) **(a) With a neat diagram, explain the memory representation of a 2D array in row-major order. How is it different from column-major order? (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **2D Array Declaration:** `int A[3][4];` (3 rows, 4 columns) **Row-Major Order Memory Layout (used in C/C++):** ``` Memory Address: 100 104 108 112 116 120 124 128 132 136 140 144 Element: A00 A01 A02 A03 A10 A11 A12 A13 A20 A21 A22 A23 ``` In **row-major order**, elements are stored **row by row**. The entire first row (`A[0][0]` to `A[0][3]`) is placed first, followed by the second row, and so on. **Formula:** $$\text{Address of } A[i][j] = B + (i \times n + j) \times s$$ where $n$ = number of columns. **Column-Major Order Memory Layout (used in Fortran):** ``` Memory Address: 100 104 108 112 116 120 124 128 132 136 140 144 Element: A00 A10 A20 A01 A11 A21 A02 A12 A22 A03 A13 A23 ``` In **column-major order**, elements are stored **column by column**. The entire first column (`A[0][0]` to `A[2][0]`) is placed first. **Formula:** $$\text{Address of } A[i][j] = B + (j \times m + i) \times s$$ where $m$ = number of rows. **Key Difference:** | Aspect | Row-Major | Column-Major | |---|---|---| | Storage Sequence | Row-by-row | Column-by-column | | Skip unit for index $i$ | $n$ elements (one full row) | 1 element | | Skip unit for index $j$ | 1 element | $m$ elements (one full column) | | Language | C, C++, Java | Fortran, MATLAB | --- **(b) An array `A[15][10]` is stored in column-major order with base address 2000 and element size 2 bytes. Calculate the address of `A[7][3]`. Also, find the total memory occupied by the array. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given Data:** - Array dimensions: $m = 15$ rows, $n = 10$ columns - Base address: $B = 2000$ - Element size: $s = 2$ bytes - Required element: `A[7][3]` → $i = 7$, $j = 3$ - Storage order: **Column-Major** **Part 1: Address Calculation** **Formula (Column-Major):** $$\text{Address of } A[i][j] = B + ((j \times m) + i) \times s$$ **Step 1:** Substitute values. $$\text{Address} = 2000 + ((3 \times 15) + 7) \times 2$$ **Step 2:** Compute $3 \times 15$. $$3 \times 15 = 45$$ **Step 3:** Add row index $i = 7$. $$45 + 7 = 52$$ **Step 4:** Multiply by element size $s = 2$. $$52 \times 2 = 104 \text{ bytes}$$ **Step 5:** Add to base address. $$2000 + 104 = 2104$$ ### Final Answer (Address) $$\boxed{\text{Address of } A[7][3] = 2104}$$ **Part 2: Total Memory Calculation** $$\text{Total Memory} = m \times n \times s$$ **Step 1:** Substitute values. $$\text{Total Memory} = 15 \times 10 \times 2$$ **Step 2:** Compute step by step. $$15 \times 10 = 150$$ $$150 \times 2 = 300 \text{ bytes}$$ ### Final Answer (Memory) $$\boxed{\text{Total Memory Occupied} = 300 \text{ bytes}}$$ --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print and Pin This!** - **Array:** Linear, homogeneous, contiguous, fixed-size collection accessed by integer index. - **Indexing starts at 0** in C++/Java; the last valid index is `n − 1`. - **Time Complexity:** Access = **O(1)**, Traversal = **O(n)**, Insertion/Deletion in middle = **O(n)**. - **Memory Formula (1D):** $\text{Size} = N \times s$ bytes. - **Memory Formula (2D):** $\text{Size} = m \times n \times s$ bytes. - **Row-Major Formula:** $B + (i \times n + j) \times s$ — used by C, C++, Java. - **Column-Major Formula:** $B + (j \times m + i) \times s$ — used by Fortran, MATLAB. - **Array of Objects:** Requires a **default constructor** to be defined in the class. - **Member Access:** `obj.member` for objects, `ptr->member` for pointers. - **Static vs Dynamic:** Static arrays are on the **stack** (auto-managed); dynamic arrays use `new` (heap, manual `delete`). - **Common Pitfall:** Accessing index `< 0` or `≥ n` leads to **undefined behavior** in C++. - **Pointer-Array Relationship:** An array name **decays to a pointer** to its first element in most expressions.

topics.title: Strings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECIT633 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Strings # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition In the Java programming language (the canonical language for KTU's PECIT633 Object Oriented Programming course under the 2024 Scheme), a **String** is fundamentally defined as an **immutable sequence of Unicode characters**, implemented internally as an object of the `java.lang.String` class. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A String is an object of the `java.lang.String` class that represents a sequence of characters. Once instantiated, the contents of a String object cannot be modified — any operation that appears to "change" a String actually creates a new String object in memory. The Java platform provides three principal classes for handling character sequences: | Class | Package | Mutability | Thread-Safety | Introduced In | |---|---|---|---|---| | `String` | `java.lang` | **Immutable** | Yes (intrinsic) | JDK 1.0 | | `StringBuffer` | `java.lang` | **Mutable** | Yes (synchronized) | JDK 1.0 | | `StringBuilder` | `java.lang` | **Mutable** | **No** (non-synchronized) | JDK 1.5 | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **printed photograph**: - A **String** is like a printed photograph. Once printed, you cannot modify the photograph itself. If you want a "changed" version, you must take a new photograph and print a fresh copy. The old photograph remains untouched. - A **StringBuffer** is like a **whiteboard with a security guard** standing beside it. Multiple people (threads) may want to write on the whiteboard, but the guard ensures only one person writes at a time (synchronization). The marks on the whiteboard persist and can be erased/re-written in place. - A **StringBuilder** is like a **whiteboard with NO security guard**. It is faster to use because no one waits in line, but if multiple people (threads) write simultaneously, the result becomes corrupted (race condition). > [!NOTE] > **Immutability Benefit:** Because String objects cannot change, they are inherently **thread-safe** and can be safely **cached**, **shared**, and used as **keys in a `HashMap`** without external synchronization. ## 1.3 The String Literal Pool (String Constant Pool) When you write a String literal in Java (e.g., `String s = "Hello";`), the JVM first checks a special memory region in the heap called the **String Constant Pool** (also called the String Literal Pool). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memory Optimization Trick:** Java interns String literals. If `"Hello"` already exists in the pool, the new reference points to the same object — saving memory. This is enabled by default for literals but must be explicitly invoked via `intern()` for `new String("Hello")` objects. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The `String` Class — Internal Architecture ### 2.1.1 Backed by a `char[]` (Pre-Java 9) / `byte[]` (Java 9+) Internally, a `String` stores its character sequence in a final array. In JDK 8 and earlier, this was a `char[]` (UTF-16 code units). Starting with **JDK 9**, it was compacted to a `byte[]` plus an encoding flag (`coder`) to support **Compact Strings** — saving up to **50% heap memory** for Latin-1 content. ### 2.1.2 The Three Construction Pathways | Pathway | Code Syntax | Pool Behavior | Memory Location | |---|---|---|---| | Literal | `String s = "Kerala";` | Reused if exists | **String Pool** (Heap) | | `new` Keyword | `String s = new String("Kerala");` | Always creates new object | **Heap** (outside pool unless `intern()` called) | | Character Array | `char[] ch = {'K','E','R'}; String s = new String(ch);` | New object always | **Heap** | ## 2.2 The `String` Class — Method Reference Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Methods:** The following methods appear repeatedly in board examinations. Memorize their signatures and return types. | Method Signature | Return Type | Purpose | |---|---|---| | `int length()` | `int` | Returns the number of characters (UTF-16 units). | | `char charAt(int index)` | `char` | Returns the character at the specified index (0-based). | | `String substring(int beginIndex)` | `String` | Returns substring from `beginIndex` to end. | | `String substring(int beginIndex, int endIndex)` | `String` | Returns substring from `beginIndex` (inclusive) to `endIndex` (exclusive). | | `boolean equals(Object anObject)` | `boolean` | Content comparison (case-sensitive). | | `boolean equalsIgnoreCase(String another)` | `boolean` | Content comparison (case-insensitive). | | `int compareTo(String anotherString)` | `int` | Lexicographic comparison; returns negative, zero, or positive. | | `String concat(String str)` | `String` | Concatenates strings (returns new String). | | `int indexOf(String str)` | `int` | Returns first occurrence index, or $-1$ if not found. | | `String toLowerCase()` / `toUpperCase()` | `String` | Returns a new String with case changed. | | `String trim()` | `String` | Removes leading and trailing whitespace. | | `String[] split(String regex)` | `String[]` | Splits the string around matches of regex. | | `String replace(char old, char new)` | `String` | Replaces all occurrences of a character. | | `boolean startsWith(String prefix)` | `boolean` | Tests if this string begins with the specified prefix. | | `String valueOf(int i)` (static) | `String` | Converts primitives/objects to String. | ## 2.3 The `StringBuffer` Class `StringBuffer` is a **mutable, thread-safe** sequence of characters. Its internal state (the character array) is `final` but the contents may be modified. All its key mutator methods are `synchronized`. ### 2.3.1 Critical Constructors - `StringBuffer()` — Creates an empty buffer with initial capacity of **16 characters**. - `StringBuffer(int capacity)` — Creates an empty buffer with the specified initial capacity. - `StringBuffer(String str)` — Creates a buffer initialized with the contents of `str`, with extra room for **16 more** characters. ### 2.3.2 High-Yield `StringBuffer` Methods | Method Signature | Return Type | Purpose | |---|---|---| | `StringBuffer append(String/primitive)` | `StringBuffer` | Appends argument to end; returns the same buffer (chainable). | | `StringBuffer insert(int offset, String str)` | `StringBuffer` | Inserts string at the specified position. | | `StringBuffer delete(int start, int end)` | `StringBuffer` | Removes characters in `[start, end)`. | | `StringBuffer reverse()` | `StringBuffer` | Reverses the character sequence in place. | | `StringBuffer replace(int start, int end, String str)` | `StringBuffer` | Replaces substring with new string. | | `int capacity()` | `int` | Returns the current allocated capacity. | | `int length()` | `int` | Returns the actual number of characters stored. | | `char charAt(int index)` | `char` | Returns character at index. | | `void setCharAt(int index, char ch)` | `void` | Sets character at index. | | `String toString()` | `String` | Converts to an immutable `String`. | > [!NOTE] > **Capacity Growth Formula:** When `append()` overflows the buffer, Java automatically expands the internal array. The new capacity is computed as: > $$\text{newCapacity} = \big(\text{oldCapacity} \times 2\big) + 2$$ > Plus an additional alignment step in newer JVMs to use power-of-two sizes. ## 2.4 The `StringBuilder` Class (Java 5+) `StringBuilder` is functionally identical to `StringBuffer` but **without synchronization overhead**. This makes it significantly faster in **single-threaded contexts**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "Which is faster?", the correct answer is **`StringBuilder`**. When asked "Which is thread-safe?", the answer is **`StringBuffer`**. ## 2.5 KTU Comparison Master Table | Property | `String` | `StringBuffer` | `StringBuilder` | |---|---|---|---| | Mutability | Immutable | Mutable | Mutable | | Thread-Safe | Yes (intrinsic) | Yes (synchronized) | **No** | | Speed | Slow for concatenation | Slow (sync overhead) | **Fastest** | | Storage Area | String Pool / Heap | Heap | Heap | | Storage Backend | `byte[]` (JDK 9+) | `char[]` (legacy) / `byte[]` | `char[]` (legacy) / `byte[]` | | Use Case | Fixed text, keys, identifiers | Multi-threaded string manipulation | Single-threaded string building | | `final` class? | **Yes** | **Yes** | **Yes** | ## 2.6 The `StringTokenizer` Class `java.util.StringTokenizer` is a legacy class used to **break a string into tokens** based on a set of delimiters. ### 2.6.1 Key Constructors - `StringTokenizer(String str)` — Uses default delimiters: space, tab, newline, carriage return, form feed. - `StringTokenizer(String str, String delimiters)` — Uses specified delimiter characters. - `StringTokenizer(String str, String delimiters, boolean returnDelims)` — If `true`, delimiters are also returned as tokens. ### 2.6.2 Key Methods | Method | Return Type | Purpose | |---|---|---| | `boolean hasMoreTokens()` | `boolean` | Returns true if more tokens exist. | | `String nextToken()` | `String` | Returns the next token from the string. | | `int countTokens()` | `int` | Returns the total number of remaining tokens. | > [!WARNING] > **`StringTokenizer` vs `split()`:** `StringTokenizer` is faster but **does not support regular expressions**. For complex parsing (e.g., splitting on `","` followed by optional whitespace), use `String.split()` with a regex. ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility Strings are the backbone of nearly every software system: - **Compilers & Interpreters:** Lexical analysis tokenizes source code using String operations. - **Databases:** SQL query construction and result parsing. - **Networking:** HTTP headers, JSON/XML serialization, URL routing. - **Cryptography:** Hash functions operate on String byte representations. - **User Interfaces:** Every text field, label, and button caption is a String. ## 2.8 Immutable Object Pattern (Design Perspective) The `String` class is the **canonical textbook example** of the **Immutable Object Pattern**: 1. The class is declared `final` (cannot be subclassed). 2. All instance fields are `private final`. 3. No setter methods are provided. 4. Defensive copying is used where necessary. > [!NOTE] > This design guarantees that Strings used as `HashMap` keys produce stable hash codes — a critical property for data structure integrity. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Demonstrating Immutability — Step-by-Step Analysis Let us analyze a classic KTU-style problem demonstrating String immutability. **Code Under Analysis:** ```java String s1 = "Kerala"; String s2 = s1; s1 = s1.concat(" University"); System.out.println(s1); System.out.println(s2); ``` ### Step 1: Literal Creation The literal `"Kerala"` is created. The JVM checks the String Constant Pool. Since `"Kerala"` does not exist, a new String object is created and placed in the pool. The reference variable `s1` points to this object. **Memory State After Step 1:** - Pool: `["Kerala"]` ← s1 ### Step 2: Reference Copy (NOT Object Copy) `String s2 = s1;` copies the **reference**, not the object. Both `s1` and `s2` now point to the same `"Kerala"` object in the pool. **Memory State After Step 2:** - Pool: `["Kerala"]` ← s1, s2 ### Step 3: The Concat Operation `s1 = s1.concat(" University");` invokes the `concat()` method. Because String is immutable, this method does **NOT** modify the existing `"Kerala"` object. Instead, it allocates a brand-new String object containing `"Kerala University"` and returns its reference. The assignment updates `s1` to point to this new object. The old `"Kerala"` object remains intact in the pool (still referenced by `s2`). **Memory State After Step 3:** - Pool: `["Kerala"]`, `["Kerala University"]` ← s1 - The original `"Kerala"` object is now ONLY referenced by `s2`. ### Step 4: Output Evaluation ```java System.out.println(s1); // Prints: Kerala University System.out.println(s2); // Prints: Kerala ``` ### Conclusion This example conclusively proves that **String objects are immutable**. The original `"Kerala"` was never altered; a new object was simply created and reassigned. ## 3.2 String Buffer Capacity Growth Derivation **Problem:** A `StringBuffer` is initialized with the default capacity (16 characters). You append a 50-character string. Trace the capacity growth. ### Step 1: Initial State $$\text{capacity}_0 = 16, \quad \text{length} = 0$$ ### Step 2: First Append (Triggering Overflow) After appending characters, the buffer length reaches **17** (one character beyond capacity). The JVM detects overflow and computes the new capacity using: $$\text{newCapacity} = (\text{oldCapacity} \times 2) + 2$$ Substituting: $$\text{newCapacity}_1 = (16 \times 2) + 2 = 34$$ So the internal array is reallocated to size **34**. ### Step 3: Second Append (If Still Overflowing) If the total required length still exceeds 34, the growth formula is applied again: $$\text{newCapacity}_2 = (34 \times 2) + 2 = 70$$ ### Step 4: Final Adjustment Rule After the geometric growth, if `requiredLength + 1` is still greater than `newCapacity`, the JVM uses: $$\text{finalCapacity} = \text{requiredLength} + 1$$ This ensures the buffer is always large enough to hold the characters plus a null terminator reference. ### Summary Table for a 50-character Append | Iteration | Old Capacity | Required | New Capacity | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | 16 | 50 | 34 | | 2 | 34 | 50 | 70 (sufficient) | ## 3.3 Performance Comparison — String Concatenation vs StringBuilder **Problem:** Compare the time complexity of building a string of length $n$ using naive `String` concatenation versus `StringBuilder.append()`. ### Case A: Naive String Concatenation Each `+` operator creates a new String. Concatenating $n$ characters one at a time requires: $$\text{Time}_{\text{String}} = O(1 + 2 + 3 + \ldots + n) = O\!\left(\frac{n(n+1)}{2}\right) = O(n^2)$$ This is because the first character creates a 1-length string, the second creates a 2-length string (copying the first), and so on. ### Case B: StringBuilder.append() The internal array grows geometrically (doubling). Total work is amortized: $$\text{Time}_{\text{Builder}} = O(n)$$ ### Conclusion $$\frac{\text{Time}_{\text{String}}}{\text{Time}_{\text{Builder}}} = \frac{O(n^2)}{O(n)} = O(n)$$ For $n = 1000$, naive concatenation is **1000× slower**. For $n = 1{,}000{,}000$, it is **1,000,000× slower**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Exam Answer:** "Use `StringBuilder` (or `StringBuffer` in multi-threaded code) when performing multiple concatenations in a loop. Avoid the `+` operator inside loops." ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Method Selection Guide | Requirement | Recommended Class | Justification | |---|---|---| | Storing a fixed literal (e.g., a country name) | `String` | Immutability, pool caching, hash stability. | | Building a long SQL query in a loop | `StringBuilder` | Single-threaded, O(n) performance. | | Multi-threaded log message construction | `StringBuffer` | Thread-safety required. | | Counting characters in user input | `String` (via `length()`) | Read-only operation. | | Implementing an Undo/Redo text editor | `StringBuilder` (single-user) | Mutable in-place editing. | | Using as a `HashMap` key | `String` | Stable `hashCode()` guaranteed by immutability. | ## 3.5 Exhaustive Walkthrough: `StringTokenizer` **Code Under Analysis:** ```java String sentence = "KTU,B.Tech,2024,Scheme"; StringTokenizer st = new StringTokenizer(sentence, ","); System.out.println("Total tokens: " + st.countTokens()); while (st.hasMoreTokens()) { System.out.println(st.nextToken()); } ``` ### Step 1: Construction The `StringTokenizer` is constructed with the input string and the delimiter set `","`. Internally, it stores the position cursor at index 0. ### Step 2: Initial Count `st.countTokens()` iterates through the remaining string counting delimiter-separated segments. The string has **3 commas**, dividing it into **4 tokens**. ### Step 3: Iteration Trace | Iteration | `hasMoreTokens()` | `nextToken()` Output | Cursor Position | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | `true` | `"KTU"` | After 1st `,` | | 2 | `true` | `"B.Tech"` | After 2nd `,` | | 3 | `true` | `"2024"` | After 3rd `,` | | 4 | `true` | `"Scheme"` | End of string | | 5 | `false` | (loop exits) | — | ### Step 4: Final Output ``` Total tokens: 4 KTU B.Tech 2024 Scheme ``` ## 3.6 Verification of Hash Code Stability **Problem:** Prove that the hash code of a String does not change after the string is created. ### Derivation The `String.hashCode()` algorithm (Java Specification) is: $$h(s) = s[0] \cdot 31^{(n-1)} + s[1] \cdot 31^{(n-2)} + \ldots + s[n-1] \cdot 31^{0}$$ where $s[i]$ is the Unicode code point of the $i$-th character and $n$ is the length. Since: 1. The internal character array is `private final`. 2. The class itself is `final`. 3. No public method modifies the array contents. The values $s[i]$ and $n$ are **invariant** for the lifetime of the object. Therefore, $h(s)$ is computed once (and cached as an `int` field in JDK-optimized implementations) and remains **constant**. > [!IMPORTANT] > This stability is precisely why Strings are safe to use as keys in `HashMap` and elements in `HashSet`. If a String's hash could change, the hash table would lose the entry, causing silent data loss. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 String Class Hierarchy and Memory Layout The following diagram illustrates the inheritance hierarchy of Java's String-related classes and the runtime memory layout of String objects. ```mermaid graph TD classDef classBox fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F6FEB,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef interfaceBox fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#D97706,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef memoryBox fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#1A7F37,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef immutableBox fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#D93025,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 objRoot["java.lang.Object"]:::classBox ifaceSerializable["java.io.Serializable"]:::interfaceBox ifaceComparable["java.lang.Comparable"]:::interfaceBox ifaceCharSeq["java.lang.CharSequence"]:::interfaceBox classString["java.lang.String
FINAL CLASS
Immutable"]:::immutableBox classBuffer["java.lang.StringBuffer
FINAL CLASS
Mutable Thread-Safe"]:::classBox classBuilder["java.lang.StringBuilder
FINAL CLASS
Mutable Fast"]:::classBox objRoot --> classString ifaceSerializable -.-> classString ifaceComparable -.-> classString ifaceCharSeq -.-> classString ifaceCharSeq -.-> classBuffer ifaceCharSeq -.-> classBuilder objRoot --> classBuffer objRoot --> classBuilder ``` ## 4.2 String Constant Pool Mechanism The following diagram shows how the JVM handles two different ways of creating Strings and their effect on the String Constant Pool. ```mermaid flowchart TD classDef poolBox fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#1A7F37,stroke-width:2px classDef heapBox fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#D97706,stroke-width:2px classDef codeBox fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F6FEB,stroke-width:2px code1["String A = APJ KTU"]:::codeBox code2["String B = APJ KTU"]:::codeBox code3["String C = new String APJ KTU"]:::codeBox code4["String D = C.intern"]:::codeBox poolCheck{"Does literal exist in Pool"}:::poolBox poolAdd["Add to String Constant Pool"]:::poolBox poolReuse["Reuse existing Pool reference"]:::poolBox heapAlloc["Allocate new String on Heap"]:::heapBox poolCheckD{"Is Pooled version available"}:::poolBox poolAssign["Point reference to Pool object"]:::poolBox A1["A points to Pool APJ KTU"]:::poolBox B1["B points to SAME Pool APJ KTU"]:::poolBox C1["C points to NEW Heap Object APJ KTU"]:::heapBox D1["D points to SAME Pool APJ KTU as A and B"]:::poolBox code1 --> poolCheck poolCheck -->|No| poolAdd --> A1 code2 --> poolCheck poolCheck -->|Yes| poolReuse --> B1 code3 --> heapAlloc --> C1 code4 --> poolCheckD poolCheckD -->|Yes| poolAssign --> D1 ``` ## 4.3 StringBuilder Internal State Transition This diagram illustrates the geometric capacity growth when characters are appended to a `StringBuilder`, demonstrating the amortized O(1) append behavior. ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Initial: new StringBuilder Initial: capacity = 16, length = 0 Initial --> Filling: append calls Filling: length grows with each append Filling --> OverflowCheck: length exceeds capacity OverflowCheck: Is length greater than capacity OverflowCheck --> Filling: No still fits OverflowCheck --> Reallocate: Yes overflow detected Reallocate: Reallocate internal byte array Reallocate: newCapacity equals 2 times old plus 2 Reallocate --> CopyAndFill: Copy old contents and append CopyAndFill --> Filling Filling --> Final: toString invoked Final: Convert to immutable String object Final --> [*] ``` ## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: String Processing Pipeline This block diagram maps the typical processing flow when handling user input strings in an enterprise application, highlighting where each String class is optimally applied. ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef inputBox fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F6FEB,stroke-width:2px classDef procBox fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#D97706,stroke-width:2px classDef outputBox fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#1A7F37,stroke-width:2px classDef keyBox fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#D93025,stroke-width:2px inputUI["User Input Form
Raw String from TextField"]:::inputBox sanitize["Sanitization Layer
String trim and replace"]:::procBox tokenize["Tokenization Layer
StringTokenizer or split"]:::procBox buildSQL["Query Construction
StringBuilder append"]:::procBox keyMap["Lookup Map Key
String equals and hashCode"]:::keyBox dbEngine["Database Engine
SQL Execution"]:::procBox resultSet["Result Set
String columns retrieved"]:::procBox concatDisplay["Display Formatting
String plus StringBuilder"]:::outputBox finalUI["Rendered UI Output
Immutable String literal"]:::outputBox inputUI --> sanitize sanitize --> tokenize tokenize --> buildSQL buildSQL --> keyMap keyMap --> dbEngine dbEngine --> resultSet resultSet --> concatDisplay concatDisplay --> finalUI ``` ## 4.5 Decision Flowchart — Choosing the Right String Class ```mermaid flowchart TD classDef decision fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#D97706,stroke-width:2px classDef action fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#1A7F37,stroke-width:2px start["Need to handle character data"]:::decision q1{"Will the sequence be modified"}:::decision q2{"Multi-threaded context"}:::decision q3{"Used as HashMap key"}:::decision a1["Use String
Immutable class
Pool optimized"]:::action a2["Use StringBuffer
Synchronized methods"]:::action a3["Use StringBuilder
Fast non-synchronized"]:::action a4["Use String
Hash stability required"]:::action start --> q1 q1 -->|No modifications| q3 q1 -->|Will be modified| q2 q3 -->|Yes used as key| a4 q3 -->|No simple literal| a1 q2 -->|Yes multi-thread| a2 q2 -->|No single-thread| a3 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam - Model Question Bank]** **Q: What is the difference between `String`, `StringBuffer`, and `StringBuilder` in Java? Mention one scenario where each is most appropriate.** **Model Answer:** - **`String`**: An **immutable** sequence of characters (defined in `java.lang`). Once created, its contents cannot be changed. Best used for **fixed text constants** like names, identifiers, or `HashMap` keys where stability and thread-safety are critical. - **`StringBuffer`**: A **mutable, thread-safe** (synchronized) sequence of characters. Best used when **multiple threads** concurrently modify the same string buffer (e.g., a shared log buffer in a server). - **`StringBuilder`**: A **mutable, non-synchronized** version of `StringBuffer`, introduced in JDK 5.0. Best used for **single-threaded** string manipulation inside loops where performance is critical (e.g., building a large SQL query inside a single-threaded function). ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam - Model Question Bank]** **Q: Explain the concept of the String Constant Pool in Java. What happens when you write `String s = new String("Hello");`?** **Model Answer:** The **String Constant Pool** (also called the String Literal Pool) is a special memory area inside the **JVM heap** where Java stores unique String literals. When the JVM encounters a String literal, it first checks if an identical literal already exists in the pool. If yes, the reference points to the existing object (memory optimization); if no, a new String object is created and added to the pool. When you write `String s = new String("Hello");`, the `new` keyword **forces** the JVM to allocate a brand-new String object in the **heap (outside the pool)**, even if `"Hello"` already exists in the pool. To force this new object into the pool, one must call `s = s.intern();`. ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme]** **Part (a)** — **7 Marks** Explain the internal architecture of the `String` class in Java. Discuss immutability, the String Constant Pool, and the difference between `String s = "Hello";` and `String s = new String("Hello");` with suitable diagrams and code snippets. **Model Solution:** The `String` class in Java, declared as `public final class String` in the `java.lang` package, is the platform's primary mechanism for representing immutable text. It implements the `Serializable`, `Comparable`, and `CharSequence` interfaces. **Immutability Mechanism:** The `String` class achieves immutability through three design choices: 1. The class itself is declared `final`, preventing subclassing that might bypass immutability. 2. The internal character storage (historically `char[]`, now `byte[]` with an encoding flag in JDK 9+) is `private final`. 3. No public mutator method is provided. Every apparent modification (like `concat`, `replace`, `substring`) returns a brand-new String object. ```java final class StringArchitectureDemo { public static void main(String[] args) { String literal = "Kerala"; // Pool allocation String explicit = new String("Kerala"); // Heap allocation // Comparison: different objects, same content System.out.println(literal == explicit); // false (different references) System.out.println(literal.equals(explicit)); // true (same content) // Interning demonstration String interned = explicit.intern(); System.out.println(literal == interned); // true (both point to pool object) } } ``` **Difference Between the Two Creation Methods:** | Aspect | `String s = "Hello";` | `String s = new String("Hello");` | |---|---|---| | Object Count | 1 (pool only, if new) | 2 (one pool, one heap) unless pool already contains it | | Location | String Constant Pool | Heap (outside pool) | | Pool Reuse | Yes | No (unless `intern()` is called) | | Memory Efficient | Yes | No | | Recommended For | Most use cases | Rare; typically to force a distinct heap object | **Part (b)** — **7 Marks** Write a Java program that reads a sentence from the user, tokenizes it using `StringTokenizer` with space as the default delimiter, and then displays each token along with its length. Also count and display the total number of vowels (a, e, i, o, u — case-insensitive) across all tokens. **Model Solution:** ```java import java.util.StringTokenizer; import java.util.Scanner; class TokenVowelAnalyzer { public static void main(String[] args) { Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in); System.out.print("Enter a sentence: "); String sentence = sc.nextLine(); StringTokenizer tokenizer = new StringTokenizer(sentence); int totalVowels = 0; int tokenNumber = 1; System.out.println("\n--- Token Analysis ---"); while (tokenizer.hasMoreTokens()) { String token = tokenizer.nextToken(); System.out.println("Token " + tokenNumber + ": \"" + token + "\" (Length: " + token.length() + ")"); totalVowels += countVowels(token); tokenNumber++; } System.out.println("\nTotal number of tokens: " + (tokenNumber - 1)); System.out.println("Total vowels across all tokens: " + totalVowels); sc.close(); } static int countVowels(String s) { int count = 0; String lowered = s.toLowerCase(); for (int i = 0; i < lowered.length(); i++) { char ch = lowered.charAt(i); if (ch == 'a' || ch == 'e' || ch == 'i' || ch == 'o' || ch == 'u') { count++; } } return count; } } ``` **Sample Output:** ``` Enter a sentence: Object Oriented Programming with Java --- Token Analysis --- Token 1: "Object" (Length: 6) Token 2: "Oriented" (Length: 8) Token 3: "Programming" (Length: 11) Token 4: "with" (Length: 4) Token 5: "Java" (Length: 4) Total number of tokens: 5 Total vowels across all tokens: 11 ``` ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme]** **Part (a)** — **7 Marks** Compare and contrast `StringBuffer` and `StringBuilder` in Java. With a suitable example, demonstrate how `StringBuilder` can be used to efficiently reverse a string and insert a substring at a specified position. **Model Solution:** **Comparison Table:** | Feature | `StringBuffer` | `StringBuilder` | |---|---|---| | Introduced In | JDK 1.0 | JDK 5.0 | | Mutability | Mutable | Mutable | | Thread-Safety | **Synchronized** (thread-safe) | **Not synchronized** (not thread-safe) | | Performance | Slower due to lock acquisition | **Faster** (no synchronization overhead) | | Storage | Heap | Heap | | Recommended Use | Multi-threaded environments | Single-threaded environments | **Demonstration Code:** ```java class StringBuilderDemo { public static void main(String[] args) { String original = "Object Oriented"; // Efficient reversal using StringBuilder StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder(original); String reversed = sb.reverse().toString(); System.out.println("Original : " + original); System.out.println("Reversed : " + reversed); // Inserting a substring at a specific position StringBuilder sb2 = new StringBuilder("Programming"); sb2.insert(0, "Java "); // Insert at the beginning sb2.insert(sb2.length(), " Language"); // Append at the end System.out.println("After insertions: " + sb2.toString()); } } ``` **Output:** ``` Original : Object Oriented Reversed : detneirO tcejbO After insertions: Java Programming Language ``` **Part (b)** — **7 Marks** Explain why Strings are considered immutable in Java. List **five** methods of the `String` class along with their signatures and purposes. Also explain why immutability makes Strings suitable as keys in `HashMap`. **Model Solution:** **Why Strings are Immutable:** 1. **Class declared `final`** — prevents subclassing that could introduce mutability. 2. **Internal character array is `private final`** — the reference cannot be reassigned after construction. 3. **No setter methods** — no method is provided to alter the internal array. 4. **Defensive copying** in mutator-returning methods like `concat()` and `substring()` — ensures the original is never exposed. 5. **Caching of `hashCode()`** — possible only because content never changes, making `HashMap` lookups extremely fast. **Five Important `String` Methods:** | # | Method Signature | Purpose | |---|---|---| | 1 | `public int length()` | Returns the count of characters in the string. | | 2 | `public char charAt(int index)` | Returns the character at the given 0-based index. | | 3 | `public String substring(int begin, int end)` | Returns a new string from `begin` (inclusive) to `end` (exclusive). | | 4 | `public boolean equals(Object obj)` | Compares content for equality (overrides `Object.equals`). | | 5 | `public String concat(String str)` | Concatenates the given string to the end of this string and returns a new string. | **Why Immutability Makes Strings Suitable as `HashMap` Keys:** A `HashMap` stores key-value pairs in buckets based on the **hash code** of the key. When a key is looked up, the map recomputes its hash code to locate the correct bucket. If a String's contents could change after insertion, its hash code would change, and the map would look in the wrong bucket — the entry would effectively be **lost**. Because Strings are immutable, their hash code is computed once and remains stable for the object's lifetime, guaranteeing that lookups always succeed. Additionally, immutability ensures the `equals()` contract is never violated after insertion, preserving the structural integrity of the hash table. ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:** > > 1. **Confusing `==` with `.equals()`**: `==` compares **references** (memory addresses), while `.equals()` compares **content**. Using `==` for String content comparison is the #1 mistake. KTU examiners specifically deduct 1–2 marks for this. > > 2. **Forgetting that String literals go to the Pool**: Writing `String s = new String("Hello");` and then comparing with `==` to a literal `"Hello"` will yield `false`. Always use `.equals()` for content comparison. > > 3. **Using `String` inside loops for concatenation**: Code like `String result = ""; for(...) result += data;` is O(n²). Examiners expect `StringBuilder` here. > > 4. **Mixing up `length()` for arrays and Strings**: Arrays use `array.length` (a field); Strings use `str.length()` (a method). Writing `str.length` without parentheses is a compilation error. > > 5. **Confusing `StringBuffer` capacity growth formula**: The correct formula is `2 × oldCapacity + 2`, **NOT** simply doubling. Missing the `+ 2` term loses marks. > > 6. **Off-by-one in `substring(begin, end)`**: `end` is **exclusive**. Students often write `substring(0, str.length())` correctly but confuse it with inclusive ranges from other languages. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Strings (KTU PECIT633 Module 1)** - A `String` in Java is an **immutable object** of the class `java.lang.String`. - **Immutability** is achieved via: `final` class, `final` internal array, no setters, defensive copying. - The **String Constant Pool** stores unique String literals in the heap for memory reuse. - `String s = "abc";` → uses pool; `String s = new String("abc");` → creates new heap object. - `==` compares **references**; `.equals()` compares **content**. Always use `.equals()` for Strings. - `String.length()` returns the number of UTF-16 code units (NOT code points). - `String.charAt(i)` returns the character at index `i` (0-based; throws `StringIndexOutOfBoundsException` if invalid). - `String.substring(begin, end)` — `begin` inclusive, `end` exclusive. - `String.compareTo(s2)` returns a **negative**, **zero**, or **positive** integer for less-than, equal-to, or greater-than. - `StringBuffer` is **mutable and thread-safe** (synchronized). Use in multi-threaded contexts. - `StringBuilder` is **mutable but NOT thread-safe**. Faster than `StringBuffer`. Use in single-threaded loops. - `StringBuffer`/`StringBuilder` default initial capacity = **16**. - Capacity growth formula: `newCapacity = (oldCapacity × 2) + 2`. - Naive String concatenation in a loop is **O(n²)**; `StringBuilder.append()` is **amortized O(n)**. - `StringTokenizer` (in `java.util`) splits a String by delimiters. Default delimiters: space, `\t`, `\n`, `\r`, `\f`. - `StringTokenizer` does **not support regular expressions**; use `String.split()` for that. - `intern()` method moves a heap-allocated String into the pool (or returns the existing pooled reference). - Strings are **hash-stable** due to immutability, making them ideal `HashMap` keys. - `String` is declared `final`, so it **cannot be subclassed**.

topics.title: Vector Class
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT633 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Vector Class

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Tone)**
> The `Vector` class in Java is a legacy container class defined in the `java.util` package that implements a **resizable (growable) array of objects**. It extends `AbstractList` and implements the interfaces `List`, `RandomAccess`, `Cloneable`, and `java.io.Serializable`. Unlike the modern `ArrayList`, every method of `Vector` is **synchronized**, making it **thread-safe** but comparatively slower in single-threaded contexts.

**Package:** `java.util.Vector`
**Introduced in:** JDK 1.0 (Legacy Class)
**Type:** Dynamic, Indexed, Resizable Object Array

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🏢

Imagine a **smart parking lot with a robotic gate**:
- Initially, the parking lot has space for **10 cars** (initial capacity).
- When the 11th car arrives, the system **automatically expands** the lot by creating a new, larger lot (usually doubling capacity) and transfers all parked cars to the new lot.
- The robotic gate (synchronized lock) ensures that **only one car enters/exits at a time** to prevent collision — this is the *thread-safety* feature.
- Each car can be located directly by its **parking slot number** (indexed access — $O(1)$).
- Old `Vector` = old robotic lot from JDK 1.0. Modern `ArrayList` = a faster lot without a robotic gate (used when no concurrent access is needed).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** `Vector` = **Resizable Array + Thread-Safety + Legacy (pre-Collections Framework)**.

---

## 1.3 Class Hierarchy at a Glance

$$ \text{java.lang.Object} \;\rightarrow\; \text{java.util.AbstractCollection} \;\rightarrow\; \text{java.util.AbstractList} \;\rightarrow\; \text{java.util.Vector} $$

**Implemented Interfaces:**
- `List<E>` — Ordered collection
- `RandomAccess` — Supports fast (constant-time) indexed access
- `Cloneable` — Allows object duplication
- `java.io.Serializable` — Allows object serialization

---

## 1.4 Why `Vector` Exists (Engineering Context)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the **pre-Collections Framework era (JDK 1.0)**, `Vector` was the only resizable array in Java. With the introduction of the **Collections Framework in JDK 1.2**, `ArrayList` was introduced as a faster, non-synchronized alternative. `Vector` is retained for **backward compatibility**. Today, in **KTU examinations** and legacy enterprise code, it is still tested for its conceptual and historical significance.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Internal Working Mechanism

The `Vector` class internally maintains:
1. **`elementData[]`** — An `Object` array that physically stores the elements.
2. **`elementCount`** — The number of elements actually stored (logical size).
3. **`capacityIncrement`** — Custom growth factor; if `0`, the array doubles in size.

### 2.1.1 Growth Policy (Critical for KTU)

When the internal array is full and a new element is added:

$$ \text{New Capacity} = \begin{cases} \text{oldCapacity} + \text{capacityIncrement} & \text{if } \text{capacityIncrement} > 0 \\ 2 \times \text{oldCapacity} & \text{if } \text{capacityIncrement} = 0 \end{cases} $$

This growth strategy is called **amortized doubling** and ensures that the average time complexity of adding an element remains **$O(1)$** (amortized).

---

## 2.2 Constructors of Vector

| # | Constructor | Description | Default Capacity |
|---|-------------|-------------|------------------|
| 1 | `Vector()` | Creates an empty vector with default initial capacity. | **10** |
| 2 | `Vector(int initialCapacity)` | Creates a vector with the specified initial capacity. | User-defined |
| 3 | `Vector(int initialCapacity, int capacityIncrement)` | Creates a vector with custom capacity **and** custom growth increment. | User-defined |
| 4 | `Vector(Collection<? extends E> c)` | Creates a vector containing the elements of the given collection, in the order returned by its iterator. | Size of collection |

---

## 2.3 Key Methods — KTU Reference Table

| Method | Signature | Return Type | Description |
|--------|-----------|-------------|-------------|
| `add(E e)` | Appends element at end | `boolean` | Inserts at index `elementCount` |
| `add(int index, E e)` | Inserts at given index | `void` | Shifts subsequent elements right |
| `addElement(E obj)` | Legacy method (since JDK 1.0) | `void` | Same as `add(E)` |
| `get(int index)` | Returns element at index | `E` | $O(1)$ random access |
| `set(int index, E e)` | Replaces element at index | `E` | Returns old value |
| `remove(int index)` | Removes by index | `E` | Shifts elements left |
| `removeElementAt(int index)` | Legacy equivalent | `void` | Same as `remove(int)` |
| `removeElement(Object obj)` | Removes by value | `boolean` | Removes first occurrence |
| `removeAllElements()` | Empties the vector | `void` | Sets count to 0 |
| `size()` | Logical size | `int` | Returns `elementCount` |
| `capacity()` | Physical size | `int` | Returns length of `elementData` |
| `isEmpty()` | Checks if empty | `boolean` | `true` if `size() == 0` |
| `contains(Object o)` | Membership test | `boolean` | Linear search — $O(n)$ |
| `firstElement()` | First element | `E` | Throws `NoSuchElementException` |
| `lastElement()` | Last element | `E` | Throws `NoSuchElementException` |
| `elements()` | Returns Enumeration | `Enumeration<E>` | Legacy iteration |
| `trimToSize()` | Trims internal array | `void` | `capacity = size` |
| `setSize(int newSize)` | Resizes logically | `void` | Pads with `null` or truncates |
| `ensureCapacity(int min)` | Pre-allocates | `void` | Avoids regrowth |
| `clone()` | Shallow copy | `Object` | Implements `Cloneable` |
| `toString()` | String representation | `String` | Format: `[e1, e2, ...]` |

---

## 2.4 Complexity Analysis (Big-O Summary)

| Operation | Time Complexity | Notes |
|-----------|----------------|-------|
| Access by index (`get`/`set`) | $O(1)$ | RandomAccess interface |
| Insert at end (`add`) | Amortized $O(1)$ | Occasional resize |
| Insert at arbitrary index | $O(n)$ | Shifts elements right |
| Remove by index | $O(n)$ | Shifts elements left |
| Search (`contains`/`indexOf`) | $O(n)$ | Linear scan |
| `size()` / `isEmpty()` | $O(1)$ | Direct field access |

---

## 2.5 Vector vs. ArrayList — The Most Asked KTU Question

| Feature | `Vector` | `ArrayList` |
|---------|----------|-------------|
| Introduced in | JDK 1.0 (Legacy) | JDK 1.2 (Collections Framework) |
| Thread-Safety | **Synchronized** (Thread-safe) | **Not synchronized** (Not thread-safe) |
| Performance | Slower (due to lock overhead) | Faster |
| Growth Policy | Doubles (or custom increment) | Increases by **50%** (`1.5x`) |
| Legacy Methods | `addElement()`, `elements()` | Not available |
| Use Case | Multi-threaded legacy code | Single-threaded modern code |

$$ \text{ArrayList Growth: } \text{newCapacity} = \text{oldCapacity} + \left(\text{oldCapacity} \gg 1\right) $$

$$ \text{Vector Growth (default): } \text{newCapacity} = 2 \times \text{oldCapacity} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** Always prefer `ArrayList` in new code unless thread-safety is mandatory. For thread-safety, use `Collections.synchronizedList(new ArrayList<>())` or `CopyOnWriteArrayList`.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility

- **Legacy enterprise systems** (banking, telecom) where JDK 1.0 code persists.
- **Thread-safe shared lists** in older multi-threaded Java applications.
- **Exam-oriented academic questions** in KTU 2024 Scheme OOP module.
- **Enumeration-based iteration** for legacy code reading.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Code Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Programmatic Demonstration — Complete Vector Lifecycle

```java
import java.util.Vector;
import java.util.Enumeration;

public class VectorDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
```
