<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION_5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between procedural programming and object-oriented programming. List any four differences. (3 Marks)**

#### Model Answer (Board-Examiner Style)
| Sl. | Procedural Programming | Object-Oriented Programming |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Based on **functions / procedures** acting on data. | Based on **objects** bundling data + behavior. |
| 2 | Follows **top-down** approach. | Follows **bottom-up** approach. |
| 3 | No access modifiers; data is largely **global**. | Uses `private`, `protected`, `public` for **encapsulation**. |
| 4 | Code reuse through **function libraries**. | Code reuse through **inheritance & polymorphism**. |

*(Any four points — ½ mark each + 1 mark for accurate terminology = 3 marks)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is polymorphism? Explain compile-time polymorphism with a suitable example. (3 Marks)**

#### Model Answer
**Polymorphism** is the ability of a single interface or method to take **multiple forms**, allowing the same operation to behave differently based on context.

**Compile-time polymorphism** is achieved through **method overloading** — multiple methods having the **same name** but **different parameter lists** (number, type, or order) within the same class. The compiler resolves the correct method at compile time.

**Example:**
```java
class MathOp {
    int add(int a, int b)         { return a + b; }
    double add(double a, double b) { return a + b; }
    int add(int a, int b, int c)   { return a + b + c; }
}
```
Calling `m.add(2,3)` → first method; `m.add(2.5,3.5)` → second method; `m.add(1,2,3)` → third. *(Definition 1 mark + explanation 1 mark + example 1 mark = 3 marks)*

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

#### (a) Explain the four fundamental concepts of Object-Oriented Programming with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer

**1. Encapsulation** *(2 marks)*
Encapsulation is the wrapping of data and methods into a single unit (class) and restricting direct access via access modifiers.
```java
class Student {
    private int rollNo;        // hidden
    public void setRoll(int r) { 
        if (r > 0) rollNo = r; 
    }
    public int getRoll() { return rollNo; }
}
```

**2. Abstraction** *(2 marks)*
Abstraction hides implementation complexity and exposes only the essential interface. Achieved through `abstract class` or `interface`.
```java
abstract class Shape {
    abstract double area();    // contract only
}
```

**3. Inheritance** *(1.5 marks)*
A subclass acquires properties of a superclass, enabling reuse and hierarchical classification.
```java
class Dog extends Animal {
    void bark() { System.out.println("Bark"); }
}
```

**4. Polymorphism** *(1.5 marks)*
Same interface, multiple implementations. Method overriding enables runtime polymorphism.

---

#### (b) Write a Java program to demonstrate the use of an interface with at least two implementing classes. Explain how it achieves multiple inheritance in Java. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer

```java
// Step 1: Define two independent interfaces
interface Printable {
    void print();
}
interface Showable {
    void show();
}

// Step 2: Class implements BOTH interfaces — multiple inheritance
class Report implements Printable, Showable {
    public void print() { System.out.println("Printing Report"); }
    public void show()  { System.out.println("Showing Report Preview"); }
}

// Step 3: Another implementer
class Photo implements Printable, Showable {
    public void print() { System.out.println("Printing Photo"); }
    public void show()  { System.out.println("Displaying Photo"); }
}

public class TestInterface {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Printable p = new Report();        // upcasting
        p.print();

        Showable s = new Photo();          // upcasting
        s.show();
    }
}
```

**Explanation (How multiple inheritance is achieved):**
Java does **not** allow `extends A, B` for classes (to avoid the *Diamond Problem* where a class inherits conflicting implementations from two parents). However, since interfaces contain **no state and (in Java 7) no implementation**, the ambiguity vanishes. A class can `implements Printable, Showable` and provide its own implementations, thereby inheriting the *type contracts* from multiple sources safely.

*(Code 4 marks + explanation of multiple inheritance 3 marks)*

---

### Question B (Alternative) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### (a) Explain the difference between abstract class and interface in Java. When would you prefer one over the other? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer

| Feature | Abstract Class | Interface |
|---|---|---|
| Keyword | `abstract class` | `interface` |
| Methods | Abstract + Concrete | Abstract (Java 7) / Default + Static (Java 8+) |
| Variables | Any type | `public static final` only |
| Constructor | Yes | No |
| Inheritance | Single (`extends`) | Multiple (`implements`) |
| Access modifiers for methods | Any | Implicitly `public` |
| When to use | Shared code + state among related classes | Contract enforcement across unrelated classes |

**Preference Guidelines** *(2 marks)*:
- Prefer **`abstract class`** when classes share **code, fields, or constructors** (true *is-a* relationship with state).
- Prefer **`interface`** when defining a **pure contract** for unrelated classes, or when **multiple inheritance of type** is required.

*(Table 4 marks + preference 3 marks)*

---

#### (b) Write a Java program demonstrating encapsulation and data abstraction for a `Vehicle` hierarchy. Use at least one abstract class and method overriding. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer

```java
// Step 1: Abstract class — Abstraction + Encapsulation
abstract class Vehicle {
    private String model;          // encapsulated
    private int speed;

    public Vehicle(String model) {
        this.model = model;
        this.speed = 0;
    }

    public String getModel()  { return model; }
    public int    getSpeed()  { return speed; }
    protected void setSpeed(int s) { this.speed = s; }

    // Abstract behavior — each vehicle defines its own
    public abstract void accelerate();
    public abstract void brake();
}

class Car extends Vehicle {
    public Car(String model) { super(model); }

    @Override
    public void accelerate() { 
        setSpeed(getSpeed() + 20); 
        System.out.println(getModel() + " Car accelerating. Speed=" + getSpeed()); 
    }
    @Override
    public void brake() { 
        setSpeed(Math.max(0, getSpeed() - 20)); 
        System.out.println(getModel() + " Car braking. Speed=" + getSpeed()); 
    }
}

class Bike extends Vehicle {
    public Bike(String model) { super(model); }

    @Override
    public void accelerate() { 
        setSpeed(getSpeed() + 10); 
        System.out.println(getModel() + " Bike accelerating. Speed=" + getSpeed()); 
    }
    @Override
    public void brake() { 
        setSpeed(Math.max(0, getSpeed() - 15)); 
        System.out.println(getModel() + " Bike braking. Speed=" + getSpeed()); 
    }
}

public class TestVehicle {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Vehicle v1 = new Car("Honda City");    // polymorphism — upcasting
        Vehicle v2 = new Bike("Royal Enfield");

        v1.accelerate();   // invokes Car.accelerate()
        v2.accelerate();   // invokes Bike.accelerate()
    }
}
```

**Concepts Demonstrated:**
- **Encapsulation:** `private` fields `model`, `speed` with controlled getters/setters.
- **Abstraction:** `Vehicle` is `abstract` with abstract methods.
- **Inheritance:** `Car` and `Bike` extend `Vehicle`.
- **Polymorphism:** `v1.accelerate()` and `v2.accelerate()` invoke different overridden implementations.

*(Code 5 marks + concept identification 2 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing Abstraction with Encapsulation:** Many students write *both* definitions interchangeably. **Examiner's tip:** Encapsulation = *data hiding + bundling* (use modifiers); Abstraction = *hiding implementation* (use `abstract`/`interface`). Mixing them up costs **1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting `@Override` annotation:** Not technically wrong, but **best practice** expected by KTU 2024 examiners; missing it can cost **½ mark**.
> 3. **Interface methods not marked `public`:** Implementers must use `public` access. Writing `void print()` instead of `public void print()` triggers a compile error and shows **poor understanding**.
> 4. **Procedural vs OOP — generic answers:** Do *not* write "OOP is better than Procedural." KTU expects **specific** differences (data handling, code reuse, security, modeling).
> 5. **No example for polymorphism:** Always give a **runnable code snippet** showing overriding or overloading — bare definitions fetch partial credit only.
> 6. **Confusing `extends` and `implements`:** A class `extends` another class; `implements` an interface. Switching them = **wrong syntax, 0 marks** for that subpart.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Interface** = pure contract; cannot be instantiated; variables are `public static final` (constants).
- ✅ A class can **implement multiple interfaces** but **extend only one class** in Java.
- ✅ **Java 8+ interfaces** support `default` and `static` methods — allow backward-compatible API evolution.
- ✅ **Encapsulation** is enforced using the `private` access modifier + public getters/setters.
- ✅ **Data abstraction** hides *how* things work; exposes *what* they do. Use `abstract class` / `interface`.
- ✅ **Inheritance** uses `extends` keyword; chain via `super()` constructor calls.
- ✅ **Method Overloading** → compile-time polymorphism → same method name, different signature, **same class**.
- ✅ **Method Overriding** → runtime polymorphism → same signature, **parent-child classes**, requires `@Override`.
- ✅ **Dynamic dispatch** is the JVM mechanism that invokes the *actual object's* overridden method at runtime.
- ✅ **Procedural paradigm** = function-centric, top-down, no built-in data hiding.
- ✅ **OOP paradigm** = object-centric, bottom-up, built-in encapsulation, inheritance, polymorphism.
- ✅ Use **interfaces** when unrelated classes must share a contract; use **abstract classes** when there is shared code/state among related classes.
- ✅ **`final` keyword** prevents inheritance (class), overriding (method), and reassignment (variable).
- ✅ The four pillars — **Abstraction, Encapsulation, Inheritance, Polymorphism** — together produce **modular, reusable, maintainable, secure** software.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Object-Oriented Programming in Java - Declaring Objects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT633 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Object-Oriented Programming in Java - Declaring Objects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Java, an **object** is a runtime entity that encapsulates both **state** (fields/attributes) and **behavior** (methods). **Declaring an object** is a **two-step (sometimes three-step) process** that involves creating a **reference variable** (a handle that holds the memory address of the object) and then using the `new` operator to allocate memory on the **heap** for the actual object instance.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Object Oriented Programming*, declaring objects is the foundational act of **instantiation** — the bridge between an abstract **class blueprint** and a concrete **in-memory entity**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 1):**
> A Java object is never created at the moment of declaration. Declaration only creates a **reference** (a pointer-like variable). Actual physical memory allocation occurs only when the `new` keyword is invoked. This distinction is a **favourite 3-mark and 7-mark question** in KTU End Semester Examinations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a **blueprint of a house** (the **Class**). You cannot live inside the blueprint — you must first build the physical house. Now, consider the following real-world analogy:

| Real-World Analogy | Java Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Address written on a paper (e.g., "House #42, MG Road") | **Reference Variable** |
| The physical house standing at that address | **Object Instance (on Heap)** |
| The act of constructing the house | **`new` keyword execution** |
| The architectural blueprint | **Class Definition** |
| Issuing the address card (registration) | **Variable Declaration** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:**
> When you write `Student s1;`, you are merely printing an *address card* that says "Future home of a Student object." The address has no building yet. When you write `s1 = new Student();`, the **construction company (JVM + `new`)** builds the actual house at that location and stamps the address onto the card. The card and the house are now linked, but they are still two separate things in memory.

### 1.3 Why "Declaring Objects" Is a Two-Step Process

$$\text{Object Creation} = \underbrace{\text{Declaration}}_{\text{Step 1: Reference Variable}} + \underbrace{\text{Instantiation}}_{\text{Step 2: Memory Allocation with } new}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Recall Rule:** *Declaration does NOT create the object. It only creates a reference. Instantiation is what actually creates the object.*

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 The Anatomy of Object Declaration — Structured Logic

The complete lifecycle of declaring and using a Java object follows **three distinct logical phases**. Each phase performs a separate duty in the JVM:

#### **Phase 1: Reference Variable Declaration**
- A variable of the class type is declared.
- Memory is allocated on the **Stack** (a Last-In-First-Out memory region used for local variables and method frames).
- The variable is initialized to the default reference value, which is `null`.
- **Syntax:** `ClassName referenceVariable;`

#### **Phase 2: Object Instantiation**
- The `new` keyword is invoked, followed by a call to a **constructor**.
- Memory is allocated on the **Heap** (a free-form memory pool where all objects live) sufficient to hold all instance variables of the class.
- All instance variables are initialized to their **default values** (`0`, `0.0`, `false`, `null`).
- The constructor body executes, performing any custom initialization.
- The `new` expression returns a **memory address** (a reference) pointing to the newly constructed object.

#### **Phase 3: Assignment of Reference**
- The memory address returned by `new` is stored in the reference variable declared in Phase 1.
- Now the reference variable on the Stack "points to" the object on the Heap.

### 2.2 KTU Reference Sheet — Syntax, Operators & Default Values

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below is the **High-Yield Cheat Sheet** for KTU board exams. Memorize every row — these are the most commonly asked definitions.

| Construct / Keyword | Syntax | Purpose / Behavior | Memory Region Affected |
|---|---|---|---|
| Class Declaration | `class ClassName { ... }` | Defines the blueprint | Method Area (Metaspace) |
| Reference Declaration | `ClassName refVar;` | Creates a reference, value = `null` | Stack |
| Instantiation | `new ClassName()` | Allocates memory, runs constructor, returns address | Heap |
| Combined Form | `ClassName refVar = new ClassName();` | One-line declaration + instantiation | Stack + Heap |
| Default Reference Value | `null` | Unassigned reference | N/A (literal) |
| `this` keyword | `this.field` | Refers to current object instance | Heap |
| Dot Operator | `refVar.member` | Accesses fields/methods of the referenced object | N/A |
| Garbage Collector | Automatic | Reclaims memory of unreferenced objects | Heap |

**Default Values Table (KTU Favourite):**

| Data Type | Default Value |
|---|---|
| `byte`, `short`, `int`, `long` | `0` (or `0L`) |
| `float`, `double` | `0.0` (or `0.0f`) |
| `char` | `'\u0000'` |
| `boolean` | `false` |
| Any Reference Type | `null` |

### 2.3 Combining the Steps — Single-Line vs Split Declaration

**Split Declaration Form (Two Statements):**
```java
Student s1;        // Phase 1: Reference on Stack, value = null
s1 = new Student(); // Phase 2 & 3: Object on Heap, address stored in s1
```

**Combined Form (One Statement — Recommended):**
```java
Student s1 = new Student(); // All three phases in one line
```

### 2.4 Multiple References to the Same Object

A single object can be referenced by **multiple reference variables**. This is known as **aliasing**:

```java
Student s1 = new Student(); // Object #1 created
Student s2 = s1;             // s2 now points to the SAME object as s1
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Aliasing Pitfall:** Changes made through `s1` are visible through `s2` because both references point to the **same memory location**. This is a classic KTU 7-mark question.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **GUI Programming (Swing/JavaFX):** Every button, label, and text field is an object declared using this exact mechanism.
- **JDBC Database Applications:** `Connection`, `Statement`, and `ResultSet` objects are declared and instantiated via `new`.
- **Spring / Spring Boot Frameworks:** Beans are essentially objects whose lifecycle is managed by the IoC container using reflection and `new`-like instantiation under the hood.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Execution Trace & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — Complete Lifecycle Trace

Consider the following Java snippet:

```java
class Box {
    int length;
    int width;

    Box(int l, int w) {
        length = l;
        width = w;
    }

    int volume() {
        return length * width;
    }
}

public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Box b1;                  // Statement 1
        b1 = new Box(5, 3);      // Statement 2
        Box b2 = new Box(2, 4);  // Statement 3
        b1 = b2;                 // Statement 4
    }
}
```

Let us trace **each statement step-by-step**, showing memory state evolution.

---

#### **Statement 1: `Box b1;`**

**Step 1.1** — Compiler checks if class `Box` is visible (imported or in same file).
**Step 1.2** — A reference variable named `b1` of type `Box` is created on the **Stack**.
**Step 1.3** — The reference `b1` is initialized to `null` (the JVM default for unassigned references).

$$\text{Stack: } b1 \rightarrow \text{null}$$

**Step 1.4** — No object exists on the Heap yet.

$$\text{Heap: } \varnothing$$

---

#### **Statement 2: `b1 = new Box(5, 3);`**

**Step 2.1** — The `new` keyword signals the JVM to allocate Heap memory sufficient for two `int` fields (`length`, `width`).
**Step 2.2** — All instance variables are initialized to default `int` value: `0`.

$$\text{Heap Memory Allocated: } \begin{Bmatrix} length = 0 \\ width = 0 \end{Bmatrix}$$

**Step 2.3** — The constructor `Box(int l, int w)` is invoked with `l = 5`, `w = 3`.
**Step 2.4** — The constructor body assigns: `length = 5`, `width = 3`.
**Step 2.5** — The `new` expression returns the **memory address** of the constructed object (let us call it `0xABC1` for illustration).
**Step 2.6** — This address is stored in `b1`.

$$\text{Stack: } b1 \rightarrow 0xABC1 \quad\quad \text{Heap: } 0xABC1 \rightarrow \begin{Bmatrix} length = 5 \\ width = 3 \end{Bmatrix}$$

---

#### **Statement 3: `Box b2 = new Box(2, 4);`**

**Step 3.1** — A new reference `b2` is created on the Stack (value: `null` initially).
**Step 3.2** — `new Box(2, 4)` allocates a **second**, independent object on the Heap at address `0xDEF2`.
**Step 3.3** — Constructor sets `length = 2`, `width = 4`.
**Step 3.4** — Address `0xDEF2` is stored in `b2`.

$$\text{Stack: } \begin{cases} b1 \rightarrow 0xABC1 \\ b2 \rightarrow 0xDEF2 \end{cases} \quad\quad \text{Heap: } \begin{cases} 0xABC1 \rightarrow (5, 3) \\ 0xDEF2 \rightarrow (2, 4) \end{cases}$$

---

#### **Statement 4: `b1 = b2;`**

**Step 4.1** — The value stored in `b2` (which is `0xDEF2`) is copied into `b1`.
**Step 4.2** — Now both `b1` and `b2` point to the **same object** at `0xDEF2`.
**Step 4.3** — The original object at `0xABC1` is **no longer referenced** by any variable.

$$\text{Stack: } \begin{cases} b1 \rightarrow 0xDEF2 \\ b2 \rightarrow 0xDEF2 \end{cases} \quad\quad \text{Heap: } \begin{cases} 0xABC1 \rightarrow \text{(UNREACHABLE - Eligible for GC)} \\ 0xDEF2 \rightarrow (2, 4) \end{cases}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Analytical Insight:** The object originally created in Statement 2 (at `0xABC1`) becomes an **orphan** (no live references). The **Garbage Collector (GC)** will reclaim this memory at some unpredictable future time. This is a **classic KTU viva question**: *"When is an object eligible for garbage collection?"*

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Object State Transitions

| Statement | `b1` (Stack) | `b2` (Stack) | Heap Object #1 (5,3) | Heap Object #2 (2,4) | GC Eligible? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| After `Box b1;` | `null` | — | — | — | No |
| After `b1 = new Box(5,3);` | `0xABC1` | — | Live | — | No |
| After `Box b2 = new Box(2,4);` | `0xABC1` | `0xDEF2` | Live | Live | No |
| After `b1 = b2;` | `0xDEF2` | `0xDEF2` | **Orphan** | Live | **Yes (Object #1)** |

### 3.3 Mathematical Abstraction — Reference Counting Model

The **JVM conceptually maintains** a reference count for every object. The object is eligible for garbage collection when:

$$ \text{ReferenceCount}(obj) = 0 $$

> [!NOTE]
> Modern JVMs use sophisticated **tracing garbage collectors** (G1, ZGC, Parallel GC) rather than simple reference counting, but the conceptual model $RC = 0$ remains valid for KTU exam answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Memory Architecture — Stack vs Heap

The following diagram illustrates how reference variables (Stack) and object instances (Heap) interact during object declaration and instantiation.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph STK["STACK MEMORY"]
        ref1["Reference Variable: b1"]
        ref2["Reference Variable: b2"]
        ref3["Reference Variable: b3"]
    end

    subgraph HEAP["HEAP MEMORY"]
        obj1["Object Instance 1\nlength = 5\nwidth = 3"]
        obj2["Object Instance 2\nlength = 2\nwidth = 4"]
        obj3["Object Instance 3\nlength = 7\nwidth = 6"]
    end

    ref1 -. "0xABC1" .-> obj1
    ref2 -. "0xDEF2" .-> obj2
    ref3 -. "0x7890" .-> obj3

    classDef stackStyle fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef heapStyle fill:#B4E5FF,stroke:#0066CC,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class STK stackStyle
    class HEAP heapStyle
```

### 4.2 Object Declaration — Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Programmer writes code:\nBox b1;"] --> B{"Is this a reference\nvariable declaration?"}
    B -- "YES" --> C["Allocate space on STACK\nfor reference variable b1"]
    C --> D["Initialize reference\nto default value: null"]
    D --> E["Object does NOT exist\non Heap yet"]
    E --> F["Programmer writes:\nb1 = new Box(5,3);"]
    F --> G{"Is new keyword\npresent?"}
    G -- "YES" --> H["Allocate space on HEAP\nfor object instance"]
    H --> I["Initialize all instance\nvariables to default values"]
    I --> J["Invoke matching constructor\nwith arguments 5 and 3"]
    J --> K["Constructor executes:\nlength=5, width=3"]
    K --> L["Return memory address\nof new object"]
    L --> M["Store address in b1"]
    M --> N["Object is now LIVE\nand accessible via b1"]
```

### 4.3 Aliasing Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Reference b1"] -. "0xDEF2" .-> C["Object Instance\nlength=2, width=4"]
    B["Reference b2"] -. "0xDEF2" .-> C

    classDef refStyle fill:#E5FFE5,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef objStyle fill:#FFD4D4,stroke:#CC0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class A,B refStyle
    class C objStyle
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** After the statement `b1 = b2;`, both references point to the **same** Heap object. Any modification through `b1.length = 99;` will be visible when reading `b2.length` because they share the same memory.

### 4.4 Garbage Collection Trigger Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Object A created:\nnew Box(5,3)"] --> S2["Reference b1\npoints to Object A"]
    S2 --> S3["b1 = b2;\nb1 now points elsewhere"]
    S3 --> S4{"Reference Count\nof Object A == 0?"}
    S4 -- "YES" --> S5["Object A is eligible\nfor Garbage Collection"]
    S5 --> S6["GC thread runs\nat unpredictable time"]
    S6 --> S7["Heap memory of\nObject A is reclaimed"]
    S7 --> S8["Memory returned\nto free pool"]

    classDef gcStyle fill:#FFE5E5,stroke:#CC0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class S5,S6,S7 gcStyle
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

*What is the difference between declaring an object and instantiating an object in Java? Provide a one-line code example for each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Declaring an Object** is the act of creating a reference variable that can hold the address of an object of a given class type. It does **not** allocate memory for the actual object. The reference variable is stored on the Stack and initialized to `null` by default.

**Instantiating an Object** is the act of physically creating the object in memory using the `new` keyword, which allocates space on the Heap and invokes the constructor.

**Code Examples:**
```java
Box b;              // Declaration (no object created yet)
b = new Box();      // Instantiation (object now exists in memory)
```

> [!TIP]
> **Mark Distribution:** Definition of declaration (1 mark), definition of instantiation (1 mark), code example (1 mark).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Supplementary]**

*Explain the role of the `new` keyword in Java. What happens if you forget to use it?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The `new` keyword performs **three** critical tasks: (1) it allocates memory on the Heap for the new object, (2) it initializes instance variables to their default values, and (3) it invokes the appropriate constructor of the class. The expression `new ClassName()` returns a reference (memory address) to the newly created object.

If the programmer forgets to use `new`, the reference variable remains `null`. Attempting to access any member (field or method) through this `null` reference will throw a **`NullPointerException`** at runtime, causing the program to terminate abnormally.

```java
Student s1;          // s1 is null
s1.getName();        // NullPointerException at runtime!
```

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, **Part B questions carry 14 marks each** with internal choice between two full questions. Each question typically has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024, S3 B.Tech CSE]**

**(a)** Explain the complete process of object creation in Java with a neat diagram showing Stack and Heap memory. Distinguish between primitive variables and reference variables. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program to demonstrate that two reference variables can point to the same object. Explain the concept of **aliasing** with this example. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Object Creation Process — Step by Step:**

**Step 1 — Declaration:** When we write `Box b1;`, the compiler reserves space on the **Stack** for a reference variable `b1`. Since no object has been created, `b1` holds the default value `null`.

**Step 2 — Instantiation:** When we write `b1 = new Box();`, three sub-steps occur:
- The JVM allocates contiguous memory on the **Heap** large enough to hold all instance variables of the `Box` class.
- All instance variables are initialized to their default values (`int → 0`, `boolean → false`, reference → `null`).
- The constructor is invoked to perform custom initialization, and the `new` expression returns the memory address of the object.

**Step 3 — Linking:** The address returned by `new` is stored in `b1`, establishing a link from the Stack to the Heap.

**Distinction Between Primitive and Reference Variables:**

| Feature | Primitive Variable | Reference Variable |
|---|---|---|
| Stores | Actual value (e.g., `int x = 5;` stores `5` directly) | Memory address of an object |
| Memory Region | Stack | Stack (the reference itself) |
| Default Value | Type-dependent (`0`, `0.0`, `false`, etc.) | `null` |
| Size | Fixed per type (e.g., `int` = 4 bytes) | Typically 4 or 8 bytes (address size) |

**Memory Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph STK["STACK"]
        P["int x = 5"]
        R["Box b1"]
    end
    subgraph HP["HEAP"]
        OBJ["Box Object\nlength = 10\nwidth = 20"]
    end
    P -. "Stores value 5 directly" .-> P
    R -. "0xA100" .-> OBJ
```

**(7 Marks Awarded: 3 marks for steps + 2 marks for table + 2 marks for diagram)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Java Program Demonstrating Aliasing:**

```java
class Student {
    String name;
    int rollNo;

    Student(String n, int r) {
        name = n;
        rollNo = r;
    }

    void display() {
        System.out.println("Name: " + name + ", Roll No: " + rollNo);
    }
}

public class AliasDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Student s1 = new Student("Arjun", 101);
        Student s2;

        s2 = s1;  // Aliasing: s2 now points to the SAME object as s1

        System.out.println("Before modification:");
        s1.display();  // Name: Arjun, Roll No: 101
        s2.display();  // Name: Arjun, Roll No: 101

        s1.rollNo = 202;  // Modify through s1

        System.out.println("After modifying s1.rollNo:");
        s1.display();  // Name: Arjun, Roll No: 202
        s2.display();  // Name: Arjun, Roll No: 202  ← Change reflected!
    }
}
```

**Explanation of Aliasing:**

When `s2 = s1;` is executed, the **memory address stored in `s1`** is copied into `s2`. Both `s1` and `s2` now point to the **same single object** on the Heap. This phenomenon is called **aliasing** — two different names referring to the same entity.

When we modify `s1.rollNo = 202;`, the change is visible through `s2.display()` because `s2` accesses the exact same memory location. This demonstrates that the variables `s1` and `s2` are not independent copies — they are two references to one object.

**(7 Marks Awarded: 3 marks for program + 2 marks for output explanation + 2 marks for aliasing concept)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, S3 B.Tech CSE - Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** What is the role of constructors in object instantiation? Differentiate between **default** and **parameterized** constructors with suitable code examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following class:
```java
class Employee {
    int id;
    String name;
    double salary;
}
```
Trace the memory state (Stack and Heap) after executing each statement below. Also identify when an object becomes eligible for garbage collection. **(7 Marks)**

```java
Employee e1 = new Employee(101, "Rahul", 50000.0);
Employee e2 = new Employee(102, "Priya", 60000.0);
Employee e3 = e1;
e1 = null;
e3 = e2;
```

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Role of Constructors:**

A **constructor** in Java is a special method that is automatically invoked when an object is created using the `new` keyword. Its primary roles are:
1. To initialize instance variables to meaningful initial values.
2. To perform any setup logic required when an object comes into existence (e.g., opening a file, allocating resources).
3. Constructors have the **same name as the class** and **do not have a return type** (not even `void`).

**Difference Between Default and Parameterized Constructors:**

| Feature | Default Constructor | Parameterized Constructor |
|---|---|---|
| Parameters | Accepts **no arguments** | Accepts **one or more arguments** |
| Purpose | Initializes object with default/sensible values | Initializes object with user-supplied values |
| Compiler Behavior | Provided **automatically by compiler** if no constructor is defined | Must be **explicitly written** by the programmer |
| Invocation | `new ClassName()` | `new ClassName(arg1, arg2, ...)` |

**Code Examples:**

```java
class Car {
    String model;
    int year;

    // Default Constructor
    Car() {
        model = "Unknown";
        year = 2024;
    }

    // Parameterized Constructor
    Car(String m, int y) {
        model = m;
        year = y;
    }
}

// Usage
Car c1 = new Car();              // Default constructor invoked
Car c2 = new Car("Honda City", 2023); // Parameterized constructor invoked
```

**(7 Marks Awarded: 2 marks for constructor role + 2 marks for table + 3 marks for code)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Memory Trace (Let addresses be illustrative):**

| After Statement | `e1` (Stack) | `e2` (Stack) | `e3` (Stack) | Heap State | GC Eligible? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `Employee e1 = new Employee(101, "Rahul", 50000.0);` | `0xA100` | — | — | Object A (Rahul, 50000) live | No |
| `Employee e2 = new Employee(102, "Priya", 60000.0);` | `0xA100` | `0xB200` | — | Object A & B both live | No |
| `Employee e3 = e1;` | `0xA100` | `0xB200` | `0xA100` | Object A & B both live | No |
| `e1 = null;` | `null` | `0xB200` | `0xA100` | Object A & B both still live (referenced by e3) | No |
| `e3 = e2;` | `null` | `0xB200` | `0xB200` | **Object A (Rahul)** has no live references | **YES — Object A** |

**Garbage Collection Conclusion:**

After the final statement `e3 = e2;`, the object originally containing **Rahul's data (101, 50000.0)** has **zero live references**. Therefore, this object becomes **eligible for garbage collection**. The JVM's Garbage Collector will reclaim its Heap memory at some indeterminate future time. The programmer cannot force GC execution directly but can **request** it via `System.gc();`.

**Visual Memory Diagram After All Statements:**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph STK["STACK"]
        E1["e1 = null"]
        E2["e2 = 0xB200"]
        E3["e3 = 0xB200"]
    end
    subgraph HP["HEAP"]
        OBJ_A["Object A: Rahul, 50000\n(GARBAGE - eligible for GC)"]
        OBJ_B["Object B: Priya, 60000\n(LIVE)"]
    end
    E2 -. "0xB200" .-> OBJ_B
    E3 -. "0xB200" .-> OBJ_B

    classDef gcStyle fill:#FFB6B6,stroke:#CC0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000,stroke-dasharray: 5 5
    classDef liveStyle fill:#B6FFB6,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class OBJ_A gcStyle
    class OBJ_B liveStyle
```

**(7 Marks Awarded: 4 marks for stepwise trace table + 2 marks for GC conclusion + 1 mark for diagram)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Losers:**
> - **Mistake 1:** Writing `Box b1 = new Box();` and claiming the object is on the **Stack**. ❌ The reference is on the Stack, the **object is on the Heap**. (Lose 2 marks)
> - **Mistake 2:** Confusing `class` (blueprint) with `object` (instance). ❌ A class is not an object. (Lose 1 mark)
> - **Mistake 3:** Saying that `new` allocates memory on the Stack. ❌ The `new` keyword **always** allocates on the **Heap**. (Lose 2 marks)
> - **Mistake 4:** Writing the default value of `char` as `'0'` instead of `'\u0000'`. (Lose 0.5 marks but shows depth)
> - **Mistake 5:** Forgetting that `null` is the default value of **all** reference types (arrays, objects, Strings, interfaces). (Lose 1 mark)
> - **Mistake 6:** Confusing **primitive** default values with **reference** default values in a comparison question. (Lose 2 marks)
> - **Mistake 7:** Failing to mention that the `new` operator **returns** a reference that must be assigned or used immediately. (Lose 1 mark)

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Read this 30 minutes before the exam:**

- ✅ **Declaration ≠ Instantiation.** Declaration creates a reference on the **Stack**; `new` creates an object on the **Heap**.
- ✅ The syntax `ClassName obj = new ClassName();` does **all three phases** in one line (declare, instantiate, assign).
- ✅ A **reference variable** holds the **memory address** of an object — it does **not** hold the object itself.
- ✅ The default value of any **reference type** (class, array, interface) is `null`.
- ✅ The `new` keyword performs: **(i)** Heap allocation, **(ii)** default initialization, **(iii)** constructor invocation, **(iv)** returns reference.
- ✅ **Aliasing** occurs when two reference variables point to the same object (`b2 = b1;`).
- ✅ An object becomes eligible for **Garbage Collection** when its **reference count drops to zero** — i.e., no live reference points to it.
- ✅ Java does **not** support direct pointer arithmetic like C/C++ — references are **type-safe** and **opaque**.
- ✅ The `this` keyword refers to the **current object instance** and is used to disambiguate between instance variables and parameters.
- ✅ The dot operator (`.`) is used to access members: `reference.field` or `reference.method()`.
- ✅ When a reference variable is declared but not yet assigned an object, using it triggers **`NullPointerException`** at runtime.
- ✅ Local reference variables live on the **Stack** within the method's frame; they are destroyed when the method returns.
- ✅ Instance variables (fields) live **inside the object** on the Heap and live as long as the object is reachable.
- ✅ The JVM does **not** guarantee **when** garbage collection will occur — only that an unreachable object **will eventually** be collected.
- ✅ You can **suggest** GC via `System.gc();` or `Runtime.getRuntime().gc();`, but it is a **request**, not a command.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Object Reference
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT633 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Object Reference

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Object Oriented Programming (with **Java** as the KTU-prescribed language for PECIT633), an **Object Reference** is a *named handle* (a variable) that stores the **memory address** of an object allocated on the **heap**, rather than the object itself. A reference variable does not hold the object's data — it holds a *pointer-like link* (abstracted away from raw memory addresses by the JVM) that points to the actual object residing in heap memory.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Concept)**
> *"An object reference is a variable that denotes, or refers to, an object. Multiple references can point to the same object. The type of the reference determines how the object can be used at compile time."*

The reference variable occupies space on the **stack memory**, while the object it points to resides in the **heap memory**. This **stack–heap separation** is a fundamental concept tested in KTU examinations.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🧠

Imagine a **remote control** for a television set.

- The **TV** = the actual *object* (with its data: channel, volume, brand).
- The **remote control** = the *object reference* (it is *not* the TV, but it *knows* which TV to operate).
- You can have **multiple remotes** (multiple references) controlling the **same TV** (same object).
- If you give your remote to a friend (passing a reference to a method), your friend can change the channel on **your** TV — because the remote still points to the same television.
- If you throw the remote in the river (set reference = `null`), you have lost access to the TV, but the TV itself still exists in the house until someone unplugs it (Garbage Collector).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule for KTU:** A reference is **NOT** the object. It is merely a *typed pointer* to where the object lives. This distinction is the source of 70% of the trick questions in Module 1 exams.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Reference Variable** | A variable of class type that holds the address of an object |
| **Stack Memory** | Where reference variables are stored (per-method, LIFO structure) |
| **Heap Memory** | Where actual objects are allocated (shared, garbage-collected) |
| **Null Reference** | A reference variable that does not point to any object |
| **Dereferencing** | Using the dot (`.`) operator to access members via the reference |
| **Aliasing** | When two or more references point to the same object |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The default value of any uninitialized reference variable in Java is **`null`**, not `0` or `undefined`. Accessing a `null` reference throws **`NullPointerException`**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Mechanics of an Object Reference

The lifecycle of a reference follows a precise four-stage protocol:

### Stage 1 — Declaration
The compiler is informed that a variable of a certain class type will exist. **No object is created at this stage**; only a slot on the stack is reserved.

```java
Student s1;   // s1 is declared but not yet pointing to any object
```

### Stage 2 — Instantiation (Object Creation)
The `new` keyword allocates memory on the **heap**, invokes the constructor, and returns the **memory address** of the newly constructed object.

```java
s1 = new Student();   // Object created on heap; s1 now points to it
```

### Stage 3 — Dereferencing (Member Access)
The dot operator (`.`) is used through the reference to read or modify the object's attributes and invoke its methods.

```java
s1.name = "Kiran";
s1.display();
```

### Stage 4 — De-reference (Loss of Reference)
When all references to an object go out of scope or are explicitly set to `null`, the object becomes **eligible for Garbage Collection (GC)**. The JVM's GC daemon reclaims the heap memory asynchronously.

```java
s1 = null;   // Object on heap is now eligible for GC
```

---

## 2.2 Critical Reference Behavior Models

### 🔹 Model A: Primitive vs. Reference Variables

This is the **single most tested distinction** in KTU Module 1. The comparison is shown in the formula sheet below.

### 🔹 Model B: Reference Assignment (Aliasing)
When one reference is assigned to another, **both point to the same object**. Modifying the object through one reference is visible through the other. This is called **aliasing**.

```java
Student s1 = new Student();
Student s2 = s1;       // s2 and s1 point to the SAME object
s2.name = "Anand";
System.out.println(s1.name);  // Prints "Anand" — because it's the same object!
```

### 🔹 Model C: Passing References to Methods
Java is **strictly pass-by-value**. However, when the value being passed is a *reference*, the method receives a **copy of the reference**. Therefore, the method can mutate the original object but **cannot** make the caller's reference point to a new object.

### 🔹 Model D: Returning References
A method can return a reference. The returned reference can be stored, chained, or passed to another method.

### 🔹 Model E: The `this` Reference
Inside any instance method or constructor, the keyword **`this`** is an implicit reference to the **current object** on which the method was invoked. It is used to:
- Disambiguate between instance variables and parameters with the same name.
- Pass the current object as an argument.
- Invoke one constructor from another (constructor chaining).

### 🔹 Model F: The `final` Reference Variable
A reference declared `final` **cannot be reassigned** to point to a different object, but the **object it points to CAN still be mutated**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Java Syntax | Behavior Rule | Exam Frequency |
|:-:|---|---|---|:-:|
| 1 | Declaration | `ClassName refVar;` | Only stack slot created | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 2 | Instantiation | `refVar = new ClassName();` | Object on heap, address in ref | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 3 | Default value | — | `null` for all reference types | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 4 | Aliasing | `s2 = s1;` | Both point to same object | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 5 | Pass-by-value | `method(s1);` | Copy of reference is passed | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 6 | `this` keyword | `this.name = name;` | Refers to current object | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 7 | `final` reference | `final Student s = new Student();` | Cannot reassign `s`, but `s.x = 5` is allowed | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 8 | `null` check | `if(s != null) { ... }` | Prevents `NullPointerException` | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 9 | Garbage Collection | `s = null;` or scope ends | Object becomes eligible for GC | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 10 | `instanceof` check | `s instanceof Student` | Safe downcast verification | ⭐⭐⭐ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** In real-world software engineering (Android app development, Spring Boot services, database ORMs like Hibernate), understanding object references is critical to prevent **memory leaks**, **concurrent modification bugs**, and **NullPointerExceptions** — three of the top 5 causes of production crashes in Java applications.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivative Analysis 1: Memory Model Derivation for Reference Assignment

We will trace, step-by-step, exactly what happens in memory when the following code segment executes:

```java
1.  Student s1 = new Student();
2.  Student s2 = new Student();
3.  s2 = s1;
4.  s1.name = "KTU";
5.  System.out.println(s2.name);
```

### Step-by-Step Memory Trace

**Step 1 →** `new Student()` is invoked. The JVM allocates a block of heap memory (say address **0xAB12**), initializes the object's fields, and stores the address in `s1`.

**Memory state after Line 1:**

| Stack (s1) | Heap Address 0xAB12 |
|---|---|
| `0xAB12` | `name = null`, `rollNo = 0` |

**Step 2 →** A *second* independent object is created at, say, address **0xCD34**, and its address is stored in `s2`.

**Memory state after Line 2:**

| Stack (s1) | Stack (s2) | Heap 0xAB12 | Heap 0xCD34 |
|---|---|---|---|
| `0xAB12` | `0xCD34` | `name = null` | `name = null` |

**Step 3 →** `s2 = s1;` is executed. The address stored in `s1` is **copied** into `s2`. The object at **0xCD34** now has **zero references** and is immediately eligible for **Garbage Collection**.

**Memory state after Line 3:**

| Stack (s1) | Stack (s2) | Heap 0xAB12 | Heap 0xCD34 |
|---|---|---|---|
| `0xAB12` | `0xAB12` | `name = null` | *(eligible for GC)* |

**Step 4 →** `s1.name = "KTU";` modifies the object at address **0xAB12** through reference `s1`. Since `s2` also points to **0xAB12**, this change is observable through `s2`.

**Step 5 →** `s2.name` evaluates to `"KTU"`.

**Output:**

$$
\texttt{KTU}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion of the Derivation:** Reference assignment does **not** copy the object — it copies the *address*. The original object at the now-orphaned address becomes immediately eligible for Garbage Collection, even if no explicit `s2 = null` is executed.

---

## 3.2 Derivative Analysis 2: Pass-by-Value Proof for Reference Arguments

Consider the following classic KTU question:

```java
class Box {
    int size = 10;
}

class Demo {
    static void modify(Box b, int n) {
        b.size = 99;        // Line A
        b = new Box();      // Line B
        b.size = 50;        // Line C
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Box myBox = new Box();
        int num = 5;
        modify(myBox, num);
        System.out.println(myBox.size + " " + num);
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Logical Deduction

**Step 1 →** Before `main` calls `modify`:
- `myBox` (in main's stack) → address **0x100**, where `size = 10`
- `num` (in main's stack) → `5`

**Step 2 →** `modify(myBox, num)` is invoked. Java creates a **new stack frame** for `modify`. The values of `myBox` (the address `0x100`) and `num` (`5`) are **copied** into new local variables `b` and `n` respectively.

| Frame | Variable | Value |
|---|---|---|
| main | myBox | 0x100 |
| main | num | 5 |
| modify | b | 0x100 |
| modify | n | 5 |

**Step 3 →** Line A executes: `b.size = 99;`. Since `b` points to address `0x100`, the object at `0x100` is mutated to `size = 99`. Because `myBox` also points to `0x100`, this change **is visible** to the caller.

**Step 4 →** Line B executes: `b = new Box();`. A new object is allocated at address **0x200`. The local variable `b` is now updated to point to `0x200`. **`myBox` is UNCHANGED** — it still points to `0x100`.

| Frame | Variable | Value |
|---|---|---|
| main | myBox | 0x100 |
| modify | b | 0x200 |

**Step 5 →** Line C executes: `b.size = 50;`. Only the object at `0x200` is affected. The object at `0x100` remains at `size = 99`.

**Step 6 →** `modify` returns. The output is:

$$
\texttt{99 5}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Takeaway:** The parameter `b` is a *local copy* of the reference. Reassigning `b` does **not** affect the caller's variable. This is the textbook definition of **pass-by-value**, and is the most heavily tested nuance in KTU university exams.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Primitive vs. Reference Comparison

| Property | Primitive Variable (`int x = 5;`) | Reference Variable (`Student s = new Student();`) |
|---|---|---|
| **What is stored?** | The actual value (`5`) | The memory address of the object |
| **Memory location** | Stack | Reference on stack; object on heap |
| **Default value** | `0`, `false`, `'\u0000'` | `null` |
| **Assignment `a = b`** | Copies the value | Copies the address (aliasing) |
| **Passed to method** | Pass-by-value (copy of value) | Pass-by-value (copy of address) |
| **Can be `final`?** | Yes (value frozen) | Yes (reference frozen, not object) |
| **Garbage collected?** | No (on stack) | The *object* is, when no refs remain |
| **Size** | Fixed (e.g., 4 bytes for `int`) | Fixed (typically 4 or 8 bytes for the address) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Memory Model for Object References

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph STACK["STACK MEMORY"]
        s1["s1 : Student"]
        s2["s2 : Student"]
    end

    subgraph HEAP["HEAP MEMORY"]
        objA["Student Object A | name = KTU | rollNo = 1"]
        objB["Student Object B | name = null | rollNo = 0"]
        objC["Student Object C | name = Anand | rollNo = 7"]
    end

    s1 --> objA
    s2 --> objC

    objB -.->|ELIGIBLE FOR GC| GC(("Garbage Collector"))

    classDef stackStyle fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef heapStyle fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef gcStyle fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class s1,s2 stackStyle
    class objA,objB,objC heapStyle
    class GC gcStyle
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Reference Lifecycle Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A(["Stage 1: DECLARATION | ClassName refVar;"]) --> B(["Stage 2: INSTANTIATION | refVar = new ClassName();"])
    B --> C(["Stage 3: DEREFERENCING | refVar.member = value;"])
    C --> D{"Is the reference still in scope?"}
    D -->|Yes| E(["Object remains on heap"])
    D -->|No, or refVar = null| F(["Object becomes ELIGIBLE for Garbage Collection"])
    F --> G(["JVM GC Daemon reclaims memory asynchronously"])
    E --> H(["Program continues using the object"])
    H --> D

    classDef stageStyle fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#6A5ACD,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decisionStyle fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#DAA520,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef gcStyle fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class A,B,C,E,H stageStyle
    class D decisionStyle
    class F,G gcStyle
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Pass-by-Value with Reference Argument (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant M as main() Stack Frame
    participant Mod as modify() Stack Frame
    participant H as Heap Memory

    M->>M: myBox (ref) = 0x100
    M->>H: Object at 0x100 created: size = 10
    M->>Mod: Pass copy of ref (0x100) as argument
    Mod->>Mod: b (local copy) = 0x100
    Mod->>H: Line A: b.size = 99
    Note over H: Object at 0x100 mutated to size = 99
    Mod->>H: Line B: b = new Box() -> 0x200
    Note over Mod: Local b now points to 0x200
    Note over M: myBox UNCHANGED, still 0x100
    Mod->>H: Line C: b.size = 50
    Mod-->>M: Return; b discarded
    M->>M: Print myBox.size (= 99)
```

---

# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]
**Differentiate between a primitive variable and a reference variable in Java. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Primitive Variable | Reference Variable |
|---|---|---|
| Storage | Stores the **actual value** directly | Stores the **memory address** of an object |
| Memory | Allocated on **stack** | Reference on stack; object on **heap** |
| Default value | Type-specific (`0`, `false`) | `null` |
| Example | `int age = 20;` | `Student s = new Student();` |

A primitive variable holds data directly, while a reference variable is a handle that points to an object on the heap. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for example, 1 mark for memory distinction.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**What is the significance of the `this` keyword in Java? Explain any two uses.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The `this` keyword is an **implicit reference** to the **current object** — i.e., the object on which the instance method or constructor was invoked.

**Use 1 — Disambiguation:** When a parameter has the same name as an instance variable, `this.variable` refers to the instance variable, while `variable` refers to the parameter. *(1.5 marks)*

**Use 2 — Constructor chaining:** `this(args)` can be used inside a constructor to invoke another constructor in the same class, enabling code reuse. *(1.5 marks)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

### 📝 Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Model]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **object reference** in Java. With a neat diagram, describe how objects are stored in memory when references are used. Discuss the difference between **pass-by-value** for primitives and references. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program to demonstrate **aliasing** and **passing references to methods**. Show the output and explain the memory state at each step. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Object Reference (Definition – 2 marks):**
An object reference in Java is a typed variable that holds the **memory address** of an object allocated on the heap. It is created using the `new` keyword and dereferenced using the dot (`.`) operator. A reference does not contain the object itself; it merely *refers* to it.

**Memory Diagram (3 marks):**

```
STACK                              HEAP
+--------+      +---------------+
|  s1    |----->|  Student obj  |
+--------+      |  name="Arun"  |
|  s2    |---+  |  rollNo=101   |
+--------+   |  +---------------+
             |  +---------------+
             +->|  Student obj  |
                |  name="Meera" |
                |  rollNo=102   |
                +---------------+
```

In this diagram, `s1` and `s2` are two references pointing to the **same** Student object — this is aliasing. The second object on the heap has no reference pointing to it and is eligible for garbage collection.

**Pass-by-Value Distinction (2 marks):**
Java is **strictly pass-by-value**. For primitives, the actual value is copied. For references, the *address* (the reference value) is copied. Hence, a method receiving a reference can mutate the original object but cannot make the caller's reference point elsewhere.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A(b):

```java
class Box {
    int size;
    Box(int s) { size = s; }
}

class ReferenceDemo {
    static void enlarge(Box b) {
        b.size = b.size * 2;     // Mutates the original object
    }

    static void replace(Box b) {
        b = new Box(999);        // Only local copy is changed
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Box b1 = new Box(10);
        Box b2 = b1;             // ALIASING: b2 also points to same object

        System.out.println("Before enlarge: b1.size = " + b1.size);
        enlarge(b1);
        System.out.println("After enlarge : b1.size = " + b1.size);
        System.out.println("After enlarge : b2.size = " + b2.size);

        replace(b1);
        System.out.println("After replace : b1.size = " + b1.size);
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Before enlarge: b1.size = 10
After enlarge : b1.size = 20
After enlarge : b2.size = 20
After replace : b1.size = 20
```

**Explanation (2 marks):**
- After `Box b2 = b1;`, both references point to the same object — this is **aliasing**.
- `enlarge(b1)` mutates the shared object, so `b2.size` also becomes `20`.
- `replace(b1)` only reassigns the local copy of the reference inside `replace()`; the caller's `b1` still points to the original object (size 20). This proves Java is pass-by-value even for reference types.

---

### 📝 Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**

**(a)** What is an **object reference**? Explain the roles of the **stack** and **heap** memory in object creation. What is **garbage collection** and when does an object become eligible for it? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the use of the **`this`** keyword and the **`final`** keyword with reference variables in Java. Write a short program to illustrate that a `final` reference cannot be reassigned but the object it refers to can be modified. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Object Reference (1.5 marks):**
An object reference is a variable that holds the memory address of an object. It is a typed pointer, abstracted by the JVM, that allows programs to access and manipulate objects on the heap.

**Stack vs. Heap (3.5 marks):**
- **Stack Memory:** Stores method-call frames, local variables (including primitive values and references), and is managed in LIFO order. Each thread has its own stack.
- **Heap Memory:** Shared across threads, it is where all objects (instances of classes) and instance variables are allocated. The heap is managed by the JVM's Garbage Collector.

When `new Student()` is executed, the object is allocated on the heap, and the address is stored in a reference variable on the stack.

**Garbage Collection (2 marks):**
**Garbage Collection (GC)** is the automatic reclamation of heap memory by the JVM. An object becomes **eligible for GC** when **no live reference** points to it — typically when:
- The reference is set to `null`, OR
- The reference is reassigned to another object, OR
- The reference goes out of scope.

The JVM's GC daemon reclaims this memory **asynchronously**; the exact time of reclamation is non-deterministic.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**`this` keyword (2 marks):**
- `this` is an implicit final reference to the current object.
- Used to disambiguate instance variables from parameters/局部 variables with the same name.
- Used to pass the current object as an argument: `display(this);`
- Used for constructor chaining: `this("default", 0);`

**`final` reference (2 marks):**
A reference declared `final` cannot be reassigned to point to a different object, but the **internal state of the object can still be modified**.

**Program (3 marks):**

```java
class Account {
    String holder;
    double balance;
    Account(String h, double b) { holder = h; balance = b; }
}

class FinalRefDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        final Account a = new Account("Kiran", 1000.0);
        // a = new Account("Other", 500);   // ❌ COMPILE ERROR: cannot reassign final
        
        a.holder = "Anand";                 // ✅ ALLOWED: modifying object
        a.balance = 5000.0;                 // ✅ ALLOWED: modifying object
        
        System.out.println(a.holder + " has Rs." + a.balance);
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Anand has Rs.5000.0
```

This demonstrates that `final` on a reference freezes the *binding*, not the *object's contents*.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks (Module 1):**
> 1. **Confusing "reference = object"** — A reference is *not* the object. Students often lose 1–2 marks for writing "a reference *is* the object."
> 2. **Believing Java is pass-by-reference** — Java is **ALWAYS pass-by-value**. For reference types, the value passed is a *copy of the address*. Conflating this with C++-style pass-by-reference is a guaranteed 2-mark penalty.
> 3. **Forgetting that `null` is the default value** — Writing "default value of a reference is `0`" is incorrect.
> 4. **Thinking `final` makes the object immutable** — A `final` reference cannot be reassigned, but its object is still mutable. This nuance is asked every semester.
> 5. **Confusing `this()` (constructor call) with `this` (reference)** — These are two distinct usages, each worth separate marks.
> 6. **Missing the garbage collection eligibility** — After a reference is reassigned, the orphaned object becomes eligible *immediately*; the GC may not run *immediately*, but eligibility is the key term.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> ✅ **Rapid Revision Checklist — Object Reference**
>
> - ☐ A **reference** is a typed handle pointing to an object on the **heap**; it is stored on the **stack**.
> - ☐ References are created implicitly when an object is instantiated using `new`.
> - ☐ The default value of any reference variable is **`null`**.
> - ☐ **Aliasing** occurs when multiple references point to the same object (`s2 = s1`).
> - ☐ Java is **pass-by-value** — for references, the *address* is copied, not the object.
> - ☐ Methods receiving a reference can **mutate the original object** but **cannot reassign the caller's reference**.
> - ☐ The **`this`** keyword refers to the **current invoking object**.
> - ☐ A **`final` reference** cannot be reassigned, but the object it points to **can be modified**.
> - ☐ An object becomes **eligible for Garbage Collection** when no live reference points to it.
> - ☐ `null` reference access throws **`NullPointerException`** at runtime.
> - ☐ Use `instanceof` before downcasting to avoid `ClassCastException`.
> - ☐ **Memory rule of thumb:** Primitives live on stack, Objects live on heap, References live on stack (pointing to heap).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT633 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)** under the KTU 2024 syllabus, a **method** is a named, reusable block of code contained within a class that performs a specific, well-defined task. A method encapsulates a sequence of statements that can be invoked (called) any number of times by referencing its name, optionally passing arguments, and optionally returning a value.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A method is a member of a class that defines an operation on objects of that class. It is the object-oriented equivalent of a *function* in procedural languages (C, Pascal). In Java, **every method must belong to a class** — there are no standalone (top-level) functions.

The general syntactic structure of a method in Java is:

```text
accessModifier static/non-static returnType methodName(parameterList) {
    // method body
    // local variable declarations
    // executable statements
    return value;   // required if returnType is not void
}
```

### Components of a Method Declaration

| Component | Mandatory? | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Access Modifier** (`public`, `private`, `protected`, default) | Optional | Defines the visibility/scope of the method across packages |
| **`static` keyword** | Optional | Indicates the method belongs to the class, not to any object instance |
| **Return Type** (`int`, `double`, `void`, `String`, etc.) | **Mandatory** | Specifies the data type of the value the method returns |
| **Method Name** | **Mandatory** | A valid Java identifier following camelCase or PascalCase convention |
| **Parameter List** | Optional (can be empty) | Comma-separated list of formal parameters with their types |
| **Method Body** | **Mandatory** | The block of statements enclosed in `{}` that defines the behavior |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — A Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: A Coffee Vending Machine**
> Imagine a coffee vending machine sitting in your college canteen.
> - The **machine itself** is the **class** (`VendingMachine`).
> - Each physical machine on campus is an **object** (instance of the class).
> - The **buttons** on the machine (`espressoButton`, `cappuccinoButton`) are the **method names** — they are pre-defined actions the machine knows how to perform.
> - The **coin slot and sugar-level knob** are the **parameters** — inputs the user supplies before the action executes.
> - The **coffee cup that drops into the tray** is the **return value** — the output of the action.
> - The **display label "Out of Order"** is a `void` method — it performs an action (showing a message) but hands back no cup.
>
> Just as you press the same espresso button hundreds of times and get the same result, you can *call* a method millions of times and the same block of code executes each time — without rewriting it.

## 1.3 Why Methods Matter — The Three Pillars

1. **Code Reusability (DRY Principle — Don't Repeat Yourself):** A method is written once and invoked many times, eliminating duplication.
2. **Modularity:** A large program is decomposed into smaller, named, manageable units. Each method solves *one* sub-problem.
3. **Abstraction:** The caller does not need to know *how* the method works internally; only *what* it accepts and *what* it returns. This is the **method contract** (signature + return type).

## 1.4 Method Signature — The KTU Board-Exam Focus

The **method signature** in Java consists of **only two** things:
1. The method name
2. The parameter list (number, type, and order of parameters)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU Note:** The **return type is NOT part of the method signature**. Two methods in the same class can have the same name and same parameter list *only if they differ in return type?* **NO** — Java will throw a *compile-time error*. The compiler identifies a method **uniquely** by name + parameter types, which is the foundation of **method overloading** (covered in Module 1).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Method — Step-by-Step Logic

A method declaration and execution follows a strict **call–return** lifecycle. Let us break it down into six structured steps:

- **Step 1 — Declaration:** The method is declared inside a class with a defined access modifier, return type, name, and parameter list.
- **Step 2 — Method Call (Invocation):** When the method is called, the JVM pauses the current execution, creates a new **stack frame** for the method, and pushes it onto the **call stack**.
- **Step 3 — Parameter Binding (Argument Passing):** Actual arguments (values or variables) supplied at the call site are copied into the formal parameters of the method. **Java ALWAYS uses Pass-by-Value** for primitive types. For object references, the *reference value* (memory address) is copied — this is colloquially called *pass-by-reference-of-the-reference*, but the technical term is still **pass-by-value**.
- **Step 4 — Body Execution:** The statements inside the method body execute sequentially from top to bottom.
- **Step 5 — Return Statement:** When a `return` statement (or the closing brace of a `void` method) is reached, the method evaluates the return expression, copies the value back to the caller's stack, and the method's stack frame is **popped** from the call stack.
- **Step 6 — Resumption:** The caller continues execution from the line immediately after the method call, optionally using the returned value.

## 2.2 The `return` Statement — The Three Laws

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Rules of `return` in Java (Frequently Asked in KTU Boards):**
> 1. A method declared with a non-`void` return type **MUST** return a value of a compatible type (or one that is implicitly convertible).
> 2. A method declared `void` may optionally use `return;` (with no value) to exit early, or it simply exits at the closing brace.
> 3. **Every code path** in a non-`void` method must reach a `return` statement — otherwise the compiler raises error *"missing return statement"*.

## 2.3 Formal vs. Actual Parameters

| Term | Also Known As | Defined At | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Formal Parameters** | Parameters, Parameter Variables | Method declaration (signature) | `int a, int b` in `int add(int a, int b)` |
| **Actual Parameters** | Arguments | Method invocation (call site) | `10, 20` in `add(10, 20)` |

## 2.4 Categories of Methods in Java

| Category | Declared With | Called Via | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Instance Method** | (no `static` keyword) | `objectName.methodName()` | Operates on the state (fields) of a specific object |
| **Static Method** | `static` keyword | `ClassName.methodName()` | Utility/helper functions; belongs to the class as a whole |
| **Accessor (Getter)** | `public returnType getX()` | `obj.getX()` | Returns the value of a private field |
| **Mutator (Setter)** | `public void setX(type x)` | `obj.setX(value)` | Modifies the value of a private field |
| **Constructor** | Name = class name, no return type | `new ClassName(args)` | Initializes a newly created object |
| **Recursive Method** | Calls itself | Same as instance/static | Solves problems by self-reference (e.g., factorial, Fibonacci) |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formal Rule / Equation | KTU Board Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Stack Frame Size** | $S_{frame} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{size}(param_i) + \text{size}(return\_addr) + \text{size}(local\_vars)$ | Conceptual only — explains memory model |
| 2 | **Pass-by-Value (Primitive)** | $formal\_param := actual\_arg$ (bit-wise copy) | High — frequently asked definition |
| 3 | **Pass-by-Value (Object Reference)** | $formal\_ref := actual\_ref$ (address copy) | High — most-confused topic in KTU papers |
| 4 | **Recurrence Relation (Factorial)** | $n! = n \times (n-1)!,\quad 0! = 1$ | Direct 7-mark question pattern |
| 5 | **Recurrence Relation (Fibonacci)** | $F(n) = F(n-1) + F(n-2),\quad F(0)=0,\ F(1)=1$ | Direct 7-mark question pattern |
| 6 | **Number of Stack Frames in Recursion** | $Frames(n) = n + 1$ (including `main`) | Asked in derivations |
| 7 | **Time Complexity of Recursive Factorial** | $T(n) = T(n-1) + O(1) \Rightarrow O(n)$ | Derivations |
| 8 | **Time Complexity of Recursive Fibonacci** | $T(n) = T(n-1) + T(n-2) \Rightarrow O(2^n)$ | Derivations |
| 9 | **Method Signature** | $Sig = \{name,\ (type_1, type_2, \ldots, type_k)\}$ | Definition questions |
| 10 | **Void Method Return Rule** | $\text{returnValue} = \emptyset$ | Frequently confused with `null` |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Embedded Systems:** Methods model hardware-level operations (e.g., `readSensor()`, `transmitPacket()`) where the *method* acts as a software abstraction over an interrupt service routine.
- **Web Backends:** Each REST API endpoint is essentially a method (`@GetMapping("/users") public List<User> getUsers() {...}`).
- **Scientific Computing:** Numerical methods such as `solveODE(...)`, `integrate(...)`, and `newtonRaphson(...)` are implemented as library methods.
- **Game Development:** Methods model game-object behaviors (`player.jump()`, `enemy.attack()`, `bullet.collideWith(wall)`).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example #1 — Method Call Mechanics

Consider the following complete Java program. We will trace the **call stack** at every line.

```java
public class Calculator {
    public static int add(int a, int b) {
        int sum = a + b;
        return sum;
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int x = 10;
        int y = 20;
        int result = add(x, y);
        System.out.println("Sum = " + result);
    }
}
```

### Stack Frame Trace (Line-by-Line)

| Line Executed | Action | Call Stack (Top → Bottom) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| `int x = 10;` | Push `x=10` to main frame | `main: x=10` | Initialization in main |
| `int y = 20;` | Push `y=20` to main frame | `main: x=10, y=20` | — |
| `add(x, y);` | Push new frame; copy values | `add: a=10, b=20` → `main: x=10, y=20` | **Pass-by-value** copies |
| `int sum = a + b;` | Local var created in add's frame | `add: a=10, b=20, sum=30` → `main: ...` | Computation |
| `return sum;` | Copy 30 back; pop add's frame | `main: x=10, y=20, result=30` | `result` is assigned the returned value |
| `System.out.println(...)` | Print "Sum = 30" | `main: x=10, y=20, result=30` | — |
| End of `main` | Pop main frame | (empty) | Program terminates |

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example #2 — Pass-by-Value vs. Reference

This is the **single most-asked KTU question** on methods. Trace the following program:

```java
public class PassTest {
    static void modifyPrimitive(int n) {
        n = n + 10;
    }

    static void modifyArray(int[] arr) {
        arr[0] = 99;
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int num = 5;
        modifyPrimitive(num);
        System.out.println("After primitive: " + num);

        int[] marks = {50, 60, 70};
        modifyArray(marks);
        System.out.println("After array: " + marks[0]);
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Analysis

**Step 1:** In `main`, `num = 5`. Stack: `main: num=5`.

**Step 2:** `modifyPrimitive(num)` is called. The *value* 5 is **copied** into formal parameter `n`. Stack now has two frames.

$$
n_{formal} \xleftarrow{\text{copy}} num_{actual} = 5
$$

**Step 3:** Inside the method, `n = n + 10` becomes `n = 15`. This changes the value of `n` in the **method's own stack frame only**. The original `num` in main remains `5`.

**Step 4:** Method returns. Output: `After primitive: 5` — proving Java does NOT modify the caller's primitive variable.

**Step 5:** Now `marks = {50, 60, 70}`. The variable `marks` in main holds a *reference* (memory address) pointing to the heap object `{50, 60, 70}`.

**Step 6:** `modifyArray(marks)` is called. The reference value is **copied** into formal parameter `arr`. Both `marks` (in main) and `arr` (in method) point to the **same heap object**.

$$
arr_{formal} \xleftarrow{\text{copy of address}} marks_{actual}
$$

**Step 7:** `arr[0] = 99` mutates the **shared heap object** to `{99, 60, 70}`. Since `marks` and `arr` point to the same object, `marks[0]` is also `99`.

**Step 8:** Output: `After array: 99`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Conclusion:** Java is **strictly pass-by-value, ALWAYS**. For primitives, the *value* is copied. For object references, the *reference (address)* is copied. The recipient can mutate the object's internal state through the copied reference, but **cannot** make the caller's variable point to a new object.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Example #3 — Recursive Method (Factorial)

We derive the **closed-form recurrence** for the factorial function and trace the call stack.

### Recurrence Definition

$$
\text{fact}(n) = 
\begin{cases}
1, & \text{if } n = 0 \text{ or } n = 1 \quad \text{(Base Case)} \\
n \times \text{fact}(n-1), & \text{if } n > 1 \quad \text{(Recursive Case)}
\end{cases}
$$

### Java Implementation

```java
public class FactorialDemo {
    static int fact(int n) {
        if (n == 0 || n == 1)
            return 1;
        return n * fact(n - 1);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int result = fact(4);
        System.out.println("4! = " + result);
    }
}
```

### Call-Stack Expansion (Unwinding)

For `fact(4)`, the **expansion phase** pushes frames onto the stack:

$$
\text{fact}(4) = 4 \times \text{fact}(3)
$$

$$
\text{fact}(3) = 3 \times \text{fact}(2)
$$

$$
\text{fact}(2) = 2 \times \text{fact}(1)
$$

$$
\text{fact}(1) = 1 \quad \text{(base case reached)}
$$

The **substitution phase** then unwinds, popping frames and substituting values back:

$$
\text{fact}(2) = 2 \times 1 = 2
$$

$$
\text{fact}(3) = 3 \times 2 = 6
$$

$$
\text{fact}(4) = 4 \times 6 = 24
$$

### Time-Complexity Derivation

Let $T(n)$ be the time to compute $\text{fact}(n)$. Each call performs one multiplication and one recursive call (excluding the base case), giving:

$$
T(n) = T(n-1) + c, \quad T(1) = c_0
$$

Solving by unrolling:

$$
T(n) = T(n-1) + c = T(n-2) + 2c = \ldots = T(1) + (n-1)c
$$

$$
\therefore T(n) = c_0 + (n-1)c = O(n)
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Method Types

| Property | Instance Method | Static Method | Constructor | Recursive Method |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Has `static`?** | No | Yes | N/A | Either |
| **Called on** | An object (`obj.m()`) | A class (`Cls.m()`) | During `new` | An object or class |
| **Can access `this`?** | Yes | No | Yes | Yes (if instance) |
| **Can access instance fields?** | Yes (directly) | Only via an object | Yes | Yes (if instance) |
| **Can access static fields?** | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Can call itself?** | Yes | Yes | No (technically possible but pointless) | Yes — that's the definition |
| **Returns a value?** | Optional | Optional | Cannot have explicit `return` | Optional |
| **Memory cost per call** | One stack frame | One stack frame | One stack frame (called by JVM) | **N+1 frames** for depth N |

---

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    B -->|public| C[Visible Everywhere]
    B -->|private| D[Visible Within Class Only]

    B -->|default| F[Visible Within Package Only]

    A --> G{Static Keyword}
    G -->|Yes| H[Belongs to Class - Called via ClassName]

    A --> J[Return Type]

    A --> M[Method Name]
    A --> N[Parameter List]

    N -->|Non-Empty| P[Comma Separated Type-Name Pairs]

    A --> Q[Method Body]
    Q --> R[Local Variable Declarations]
    Q --> S[Executable Statements]
    Q --> T[Return Statement if Non-Void]
```
