NRVO applies. **Step 4 — Trace the algebra:** $$ \vec{c} = \begin{pmatrix} a_x + b_x \\ a_y + b_y \\ a_z + b_z \end{pmatrix} $$ **Step 5 — Verify no aliasing:** Since `rhs` is passed as `const Vector3D&` and the result is a brand-new prvalue, no aliasing occurs and the operation is exception-safe. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Memory Flow — Returning an Object by Value (with NRVO) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[main: Number n = makeNumber 5] --> B[Call makeNumber] B --> C[Stack frame: local object Number] C --> D{Return statement} D --> E[NRVO: construct directly in main's n slot] E --> F[makeNumber frame destroyed] F --> G[main continues with n initialized] G --> H[End of main: n destructor invoked] ``` **Caption:** The `local` object is *not* copied into a temporary — modern compilers (post-C++17) construct it directly in `main`'s storage slot for `n`, eliminating the copy entirely. ## 4.2 Function Call Stack — Return by Value (Pre-C++17 Path) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Main as main frame participant Callee as makeNumber frame participant Temp as Hidden Temporary Main->>Callee: invoke makeNumber(5) Callee->>Callee: construct local Number(5) Callee->>Temp: copy/move local into temporary Callee-->>Main: return address Main->>Main: copy/move temporary into n Main->>Temp: destroy temporary Callee->>Callee: destroy local Main->>Main: continue execution ``` ## 4.3 Decision Architecture — Choosing a Return Mechanism ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Function needs to return an object] --> Q1{Is the object
newly created
in the function?} Q1 -- Yes --> V[Return by value T func] Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is the object
a member of
this class?} Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Caller must
modify it?} Q3 -- Yes --> R1[Return by reference T& func] Q3 -- No --> R2[Return const reference const T& func] Q2 -- No --> Q4{Can the object
be missing?} Q4 -- Yes --> P[Return pointer T* func or std optional T] Q4 -- No --> R1 V --> Note1[Safe default, NRVO may elide copy] R1 --> Note2[Caller and callee share same object] R2 --> Note3[Read-only access, no copy] P --> Note4[Indicates optionality via nullptr] ``` ## 4.4 Dangling Reference — What NOT to Do ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Function f returns T&] --> B[Compiler: take address of local x] B --> C[Function returns, x destroyed] C --> D[Caller's reference now points to freed stack] D --> E[Undefined Behaviour at use-site] style E fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > This **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** depicts the lifetime hazard of returning a reference to a local. In KTU exams, even if asked to "show the code", always annotate with a note about why this is dangerous. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 Marks Each) ### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **Differentiate between returning an object by value and returning an object by reference. When would you prefer one over the other?** **Model Answer (3-mark structure):** | Aspect | Return by Value | Return by Reference | |---|---|---| | Syntax | `T func()` | `T& func()` | | Object handed over | A **copy** (or moved prvalue) of the callee's object | An **alias** to an existing object | | Lifetime | Caller fully owns; temporary destroyed after expression | Caller and callee share the same object; object must outlive the call | | Cost | Copy/move cost (possibly elided by RVO) | Negligible — just a pointer | | Risk | Safe even for locals | **Dangling reference** if used on a local | **Preference Rule:** Use *return by value* when the function constructs a new object (e.g., a `+` operator, factory method). Use *return by reference* only when the object exists outside the function (e.g., a member accessor) and the caller needs to read or modify that same object efficiently. --- ### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **What is NRVO? Explain with a small C++ example.** **Model Answer:** NRVO stands for **Named Return Value Optimisation**, a compiler technique that constructs a named local variable *directly* in the memory slot reserved by the caller for the return value, thereby eliminating the copy. ```cpp class Box { /* ... */ }; Box makeBox() { Box b(10); // local 'b' is named return b; // NRVO: 'b' built directly in caller's slot } int main() { Box myBox = makeBox(); // zero copies if NRVO applied } ``` > When NRVO applies, the local `b` and the caller's `myBox` refer to the **same memory location**, and only **one** construction and **one** destruction occur. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) > **KTU ESE Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Each question carries **14 marks** split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. --- ### ⭐ Question A — 14 Marks (Choice 1) **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]** **(a)** Design a `Time` class with `hours`, `minutes`, and `seconds` as integer members. Implement: - A default constructor and a parameterised constructor. - A member function `add(Time t1, Time t2)` that **returns a `Time` object** representing the sum, properly normalising the result (e.g., 70 seconds $\rightarrow$ 1 min 10 sec). - A `display()` function. Explain why you chose the return type for `add()`. **\[7 marks\]** **(b)** Write a program that demonstrates what happens if a function returns a **reference to a local object**. Show the resulting undefined behaviour, and explain two ways to fix it. **\[7 marks\]** --- #### Model Solution for Q.A(a) ```cpp #include using namespace std; class Time { int hrs, mins, secs; void normalise() { if (secs >= 60) { mins += secs / 60; secs %= 60; } if (mins >= 60) { hrs += mins / 60; mins %= 60; } if (hrs >= 24) hrs %= 24; } public: Time(int h = 0, int m = 0, int s = 0) : hrs(h), mins(m), secs(s) { normalise(); } Time add(Time t1, Time t2) { Time sum; sum.secs = t1.secs + t2.secs; sum.mins = t1.mins + t2.mins; sum.hrs = t1.hrs + t2.hrs; sum.normalise(); return sum; // RETURN BY VALUE — safe, NRVO-eligible } void display() const { cout << hrs << "h " << mins << "m " << secs << "s\n"; } }; int main() { Time t1(10, 50, 40), t2(2, 20, 35); Time t3 = t1.add(t1, t2); t3.display(); // 13h 11m 15s } ``` **Justification of Return Type (worth 2 marks in the 7):** The function `add` constructs a **brand-new** `Time` object `sum` that has no prior existence in the caller. Therefore the safe and idiomatic choice is **return by value**. The compiler will apply NRVO, eliminating the copy. Returning a reference here would be a *dangling reference* since `sum` is destroyed at the closing brace of `add`. **Normalisation logic — full derivation:** $$ secs_{total} = t1.secs + t2.secs = 40 + 35 = 75 $$ $$ mins_{carry} = \left\lfloor \frac{75}{60} \right\rfloor = 1, \quad secs_{final} = 75 \bmod 60 = 15 $$ $$ mins_{total} = t1.mins + t2.mins + mins_{carry} = 50 + 20 + 1 = 71 $$ $$ hrs_{carry} = \left\lfloor \frac{71}{60} \right\rfloor = 1, \quad mins_{final} = 71 \bmod 60 = 11 $$ $$ hrs_{final} = t1.hrs + t2.hrs + hrs_{carry} = 10 + 2 + 1 = 13 $$ **Final output:** `13h 11m 15s` ✔ --- #### Model Solution for Q.A(b) ```cpp #include using namespace std; class Data { int x; public: Data(int v) : x(v) {} int getX() const { return x; } }; // DANGEROUS: returns reference to a local Data& makeBad() { Data d(99); // local — destroyed on return return d; // d is now dangling! } int main() { Data& ref = makeBad(); // ref is dangling cout << ref.getX() << endl; // UNDEFINED BEHAVIOUR } ``` **Two ways to fix:** **Fix 1 — Return by Value** (safest, idiomatic): ```cpp Data makeGood() { Data d(99); return d; // NRVO: no copy, no dangling } ``` **Fix 2 — Return a Reference to a Static / Member / Caller-Owned Object:** ```cpp Data& makeStatic() { static Data d(99); // lives for program duration return d; } ``` > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully:** > 1. **Do NOT** write `return d;` where `d` is a non-static local and the return type is `T&`. You will lose **3 marks instantly** and the rest of the answer will be marked sceptically. > 2. **Do NOT** forget to justify *why* NRVO / RVO applies. Examiners award marks for the **reasoning**, not just the code. > 3. In the `add()` function, **always** show normalisation — a common omission costs 2 marks. > 4. If the question says "design a class", include **constructors, accessors, and at least one mutator** — partial designs are penalised. --- ### ⭐ Question B — 14 Marks (Choice 2 — Alternative Path) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]** **(a)** What is **copy elision**? Differentiate between **RVO** and **NRVO** with C++ examples. Why is copy elision particularly important when a class manages dynamic memory (e.g., a raw pointer member)? **\[7 marks\]** **(b)** A class `Matrix` has a `double* data` member and a size `n`. Write a complete class with: - A constructor allocating `n` doubles. - A copy constructor performing **deep copy**. - A function `Matrix transpose()` that **returns a new transposed matrix by value**. - A destructor that releases the memory. Show a `main()` that adds two matrices using `operator+` returning **by value**. Explain why the Rule of Three must be respected here. **\[7 marks\]** --- #### Model Solution for Q.B(a) **Copy Elision:** A compiler optimisation in which the compiler **omits** one or more copy/move constructor calls, constructing objects directly in their final destination. | Type | Stands For | Trigger | |---|---|---| | RVO | Return Value Optimisation | Returning a *prvalue* (unnamed temporary) | | NRVO | Named Return Value Optimisation | Returning a *named local* variable | **RVO Example:** ```cpp Matrix make() { return Matrix(3, 3); } // prvalue → RVO (C++17 mandatory) ``` **NRVO Example:** ```cpp Matrix make() { Matrix m(3, 3); // named return m; // NRVO (typically applied, not mandatory) } ``` **Why It Matters for Resource-Managing Classes:** If `Matrix` owns `double* data`, every naïve copy involves: 1. A `new double[n]` allocation. 2. A `memcpy` of `n * 8` bytes. 3. A `delete[]` of the temporary. For a $1024 \times 1024$ matrix: $$ \text{Bytes copied} = 1024 \times 1024 \times 8 = 8\,388\,608 \text{ bytes} \approx 8\,\text{MiB} $$ $$ \text{Allocations} = 2 \text{ (copy + temporary)} $$ With copy elision: $$ \text{Bytes copied} = 0, \quad \text{Allocations} = 1 $$ This is a **$\sim 50\%$** reduction in heap traffic and **$\sim 100\%$** reduction in memory bandwidth — critical in high-performance computing. --- #### Model Solution for Q.B(b) ```cpp #include using namespace std; class Matrix { int n; double* data; public: Matrix(int size = 1) : n(size), data(new double[size * size]) { for (int i = 0; i < n * n; ++i) data[i] = 0.0; } // Deep-copy constructor — Rule of Three (1/3) Matrix(const Matrix& other) : n(other.n), data(new double[other.n * other.n]) { for (int i = 0; i < n * n; ++i) data[i] = other.data[i]; cout << "Deep copy ctor\n"; } // Copy assignment — Rule of Three (2/3) Matrix& operator=(const Matrix& other) { if (this != &other) { delete[] data; n = other.n; data = new double[n * n]; for (int i = 0; i < n * n; ++i) data[i] = other.data[i]; } return *this; } // Destructor — Rule of Three (3/3) ~Matrix() { delete[] data; } void set(int i, int j, double v) { data[i * n + j] = v; } double get(int i, int j) const { return data[i * n + j]; } int size() const { return n; } // Returns NEW transposed matrix BY VALUE Matrix transpose() const { Matrix t(n); for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) for (int j = 0; j < n; ++j) t.set(j, i, get(i, j)); return t; // NRVO-eligible } // Operator+ returning by value Matrix operator+(const Matrix& rhs) const { Matrix result(n); for (int i = 0; i < n * n; ++i) result.data[i] = data[i] + rhs.data[i]; return result; // NRVO-eligible } void print() const { for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) { for (int j = 0; j < n; ++j) cout << get(i, j) << " "; cout << "\n"; } } }; int main() { Matrix a(2), b(2); a.set(0,0,1); a.set(0,1,2); a.set(1,0,3); a.set(1,1,4); b.set(0,0,5); b.set(0,1,6); b.set(1,0,7); b.set(1,1,8); Matrix c = a + b; // operator+ returns by value c.print(); cout << "---\n"; Matrix t = a.transpose(); // transpose returns by value t.print(); } ``` **Why the Rule of Three Applies:** Since `Matrix` owns a raw pointer (`data`), three functions must be defined together: 1. **Destructor** — releases `data` to prevent memory leaks. 2. **Copy Constructor** — performs a *deep copy* so the new object has its own `data` buffer. 3. **Copy Assignment Operator** — releases the old `data` first, then deep-copies from `rhs`. If **any one** is missing while the others are defined, the class will either leak memory or double-free. Forgetting the copy constructor while defining a destructor is the **classic KTU trap** — and the reason "Returning Objects" is tightly coupled with the Rule of Three in your Module 2 syllabus. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfalls (Q.B):** > 1. **Shallow copy in the copy constructor** = full mark loss for that sub-part. Always show the `new[]` + loop. > 2. **Forgetting the self-assignment check (`if (this != &other)`)** in `operator=` loses 1 mark. > 3. **Returning a reference to a local from `transpose()` or `operator+`** is the *single most common mistake* in this question. The return type **must** be `Matrix`, not `Matrix&`. > 4. **Skipping the Rule-of-Three explanation** loses 1 mark. The reasoning matters as much as the code. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Three return mechanisms** exist in C++: by *value*, by *reference*, by *pointer*; plus the read-only variant `const T&`. - ✅ **Return by value** is the **safe default** for objects constructed inside the function. Use NRVO/RVO to minimise copies. - ✅ **Return by reference** is used when the object **already exists** outside the function (member data, static, or caller-owned). It is an **l-value**. - ✅ **Never return a reference (or pointer) to a local variable** — it produces a *dangling reference* and undefined behaviour. - ✅ **Copy elision** (RVO/NRVO) can eliminate copies; **C++17 made prvalue RVO mandatory**, but NRVO remains an optimisation. - ✅ **Resource-managing classes** must obey the **Rule of Three** (or Five, in C++11 with move semantics) when returned by value. - ✅ **Return by `const T&`** in getters avoids copying large objects while preventing modification. - ✅ **Return by pointer** signals **optionality**; the caller must check for `nullptr`. - ✅ When tracing object lifetimes, always count: **constructions, copy constructions, move constructions, destructions** — in that order — for full marks. - ✅ In `operator+`, `operator-`, `operator*` for arithmetic types, **return by value** — never by reference — because the result is a new object. > **End of Module 2 Notes — Returning Objects | KTU 2024 Scheme (PECIT633)**

topics.title: Recursion, Static Members
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT633 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Recursion, Static Members

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Recursion — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Recursion** is a programming technique in which a **function (or method) invokes a copy of itself** — either **directly** (the function calls itself by name) or **indirectly** (through one or more intermediate functions) — to solve a problem by reducing it to **smaller, similar sub-problems** until a terminating condition (the **base case**) is reached.

Every legal recursive function in a language like **C++** or **Java** must contain **two mandatory, non-overlapping components**:

1. **Base Case (Terminating Condition)** — the smallest, simplest instance of the problem whose answer is known *a priori*. It stops the recursion.
2. **Recursive Case (Inductive Step)** — the function calls itself with a strictly *smaller* / *closer-to-base* input, transforming the problem toward the base case.

Formally, if $P(n)$ denotes the problem of size $n$:

$$
\text{Recursion} = \begin{cases} \text{Base Case: } P(k) = \text{known answer} & \text{if } n = k \\ \text{Recursive Case: } P(n) = F\big(P(n-1), P(n-2), \dots\big) & \text{if } n > k \end{cases}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Recursion is examined under **CO2 – Apply Object Oriented Concepts to Solve Computational Problems**. Students must be able to *trace* recursive calls, *predict* the output, and *analyse* the **time and space complexity** using the **recurrence relation** $T(n) = a \cdot T\!\left(\frac{n}{b}\right) + f(n)$ form (Master Theorem basics).

---

## 1.2 Static Members — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> A **Static Member** (in C++) or **Static Field / Static Method** (in Java) is a class-level entity that is **shared by every object (instance) of that class**, rather than being duplicated inside each object. A static member is **allocated exactly once** — when the class is first loaded into memory — and is accessed via the *class name* rather than via any *object reference*.

There are three semantic variants encountered in KTU papers:

| Variant | Scope / Lifetime | Storage |
|---|---|---|
| **Static Data Member** (class variable) | Belongs to class; lives for program duration | **Data Segment** (`.bss` / `.data`) |
| **Static Member Function** | Class-level; cannot access `this` | **Code Segment** with single entry point |
| **Static Local Variable** | Block-scope, but persists across calls | **Data Segment** (initialized once) |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

### 🪞 Recursion — The "Matryoshka Doll" Analogy
Imagine a set of **Russian nesting dolls**. To count them, you say: *"I'll count the dolls by opening the current doll, and if I find another doll inside, I'll count those using the **same counting rule**, until I find a doll that contains nothing (the base case)."* Each open doll is a *function call frame* pushed onto the *call stack*; as you finish counting, you close them (pop frames). The smallest empty doll is the **base case** that stops the recursion.

### 🏢 Static Members — The "Office Notice Board" Analogy
Think of a class as an *office building* and its objects as *employees*. A normal (instance) variable is a *personal cubicle* — every employee has their own. A **static member** is a *shared notice board* in the lobby — **only one exists** for the entire building, and *every* employee reads/writes the **same** board. Putting a notice on the board (modifying the static) makes it instantly visible to all employees, even those who have not yet entered the building.

---

## 1.4 Why These Concepts Matter in OOP

> [!TIP]
> **Recursion** is the natural way to express algorithms on **recursive data structures** (linked lists, trees, graphs) and forms the basis of **divide-and-conquer** (Merge Sort, Quick Sort, Binary Search) and **backtracking** (N-Queens, Sudoku) paradigms.
>
> **Static Members** enable **shared state**, **utility methods** (e.g., `Math.sqrt`, `Collections.sort`), **counters and IDs**, the **Singleton design pattern**, and **factory methods** — all of which are heavily tested in KTU's OOP module.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Recursive Call — Step-by-Step Logic

A recursive call, when executed at the **machine level**, performs the following five steps:

1. **Argument Binding** — The actual arguments of the recursive call are evaluated and bound to the formal parameters of the *new* activation record (stack frame).
2. **Stack Frame Push** — A new **activation record** (containing return address, saved frame pointer, local variables, parameters) is pushed onto the **call stack** (typically in the high-memory region growing downward in x86).
3. **Control Transfer** — The instruction pointer (IP / PC) jumps to the entry of the function with the new parameters.
4. **Recursive Descent** — Steps 1–3 repeat until the **base case** is encountered. At this point, the function begins returning values.
5. **Stack Frame Pop & Return** — Each return value is propagated to the *caller's* frame; the frame is then popped. The control returns to the saved return address.

> [!WARNING]
> **Memory Footprint:** A recursion of depth $d$ consumes $\mathcal{O}(d)$ stack space. For $n = 10^6$, naïve recursion (e.g., Fibonacci) will **overflow the default stack** (typically 1 MB ≈ 8 MB → 10,000–100,000 frames). This is the most common cause of *Segmentation Fault* / *StackOverflowError* in OOP labs.

---

## 2.2 Classification of Recursion (KTU High-Yield)

| Type | Definition | Classic Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Direct Recursion** | Function $f$ calls $f$ itself. | `factorial(n)` calls `factorial(n-1)` |
| **Indirect (Mutual) Recursion** | $f \to g \to f$ (cyclic chain). | `isEven(n) ↔ isOdd(n)` |
| **Tail Recursion** | Recursive call is the **last** operation; nothing remains to do after it. | `factorialTail(n, acc)` |
| **Non-Tail Recursion** | Some computation remains after the recursive call returns. | `factorial(n) = n * factorial(n-1)` |
| **Head Recursion** | Recursive call is the **first** operation; nothing before it. | `print(n); rec(n-1)` |
| **Tree (Nested) Recursion** | Function makes **more than one** recursive call per invocation. | Fibonacci: $F(n)=F(n-1)+F(n-2)$ |
| **Backtracking** | Recursion explores choices and **undoes** (backtracks) on failure. | N-Queens, Rat-in-Maze |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU favourite:** Trace the call tree of `fib(5)` and show that it makes **$F_{n+1} - 1$** total calls (e.g., `fib(5)` → 14 calls) → this directly tests understanding of **exponential time complexity** $T(n) = T(n-1) + T(n-2) + \Theta(1) = \mathcal{O}(2^n)$.

---

## 2.3 Recurrence Relations & Complexity Cheat Sheet

The number of operations / time taken by a recursive algorithm is given by a **recurrence relation** $T(n)$. The following table lists the canonical recurrences you *must* memorise for the KTU exam.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical LaTeX Convention:** In all formulas below, **never** use the literal `|` character — write `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent markdown table breakage.

| Algorithm | Recurrence | Closed-Form Time | Space (Stack) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Factorial | $T(n) = T(n-1) + c$ | $\Theta(n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ |
| Fibonacci (naïve) | $T(n) = T(n-1) + T(n-2) + c$ | $\Theta(\varphi^{n})$ where $\varphi \approx 1.618$ | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ |
| Sum of $n$ numbers | $T(n) = T(n-1) + c$ | $\Theta(n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ |
| Binary Search | $T(n) = T(\tfrac{n}{2}) + c$ | $\Theta(\log n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ |
| Merge Sort | $T(n) = 2T(\tfrac{n}{2}) + \Theta(n)$ | $\Theta(n \log n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ |
| Tower of Hanoi | $T(n) = 2T(n-1) + 1$ | $\Theta(2^{n})$ | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ |
| Power (fast) | $T(n) = T(\tfrac{n}{2}) + c$ | $\Theta(\log n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ |
| GCD (Euclidean) | $T(a,b) = T(b, a \bmod b) + c$ | $\Theta(\log \min(a,b))$ | $\Theta(\log \min(a,b))$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Master Theorem (intro form):** For $T(n) = aT(\tfrac{n}{b}) + f(n)$ with $a \geq 1, b > 1$:
> Compare $f(n)$ with $n^{\log_{b} a}$. If $f(n) = n^{\log_{b} a} \cdot (\log n)^{k}$, then $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_{b} a} \cdot (\log n)^{k+1})$.

---

## 2.4 Static Members — Operational Rules

1. **Single Storage Location** — All objects of a class *share* the same static variable. Memory is allocated in the **static/data segment** at program start (or at first class load) and deallocated only at program termination.
2. **Class-Scope, Not Object-Scope** — A static member exists even **before** any object of the class is instantiated. It can be accessed using the *class name* with the scope-resolution operator `::` (C++) or `.` (Java).
3. **No `this` Pointer in Static Functions** — A static member function has **no implicit object**, so it **cannot** access non-static members directly. (It *can* access them through an explicit object reference passed as a parameter.)
4. **Initialization** — In C++, a static data member must be **defined** (not just declared) outside the class using the scope-resolution operator. In Java, it is initialized either at declaration or inside a `static` initializer block.
5. **Access Control Respected** — `public static` members are globally accessible; `private static` members follow normal encapsulation.
6. **Static Local Variables** — In a function, `static int x = 0;` causes `x` to be initialized **exactly once** (on the first call) and to retain its value across subsequent calls. Useful for *call counters* and *memoization* inside functions.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Recursion** powers **compiler parsers** (recursive descent parsing), **file-system traversal** (`ls -R`), **XML/JSON parsing** (nested tags), **AI game trees** (minimax with alpha-beta pruning), and **fractal image generation** (Mandelbrot, Sierpinski triangle).
- **Static Members** are used to implement **Singleton pattern** (one shared configuration), **factory methods** (`Calendar.getInstance()`), **shared caches** and **ID-generators**, and **mathematical utility classes** (`java.lang.Math`, `std::numeric_limits`).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Trace — `factorial(4)`

We trace the call `factorial(4)` written in C++:

```cpp
int factorial(int n) {
    if (n <= 1)              // BASE CASE
        return 1;
    return n * factorial(n-1); // RECURSIVE CASE
}
```

### Call-Tree Expansion

We model the execution as a *dendrogram* of stack frames. The arrow `↓` indicates a function call (push); the arrow `↑` indicates a return (pop).

| Step | Action | Call Stack (top → bottom) | Return Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `main()` calls `factorial(4)` ↓ | `factorial(4)` | — |
| 2 | `n=4 > 1`, so call `factorial(3)` ↓ | `factorial(4) → factorial(3)` | — |
| 3 | `n=3 > 1`, so call `factorial(2)` ↓ | `factorial(4) → factorial(3) → factorial(2)` | — |
| 4 | `n=2 > 1`, so call `factorial(1)` ↓ | `factorial(4) → factorial(3) → factorial(2) → factorial(1)` | — |
| 5 | **Base case hit** — `n=1 ≤ 1` → return `1` ↑ | `factorial(4) → factorial(3) → factorial(2)` | `1` |
| 6 | Compute `2 * 1 = 2` ↑ | `factorial(4) → factorial(3)` | `2` |
| 7 | Compute `3 * 2 = 6` ↑ | `factorial(4)` | `6` |
| 8 | Compute `4 * 6 = 24` ↑ | (empty) | `24` |

**Final result:** `24` ✅

### Recurrence Derivation

Let $T(n)$ be the number of multiplications performed by `factorial(n)`.

$$
T(n) = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } n \le 1 \quad \text{(base case, no multiplication)} \\ 1 + T(n-1) & \text{if } n > 1 \quad \text{(one multiplication + recursive call)} \end{cases}
$$

Unrolling the recurrence by substitution:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= 1 + T(n-1) \\
     &= 1 + 1 + T(n-2) = 2 + T(n-2) \\
     &= 3 + T(n-3) \\
     &\;\;\vdots \\
     &= (n-1) + T(1) \\
     &= (n-1) + 0 = n - 1
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence $T(n) = n - 1 \in \Theta(n)$, and since there are $n$ simultaneous stack frames at peak depth, **auxiliary space** $= \mathcal{O}(n)$.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Trace — `fib(4)` (Naïve Fibonacci)

The naïve recursive Fibonacci is:

```cpp
int fib(int n) {
    if (n <= 1) return n;     // Base cases: fib(0)=0, fib(1)=1
    return fib(n-1) + fib(n-2); // Two recursive calls — TREE recursion
}
```

### Call-Tree (Dendrogram) for `fib(4)`

```
                          fib(4)
                        /        \
                   fib(3)          fib(2)
                  /     \         /     \
              fib(2)   fib(1)  fib(1)  fib(0)
              /    \     |       |       |
          fib(1) fib(0)  1       1       0
            |      |
            1      0
```

### Sub-evaluation Table (Post-Order Traversal)

Following the call tree from **leaves to root**:

| Sub-call | Result | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `fib(0)` | `0` | base case |
| `fib(1)` | `1` | base case (executed 3 times) |
| `fib(2)` | `1 + 0 = 1` | left child `fib(1)` + right child `fib(0)` |
| `fib(2)` | `1 + 0 = 1` | second instance (right subtree of root) |
| `fib(3)` | `1 + 1 = 2` | `fib(2)+fib(1) = 1+1` |
| `fib(4)` | `2 + 1 = 3` | `fib(3)+fib(2) = 2+1` |

**Output:** `3`

### Why Naïve Fibonacci is $\Theta(2^n)$

Counting the total number of calls $C(n)$:

$$
C(n) = \begin{cases} 1 & n \le 1 \\ 1 + C(n-1) + C(n-2) & n > 1 \end{cases}
$$

This is the same recurrence as the Fibonacci sequence itself, so $C(n) \approx F_{n+1}$. The closed form (Binet's formula) is:

$$
F_{n} = \frac{\varphi^{n} - \psi^{n}}{\sqrt{5}}, \quad \text{where } \varphi = \frac{1+\sqrt{5}}{2} \approx 1.618, \; \psi = \frac{1-\sqrt{5}}{2} \approx -0.618
$$

Since $\vert \psi \vert < 1$, the dominant term is $\varphi^{n} / \sqrt{5}$, so $C(n) \in \Theta(\varphi^{n}) \subseteq \Theta(2^{n})$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memoization (Dynamic Programming) Fix:** Store each computed `fib(k)` in an array; reuse it. This converts the recurrence to $T(n) = T(n-1) + \Theta(1) = \Theta(n)$ — a dramatic KTU-favourite transformation.

---

## 3.3 Tower of Hanoi — Recurrence Derivation

**Problem:** Move $n$ disks from source peg $A$ to destination peg $C$ using auxiliary peg $B$. Rules: (i) only one disk at a time, (ii) never place a larger disk on a smaller one.

**Recursive Insight:** To move $n$ disks from $A \to C$:
1. Move top $n-1$ disks from $A \to B$ (using $C$).
2. Move the largest disk from $A \to C$ (1 move).
3. Move the $n-1$ disks from $B \to C$ (using $A$).

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= 2 \cdot T(n-1) + 1 \\
T(1) &= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Unrolling:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= 2T(n-1) + 1 \\
     &= 2\big(2T(n-2) + 1\big) + 1 = 4T(n-2) + 2 + 1 \\
     &= 4\big(2T(n-3) + 1\big) + 2 + 1 = 8T(n-3) + 4 + 2 + 1 \\
     &\;\;\vdots \\
     &= 2^{n-1} T(1) + \sum_{i=0}^{n-2} 2^{i} \\
     &= 2^{n-1} + (2^{n-1} - 1) = 2^{n} - 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Closed form:** $T(n) = 2^{n} - 1$ moves — minimal, and provably optimal. For $n = 64$ (the original legend), this is $2^{64}-1 \approx 1.8 \times 10^{19}$ moves.

---

## 3.4 Static Members — Full Worked Example (C++ vs Java)

### C++ Implementation

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

class Employee {
private:
    int id;                  // instance variable
    string name;             // instance variable
    static int nextId;       // static data member (shared)
    static int count;        // static data member (shared)
public:
    Employee(string n) : name(n) {
        id = ++nextId;       // pre-increment; assign unique ID
        count++;
    }
    ~Employee() { count--; }
    static int getCount() {  // static member function
        return count;
        // NOTE: cannot access 'id' or 'name' here — no `this`
    }
    void display() const {
        cout << "ID=" << id << ", Name=" << name
             << ", Total Employees=" << count << endl;
    }
};

// Mandatory definitions of static data members (outside class)
int Employee::nextId = 1000;
int Employee::count  = 0;

int main() {
    cout << "Initial count = " << Employee::getCount() << endl; // 0
    Employee e1("Alice"), e2("Bob"), e3("Charlie");
    e1.display();  // ID=1001 ...
    e2.display();  // ID=1002 ...
    e3.display();  // ID=1003 ...
    cout << "Final count = " << Employee::getCount() << endl;   // 3
    return 0;
}
```

### Java Equivalent

```java
class Employee {
    private int id;                      // instance
    private String name;                 // instance
    private static int nextId = 1000;    // class variable
    private static int count  = 0;       // class variable

    public Employee(String n) {
        name = n;
        id = ++nextId;
        count++;
    }
    public static int getCount() {       // static method
        return count;
    }
    public void display() {
        System.out.println("ID=" + id + ", Name=" + name
                           + ", Total=" + count);
    }
    static { /* static initializer block — runs once on class load */
        System.out.println("Employee class loaded.");
    }
}
```

### Memory Layout Diagram (textual)

```
DATA SEGMENT (Static)
 ┌───────────────────────────────┐
 │  Employee::nextId = 1001      │ ◄── shared, one copy
 │  Employee::count  = 3         │ ◄── shared, one copy
 └───────────────────────────────┘
        ▲           ▲          ▲
        │           │          │
   ┌────┴──┐   ┌────┴──┐  ┌────┴──┐
   │  e1   │   │  e2   │  │  e3   │   (each on HEAP / stack)
   │ id=1  │   │ id=2  │  │ id=3  │   instance data — UNIQUE
   │name=A │   │name=B │  │name=C │
   └───────┘   └───────┘  └───────┘
```

### Why `static getCount()` Cannot Use `id`

$$
\text{Without an object} \Rightarrow \nexists \, \text{this pointer} \Rightarrow \text{cannot resolve} \; \texttt{this->id}
$$

Static methods belong to the **class type**, not any particular instance. They can be called *even when zero objects exist* (`Employee::getCount()`), so any access to per-instance state would be ambiguous.

---

## 3.5 Static Local Variable — Analytical Example

```cpp
void counter() {
    static int callCount = 0;   // initialized ONCE on first call
    callCount++;
    cout << "Called " << callCount << " times" << endl;
}
```

| Invocation # | Value of `callCount` after call | Output |
|---|---|---|
| 1st | 1 | `Called 1 times` |
| 2nd | 2 | `Called 2 times` |
| 3rd | 3 | `Called 3 times` |
| 100th | 100 | `Called 100 times` |

> [!TIP]
> Compare with an automatic `int callCount = 0;` which is re-initialized on *every* call. This distinction is a **favourite 3-mark question** in KTU exams — the examiner tests whether you understand **lifetime vs scope**.

---

## 3.6 Power Function — Logarithmic Recursion (Fast Exponentiation)

To compute $x^{n}$ efficiently, observe:

$$
x^{n} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } n = 0 \\ (x^{n/2})^{2} & \text{if } n > 0, \, n \text{ even} \\ x \cdot (x^{(n-1)/2})^{2} & \text{if } n > 0, \, n \text{ odd} \end{cases}
$$

This yields the recurrence $T(n) = T(\lfloor n/2 \rfloor) + \Theta(1) = \Theta(\log n)$, replacing the naïve $\Theta(n)$ linear recursion. **This is a classic KTU Part B question.**

---

## 3.7 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Recursion vs Iteration

| Criterion | Recursion | Iteration |
|---|---|---|
| **Termination** | Base case | Loop condition |
| **State storage** | Call stack (implicit) | Loop counter / accumulator (explicit) |
| **Memory** | $\mathcal{O}(\text{depth})$ extra | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ extra |
| **Speed** | Slower (push/pop overhead) | Faster (no stack frames) |
| **Code clarity** | Naturally expresses divide-and-conquer, trees, backtracking | Better for linear scans |
| **Tail-recursion opt.** | Compiler can convert to loop (C++: `-O2`; Java: limited) | Already a loop |
| **Risk** | Stack overflow | Infinite loop (still bad, but no memory issue) |
| **Mutual conversion** | Any tail recursion → loop; some loops → recursion (using explicit stack) | — |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Recursion Call-Stack Lifecycle (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Program Start: main calls factorial 4] --> B{Push factorial 4 onto stack}
    B --> C{Push factorial 3 onto stack}
    C --> D{Push factorial 2 onto stack}
    D --> E{Push factorial 1 onto stack}
    E --> F{Base Case n less than or equal 1}
    F --> G[Return 1 to caller factorial 2]
    G --> H[Pop factorial 1 frame]
    H --> I[Compute 2 times 1 equals 2]
    I --> J[Return 2 to caller factorial 3]
    J --> K[Pop factorial 2 frame]
    K --> L[Compute 3 times 2 equals 6]
    L --> M[Return 6 to caller factorial 4]
    M --> N[Pop factorial 3 frame]
    N --> O[Compute 4 times 6 equals 24]
    O --> P[Return 24 to main]
    P --> Q[Pop factorial 4 frame]
    Q --> R[Program End: result is 24]
```

## 4.2 Naïve Fibonacci Recursion Tree (`fib(4)`)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[fib 4] --> left1[fib 3]
    start --> right1[fib 2]
    left1 --> l2a[fib 2]
    left1 --> l2b[fib 1 returns 1]
    right1 --> r2a[fib 1 returns 1]
    right1 --> r2b[fib 0 returns 0]
    l2a --> l3a[fib 1 returns 1]
    l2a --> l3b[fib 0 returns 0]
```

> The diagram makes the **overlapping sub-problem** visible: `fib(2)` is computed **twice** and `fib(1)` is computed **three times** — this is the root cause of exponential blow-up and the motivation for **memoization / dynamic programming**.

## 4.3 Static vs Instance Member — Memory Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DS["DATA SEGMENT - Static Region"]
        S1[class Employee nextId = 1001]
        S2[class Employee count = 3]
    end
    subgraph OBJ1["Object e1 - HEAP"]
        O1A[id = 1001]
        O1B[name = Alice]
    end
    subgraph OBJ2["Object e2 - HEAP"]
        O2A[id = 1002]
        O2B[name = Bob]
    end
    subgraph OBJ3["Object e3 - HEAP"]
        O3A[id = 1003]
        O3B[name = Charlie]
    end
    S1 -.shared by.-> OBJ1
    S1 -.shared by.-> OBJ2
    S1 -.shared by.-> OBJ3
    S2 -.shared by.-> OBJ1
    S2 -.shared by.-> OBJ2
    S2 -.shared by.-> OBJ3
```

## 4.4 Recursive Problem-Solving Pipeline (Generic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Identify Base Case - smallest unsplittable problem] --> P2[Identify Recursive Case - express P of n in terms of P of smaller n]
    P2 --> P3[Ensure strictly decreasing argument - guarantees progress toward base]
    P3 --> P4[Code: function calls itself with smaller arg]
    P4 --> P5{Trace and verify on small inputs n = 0, 1, 2, 3}
    P5 -- pass --> P6[Derive recurrence T of n and solve]
    P5 -- fail --> P1
    P6 --> P7[State Time and Space complexity]
```

## 4.5 Tail vs Non-Tail Recursion

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A["Tail Recursion: nothing to do after call"]
        A1["factorialTail n a = if n==0 then a else factorialTail n-1 n times a"]
    end
    subgraph B["Non-Tail Recursion: multiplication after call"]
        B1["factorial n = if n less than 1 then 1 else n times factorial n-1"]
    end
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap
