## 📘 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between recursion and iteration. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Recursion | Iteration |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Function calls itself | Loop executes a block repeatedly |
| Memory | $\mathcal{O}(d)$ stack frames (depth $d$) | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ auxiliary |
| Speed | Slower (function call overhead) | Faster |
| Code size | Shorter for divide-and-conquer | Shorter for linear scans |

- **Advantage of recursion:** Naturally expresses solutions to problems with **recursive structure** (trees, graphs, divide-and-conquer).
- **Disadvantage of recursion:** Risk of **stack overflow** for large inputs; higher memory and time overhead.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is a static data member? Why must it be defined outside the class in C++?**

**Model Answer:**
A **static data member** is a class-level variable that is **shared by all objects** of the class; only **one copy** exists regardless of how many objects are created.

In C++, the class declaration (`class Employee { static int count; };`) is **merely a declaration** that tells the compiler a static member *exists*. The **definition** (memory allocation) must be provided in **exactly one translation unit** (`.cpp` file) using the scope-resolution operator:

```cpp
int Employee::count = 0;
```

This avoids the **"undefined reference" linker error** that occurs if the static is only declared and never defined. *(In Java, no such explicit definition is required.)*

---

## 📗 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **❓ Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Write a recursive C++ function `int power(int x, int n)` that computes $x^{n}$ using the **fast-exponentiation** technique (logarithmic recursion). Trace its execution for `power(2, 5)` showing all recursive calls and returned values. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Analyse its **time complexity** by deriving the recurrence relation $T(n)$ and solving it. Compare it with the naïve $\mathcal{O}(n)$ linear recursive version. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

int power(int x, int n) {
    if (n == 0)            return 1;                       // base
    if (n % 2 == 0)        return power(x, n/2) * power(x, n/2); // even
    else                   return x * power(x, n/2) * power(x, n/2); // odd
}

int main() {
    cout << power(2, 5) << endl;   // output 32
    return 0;
}
```

> **Note:** The above naive `O(n)` style is for clarity. The *truly* optimized version halves `n` once and reuses the same intermediate:
```cpp
int half = power(x, n/2);
if (n % 2 == 0) return half * half;
else            return x * half * half;
```

**Trace table for `power(2, 5)` (using the optimized half-reuse form):**

| Call | Argument `n` | Parity | Action | Returns |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `power(2, 5)` | odd | `x * half² = 2 * half²` | — |
| 2 | `power(2, 2)` | even | `half² = h²` | — |
| 3 | `power(2, 1)` | odd | `x * half² = 2 * half²` | — |
| 4 | `power(2, 0)` | base | `1` | `1` |
| 3 (unwind) | — | — | `2 * 1² = 2` | `2` |
| 2 (unwind) | — | — | `2² = 4` | `4` |
| 1 (unwind) | — | — | `2 * 4² = 32` | `32` |

**Final output:** `32` ✅

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

Let $T(n)$ be the number of multiplications in `power(x, n)`.

**Recurrence:**

$$
T(n) = \begin{cases} 0 & n = 0 \\ 1 + T(\lfloor n/2 \rfloor) & n \ge 1 \end{cases}
$$

The `+1` accounts for at most **two multiplications** $\times$ `half` $\times$ `half` (counted as constant) plus the single recursive call.

**Solve by repeated substitution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= 1 + T(\lfloor n/2 \rfloor) \\
     &= 1 + 1 + T(\lfloor n/4 \rfloor) = 2 + T(\lfloor n/4 \rfloor) \\
     &= 3 + T(\lfloor n/8 \rfloor) \\
     &\;\;\vdots \\
     &= k + T(\lfloor n / 2^{k} \rfloor)
\end{aligned}
$$

The recursion bottoms out when $\lfloor n / 2^{k} \rfloor = 1$, i.e., $k = \lfloor \log_{2} n \rfloor$.

**Closed form:**

$$
T(n) = \lfloor \log_{2} n \rfloor \in \Theta(\log n)
$$

**Comparison with naïve $\mathcal{O}(n)$ recursion:**

| Version | Recurrence | Time | Stack depth |
|---|---|---|---|
| Naïve `x * power(x, n-1)` | $T(n) = T(n-1) + 1$ | $\Theta(n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ |
| Fast `power(x, n/2)` | $T(n) = T(n/2) + 1$ | $\Theta(\log n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ |

For $n = 10^{9}$: naïve → $10^{9}$ operations; fast → $\approx 30$ operations. **Huge difference** — and a KTU favourite illustration of algorithmic design.

---

### **❓ Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **static data members** and **static member functions** in C++ with a suitable example. State the rules for accessing static members. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete C++ program to maintain a `BankAccount` class where every account is assigned an **auto-incremented 6-digit account number** using a `static long nextAccountNo = 100000;`. Implement deposit, withdraw, and a static function `getTotalAccounts()` that returns the count of active accounts. Show the output for creating 3 accounts. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

**Static Data Member:** A class variable that is *shared* across all objects. It is **declared inside** the class with the `static` keyword and **defined outside** the class (exactly once).

**Static Member Function:** A class-level function that does **not** receive an implicit `this` pointer. It can access only static members directly; it must use an explicit object to access non-static members.

**Rules for access:**

1. A static member belongs to the *class*, not to any object — accessed via `ClassName::member`.
2. It can also be accessed via any object (`obj.member`), but this is discouraged.
3. Static member functions **cannot** be declared `const` (since `const` applies to the object).
4. Static member functions **cannot** access non-static data members or call non-static member functions directly.
5. A static data member has **static storage duration** — its lifetime extends from class definition to program termination.

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

class BankAccount {
private:
    long accountNo;            // instance
    string holder;             // instance
    double balance;            // instance
    static long nextAccountNo; // shared
    static int totalAccounts;  // shared
public:
    BankAccount(string name, double opening) {
        holder   = name;
        balance  = opening;
        accountNo = ++nextAccountNo;     // auto-increment
        totalAccounts++;
    }
    void deposit(double amt) {
        if (amt > 0) balance += amt;
    }
    bool withdraw(double amt) {
        if (amt > 0 && amt <= balance) { balance -= amt; return true; }
        return false;
    }
    void display() const {
        cout << "AccNo=" << accountNo
             << ", Holder=" << holder
             << ", Balance=" << balance << endl;
    }
    static int getTotalAccounts() {   // static function
        return totalAccounts;
    }
};

long BankAccount::nextAccountNo = 100000;   // definition
int  BankAccount::totalAccounts  = 0;        // definition

int main() {
    BankAccount a1("Arjun",  5000);
    BankAccount a2("Meera", 10000);
    BankAccount a3("Ravi",   2500);
    a1.deposit(2000); a2.withdraw(1500); a3.deposit(500);
    a1.display(); a2.display(); a3.display();
    cout << "Total active accounts = "
         << BankAccount::getTotalAccounts() << endl;
    return 0;
}
```

**Expected Output:**

```
AccNo=100001, Holder=Arjun,  Balance=7000
AccNo=100002, Holder=Meera,  Balance=8500
AccNo=100003, Holder=Ravi,   Balance=3000
Total active accounts = 3
```

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to define** `BankAccount::nextAccountNo` outside the class → *linker error* (zero marks for execution).
> 2. Writing `static int totalAccounts = 0;` *and* trying to assign it again in a constructor — it gets re-initialised.
> 3. Trying to call `display()` (non-static) from `getTotalAccounts()` (static) — illegal.
> 4. Accessing `nextAccountNo` as a normal variable inside `main()` via `a1.nextAccountNo` — compiles but loses KTU marks (use `BankAccount::nextAccountNo`).
> 5. Declaring the static as `static const long nextAccountNo = 100000;` and then doing `++nextAccountNo` — **illegal** for `const`!

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **recursive function** must always have a **base case** that is **guaranteed to be reached**; otherwise the program **runs forever until stack overflow**.
- ✅ Recursion uses the **call stack**; memory = $\mathcal{O}(\text{depth})$; time governed by a **recurrence relation**.
- ✅ **Naïve Fibonacci** is $\Theta(\varphi^{n})$ — a **classical example** of why memoization matters.
- ✅ **Tower of Hanoi** minimum moves = $2^{n}-1$ — a clean closed-form derivation.
- ✅ **Tail recursion** (recursive call is the *last* operation) **can be converted to a loop** by the compiler (C++ with `-O2`).
- ✅ **Static data members** are **shared** across all objects and stored in the **data segment**; only **one copy** exists.
- ✅ In C++, a static data member is **declared inside** the class but **defined outside** using the scope-resolution operator `ClassName::member`.
- ✅ In Java, the static field is declared and initialised *inside* the class (or in a `static {}` block) and is accessed via `ClassName.member`.
- ✅ A **static member function** has **no `this` pointer** — it can access **only static members directly**.
- ✅ A **static local variable** inside a function is **initialised once** and retains its value across calls (lifetime = program, scope = block).
- ✅ **Singleton design pattern** relies on a `private static instance` member and a `public static accessor` method.
- ✅ Recurrence relations to memorise: $T(n)=T(n-1)+c \Rightarrow \Theta(n)$; $T(n)=2T(n/2)+c \Rightarrow \Theta(n)$; $T(n)=2T(n-1)+1 \Rightarrow \Theta(2^{n})$; $T(n)=T(n-1)+T(n-2)+c \Rightarrow \Theta(\varphi^{n})$.
- ✅ For **fast exponentiation**, the trick is $x^{n} = (x^{\lfloor n/2 \rfloor})^{2}$ — reducing the problem to **half-size**, giving $\Theta(\log n)$.
- ✅ **Mutual (indirect) recursion** requires **forward declaration** in C++ so the compiler knows the function exists.
- ✅ Static members follow **normal access control** — a `private static` is still inaccessible outside the class.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Final Variables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT633 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Final Variables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the Java programming language, a **Final Variable** is a variable whose value cannot be modified after it is assigned — exactly once. The `final` keyword applied to a variable declaration enforces **immutability of the reference** (for object references) or **immutability of the value** (for primitive types). Once the Java Virtual Machine (JVM) initializes a final variable, any attempt to reassign it results in a **compile-time error**, classified under `JLS §4.12.4` (final variables).

> [!NOTE]
> **Official JLS Definition (Paraphrased):** A variable can be declared `final`. A final variable may only be assigned to once. This assignment must be *definitely assigned* before the variable is used (a property enforced by the Java compiler's *definite assignment analysis*).

## 1.2 Categories of Final Variables

KTU's OOP module classifies final variables into **three primary categories** based on declaration context:

| # | Category | Scope | Initialization Site |
|---|----------|-------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Final Local Variable** | Inside a method/block | Before use, within the method body |
| 2 | **Final Instance Variable** | Per-object (Heap) | At declaration OR in every constructor OR in an instance initializer block |
| 3 | **Final Static Variable** | Class-level (Method Area) | At declaration OR in a static initializer block |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Blank Final Variable:** A final variable that is *declared but not initialized at the point of declaration* is called a **Blank Final Variable**. It must be assigned exactly once in one of the legal initialization sites before being read.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are signing a **legal contract** with a real-estate company to buy a flat:

- The **contract value (final variable)** is written in pen. Once both parties sign, no one can legally change the price scribbled on that page. You can build a house on it (use the value), but you cannot alter the price once inked.
- If the contract states *"Flat No. B-204"* (the reference), the flat number cannot be changed. However, you are free to **renovate the interior** of Flat B-204 — this represents that the *object's internal state can change*, but the *reference binding is permanent*.

> This is the essence of `final` — the **binding is permanent**, but the **contents of what it points to may still mutate** (for non-immutable objects).

## 1.4 Key Distinctions Students Must Internalize

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - `final` on a **primitive** → the value is constant.
> - `final` on an **object reference** → the reference is constant, **NOT the object itself**.
> - `final` is **not the same as** the `const` keyword in C/C++. Java has **no `const` keyword** (it is reserved but unused).
> - `final` ≠ `static`. They are independent modifiers and can be combined as `static final` to create a *class constant*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — The "Why" and "How"

The Java compiler enforces `final` through three sequential checks (the *definite assignment rule*):

1. **Declaration Phase** — The variable is registered in the symbol table with the `FINAL` flag set.
2. **Assignment Tracking** — The compiler tracks every *write* to the variable using a static data-flow analysis.
3. **Read Verification** — Before any *read* (use) of the variable, the compiler guarantees the variable has been *definitely assigned* exactly once.

**Why does Java use `final`?**
- **Thread Safety:** Immutable references are inherently thread-safe because no write can occur after publication.
- **Design Intent:** Communicates to other developers that the value is a *design constant* (e.g., `MAX_SIZE = 100`).
- **Compiler Optimization:** The compiler can inline `final` primitive values, eliminating redundant memory lookups.
- **Inner Class Capture:** A local variable accessed from an *inner (non-static) class* must be `final` (or *effectively final* since Java 8) to prevent *inconsistency* between the inner class copy and the outer scope.

## 2.2 The Three Initialization Rules

### Rule 1 — Final Local Variable
A final local variable **must be definitely assigned before it is used**, but unlike instance/static finals, it can be assigned *conditionally* in different code paths (as long as the compiler proves it is assigned on every path leading to the read).

### Rule 2 — Final Instance Variable
A final instance variable **must be assigned exactly once** in **one of these three places**:
- (a) At the point of declaration.
- (b) In **every** constructor of the class.
- (c) In an **instance initializer block**.

### Rule 3 — Final Static Variable
A final static variable **must be assigned exactly once** in **one of these two places**:
- (a) At the point of declaration.
- (b) In a **static initializer block** (`static { }`).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this restriction?** Static variables are initialized *before any object is constructed*. Hence, they cannot legally be assigned inside an instance constructor — the static field would belong to a class, not an object.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Property | Final Local | Final Instance | Final Static |
|----------|-------------|----------------|--------------|
| Memory Location | Stack | Heap (per object) | Method Area (class) |
| Default Value? | **No** (must assign) | **No** (must assign) | **No** (must assign) |
| Multiple Objects Share? | No (per method call) | No (per object) | **Yes** (per class) |
| Blank Final Allowed? | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Can be reassigned? | No (compile error) | No (compile error) | No (compile error) |
| Common Use | Loop counters, parameters | Immutable POJO fields | Mathematical constants (e.g., `PI`) |
| Syntax | `final int x = 5;` | `final int x;` (in class) | `static final double PI = 3.14;` |

### KTU Memory Aid Formula

$$
\text{Number of assignments to a `final` variable} \;=\; 1
$$

$$
\text{Reads} \;-\; \text{Writes} \;=\; \text{Complies with JLS } \S 4.12.4
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reference Finality Rule (Object References):**
> If `final MyClass obj = new MyClass();` is declared, then `obj` cannot be reassigned to point to a *different* object, but `obj.setData(...)` is perfectly legal because the **object's internal state is not bound by `final`**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Embedded Systems & IoT:** Hardware register addresses that must never be accidentally overwritten.
- **Cryptographic Libraries:** Algorithm constants like `AES_BLOCK_SIZE = 16` declared `public static final`.
- **Configuration Management:** API endpoint base URLs, retry counts, and timeouts that should never change at runtime.
- **Functional Programming (Java 8+):** Lambdas and streams rely on *effectively final* variables for closure capture.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analysis & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Initialization Logic — Exhaustive Walkthrough

Consider the following canonical KTU-style code analysis problem:

```java
class Sensor {
    final int id;                 // Blank final instance variable
    final static String TYPE;     // Blank final static variable

    static {
        TYPE = "TEMPERATURE";     // (1) Static initializer
    }

    Sensor(int id) {
        this.id = id;             // (2) Constructor assignment
    }
}
```

**Compiler Analysis (Definite Assignment Tracking):**

**Step 1 — Static Phase (Class Loading):**
The JVM loads `Sensor.class` into the Method Area. The `static final` field `TYPE` is identified as *uninitialized*. The static initializer block executes, setting `TYPE = "TEMPERATURE"`. After this, the `FINAL` flag is permanently locked for `TYPE`.

**Step 2 — Instance Phase (Object Construction):**
For every `new Sensor(7)` invocation:
- Memory is allocated on the Heap for the new object.
- The `final int id` field starts in an *unassigned* state.
- The constructor `Sensor(int id)` runs `this.id = id;`, performing the *one and only* legal assignment.
- After the constructor completes, the `FINAL` flag locks `id` permanently.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If the constructor were `Sensor(int id) { /* forgot this.id = id; */ }`, the compiler would emit: *"variable id might not have been initialized"*.

## 3.2 Reassignment Analysis — The Compile-Time Gate

The following matrix evaluates **every legal/illegal operation** on a `final` variable. KTU frequently tests this:

| # | Code Snippet | Verdict | Reason |
|---|--------------|---------|--------|
| 1 | `final int x = 5; x = 10;` | **Compile Error** | Reassignment of final primitive |
| 2 | `final int[] arr = {1,2,3}; arr[0] = 99;` | **Legal** | Modifying array contents, not the reference |
| 3 | `final int[] arr = {1,2,3}; arr = new int[5];` | **Compile Error** | Reassignment of final reference |
| 4 | `final String s = "hi"; s.concat("!");` | **Legal** | `concat` returns a new String; `s` is not reassigned |
| 5 | `final List<String> l = new ArrayList<>(); l.add("a");` | **Legal** | Mutating the list object, not rebinding the reference |
| 6 | `void m(final int p) { p = 5; }` | **Compile Error** | Final parameters cannot be reassigned |

## 3.3 Blank Final Variable — Definite Assignment Proof

For a blank final instance variable, the compiler must verify the **"exactly once"** invariant across **all constructors**. Consider:

```java
class Device {
    final String serial;
    Device(String s) { this.serial = s; }
    Device()        { this.serial = "DEFAULT"; }
}
```

**Compiler's Logical Proof:**

$$
\forall \, c \in \text{Constructors}(Device), \;\; \exists \; \text{path} \in c \;\; \text{such that} \;\; \text{this.serial} := \text{value}
$$

In both constructors, the path `this.serial = ...` is reachable, hence the blank final is *definitely assigned*. If we added a third constructor that did **not** assign `serial`, the compiler would reject the class.

## 3.4 Effectively Final (Java 8+ NEP Context)

> [!NOTE]
> Although this is technically a Java 8 concept, KTU's 2024 NEP-aligned syllabus touches on it under *Final Variables* because it is the direct successor concept.

A local variable that is **not declared `final` but is never reassigned** is called *effectively final*. It is treated exactly like a `final` variable for the purpose of *lambda capture* and *inner class access*.

```java
int counter = 10;   // Effectively final — never reassigned
Runnable r = () -> System.out.println(counter);  // Legal in Java 8+
```

The moment you write `counter++;` in the enclosing method, the variable **loses** its effectively-final status, and the lambda fails to compile.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Block Diagram — Final Variable Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Final Variable in Java]:::root
    A --> B[Final Local Variable]
    A --> C[Final Instance Variable]
    A --> D[Final Static Variable]
    A --> E[Final Parameter]
    A --> F[Blank Final Variable]

    B --> B1[Declared inside method or block]
    B --> B2[Must be assigned before use]
    B --> B3[Stack memory]

    C --> C1[Declared at class level non static]
    C --> C2[Initialized in constructor or initializer block]
    C --> C3[Heap memory per object]

    D --> D1[Declared as static final]
    D --> D2[Initialized in static block or at declaration]
    D --> D3[Method Area class level]

    E --> E1[Passed to method or constructor]
    E --> E2[Cannot be reassigned inside method]
    E --> E3[Stack memory]

    F --> F1[Declared without initialization]
    F --> F2[Must be assigned exactly once]
    F --> F3[Subtype of local instance or static]

    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
    classDef sub fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#3b82f6,color:#0c1e3e
    class B1,B2,B3,C1,C2,C3,D1,D2,D3,E1,E2,E3,F1,F2,F3 sub
```

## 4.2 Lifecycle Flow — Definite Assignment Verification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Parser sees final keyword]:::stage --> P2[Registers FINAL flag in symbol table]:::stage
    P2 --> P3[Definite Assignment Analyzer activated]:::stage
    P3 --> P4{All paths assign exactly once?}:::decision
    P4 -- Yes --> P5[Class compiles successfully]:::success
    P4 -- No --> P6[Compile time error variable may not be initialized]:::error
    P4 -- Multiple --> P7[Compile time error variable might already be assigned]:::error

    classDef stage fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#f59e0b,color:#78350f
    classDef decision fill:#fde68a,stroke:#d97706,color:#78350f
    classDef success fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#16a34a,color:#14532d
    classDef error fill:#fecaca,stroke:#dc2626,color:#7f1d1d
    class P1,P2,P3 stage
    class P4 decision
    class P5 success
    class P6,P7 error
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Final Variable Memory Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MethodArea["Method Area Class Level"]
        SF[static final TYPE equals TEMPERATURE]:::static
    end

    subgraph HeapObj1["Heap Object 1"]
        IF1[final int id equals 101]:::instance
    end

    subgraph HeapObj2["Heap Object 2"]
        IF2[final int id equals 202]:::instance
    end

    subgraph StackFrame["Stack Frame main method"]
        LV1[final int x equals 5]:::local
        LV2[final Sensor s points to HeapObj1]:::local
    end

    SF -.shared across all objects.-> HeapObj1
    SF -.shared across all objects.-> HeapObj2
    LV2 -.reference cannot be rebound.-> HeapObj1
    HeapObj1 -.internal state may still mutate.-> HeapObj1

    classDef static fill:#e9d5ff,stroke:#7c3aed,color:#3b0764
    classDef instance fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#2563eb,color:#1e3a8a
    classDef local fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#ea580c,color:#7c2d12
    class SF static
    class IF1,IF2 instance
    class LV1,LV2 local
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate between `final`, `finally`, and `finalize` in Java.**

**Model Answer:**

- **`final`** — A **modifier** keyword. It can be applied to a **variable** (value/reference cannot change), a **method** (cannot be overridden), or a **class** (cannot be subclassed).
- **`finally`** — A **block** used in exception handling. The code inside a `finally` block executes regardless of whether an exception is thrown or caught, typically used for resource cleanup (e.g., closing files or database connections).
- **`finalize`** — A **method** inherited from `java.lang.Object`. The JVM calls it *just before* an object is garbage-collected. It has been **deprecated since Java 9** in favor of `try-with-resources` and `Cleaner` API.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Mistake:** Writing "finalize is a constructor" or confusing `final` with the C++ `const` keyword. KTU examiners specifically deduct marks for this.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is a blank final variable in Java? Give an example.**

**Model Answer:**

A **blank final variable** is a `final` variable that is *declared without an initializer*. The Java compiler enforces that such a variable must be assigned *exactly once* in one of the legal initialization sites depending on its category:

- Blank final **instance variable** → assigned in the constructor or an instance initializer block.
- Blank final **static variable** → assigned in a static initializer block.

**Example:**
```java
class Student {
    final int rollNo;          // Blank final instance variable
    final static String COLLEGE; // Blank final static variable

    static {
        COLLEGE = "KTU Kerala";
    }

    Student(int r) {
        rollNo = r;            // Legal single assignment in constructor
    }
}
```

If the constructor failed to assign `rollNo`, the compiler would report *"variable rollNo might not have been initialized"*.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the different types of final variables in Java with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is the difference between declaring a variable as `final` and as `static`? Can a variable be both `final` and `static`? Illustrate with an example. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Java supports four major categories of final variables:

**1. Final Local Variable** — Declared inside a method, constructor, or block. Must be definitely assigned before use, but assignment may occur in conditional branches.

```java
void calculate(int n) {
    final int result;          // Blank final local variable
    if (n > 0) {
        result = n * 2;
    } else {
        result = 0;
    }
    System.out.println(result); // Legal — definitely assigned on all paths
}
```

**2. Final Instance Variable** — Belongs to each object. Must be assigned exactly once in every constructor, in an instance initializer block, or at the point of declaration.

```java
class Account {
    final long accountNumber;  // Blank final instance variable
    Account(long num) {
        this.accountNumber = num;  // Single legal assignment
    }
}
```

**3. Final Static Variable** — Belongs to the class, shared across all objects. Must be assigned in a static block or at declaration. Commonly used for class-level constants.

```java
class MathConstants {
    public static final double PI = 3.14159265358979;
}
```

**4. Final Parameter** — A method parameter declared `final` cannot be reassigned inside the method body. Useful for inner-class and lambda capture.

```java
void process(final int id) {
    // id = 99;  // COMPILE ERROR
    System.out.println(id);
}
```

**(b)** The modifiers `final` and `static` are **independent** and serve different purposes:

| Aspect | `final` | `static` |
|--------|---------|----------|
| Meaning | Value/reference cannot be reassigned | Variable is shared across all objects; belongs to the class |
| Memory | Per object (instance) or per call (local) | One copy per class in Method Area |
| Default value? | No | No |
| Common use | Constants, design contracts | Counters, class-level metadata |

**Yes**, a variable can be **both** `final` and `static`. This combination produces a **class constant** — a single memory copy that cannot be reassigned. This is the standard idiom for mathematical and configuration constants:

```java
class Configuration {
    public static final int MAX_CONNECTIONS = 100;
    public static final String API_URL = "https://api.ktu.edu.in";
    public static final long SESSION_TIMEOUT_MS = 3600000L;
}
```

These are conventionally named in **UPPER_SNAKE_CASE**, accessed as `Configuration.MAX_CONNECTIONS`, and are inlined by the Java compiler for performance optimization.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – June 2023 Retest]

**(a)** Explain the concept of blank final variables in Java. Why are they useful? Provide a code example demonstrating initialization in a constructor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss with examples what happens when we try to modify a final variable's value and a final object reference's internal state. Why is this distinction important? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** A **blank final variable** is a `final` variable declared *without* an initial value. The Java compiler enforces the invariant: *it must be assigned exactly once before any read occurs*.

**Why are they useful?**
- They allow **per-object immutability** where the value is not known until construction time (e.g., a unique `id`, `serialNumber`, or `hashCode`).
- They preserve the immutability guarantee while permitting *runtime-derived* initialization.
- They are the only legal way to assign a `final` field based on a constructor argument.

**Code Example:**
```java
class Employee {
    final int empId;        // Blank final
    final String empName;   // Blank final

    Employee(int id, String name) {
        this.empId = id;     // Single legal assignment
        this.empName = name; // Single legal assignment
    }

    void promote() {
        // this.empId = 999;  // COMPILE ERROR: cannot assign to final
    }
}
```

**(b)** The distinction between modifying a final variable's *value* versus a final reference's *internal state* is one of the most frequently tested concepts in KTU exams.

**Case 1: Modifying a Final Primitive's Value**
```java
final int count = 10;
count = 20;   // COMPILE ERROR: cannot assign a value to final variable count
```
The compiler rejects this because `count` is *the* value, and reassignment violates the JLS contract.

**Case 2: Modifying the Object a Final Reference Points To**
```java
final int[] numbers = {1, 2, 3};
numbers[0] = 99;     // LEGAL — modifying the array's contents
System.out.println(numbers[0]); // Prints 99

numbers = new int[5]; // COMPILE ERROR — rebinding the reference
```
The reference `numbers` is permanently bound to the array object, but the **array's elements are mutable**.

**Case 3: Modifying a Mutable Object's Fields**
```java
class Point { int x, y; }

final Point p = new Point();
p.x = 50;   // LEGAL — modifying field of the object
p = new Point(); // COMPILE ERROR — rebinding
```

**Why is this distinction important?**
- It teaches the **difference between reference immutability and object immutability**.
- It explains why `String` in Java is *truly* immutable (its fields are private and final, and no mutator methods are exposed), while a `final StringBuilder` is *not* truly immutable (its internal buffer can be modified).
- It is foundational for understanding **defensive copying**, **persistent data structures**, and **functional programming idioms** in Java.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing `final` with `static`:** Writing "`final` means the variable is shared across all objects" — **WRONG**. That is `static`. `final` means *unmodifiable after assignment*.
> 2. **Saying `final` makes objects immutable:** It only makes the *reference* immutable. Internal state can still change. Examiners deduct up to **3 marks** for this error.
> 3. **Forgetting that blank final instance variables must be assigned in *every* constructor:** If a class has 3 constructors and only 2 initialize the field, the code does not compile.
> 4. **Confusing `finalize()` with a destructor:** `finalize` is deprecated and called by the GC at an unpredictable time. It is **not** a C++ destructor.
> 5. **Misspelling keywords in code:** Examiners reading handwritten answer sheets often find students writing `Final` (capital F) or `FINAL` — these are **invalid** in Java.
> 6. **Ignoring the `static final` order convention:** Java accepts both orders, but KTU's official answer key uses `public static final` for class constants.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **`final` variable = unmodifiable binding** (value for primitives, reference for objects). One assignment. Lifetime-locked.
- **Three categories:** final **local** (method scope), final **instance** (per object, must be assigned in every constructor or initializer), final **static** (per class, must be assigned in static block or at declaration).
- **Blank final** = declared without initialization. Must be assigned exactly once in a legal site.
- **`final` ≠ `static`.** Combine them as `static final` to create a **class constant** (convention: UPPER_SNAKE_CASE).
- **Final reference ≠ immutable object.** The object a final reference points to can still mutate its internal state. To get true immutability, all fields of the object must be `private final` with no mutators.
- **Final parameters** are required for variables used in **inner classes** (pre-Java 8) and recommended for **lambda capture** (effectively final in Java 8+).
- **Memory location:** Local final → Stack. Instance final → Heap (per object). Static final → Method Area (per class).
- **Compile-time enforcement:** All `final` rules are checked at compile time, not runtime, making them zero-cost abstractions.
- **Common interview/KTU trick question:** *"Can a final variable be declared without initialization?"* — **Yes**, if it is a *blank final* and assigned later in a constructor or initializer block.
- **Forgotten fact:** A final local variable's *initialization need not happen at declaration* — but it must be **definitely assigned** on every execution path leading to its use.
- **Real-world idiom:** Use `public static final` for application-wide constants like `MAX_RETRY_COUNT`, `DEFAULT_TIMEOUT`, `API_BASE_URL`.
- **Deprecated but asked:** Know that `finalize()` is *deprecated* since Java 9 — modern code uses *try-with-resources* or `java.lang.ref.Cleaner`.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inner Classes, Inheritance - Super Class
Sub Class
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECIT633 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Sub Class # Module 2 — Sub Class (Subclass in Object-Oriented Programming) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECIT633 — Module 2)** > A **Sub Class** (also called a *child class*, *derived class*, or *extended class*) is a class that **inherits attributes and behaviors (fields and methods)** from another class, referred to as the **Super Class** (parent/base class). The mechanism that enables this relationship is called **Inheritance**, and it is one of the four foundational pillars of Object-Oriented Programming (OOP): **Abstraction, Encapsulation, Inheritance, Polymorphism**. In the **Java** programming language (the reference implementation language prescribed by the KTU 2024 Scheme for PECIT633), a subclass is declared using the **`extends`** keyword, which enforces single inheritance between classes. Interfaces, on the other hand, use the `implements` keyword and allow multiple inheritance of type. ### Conceptual Analogy — The "Family Tree" Model Imagine a real-world **biological family tree**: - A `Parent` possesses certain characteristics — surname, eye color, blood group, profession. - A `Child` automatically inherits these traits at birth, but may also possess its own unique traits (e.g., a unique talent). - The child may *override* a behavior of the parent (e.g., a softer voice) while still retaining the inherited family name. In OOP, the **Parent Class** is the **Super Class**, and the **Child Class** is the **Sub Class**. The child gets: 1. All **non-private** fields (data members). 2. All **non-private** methods (behaviors). 3. The ability to **add** new fields/methods. 4. The ability to **override** inherited methods to provide specialized behavior. This represents an **"IS-A" relationship** in UML terminology — e.g., `Dog IS-A Animal`, `Car IS-A Vehicle`, `SavingsAccount IS-A BankAccount`. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key KTU 2024 Highlight:** > In Java, every class implicitly inherits from the `java.lang.Object` class. Hence, even if you declare a class without `extends`, it is technically a subclass of `Object`. This guarantees that every Java class inherits methods like `toString()`, `equals()`, `hashCode()`, and `getClass()`. ### Formal Syntax (Java) for Declaring a Sub Class ```java accessModifier class SubClassName extends SuperClassName { // inherited members are accessible here // new fields and methods can be added // inherited methods can be overridden } ``` ### Hierarchy of Terminology — At a Glance | Term | Synonyms | Role | |---|---|---| | **Super Class** | Parent Class, Base Class, Ancestor | Donates members | | **Sub Class** | Child Class, Derived Class, Heir | Receives + extends members | | **Inheritance** | Generalization (parent view), Specialization (child view) | The IS-A mechanism | | **Single Inheritance** | One parent → One child | Default in Java classes | | **Multilevel Inheritance** | Grandparent → Parent → Child | Allowed in Java | | **Hierarchical Inheritance** | One parent → Many children | Allowed in Java | | **Multiple Inheritance** | Multiple parents → One child | **Not allowed** with classes in Java; achieved via interfaces | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of Sub Class Creation When the JVM encounters a subclass declaration, the following logical sequence is executed: 1. **Class Declaration Phase:** The compiler recognizes the `extends` keyword and registers the subclass as a *child type* of the superclass. 2. **Member Inheritance Resolution:** All `public`, `protected`, and *package-private* (default) members of the superclass are made accessible inside the subclass scope. `private` members are **not inherited** but can be accessed via public/protected getters/setters. 3. **Method Binding Setup:** The subclass can either: - **Inherit** a method as-is. - **Override** it by providing a new implementation with the same signature (annotations like `@Override` ensure correctness). - **Hide** it by declaring a static method with the same signature (static methods are not overridden; they are hidden). 4. **Constructor Chaining:** When an object of the subclass is instantiated, the **superclass constructor is invoked first** (implicitly via `super()` or explicitly via `super(args)`), followed by the subclass constructor body. 5. **Type Compatibility:** A subclass reference is **assignable** to a superclass variable (upcasting is implicit), but a superclass reference to a subclass object requires **explicit downcasting**. > [!NOTE] > **The "Why" Behind Sub Classes (Engineering Justification):** > Subclasses enable **code reuse**, **polymorphic dispatch**, and **extensibility**. In real-world KTU-aligned engineering projects (e.g., payroll systems, sensor networks, IoT device hierarchies), subclasses model real taxonomic hierarchies such as `Sensor → TemperatureSensor → DS18B20Sensor`, reducing code duplication by **70–90%** in well-designed systems. ### 2.2 The `super` Keyword — The Sub Class's Hotline to Its Parent The `super` keyword in Java is a **reference variable** that refers to the immediate superclass. It has three distinct uses: | Use | Syntax | Purpose | |---|---|---| | **Calling superclass constructor** | `super();` or `super(args);` | Must be the **first statement** in the subclass constructor | | **Accessing superclass field** | `super.fieldName;` | Resolves name ambiguity when subclass shadows a parent field | | **Invoking superclass method** | `super.methodName(args);` | Calls the parent's version even after overriding | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Insight:** > If a superclass has only a **parameterized constructor** and no default `no-arg` constructor, the subclass constructor **must** explicitly invoke `super(args)` as its first statement — otherwise, a *compile-time error* occurs. ### 2.3 Access Modifier Visibility Matrix in Inheritance The visibility of inherited members depends on the access modifier declared in the superclass. The following table consolidates KTU-high-yield knowledge: | Superclass Member Modifier | Accessible in Same Package Subclass? | Accessible in Different Package Subclass? | Accessible Outside Package (Non-Subclass)? | |---|---|---|---| | `public` | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | | `protected` | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | | *default* (no modifier) | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ No | | `private` | ❌ No | ❌ No | ❌ No | > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often assume `private` members are inherited. They are **not directly accessible** in the subclass. They exist in the subclass object (memory-wise) but require public/protected accessor methods for read/write operations. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Sub Class Design Rules | # | Rule / Formula | Description | |---|---|---| | 1 | $\text{SubClass Object Size} = \text{SuperClass Fields} + \text{SubClass Fields}$ | Each subclass object in memory contains both its own fields AND the inherited fields of all superclasses in the chain. | | 2 | $\text{IS-A Relationship}: \text{SubClass} \rightarrow \text{SuperClass}$ | A subclass object passes the `instanceof` check against its superclass. | | 3 | **Single Inheritance Constraint**: $\text{extends clause} \rightarrow 1 \text{ class}$ | A Java class can extend **only one** class. | | 4 | **Constructor Chain Depth** $D$ | If inheritance chain has depth $D$ (number of classes), then $D$ constructors execute from root to leaf during object creation. | | 5 | **Polymorphic Substitution** | If $B \text{ extends } A$, then `A obj = new B();` is valid. Method calls resolve to $B$'s overridden version at runtime (**dynamic dispatch**). | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **GUI Frameworks:** JavaFX/Swing — `JButton extends AbstractButton extends JComponent extends Container extends Component`. - **Embedded Systems (IoT):** Device → Sensor → TemperatureSensor → DS18B20. - **Banking Software:** `Account` → `SavingsAccount`, `CurrentAccount`, `FixedDepositAccount`. - **Game Development:** `Entity` → `Player`, `Enemy`, `NPC`, `Projectile`. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example — Designing a Sub Class Hierarchy Let's derive a complete subclass implementation step by step. Consider a **University Management System** with the following KTU-aligned design: #### Step 1 — Define the Super Class ```java // File: Person.java public class Person { // Fields — encapsulated with protected access for subclass visibility protected String name; protected int age; // Constructor public Person(String name, int age) { this.name = name; this.age = age; } // Method to be overridden by subclasses public void displayInfo() { System.out.println("Name: " + name + ", Age: " + age); } // Method to be inherited as-is public void celebrateBirthday() { age++; System.out.println(name + " is now " + age + " years old."); } } ``` #### Step 2 — Define the Sub Class `Student` ```java // File: Student.java public class Student extends Person { private String rollNumber; private double cgpa; // Constructor — explicitly chaining to superclass public Student(String name, int age, String rollNumber, double cgpa) { super(name, age); // Step 1: call superclass constructor this.rollNumber = rollNumber; // Step 2: initialize subclass fields this.cgpa = cgpa; } // Overriding displayInfo() to extend behavior @Override public void displayInfo() { super.displayInfo(); // Reuse parent's logic System.out.println("Roll No: " + rollNumber + ", CGPA: " + cgpa); } // Subclass-specific method public void study() { System.out.println(name + " is studying."); } } ``` #### Step 3 — Trace the Constructor Execution Order When you execute `new Student("Anu", 20, "S101", 9.2)`, the JVM performs: $$ \text{Execution Sequence} = \text{Object}() \rightarrow \text{Person}("Anu", 20) \rightarrow \text{Student}("S101", 9.2) $$ The mathematical representation of constructor chaining is: $$ C_{\text{Student}} = \text{super}(n, a) \oplus S_{\text{init}} $$ where $C_{\text{Student}}$ is the Student constructor, $\text{super}(n, a)$ invokes the parent, and $S_{\text{init}}$ is the Student-specific initialization. #### Step 4 — Polymorphic Assignment Demonstration ```java public class Main { public static void main(String[] args) { // Polymorphic upcasting — implicit, always safe Person p = new Student("Rahul", 21, "S102", 8.7); p.displayInfo(); // Calls Student's overridden version (dynamic dispatch) p.celebrateBirthday(); // Calls inherited method // To access subclass-specific methods, downcast explicitly if (p instanceof Student) { Student s = (Student) p; s.study(); } } } ``` **Output:** ``` Name: Rahul, Age: 21 Roll No: S102, CGPA: 8.7 Rahul is now 22 years old. Rahul is studying. ``` ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Types of Inheritance with Java Examples | Inheritance Type | Java Class Diagram | Allowed in Java? | KTU Example | |---|---|---|---| | **Single** | `A → B` | ✅ Yes | `Dog extends Animal` | | **Multilevel** | `A → B → C` | ✅ Yes | `Vehicle → Car → SportsCar` | | **Hierarchical** | `A → B`, `A → C` | ✅ Yes | `Shape → Circle`, `Shape → Rectangle` | | **Multiple** | `A → C`, `B → C` | ❌ No (use interfaces) | Not allowed with classes | | **Hybrid** | Combination | ❌ No (Diamond problem) | Use interface hierarchy | ### 3.3 Memory Layout Analysis of a Sub Class Object For a subclass object, the memory allocation follows this principle: $$ \text{Heap Memory Allocation} = \underbrace{\text{Superclass Fields}}_{\text{Region 1}} + \underbrace{\text{Subclass Fields}}_{\text{Region 2}} + \underbrace{\text{VMT Pointer}}_{\text{v-table}} $$ where **VMT (Virtual Method Table)** enables dynamic dispatch of overridden methods at runtime. > [!NOTE] > The Object class header (16 bytes on 64-bit JVMs) is also added to every Java object, including subclass instances. ### 3.4 Step-by-Step Method Overriding Rules (KTU Mandatory) 1. **Same name** as the superclass method. 2. **Same parameter list** (covariant return types are allowed since Java 5). 3. **Cannot reduce visibility** (e.g., `public → private` is invalid). 4. **Cannot override `static` methods** — they are *hidden*, not overridden. 5. **Cannot override `final` methods** — `final` prevents inheritance of behavior. 6. **Constructor cannot be overridden.** 7. Use the `@Override` annotation for compile-time safety. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Class Hierarchy Diagram — University System ```mermaid classDiagram direction LR class Object { <> +toString() String +equals(Object) boolean +hashCode() int } class Person { #name: String #age: int +Person(String, int) +displayInfo() void +celebrateBirthday() void } class Student { -rollNumber: String -cgpa: double +Student(String, int, String, double) +displayInfo() void +study() void } class Faculty { -employeeId: String -department: String +Faculty(String, int, String, String) +displayInfo() void +teach() void } Object <|-- Person Person <|-- Student Person <|-- Faculty ``` ### 4.2 Inheritance Type Architecture (Block-Level Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Inheritance Types in Java] A --> B[Single Inheritance] A --> C[Multilevel Inheritance] A --> D[Hierarchical Inheritance] A --> E[Multiple Inheritance NOT Allowed with Classes] A --> F[Achieved via Interfaces] B --> B1[Super A] B1 --> B2[Sub B extends A] C --> C1[Grand A] C1 --> C2[Parent B extends A] C2 --> C3[Child C extends B] D --> D1[Base A] D1 --> D2[Sub1 B extends A] D1 --> D3[Sub2 C extends A] E --> E1[Interface I1] E --> E2[Interface I2] E1 --> E3[Class D implements I1, I2] E2 --> E3 ``` ### 4.3 Method Dispatch Flow (Dynamic Polymorphism in Subclasses) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Client participant SuperRef as Person ref participant StudentObj as Student Object participant FacultyObj as Faculty Object Client->>SuperRef: Person p = new Student() Note over SuperRef: Compile-time type: Person Note over SuperRef: Runtime type: Student Client->>SuperRef: p.displayInfo() SuperRef->>StudentObj: Lookup in v-table StudentObj-->>Client: Executes Student.displayInfo() Client->>SuperRef: p = new Faculty() SuperRef->>FacultyObj: Lookup in v-table FacultyObj-->>Client: Executes Faculty.displayInfo() Note over Client, FacultyObj: Same call, different behavior
DYNAMIC DISPATCH achieved ``` ### 4.4 Constructor Chaining Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([new Student called]) --> Step1[Allocate heap memory] Step1 --> Step2[JVM invokes Student constructor] Step2 --> Step3{First statement is super call?} Step3 -->|Yes - explicit| Step4[Execute super args] Step3 -->|No - implicit| Step5[Insert default super] Step5 --> Step4 Step4 --> Step6[Execute Person constructor body] Step6 --> Step7[Return to Student constructor] Step7 --> Step8[Execute remaining Student body] Step8 --> Done([Object ready]) ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** *Define a subclass. How is it declared in Java? Give one example.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A **subclass** is a class that inherits properties and behaviors from another class called the **superclass**, using the mechanism of inheritance. It establishes an *IS-A* relationship, promoting code reuse and extensibility. In Java, a subclass is declared using the `extends` keyword. **Example (1 Mark):** ```java class Animal { /* superclass */ } class Dog extends Animal { /* subclass */ } ``` Here, `Dog` is the subclass that inherits all non-private members of `Animal` and may add or override them. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *Explain the difference between method overriding and method hiding in Java.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **Method Overriding (1 Mark):** Occurs when an instance method in the subclass has the same signature as in the superclass. Resolved at **runtime** via dynamic dispatch. Uses `@Override` annotation. - **Method Hiding (1 Mark):** Occurs when a `static` method in the subclass has the same signature as in the superclass. Resolved at **compile-time** based on reference type. Cannot use `@Override`. - **Key Difference (1 Mark):** Overriding supports runtime polymorphism; hiding does not. Overridden methods are instance-bound; hidden methods are class-bound. --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Module carries a 14-mark question with internal choice. Below, Question A and Question B are independent alternatives. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper] **(a)** Explain the concept of inheritance in Java. Discuss different types of inheritance supported by Java with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Write a Java program to create a superclass `Shape` with a method `area()`. Create two subclasses `Circle` and `Rectangle` that override the `area()` method to compute and display the respective areas. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a)** *Inheritance is the OOP mechanism that allows a class (subclass) to acquire the properties and methods of another class (superclass). It establishes an "IS-A" relationship and supports code reuse. Java supports:* | Type | Example | Allowed in Java? | |---|---|---| | Single | `B extends A` | ✅ | | Multilevel | `C extends B extends A` | ✅ | | Hierarchical | `B,C extends A` | ✅ | | Multiple | `C extends A, B` | ❌ (use interfaces) | *(5 marks for explanation + 2 marks for examples)* **(b)** Complete Java Code: ```java class Shape { public void area() { System.out.println("Area calculation in base class"); } } class Circle extends Shape { private double radius; public Circle(double radius) { this.radius = radius; } @Override public void area() { double result = Math.PI * radius * radius; System.out.println("Circle Area: " + result); } } class Rectangle extends Shape { private double length, width; public Rectangle(double length, double width) { this.length = length; this.width = width; } @Override public void area() { double result = length * width; System.out.println("Rectangle Area: " + result); } } public class Main { public static void main(String[] args) { Shape s; // polymorphic reference s = new Circle(5.0); s.area(); // Circle's version s = new Rectangle(4.0, 6.0); s.area(); // Rectangle's version } } ``` **Output:** ``` Circle Area: 78.53981633974483 Rectangle Area: 24.0 ``` *(3 marks for class declarations + 2 marks for overrides + 2 marks for polymorphic main method)* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Alternative] **(a)** Explain the `super` keyword in Java. List its uses with examples. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Design a superclass `Employee` with fields `name`, `salary`, and methods to display details. Create a subclass `Manager` that adds a `bonus` field, overrides the display method, and includes a method `totalSalary()`. Write the complete Java program. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a)** The `super` keyword is a reference variable that refers to the immediate superclass. **Three uses (2 marks each):** 1. **Calling Superclass Constructor:** Must be the first statement in subclass constructor. ```java class B extends A { B() { super(); /* explicit call */ } } ``` 2. **Accessing Superclass Field:** Resolves name ambiguity. ```java super.fieldName; ``` 3. **Invoking Superclass Method:** Calls overridden parent's version. ```java super.displayInfo(); ``` **(b)** Complete Java Code: ```java class Employee { protected String name; protected double salary; public Employee(String name, double salary) { this.name = name; this.salary = salary; } public void displayDetails() { System.out.println("Name: " + name + ", Salary: " + salary); } } class Manager extends Employee { private double bonus; public Manager(String name, double salary, double bonus) { super(name, salary); // superclass constructor call this.bonus = bonus; } @Override public void displayDetails() { super.displayDetails(); // reuse parent logic System.out.println("Bonus: " + bonus); } public double totalSalary() { return salary + bonus; } } public class Test { public static void main(String[] args) { Manager m = new Manager("Priya", 50000, 15000); m.displayDetails(); System.out.println("Total Salary: " + m.totalSalary()); } } ``` **Output:** ``` Name: Priya, Salary: 50000.0 Bonus: 15000.0 Total Salary: 65000.0 ``` *(2 marks super + 2 marks field + 3 marks subclass design + 2 marks main method + 2 marks correct output)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting `super(args)` call** when superclass has only parameterized constructors → compile-time error → 0 marks. > 2. **Reducing visibility during override** (`public → private`) → compile-time error. > 3. **Confusing `this` and `super`** — `this` refers to current object, `super` refers to parent. > 4. **Assuming `private` members are inherited** — they are not directly accessible. > 5. **Using `extends` with multiple classes** — Java does not support multiple inheritance with classes. > 6. **Missing `@Override` annotation** — not an error, but loses method-overriding clarity marks. --- ### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ A **subclass** inherits non-private members from its superclass using `extends`. - ✅ It supports **code reuse**, **polymorphism**, and **extensibility**. - ✅ Java supports **single, multilevel, and hierarchical** inheritance with classes — **NOT multiple**. - ✅ **Constructors are NOT inherited** but are chained via `super()`. - ✅ **`private` members are not directly accessible** in the subclass. - ✅ **Method overriding** enables runtime polymorphism; **method hiding** does not. - ✅ **`final` methods cannot be overridden**; **`final` classes cannot be subclassed**. - ✅ The **Object class** is the ultimate superclass of every Java class. - ✅ **Upcasting** (subclass → superclass) is implicit; **downcasting** requires explicit cast + `instanceof` check. - ✅ Use the **`@Override` annotation** for compile-time safety during overriding. - ✅ **Covariant return types** are allowed in overrides since Java 5 (e.g., overriding `Animal getAnimal()` with `Dog getAnimal()`). - ✅ Always place `super(args)` as the **first statement** in a subclass constructor.

topics.title: Types of Inheritance
The super Keyword
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT633 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** The super Keyword

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme — Syllabus Definition**
> The `super` keyword in Java is a built-in reference variable that is implicitly provided by the Java compiler and refers to the **immediate superclass (parent class)** object. It acts as a bridge between a subclass and its direct parent, enabling access to the parent's hidden (shadowed) fields, overridden methods, and constructors.

In Object-Oriented Programming (OOP), inheritance forms an "is-a" hierarchy. When a subclass inherits from a superclass, a naming conflict can arise if both the subclass and superclass declare entities (variables or methods) with the same identifier. The `super` keyword resolves this ambiguity by explicitly pointing to the parent class's member, bypassing the subclass's version.

The `super` keyword has **three primary use cases** in Java:
1. **`super.variableName`** → Access the parent class's field (instance variable).
2. **`super.methodName()`** → Invoke the parent class's method (especially an overridden one).
3. **`super(arguments)`** → Call the parent class's constructor (constructor chaining).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Family Tree**

Imagine a child (Subclass) who inherits traits, surname, and ancestral property from their father (Superclass). 

- **The Surname Conflict**: Both the father and the son have a name. If you call the son by his first name, you get the son's identity. But if you want to specifically refer to the **father's** name (e.g., for legal documents), you use `super` → "I am referring to my **parent's** version."
- **The Property Conflict**: The father has a house. The son built a new house with the same name. If a visitor asks, "Whose house is this?", pointing to the original ancestral house requires `super.house`.
- **The Construction Link**: When building a multi-story building (Subclass), the foundation (Superclass constructor) **must** be laid first. `super()` is the crane that brings down the foundation pieces before the upper floors (subclass constructor body) can be constructed.

Thus, **`super` is a respectful way for the child class to acknowledge and utilize its parent's resources while still maintaining its own specialized identity.**

---

### 1.3 Why Is `super` Necessary? — The Problem It Solves

In Java, when a subclass **overrides** a method or **shadows** a variable of the superclass, the subclass's version takes priority. This creates a problem:

$$ \text{Without `super`} \implies \text{Parent's hidden version is inaccessible} $$

$$ \text{With `super`} \implies \text{Explicit, unambiguous access to parent's version} $$

| Scenario | What Happens Without `super` | What `super` Solves |
|---|---|---|
| Variable shadowing | `x` always refers to subclass's `x` | `super.x` retrieves parent's `x` |
| Method overriding | `display()` always runs subclass version | `super.display()` invokes parent's version |
| Constructor invocation | Compiler auto-calls `super()` (no-arg) only if available | Explicit `super(args)` enables parameterized parent construction |

---

### 1.4 Important Rules and Constraints

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Examiner's Rules to Memorize:**
> 1. `super` refers to the **immediate** parent class only (not grandparents, unless chained via parent).
> 2. `super` **cannot** be used inside a `static` method or `static` block (because `super` is an instance reference).
> 3. `super()` (constructor call) **must** be the **first statement** in a subclass constructor.
> 4. If a constructor does not explicitly call `super()` or `this()`, the compiler **automatically inserts** `super()` (the no-argument call).
> 5. `super` is **not** a real object — it is a reference variable, so it cannot be assigned to another reference or used in expressions like casting checks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Three Operational Modes of `super`

#### **Mode 1 — `super` to Access Parent Class Variables (Field Shadowing)**

When a subclass declares an instance variable with the **same name** as the superclass, the subclass variable **shadows** (hides) the parent's variable. To access the shadowed parent variable, the syntax is:

```java
super.fieldName;
```

**Operational Logic Steps:**
1. The JVM looks up the variable identifier in the subclass first.
2. If found, it uses the subclass's version (default resolution).
3. If the developer writes `super.fieldName`, the JVM **skips** the subclass lookup and directly accesses the variable in the immediate parent class.

---

#### **Mode 2 — `super` to Invoke Parent Class Methods (Method Overriding)**

When a subclass **overrides** a method of the parent, calling the method by name invokes the subclass version. To explicitly call the parent's overridden method:

```java
super.methodName(arguments);
```

**Operational Logic Steps:**
1. Used to **extend** the parent's behavior rather than completely replace it.
2. The subclass method first performs its specialized logic, then calls `super.methodName()` to invoke the parent's original implementation.
3. This implements the **"extend and add"** pattern, common in template-method designs.

---

#### **Mode 3 — `super()` to Invoke Parent Class Constructor**

A subclass constructor **must** invoke a parent class constructor to ensure proper initialization of inherited fields.

```java
super(argumentList);   // Must be the first line in subclass constructor
```

**Operational Logic Steps:**
1. When an object of the subclass is created, the constructor chain begins from the **topmost superclass** and proceeds **downward** to the subclass.
2. Each constructor calls its parent's constructor using `super(...)` (explicitly or implicitly).
3. This guarantees that inherited fields are initialized **before** the subclass's own initialization code executes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Constructor Chain Rule (Constructor Chaining):**
> If a subclass constructor does **not** explicitly call `super()` or `this()`, the compiler automatically inserts a call to the **no-argument constructor** of the parent. If the parent has no no-arg constructor, a **compilation error** occurs.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Syntax Form** | **Purpose** | **Placement Rule** | **Example** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| `super.field` | Access shadowed parent variable | Anywhere in subclass (instance context) | `super.brand;` |
| `super.method(args)` | Invoke overridden parent method | Anywhere in subclass (instance context) | `super.display();` |
| `super(args)` | Invoke parent constructor | **Must be FIRST statement** in subclass constructor | `super("Hello");` |
| `super()` | Implicit no-arg parent call | Auto-inserted by compiler if no `super` or `this` is written | Compiled automatically |

| **Constraint** | **Rule** | **Violation Consequence** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| `super` in static context | ❌ Prohibited | Compile-time error |
| `super()` placement | Must be first line of constructor | Compile-time error |
| Multiple `super()` calls | Only **one** allowed per constructor | Compile-time error |
| `super` + `this()` in same constructor | **Impossible** (both must be first) | Compile-time error |

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The `super` keyword is foundational in several real engineering scenarios:

- **GUI Frameworks (Swing/JavaFX)**: When extending `JFrame`, the custom constructor invokes `super("Window Title")` to set the parent frame's title.
- **Exception Handling**: Custom exceptions extend `Exception`. `super(message)` passes the error string to the parent's constructor.
- **JDBC Programming**: When creating custom data source classes, `super(config)` initializes the inherited connection pool from the base class.
- **Game Development**: A `Boss` class extending `Enemy` calls `super(health, damage)` to initialize base combat stats before adding unique boss abilities.

---

### 2.4 The "Why" Behind the Design

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rationale:**
> Java designers created `super` (alongside `this`) to provide **explicit, unambiguous navigation** through the inheritance hierarchy. Without `super`, programmers would have no standardized way to disambiguate name conflicts caused by overriding and shadowing — leading to fragile, error-prone code.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — `super` with Variable Shadowing

**Problem Statement:** Demonstrate how `super` resolves variable shadowing in a parent-child class relationship.

**Step-by-Step Construction:**

**Step 1** — Define the parent class `Vehicle` with an instance variable `maxSpeed`.

```java
class Vehicle {
    int maxSpeed = 120;   // Parent class variable
}
```

**Step 2** — Define the child class `Car` that extends `Vehicle` and declares a **same-named** variable (shadowing).

```java
class Car extends Vehicle {
    int maxSpeed = 180;   // Shadows the parent's maxSpeed
}
```

**Step 3** — Add a `display()` method that uses **both** the child's and the parent's variable.

```java
class Car extends Vehicle {
    int maxSpeed = 180;

    void display() {
        System.out.println("Child maxSpeed   : " + maxSpeed);
        System.out.println("Parent maxSpeed  : " + super.maxSpeed);
    }
}
```

**Step 4** — Logical evaluation when `new Car().display()` is called:

$$
\text{Expression `maxSpeed`} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{JVM searches subclass first} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Finds 180}
$$

$$
\text{Expression `super.maxSpeed`} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{JVM skips subclass} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Searches parent} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Finds 120}
$$

**Final Output Trace:**

| Expression | Lookup Path | Value Retrieved |
|:---:|:---|:---:|
| `maxSpeed` | Car → Vehicle (not needed) | **180** |
| `super.maxSpeed` | Vehicle (directly) | **120** |

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — `super` with Method Overriding

**Problem Statement:** Demonstrate the "extend and add" pattern using `super.display()` inside an overridden method.

**Step-by-Step Construction:**

**Step 1** — Parent class `Animal` defines a `sound()` method.

```java
class Animal {
    void sound() {
        System.out.println("Animal makes a sound.");
    }
}
```

**Step 2** — Child class `Dog` overrides `sound()` and calls the parent's version using `super`.

```java
class Dog extends Animal {
    @Override
    void sound() {
        super.sound();   // Calls parent's sound()
        System.out.println("Dog barks.");
    }
}
```

**Step 3** — Execution flow when `new Dog().sound()` is invoked:

$$
\text{Step 1: } \text{Entry to Dog.sound()}
$$

$$
\text{Step 2: } \text{Execute super.sound()} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Control transfers to Animal.sound()}
$$

$$
\text{Step 3: } \text{Animal.sound() prints: "Animal makes a sound."}
$$

$$
\text{Step 4: } \text{Control returns to Dog.sound()}
$$

$$
\text{Step 5: } \text{Execute remaining statement} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Prints: "Dog barks."}
$$

**Output Verification:**

```
Animal makes a sound.
Dog barks.
```

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — `super()` for Constructor Chaining

**Problem Statement:** Demonstrate explicit parent constructor invocation with arguments.

**Step-by-Step Construction:**

**Step 1** — Define parent class `Person` with a parameterized constructor.

```java
class Person {
    String name;

    Person(String name) {
        this.name = name;
        System.out.println("Person constructor called. Name = " + name);
    }
}
```

**Step 2** — Define child class `Student` that explicitly invokes the parent's parameterized constructor.

```java
class Student extends Person {
    int rollNo;

    Student(String name, int rollNo) {
        super(name);             // MUST be the first statement
        this.rollNo = rollNo;
        System.out.println("Student constructor called. Roll = " + rollNo);
    }
}
```

**Step 3** — Constructor chain execution when `new Student("Anu", 101)` is called:

$$
\text{Execution Order (Top-Down Initialization)}
$$

$$
\text{Call: } \texttt{Student("Anu", 101)}
$$

$$
\Downarrow
$$

$$
\texttt{super("Anu")} \;\Rightarrow\; \texttt{Person("Anu")} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Initializes `name = "Anu"`}
$$

$$
\Updownarrow
$$

$$
\text{Control returns to Student constructor} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Initializes `rollNo = 101`}
$$

$$
\Updownarrow
$$

$$
\text{Object fully constructed.}
$$

**Output Verification:**

```
Person constructor called. Name = Anu
Student constructor called. Roll = 101
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Observation:** The parent constructor executes **first**, ensuring `name` is initialized before the subclass logic uses it. This is the **Initialization-before-Use guarantee** that `super()` enforces.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — `super` vs `this`

| **Aspect** | **`this` keyword** | **`super` keyword** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Refers to | Current class object | Immediate parent class object |
| Variable access | `this.variable` → current class variable | `super.variable` → parent class variable |
| Method call | `this.method()` → current class method | `super.method()` → parent class method |
| Constructor call | `this(args)` → overloaded constructor in same class | `super(args)` → parent class constructor |
| Placement rule | `this()` must be first in constructor | `super()` must be first in constructor |
| Static context | ❌ Not allowed | ❌ Not allowed |
| Coexistence | `this` and `super` can both appear in a constructor, but `super()` and `this()` are **mutually exclusive** | Same |

---

### 3.5 The Three-Tier Inheritance Chain (Multi-Level)

When a chain `GrandParent → Parent → Child` exists, only **one level** is accessed per `super` reference.

$$
\texttt{GrandParent} \;\xrightarrow{\texttt{extends}}\; \texttt{Parent} \;\xrightarrow{\texttt{extends}}\; \texttt{Child}
$$

From within `Child`, `super.member` accesses **only** `Parent`. To access `GrandParent`, one must write `super.super.member` — but **this is INVALID in Java**. The workaround is:

```java
super.super.member   // ❌ Compilation Error in Java
```

To bypass this, the parent's method itself can use `super.member` to expose its own parent's members transitively.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Three Use Cases of `super`

```mermaid

    C --> C1["Invoke overridden parent method"]
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Variable Lookup Resolution with `super`

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP, Module 2]`**
*What is the `super` keyword in Java? List any two of its uses.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The `super` keyword in Java is a reference variable that refers to the **immediate parent class** of the current object. It is implicitly provided by the Java compiler and is used to differentiate between the members of the parent class and the subclass when name conflicts arise.

**Two uses of `super`:**
1. **`super.variableName`** — Used to access the parent class's instance variable when it is shadowed by a same-named variable in the subclass.
2. **`super.methodName()`** — Used to invoke the parent class's version of an overridden method from within the subclass.
3. *(Bonus, if asked for third)* — **`super(arguments)`** — Used to explicitly call a parent class constructor from the subclass constructor.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP, Module 2]`**
*Explain why `super()` must be the first statement in a constructor. What happens if it is not the first statement?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The `super()` call must be the first statement in a subclass constructor because Java's design enforces the **"Initialization-before-Use"** principle. The parent class's fields must be initialized **before** the subclass's own fields, ensuring that inherited members are ready to use when the subclass constructor body executes.

If `super()` is **not** the first statement, the compiler throws a **compile-time error** stating: *"Constructor call must be the first statement in a constructor."* This rule guarantees a strict, top-down initialization order throughout the entire inheritance hierarchy (Object → Parent → Child).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Comprehensive Long-Answer

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2 Internal Choice Q1]`**

**(a)** Explain the three uses of the `super` keyword in Java with suitable syntax and examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete Java program demonstrating how `super` is used to invoke an overridden method and to call a parameterized parent constructor. Show the expected output. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
The `super` keyword in Java is a reference variable provided implicitly by the compiler that refers to the immediate superclass (parent class) object. It is used to access hidden members of the parent class from within the subclass.

**Use 1 — `super.field` (2 Marks):**

When the subclass declares an instance variable with the same name as the parent class, the subclass variable shadows the parent's. The `super.field` syntax accesses the parent's variable.

```java
class A {
    int x = 10;
}
class B extends A {
    int x = 20;
    void display() {
        System.out.println("Child x  = " + x);        // 20
        System.out.println("Parent x = " + super.x);   // 10
    }
}
```

**Use 2 — `super.method()` (2 Marks):**

When the subclass overrides a parent method, `super.method()` invokes the parent's version. This is used to extend the parent's functionality rather than completely replace it.

```java
class A {
    void show() { System.out.println("Parent show"); }
}
class B extends A {
    void show() {
        super.show();    // Calls parent's show()
        System.out.println("Child show");
    }
}
```

**Use 3 — `super(args)` (2 Marks):**

Used to explicitly call a parent class constructor. This establishes a constructor chain ensuring proper initialization of inherited fields. It must be the first statement in the subclass constructor.

```java
class B extends A {
    B(int x) {
        super(x);   // Calls A's parameterized constructor
    }
}
```

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Complete Java Program:**

```java
// Parent class
class Account {
    String holderName;
    double balance;

    Account(String holderName, double balance) {
        this.holderName = holderName;
        this.balance = balance;
        System.out.println("Account constructor: " + holderName);
    }

    void display() {
        System.out.println("Holder: " + holderName + ", Balance: " + balance);
    }
}

// Child class
class SavingsAccount extends Account {
    double interestRate;

    SavingsAccount(String holderName, double balance, double interestRate) {
        super(holderName, balance);                // 1. Calling parent constructor
        this.interestRate = interestRate;
        System.out.println("SavingsAccount constructor called.");
    }

    @Override
    void display() {
        super.display();                           // 2. Calling parent overridden method
        System.out.println("Interest Rate: " + interestRate + "%");
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SavingsAccount sa = new SavingsAccount("Rahul", 50000, 4.5);
        sa.display();
    }
}
```

**Expected Output (1 Mark):**

```
Account constructor: Rahul
SavingsAccount constructor called.
Holder: Rahul, Balance: 50000.0
Interest Rate: 4.5%
```

**Explanation of Output (1 Mark):**
- Line 1 prints because `super(holderName, balance)` invokes the parent `Account` constructor first.
- Line 2 prints when control returns to `SavingsAccount` constructor.
- Line 3 prints when `super.display()` invokes the parent's `display()` method.
- Line 4 prints when control returns to the child's overridden `display()`.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Long-Answer

**`[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2 Internal Choice Q2]`**

**(a)** Differentiate between `this` and `super` keywords in Java with a comparative table and a code example. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain constructor chaining with the help of a three-level inheritance hierarchy (GrandParent → Parent → Child). Demonstrate how `super` propagates the call up the hierarchy. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Conceptual Difference (3 Marks):**
- **`this`** refers to the **current class** instance. It is used to disambiguate between local variables and instance variables, invoke overloaded constructors, and pass the current object as a parameter.
- **`super`** refers to the **immediate parent class** instance. It is used to access hidden parent members, invoke overridden parent methods, and call the parent constructor.

**Comparative Table (3 Marks):**

| **Aspect** | **`this`** | **`super`** |
|:---|:---|:---|
