| Refers to | Current class object | Parent class object |
| Variable access | Resolves name conflict with local variables | Resolves shadowed parent variable |
| Method call | `this.method()` → current class | `super.method()` → parent class |
| Constructor | `this(args)` → overloaded constructor in same class | `super(args)` → parent constructor |
| Placement | `this()` must be first in constructor | `super()` must be first in constructor |
| Static context | Not allowed | Not allowed |

**Code Example (1 Mark):**

```java
class Box {
    int length;
    Box(int length) {
        this.length = length;   // this refers to instance variable
    }
}
class ColoredBox extends Box {
    String color;
    ColoredBox(int length, String color) {
        super(length);          // super refers to parent's constructor
        this.color = color;     // this refers to instance variable
    }
}
```

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Conceptual Explanation (2 Marks):**

Constructor chaining is the process by which a constructor call propagates through the inheritance hierarchy. When a subclass object is created, the constructor of the topmost superclass is invoked first, followed by each subclass constructor in descending order. The `super()` call is the mechanism that enables this propagation.

**Three-Level Hierarchy Code (4 Marks):**

```java
class GrandParent {
    GrandParent() {
        System.out.println("GrandParent constructor");
    }
}

class Parent extends GrandParent {
    Parent() {
        super();   // Calls GrandParent constructor
        System.out.println("Parent constructor");
    }
}

class Child extends Parent {
    Child() {
        super();   // Calls Parent constructor (which in turn calls GrandParent)
        System.out.println("Child constructor");
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Child c = new Child();
    }
}
```

**Execution Flow Trace (1 Mark):**

$$
\texttt{new Child()} \;\Rightarrow\; \texttt{Child()} \;\Rightarrow\; \texttt{super()} \;\Rightarrow\; \texttt{Parent()} \;\Rightarrow\; \texttt{super()} \;\Rightarrow\; \texttt{GrandParent()}
$$

**Expected Output:**

```
GrandParent constructor
Parent constructor
Child constructor
```

**Key Insight (KTU Examiner's Tip):**
Even though we called `new Child()`, **all three constructors** executed — proving that `super()` establishes an automatic, top-down initialization chain.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make — Mark Loss Hotspots:**
> 1. **Forgetting the first-statement rule**: Writing `this.name = name;` before `super(name)` → **Compilation error, 2 marks lost**.
> 2. **Confusing `super` with `this`**: Using `super()` to call an overloaded constructor in the **same** class. → `super()` only calls the **parent**; for same-class, use `this()`.
> 3. **Trying `super.super`**: Students often write `super.super.x` in multi-level inheritance → **Not allowed in Java**. Lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Using `super` inside a `static` method**: Causes a compile error → Lose 2 marks.
> 5. **Forgetting constructor chain**: Declaring only a parameterized constructor in the parent without calling it explicitly from the child → Compile error "no suitable constructor found for super()". Lose 2 marks.
> 6. **Output-trace errors in multi-level chains**: Failing to list constructors in correct execution order → Lose up to 2 marks on Part B.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ `super` is a reference to the **immediate parent class**, not the grandparent.
- ✅ Three forms: `super.field`, `super.method()`, `super(args)`.
- ✅ `super(args)` **must** be the **first statement** in a subclass constructor.
- ✅ If no `super()` or `this()` is written, the compiler **auto-inserts** the no-arg `super()`.
- ✅ `super` and `this()` **cannot coexist** in the same constructor (both must be first).
- ✅ `super` **cannot** be used in `static` methods or `static` blocks.
- ✅ Constructor chaining goes **top-down**: Object → … → Parent → Child.
- ✅ `super` is used to **extend** parent behavior, not **replace** it (extend-and-add pattern).
- ✅ Java does **NOT** support `super.super.member` — only one level up per `super`.
- ✅ In the absence of a no-arg parent constructor, an explicit `super(args)` call is **mandatory**.
- ✅ Common interview question: *"Can you use `this()` and `super()` in the same constructor?"* → **No**, because both must occupy the first position.

> **Final Mnemonic for Exam Day:**
> **"S-U-P-E-R"**
> - **S**uper refers to parent
> - **U**niquely accesses hidden members
> - **P**osition is always first (for constructor calls)
> - **E**xplicit call is sometimes mandatory
> - **R**estricted to instance contexts only
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: protected Members
Calling Order of Constructors, Method Overriding
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Constructor Calling Order & Method Overriding

## 1.1 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constructor Calling Order (in Inheritance):** The deterministic sequence in which constructors of parent and child classes are invoked when an object of a derived class is instantiated. In Java/C++, the order is **top-down** along the class hierarchy (from the root superclass down to the most derived class), ensuring that inherited members are fully initialized before the subclass constructor body executes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Method Overriding:** A runtime polymorphism mechanism in which a subclass provides a specific implementation of a method that is **already declared** in its superclass. The overridden method in the child class must have the **same signature** (name, parameter list, and return type) as the parent method, enabling **Dynamic Method Dispatch**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping (PECIT633 – Module 2):**
> This topic falls under *Inheritance & Polymorphism* and directly maps to:
> - **CO2:** Apply object-oriented concepts such as inheritance, polymorphism, and abstract classes to model real-world systems.
> - **Bloom's Level:** Apply (L3) → Analyze (L4) → Evaluate (L5)

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition for First-Time Learners

### 🏗️ Analogy 1: Constructors → "Building a House from Roof Down"
Imagine constructing a multi-story building. Before the **2nd floor** (child class) can be built, the **1st floor** (parent class) foundation must be completely laid and inspected. Similarly:
- The **base/parent class** constructor runs **first** to initialize inherited fields.
- Then, the **derived class** constructor runs to initialize its own specialized fields.
- If the hierarchy is `Grandparent → Parent → Child`, the order is **Grandparent → Parent → Child**.

### 👨‍👩‍👧 Analogy 2: Method Overriding → "A Child Refining the Family Recipe"
A family recipe (method) is passed down from grandparent to parent. The child (subclass) decides to **override** the recipe with their own twist — same dish name (signature), different cooking technique (implementation). When someone serves the dish using the child's kitchen, the **child's version** is served, not the grandparent's.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology Glossary

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `super()` | Explicit call to the immediate parent class constructor |
| `super.method()` | Explicit call to the overridden parent method |
| Dynamic Dispatch | JVM selects the overridden method at **runtime** based on actual object type |
| Static Binding | Method call resolved at **compile time** (e.g., `static`, `private`, `final` methods) |
| Liskov Substitution | Subclass objects must be substitutable for superclass objects |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis

## 2.1 Constructor Calling Order — Operational Rules

### Step-by-Step Logic Flow:
1. **Trigger:** A `new ChildClass()` statement is encountered.
2. **Compiler Action:** Inserts an implicit `super()` call as the **first statement** of the child constructor (if no explicit `super()` is written).
3. **Recursive Upward:** This `super()` invokes the parent constructor, which in turn calls its own parent constructor — chain reaction up to `Object` class.
4. **Execution Downward:** Once the chain reaches `Object`, constructors execute **top-down** in reverse order: `Object → Grandparent → Parent → Child`.
5. **Object Ready:** After `Child` constructor finishes, the fully initialized object reference is returned.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Rule:** In Java, if a parent class has **no default (no-argument) constructor**, the child class **must** explicitly call `super(args)` as the first line, or a compile-time error occurs.

### Why This Order?
- Ensures **inherited fields** are initialized **before** the subclass uses them.
- Prevents access to **uninitialized** superclass members.
- Guarantees a **consistent and predictable object state**.

---

## 2.2 Method Overriding — Operational Rules

### Rules a KTU Examiner Strictly Checks:
1. **Same method name** in parent and child class.
2. **Identical parameter list** (number, type, order). Otherwise, it becomes *overloading*, not overriding.
3. **Covariant return type** allowed (Java 5+) — return type can be a subtype of the parent's return type.
4. **Access modifier** in child **cannot be more restrictive** than parent (e.g., `protected` → `private` is invalid).
5. **Cannot override `static`, `final`, or `private` methods** of the parent.
6. **Cannot override constructors** (constructors are not inherited).
7. **Use `@Override` annotation** for compile-time safety.

### Dynamic Method Dispatch (Runtime Polymorphism)
- Reference type is decided at **compile time**.
- Actual method invoked is decided at **runtime** based on the **object's actual type**, not the reference type.
- Implemented via the **JVM's vtable (virtual method table)** mechanism.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Aspect | Constructors | Method Overriding |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Initialize object state | Provide specific behavior in subclass |
| Inheritance | Not inherited, but called via `super()` | Inherited and can be overridden |
| Signature | Must match class name, no return type | Must match parent method signature |
| Binding Time | Compile-time call to `super()`, runtime execution order | Dynamic dispatch at **runtime** |
| Order Rule | Top-down (root → leaf) | Determined by object type at runtime |
| Access Rules | Cannot be `private` if class is `final` subclass | Cannot reduce visibility |
| `static` Allowed? | No static constructors | Cannot override `static` methods (can hide them) |
| `final` Allowed? | No `final` constructors | Cannot override `final` methods |

### Real-World Engineering Utility:
- **GUI Frameworks (Swing/JavaFX):** Frameworks call overridden `paint()` methods during rendering.
- **Enterprise Java (Spring):** Template Method pattern relies on constructor chaining and method overriding.
- **Game Development:** Character class hierarchies override `render()` or `update()` methods.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Trace & Analytical Walkthrough

## 3.1 Exhaustive Trace: Constructor Calling Order

### Java Code:
```java
class A {
    A() { System.out.println("A constructor"); }
}
class B extends A {
    B() { System.out.println("B constructor"); }
}
class C extends B {
    C() { System.out.println("C constructor"); }
}
public class Test {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        C obj = new C();
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Execution Trace:

| Step | Action | Output | Stack State |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `main()` executes `new C()` | — | main() |
| 2 | C() constructor invoked, implicitly calls `super()` → B() | — | main → C |
| 3 | B() constructor invoked, implicitly calls `super()` → A() | — | main → C → B |
| 4 | A() constructor invoked, implicitly calls `super()` → Object() | — | main → C → B → A |
| 5 | Object() constructor body executes | — | main → C → B → A |
| 6 | A() constructor body executes | `A constructor` | main → C → B |
| 7 | B() constructor body executes | `B constructor` | main → C |
| 8 | C() constructor body executes | `C constructor` | main |
| 9 | `obj` reference assigned, object returned | — | — |

### Final Output:
```
A constructor
B constructor
C constructor
```

### Mathematical Representation of Constructor Order:
Let $H = \{C_0, C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_n\}$ be the inheritance chain where $C_0$ is `Object` and $C_n$ is the most derived class. The execution order is:

$$
\text{Order} = (C_0 \rightarrow C_1 \rightarrow C_2 \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow C_n)
$$

Verified by:
$$
\text{For } n = 2: \quad \text{Order} = (\text{Object} \rightarrow A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C)
$$

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Trace: Method Overriding with Dynamic Dispatch

### Java Code:
```java
class Animal {
    void sound() { System.out.println("Animal makes a sound"); }
}
class Dog extends Animal {
    @Override
    void sound() { System.out.println("Dog barks"); }
}
class Cat extends Animal {
    @Override
    void sound() { System.out.println("Cat meows"); }
}
public class TestOverride {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Animal myAnimal;
        myAnimal = new Dog();
        myAnimal.sound();
        myAnimal = new Cat();
        myAnimal.sound();
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Execution:

| Step | Statement | Reference Type | Object Type | Method Called | Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `myAnimal = new Dog();` | `Animal` | `Dog` | — | — |
| 2 | `myAnimal.sound();` | `Animal` | `Dog` | `Dog.sound()` | `Dog barks` |
| 3 | `myAnimal = new Cat();` | `Animal` | `Cat` | — | — |
| 4 | `myAnimal.sound();` | `Animal` | `Cat` | `Cat.sound()` | `Cat meows` |

### Key Insight:
- **Compile time:** Compiler checks that `sound()` exists in `Animal` (reference type) — ✅ valid.
- **Runtime:** JVM looks at the **actual object** (`Dog`, then `Cat`) and invokes the overridden version.

### Dynamic Dispatch Formula:
Let $R$ = reference type, $O$ = actual object type, $M$ = method.

$$
\text{Method Invoked} = M_{\text{implementation of } O} \quad \text{where} \quad M \in R \land M \text{ is overridden in } O
$$

---

## 3.3 Advanced Scenario: Constructor + Overriding Combined

```java
class Parent {
    Parent() { 
        System.out.println("Parent constructor");
        show();
    }
    void show() { System.out.println("Parent show"); }
}
class Child extends Parent {
    int x = 10;
    Child() { 
        System.out.println("Child constructor, x = " + x);
    }
    @Override
    void show() { System.out.println("Child show, x = " + x); }
}
```

### Trace:

| Step | Action | Output |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `new Child()` triggers `super()` → `Parent()` | — |
| 2 | `Parent()` body: `System.out.println("Parent constructor")` | `Parent constructor` |
| 3 | `Parent()` body: `show()` → Dynamic dispatch → `Child.show()` | `Child show, x = 0` |
| 4 | `Parent()` returns, control back to `Child()` | — |
| 5 | `Child` instance variable `x` initialized to `10` | — |
| 6 | `Child()` body executes | `Child constructor, x = 10` |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Trap:** In Step 3, `x` prints as **0**, not 10! This is because `x` is initialized **after** the `Parent` constructor returns. Calling overridable methods from a constructor can lead to subtle bugs.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Constructor Calling Order — Class Hierarchy Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Object Class"] -->|"super() implicit"| B["Parent Class"]
    B -->|"super() implicit"| C["Child Class"]
    C -->|"new Child()"| D["Instance Created"]
    
    A -.->|Step 1: Execute| E["Object init complete"]
    B -.->|Step 2: Execute| F["Parent fields initialized"]
    C -.->|Step 3: Execute| G["Child fields initialized"]
    
    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#F0F0F0,stroke:#666
    style F fill:#F0F0F0,stroke:#666
    style G fill:#F0F0F0,stroke:#666
```

## 4.2 Method Overriding — Dynamic Dispatch Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Reference: Animal myAnimal"] -->|compile time check| B["Method sound exists in Animal"]
    A -->|runtime: actual object| C{Object Type}
    C -->|new Dog| D["Dog.sound() invoked"]
    C -->|new Cat| E["Cat.sound() invoked"]
    C -->|new Animal| F["Animal.sound() invoked"]
    
    D --> G["Output: Dog barks"]
    E --> H["Output: Cat meows"]
    F --> I["Output: Animal makes a sound"]
    
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
    style E fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style F fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333
```

## 4.3 Memory Layout — vtable (Virtual Method Table) Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Animal vtable"] -->|slot 0| B["Object.toString()"]
    A -->|slot 1| C["Object.equals()"]
    A -->|slot 2| D["Animal.sound()"]
    
    E["Dog vtable"] -->|slot 0| F["Object.toString()"]
    E -->|slot 1| G["Object.equals()"]
    E -->|slot 2| H["Dog.sound() OVERRIDDEN"]
    
    I["Cat vtable"] -->|slot 0| J["Object.toString()"]
    I -->|slot 1| K["Object.equals()"]
    I -->|slot 2| L["Cat.sound() OVERRIDDEN"]
    
    style H fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style L fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The JVM uses a **vtable per class** to resolve overridden methods. Each subclass gets its own vtable where overridden methods point to the subclass implementation, enabling O(1) dynamic dispatch.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

---

## 📝 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain the order in which constructors are invoked in a multi-level inheritance hierarchy with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**
In multi-level inheritance, when an object of the most derived class is created, constructors are invoked in a **top-down manner** starting from the root superclass down to the derived class. For example, in a hierarchy `A → B → C`, creating `new C()` invokes constructors in the order: `A() → B() → C()`. This ensures that inherited members are fully initialized before the subclass constructor body executes. The `super()` call (implicit or explicit) chains the constructor calls upward, and execution flows downward.

*(Marks: Definition 1M, Example 1M, Order explanation 1M)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is method overriding? List any four rules that must be followed while overriding a method.**

**Model Answer:**
**Method Overriding** is a mechanism where a subclass provides a specific implementation of a method already declared in its superclass, enabling runtime polymorphism.

**Rules:**
1. Method name and parameter list must be identical to the parent method.
2. Return type must be the same or covariant (subtype).
3. Access modifier cannot be more restrictive than the parent.
4. `static`, `final`, and `private` methods cannot be overridden.
5. Use `@Override` annotation for compile-time verification.

*(Marks: Definition 1M, Rules 2M — 0.5M each for any 4)*

---

---

## 📝 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of constructor calling order in inheritance with a Java program demonstrating multi-level inheritance. Trace the output step-by-step.

**Model Answer:**

**Concept:** In inheritance, when a derived class object is created, the Java compiler automatically inserts a `super()` call as the first statement of the subclass constructor. This triggers a chain reaction where parent constructors are invoked in sequence from the top of the hierarchy to the bottom, ensuring proper initialization.

**Java Program:**
```java
class Vehicle {
    Vehicle() { System.out.println("Vehicle constructor"); }
}
class Car extends Vehicle {
    Car() { System.out.println("Car constructor"); }
}
class SportsCar extends Car {
    SportsCar() { System.out.println("SportsCar constructor"); }
}
public class Test {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SportsCar sc = new SportsCar();
    }
}
```

**Step-by-Step Trace:**

| Step | Action | Output |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `new SportsCar()` triggers implicit `super()` → `Car()` | — |
| 2 | `Car()` triggers implicit `super()` → `Vehicle()` | — |
| 3 | `Vehicle()` body executes | `Vehicle constructor` |
| 4 | Control returns to `Car()` body | `Car constructor` |
| 5 | Control returns to `SportsCar()` body | `SportsCar constructor` |

**Final Output:**
```
Vehicle constructor
Car constructor
SportsCar constructor
```

**Conclusion:** Constructors execute in **top-down order** (root → leaf), guaranteeing that inherited members are initialized before the subclass uses them. *(7 Marks: Concept 2M, Code 2M, Trace 2M, Conclusion 1M)*

---

**b) [7 Marks]** What is method overriding? Write a Java program to demonstrate runtime polymorphism using method overriding. Explain dynamic method dispatch.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Method overriding occurs when a subclass provides a specific implementation of a method already defined in its superclass with the same signature.

**Java Program:**
```java
class Shape {
    void draw() { System.out.println("Drawing Shape"); }
    double area() { return 0; }
}
class Circle extends Shape {
    double radius = 5;
    @Override
    void draw() { System.out.println("Drawing Circle"); }
    @Override
    double area() { return Math.PI * radius * radius; }
}
class Rectangle extends Shape {
    double length = 4, width = 6;
    @Override
    void draw() { System.out.println("Drawing Rectangle"); }
    @Override
    double area() { return length * width; }
}
public class TestPoly {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Shape s;
        s = new Circle();
        s.draw();
        System.out.println("Area: " + s.area());
        s = new Rectangle();
        s.draw();
        System.out.println("Area: " + s.area());
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Drawing Circle
Area: 78.53981633974483
Drawing Rectangle
Area: 24.0
```

**Dynamic Method Dispatch Explanation:**
- At **compile time**, the compiler verifies that `draw()` and `area()` exist in the `Shape` reference type.
- At **runtime**, the JVM examines the **actual object type** (`Circle` or `Rectangle`) and invokes the overridden version.
- This is implemented using a **vtable (virtual method table)** per class, enabling O(1) method resolution.
- This mechanism is the foundation of **runtime polymorphism** in Java.

*(7 Marks: Definition 1M, Code 2M, Output 1M, Dispatch explanation 3M)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a Java example how the `super` keyword is used to call parent class constructors and overridden methods. Why is the `super()` call mandatory as the first statement?

**Model Answer:**

**Java Program Demonstrating `super` Keyword:**
```java
class Employee {
    String name;
    double salary;
    Employee(String name, double salary) {
        this.name = name;
        this.salary = salary;
        System.out.println("Employee constructor called");
    }
    void display() {
        System.out.println("Name: " + name + ", Salary: " + salary);
    }
}
class Manager extends Employee {
    String department;
    Manager(String name, double salary, String department) {
        super(name, salary); // Calling parent constructor
        this.department = department;
        System.out.println("Manager constructor called");
    }
    @Override
    void display() {
        super.display(); // Calling parent method
        System.out.println("Department: " + department);
    }
}
public class TestSuper {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Manager m = new Manager("Alice", 75000, "IT");
        m.display();
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Employee constructor called
Manager constructor called
Name: Alice, Salary: 75000.0
Department: IT
```

**Why `super()` Must Be First Statement:**
1. **Initialization Safety:** Parent class fields must be initialized before child class uses them.
2. **Java Language Specification:** The compiler enforces this rule to prevent accessing uninitialized superclass members.
3. **Consistency:** Ensures predictable object construction order across the inheritance hierarchy.
4. **Compile-time Enforcement:** If violated, Java throws a compile-time error: *"Constructor call must be the first statement in a constructor."*

*(7 Marks: Code 3M, Output 1M, Explanation 3M)*

---

**b) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between method overloading and method overriding with a comprehensive Java example. Create a table comparing at least six features.

**Model Answer:**

**Java Example:**
```java
class Calculator {
    // Method Overloading (compile-time polymorphism)
    int add(int a, int b) { return a + b; }
    double add(double a, double b) { return a + b; }
    int add(int a, int b, int c) { return a + b + c; }
}
class AdvancedCalculator extends Calculator {
    // Method Overriding (runtime polymorphism)
    @Override
    int add(int a, int b) { 
        System.out.println("AdvancedCalculator.add called");
        return a + b + 10; // Enhanced functionality
    }
}
public class TestOverloadOverride {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Calculator calc = new AdvancedCalculator();
        System.out.println(calc.add(5, 3));        // Overridden: 18
        System.out.println(calc.add(2.5, 3.5));    // Overloaded: 6.0
        System.out.println(calc.add(1, 2, 3));     // Overloaded: 6
    }
}
```

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Method Overloading | Method Overriding |
|---|---|---|
| Polymorphism Type | Compile-time (static) | Runtime (dynamic) |
| Class Scope | Same class | Across parent-child classes |
| Parameter List | Must differ | Must be identical |
| Return Type | Can differ | Must be same or covariant |
| Access Modifier | No restriction | Cannot be more restrictive |
| Inheritance Required | No | Yes |
| Binding Mechanism | Static binding | Dynamic dispatch |
| `static` Methods | Can be overloaded | Cannot be overridden (can be hidden) |
| `final` Methods | Can be overloaded | Cannot be overridden |
| Purpose | Increase readability, add flexibility | Provide specific behavior in subclass |

*(7 Marks: Code 2M, Output 1M, Table 4M — 0.5M per relevant row)*

---

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting `super()` in multi-level inheritance:** When the parent has only a parameterized constructor, students often forget to call `super(args)`, causing compile-time errors. **(-2 to -3 marks)**
> 2. **Confusing overloading with overriding:** Writing a method in the same class with different parameters is **overloading**, not overriding. Examiners deduct full marks for this confusion.
> 3. **Access modifier violation:** Reducing visibility (e.g., `public` → `private`) in the child class is a **compile-time error**. **(-2 marks)**
> 4. **Calling overridable methods from constructors:** This leads to subtle bugs where subclass fields appear uninitialized (as shown in Section 3.3).
> 5. **Missing `@Override` annotation:** While not mandatory, its absence can cause silent bugs if the method signature changes in the parent.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📌 Key Things to Remember (KTU 2024 Module 2):**

- ✅ **Constructor Order:** Top-down execution (root class → most derived class). `super()` chaining is implicit.
- ✅ **Method Overriding:** Same signature, covariant return types allowed, access cannot be more restrictive.
- ✅ **Dynamic Dispatch:** Runtime resolution based on **actual object type**, not reference type.
- ✅ **`super()` Rule:** Must be the **first statement** in a constructor (if used explicitly).
- ✅ **Cannot Override:** `static`, `final`, `private` methods, and constructors.
- ✅ **vtable Mechanism:** JVM uses virtual method tables for O(1) dynamic dispatch.
- ✅ **Trap to Avoid:** Calling overridable methods from constructors leads to uninitialized field access.
- ✅ **Annotation:** Always use `@Override` for compile-time safety and code clarity.
- ✅ **Liskov Substitution Principle:** Subclass objects must be substitutable for superclass references.
- ✅ **Common Exam Pattern:** Trace programs with multi-level inheritance + method overriding combined scenarios.

---

**🎓 KTU 2024 Preparation Tip:** Practice tracing **multi-level inheritance with constructor chaining** combined with **method overriding** — this is the most frequently asked 14-mark question pattern in KTU ESE examinations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamic Method Dispatch
Using final with Inheritance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECIT633 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Using final with Inheritance ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) In Java, the **`final`** keyword is a non-access modifier that can be applied to **classes, methods, and variables** to restrict modification. When used in the context of **inheritance**, the `final` keyword enforces **terminal restriction** in the class hierarchy — preventing further extension (for classes) or behavioral alteration (for methods) by any subclass. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus for **PECIT633 – Object Oriented Programming (Module 2: Inheritance & Polymorphism)**, the three applications of `final` with respect to inheritance are: | Application | Effect on Inheritance | KTU Definition | |---|---|---| | `final` class | Cannot be inherited (subclassed) | Sealed terminus of the hierarchy | | `final` method | Cannot be overridden in a subclass | Fixed behavior, inherited as-is | | `final` variable | Cannot be reassigned after initialization | Compile-time constant, used in inherited fields | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The `final` keyword with inheritance is essential for understanding **immutability**, **security**, and **design contract enforcement** in OOP. It is frequently tested in Part A (3-mark) questions and is a high-yield topic for Part B (14-mark) answers. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **royal family lineage** where the founding king issues three decrees: 1. **"This royal house shall not produce heirs"** → A **`final` class**. The lineage terminates; no future dynasty can be founded from this house. 2. **"This specific family tradition is sacred and unchangeable"** → A **`final` method**. Children can inherit the tradition but are forbidden from modifying how it is performed. 3. **"The family crest shall never be redrawn"** → A **`final` variable**. Its value is fixed the moment it is created and remains constant through every generation. This is exactly how Java treats `final` — it is a **decree of immutability** within the inheritance tree. > [!NOTE] > **Critical Distinction:** A `final` *method* CAN still be **inherited** by a subclass and **overloaded** with new parameters. It is **overriding** (changing the body) that is forbidden — not inheritance itself. ### 1.3 Why Java Provides the `final` Keyword — The Design Rationale - **Security:** Prevents malicious or accidental alteration of critical class behavior (e.g., `java.lang.String` is `final` so it cannot be spoofed). - **Immutability:** Enables thread-safe, predictable objects. - **Design Intent:** Communicates to other developers that this class/method is a complete, terminal implementation. - **Performance (Historical):** Allowed the JIT compiler to inline `final` methods directly. Modern JVMs are smarter, but the design intent remains. > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Misconception:** Many students think `final` makes a class "private" or "hidden." It does not! A `final` class can still be `public` and fully accessible — it just cannot be **extended**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three Pillars of `final` with Inheritance #### Pillar 1 — The `final` Class (Terminus of Hierarchy) **Syntax:** ```java final class ClassName { // class body } ``` **Effect:** A `final` class **cannot be subclassed**. Any attempt to use `extends` on a `final` class produces a **compile-time error**. **Real-world Java examples (must memorize for KTU):** - `java.lang.String` - `java.lang.Math` - All wrapper classes: `Integer`, `Double`, `Boolean`, `Long`, `Float`, `Character`, `Byte`, `Short` - `java.lang.System` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Favourite Question:** *"Why is the String class declared as final in Java?"* — Answer: to prevent subclassing that could compromise the immutability, security, and hashcode behaviour of String objects (especially in the String Constant Pool). **Rules & Restrictions for `final` classes:** | Rule | Status | |---|---| | Can a `final` class be instantiated? | **Yes** ✅ | | Can a `final` class have a constructor? | **Yes** ✅ | | Can a `final` class be `abstract`? | **No** ❌ (compile error — contradictory modifiers) | | Can a `final` class be inherited (extended)? | **No** ❌ | | Can a `final` class implement an interface? | **Yes** ✅ | | Can a `final` class contain `final` methods? | **Yes** (redundant but valid) | | Can a `final` class contain non-final methods? | **Yes** ✅ | #### Pillar 2 — The `final` Method (Sealed Behaviour) **Syntax:** ```java class Parent { final void display() { System.out.println("This cannot be overridden."); } } ``` **Effect:** A `final` method is **inherited normally** by all subclasses but **cannot be overridden** (redefined with the same signature) in any subclass. **Rules & Restrictions for `final` methods:** | Rule | Status | |---|---| | Can a `final` method be inherited? | **Yes** ✅ | | Can a `final` method be overridden? | **No** ❌ | | Can a `final` method be overloaded? | **Yes** ✅ (different parameter list) | | Can a `final` method be hidden (static final)? | Static methods are not overridden anyway — they are hidden | | Can constructors be `final`? | **No** ❌ (constructors are never inherited, so `final` is meaningless) | | Can a `final` method be `abstract`? | **No** ❌ (contradictory — `abstract` must be overridden, `final` forbids it) | > [!NOTE] > **Template Method Pattern:** The `final` method is the cornerstone of the **Template Method design pattern** in OOP. The base class defines the algorithm skeleton as `final`, while allowing subclasses to override specific steps (which are non-final). #### Pillar 3 — The `final` Variable (Compile-Time Constant) **Syntax:** ```java final int MAX_VALUE = 100; // final instance variable static final double PI = 3.14159; // final static variable (constant) final void method(final int x) {} // final parameter ``` **Effect:** A `final` variable can be assigned **only once**. After that, it becomes a read-only constant. **Special case — Blank Final Variable:** A `final` variable declared without initialization must be initialized in **every constructor** of the class (a KTU favourite tricky question). ### 2.2 KTU Formula / Rule Cheat Sheet | Concept | Syntax Trigger | Compile-Time Rule | Runtime Rule | |---|---|---|---| | Final Class | `final class X` | Cannot use `extends X` | N/A | | Final Method | `final void m()` | Cannot override `m()` | Method is dispatched normally | | Final Variable | `final int x = 5;` | Must be assigned exactly once | Read-only | | Final Parameter | `void m(final int x)` | Cannot reassign `x` inside method | Read-only inside scope | | Final + Abstract | `final abstract` | Compile error | N/A | | Final + Static | `static final` | Valid — creates a global constant | Stored in class method area | | Constructor + Final | `final Constructor()` | Compile error | N/A | ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Banking Systems:** Account base class can declare `final double MIN_BALANCE = 500.0;` — preventing any subclass from lowering the minimum balance invariant. - **Cryptographic Libraries:** The `Key` class in `java.security` is final to prevent forging of cryptographic identities. - **GUI Frameworks:** JavaFX and Swing often use `final` methods internally to lock down event-dispatch behaviour. - **Embedded & Real-Time Systems:** The `final` keyword guarantees deterministic behaviour, vital in safety-critical systems (automotive, aerospace, medical devices). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Analytical Trace — The Decision Tree When the Java compiler encounters a `final` declaration in an inheritance context, it applies the following logical decision sequence: $$ \text{Compiler Decision Tree for `final` in Inheritance} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Is the declaration a CLASS?} \rightarrow \text{Yes} &\Rightarrow \text{Scan for `extends` clauses in entire codebase.} \\ &\Rightarrow \text{If any subclass exists} \Rightarrow \textbf{CompileError: cannot inherit from final} \\ \text{Is the declaration a METHOD?} \rightarrow \text{Yes} &\Rightarrow \text{Scan all subclasses for method with same signature.} \\ &\Rightarrow \text{If override detected} \Rightarrow \textbf{CompileError: cannot override the final method} \\ \text{Is the declaration a VARIABLE?} \rightarrow \text{Yes} &\Rightarrow \text{Scan all assignment statements in scope.} \\ &\Rightarrow \text{If reassignment detected} \Rightarrow \textbf{CompileError: cannot assign a value to final variable} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > All three decisions happen at **compile time**, not runtime. This makes `final` a powerful static-analysis and design-contract tool. ### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Final Class (Exhaustive Trace) **Source Code:** ```java // File: Vehicle.java final class Vehicle { int speed = 60; void run() { System.out.println("Vehicle is running"); } } // File: Car.java class Car extends Vehicle { // ❌ LINE 6 — COMPILE ERROR public static void main(String[] args) { Vehicle v = new Vehicle(); v.run(); } } ``` **Compiler Trace (Step-by-Step):** | Step | Compiler Action | Outcome | |---|---|---| | 1 | Lexer scans `Vehicle.java` | Token `final` recognized before `class` | | 2 | Symbol table stores `Vehicle` with flag `isFinal = true` | Final flag set | | 3 | Lexer scans `Car.java` | Token `extends` recognized | | 4 | Compiler checks `Vehicle` in symbol table | `isFinal = true` detected | | 5 | Compiler emits diagnostic | `error: cannot inherit from final Vehicle` | | 6 | Build halts | `Car.class` is not produced | **Resolution:** Either remove `final` from `Vehicle` or redesign `Car` to **use composition** (has-a) instead of inheritance (is-a). ```java class Car { private Vehicle v = new Vehicle(); // ✅ Composition (HAS-A) public static void main(String[] args) { Car c = new Car(); c.v.run(); } } ``` ### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Final Method (Exhaustive Trace) **Source Code:** ```java class BankAccount { final void calculateInterest() { // LOCKED behaviour System.out.println("Standard interest = 4%"); } } class SavingsAccount extends BankAccount { @Override void calculateInterest() { // ❌ COMPILE ERROR System.out.println("Custom interest = 6%"); } } ``` **Compiler Trace (Step-by-Step):** | Step | Compiler Action | Outcome | |---|---|---| | 1 | Parse `BankAccount` | Method `calculateInterest` marked `isFinal = true` | | 2 | Parse `SavingsAccount extends BankAccount` | Inheritance established | | 3 | Encounter `@Override` annotation | Compiler begins override verification | | 4 | Compare signatures: `(void calculateInterest())` vs `(void calculateInterest())` | Exact match → considered override | | 5 | Check `isFinal` flag of parent method | `true` detected | | 6 | Emit diagnostic | `error: calculateInterest() in SavingsAccount cannot override calculateInterest() in BankAccount; overridden method is final` | **Resolution Options:** - **Option A:** Remove `final` from parent's method. - **Option B:** Remove the override in the child (inherit as-is). - **Option C:** Rename the child's method (this is **overloading by new name**, not overriding). ### 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Final Variable in Inheritance **Source Code:** ```java class Company { final String COMPANY_NAME = "KTU Pvt Ltd"; } class Branch extends Company { void changeName() { COMPANY_NAME = "New Name"; // ❌ COMPILE ERROR } } ``` **Compiler Trace:** | Step | Compiler Action | Outcome | |---|---|---| | 1 | `COMPANY_NAME` declared with `final` | Field is read-only | | 2 | Memory is allocated and value `"KTU Pvt Ltd"` assigned at object construction | One-time initialization | | 3 | `Branch` inherits `COMPANY_NAME` | Field accessible, value is `"KTU Pvt Ltd"` | | 4 | `Branch.changeName()` attempts reassignment | Compiler sees `=` operator on a `final` field | | 5 | Emit diagnostic | `error: cannot assign a value to final variable COMPANY_NAME` | > [!TIP] > **KTU Trick Question:** *"Can a subclass access a private final variable of its parent?"* — **No**, because `private` restricts access even to subclasses. The subclass must use a `public/protected` getter. ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Combining `final` with Other Modifiers | Combination | Valid? | Reason | |---|---|---| | `final` + `abstract` (class) | ❌ | Contradictory — `abstract` mandates subclassing, `final` forbids it | | `final` + `abstract` (method) | ❌ | Same contradiction | | `final` + `static` (variable) | ✅ | Creates a global constant (e.g., `Math.PI`) | | `final` + `static` (method) | ✅ | Valid; cannot be overridden nor hidden by instance methods | | `final` + `private` (method) | ✅ | Redundant — `private` methods are already non-overridable | | `final` + `synchronized` (method) | ✅ | Common in multi-threaded design | | `final` + `native` (method) | ✅ | Used in JNI (Java Native Interface) | | `final constructor` | ❌ | Constructors are never inherited, so `final` is meaningless | | `final` + `interface` | ❌ | All interfaces are inherently abstract | | `final` + `enum` | ❌ | All enums are implicitly final | ### 3.6 Blank Final Variable — Special Inheritance Case A **blank final** is a `final` variable declared without an initializer. In the inheritance context, every constructor of every subclass must eventually initialize it (typically through `super()`). ```java class Person { final int id; // blank final Person(int id) { this.id = id; // ✅ initialized here } } class Student extends Person { Student(int id) { super(id); // ✅ passes id upward to initialize parent's blank final } } ``` **Compiler Trace:** | Step | Action | Outcome | |---|---|---| | 1 | `Person` constructor initializes `id` | Blank final assigned exactly once | | 2 | `Student` calls `super(id)` | Constructor chain reaches `Person` | | 3 | `Person` constructor assigns `id` | Final field now has a value | | 4 | Any further assignment in `Student` | ❌ Compile error | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Decision Flow of `final` in Inheritance ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Encounter keyword: final] --> B{What is being
declared?} B -- CLASS --> C[Mark class as
isFinal = true] B -- METHOD --> D[Mark method as
isFinal = true] B -- VARIABLE --> E[Mark variable as
isFinal = true] C --> F[Compiler checks:
any extends clause?] F -- YES found --> G[COMPILE ERROR:
cannot inherit from final] F -- NONE found --> H[Valid: Class can be
instantiated normally] D --> I[Compiler checks:
any subclass override?] I -- YES override found --> J[COMPILE ERROR:
cannot override final method] I -- NONE found --> K[Valid: Method is
inherited as-is] E --> L[Compiler checks:
any reassignment?] L -- YES reassign --> M[COMPILE ERROR:
cannot assign to final] L -- NO reassign --> N[Valid: Variable is
read-only constant] H --> O[Program compiles
and runs] K --> O N --> O ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Class Hierarchy — Effect of `final` in Hierarchy ```mermaid classDiagram class Animal { +final int MAX_AGE +final void breathe() +void sound() } note for Animal "Final method breathe() cannot be overridden\nFinal variable MAX_AGE is constant" class Dog { +void sound() } note for Dog "Dog CAN override sound()\nDog CANNOT override breathe()\nDog CANNOT reassign MAX_AGE" class Puppy { } note for Puppy "Puppy inherits all final\nand non-final members as-is" class LockedCreature { } note for LockedCreature "❌ Compile error:\ncannot extend Animal\nbecause Animal is final" Animal <-- Dog : extends Animal <-- Puppy : extends Animal <-- LockedCreature : BLOCKED (final) ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Template Method Using `final` ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Client participant Base as AbstractReport (Template) participant Child as PDFReport (Subclass) Client->>Base: generateReport() activate Base Base->>Base: final void printHeader() [LOCKED] Note over Base: Header logic cannot be changed Base->>Child: abstract void formatBody() activate Child Child->>Child: formatBody() [CUSTOM] deactivate Child Base->>Base: final void printFooter() [LOCKED] deactivate Base Note over Client,Child: Only the middle step is customizable;\nheader and footer are sealed by final ``` ### 4.4 Mermaid State Diagram — Lifecycle of a `final` Variable ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Declared : final int x Declared --> BlankFinal : No initializer Declared --> Initialized : x = 10 BlankFinal --> Initialized : Assigned in constructor
(only once allowed) Initialized --> ReadOnly : Locked for life ReadOnly --> [*] Initialized --> CompileError : Attempted reassignment CompileError --> [*] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **State any three differences between a `final` class and an `abstract` class in Java.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | # | `final` Class | `abstract` Class | |---|---|---| | 1 | Declared using the `final` keyword; cannot be inherited (no subclass can extend it). | Declared using the `abstract` keyword; **must** be inherited to be used. | | 2 | All methods can have a concrete implementation; no method is required to be abstract. | Can contain both abstract methods (no body) and concrete methods. | | 3 | Objects of a `final` class **can** be directly instantiated (e.g., `new String("abc")`). | Objects **cannot** be instantiated directly; only its concrete subclasses can be instantiated. | | 4 | Cannot be combined with `abstract` modifier — causes compile error. | Can be combined with `final`? **No** — they are contradictory. | **Mark Distribution:** 1 mark per difference × 3 = 3 marks ✅ --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Explain what happens when we try to override a `final` method in Java. Give a suitable example.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** When a method is declared as `final` in a parent class, **any attempt to override it in a subclass results in a compile-time error**. The compiler emits a diagnostic message: *"overridden method is final"*. **Example (2 marks for code + 1 mark for explanation):** ```java class Parent { final void display() { // final method System.out.println("Parent display"); } } class Child extends Parent { void display() { // ❌ COMPILE ERROR System.out.println("Child display"); } } ``` **Explanation:** The `display()` method in `Parent` is sealed with `final`. Java forbids the `Child` class from providing a different implementation, ensuring the parent's behaviour is preserved throughout the inheritance chain. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question has two sub-parts: (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]** **(a)** Explain the concept of the `final` keyword in Java. Discuss in detail the use of `final` with classes and methods, with appropriate code examples. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write a Java program to demonstrate that: - (i) A `final` method **can** be inherited and overloaded but **cannot** be overridden. **(3 marks)** - (ii) A `final` variable behaves as a constant and cannot be reassigned. **(2 marks)** - (iii) Attempting to extend a `final` class produces a compile-time error. **(2 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question A:** **Part (a) — 7 marks** **Definition (2 marks):** The `final` keyword in Java is a non-access modifier used to restrict modification. When applied to a class, it prevents inheritance. When applied to a method, it prevents overriding. When applied to a variable, it makes the variable a constant. **Final Class — Explanation + Code (2.5 marks):** ```java final class MathConstants { // sealed class static final double PI = 3.14159; static final double E = 2.71828; } // class Geometry extends MathConstants { } // ❌ COMPILE ERROR ``` Any class declared `final` cannot be subclassed. The Java standard library uses this for `String`, `Math`, and all wrapper classes to maintain immutability and security. **Final Method — Explanation + Code (2.5 marks):** ```java class Account { final void welcomeMessage() { // sealed behaviour System.out.println("Welcome to KTU Bank."); } } class SavingsAccount extends Account { // void welcomeMessage() { } // ❌ COMPILE ERROR void customMessage() { // ✅ allowed: new method System.out.println("Savings account benefits apply."); } } ``` A `final` method is inherited as-is by subclasses. It can be **overloaded** (new parameters) but not **overridden** (same signature). **Mark split for part (a):** Definition 2 + Final Class 2.5 + Final Method 2.5 = **7 marks** ✅ --- **Part (b) — 7 marks** **(i) Final method — inherit, overload, but no override (3 marks):** ```java class Shape { final void draw() { // final method System.out.println("Drawing a shape."); } final void draw(int sides) { // overloading the final method ✅ System.out.println("Drawing a shape with " + sides + " sides."); } } class Circle extends Shape { // void draw() { } // ❌ cannot override void draw(String colour) { // ✅ can overload further System.out.println("Drawing a " + colour + " circle."); } } ``` **Mark split:** Code 2 marks + Explanation 1 mark = **3 marks** ✅ **(ii) Final variable — constant behaviour (2 marks):** ```java class Config { static final String APP_NAME = "KTU-OOP"; // global constant final int MAX_USERS; // blank final Config(int max) { this.MAX_USERS = max; } // assigned once } public class TestConfig { public static void main(String[] args) { Config c = new Config(100); System.out.println(c.MAX_USERS); // 100 // c.MAX_USERS = 200; // ❌ COMPILE ERROR } } ``` **Mark split:** Code 1.5 marks + Explanation 0.5 mark = **2 marks** ✅ **(iii) Extending a final class (2 marks):** ```java final class SecurityToken { String token = "abc123"; } class FakeToken extends SecurityToken { // ❌ COMPILE ERROR // error: cannot inherit from final SecurityToken } ``` **Mark split:** Code 1.5 marks + Error explanation 0.5 mark = **2 marks** ✅ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** What is the significance of the `final` keyword in inheritance? Explain with real-world examples from the Java standard library. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Consider the following scenario: ```java class University { final String UNIVERSITY_NAME = "APJ Abdul Kalam Technological University"; final void displayMotto() { System.out.println("Inspiring Innovation."); } } ``` Write a Java program that: - (i) Creates a subclass `Department` that attempts to override `displayMotto()`. Show the compile error. **(2 marks)** - (ii) Creates a subclass `Department` that attempts to reassign `UNIVERSITY_NAME`. Show the compile error. **(2 marks)** - (iii) Creates a `final` class `ExaminationCell` and another class attempting to extend it. Show the compile error. **(3 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question B:** **Part (a) — 7 marks** The `final` keyword enforces **design contracts** within an inheritance hierarchy. It guarantees that critical behaviour or structure cannot be altered by subclasses, ensuring **security, immutability, and predictability**. **Real-world examples from Java standard library (5 marks):** 1. **`java.lang.String`** — Declared `final` to prevent subclasses from altering hash-code behaviour, which would break the `HashMap` and `HashSet` data structures that rely on `String.hashCode()`. **(1.5 marks)** 2. **`java.lang.Math`** — Declared `final` because all its members are `static` (utility methods). Allowing subclassing would enable overriding static methods in confusing ways. **(1.5 marks)** 3. **Wrapper classes (`Integer`, `Long`, `Double`, `Boolean`, etc.)** — Declared `final` to enforce immutability, which is critical for caching and pooling. **(1 mark)** 4. **`java.lang.System`** — Declared `final` because it represents the system itself; subclassing it would be semantically meaningless and dangerous. **(1 mark)** **Mark split:** Introduction 2 marks + Examples 5 marks = **7 marks** ✅ --- **Part (b) — 7 marks** **(i) Override final method (2 marks):** ```java class Department extends University { @Override void displayMotto() { // ❌ COMPILE ERROR System.out.println("New motto."); } } ``` **Compiler error:** ``` error: displayMotto() in Department cannot override displayMotto() in University overridden method is final ``` **Mark split:** Code 1.5 marks + Error message 0.5 mark = **2 marks** ✅ **(ii) Reassign final variable (2 marks):** ```java class Department extends University { Department() { UNIVERSITY_NAME = "Some Other University"; // ❌ COMPILE ERROR } } ``` **Compiler error:** ``` error: cannot assign a value to final variable UNIVERSITY_NAME ``` **Mark split:** Code 1.5 marks + Error message 0.5 mark = **2 marks** ✅ **(iii) Extend final class (3 marks):** ```java final class ExaminationCell { void conductExam() { System.out.println("Exams are being conducted."); } } class ResultCell extends ExaminationCell { // ❌ COMPILE ERROR // ... } ``` **Compiler error:** ``` error: cannot inherit from final ExaminationCell ``` **Mark split:** Code 2 marks + Error message + explanation 1 mark = **3 marks** ✅ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Marks are deducted (typically 1 mark per error)** if students confuse `final` with `finally` (exception handler) or `finalize()` (Garbage Collection method). These are three **completely different** Java keywords/methods. > 2. **Loses 0.5–1 mark** if the student writes that a `final` class "cannot be instantiated" — it CAN. The restriction is on **subclassing**, not instantiation. > 3. **Loses 0.5 mark** if the student claims `final` methods cannot be overloaded — they CAN (with different parameters). Only **overriding** is forbidden. > 4. **Loses 1 mark** if no real-world example is cited (e.g., `String`, `Math`, wrapper classes) in a "significance" question. > 5. **Loses 0.5 mark** if the exact compile-time error message is not reproduced accurately — KTU examiners expect the precise diagnostic wording. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute KTU Revision Summary — Memorize Before Exam:** - ⭐ **`final` class** → Cannot be extended. Examples: `String`, `Math`, all 8 wrapper classes, `System`. - ⭐ **`final` method** → Cannot be overridden, but **can** be inherited and **overloaded**. - ⭐ **`final` variable** → Can be assigned **exactly once**; after that, read-only. - ⭐ **`final` + `abstract`** → **Impossible** (contradictory modifiers — compile error). - ⭐ **Constructors** → Can **never** be `final` (they aren't inherited). - ⭐ **Interfaces** → Can **never** be `final` (all interfaces are implicitly abstract). - ⭐ **Enums** → Are **implicitly final**; cannot be declared `final` again. - ⭐ **Blank final variable** → Must be initialized in **every constructor**. - ⭐ **Compile-time enforcement** → All `final` violations are caught at compile time, not runtime. - ⭐ **Three completely different keywords** → `final` (modifier), `finally` (exception block), `finalize()` (GC method) — **do not confuse**. - ⭐ **Template Method Pattern** → The classic OOP design use of `final` methods. - ⭐ **KTU favourite code pattern** → A class extending `String` or `Math` — always a compile error. - ⭐ **Security reasoning** → The most common KTU theory answer: "to prevent subclassing and ensure immutability/security." - ⭐ **Default values** → A `final` instance variable **does not** get a default value (unlike non-final fields); it must be explicitly assigned. - ⭐ **Final parameters** → Useful for inner classes (anonymous classes can only access `final` or effectively final local variables). --- > **END OF NOTES — PECIT633 Module 2: Using `final` with Inheritance** > *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme | B.Tech CSE / Allied Branches*

topics.title: Packages and Interfaces - Defining a Package
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT633 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Packages and Interfaces - Defining a Package

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> A **package** in Java is a *namespace* that organizes a set of related **classes**, **interfaces**, **enumerations**, and **sub-packages**. It is a *mechanism for encapsulation* that provides a logical grouping of types that are semantically related, thereby preventing name collisions, controlling access visibility, and enabling easier location and reuse of code.

Formally, a package acts as both:
1. A **logical grouping mechanism** (developer perspective) — used to organize related source code units under a common name.
2. A **physical mapping mechanism** (filesystem perspective) — a Java package corresponds directly to a **directory/folder** structure in the underlying operating system.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Keyword:** The *defining statement* of a package is the single line: `package packageName;` — this statement **must be the first non-comment, non-whitespace line** in the source file.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Postal System"

Imagine the **Indian Postal System** as an analogy:

| Postal System Component | Java Package Equivalent |
|---|---|
| **Country → State → City → Street → House** | **Package hierarchy:** `com.ktu.oop.module3` |
| Two houses can have the same door number in *different cities* without conflict | Two classes can have the same name in *different packages* (e.g., `java.util.Date` and `java.sql.Date`) |
| A registered post requires knowing the *complete address* | A Java class requires its *Fully Qualified Name (FQN)*, e.g., `com.ktu.oop.MyClass` |
| Mail sorting by regional hubs | Compilation by package directory mapping |

Just as a letter addressed to "House 12, MG Road" is ambiguous (which state? which city?), a Java class named `Date` is ambiguous unless qualified by its package. **Packages provide that unambiguous address.**

---

## 1.3 Why Packages Exist — The Four Pillars

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently award marks for listing the *reasons* for using packages. Memorize these **four pillars**:

1. **Logical Organization** — Group related classes (e.g., all GUI classes in `javax.swing`).
2. **Name Conflict Resolution (Namespace Management)** — Allows two classes with the same simple name to coexist in different packages.
3. **Access Protection (Encapsulation at Package Level)** — Provides *package-private* (default) access modifier.
4. **Reusability and Distribution** — Facilitates modular code distribution (e.g., JAR files).

---

## 1.4 Physical & Logical Mapping

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule of KTU Examinations:**
> The package name **must exactly mirror** the directory structure on the file system (separated by dots `.` in code, by slashes `/` or `\` on disk).

For example, the declaration `package com.ktu.oop.module3;` requires the file to be located at:
```
com/ktu/oop/module3/MyClass.java
```

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference

## 2.1 Classification of Packages

Java packages fall into two broad categories:

### A. Built-in (Predefined) Packages
Bundled with the **Java Development Kit (JDK)**. Key examples to memorize:

| Package | Purpose | Critical Classes |
|---|---|---|
| `java.lang` | Core language fundamentals (auto-imported) | `String`, `Math`, `Object`, `System` |
| `java.util` | Utility classes and data structures | `ArrayList`, `HashMap`, `Scanner`, `Date` |
| `java.io` | Input/Output streams | `File`, `FileReader`, `BufferedReader` |
| `java.sql` | Database access (JDBC) | `Connection`, `Statement`, `ResultSet` |
| `javax.swing` | GUI components | `JFrame`, `JButton`, `JPanel` |
| `java.net` | Networking | `Socket`, `URL`, `InetAddress` |
| `java.applet` | Applet programming | `Applet`, `AudioClip` |

> [!NOTE]
> **`java.lang` is the only package that is automatically imported** into every Java program. For all other built-in packages, you must use an `import` statement.

### B. User-Defined Packages
Packages created by the programmer to organize their own application code. **This is the primary focus of the KTU Module 3 syllabus under "Defining a Package".**

---

## 2.2 Syntax Rules for Defining a Package

The general syntax for defining a package is:

```java
package <packageName>;
```

where `<packageName>` is a valid Java identifier using **dot-separated hierarchical naming**, e.g., `com.ktu.cse.s3.oop`.

### Mandatory Rules (High-Frequency Exam Content):

1. The `package` statement **must be the first executable line** of the source file.
2. **Only one `package` statement** is allowed per source file (i.e., a class belongs to exactly one package).
3. The **directory hierarchy must match** the dotted package name.
4. The **source file name must match** the `public` class name (if the class is declared public).
5. Package names are conventionally written in **all lowercase letters**.
6. By convention, package names begin with the **reverse domain name** of the organization (e.g., `com.ktu` for APJ Abdul Kalam Technological University).

---

## 2.3 The CLASSPATH Variable

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CLASSPATH** is an environment variable that tells the **Java compiler (`javac`)** and the **Java Virtual Machine (`java`)** *where to search for user-defined package class files* (`.class` files).

Without proper CLASSPATH configuration, the JVM will fail with a `NoClassDefFoundError` when attempting to run classes from user-defined packages.

### CLASSPATH Reference Table

| Element | Description |
|---|---|
| **Default value** | Current working directory (`.`) |
| **Syntax (Windows)** | `set CLASSPATH=.;C:\myPackages` |
| **Syntax (Linux/Mac)** | `export CLASSPATH=.:/home/user/myPackages` |
| **Compile-time use** | Resolves references to classes in other packages |
| **Run-time use** | Locates compiled `.class` files at execution |
| **Modern alternative** | Use `-cp` or `--class-path` flag with `javac`/`java` commands |

> [!NOTE]
> The **dot (`.`)** in the CLASSPATH represents the *current directory*. It is **mandatory** to include it so that classes in the current working directory are discoverable.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Syntax / Rule | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Package declaration | `package a.b.c;` | `package com.ktu.oop;` |
| Importing a class | `import a.b.c.ClassName;` | `import java.util.Scanner;` |
| Importing entire package | `import a.b.c.*;` | `import java.io.*;` |
| Fully Qualified Name usage | `a.b.c.ClassName obj = new a.b.c.ClassName();` | `java.util.Date d = new java.util.Date();` |
| Static import | `import static java.lang.Math.*;` | `sqrt(25);` instead of `Math.sqrt(25);` |
| Directory mapping | `a.b.c` → `a/b/c/` | `com.ktu.oop` → `com/ktu/oop/` |
| File name rule | `public class X` must be in `X.java` | `MyClass.java` contains `public class MyClass` |
| Auto-imported package | `java.lang` only | No `import` needed for `String` |
| Default access level | Package-private (no modifier) | `int x;` is visible only within the same package |
| CLASSPATH separator | `:` (Unix) / `;` (Windows) | `.:/home/lib;C:\lib` |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In professional software engineering (relevant for KTU B.Tech placements and industry), packages enable:

- **Microservices Architecture** — Each service is a package/module.
- **Library Distribution** — Java's standard library itself is a set of packages distributed as `rt.jar` / `modules`.
- **Team Collaboration** — Multiple developers can work on `com.company.frontend` and `com.company.backend` packages in parallel without merge conflicts on class names.
- **Versioning & Modularization** — With the Java Platform Module System (JPMS, Java 9+), packages form the basis of modular deployment.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Procedure, Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Complete Procedure for Defining a User-Defined Package

The following is the **exhaustive, KTU-board-style step-by-step procedure** to define, compile, and execute a class within a user-defined package. Every step is mandatory and may be asked as a 7-mark sub-question in the ESE (End Semester Examination).

### STEP 1: Choose a Package Name
Decide on a logically meaningful, dot-separated, all-lowercase package name.
Example: `com.ktu.oop.geometry`

### STEP 2: Create the Directory Hierarchy
On the filesystem, create folders matching the dot-separated package name. Each dot (`.`) becomes a directory separator (`/` on Unix/Mac, `\` on Windows).

```text
myproject/
└── com/
    └── ktu/
        └── oop/
            └── geometry/
```

### STEP 3: Write the Java Source File
Create a Java file inside the deepest directory. The `package` statement **must be the very first line** (after any license comment, but before any `import` or `class` declaration).

**File: `myproject/com/ktu/oop/geometry/Circle.java`**

```java
package com.ktu.oop.geometry;

public class Circle {
    private double radius;

    public Circle(double radius) {
        this.radius = radius;
    }

    public double area() {
        return Math.PI * radius * radius;
    }

    public double perimeter() {
        return 2 * Math.PI * radius;
    }
}
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Subtle KTU Pitfall:** The directory path `com/ktu/oop/geometry/` mirrors the package declaration `com.ktu.oop.geometry`. If the file is placed in the *wrong* directory, the class will be **silently placed in the wrong package** at runtime, causing `ClassNotFoundException`.

### STEP 4: Create a Client Class (in a Different Package)
To demonstrate cross-package access, create a client that uses `Circle`. Place it in a *different* package.

**File: `myproject/com/ktu/test/TestCircle.java`**

```java
package com.ktu.test;

import com.ktu.oop.geometry.Circle;   // Explicit single-class import

public class TestCircle {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Circle c = new Circle(5.0);
        System.out.println("Area = " + c.area());
        System.out.println("Perimeter = " + c.perimeter());
    }
}
```

### STEP 5: Compile the Source Files
Open a terminal, navigate to the **root** directory (`myproject/`), and compile using `javac` with the `-d` flag to specify the output directory for `.class` files.

```bash
cd myproject
javac -d . com/ktu/oop/geometry/Circle.java
javac -d . com/ktu/test/TestCircle.java
```

The `-d .` flag instructs `javac` to **recreate the package directory structure** under the current directory and place the compiled `.class` files accordingly. The result is:

```text
myproject/
├── com/
│   ├── ktu/
│   │   ├── oop/
│   │   │   └── geometry/
│   │   │       └── Circle.class
│   │   └── test/
│   │       └── TestCircle.class
```

### STEP 6: Execute the Program
Run the class from the **root directory** using its *fully qualified name*:

```bash
java com.ktu.test.TestCircle
```

**Expected Output:**
```text
Area = 78.53981633974483
Perimeter = 31.41592653589793
```

### STEP 7: (Alternative) Set CLASSPATH Manually
If running from a different directory, configure CLASSPATH to point to the package root:

```bash
# Linux / Mac
export CLASSPATH=/path/to/myproject:.

# Windows
set CLASSPATH=C:\path\to\myproject;.
```

Or, equivalently, use the `-cp` flag inline:

```bash
java -cp /path/to/myproject:. com.ktu.test.TestCircle
```

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Common Variations

The following matrix catalogs the **four principal ways** to access a class residing in a different package, complete with their trade-offs. This is a classic KTU 14-mark question pattern.

| # | Method | Syntax | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Fully Qualified Name (FQN)** | `java.util.ArrayList list = new java.util.ArrayList();` | No `import` needed; unambiguous | Verbose, reduces readability |
| 2 | **Single-Type Import** | `import java.util.ArrayList;` then `ArrayList list = new ArrayList();` | Clean, explicit, KTU-preferred | Requires one line per class |
| 3 | **On-Demand (Wildcard) Import** | `import java.util.*;` then `ArrayList list = new ArrayList();` | Imports all classes of the package in one line | Slight compile-time overhead; name-collision risk with `java.awt.*` |
| 4 | **Static Import** | `import static java.lang.Math.*;` then `sqrt(16);` | Allows direct access to static members | Overuse harms code clarity |

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Package Access Levels

Java has **four access levels**. Three of them depend on **package boundaries** — this is why defining a package is so foundational. We derive them below:

| Access Modifier | Same Class | Same Package (Subclass) | Same Package (Non-Subclass) | Different Package (Subclass) | Different Package (Non-Subclass) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `private` | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| *default* (no modifier) | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ |
| `protected` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ |
| `public` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The *default* access level (no keyword) is often called **"package-private"** because it grants visibility to all classes within the *same package* but nowhere else. This is the *only* access control that exists purely due to the package concept.

---

## 3.4 Sample Numerical / Logical Evaluation

**Question:** A programmer writes the package declaration `package utils.tools.io;`. In which directory must the `.java` file be placed, and what is its fully qualified class name if the class is named `FileHelper`?

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1:** Translate each dot in the package name to a directory separator.
$$ \text{utils.tools.io} \longrightarrow \text{utils/tools/io/} $$

**Step 2:** The file `FileHelper.java` must reside in the directory:
$$ \texttt{utils/tools/io/FileHelper.java} $$

**Step 3:** The fully qualified class name (FQN) becomes:
$$ \texttt{utils.tools.io.FileHelper} $$

**Step 4:** To import and use it elsewhere:
```java
import utils.tools.io.FileHelper;
```

**Step 5:** Compilation (from project root):
```bash
javac utils/tools/io/FileHelper.java
```

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: Package Directory Mapping Architecture

The diagram below illustrates the **block-level mapping** between Java source code (logical) and the file system (physical) for a project with two packages.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef root fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef pkg fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef file fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef client fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000

    A["myproject_ROOT_DIR"]:::root
    B["com_DIR"]:::pkg
    C["ktu_DIR"]:::pkg
    D1["oop_DIR"]:::pkg
    D2["test_DIR"]:::pkg
    E1["geometry_DIR"]:::pkg
    F1["Circle.java"]:::file
    F1b["Circle.class"]:::file
    F2["TestCircle.java"]:::file
    F2b["TestCircle.class"]:::file
    G["Console_Run_Command"]:::client

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D1
    C --> D2
    D1 --> E1
    E1 --> F1
    E1 --> F1b
    D2 --> F2
    D2 --> F2b
    G -->|java com.ktu.test.TestCircle| F2b
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The yellow node `myproject_ROOT_DIR` is the parent directory from which `javac` and `java` are invoked.
- Each blue folder represents one level of the dot-separated package name.
- Green files represent source (`.java`) and compiled (`.class`) artifacts.
- The pink `Console_Run_Command` node shows the invocation triggering the `TestCircle.class` in the `com.ktu.test` package.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Compilation and Execution Pipeline

This schematic depicts the **end-to-end workflow** from writing a packaged class to successfully executing it.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef step fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef tool fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef err fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000

    S1["Step1_Author_Source_Code_with_package_statement"]:::step
    S2["Step2_Save_File_in_Correct_Directory_Hierarchy"]:::step
    S3["Step3_Run_javac_with_dash_d_Flag"]:::step
    S4["Step4_Verify_Generated_class_File_in_Matching_Dir"]:::step
    S5["Step5_Run_java_with_Fully_Qualified_ClassName"]:::step

    T1["JAVAC_Compiler"]:::tool
    T2["JVM_Java_Virtual_Machine"]:::tool

    O1["Console_Output_Displayed"]:::out

    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> T1
    T1 --> S4
    S4 --> S5
    S5 --> T2

    S3 -.->|wrong import| E2
    S5 -.->|wrong path| E1
```

**Pipeline Logic:**
- The path through solid arrows is the *happy path* to successful execution.
- The dashed red arrows indicate the two most common **KTU pitfall errors** students encounter:
  - `Cannot find symbol` — caused by a missing or incorrect `import` statement.
  - `NoClassDefFoundError` — caused by a missing CLASSPATH entry or wrong invocation directory.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Package Hierarchy Tree

This diagram shows how a complex real-world application would organize its packages hierarchically. It mirrors the structure used in enterprise Java applications (relevant for KTU students preparing for placement interviews).

- Sub-packages segregate concerns: data models, data access objects (DAO), business services, user interface, and custom exceptions.
