# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **What is a package in Java? List any two advantages of using packages.**

### Model Answer (3 marks):
A package is a *namespace* that organizes a set of related classes and interfaces in Java. It provides a logical grouping of types and maps directly to a directory structure on the file system.
**Advantages (any two):**
1. **Namespace management** — Prevents name conflicts by allowing classes with the same name to exist in different packages (e.g., `java.util.Date` and `java.sql.Date`).
2. **Access control** — Provides the *package-private* (default) access level, which restricts visibility to classes within the same package.
3. **Logical organization** — Groups related classes for easier maintenance and comprehension.
*(2 marks: 1 for definition + 1 mark per advantage)*

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **Explain the significance of the `package` statement in Java. State the rules that must be followed while writing a package statement.**

### Model Answer (3 marks):
The `package` statement declares the package to which a class belongs. It must appear as the first executable line of the source file.
**Rules (any two for 2 marks):**
1. It must be the **first statement** in the source file (after a package or import statement, but before any class definition).
2. Only **one package** can be declared per source file.
3. The **directory hierarchy** on the file system must mirror the dotted package name.
4. Package names should be **all lowercase** and follow reverse-domain naming convention.
*(1 mark: significance + 2 marks: rules)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
> `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]`
> **(a)** Explain the procedure for creating a user-defined package in Java. Describe the role of CLASSPATH in accessing a user-defined package. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Write a Java program to create a package named `com.ktu.oop.shapes` containing a class `Rectangle` with methods to calculate `area()` and `perimeter()`. Write another class in a different package that imports and uses this `Rectangle` class. *(7 marks)*

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Procedure for Creating a User-Defined Package (4 marks):**
1. **Choose a package name** using the reverse-domain convention (e.g., `com.ktu.oop.shapes`).
2. **Create the directory hierarchy** matching the dotted package name:
   `com/ktu/oop/shapes/`
3. **Write the source file** with `package com.ktu.oop.shapes;` as the **first line**.
4. **Compile** using `javac -d . Rectangle.java` — the `-d` flag regenerates the package directory and places the `.class` file inside it.
5. **Set CLASSPATH** to the root directory containing the package structure, then run the class using its **fully qualified name**:
   `java com.ktu.oop.shapes.Rectangle`

**Role of CLASSPATH (3 marks):**
- CLASSPATH is an **environment variable** that tells the compiler and JVM *where to find* user-defined classes.
- It is searched *when classes from external packages are needed*.
- Syntax:
  - Windows: `set CLASSPATH=.;C:\myproject`
  - Linux: `export CLASSPATH=.:/home/user/myproject`
- The dot (`.`) refers to the **current directory** and is essential.
- Alternatively, the `-cp` flag with `javac`/`java` can be used *inline*.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Create the package directory**
```text
myproject/
└── com/ktu/oop/shapes/
```

**Step 2: Create `Rectangle.java` inside `com/ktu/oop/shapes/`**

```java
package com.ktu.oop.shapes;

public class Rectangle {
    private double length;
    private double width;

    public Rectangle(double length, double width) {
        this.length = length;
        this.width = width;
    }

    public double area() {
        return length * width;
    }

    public double perimeter() {
        return 2 * (length + width);
    }
}
```

**Step 3: Create `TestRectangle.java` in a different package directory `com/ktu/test/`**

```java
package com.ktu.test;

import com.ktu.oop.shapes.Rectangle;   // Importing user-defined package class

public class TestRectangle {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Rectangle r = new Rectangle(10.0, 5.0);
        System.out.println("Area = " + r.area());
        System.out.println("Perimeter = " + r.perimeter());
    }
}
```

**Step 4: Compile from project root**
```bash
javac -d . com/ktu/oop/shapes/Rectangle.java
javac -d . com/ktu/test/TestRectangle.java
```

**Step 5: Execute**
```bash
java com.ktu.test.TestRectangle
```

**Expected Output:**
```text
Area = 50.0
Perimeter = 30.0
```

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):**
- Package declaration and directory structure: 2 marks
- Correct Rectangle class with methods: 2 marks
- Importing and using from different package: 2 marks
- Compilation and execution explanation: 1 mark

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*
> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]`
> **(a)** Differentiate between *built-in packages* and *user-defined packages* in Java. Give two examples of each. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** What is the role of the `import` statement in Java? Explain with examples: (i) Single-type import, (ii) On-demand import, (iii) Static import, and (iv) Fully qualified name without import. *(7 marks)*

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

| Feature | Built-in Packages | User-Defined Packages |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Bundled with the JDK | Created by the programmer |
| **Examples** | `java.lang`, `java.util`, `java.io`, `java.sql` | `com.ktu.banking.model`, `com.ktu.oop.geometry` |
| **Import required** | All require `import` *except* `java.lang` (auto-imported) | Always require `import` for cross-package access |
| **Modification** | Read-only, cannot be altered | Fully modifiable by the developer |
| **Purpose** | Provide standard functionality | Organize application-specific code |
| **Location** | Inside the JDK installation (e.g., `jdk/lib/rt.jar` historically) | In the developer's project directory |
| **Documentation** | Official Java API docs | Developer-written Javadoc |

*(4 marks: differentiation table with at least 4 valid points + 2 marks: two examples of each = 1 mark per category)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Role of `import` statement:**
The `import` statement makes classes from other packages **accessible without using their fully qualified name**, thereby improving code readability. It is a *compile-time directive* (does not increase runtime performance).

**Four ways to access external classes:**

**(i) Single-Type Import (2 marks)**
```java
import java.util.Scanner;

public class Demo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);   // Direct use, no FQN needed
    }
}
```

**(ii) On-Demand (Wildcard) Import (2 marks)**
```java
import java.util.*;

public class Demo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        ArrayList<String> list = new ArrayList<>();  // Multiple classes available
        HashMap<Integer, String> map = new HashMap<>();
    }
}
```
*Imports all public classes from `java.util` in one statement.*

**(iii) Static Import (1.5 marks)**
```java
import static java.lang.Math.*;

public class Demo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        double r = sqrt(25);          // Direct use of static method without class prefix
        System.out.println(PI);       // Direct use of static constant
    }
}
```

**(iv) Fully Qualified Name Without Import (1.5 marks)**
```java
public class Demo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        java.util.Scanner sc = new java.util.Scanner(System.in);  // No import statement
    }
}
```
*The class is used directly with its package prefix — no `import` line is required.*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Wrong Directory Placement** — Students frequently place the source file in the *wrong directory*, leading to silent package mismatch and runtime `ClassNotFoundException`. **Always verify** that the file's directory path matches the package declaration.
> 2. **Forgetting `-d .` flag** — Without `-d`, the `.class` file is generated in the *same directory* as the source, breaking the package structure.
> 3. **Invoking from wrong directory** — The `java` command must be run from the *root* of the package tree, not from inside a sub-directory.
> 4. **Capitalization in package names** — Package names are case-sensitive. `Com.Ktu.Oop` is **different** from `com.ktu.oop`. Conventionally, **all lowercase** is mandatory.
> 5. **Public class file name mismatch** — If a class is declared `public`, the file name **must** match the class name exactly (including case). Otherwise, compilation fails.
> 6. **Confusing `import` with file inclusion** — `import` does *not* insert file contents; it merely provides a *name shortcut* for the compiler.
> 7. **Assuming `java.lang` packages need imports** — They do *not*; the JVM auto-imports `java.lang.*`.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Memorize Before the Exam:**

- ✅ A **package** is a *namespace* for organizing related classes/interfaces and corresponds to a **directory structure**.
- ✅ The `package` declaration must be the **first executable line** of the source file.
- ✅ Only **one package** per source file is permitted.
- ✅ The package name's dots (`.`) map to **directory separators** (`/` or `\`).
- ✅ **Convention**: Package names are **all lowercase** and use **reverse-domain** naming (e.g., `com.ktu.oop`).
- ✅ **Built-in packages**: `java.lang` (auto-imported), `java.util`, `java.io`, `java.sql`, `javax.swing`, `java.net`, `java.applet`.
- ✅ **User-defined packages** are created using the `package` keyword and require proper directory mapping.
- ✅ **CLASSPATH** is the environment variable that locates user-defined classes; set it to the **package root directory**.
- ✅ The **dot (`.`)** in CLASSPATH represents the **current directory** and must be included.
- ✅ Compile packaged classes using `javac -d . FileName.java` from the project root.
- ✅ Run packaged classes using `java fully.qualified.ClassName` from the project root.
- ✅ **Four ways to access external classes**: FQN, single-type import, on-demand import (`*`), static import.
- ✅ **Access control** connection: *default* (package-private) access is granted solely based on package membership.
- ✅ A `public` class name **must match** the file name.
- ✅ `import` is a *compile-time directive* — it does not affect runtime behavior.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CLASSPATH
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT633 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** CLASSPATH

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **CLASSPATH** is an environment variable (or a command-line `-classpath` / `-cp` option) used by the **Java Compiler (`javac`)**, the **Java Virtual Machine (JVM)**, and supporting JDK tools to locate and load **user-defined classes**, **third-party libraries (JAR/WAR/EAR files)**, and **package directories** that are not part of the standard Java Platform (JDK/JRE) library.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context for *Object Oriented Programming (PECIT633)*, CLASSPATH is introduced as the **search-path mechanism** that enables Java's dynamic class loading subsystem (`ClassLoader`) to resolve symbolic class references into actual binary `.class` files at compile-time and run-time.

**Key Identifier:** It is fundamentally a **colon-separated (`:`) list of directories, JAR archives, and ZIP files** on **Unix/Linux/macOS** systems, and a **semicolon-separated (`;`)** list on **Windows** systems.

> [!NOTE] 
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 3):** CLASSPATH is discussed under the broader context of *Packages, Access Modifiers, and Exception Handling*. Understanding CLASSPATH is essential for compiling and running Java programs that depend on external libraries, user-defined packages, and modular class hierarchies.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a librarian in a massive library containing **millions of books** (the Java Standard Library). A student walks in and asks: *"I need the book `MyCustomUtility.class` — where is it?"*

You, the librarian (the JVM), immediately:
1. **Check the in-house reference section** → *Standard JDK classes (`java.lang`, `java.util`, etc.)* — these are **automatically found** without any external path.
2. **Check the list of "external shelves" written on a clipboard** → This clipboard is your **CLASSPATH**. It contains locations like `D:\MyProjects\bin`, `C:\libs\appframework.jar`, etc.
3. Walk to each listed location **in order** until the book is found. If not found anywhere, you throw a `ClassNotFoundException`.

> **CLASSPATH = the librarian's clipboard listing external shelf locations to search.** 📚

### 1.3 Core Components the CLASSPATH Resolves

| Component Type | File Extension | Example |
|----------------|----------------|---------|
| **Compiled Java class** | `.class` | `bin/com/ktu/utils/Helper.class` |
| **Package root directory** | `/` (folder) | `C:/project/src` (the package's **root**, not the package folder itself) |
| **Java Archive** | `.jar` | `C:/libs/mysql-connector.jar` |
| **Web Archive** | `.war` | `C:/apps/app.war` (used in enterprise deployment) |
| **ZIP Archive** | `.zip` | `C:/libs/legacy.zip` |

> [!NOTE] 
> **Default CLASSPATH:** When Java is first installed, the default CLASSPATH is the **current working directory (`.`)**, meaning Java only looks for classes in the folder from which you invoked `java` or `javac`. Setting CLASSPATH properly is what allows Java to find **anything beyond** the current folder.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 The Three (3) Distinct Ways to Set CLASSPATH

In KTU board examinations, students are often asked to **list and explain** the mechanisms for setting the CLASSPATH. Mastering these three is mandatory.

#### **Method 1: Permanently via Environment Variable (OS-Level)**

This sets the CLASSPATH **globally for all sessions** on the machine.

**On Windows (System Properties → Environment Variables):**
```text
Variable name:  CLASSPATH
Variable value: .;C:\ktu\libs\*;C:\project\out
```

**On Linux/macOS (in `~/.bashrc` or `~/.zshrc`):**
```bash
export CLASSPATH=".:/home/student/ktu/libs/*:/home/student/project/out"
```

#### **Method 2: Temporarily via Command Line (Recommended for KTU Labs)**

This is the **KTU lab-exam favourite** because it is non-permanent and only affects the current terminal session.

**Syntax:**
```bash
javac -classpath ".;C:\libs\*" MyProgram.java
java  -cp       ".;C:\libs\*" MyProgram
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **`-cp` is the modern shorthand for `-classpath`** — both are functionally identical. The KTU 2024 syllabus accepts either notation.

#### **Method 3: Manifest File (Inside a JAR — Advanced)**

When you package your application into a **runnable JAR**, you can embed the CLASSPATH inside the JAR's `META-INF/MANIFEST.MF` file using the `Class-Path` attribute.

```text
Manifest-Version: 1.0
Main-Class: com.ktu.main.AppEntry
Class-Path: libs/mysql-connector.jar libs/app-utils.jar
```

> The JAR then becomes **self-contained** — users only need to run `java -jar MyApp.jar`.

### 2.2 Path Separators — The Most-Confused KTU Pitfall

| Operating System | Directory Separator | CLASSPATH Entry Separator | Example |
|------------------|---------------------|--------------------------|---------|
| **Windows** | `\` (backslash) | **`;`** (semicolon) | `.;C:\libs\*;D:\out` |
| **Linux / macOS** | `/` (forward slash) | **`:`** (colon) | `.:/home/libs/*:/home/out` |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Exam Mistake:** Writing a colon `:` instead of semicolon `;` (or vice versa) in the CLASSPATH. The JVM **will not throw a helpful error** — it will simply fail to find classes, producing `ClassNotFoundException` or `NoClassDefFoundError`. Always double-check the separator based on the OS.

### 2.3 Wildcard `*` — Including All JARs in a Folder

A powerful (and frequently tested) feature is the **wildcard `*`**, which tells the JVM/Javac: *"Include every JAR file inside this directory."*

```bash
# On Windows — load every JAR from C:\libs
set CLASSPATH=C:\libs\*

# On Linux — load every JAR from /opt/libs
export CLASSPATH=/opt/libs/*
```

**Rules for the Wildcard:**

1. The `*` must be at the **end** of the path — `C:\libs\*` ✅, `C:\libs\*\sub` ❌.
2. It only matches **JARs and ZIPs** — it does **not** recursively descend into subfolders.
3. **Since Java 6**, the wildcard is supported; before Java 6, you had to enumerate every JAR manually.
4. The wildcard is **expanded by the launcher**, not by the JVM — so it works with `java` and `javac` but may **not work** in some IDEs in older configurations.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

| # | Concept | Rule / Formula | Engineering Utility |
|---|---------|----------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **CLASSPATH definition** | Search path for non-JDK classes | Enables modular, decoupled Java applications |
| 2 | **Windows separator** | `;` (semicolon) | Cross-platform command writing |
| 3 | **Linux/macOS separator** | `:` (colon) | Cross-platform command writing |
| 4 | **Default value** | `.` (current directory) | Ensures zero-config local development |
| 5 | **Wildcard** | `*` (matches all JARs in a folder) | Simplifies deployment scripts in CI/CD |
| 6 | **Compile-time flag** | `javac -classpath <path>` | Resolves imports at compilation |
| 7 | **Run-time flag** | `java -cp <path>` | Resolves classes at execution |
| 8 | **Environment variable** | `CLASSPATH` (OS-level) | Sets default for all invocations |
| 9 | **JAR manifest** | `Class-Path:` attribute in `MANIFEST.MF` | Distributable, self-contained JARs |
| 10 | **Bootstrap classes** | **Never affected** by CLASSPATH | Security and integrity of `java.lang.*` |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | CLASSPATH Use Case |
|--------|---------------------|
| **Web Applications (Servlets/JSP)** | Tomcat, Jetty use their own classloader hierarchies layered on top of CLASSPATH |
| **Android Development** | `dx` tool uses CLASSPATH to locate user classes for `.dex` conversion |
| **Enterprise (Spring/Hibernate)** | Bundled JARs (e.g., `spring-core.jar`) must be on CLASSPATH |
| **Build Tools (Maven/Gradle)** | Internally manage CLASSPATH via `pom.xml` / `build.gradle` — but understanding the underlying mechanism is essential for debugging |
| **Big Data (Hadoop, Spark)** | Worker JVMs ship JARs via distributed CLASSPATH mechanisms |
| **Microservices (Docker)** | Dockerfile's `COPY` + `CLASSPATH` env var = containerized Java apps |

## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Process: How CLASSPATH Resolution Works

### 3.1 The ClassLoader Hierarchy (Bootstrap → Extension → Application)

When the JVM receives a class request, it follows a **strict parent-delegation chain**. The CLASSPATH only governs the **bottom (Application) classloader** in the chain.

$$\text{Class Request} \longrightarrow \text{BootstrapClassLoader} \longrightarrow \text{PlatformClassLoader} \longrightarrow \text{ApplicationClassLoader} \longrightarrow \text{ClassNotFoundException}$$

**Step-by-step resolution logic:**

**Step 1 — Bootstrap ClassLoader** (Native C/C++ code in `jrt-fs` / `rt.jar`)
- Loads **core JDK classes** from `java.lang.*`, `java.util.*`, etc.
- **Ignores CLASSPATH entirely** — these classes are protected for security.
- If found → load class. If not → pass to next loader.

**Step 2 — Platform (Extension) ClassLoader**
- Loads from `${java.home}/lib/ext` (deprecated path) or `java.module.path`.
- Again, **independent of CLASSPATH**.

**Step 3 — Application (System) ClassLoader**
- This is the **only loader that consults CLASSPATH**.
- It searches the path entries **in the order they appear** in the CLASSPATH string.
- The **first match wins** — if a class exists in two JARs, the one appearing earlier is loaded.

**Step 4 — Failure Mode**
- If no classloader finds the class, the JVM throws a **`ClassNotFoundException`** (checked exception) or **`NoClassDefFoundError`** (error at link time).

### 3.2 Worked Example: Resolving `com.ktu.utils.MathHelper`

Suppose you compile and run the following program:

```java
// File: src/com/ktu/main/Calculator.java
package com.ktu.main;

import com.ktu.utils.MathHelper;  // ← USER-DEFINED class

public class Calculator {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println(MathHelper.square(5));
    }
}
```

**File layout on disk:**

```
D:\KtuProject\
├── src\
│   ├── com\ktu\main\Calculator.java
│   └── com\ktu\utils\MathHelper.java
└── out\                    ← compiled .class files go here
```

**Step A — Compilation (from `D:\KtuProject`)**

```bash
javac -d out -cp . src\com\ktu\main\Calculator.java src\com\ktu\utils\MathHelper.java
```

After compilation, the `out/` folder contains:
```
out\com\ktu\main\Calculator.class
out\com\ktu\utils\MathHelper.class
```

**Step B — Execution**

```bash
java -cp ".;out" com.ktu.main.Calculator
```

**Step C — Tracing the ClassLoader's Search**

The `ApplicationClassLoader` receives the request for `com.ktu.utils.MathHelper`. Its search sequence is:

$$\text{1. } . \text{ (current dir = D:\KtuProject) } \rightarrow \text{Not found}$$

$$\text{2. } \text{out} \text{ (D:\KtuProject\out) } \rightarrow \text{Found! } \text{out\com\ktu\utils\MathHelper.class}$$

**Step D — Match Resolution Detail**

The ClassLoader constructs the **fully-qualified class path** as:

$$\text{package-path} + \text{class-name-with-dots-replaced-by-slashes} + \text{.class}$$

Applying the formula:

$$\text{"out"} + \text{"com/ktu/utils"} + \text{"MathHelper"} + \text{".class"} \Longrightarrow \text{"out/com/ktu/utils/MathHelper.class"}$$

The file exists → class is **loaded, linked, and initialized**.

### 3.3 Why Order Matters — The "JAR Shadowing" Problem

If your CLASSPATH is `A.jar;B.jar` and **both** contain `org.apache.log4j.Logger.class`, the **class from `A.jar` is loaded** because `A.jar` appears first. This is called **JAR shadowing** and is a notorious source of bugs in enterprise applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners love to ask: *"What happens if the same class exists in two JARs on the CLASSPATH?"* — Answer: **The one appearing first in the path is loaded; the second is shadowed and never used.**

### 3.4 Full Trace — From `java` Command to Class Execution

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
│  java -cp ".;out" com.ktu.main.X    │
└──────────────┬──────────────────────┘
               │
               ▼
       ┌─────────────────┐
       │ Java Launcher   │
       │ (java.c)        │
       └────────┬────────┘
                │ parses -cp, builds String[] classpath
                │   = {".", "out"}
                ▼
       ┌──────────────────────┐
       │ ApplicationClassLoader│  ← initial entry point
       │ parent = PlatformCL   │
       └────────┬─────────────┘
                │
                ▼
        [1] Find "com/ktu/main/X.class" in "."
                │ not found
                ▼
        [2] Find "com/ktu/main/X.class" in "out"
                │ FOUND
                ▼
        ┌──────────────────────┐
        │ Define class X       │
        │ Link (verify, prepare)│
        │ Initialize (static)  │
        └────────┬─────────────┘
                 │
                 ▼
         X.main(String[] args) is invoked
```

### 3.5 Setting CLASSPATH Inside Java Code (Reflection Use-Case)

In rare advanced cases, you may dynamically augment the CLASSPATH at runtime (e.g., plugin loaders). This is done via the `URLClassLoader`:

```java
import java.net.URL;
import java.net.URLClassLoader;

public class PluginLoader {
    public static Class<?> loadFromJar(String jarPath, String className) throws Exception {
        URL jarUrl = new java.io.File(jarPath).toURI().toURL();
        URLClassLoader loader = new URLClassLoader(new URL[]{jarUrl}, 
                                                   PluginLoader.class.getClassLoader());
        return loader.loadClass(className);
    }
}
```

> This **bypasses** the static CLASSPATH and creates a dynamic classpath at runtime — useful for hot-reloadable plugin architectures.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 CLASSPATH Resolution Flow (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Java Command: java -cp .;out com.ktu.main.Calculator] --> B[Java Launcher parses -cp]
    B --> C[Classpath Array = dot, out]
    C --> D[ApplicationClassLoader initialized]
    D --> E[Request: com.ktu.utils.MathHelper]
    E --> F{Check Bootstrap CL}
    F -->|Not a JDK class| G{Check Platform CL}
    G -->|Not in java.home/lib| H{Check Entry 1: dot .}
    H -->|Not found| I{Check Entry 2: out}
    I -->|FOUND: out/com/ktu/utils/MathHelper.class| J[Load class into Method Area]
    I -->|Not found| K[Throw ClassNotFoundException]
    J --> L[Link and Verify bytecode]
    L --> M[Initialize static fields and blocks]
    M --> N[Return Class object to JVM]
    N --> O[main method executes]
```

### 4.2 Three Methods of Setting CLASSPATH (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Setting CLASSPATH in Java] --> B[Method 1: Environment Variable]
    A --> C[Method 2: Command-Line Flag]
    A --> D[Method 3: JAR Manifest]
    
    B --> B1["Windows: set CLASSPATH=.;libs*"]
    B --> B2["Linux: export CLASSPATH=.:libs/*"]
    B --> B3[Permanent, OS-wide, persistent]
    
    C --> C1["javac -classpath path source.java"]
    C --> C2["java -cp path ClassName"]
    C --> C3[Temporary, session-only]
    
    D --> D1[MANIFEST.MF inside JAR]
    D --> D2["Class-Path: libs/a.jar libs/b.jar"]
    D --> D3[Self-contained runnable JAR]
```

### 4.3 ClassLoader Hierarchy vs. CLASSPATH Scope (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L1[Top Tier - Ignores CLASSPATH]
        BC[Bootstrap ClassLoader]
        BC --> BC1[Loads java.lang, java.util]
        BC --> BC2[Loads from jrt-fs / rt.jar]
    end
    
    subgraph L2[Middle Tier - Ignores CLASSPATH]
        PC[Platform ClassLoader]
        PC --> PC1[Loads java.sql, javax.crypto]
        PC --> PC2[Loads from java.module.path]
    end
    
    subgraph L3[Bottom Tier - USES CLASSPATH]
        AC[Application ClassLoader]
        AC --> AC1[Loads com.ktu.utils.*]
        AC --> AC2[Loads third-party JARs]
        AC --> AC3[Searches CLASSPATH entries in order]
    end
    
    BC --> PC
    PC --> AC
```

### 4.4 JAR Shadowing — First-Match-Wins Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[CLASSPATH: A.jar;B.jar;C.jar] --> B[Search A.jar]
    B --> C{Class found?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Load from A.jar]
    C -->|No| E[Search B.jar]
    E --> F{Class found?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Load from B.jar]
    F -->|No| H[Search C.jar]
    H --> I{Class found?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Load from C.jar]
    I -->|No| K[Throw ClassNotFoundException]
    
    D --> L[Continue execution]
    G --> L
    J --> L
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB

        W1[Path separator: backslash]
        W2[CLASSPATH separator: semicolon]
        W3["Example: .;C:\libs\*;D:\out"]
    end
    
    subgraph LIN[Linux / macOS]
        L1[Path separator: forward slash]
        L2[CLASSPATH separator: colon]
        L3["Example: .:/home/libs/*:/home/out"]
    end
    
    WIN --> X[Single Java Command File]

    X --> Y[Use java.io.File.pathSeparator to write portable code]
```
