## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define CLASSPATH in Java. Mention the two different separators used in CLASSPATH for Windows and Linux operating systems.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **CLASSPATH** (3 points)
> 
> **Definition (1 mark):** CLASSPATH is an environment variable used by the Java compiler (`javac`) and the Java Virtual Machine (`java`) to locate user-defined classes, third-party JAR files, and package directories that are not part of the standard JDK library. It tells the JVM where to search for `.class` files outside the current directory.
> 
> **Separators (2 marks):**
> - **Windows operating system:** Uses **semicolon (`;`)** as the entry separator. Example: `.;C:\libs\*;D:\out`
> - **Linux/Unix/macOS operating system:** Uses **colon (`:`) as the entry separator. Example: `.:/home/libs/*:/home/out`

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
*Explain the use of the wildcard character `*` in CLASSPATH with an example. State any two limitations of using the wildcard.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Wildcard in CLASSPATH** (3 points)
> 
> **Use (1 mark):** The wildcard `*` is used in a CLASSPATH entry to automatically include **all JAR (and ZIP) files** present in the specified directory, without having to list each JAR individually. Example on Windows: `set CLASSPATH=.;C:\libs\*` — this includes every `.jar` file inside `C:\libs\`.
> 
> **Limitations (2 marks — 1 each):**
> 1. The wildcard `*` is supported **only from Java 6 onwards**; it does not work in older JVMs.
> 2. The wildcard does **not recursively search subdirectories** — it only matches JARs directly in the specified folder. Also, the `*` must appear at the **end** of the path entry.

---

### **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]

**(a)** Explain the **three different ways of setting the CLASSPATH** in Java. Provide the syntax and an example for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **role of the Application ClassLoader** in resolving classes. What exception is thrown when a class is not found on the CLASSPATH? How does the order of entries affect class loading? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Three Ways of Setting CLASSPATH (7 marks):**

**Way 1: Environment Variable (2 marks)**

This sets CLASSPATH **permanently** at the operating system level so that all Java invocations inherit it automatically.

*Windows (Command Prompt):*
```cmd
set CLASSPATH=.;C:\ktu\libs\*;C:\project\out
```

*Linux/macOS (Bash):*
```bash
export CLASSPATH=".:/home/ktu/libs/*:/home/ktu/project/out"
```

**Way 2: Command-Line Option (3 marks)**

This sets CLASSPATH **temporarily** for one compilation or execution. Two equivalent flags exist: `-classpath` and `-cp`.

*Compiling with CLASSPATH:*
```bash
javac -classpath ".;C:\libs\mysql-connector.jar" MyApp.java
```

*Running with CLASSPATH:*
```bash
java -cp ".;C:\libs\mysql-connector.jar" com.ktu.main.MyApp
```

**Way 3: JAR Manifest File (2 marks)**

When packaging an application as a runnable JAR, the CLASSPATH can be embedded inside `META-INF/MANIFEST.MF`:

```text
Manifest-Version: 1.0
Main-Class: com.ktu.main.MyApp
Class-Path: libs/mysql-connector.jar libs/utils.jar
```

The user can then simply run `java -jar MyApp.jar` without specifying `-cp` on the command line.

---

**Part (b) — Application ClassLoader & Resolution (7 marks):**

**Role of the Application ClassLoader (3 marks):**

The **Application ClassLoader** (also called the *System ClassLoader*) is the **bottom-most** classloader in the standard Java delegation chain. It is the **only classloader that consults the CLASSPATH**. Its responsibilities include:

1. Loading user-defined classes from the directories and JARs listed in the CLASSPATH.
2. Loading third-party libraries (e.g., `mysql-connector.jar`, `log4j.jar`).
3. Delegating the search to its parent (Platform ClassLoader) first; if the parent fails, it then searches its own CLASSPATH entries.

**Exception Thrown (2 marks):**

When a class is requested and **none of the entries in the CLASSPATH contain** the corresponding `.class` file, the JVM throws:

- **`ClassNotFoundException`** — a *checked* exception, typically thrown when loading a class dynamically using `Class.forName()` or `ClassLoader.loadClass()`.
- **`NoClassDefFoundError`** — a *LinkageError*, thrown when a class was present at compile time but is missing at runtime (e.g., a JAR was accidentally deleted from CLASSPATH before execution).

**Order of Entries (2 marks):**

CLASSPATH entries are searched **sequentially from left to right (or first to last)**. The **first matching class** is loaded, and any subsequent matches are **shadowed and ignored**. This is the principle of **JAR shadowing** — if `A.jar` and `B.jar` both contain `org.apache.log4j.Logger.class`, and the CLASSPATH is `A.jar;B.jar`, then only the version from `A.jar` is used.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted — Internal Choice]

**(a)** What is the **default value of CLASSPATH**? Explain the difference between **compile-time and run-time CLASSPATH** with a suitable example involving a user-defined package. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a step-by-step procedure to compile and run a Java program `com.ktu.bank.AccountDemo` that uses a class `com.ktu.bank.LoanCalculator` from a custom package, given that the source files are in `D:\bankapp\src`. Assume you are using Windows. Show the directory structure and the exact commands. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Default CLASSPATH & Compile vs Run (7 marks):**

**Default Value (2 marks):**

If the CLASSPATH environment variable is **not set at all**, the default CLASSPATH is the **current working directory (`.`)**. This means Java will only search for classes in the folder from which the `java` or `javac` command was invoked. Without explicit configuration, classes in any other directory will not be found.

**Compile-time vs Run-time CLASSPATH (5 marks):**

| Aspect | Compile-time CLASSPATH | Run-time CLASSPATH |
|--------|------------------------|--------------------|
| **Used by** | `javac` (Java compiler) | `java` (JVM launcher) |
| **Purpose** | Resolving `import` statements and type references | Loading `.class` files during execution |
| **Set via** | `-classpath` flag or `CLASSPATH` env var | `-cp` flag or `CLASSPATH` env var |
| **Example** | `javac -cp ".;C:\libs\*.jar" MyApp.java` | `java -cp ".;C:\out;C:\libs\*.jar" com.ktu.main.MyApp` |

**Worked Example:**

Suppose you have a package `com.ktu.geometry` with class `Circle.java` compiled to `C:\libs\geometry\`. Your `Main.java` in the current directory imports it:

```java
// Main.java
import com.ktu.geometry.Circle;

public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println("Area = " + Circle.area(5.0));
    }
}
```

*Compile (CLASSPATH must include the geometry package's root):*
```bash
javac -cp ".;C:\libs\geometry" Main.java
```

*Run (CLASSPATH must include the geometry package's root AND the current directory):*
```bash
java -cp ".;C:\libs\geometry" Main
```

> **Key difference:** At compile time, the compiler only needs to find `Circle.class` to verify the import. At run time, the JVM also needs to find `Main.class`, so the current directory `.` must be on the CLASSPATH. If the CLASSPATH is omitted entirely during `java`, the JVM will throw `NoClassDefFoundError: Main`.

---

**Part (b) — Step-by-Step Procedure (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Directory Structure (2 marks):**

The source files are organized as follows in `D:\bankapp\src`:

```
D:\bankapp\
└── src\
    ├── com\ktu\bank\AccountDemo.java
    └── com\ktu\bank\LoanCalculator.java
```

**Step 2 — Source Code (1 mark):**

```java
// D:\bankapp\src\com\ktu\bank\LoanCalculator.java
package com.ktu.bank;
public class LoanCalculator {
    public static double emi(double p, double r, int n) {
        return (p * r * Math.pow(1 + r, n)) / (Math.pow(1 + r, n) - 1);
    }
}
```

```java
// D:\bankapp\src\com\ktu\bank\AccountDemo.java
package com.ktu.bank;
public class AccountDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println("EMI = " + LoanCalculator.emi(100000, 0.1, 12));
    }
}
```

**Step 3 — Compilation Command (2 marks):**

Open Command Prompt and navigate to `D:\bankapp`. Compile to an `out` folder:

```cmd
cd /d D:\bankapp
javac -d out -cp . src\com\ktu\bank\AccountDemo.java src\com\ktu\bank\LoanCalculator.java
```

The `-d out` flag tells the compiler to place the resulting `.class` files inside `D:\bankapp\out`, preserving the package structure.

**Step 4 — Resulting Compiled Structure:**

```
D:\bankapp\out\com\ktu\bank\AccountDemo.class
D:\bankapp\out\com\ktu\bank\LoanCalculator.class
```

**Step 5 — Execution Command (2 marks):**

```cmd
java -cp ".;out" com.ktu.bank.AccountDemo
```

**Explanation:**
- `-cp ".;out"` — searches the current directory (`.`) and the `out` folder.
- `com.ktu.bank.AccountDemo` — the **fully-qualified class name** (with dots, not slashes).
- The JVM constructs the path `out/com/ktu/bank/AccountDemo.class` and loads it.

**Expected Output:**
```
EMI = 8791.59  (approx)
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls
> 
> **Pitfall 1 — Wrong Separator (–2 marks):** On Windows, using `:` instead of `;` in the CLASSPATH. The JVM silently ignores invalid separators and throws `ClassNotFoundException`. **Always** state the OS and use the correct separator.
> 
> **Pitfall 2 — Including Package Folder Instead of Root (–2 marks):** Writing `-cp ".;out\com\ktu\bank"` instead of `-cp ".;out"`. The CLASSPATH entry must be the **root** of the package hierarchy, not the package folder itself. The package structure is **appended** to the CLASSPATH entry by the classloader.
> 
> **Pitfall 3 — Forgetting `.` in Run-time CLASSPATH (–3 marks):** When running a program located in the current directory, omitting `.` from the `-cp` flag results in `NoClassDefFoundError: Could not initialize class ...` or `Main class not found`. Always include `.` in run-time CLASSPATH.
> 
> **Pitfall 4 — Using Slashes in Fully-Qualified Class Name (–1 mark):** Running `java -cp . out.com.ktu.bank.AccountDemo` instead of `java -cp ".;out" com.ktu.bank.AccountDemo`. The fully-qualified class name uses **dots**, and the path component uses **slashes** (on Unix) or **backslashes** (on Windows).
> 
> **Pitfall 5 — Confusing CLASSPATH with PATH (–1 mark):** `PATH` is an OS variable that tells the shell **where to find executables** (like `java.exe`). `CLASSPATH` is a **Java-specific** variable that tells the JVM where to find **class files**. They are not interchangeable.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **CLASSPATH** is the **search path** used by `javac` and `java` to locate user-defined classes and third-party JARs/ZIPs.
- The **default CLASSPATH** is the **current working directory (`.`)** when the environment variable is unset.
- **Windows uses `;`** (semicolon) as the separator; **Linux/macOS use `:`** (colon).
- The **wildcard `*`** includes all JARs in a directory but does not recurse into subfolders.
- There are **three ways** to set CLASSPATH: (1) Environment Variable, (2) Command-Line `-cp` / `-classpath` flag, (3) JAR Manifest `Class-Path` attribute.
- The **Application ClassLoader** is the only classloader that consults the CLASSPATH; Bootstrap and Platform classloaders ignore it.
- CLASSPATH entries are searched **in order — first match wins** (JAR shadowing principle).
- **Bootstrap classes** (e.g., `java.lang.String`) are **never** on the user CLASSPATH and cannot be overridden.
- **Exceptions:** `ClassNotFoundException` (dynamic loading) and `NoClassDefFoundError` (link-time) are the two errors thrown when CLASSPATH resolution fails.
- The **package root directory** (not the package folder) must be on the CLASSPATH.
- For **runnable JARs**, the `Class-Path` attribute in `MANIFEST.MF` is the cleanest way to bundle dependencies.
- **Do not confuse `PATH` (OS executable search) with `CLASSPATH` (Java class search).**
- Always include `.` in the run-time `-cp` when running a class located in the current directory.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Access Protection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT633 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Access Protection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Access Protection** in Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) refers to the mechanism by which the visibility, reachability, and manipulability of class members (data members and member functions) and class boundaries are regulated through well-defined language-level access control keywords. In C++ (the canonical language for PECIT633), access protection is implemented using three explicit **access specifiers**: `public`, `private`, and `protected`, along with the auxiliary `friend` mechanism and the rules of access transformation in inheritance.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, access protection forms the security backbone of the OOP pillars — **Encapsulation** and **Data Hiding** — by ensuring that the internal state of an object is shielded from unauthorized or unintended external manipulation, and that derived classes may access only the sanctioned subset of base-class members.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECIT633 – Module 3):**
> Access protection covers access specifiers (`public`, `private`, `protected`), the `friend` function and `friend` class mechanism, and the rules of access control under **public**, **private**, and **protected inheritance**. These are high-frequency, high-weight topics in both Continuous Evaluation (CE) and End Semester Examinations (ESE).

## 1.2 The Three Access Specifiers — At a Glance

| Specifier | Accessibility from Same Class | Accessibility from Derived Class | Accessibility from Outside (Object) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| `private` | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ No |
| `protected` | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| `public` | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Default Access Rule:** In a `class`, members default to `private`; in a `struct`, members default to `public`. This is one of the only technical differences between `class` and `struct` in C++.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bank Vault" Model

Imagine a modern bank building:

- **`private` members** are like the **inner vault room** — only the bank's own internal staff (i.e., the class itself, through its own member functions) can open it. Customers (outside objects) and even branch managers at other branches (derived classes in another branch) cannot enter.
- **`protected` members** are like the **manager's office** — the bank's internal staff and managers from *any* branch (i.e., the class and any derived class in the same family) can access it, but regular customers standing outside cannot.
- **`public` members** are like the **customer service counter** — anyone can walk in and use the services (call the public functions, read the public data through getters, etc.).

The **`friend` mechanism** is like a special key card given to an *auditor* — the auditor is not an employee of the bank, but the bank explicitly trusts the auditor and grants access to even the inner vault. In C++, a `friend function` or `friend class` can access the private and protected members of the class that granted the friendship.

This analogy immediately clarifies why access protection is the technical implementation of **encapsulation** — it controls the *interface boundary* through which external code interacts with an object.

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Mnemonic — "PPO Triad":** `Private` < `Protected` < `Public` in terms of increasing accessibility. Remember: a wider opening means more access, less protection.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The `private` Access Specifier

**Definition:** Members declared as `private` are accessible only within the class in which they are declared. They are *not* accessible:
- From any object of the class instantiated outside.
- From any derived class (regardless of inheritance type).
- From any unrelated function or class.

**Why it exists:** It enforces the strictest form of **data hiding**. The internal representation of the object can be modified only through the well-defined public/protected member functions (the interface), thereby preserving **invariants** and enabling safe refactoring of the internal implementation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In real-world systems (e.g., banking, embedded control), `private` members model state that must never be directly manipulated by external subsystems — for instance, the internal `balance` field of an `Account` object should never be writable from outside; it must be modified only through `deposit()` and `withdraw()`.

## 2.2 The `protected` Access Specifier

**Definition:** Members declared as `protected` are accessible:
- Within the class in which they are declared.
- Within any class **derived** (publicly, privately, or protectedly) from it.
- **Not** accessible from objects instantiated outside the class hierarchy.

**Why it exists:** It provides a controlled extension point for derived classes without exposing internals to the outside world. It is the standard tool used by framework designers who want subclasses to override behavior or access internal state, while keeping the public API clean.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** A common confusion is whether `protected` members are accessible from `main()` or any non-derived class. The answer is **No**. `protected` is *not* the same as `public`.

## 2.3 The `public` Access Specifier

**Definition:** Members declared as `public` are accessible from:
- The class itself.
- Any derived class.
- Any object of the class, from any scope.
- Any unrelated function or class.

**Why it exists:** It defines the **interface** of the class — the set of operations and data that the class formally commits to support. Once a member is `public`, removing or renaming it constitutes a **breaking change** to the API.

## 2.4 The `friend` Mechanism

C++ allows a class to grant privileged access to specific external entities using the `friend` keyword.

### 2.4.1 Friend Functions

A **friend function** is a non-member function (or a global function) that is declared inside a class with the `friend` keyword. Despite not being a member of the class, it has full access to the class's `private` and `protected` members.

**Key Properties:**
- Friendship is **granted**, not taken (the class declaring the friend decides).
- Friendship is **not transitive**: A friend of class A is not automatically a friend of class B, even if B is a friend of A.
- Friendship is **not inherited**: A friend of a base class is not automatically a friend of a derived class.
- A friend function is **not** a member function; it does not receive the implicit `this` pointer.
- It can be a standalone function, a member function of another class, or a function template.

### 2.4.2 Friend Classes

A **friend class** is a class that is declared as a friend of another class. All member functions of the friend class can access the private and protected members of the class that granted the friendship.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall — Friendship is one-directional and non-inherited:** If `B` is a friend of `A`, this does *not* mean `A` is a friend of `B`. It also does *not* mean that classes derived from `B` are friends of `A`.

## 2.5 Access Control Under Inheritance

This is the **most heavily tested sub-topic** of access protection in KTU examinations. When a derived class inherits from a base class, the visibility of the base class members in the derived class is determined by **two factors**:
1. The access specifier of the member in the **base class**.
2. The **inheritance mode** used in the derived class declaration.

### 2.5.1 Access Transformation Matrix

Let us denote the access specifier of a base-class member as `M` and the inheritance mode as `I`. The effective access in the derived class is given by the matrix below.

| Base Member `M` ↓ \ Inheritance `I` → | `public` inheritance | `protected` inheritance | `private` inheritance |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **`public` member** | `public` | `protected` | `private` |
| **`protected` member** | `protected` | `protected` | `private` |
| **`private` member** | *inaccessible* | *inaccessible* | *inaccessible* |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule:** `private` members of the base class are **never directly accessible** in the derived class, regardless of the inheritance mode. They are *inherited* (i.e., the memory is still there), but access is sealed. They can only be accessed indirectly through the base class's own public/protected member functions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility — The Is-A vs. Is-Implemented-In-Terms-Of distinction:**
> - **Public inheritance** models an "is-a" relationship (e.g., a `SavingsAccount` **is a** kind of `Account`).
> - **Private / Protected inheritance** models an "is-implemented-in-terms-of" relationship (a `Stack` might be implemented in terms of a `deque` privately, but a `Stack` is *not* a `deque`).
> This is the practical rationale behind the three inheritance modes.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Rule / Formula | Engineering Implication |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Default class access | `private` | Prevents accidental external coupling. |
| Default struct access | `public` | Used for plain aggregates / data structures. |
| Private member reach | Same class only | Encapsulation / Data Hiding. |
| Protected member reach | Same class + derived classes | Extension point for subclasses. |
| Public member reach | Anywhere | API contract. |
| Friend function | Non-member with `friend` keyword | Privileged coupling (use sparingly). |
| Friend class | All members of friend class are privileged | Tighter coupling between two cooperating classes. |
| Friendship transitivity | **None** | Modular trust boundary. |
| Friendship inheritance | **None** | Privileges do not flow down hierarchies. |
| `private` × any inheritance | Always *inaccessible* in derived | Truly sealed. |
| `protected` × `public` inheritance | Stays `protected` | Derived may still use. |
| `protected` × `private` inheritance | Becomes `private` | Restricted even further. |
| `public` × `private` inheritance | Becomes `private` | Interface is sealed. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides exhaustive, line-by-line analytical reasoning and code-level derivations for the central access-protection scenarios tested by KTU.

## 3.1 Derivation: Access of `private` Members in a Derived Class

**Claim:** A `private` data member of a base class is never directly accessible in the body of a derived class, regardless of whether inheritance is `public`, `protected`, or `private`.

**Step-by-step logical proof:**

Consider a base class `B` with a private member `int x;` and a derived class `D` inheriting from `B`.

```cpp
class B {
    int x; // private by default in class
};
class D : public B { // try any inheritance mode
    void f() {
        x = 10;       // STEP 1: Compile attempt
    }
};
```

**Step 1 — Member resolution phase:**
The compiler looks up the identifier `x` in the lexical scope of `D::f()`. It finds that `x` is inherited from `B`, and it consults the access specifier of `x` in `B`, which is `private`.

**Step 2 — Access check phase:**
The compiler applies the rule:

$$
\text{Access}(x) = \text{accessibility of } x \text{ in } B \text{ as seen from } D
$$

For `private` members in `B`:

$$
\text{Access}(x) = \begin{cases} \text{accessible} & \text{if context scope} \subseteq B \\ \text{inaccessible} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

Since the current context is `D::f()`, the scope is not contained within `B`. Therefore:

$$
\text{Access}(x) = \text{inaccessible}
$$

**Step 3 — Diagnostic output:**
The compiler emits: `error: 'x' is a private member of 'B'`. This is true even under `public` inheritance, because the inheritance mode only transforms `public` and `protected` members; it does not resurrect `private` ones.

**Conclusion:** Hence proved. `private` members of a base class are inaccessible from any derived class scope.

## 3.2 Derivation: Access Transformation Under `protected` Inheritance

**Claim:** When a class is inherited using `protected` inheritance, the `public` and `protected` members of the base class become `protected` in the derived class.

**Proof by table construction:**

Let base class `B` declare a member `m` with access specifier `A_B`, and derived class `D` inherit `B` using mode `M`. The compiler computes:

$$
A_D(m) = T(A_B, M)
$$

where $T$ is the access transformation function defined by the C++ standard:

$$
T(A_B, M) = \begin{cases}
\text{public}      & \text{if } A_B = \text{public}      \text{ and } M = \text{public} \\
\text{protected}   & \text{if } A_B = \text{public}      \text{ and } M = \text{protected} \\
\text{private}     & \text{if } A_B = \text{public}      \text{ and } M = \text{private} \\
\text{protected}   & \text{if } A_B = \text{protected}   \text{ and } M = \text{public} \\
\text{protected}   & \text{if } A_B = \text{protected}   \text{ and } M = \text{protected} \\
\text{private}     & \text{if } A_B = \text{protected}   \text{ and } M = \text{private} \\
\text{inaccessible} & \text{if } A_B = \text{private}   \text{ (any } M \text{)}
\end{cases}
$$

For the case of interest ($A_B = \text{public}$ and $M = \text{protected}$), we apply the second row:

$$
T(\text{public}, \text{protected}) = \text{protected}
$$

Hence, under `protected` inheritance, all `public` members of `B` effectively become `protected` in `D`. This means they remain accessible to further subclasses of `D` but are no longer part of `D`'s public interface to the outside world.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Complete Code Walkthrough

The following program exhaustively demonstrates all the access-protection rules in a single compilable example.

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

class Base {
private:
    int pv = 1;             // accessible only inside Base
protected:
    int pt = 2;             // accessible in Base and any derived class
public:
    int pb = 3;             // accessible from anywhere
    void showBase() { cout << "Base: " << pv << " " << pt << " " << pb << endl; }
};

class DerivedPub : public Base {
    // pt remains protected, pb remains public
public:
    void accessTest() {
        // pv = 10;  // ERROR: private member of Base
        pt = 20;          // OK: protected, accessible in derived
        pb = 30;          // OK: public, accessible
    }
};

class DerivedPriv : private Base {
    // pt becomes private, pb becomes private
public:
    void accessTest() {
        // pv = 10; // ERROR: private member of Base
        pt = 20;        // OK: still accessible in DerivedPriv itself
        pb = 30;        // OK: still accessible in DerivedPriv itself
    }
};

class GrandChild : public DerivedPub {
public:
    void accessTest() {
        pt = 200;        // OK: pt is protected in DerivedPub
        pb = 300;        // OK: pb is public in DerivedPub
    }
};
```

**Line-by-line analytical commentary:**

- Line 7: `int pv = 1;` — declared `private`. Invisible to `DerivedPub`, `DerivedPriv`, `GrandChild`, and `main()`.
- Line 9: `int pt = 2;` — declared `protected`. Visible to `Base` and all its derived classes.
- Line 11: `int pb = 3;` — declared `public`. Visible to everyone.
- Line 15–18: `DerivedPub : public Base` — inheritance mode is `public`. According to the transformation function $T$:
  - $T(\text{public}, \text{public}) = \text{public}$ → `pb` stays public.
  - $T(\text{protected}, \text{public}) = \text{protected}$ → `pt` stays protected.
  - $T(\text{private}, \text{public}) = \text{inaccessible}$ → `pv` stays inaccessible.
- Line 24–30: `DerivedPriv : private Base` — inheritance mode is `private`. Apply the transformation:
  - $T(\text{public}, \text{private}) = \text{private}$ → `pb` becomes private.
  - $T(\text{protected}, \text{private}) = \text{private}$ → `pt` becomes private.
  - Note: the *base subobject* still exists, but its public interface is now sealed from the outside.
- Line 32–38: `GrandChild : public DerivedPub` — confirms the transitive effect: since `pt` was preserved as `protected` in `DerivedPub`, it remains accessible in `GrandChild`.

## 3.4 Friend Function — Analytical Walkthrough

```cpp
class Box {
private:
    int width;
public:
    Box(int w) : width(w) {}
    friend void printWidth(Box b);   // friend declaration
};

void printWidth(Box b) {
    cout << "Width: " << b.width;    // OK: friend function
}

int main() {
    Box b1(10);
    printWidth(b1);                  // OK
    // cout << b1.width;            // ERROR: width is private
    return 0;
}
```

**Analytical commentary:**

- Line 5: `friend void printWidth(Box b);` — this is a *declaration* inside the class. It tells the compiler: "The function `printWidth` is to be granted access to all my private and protected members."
- Line 9: Accessing `b.width` succeeds because `printWidth` is now a friend. The function itself is *not* a member of `Box`; it does not have a `this` pointer, and it is called without the dot operator (line 16).
- Line 19: Accessing `b1.width` from `main()` fails, because `main()` is neither a member of `Box` nor a friend of `Box`.

> [!TIP]
> **Symmetry Check for Exams:** If `B` declares `A` as a friend, can `A` declare `B` as a friend and access `B`'s privates? **No** — friendship is *not* symmetric unless explicitly declared both ways.

## 3.5 Friend Class — Analytical Walkthrough

```cpp
class Storage; // forward declaration

class Display {
public:
    void showStorage(Storage& s);
};

class Storage {
private:
    int secret = 42;
    friend class Display; // grants access to ALL member functions of Display
};

void Display::showStorage(Storage& s) {
    cout << "Secret: " << s.secret; // OK because Display is a friend
}
```

**Analytical commentary:**

- Line 8: `friend class Display;` — grants friendship to the entire `Display` class. Every member function of `Display` may now access the privates of `Storage`.
- Line 13: Inside `Display::showStorage`, access to `s.secret` is permitted due to the friend declaration.
- Note: Even though `Display` uses `Storage` and vice versa (a circular dependency), the forward declaration on line 1 resolves it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** Friends weaken encapsulation. Use them only when two classes are *tightly coupled by design* (e.g., iterators and containers, smart pointers and their target types, or operator overloads). Avoid using them merely as a workaround for poor design.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Access Decision Flowchart

This flowchart encodes the entire access-resolution algorithm used by the C++ compiler when a derived class (or external scope) attempts to access an inherited member.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Access attempt on inherited member m] --> B{What is the access specifier of m in the base class?}
    B -- private --> C[Result: INACCESSIBLE in derived class]
    B -- protected --> D{What is the inheritance mode?}
    B -- public --> D
    D -- public inheritance --> E[Stays protected if originally protected; stays public if originally public]
    D -- protected inheritance --> F[Both public and protected become protected in derived]
    D -- private inheritance --> G[Both public and protected become private in derived]
    E --> H[Now check the access context]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H{Where is the access being made?}
    H -- Inside a further derived class --> I{Is the current effective access public or protected?}
    H -- From main or unrelated code --> I
    I -- yes --> J[Result: ALLOWED]
    I -- no --> K[Result: DENIED compile error]
    C --> L[Compile Error: member is private in base]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Access Protection Layers

The following block diagram maps the *layers* of access protection as they appear in a typical C++ class design, from the most restrictive (innermost) to the most permissive (outermost), and shows which external entities can reach each layer.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ClassX["Class X"]
        L1["Layer 1: PRIVATE members<br/>- internal data<br/>- helper methods"]  --> L2["Layer 2: PROTECTED members<br/>- extension hooks for derived classes<br/>- virtual overridable functions"]
        L2 --> L3["Layer 3: PUBLIC members<br/>- constructors, destructors<br/>- public API methods<br/>- static factory functions"]
    end

    F1["Friend function f1"] -. "granted access to L1, L2, L3" .-> L1
    F2["Friend class FC"] -. "all its methods granted access to L1, L2, L3" .-> L1
    D1["Derived class D1 (public)"] --> L2
    D1 --> L3
    D1 -. "cannot access L1 directly" .-> L1
    EXT["External code / main()"] --> L3
    EXT -. "cannot access L1 or L2" .-> L1
```

## 4.3 Inheritance-Mode Transformation Diagram

This diagram visually demonstrates the *public → protected → private* transformation that occurs when the same base-class member is inherited under different modes.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Base["Base Class B"]
        BM1["public member m1"]
        BM2["protected member m2"]
        BM3["private member m3"]
    end

    BM1 -- "public inheritance" --> DP1["public in DerivedPub"]
    BM1 -- "protected inheritance" --> DPR1["protected in DerivedProt"]
    BM1 -- "private inheritance" --> DPRV1["private in DerivedPriv"]

    BM2 -- "public inheritance" --> DP2["protected in DerivedPub"]
    BM2 -- "protected inheritance" --> DPR2["protected in DerivedProt"]
    BM2 -- "private inheritance" --> DPRV2["private in DerivedPriv"]

    BM3 -- "any inheritance" --> DX["INACCESSIBLE in all derived classes"]
```

## 4.4 Friend Mechanism — Relationship Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Class A"] -- "friend class B (granted)" --> B["Class B"]
    A -. "B is NOT automatically a friend of A" .-> X["(Asymmetric)"]
    B -. "A is NOT automatically a friend of B" .-> Y["(Asymmetric)"]
    C["Derived class of B"] -. "NOT a friend of A by inheritance" .-> A
    D["Class D, also a friend of A"] -. "D is NOT a friend of B" .-> B
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows show the *granted* friendship direction. Dashed arrows show the common student misconceptions that are explicitly *false*. This is a high-yield visual for the 7-mark questions on friend semantics.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

This section mirrors the KTU 2024 Scheme assessment structure: short-answer Part A (3 marks) and detailed Part B (14 marks) with internal choice. Each question is tagged with a simulated past-year KTU reference and mapped to Course Outcomes (CO) and Bloom's Level.

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper, CO3, Bloom Level: Remember/Understand]**
Differentiate between `private` and `protected` access specifiers in C++ with respect to their accessibility from derived classes and from outside the class.

**Model Answer:**

`private` and `protected` are both non-public access specifiers in C++, meaning that members declared with either keyword cannot be accessed from outside the class through an object. The critical difference lies in their accessibility from **derived classes**.

- **`private` members** are accessible **only within the class in which they are declared**. They are not accessible from any derived class, even if the inheritance is public. They exist in the derived class's memory footprint but are sealed from direct access.
- **`protected` members** are accessible within the class in which they are declared and also within any class **derived** from it (via public, protected, or private inheritance). They serve as controlled extension points for subclasses.

**Example:**

```cpp
class Base {
private:   int a;     // not accessible in Derived
protected: int b;     // accessible in Derived
};
class Derived : public Base {
    void f() {
        // a = 5;  // ERROR
        b = 10;     // OK
    }
};
```

| Context | `private` | `protected` |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Same class | ✅ | ✅ |
| Derived class | ❌ | ✅ |
| Outside (via object) | ❌ | ❌ |

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question Paper, CO3, Bloom Level: Understand]**
What is a `friend` function in C++? State any two of its key properties.

**Model Answer:**

A **friend function** in C++ is a non-member function that is granted special privileged access to the `private` and `protected` members of a class. It is declared inside the class body by prefixing its prototype with the `friend` keyword.

**Key Properties (any two):**

1. **Friendship is not mutual:** If class `A` declares class `B` as a friend, it does not mean `A` is automatically a friend of `B`. The relationship is one-directional.
2. **Friendship is not inherited:** A friend of a base class is not automatically a friend of its derived classes. Likewise, friends of a derived class are not friends of the base.
3. **Friendship is not transitive:** If `A` is a friend of `B`, and `B` is a friend of `C`, `A` is **not** automatically a friend of `C`.
4. **A friend function is not a member function:** It does not have a `this` pointer and is called like a normal function (e.g., `printWidth(b1);` rather than `b1.printWidth();`).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A very common mistake is to assume that friendship is symmetric. If a student writes "If A is a friend of B, then B can also access A's privates" they will lose **1 mark** in a 3-mark question and the conceptual penalty in a 14-mark question.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Detailed Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern Reminder:** Part B questions carry **14 marks** and contain internal choice. Each question typically has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. The model answers below fully address both sub-parts with step-by-step reasoning.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Access Specifiers & Inheritance Mode

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Part B, CO3, Bloom Level: Apply/Analyze]**

**(a)** Explain the three access specifiers in C++ with suitable examples. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a suitable example program, demonstrate how the access specifier of a base class member changes under `public`, `protected`, and `private` inheritance. *(7 marks)*

---

#### (a) Model Answer — Three Access Specifiers

**Definition & Behavior:**

In C++, the three access specifiers — `public`, `private`, and `protected` — control the visibility and accessibility of class members.

1. **`private` (Most Restrictive):** Members declared `private` are accessible only within the class that declares them. They cannot be accessed by derived classes, friend functions (unless explicitly granted), or external code. Default access in a `class`.

2. **`protected` (Intermediate):** Members declared `protected` are accessible within the declaring class and within any class that derives from it (directly or transitively). They remain inaccessible from outside the class hierarchy.

3. **`public` (Most Permissive):** Members declared `public` form the public interface of the class. They are accessible from any scope — from the same class, from derived classes, from friend functions, and from external code using the dot/arrow operator on an object.

**Example Program:**

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

class Account {
private:
    double balance;            // strictly internal
protected:
    int accountNumber;         // accessible to derived account types
public:
    string holderName;         // public interface
    Account(double b, int n, string h) : balance(b), accountNumber(n), holderName(h) {}
    void deposit(double amt)   { if (amt > 0) balance += amt; }
    void showBalance() const   { cout << "Balance: " << balance << endl; }
};

class SavingsAccount : public Account {
    double interestRate;
public:
    SavingsAccount(double b, int n, string h, double r)
        : Account(b, n, h), interestRate(r) {}
    void showAccountNumber() {
        // balance = 100;        // ERROR: private, not accessible
        cout << "Acc#: " << accountNumber;  // OK: protected
        // holderName is accessible
    }
};

int main() {
    SavingsAccount sa(5000, 12345, "Anu", 4.5);
    cout << sa.holderName;        // OK: public
    // cout << sa.accountNumber;  // ERROR: protected
    // cout << sa.balance;        // ERROR: private
    sa.deposit(1000);
    sa.showBalance();
    return 0;
}
```

**Line-by-line explanation:**
- Line 5: `balance` is `private` — protected from any external or derived-class tampering.
- Line 7: `accountNumber` is `protected` — usable by derived account types like `SavingsAccount`.
- Line 9: `holderName` is `public` — part of the freely accessible interface.
- Lines 27–28 in `main()`: only `holderName` (public) is accessible; `accountNumber` and `balance` are inaccessible.

---

#### (b) Model Answer — Access Transformation Under Inheritance Modes

**Code:**

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

class Base {
public:
    int pub = 1;
protected:
    int prot = 2;
private:
    int priv = 3;
};

class PubDer : public Base {
    // pub   -> public
    // prot  -> protected
    // priv  -> inaccessible
public:
    void test() {
        cout << pub << " " << prot; // OK
        // cout << priv;           // ERROR
    }
};

class ProtDer : protected Base {
    // pub   -> protected
    // prot  -> protected
    // priv  -> inaccessible
public:
    void test() {
        cout << pub << " " << prot; // OK
    }
};

class PrivDer : private Base {
    // pub   -> private
    // prot  -> private
    // priv  -> inaccessible
public:
    void test() {
        cout << pub << " " << prot; // OK (still accessible in PrivDer itself)
    }
};

int main() {
    PubDer  a;  cout << a.pub;   // OK: pub is public in PubDer
    // ProtDer b;  cout << b.pub; // ERROR: pub is protected in ProtDer
    // PrivDer c;  cout << c.pub; // ERROR: pub is private in PrivDer
}
```

**Analytical Trace Table:**

| Base Member | `public` inheritance | `protected` inheritance | `private` inheritance |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| `int pub` (public) | public | protected | private |
| `int prot` (protected) | protected | protected | private |
| `int priv` (private) | inaccessible | inaccessible | inaccessible |

**Engineering Interpretation:**
- `PubDer` preserves the "is-a" relationship: the public API of `Base` is exposed.
- `ProtDer` and `PrivDer` represent "is-implemented-in-terms-of" — the inheritance is hidden from external users.
- The private member `priv` is never directly accessible, confirming the Golden Rule.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Friend Mechanism & Protected Members

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Part B, CO3, Bloom Level: Apply/Analyze]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of a `friend` function in C++. Write a C++ program that declares a non-member function as a friend of a class and uses it to compute the sum of two private data members. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain `protected` access specifier. How does it differ from `private`? Show with an example how a `protected` member of a base class is accessed in a derived class. *(7 marks)*

---

#### (a) Model Answer — Friend Function

**Concept:**

A `friend` function is a function that is not a member of a class but is granted access to the class's `private` and `protected` members. The class *grants* the friendship by declaring the function with the `friend` keyword inside its body. A friend function:
- Is declared inside the class but defined outside.
- Does not have a `this` pointer.
- Has privileged access to all members of the granting class.
- Is invoked like a regular function.

**Program:**

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

class Pair {
private:
    int a, b;
public:
    Pair(int x, int y) : a(x), b(y) {}
    // Friend function declaration
    friend int computeSum(Pair p);
};

// Friend function definition (not a member)
int computeSum(Pair p) {
    return p.a + p.b;   // OK: friend can access private members
}

int main() {
    Pair p1(15, 27);
    cout << "Sum = " << computeSum(p1) << endl;  // Output: Sum = 42
    return 0;
}
```

**Line-by-line analysis:**

- Line 4: `a` and `b` are `private`, so they are normally inaccessible from outside `Pair`.
- Line 8: `friend int computeSum(Pair p);` — the class `Pair` grants friendship to `computeSum`. The function is declared here but defined outside the class.
- Line 12: The function accesses `p.a` and `p.b` — this would be a compile error without the friend declaration.
- Line 18: `computeSum(p1)` is called like a normal function (not `p1.computeSum()`), because it is *not* a member function.

**Properties of `friend` functions (for completeness):**
- Not symmetric — `Pair` is not automatically a friend of `computeSum`.
- Not transitive — friends of friends are not friends.
- Not inherited — friends of a base class are not friends of the derived class.

---

#### (b) Model Answer — `protected` vs. `private`

**`protected` Access Specifier:**

The `protected` access specifier is a hybrid of `private` and `public`. Members declared as `protected`:
- **Are** accessible from within the class that declares them.
- **Are** accessible from any class that derives (publicly, protectedly, or privately) from the declaring class.
- **Are not** accessible from outside the class hierarchy through an object.

**Difference between `private` and `protected`:**

| Aspect | `private` | `protected` |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Accessible in same class | Yes | Yes |
| Accessible in derived class | No | Yes |
| Accessible from outside (object) | No | No |

The single distinguishing feature is **derived-class accessibility**: `private` is sealed from derived classes, while `protected` is open to them.

**Example — `protected` Member Access in a Derived Class:**

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

class Animal {
protected:
    string name;
    int age;
public:
    Animal(string n, int a) : name(n), age(a) {}
};

class Dog : public Animal {
    string breed;
public:
    Dog(string n, int a, string b) : Animal(n, a), breed(b) {}
    void display() {
        cout << "Name: " << name << ", Age: " << age << ", Breed: " << breed << endl;
        // Accessing protected members 'name' and 'age' from the derived class — OK
    }
};

int main() {
    Dog d("Bruno", 5, "Labrador");
    d.display();
    // cout << d.name;  // ERROR: protected, not accessible from main()
    return 0;
}
```

**Output:**
```
Name: Bruno, Age: 5, Breed: Labrador
```

**Line-by-line analysis:**
- Line 5: `name` and `age` are `protected` in `Animal`.
- Line 14: Inside `Dog::display()`, both `name` and `age` are accessible because `Dog` inherits from `Animal`.
- Line 23: From `main()`, `d.name` is **not** accessible, because `protected` is not part of the public interface. This is the key point of difference from `public`.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part B):**
> - In sub-part (a), students often forget to write the *friend* declaration inside the class and only write the global function — this loses **2 marks** because the friend declaration is the heart of the mechanism.
> - In sub-part (b), students sometimes confuse `protected` with `public` and try to access the member from `main()` — this loses **1–2 marks** and weakens the conceptual distinction.
> - Forgetting to mention that `protected` is **not** accessible from outside the class (only from derived classes) is a recurring deduction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this checklist during the last 5 minutes before the exam.**

- ✅ **Three access specifiers:** `public` (open to all), `protected` (open to derived only), `private` (open to same class only).
- ✅ **Default access:** `class` → `private`; `struct` → `public`.
- ✅ **`private` members of a base class are NEVER directly accessible in a derived class** — under ANY inheritance mode.
- ✅ **Inheritance modes and `public` member transformation:** public → public, protected → private, private → private.
- ✅ **Inheritance modes and `protected` member transformation:** public → protected, protected → protected, private → private.
- ✅ **`friend` is not a member function** — no `this` pointer, called like a global function.
- ✅ **Friendship is not symmetric, not transitive, and not inherited.**
- ✅ **`friend` classes** grant access to ALL their member functions, but the privilege is one-directional.
- ✅ **Public inheritance** models "is-a"; **private/protected inheritance** models "is-implemented-in-terms-of".
- ✅ **Engineering wisdom:** Prefer `private` data + `public` accessors (getters/setters) to enforce invariants. Use `protected` only for genuine extension points. Use `friend` sparingly, only for tightly coupled collaborators.
- ✅ **Common compile-error phrases to recognize in exams:** *"is a private member of..."*, *"is protected within this context"*, *"friend declaration specifier is incompatible with default access"*.
- ✅ **The order of access openness from strictest to loosest:** `private` < `protected` < `public` — the **PPO Triad**.
- ✅ **The most-tested KTU fact:** `private` inheritance makes the *public* and *protected* members of the base class *private* in the derived class — and that the derived class is **not** a subtype of the base class in the "is-a" sense.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Importing Packages, Interfaces - Interfaces vs Abstract Classes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT633 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Importing Packages, Interfaces - Interfaces vs Abstract Classes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Importing Packages — Formal Definition

A **package** in Java is a **namespace** that organizes a set of related classes, interfaces, enumerations, and sub-packages. Importing a package is the mechanism by which a Java source file (`.java`) gains **visibility** to pre-compiled classes stored in the standard library, third-party JARs, or user-defined folders, without using their fully qualified class names (FQCN) every time.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (verbatim syllabus-aligned):**
> *"A package is a grouping of related types (classes, interfaces, enumerations, and annotations) providing access protection and namespace management. The `import` statement makes types from another package accessible by their simple name."*

**Two import mechanisms recognized by the KTU syllabus:**

| Mechanism | Syntax | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Type-import-on-demand** | `import java.util.*;` | Imports all top-level public types of the package |
| **Single-type-import** | `import java.util.ArrayList;` | Imports exactly one specific type |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cupboard Drawer" Model

Imagine a giant office cupboard with **labeled drawers**: one drawer named `java.util`, another named `java.io`, and so on. Every drawer contains neatly filed folders (classes like `ArrayList`, `Scanner`, `HashMap`).

- **Without `import`:** To use `ArrayList`, you must say: *"Open the cupboard, go to drawer `java.util`, pull out the folder named `ArrayList`"* → `java.util.ArrayList list = new java.util.ArrayList();`
- **With `import java.util.ArrayList;`:** You are essentially saying *"I will be using folders from the `java.util` drawer today, just hand them to me when I call their name"* → `ArrayList list = new ArrayList();`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **`import` is NOT `#include` of C/C++.** It does **not** physically copy code. It is purely a **compiler directive** that resolves shorthand names to FQCNs during compilation. No import = no runtime cost.

### 1.3 Interfaces — Formal Definition

An **interface** in Java is a **purely abstract contract** (until Java 8) that specifies a set of **method signatures** (and constants) that a class must implement. From Java 8 onwards, interfaces may also contain `default` and `static` methods with bodies.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:**
> *"An interface is a reference type in Java, similar to a class, that can contain only constants, method signatures, default methods, static methods, and nested types. It cannot be instantiated — it can only be implemented by classes or extended by other interfaces."*

### 1.4 Interfaces — Intuitive Analogy (The "Job Description" Model)

An **interface is like a job description posted on a company notice board.** It says:

- "The candidate **MUST HAVE** these skills (method signatures)"
- "A skill labelled *abstract* means: the company does not care *how* you do it, only *that* you can do it"
- "Any class that *signs* this job description (i.e., `implements` the interface) **promises** to provide all the listed skills"

**Example:** A `Drone` interface may demand `takeOff()`, `land()`, and `rotate()`. A `Quadcopter` class implements `Drone` and provides its own propeller-based logic. A `FixedWingUAV` class implements the same `Drone` interface but uses different aerodynamics. **Both are drones**, but they fly differently.

### 1.5 Abstract Classes — Formal Definition

An **abstract class** in Java is a class declared with the `abstract` keyword that **cannot be instantiated directly** and may contain a **mixture of implemented (concrete) methods and unimplemented (abstract) methods**. It is meant to be **extended** by subclasses that provide implementations for the abstract methods.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:**
> *"An abstract class is a class that is declared abstract — it may or may not include abstract methods. Abstract classes cannot be instantiated, but they can be subclassed."*

### 1.6 Abstract Classes — Intuitive Analogy (The "Partially Complete Blueprint" Model)

An **abstract class is a partially built house blueprint.** The architect (abstract class) has:

- Already drawn the **foundation, walls, and roof** → these are *concrete methods* (fully implemented)
- Left certain rooms (e.g., the **interior design**) as labelled blanks saying *"To be designed by the homeowner"* → these are *abstract methods* (no body)

A **regular concrete subclass** (e.g., `ModernInterior`) must fill in the blanks. The architect may also enforce certain rooms (abstract methods) that **every homeowner must design**, but the homeowner is **free to design them however they like**.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pre-Exam Distinction (High-Yield for KTU):**
> - **Interface** = **100% contract** (purely *what must be done*, traditionally nothing on *how*).
> - **Abstract Class** = **Partial contract + partial implementation** (some *what* + some *how*).
> - A class can **extend only ONE class** (abstract or otherwise), but can **implement MANY interfaces**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of an `import` Statement — Step-by-Step Mechanics

When the Java compiler (`javac`) encounters `import java.util.ArrayList;`, the following **5-stage resolution** occurs:

1. **Lexical Parsing:** The compiler tokenizes the source file. The token `ArrayList` is flagged as a *single-type-import-on-demand candidate*.
2. **Classpath Search:** The compiler searches the **classpath** (set by `CLASSPATH` env variable, `-cp` flag, or IDE) for the package `java/util/`.
3. **Type Resolution:** Inside `java/util/`, the compiler locates the compiled `.class` file `ArrayList.class`.
4. **FQCN Binding:** The compiler binds the simple name `ArrayList` to the fully qualified name `java.util.ArrayList` within the current compilation unit.
5. **Compile-Time Verification:** If `ArrayList.class` is not found → `error: cannot find symbol` (compile-time error, **not** runtime).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Java implicitly imports `java.lang.*` for every source file.** This is why you never need to write `import java.lang.String;`. All classes in `java.lang` (e.g., `String`, `System`, `Math`, `Object`) are auto-available.

### 2.2 Static Imports — Special Import Variant

Introduced in **Java 5**, static imports allow access to **static members** (fields/methods) of a class without class-name qualification.

**Example:**

```java
import static java.lang.Math.PI;
import static java.lang.Math.sqrt;

public class Circle {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        double area = PI * sqrt(25);   // No need for Math.PI or Math.sqrt
    }
}
```

### 2.3 Package Naming Conventions & Directory Structure

| Convention | Rule | Example |
|---|---|---|
| All **lowercase** | Avoid clash with class names | `com.ktu.pcit.oop` |
| **Reverse-DNS** prefix | Globally unique | `in.ac.ktu.pcit` |
| **Folders mirror packages** | Directory hierarchy = package hierarchy | `com/ktu/pcit/oop/MyClass.java` |
| One **public class per file** | Public class name = filename | `MyClass.java` → `public class MyClass` |

### 2.4 Interface Structure — Component Anatomy

An interface can contain the following members:

| Member Type | Allowed? | Default Qualifier | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Abstract methods** | ✅ Yes | `public abstract` (implicit) | No body, ends with `;` |
| **Default methods** (Java 8+) | ✅ Yes | `public` (implicit) | Has body, marked `default` |
| **Static methods** (Java 8+) | ✅ Yes | `public` (implicit) | Has body, called via interface name |
| **Constant fields** | ✅ Yes | `public static final` (implicit) | Must be initialized at declaration |
| **Private methods** (Java 9+) | ✅ Yes | `private` | Helper methods for default methods |
| **Instance variables** | ❌ No | — | Not allowed (state lives in implementing class) |
| **Constructor** | ❌ No | — | Interfaces cannot be instantiated |

### 2.5 Abstract Class Structure — Component Anatomy

| Member Type | Allowed? | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Abstract methods** | ✅ Yes | Declared with `abstract`, no body |
| **Concrete methods** | ✅ Yes | Fully implemented methods |
| **Instance variables** | ✅ Yes | State that subclasses inherit |
| **Static variables & methods** | ✅ Yes | Shared across all subclasses |
| **Constructors** | ✅ Yes | Called via `super(...)` from subclass |
| **Final methods** | ✅ Yes | Cannot be overridden by subclasses |

### 2.6 The "Golden Rules" of Interface vs Abstract Class (KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 — Memorize This Table for ESE. It Appears Almost Every Year.**

| # | Rule | Interface | Abstract Class |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Keyword** | `interface` | `abstract class` |
| 2 | **Instantiation** | ❌ Not allowed | ❌ Not allowed |
| 3 | **Method bodies (pre-Java 8)** | ❌ All abstract | ✅ Mix of abstract + concrete |
| 4 | **Method bodies (Java 8+)** | ✅ `default` & `static` allowed | ✅ All types allowed |
| 5 | **Instance variables** | ❌ Only `public static final` constants | ✅ Any access modifier |
| 6 | **Constructor** | ❌ None | ✅ Yes |
| 7 | **Multiple inheritance** | ✅ A class can implement **N** interfaces | ❌ A class can extend **only 1** class |
| 8 | **Inheritance keyword** | `implements` (class→interface) | `extends` (class→class) |
| 9 | **Access modifiers for methods** | `public` only (pre-Java 9) | Any (`public`, `protected`, `private`) |
| 10 | **Speed (loose rule)** | Slightly slower (extra indirection) | Slightly faster (direct method dispatch) |
| 11 | **Use case** | Define a **contract/capability** (e.g., `Comparable`, `Serializable`) | Define a **common base with shared state/code** (e.g., `HttpServlet`) |
| 12 | **Added in Java** | JDK 1.0 | JDK 1.0 |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Concept | Industry Use-Case |
|---|---|
| **Packages** | Modular deployment in **Maven/Gradle** projects; namespace isolation in microservices |
| **Interfaces** | **JDBC** (`Connection`, `Statement` interfaces) — backend-agnostic DB access; **Strategy Design Pattern** |
| **Abstract Classes** | **Spring Framework** templates (`JdbcTemplate`, `RestTemplate`); **JUnit** test scaffolding |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Decision Tree: "Should I use Interface or Abstract Class?"

```
                    START
                      │
        ┌─────────────┴─────────────┐
        │                           │
   Do you need to share         Do you need
   STATE (instance vars)        SHARED CODE
   among related classes?       (concrete methods)
        │                           │
       YES                         YES
        │                           │
   USE ABSTRACT                   USE ABSTRACT
      CLASS                         CLASS
        │                           │
        └─────────────┬─────────────┘
                      │
        Do you need MULTIPLE
        inheritance of type?
        (i.e., class must be >1 "thing")
                      │
                ┌─────┴─────┐
               YES          NO
                │            │
            USE BOTH:    USE EITHER
        Abstract class +     (default to
         N Interfaces         INTERFACE
                                for new code)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modern Java Best Practice (Post-Java 8):** Prefer **interfaces with `default` methods** over abstract classes for new code, because interfaces support multiple inheritance. Use **abstract classes** only when you genuinely need shared state (instance fields) or constructors.

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Building a Package and Importing It

**Step 1: Create the package directory structure**

```
C:\JavaProjects\
   └── src\
       └── com\ktu\geometry\
           ├── Shape.java
           └── Circle.java
   └── app\
       └── MainApp.java
```

**Step 2: Define the package `com.ktu.geometry.Shape`**

```java
// File: com/ktu/geometry/Shape.java
package com.ktu.geometry;

public interface Shape {
    double PI_VALUE = 3.14159;             // implicitly public static final
    double area();                          // implicitly public abstract
    double perimeter();                     // implicitly public abstract

    default String describe() {             // Java 8+ default method
        return "A geometric shape";
    }

    static Shape createUnitCircle() {       // Java 8+ static factory
        return new Circle(1.0);
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step logical analysis:**

- Line `package com.ktu.geometry;` → tells compiler this class lives in the `com.ktu.geometry` namespace.
- `public interface Shape` → contract accessible from any package.
- `double PI_VALUE = 3.14159;` → **implicitly** `public static final`. Every implementing class sees this constant.
- `double area();` → **implicitly** `public abstract`. No body.
- `default String describe()` → concrete body, available to all implementing classes unless overridden.
- `static Shape createUnitCircle()` → utility factory method called as `Shape.createUnitCircle()`.

**Step 3: Implement the interface in `Circle.java`**

```java
// File: com/ktu/geometry/Circle.java
package com.ktu.geometry;

public class Circle implements Shape {
    private double radius;

    public Circle(double radius) {
        this.radius = radius;
    }

    @Override
    public double area() {
        return PI_VALUE * radius * radius;
    }

    @Override
    public double perimeter() {
        return 2 * PI_VALUE * radius;
    }
}
```

**Logical analysis of each line:**

- `implements Shape` → Circle promises to provide `area()` and `perimeter()`. If it doesn't → **compile error**.
- `@Override` → annotation; tells compiler to verify that the method actually overrides an interface method (catches typos).
- `PI_VALUE` is accessed directly from the interface (no `Shape.PI_VALUE` needed because it's inherited as a static constant).

**Step 4: Compile and use from a different package**

```java
// File: app/MainApp.java
package app;

import com.ktu.geometry.Shape;             // single-type import
import com.ktu.geometry.Circle;            // single-type import

public class MainApp {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Shape s = new Circle(5.0);          // polymorphism: interface reference, impl object
        System.out.println("Area: " + s.area());
        System.out.println(s.describe());   // inherited default method
    }
}
```

**Compile sequence (terminal commands):**

```
cd C:\JavaProjects\src
javac com/ktu/geometry/Shape.java
javac com/ktu/geometry/Circle.java
cd ..
javac -cp src app/MainApp.java
java -cp src;app app.MainApp
```

**Expected output:**

```
Area: 78.53975
A geometric shape
```

### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Abstract Class with Mixed Methods

```java
package com.ktu.vehicles;

public abstract class Vehicle {
    protected int speed;                      // shared STATE (instance var)
    protected String fuelType;

    public Vehicle(int speed, String fuelType) {  // CONSTRUCTOR
        this.speed = speed;
        this.fuelType = fuelType;
    }

    public void displayInfo() {                  // CONCRETE method
        System.out.println("Speed: " + speed + " km/h, Fuel: " + fuelType);
    }

    public abstract double calculateMileage();   // ABSTRACT method — no body
}
```

**Subclass implementation:**

```java
package com.ktu.vehicles;

public class ElectricCar extends Vehicle {
    private double batteryCapacity;

    public ElectricCar(int speed, double batteryCapacity) {
        super(speed, "Electric");                // calls abstract class constructor
        this.batteryCapacity = batteryCapacity;
    }

    @Override
    public double calculateMileage() {
        return batteryCapacity * 5.0;            // arbitrary formula
    }
}
```

**Logical analysis:**

- `extends Vehicle` → single inheritance from abstract class.
- `super(speed, "Electric")` → MUST be the first statement. Initializes inherited `speed` and `fuelType`.
- `calculateMileage()` MUST be overridden — otherwise `ElectricCar` must also be declared `abstract`.

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Proof: Why Multiple Inheritance Works Only with Interfaces

Consider two interfaces:

```java
interface Flyable { void fly(); }
interface Swimmable { void swim(); }
```

A class can do both:

```java
class Duck implements Flyable, Swimmable {     // ✅ N interfaces allowed
    public void fly() { System.out.println("Flapping wings"); }
    public void swim() { System.out.println("Paddling feet"); }
}
```

But for classes:

```java
class AmphibiousCar extends Boat, Car { }       // ❌ COMPILE ERROR
```

**Why?** If `Boat` and `Car` both define a method `startEngine()`, the compiler cannot decide **which** version `AmphibiousCar` inherits — the **"Diamond Problem."** Interfaces sidestep this because their methods are (historically) abstract, leaving the implementing class to resolve the conflict.

**Java 8+ resolution rule for default method conflicts:** If a class inherits two interfaces with the same `default` method, the class **must explicitly override** it:

```java
interface A { default void show() { System.out.println("A"); } }
interface B { default void show() { System.out.println("B"); } }

class C implements A, B {
    @Override
    public void show() {
        A.super.show();       // explicitly choose A's version
    }
}
```

### 3.5 Analytical Formula Sheet (LaTeX-Isolated)

$$
\text{Inheritance Depth Constraint:} \quad \forall \text{ class } C, \quad \vert \text{extends}(C) \vert \leq 1
$$

$$
\text{Interface Implementation Count:} \quad \forall \text{ class } C, \quad \vert \text{implements}(C) \vert \geq 0
$$

$$
\text{Abstract Method Fulfillment:} \quad \text{isAbstract}(C) = \text{false} \iff \forall m \in \text{abstractMethods}(P), \; m \in \text{overridden}(C)
$$

Where $P$ is the parent (abstract class or interface), and $C$ is the concrete subclass.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Package Import Resolution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source File .java] --> B[Compiler Reads import Statement]
    B --> C{Classpath Search}
    C -->|Found| D[Bind Simple Name to FQCN]
    C -->|Not Found| E[Compile Error: cannot find symbol]
    D --> F[Compilation Continues]
    F --> G[Bytecode .class Generated]
    G --> H[Runtime: JVM Uses Fully Qualified Names]
```

### 4.2 Interface vs Abstract Class — Structural Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INTF[Interface Shape]
        I1[Constant: PI_VALUE 3.14]
        I2[Abstract: area]
        I3[Abstract: perimeter]
        I4[Default: describe]
        I5[Static: createUnitCircle]
    end

    subgraph ABS[Abstract Class Vehicle]
        A1[Field: speed]
        A2[Field: fuelType]
        A3[Constructor: Vehicle]
        A4[Concrete: displayInfo]
        A5[Abstract: calculateMileage]
    end

    subgraph IMPL[Implementing Classes]
        C1[Circle implements Shape]
        C2[Rectangle implements Shape]
        E1[ElectricCar extends Vehicle]
        E2[PetrolCar extends Vehicle]
    end

    INTF -. implements .-> C1
    INTF -. implements .-> C2
    ABS -. extends .-> E1
    ABS -. extends .-> E2
```

### 4.3 Decision Flow — Choosing Interface vs Abstract Class

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START([New Type to Design]) --> Q1{Is shared STATE needed?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| USE_ABS[Use ABSTRACT CLASS]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Need multiple type inheritance?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| USE_INT[Use INTERFACE]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Need shared concrete code?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| USE_INT_DEF[Use INTERFACE with default methods]
    Q3 -->|No| USE_INT_SIMPLE[Use plain INTERFACE]

    USE_ABS --> DONE([End])
    USE_INT --> DONE
    USE_INT_DEF --> DONE
    USE_INT_SIMPLE --> DONE
```

### 4.4 Memory Layout — Interface vs Class vtable

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OBJ_CIRCLE[Circle Object Heap Memory]
        OC1[Field: radius = 5.0]
    end

    subgraph VTABLE_CIRCLE[Circle vtable Method Table]
        VT1[area points to Circle.area]
        VT2[perimeter points to Circle.perimeter]
        VT3[describe points to Shape.describe default]
        VT4[Object.toString points to Circle.toString]
    end

    OBJ_CIRCLE --> VTABLE_CIRCLE
```
