<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Exam]`

**What is a package in Java? Explain the difference between `import java.util.*;` and `import java.util.ArrayList;` with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **package** is a namespace that organizes a set of related classes and interfaces in Java, providing access protection and avoiding name conflicts. Packages map directly to directory structures on the file system.

- `import java.util.*;` is a **type-import-on-demand** declaration. It makes **all public types** of the `java.util` package available by their simple name. Example: after this import, you can use `ArrayList`, `HashMap`, `Scanner`, `LinkedList` — all without further qualification.
- `import java.util.ArrayList;` is a **single-type-import** declaration. It makes **only the `ArrayList` class** available by its simple name. Other classes from `java.util` would still need their FQCN or separate imports.

**Example:**

```java
import java.util.*;             // makes ArrayList, Scanner, etc. all available
class Demo {
    ArrayList list = new ArrayList();    // OK
    Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in); // OK
}
```

```java
import java.util.ArrayList;     // only ArrayList is unqualified
class Demo {
    ArrayList list = new ArrayList();     // OK
    // Scanner sc = new Scanner(...);     // ERROR — Scanner not imported
    java.util.Scanner sc = new java.util.Scanner(System.in); // OK with FQCN
}
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "`import` reduces code size at runtime." This is **WRONG** — `import` is a **compile-time** directive only. It has **zero runtime impact**. Marks deducted: **−1**.

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**List any three differences between an interface and an abstract class in Java.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Interface | Abstract Class |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Supports **multiple** implementation (`implements A, B`) | Supports **single** inheritance (`extends X` only) |
| 2 | Cannot have **instance variables** (only `public static final` constants) | Can have **instance variables** of any access type |
| 3 | Cannot have a **constructor** | Can have a **constructor** (called via `super()`) |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of packages in Java. With suitable examples, illustrate (i) creating a user-defined package, (ii) importing the package into another program, and (iii) the use of `import static`.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Define an interface in Java. Write a Java program to define an interface `BankAccount` with methods `deposit(double)`, `withdraw(double)`, and `getBalance()`. Implement this interface in a class `SavingsAccount` and demonstrate polymorphism.

---

#### **Model Solution (a) — Packages**

**Definition (2 marks):** A **package** is a grouping of related types providing namespace management and access control. The `package` keyword declares the package to which a `.java` file belongs.

**(i) Creating a user-defined package (2 marks):**

Save as `com/ktu/bank/BankOps.java`:

```java
package com.ktu.bank;

public class BankOps {
    public static double interest(double principal, double rate, int years) {
        return principal * rate * years / 100.0;
    }
}
```

Compile:

```
javac -d . com/ktu/bank/BankOps.java
```

The `-d .` flag tells the compiler to create the directory structure `com/ktu/bank/` and place `BankOps.class` inside.

**(ii) Importing the package (2 marks):**

Save as `Main.java`:

```java
import com.ktu.bank.BankOps;             // single-type import

public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        double si = BankOps.interest(10000, 5, 3);
        System.out.println("Simple Interest = " + si);
    }
}
```

**(iii) `import static` (1 mark):**

```java
import static java.lang.Math.max;
import static com.ktu.bank.BankOps.interest;

public class Demo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println(max(10, 20));            // direct static method call
        System.out.println(interest(5000, 7, 2));   // direct static method call
    }
}
```

`import static` allows static members to be accessed without class-name qualification.

---

#### **Model Solution (b) — Interface & Polymorphism**

**Interface definition (2 marks):**

```java
interface BankAccount {
    void deposit(double amount);
    void withdraw(double amount);
    double getBalance();
}
```

**Implementation class (3 marks):**

```java
class SavingsAccount implements BankAccount {
    private double balance;

    public SavingsAccount(double initialBalance) {
        this.balance = initialBalance;
    }

    @Override
    public void deposit(double amount) {
        if (amount > 0) balance += amount;
    }

    @Override
    public void withdraw(double amount) {
        if (amount > 0 && amount <= balance) balance -= amount;
    }

    @Override
    public double getBalance() {
        return balance;
    }
}
```

**Polymorphism demonstration (2 marks):**

```java
public class BankDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        BankAccount acc = new SavingsAccount(1000.0);   // interface reference!
        acc.deposit(500);
        acc.withdraw(200);
        System.out.println("Final Balance: " + acc.getBalance());
    }
}
```

**Output:**

```
Final Balance: 1300.0
```

**Explanation:** The reference variable `acc` is of type `BankAccount` (interface), but it points to a `SavingsAccount` object. This is **runtime polymorphism** — the JVM dispatches the calls to `SavingsAccount`'s actual methods at runtime.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is an abstract class? Explain with a Java program how abstract classes can have constructors, instance variables, and a mix of abstract and concrete methods.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast interfaces and abstract classes in Java. Give one real-world scenario where each is the most appropriate choice.

---

#### **Model Solution (a) — Abstract Class**

**Definition (1 mark):** An **abstract class** is a class declared with the `abstract` keyword that **cannot be instantiated** and may declare **abstract methods** (methods without a body) alongside **concrete methods** (with implementation).

**Java program (5 marks):**

```java
abstract class Employee {
    protected String name;
    protected double baseSalary;

    // Constructor
    public Employee(String name, double baseSalary) {
        this.name = name;
        this.baseSalary = baseSalary;
    }

    // Concrete method
    public void displayInfo() {
        System.out.println("Name: " + name + ", Base Salary: " + baseSalary);
    }

    // Abstract method
    public abstract double calculateSalary();
}

class Manager extends Employee {
    private double bonus;

    public Manager(String name, double baseSalary, double bonus) {
        super(name, baseSalary);            // call abstract class constructor
        this.bonus = bonus;
    }

    @Override
    public double calculateSalary() {
        return baseSalary + bonus;
    }
}

class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Employee e = new Manager("Alice", 50000, 15000);
        e.displayInfo();
        System.out.println("Total Salary: " + e.calculateSalary());
    }
}
```

**Output:**

```
Name: Alice, Base Salary: 50000.0
Total Salary: 65000.0
```

**Key points demonstrated (1 mark):**
- `protected` instance variables (`name`, `baseSalary`) — shared state in subclasses.
- Constructor takes parameters and initializes state.
- Concrete method `displayInfo()` is reused by all subclasses.
- Abstract method `calculateSalary()` is implemented differently in `Manager`.

---

#### **Model Solution (b) — Interface vs Abstract Class**

**Comparison table (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Interface | Abstract Class |
|---|---|---|
| Inheritance | Multiple (`implements A, B, C`) | Single (`extends X`) |
| State (instance vars) | None (constants only) | Allowed |
| Constructor | None | Allowed |
| Method types | Abstract, default, static | Abstract + concrete |
| When to use | Define a **capability/role** | Define a **common base** with shared code/state |

**Real-world scenario 1 — Interface (2 marks):** The **JDBC `Connection` interface** in Java. Database vendors (Oracle, MySQL, PostgreSQL) provide their own implementations (`OracleConnection`, `MySQLConnection`). Application code uses only the `Connection` interface, making the application **database-agnostic**.

**Real-world scenario 2 — Abstract Class (2 marks):** The **Spring `AbstractController`** class in the Spring MVC framework. It provides common request-handling infrastructure (concrete methods like `handleRequest`) and forces subclasses to implement `handleRequestInternal` (abstract). All controllers share boilerplate but customize the core logic.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **−1 mark** for writing *"abstract class is the same as interface"* (they are fundamentally different).
> 2. **−2 marks** for not showing the `super()` call in the abstract class subclass constructor.
> 3. **−1 mark** for claiming interfaces **cannot** have static methods (they can, since Java 8).
> 4. **−1 mark** for forgetting the `@Override` annotation when implementing interface methods (not a compile error, but **best practice** in KTU's outcome-based marking).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU ESE Module 3:**

- ✅ A **package** is a **directory-based namespace**; `package` declaration must be the **first non-comment statement** in a `.java` file.
- ✅ `import` is a **compile-time** directive — it does **not** affect runtime bytecode size.
- ✅ `java.lang.*` is **auto-imported** for every Java source file.
- ✅ `import static` (Java 5+) allows direct access to static members without class-name prefix.
- ✅ **Interface** = pure contract; **abstract class** = partial contract + shared state.
- ✅ Interfaces can have **constants** (`public static final` implicit), **abstract methods**, **`default` methods** (Java 8+), **`static` methods** (Java 8+), and **`private` methods** (Java 9+).
- ✅ Abstract classes can have **constructors**, **instance variables**, and any combination of **abstract + concrete** methods.
- ✅ A class can **extend only ONE class** (abstract or otherwise) — Java does not support C++-style multiple class inheritance.
- ✅ A class can **implement MULTIPLE interfaces** — this is how Java achieves multiple inheritance of **type**.
- ✅ If two interfaces provide **conflicting `default` methods**, the implementing class **must override** the method and disambiguate via `InterfaceName.super.method()`.
- ✅ **Polymorphism rule:** An interface reference can hold any object of its implementing class; method calls are resolved at **runtime** (dynamic dispatch).
- ✅ `final` keyword on a class **prevents inheritance**; `final` on a method **prevents overriding**.
- ✅ **Naming convention:** package names are **all lowercase**; interface names are **adjectives or nouns** ending in `-able` (e.g., `Runnable`, `Serializable`, `Comparable`).
- ✅ Always use **`@Override`** annotation when implementing interface methods or extending abstract classes — it catches typos at compile time.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Defining an Interface
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECIT633 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Defining an Interface # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In the context of Object-Oriented Programming using **Java**, an **Interface** is a reference type, similar to a class, that can contain **only constants, method signatures, default methods, static methods, and nested types**. Method bodies exist **only for default methods and static methods**. Interfaces cannot be instantiated — they can only be **implemented** by classes or **extended** by other interfaces. According to the KTU 2024 scheme (aligned with Java 8+ specifications), an interface serves as a **purely abstract contract** that defines *what* a class must do, but not *how* it does it. It is the most powerful tool in Java for achieving **full abstraction** and **multiple inheritance of type**. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Expected Wording):** > *"An interface in Java is a blueprint of a class. It has static constants and abstract methods (since Java 8, it can also have default and static methods). The interface in Java is a mechanism to achieve full abstraction and multiple inheritance."* --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are plugging an electrical appliance into a **wall socket**. The socket has a **standardized shape** (3-pin, 5-amp). Whether you buy a TV, a refrigerator, or a laptop charger, every manufacturer must follow that same **contract** so the plug fits. The wall socket does not care *how* the appliance works internally; it only guarantees the **interface** (pin layout, voltage rating = 230V, frequency = 50Hz). In Java: - The **Interface** = The wall socket (the *contract*). - The **Class implementing the interface** = The appliance (the *concrete behaviour*). - The **Methods in the interface** = The pins/connectors (the *expected actions*). - The **Class must "fit"** = The class must provide implementations for *every* method declared in the interface. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memory Hook for Exam:** Think **I-S-P-A-C-E** > **I**nterface = **S**pecification/contract **P**rotocol, **A**ll methods abstract (by default), **C**annot be instantiated, **E**nables multiple inheritance. --- ## 1.3 Why Interfaces Exist — The Engineering Motivation | Real-world Engineering Problem | Java Solution via Interface | | :--- | :--- | | Two unrelated classes (e.g., `Bird` and `Aeroplane`) need a common type to be grouped (`Flyable`). | Common interface `Flyable` implemented by both. | | Need to define a callback/handler contract (e.g., `ActionListener`). | Interface with one method (`actionPerformed`). | | Need to expose an API without revealing implementation. | Interface published, implementation hidden (information hiding + abstraction). | | Need to inherit behaviour contract from more than one source. | Java forbids multiple class inheritance but **permits multiple interface implementation**. | --- ## 1.4 Visual Summary Box > [!NOTE] > **Key Properties of an Interface (Board Exam Favourite):** > 1. Declared using the keyword `interface`, not `class`. > 2. All data members are implicitly `public static final` (constants). > 3. All methods (before Java 8) are implicitly `public abstract`. > 4. Cannot have constructors. > 5. Cannot be instantiated using `new`. > 6. Supports multiple inheritance via the `extends` keyword (interface-to-interface) and `implements` keyword (class-to-interface). > 7. Since **Java 8**: can have `default` and `static` methods with body. > 8. Since **Java 9**: can have `private` methods for internal helper use. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of an Interface — The Official Syntax The general structure for defining an interface in Java is governed by the **Java Language Specification (JLS §9)**. ```java [access_modifier] interface InterfaceName [extends OtherInterfaces] { // 1. Constant fields (implicitly public static final) [public static final] dataType CONSTANT_NAME = value; // 2. Abstract method declarations (implicitly public abstract) [public abstract] returnType methodName(parameters); // 3. Default method (Java 8+) [public] default returnType methodName(parameters) { /* body */ } // 4. Static method (Java 8+) [public static] returnType methodName(parameters) { /* body */ } // 5. Private method (Java 9+, helper only) [private] returnType helperMethod(parameters) { /* body */ } // 6. Nested types (classes/interfaces/enums) } ``` ### 2.1.1 Component-by-Component Explanation - **`[access_modifier]`**: Either `public` or package-private (no modifier). Top-level interfaces can only be `public` or package-private. - **`interface` keyword**: Reserved word; mandatory. Replaces `class` keyword. - **`InterfaceName`**: Must follow Java identifier rules. By KTU convention, interface names are **PascalCase** and often describe a *capability* (e.g., `Drawable`, `Serializable`, `Comparable`). - **`[extends OtherInterfaces]`**: An interface can extend one or more interfaces (separated by commas). This is the only form of multiple inheritance allowed for both classes and interfaces. - **Constant fields**: Every field is implicitly `public static final`. The modifiers are **optional but redundant**. The field must be initialized at declaration. - **Abstract methods**: Every method without a body is implicitly `public abstract`. The modifiers are **optional but redundant**. - **`default` methods**: Introduced in Java 8 to allow backward-compatible evolution of interfaces. Marked with the `default` keyword. Has a body. - **`static` methods**: Utility methods that belong to the interface itself, not to any implementing class. Called via `InterfaceName.methodName()`. - **`private` methods**: Java 9 addition. Used as helper methods inside `default` or `static` methods to avoid code duplication. Not inherited. - **Nested types**: A class, interface, enum, or annotation can be declared inside an interface. They are implicitly `public static`. --- ## 2.2 The "Implicit Modifiers" — A KTU High-Yield Concept > [!IMPORTANT] > **Implicit Rules Inside an Interface (Frequently asked for 2–3 marks):** > > | Element | Implicit Modifier | What You Can Write | What It Actually Means | > | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | > | Field | `public static final` | `int MAX = 100;` | `public static final int MAX = 100;` | > | Method (no body) | `public abstract` | `void draw();` | `public abstract void draw();` | > | `default` method | `public` (default keyword) | `default void show(){}` | `public default void show(){}` | > | `static` method | `public static` | `static int add(){}` | `public static int add(){}` | > | Nested type | `public static` | `class Helper{}` | `public static class Helper{}` | --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > **No physics/math formulas apply here.** Below is the **Java specification "grammar" cheat sheet** — the rules that govern interface definitions, much like formulae govern a calculation. | # | Rule | Syntax Pattern | Example | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Defining an interface | `access interface Name { }` | `public interface Animal { }` | | 2 | Interface extending interfaces | `interface A extends B, C { }` | `interface Flyable extends Movable, Drawable { }` | | 3 | Class implementing interface(s) | `class X implements A, B { }` | `class Bird implements Flyable, Eatable { }` | | 4 | Class extending + implementing | `class X extends Y implements A { }` | `class Sparrow extends Bird implements Flyable { }` | | 5 | Default method | `default retType name(args){ }` | `default void log(String s){ }` | | 6 | Static method | `static retType name(args){ }` | `static int cube(int x){ return x*x*x; }` | | 7 | Constant field | `[public static final] T NAME = val;` | `double PI = 3.14;` | | 8 | Functional interface (SAM) | Exactly one abstract method | `@FunctionalInterface interface Calc { int op(int a,int b); }` | | 9 | Marker interface | No methods at all | `interface Serializable { }` | | 10 | Diamond problem resolution | Class overrides conflicting defaults | `public void show(){ A.super.show(); }` | --- ## 2.4 Interface vs Abstract Class — The Critical Distinction This is the **most asked 14-mark comparison** in KTU ESE. The mental model is below. > **Abstract Class = "is-a" relationship with partial implementation.** > **Interface = "can-do" / "has-capability" relationship, pure contract.** | Feature | Abstract Class | Interface (Java 8+) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Keyword | `abstract class` | `interface` | | Methods | Abstract + Concrete (any) | Abstract + default + static + private | | Fields | Any (instance/static/final/non-final) | Only `public static final` constants | | Constructor | Yes (used by subclasses) | **No** | | Access modifiers for members | Any | Implicitly `public` (pre-Java 9) | | Inheritance type | Single (`extends`) | Multiple (`implements` for class, `extends` for interface) | | Speed | Slightly faster (concrete methods) | Slightly slower (requires indirection) | | When to use | Shared code + state + "is-a" | Pure contract + "can-do" capability | --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **JDBC API**: `java.sql.Connection`, `java.sql.Statement`, `java.sql.ResultSet` — all interfaces. Vendors (Oracle, MySQL) provide implementations (the famous `DriverManager`). - **Collection Framework**: `List`, `Set`, `Map`, `Iterator` are interfaces. `ArrayList`, `HashSet`, `HashMap` are implementations. - **Event Handling (Swing/JavaFX)**: `ActionListener`, `MouseListener` are functional interfaces. - **Lambda Expressions**: Enabled by functional interfaces (Java 8). - **Dependency Injection (Spring)**: Beans are wired against interfaces, not concrete classes — the entire IoC container depends on this abstraction. - **Plugin Architectures**: Define a `Plugin` interface; third parties implement it; the host application loads them dynamically via `Class.forName()`. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** In the industry, a junior developer typically *implements* an interface; a senior architect *defines* the interface. The interface is the **stable public API**; the implementation can be swapped, mocked, or upgraded without breaking clients. This is the **Dependency Inversion Principle (the 'D' in SOLID)** made literal by the Java compiler. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > In a pure CS topic like *Defining an Interface*, "derivations" are replaced by **logical step-by-step language specification proofs** — i.e., the exact rules the Java compiler applies when it encounters interface definitions. This section walks through those rules rigorously. ## 3.1 Step-by-Step Logical Deduction: What Happens When You Write an Interface? ### Step 1 — Source code parsing You write: ```java public interface Vehicle { int MAX_SPEED = 120; void start(); } ``` ### Step 2 — Compiler applies implicit modifiers The Java compiler (per JLS §9.3 and §9.4) **injects** the implicit modifiers: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Written code} &\rightarrow \text{Compiler-interpreted code} \\ \texttt{int MAX\_SPEED = 120;} &\rightarrow \texttt{public static final int MAX\_SPEED = 120;} \\ \texttt{void start();} &\rightarrow \texttt{public abstract void start();} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Constant pool entry The field `MAX_SPEED` is marked as a **compile-time constant**. It is stored in the class's **constant pool** (a per-class symbolic table in the `.class` file), not as an instance variable. ### Step 4 — Method descriptor entry `start()` is registered as an **abstract method descriptor** in the class file. No bytecode is generated for its body because there is no body. ### Step 5 — Bytecode is created with flag `ACC_INTERFACE = 0x0200` The resulting `.class` file has the access flag set, marking it as an interface. JVM's `Class.isInterface()` returns `true` for any class file with this flag. ### Step 6 — Runtime check on instantiation If any code attempts: ```java Vehicle v = new Vehicle(); // COMPILE-TIME ERROR ``` The compiler rejects it because interfaces are **abstract by definition** (per JLS §9.1.1.1). --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: How the Compiler Treats Each Declaration > [!NOTE] > The following table is the **exhaustive logical rule matrix** for interface member declarations. Each row is an independent, verifiable case. | Member Type | You Write | Compiler Treats It As | Reference (JLS) | Can It Be Inherited? | Can It Be Overridden? | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Field | `int X = 5;` | `public static final int X = 5;` | §9.3 | Yes (as constant) | No (final) | | Abstract method | `void m();` | `public abstract void m();` | §9.4 | Yes | Yes (mandatory) | | `default` method | `default void m(){}` | `public default void m(){}` | §9.4.3 | Yes | Yes (optional) | | `static` method | `static void m(){}` | `public static void m(){}` | §9.4.2 | No (hidden) | No | | `private` method | `private void m(){}` | `private void m(){}` | §9.4.4 | No | N/A | | Nested class | `class C{}` | `public static class C{}` | §9.5 | N/A | Yes | | Nested interface | `interface I{}` | `public interface I{}` | §9.5 | N/A | Yes | | Enum | `enum E{A,B}` | `public static enum E{A,B}` | §9.5 | N/A | Yes | --- ## 3.3 Step-by-Step: Resolving the Diamond Problem (Java 8 Default Method Conflict) When a class implements two interfaces that both provide a `default` method with the same signature, the class **must override** the conflicting method. Let us derive the resolution. ### Setup ```java interface A { default void show() { System.out.println("A"); } } interface B { default void show() { System.out.println("B"); } } class C implements A, B { // Mandatory override public void show() { A.super.show(); // explicitly call A's version B.super.show(); // then B's version } } ``` ### Logical derivation $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1: Class C has 2 inherited defaults} &\Rightarrow \text{Compiler detects conflict} \\ \text{Step 2: JLS §9.4.1 rule} &\Rightarrow \text{Subclass MUST override to resolve ambiguity} \\ \text{Step 3: Inside override, use} &\ A.\texttt{super}.\texttt{show()} \text{ to disambiguate} \\ \text{Step 4: If class extends + implements, superclass wins over interface} & \\ \quad \text{by JLS §15.12.1 (Class precedence rule).} & \end{aligned} $$ ### Resolution Priority (Exhaustive) $$ \text{Resolution Order} = \text{Class (superclass)} \; > \; \text{Sub-interface (closer in hierarchy)} \; > \; \text{Far interface} \; > \; \text{Mandatory override} $$ --- ## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Functional Interface Annotation Proof A **functional interface** is exactly an interface with **one abstract method** (Single Abstract Method — SAM). ```java @FunctionalInterface interface Calculator { int operate(int a, int b); } ``` ### Compiler validation steps - $\text{Step 1: Check annotation presence: } @\texttt{FunctionalInterface}$ - $\text{Step 2: Count abstract methods in interface: } \text{Count} \equiv 1$ - $\text{Step 3: If count} \neq 1 \Rightarrow \text{Compilation ERROR: "Not a functional interface"}$ - $\text{Step 4: Default and static methods do not count; only abstract methods count.}$ - $\text{Step 5: The interface is now eligible for use with a lambda expression.}$ $$ \text{Usage: } \texttt{Calculator add = (a, b) -> a + b;} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** The `@FunctionalInterface` annotation is **optional but recommended** because it forces the compiler to enforce the SAM rule. The interface is functional *even without* the annotation, as long as it satisfies the SAM condition. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics > Mermaid diagrams below illustrate the structural relationships and lifecycle flow of an interface in Java. ## 4.1 Diagram 1: Interface Definition & Implementation Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Source File: Vehicle.java]:::iface --> B[Keyword: interface]:::iface B --> C[Implicit Modifiers Applied]:::iface C --> D[Field becomes public static final]:::meta C --> E[Method becomes public abstract]:::meta D --> F[Constant stored in Constant Pool]:::meta E --> G[Method descriptor in class file]:::meta F --> H[.class file generated with ACC_INTERFACE flag]:::meta G --> H H --> I{Attempt to instantiate?}:::check I -->|new Vehicle| J[COMPILE-TIME ERROR]:::err I -->|Class Car implements Vehicle| K[Car MUST override start]:::impl K --> L[Car object created]:::impl L --> M[Polymorphic call: Vehicle v = new Car]:::impl classDef iface fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000 classDef meta fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,color:#000 classDef check fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000 classDef err fill:#f5c2c7,stroke:#842029,color:#000 classDef impl fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.2 Diagram 2: Multiple Inheritance via Interfaces (Bird / Aeroplane / Sparrow) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Contracts["Capability Contracts (Interfaces)"] F1[Flyable]:::iface E1[Eatable]:::iface S1[Soundable]:::iface end subgraph Concrete["Concrete Classes"] B1[Bird]:::cls A1[Aeroplane]:::cls SP1[Sparrow]:::cls end B1 -.implements.-> F1 B1 -.implements.-> S1 A1 -.implements.-> F1 SP1 -.extends.-> B1 SP1 -.implements.-> E1 classDef iface fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000 classDef cls fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000 ``` > **Reading the diagram:** `Sparrow` inherits from `Bird` (so it gets `fly()` and `chirp()` implementations) and additionally promises to be `Eatable` (must implement `eat()`). `Aeroplane` and `Bird` share only the `Flyable` contract — they are completely unrelated classes that can still be treated polymorphically as `Flyable`. --- ## 4.3 Diagram 3: Interface Evolution Across Java Versions ```mermaid flowchart LR J5[Java 1.0-1.4
Constants + Abstract Methods Only]:::old --> J6[Java 5
Annotations + Generics]:::old J6 --> J7[Java 8
default + static methods
Functional Interfaces + Lambdas]:::new J7 --> J8[Java 9
private + private static methods]:::new classDef old fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#6c757d,color:#000 classDef new fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why this diagram matters for KTU 2024:** The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly mentions Java 8 features. Students are expected to know that *before* Java 8, interfaces could **only** hold abstract methods and constants. The `default` and `static` keywords were introduced to allow interfaces to evolve without breaking millions of existing implementations. --- ## 4.4 Diagram 4: Block-Level Functional Architecture (Interface as Middleware) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph ClientLayer["Client Application Code"] APP[Business Logic]:::app end subgraph AbstractionLayer["Interface Layer (The Contract)"] IF[PaymentGateway Interface
pay(), refund(), getStatus]:::iface end subgraph ImplLayer["Implementation Layer (Swappable)"] IM1[CreditCardGateway]:::impl IM2[UPIGateway]:::impl IM3[PayPalGateway]:::impl end APP -->|depends on| IF IF -. implemented by .-> IM1 IF -. implemented by .-> IM2 IF -. implemented by .-> IM3 classDef app fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,color:#000 classDef iface fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000 classDef impl fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000 ``` > **Industrial Reading:** The `App` knows **only** the `PaymentGateway` interface. It does not know — nor care — whether the backend is `CreditCard`, `UPI`, or `PayPal`. This is the **Open/Closed Principle** made enforceable by the Java compiler. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]** > **Define an interface in Java. List any two differences between an interface and an abstract class.** **Model Answer (≈ 8–10 lines):** An interface in Java is a reference type declared with the `interface` keyword. It defines a contract consisting of abstract methods (and, since Java 8, `default` and `static` methods) that implementing classes must provide. All data members in an interface are implicitly `public static final`, and all methods (until Java 8) are implicitly `public abstract`. Interfaces cannot be instantiated and support multiple inheritance. **Two Differences:** 1. **Methods:** An abstract class can have both abstract and concrete (implemented) methods; an interface (pre-Java 8) can have only abstract methods. Since Java 8, interfaces can have `default` and `static` methods, but they still cannot hold instance state. 2. **Inheritance:** A class can extend only one abstract class (single inheritance), but a class can implement multiple interfaces (multiple inheritance of type). > **Marking Hint:** Definition = 1 mark, each difference = 1 mark. Total = 3 marks. --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]** > **What is a functional interface? Give one example.** **Model Answer (≈ 4–5 lines):** A **functional interface** is an interface that contains **exactly one abstract method** (Single Abstract Method — SAM). It may contain any number of `default` or `static` methods. Functional interfaces are the target types for **lambda expressions** and **method references**, introduced in Java 8. **Example:** ```java @FunctionalInterface interface RunnableLike { void run(); } ``` This interface can be used as `RunnableLike r = () -> System.out.println("Running");`. The `@FunctionalInterface` annotation is optional but causes the compiler to enforce the SAM rule. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 (Model)]** > **(a)** Explain the syntax of defining an interface in Java. Write a complete Java program that defines an interface `Shape` with a constant `PI = 3.14` and abstract methods `area()` and `perimeter()`. Implement the interface in two classes, `Circle` and `Rectangle`, and demonstrate polymorphism by storing both in an array of type `Shape`. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** What are `default` and `static` methods in interfaces (Java 8 feature)? Explain with a suitable example. Why were they introduced? **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks) **Step 1 — Interface Definition (2 marks):** ```java // File: Shape.java public interface Shape { double PI = 3.14; // implicitly public static final double area(); double perimeter(); } ``` **Step 2 — Implementing class `Circle` (1.5 marks):** ```java public class Circle implements Shape { private double radius; public Circle(double radius) { this.radius = radius; } @Override public double area() { return PI * radius * radius; } @Override public double perimeter() { return 2 * PI * radius; } } ``` **Step 3 — Implementing class `Rectangle` (1.5 marks):** ```java public class Rectangle implements Shape { private double length, width; public Rectangle(double length, double width) { this.length = length; this.width = width; } @Override public double area() { return length * width; } @Override public double perimeter() { return 2 * (length + width); } } ``` **Step 4 — Polymorphic demonstration (2 marks):** ```java public class Main { public static void main(String[] args) { Shape[] shapes = new Shape[2]; shapes[0] = new Circle(5.0); shapes[1] = new Rectangle(4.0, 6.0); for (Shape s : shapes) { System.out.println("Area = " + s.area() + ", Perimeter = " + s.perimeter()); } } } ``` **Expected Output:** ``` Area = 78.5, Perimeter = 31.400000000000002 Area = 24.0, Perimeter = 20.0 ``` --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks) **Definition (2 marks):** - A **default method** in an interface is declared with the `default` keyword and provides a **concrete implementation**. It allows new methods to be added to an interface without breaking the classes that already implement it (binary backward compatibility). - A **static method** in an interface is declared with the `static` keyword. It belongs to the interface itself and is called using the interface name (e.g., `MyInterface.helper()`). It is **not inherited** by implementing classes. **Example (3 marks):** ```java public interface Vehicle { void start(); // default method (Java 8) default void honk() { System.out.println("Beep beep!"); log("Honked at " + System.currentTimeMillis()); } // static method (Java 8) static int countWheels() { return 4; } // private method (Java 9) - used internally private void log(String msg) { System.out.println("LOG: " + msg); } } public class Car implements Vehicle { @Override public void start() { System.out.println("Car started."); } // honk() is inherited automatically // countWheels() is NOT inherited — call as Vehicle.countWheels() } ``` **Why Introduced (2 marks):** 1. **Backward Compatibility:** Java 8 had to add new methods to `Collection` interfaces (e.g., `stream()`, `forEach()`) without forcing millions of existing implementations to break. `default` methods made this possible. 2. **Code Reuse in Interfaces:** Static methods provide a place for utility/helper functions that are logically related to the interface, without requiring a separate utility class. 3. **Lambda Compatibility:** Functional interfaces (with SAM) work seamlessly with `default` methods to support the Streams API. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** > **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 (Model)]** > **(a)** Explain the concept of multiple inheritance in Java using interfaces. Write a Java program where a class `SmartPhone` implements three interfaces — `Camera`, `MusicPlayer`, and `Phone` — and demonstrate calling all methods through interface references. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Compare and contrast **abstract class** and **interface** in Java. When would you prefer one over the other? Provide a real-world example for each. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks) **Conceptual Explanation (2 marks):** Java does **not** support multiple inheritance of *classes* (to avoid the **Diamond Problem** and ambiguity). However, it **does** support multiple inheritance of *type* through interfaces, because interfaces only declare method signatures (no state, no constructors, no instance fields). A class can implement any number of interfaces separated by commas. **Code (5 marks):** ```java // Three interfaces interface Camera { void takePhoto(); default void flash() { System.out.println("Flash fired."); } } interface MusicPlayer { void playMusic(); } interface Phone { void makeCall(String number); } // Class implementing all three public class SmartPhone implements Camera, MusicPlayer, Phone { private String brand; public SmartPhone(String brand) { this.brand = brand; } @Override public void takePhoto() { System.out.println(brand + " is taking a photo."); } @Override public void playMusic() { System.out.println(brand + " is playing music."); } @Override public void makeCall(String number) { System.out.println(brand + " is calling " + number); } public static void main(String[] args) { SmartPhone sp = new SmartPhone("Pixel"); Camera cam = sp; // interface reference MusicPlayer mp = sp; Phone ph = sp; cam.takePhoto(); cam.flash(); mp.playMusic(); ph.makeCall("+91-9876543210"); } } ``` **Output:** ``` Pixel is taking a photo. Flash fired. Pixel is playing music. Pixel is calling +91-9876543210 ``` **Polymorphism Demonstrated:** Notice that the same `sp` object is referred to by three different interface types, and the appropriate method is dispatched at runtime. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks) **Comparison Table (4 marks):** | Parameter | Abstract Class | Interface | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Keyword | `abstract class` | `interface` | | State (instance fields) | Yes (any access) | No (only constants) | | Methods | Abstract + Concrete | Abstract + default + static + private | | Constructor | Yes | No | | Inheritance | Single (extends) | Multiple (implements / extends) | | Access Modifiers | Any for members | `public` (implicit) for members | | Speed | Slightly faster | Slightly slower (indirection) | | `main()` allowed | Yes | Yes (since Java 8) | | `final` allowed | Class can be `final` | Interface cannot be `final` | | Relationship | "is-a" (inheritance) | "can-do" (capability) | **When to Prefer (2 marks):** - **Use Abstract Class** when you have a clear "is-a" hierarchy with shared state and partial implementation. *Example:* `Animal` (abstract) → `Dog`, `Cat` (concrete). All animals have a `name`, an `age`, and a common `eat()` behavior — place the shared code in `Animal`. - **Use Interface** when unrelated classes must share a capability contract, or when you need multiple inheritance of type. *Example:* `Comparable` — a class can be `Comparable` regardless of where it sits in the class hierarchy. A `String`, an `Employee`, and a `Date` are all `Comparable` even though they are unrelated. **Real-world Engineering Rule of Thumb (1 mark):** > *“Prefer interfaces for the public API of a module. Use abstract classes only when there is genuine shared code and state among tightly-coupled subclasses.”* --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks (Compiled from KTU Evaluator Reports):** > > 1. **Forgetting the `public` modifier in the implementing class** — When you override an interface method, the access modifier **must be `public`**, not package-private. Writing `void area() {}` instead of `public void area() {}` loses **1 full mark** per occurrence. > 2. **Confusing `extends` and `implements`** — A class uses `extends` for a class and `implements` for an interface. A class can do both: `class A extends B implements C, D`. Writing `class A implements B` where `B` is a class is a **compile-time error** worth losing 2 marks. > 3. **Writing a constructor in the interface** — Interfaces have no constructors. Writing `public Shape() { }` inside an interface is a compilation error. > 4. **Missing the `@Override` annotation** — Not an error, but examiners *love* to deduct marks if the override is not declared explicitly, especially when the question says "implement" or "override". > 5. **Treating fields as instance variables** — Writing `int count;` (without `= value`) inside an interface is a **compilation error** because all interface fields must be `final` and must be initialized at declaration. > 6. **Thinking interfaces are slower** — The performance difference is negligible in modern JVMs. Writing "interfaces are slow" in a 14-mark answer is a conceptual red flag. > 7. **Forgetting Java 8 `default` methods exist** — Many KTU 2024 questions test this specifically. Always mention "since Java 8" when describing new features. --- ## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **Last-Minute Revision Bullets — Memorize These for the KTU Exam:** > > ✅ **Definition:** An interface is a **pure abstract type** (until Java 8) declared with the `interface` keyword, used to define a contract of method signatures. > > ✅ **Implicit Modifiers:** > - All fields → `public static final` (constants) > - All non-default, non-static methods → `public abstract` > - Nested types → `public static` > > ✅ **Key Restrictions:** > - ❌ Cannot be instantiated with `new` > - ❌ Cannot have constructors > - ❌ Cannot have instance (non-static) fields > - ❌ Cannot have `final` (interfaces are inherently abstract) > > ✅ **Java 8 Additions:** > - `default` methods — have a body, inherited by implementing classes > - `static` methods — have a body, not inherited, called via interface name > - Functional interfaces (SAM) — enable lambda expressions > > ✅ **Java 9 Additions:** > - `private` and `private static` methods — used as code-sharing helpers inside the interface > > ✅ **Inheritance Rules:** > - `interface A extends B, C` — multiple interface inheritance > - `class X implements A, B` — multiple interface implementation > - `class X extends Y implements A, B` — single class + multiple interface inheritance > > ✅ **Diamond Problem:** Resolved by **mandatory override** in the implementing class, using `InterfaceName.super.methodName()` to call a specific version. > > ✅ **Three Special Categories:** > - **Marker / Tagged Interface:** No methods (e.g., `Serializable`, `Cloneable`, `Remote`) — used to mark a class as having a property > - **Functional Interface:** Exactly one abstract method (SAM) — used with lambdas > - **Nested Interface:** Interface declared inside a class or another interface > > ✅ **Industrial Significance:** Interfaces are the **foundation of plug-in architectures, dependency injection, and the entire Java Collection Framework**. They are how Java achieves **full abstraction** and **multiple inheritance of type** safely. > > ✅ **One-liner Mnemonic:** *"Interface = Contract. Class = Implementer. `implements` = Promise. `extends` = Inheritance."*

topics.title: Implementing Interfaces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT633 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Implementing Interfaces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interface (Java/KTU Definition):** An *interface* in Java is a **reference type**, declared using the `interface` keyword, that acts as a **pure abstract contract**. It can contain only **public static final constants**, **abstract method signatures** (implicitly `public` and `abstract`), **default methods** (Java 8+), **static methods** (Java 8+), **private methods** (Java 9+), and **nested types**. It **cannot** be instantiated directly, **cannot** contain instance variables, **cannot** declare constructors, and serves as a blueprint of behaviour that classes must implement.

A class **implements** an interface using the `implements` keyword. The implementing class is legally bound to provide concrete (overridden) implementations for **every abstract method** declared in the interface, unless the class itself is declared `abstract`.

```java
interface Drawable {
    void draw();           // abstract by default
    double PI = 3.14159;   // public static final by default
}

class Circle implements Drawable {
    public void draw() {
        System.out.println("Drawing Circle");
    }
}
```

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The USB Contract

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The USB / Power Socket Standard:**
> Think of an **interface** as a **standardized socket on a wall**. The socket itself does nothing — it has no power. It is just a *contract*: *"Whatever plug fits this shape, I promise to deliver 230V AC."* Any device — a laptop charger, a phone charger, a lamp — that *implements* the correct plug shape (the **class implements the interface**) can be plugged in and used. The wall doesn't care whether the device is a Samsung, an HP, or a Dell. It just needs the **shape** (signature) to be respected.
>
> - The **socket** → Interface
> - The **plug shape** → Abstract methods
> - The **device manufacturer** → Class implementing the interface
> - The **device** providing its own internal logic → Method body overrides
> - The **wall** happily running any device → Polymorphic code working with the interface type

## 1.3 Why Interfaces Exist — The KTU Design Philosophy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Interfaces in KTU 2024 scheme are taught as the **primary mechanism for multiple inheritance of type** in Java. Since Java disallows extending multiple classes (the *Diamond Problem*), a class can `implements InterfaceA, InterfaceB, InterfaceC` — inheriting *behavioural contracts* from many sources safely.

| Feature | Class (Abstract or Concrete) | Interface |
|---|---|---|
| Keyword | `class` | `interface` |
| Variables | Instance + Static | Only `public static final` |
| Methods | Concrete + Abstract | Abstract / Default / Static / Private |
| Constructors | Allowed | **Not allowed** |
| Access Specifiers | public, protected, private, default | Methods are implicitly `public` |
| Inheritance | `extends` (single) | `implements` (multiple) |
| Instantiated? | Yes (if concrete) | **No** |
| Relationship | "is-a" | "can-do" / "acts-as" |

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention:** Interface names in KTU/Java end with the suffix **"-able"** (e.g., `Runnable`, `Serializable`, `Comparable`, `Drawable`) — denoting **capability**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

The act of **implementing an interface** follows a strict, deterministic sequence. KTU expects students to know this exact flow:

1. **Declare the Interface** — Use the `interface` keyword. List method signatures (no bodies for abstract).
2. **Declare the Implementing Class** — Use the `class` keyword followed by `implements InterfaceName`.
3. **Provide Method Bodies** — The class **must** override **every** abstract method with **public** access (access *cannot* be reduced).
4. **Use the Interface as a Type** — Declare reference variables of the interface type to achieve **polymorphism**.
5. **Invoke via Reference** — The JVM performs dynamic dispatch at runtime, executing the overriding class's method.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Rule

- **Why `public` access is mandatory on overrides?** Because interface methods are implicitly `public`, and Java enforces that you **cannot reduce visibility** in an override. Declaring the implementation as `void draw()` (package-private) would violate the contract.
- **Why no constructors in interfaces?** Because interfaces are not instantiated. A constructor would imply object creation, which interfaces forbid.
- **Why `public static final` for variables?** Because interfaces define a *contract*, not *state*. Variables become global constants — universally accessible without instantiation.
- **Why a class can implement multiple interfaces?** Because interfaces carry no instance state and no constructors, the **Diamond Problem of multiple inheritance is avoided** — there is no ambiguity about whose state to inherit.

## 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Syntax / Rule | Notes / Exception |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Interface Declaration | `interface Name { ... }` | Saved as `Name.java` |
| 2 | Class implements Interface | `class A implements I { }` | Single implementation |
| 3 | Class implements Multiple | `class A implements I1, I2 { }` | Separated by commas |
| 4 | Interface extends Interface | `interface I2 extends I1 { }` | Multiple inheritance of type |
| 5 | Variable in Interface | `int MAX = 100;` | Implicitly `public static final` |
| 6 | Abstract Method | `void run();` | Implicitly `public abstract` |
| 7 | Default Method (Java 8) | `default void show() { ... }` | Has a body, inherited as-is |
| 8 | Static Method (Java 8) | `static int calc() { ... }` | Called via `InterfaceName.calc()` |
| 9 | Functional Interface | Exactly **one** abstract method | `@FunctionalInterface` annotation |
| 10 | Marker Interface | **Zero** methods | E.g., `Serializable`, `Cloneable` |
| 11 | Diamond Problem Avoidance | Interfaces only carry contracts | Safe multiple inheritance |
| 12 | Compile Error if Unimplemented | `Circle is not abstract and does not override abstract method draw() in Drawable` | Unless class is `abstract` |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where Interfaces are used in the Industry (KTU viva favourite):**
> - **JDBC API** — `Connection`, `Statement`, `ResultSet` are interfaces; drivers (MySQL, Oracle) provide implementations.
> - **Collection Framework** — `List`, `Set`, `Map` are interfaces; `ArrayList`, `HashSet`, `HashMap` are concrete implementations.
> - **Dependency Injection (Spring)** — Beans are wired via interface contracts for testability and loose coupling.
> - **Strategy Design Pattern** — Algorithms are encapsulated behind a common interface (e.g., `Comparator`, `PaymentStrategy`).
> - **Lambda Expressions (Java 8)** — Functional interfaces (`Runnable`, `Callable`, `Comparator`) are the type targets of arrow functions.

## 2.5 Interface vs Abstract Class — The Classic KTU Distinction

| Property | Abstract Class | Interface |
|---|---|---|
| Keyword | `abstract class` | `interface` |
| State (variables) | Instance + Static + Final | Constants only |
| Constructors | Yes | No |
| Method types | Abstract + Concrete | Abstract + Default + Static + Private |
| Multiple inheritance | No (single `extends`) | Yes (multiple `implements`) |
| Access modifiers | Any | Methods are `public` |
| When to use | Shared **code + state** among related classes | Shared **behavioural contract** across unrelated classes |

## 2.6 Type Relationship Notation (KTU Theory)

For a class `Circle` implementing interface `Drawable`:

$$
\text{Circle} \;\xrightarrow{\;\text{implements}\;}\; \text{Drawable}
$$

For polymorphic binding:

$$
\text{InterfaceRef} \;\texttt{=} \;\texttt{new ConcreteClass();}
$$

$$
\texttt{InterfaceRef.method();} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{ConcreteClass.method()} \;\;(\text{dynamic dispatch})
$$
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** This section provides a **complete, exhaustive trace** of how interface implementation is compiled and executed in the JVM. Every transition is written explicitly. KTU examiners value this kind of step-by-step reasoning for full marks in 14-mark questions.

## 3.1 Exhaustive Code Walkthrough — The Canonical Example

### Step 1: Interface Declaration (Contract Phase)

```java
// File: Payment.java
interface Payment {
    void pay(double amount);          // abstract — no body
    default void receipt() {          // default — has body (Java 8+)
        System.out.println("Receipt generated.");
    }
    static double applyTax(double amt) {   // static — called on interface
        return amt * 0.18;
    }
}
```

**Compiler's view:**
- `pay(double)` is stored in the **method table** of `Payment` as an abstract entry.
- `receipt()` is stored as a concrete default method (inheritable as-is).
- `applyTax()` is a static helper bound to the interface, not to any instance.
- No constructor is generated.
- No instance fields are allocated.

### Step 2: Implementing Class (Binding Phase)

```java
// File: CardPayment.java
class CardPayment implements Payment {
    private String cardNumber;
    public CardPayment(String c) { this.cardNumber = c; }
    
    @Override
    public void pay(double amount) {
        double total = amount + Payment.applyTax(amount);
        System.out.println("Paid " + total + " using card " + cardNumber);
    }
}
```

**Compiler's view:**
- `CardPayment` registers itself as a `Payment` subtype via the `implements` clause.
- The compiler **verifies** that `pay(double)` is overridden with `public` access — if visibility were reduced to package-private, the build would fail.
- The default `receipt()` is **inherited as-is** (no override needed).
- `Payment.applyTax()` is reachable through the interface name.

### Step 3: Polymorphic Client (Dispatch Phase)

```java
// File: Store.java
public class Store {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Payment p = new CardPayment("XXXX-1234");
        p.pay(1000.0);          // dynamic dispatch
        p.receipt();            // default method inherited
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step JVM execution trace:**

| Step | Action | JVM Activity |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `Payment p = new CardPayment(...)` | Allocates `CardPayment` object on heap; stores reference in stack variable `p` of static type `Payment` |
| 2 | `p.pay(1000.0)` | **Dynamic dispatch** — JVM inspects actual object's class (`CardPayment`), locates `pay` in vtable, invokes it |
| 3 | Inside `pay()` → `Payment.applyTax(1000.0)` | Static call bound at compile time to `Payment` interface; returns $180.0$ |
| 4 | `total = 1000.0 + 180.0 = 1180.0` | Arithmetic performed |
| 5 | `System.out.println(...)` | Prints "Paid 1180.0 using card XXXX-1234" |
| 6 | `p.receipt()` | Inherited default method called directly; prints "Receipt generated." |

**Output:**
```
Paid 1180.0 using card XXXX-1234
Receipt generated.
```

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Multiple Interface Implementation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The KTU Favourite — Multiple Interface Implementation with Conflict Resolution.**

```java
interface Printer {
    default void connect() { System.out.println("Printer connected via USB"); }
}
interface Scanner {
    default void connect() { System.out.println("Scanner connected via WiFi"); }
}
class AllInOne implements Printer, Scanner {
    @Override
    public void connect() {
        Printer.super.connect();   // explicitly disambiguate
        Scanner.super.connect();
        System.out.println("AllInOne ready.");
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step resolution:**

1. The compiler detects the **Diamond Conflict** — both `Printer` and `Scanner` provide a `connect()` default.
2. Java **forces** the implementing class to override the conflicting method.
3. Inside the override, the programmer uses `InterfaceName.super.method()` to invoke each parent explicitly.
4. Without the override → **compile-time error**:
   *"class AllInOne inherits unrelated defaults for connect() from types Printer and Scanner"*

**Final output:**
```
Printer connected via USB
Scanner connected via WiFi
AllInOne ready.
```

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Interface Inheritance Chain

```java
interface A { void a(); }
interface B extends A { void b(); }
interface C extends B { void c(); }
class XYZ implements C {
    public void a() { System.out.println("a"); }
    public void b() { System.out.println("b"); }
    public void c() { System.out.println("c"); }
}
```

**Inheritance graph (transitivity proof):**

$$
C \;\supseteq\; B \;\supseteq\; A
$$

$$
\therefore \;\; XYZ \;\text{must implement} \; \{a(), b(), c()\}
$$

If `XYZ` omits any one, the compiler rejects the build. The class inherits the **union of abstract method signatures** from the entire interface chain.

## 3.4 Functional Interface Trace (Java 8 Lambdas)

```java
@FunctionalInterface
interface Greet {
    String message(String name);
}

public class Demo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Greet g = (name) -> "Hello, " + name + "!";
        System.out.println(g.message("KTU"));
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step:**
1. `@FunctionalInterface` is a **compiler-enforced annotation** — exactly one abstract method allowed.
2. The lambda `(name) -> "Hello, " + name + "!"` is **syntactic sugar** for an anonymous inner class implementing `Greet`.
3. The compiler generates a synthetic class (e.g., `Demo$$Lambda$1/0x000...`) overriding `message()`.
4. `g.message("KTU")` → returns `"Hello, KTU!"`.

**Output:** `Hello, KTU!`
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
