# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Define an interface in Java. Why is multiple inheritance allowed through interfaces but not through classes?**

**Model Answer:**

An **interface** in Java is a reference type declared using the `interface` keyword that contains only **abstract method signatures**, **public static final constants**, and (since Java 8) **default** and **static methods**. It represents a pure **behavioural contract** without any instance state.

Multiple inheritance is allowed through interfaces because interfaces carry **no instance variables and no constructors**. Hence the **Diamond Problem** — ambiguity about which parent's state to inherit — **cannot arise**. A class can safely `implements I1, I2, I3` and inherit the union of method contracts.

In contrast, classes have **state** (instance variables) and **constructors**; multiple class inheritance would create ambiguity in field initialization and memory layout, so Java **disallows it**.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for explaining interface safety, 1 for class ambiguity.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: What is a functional interface? Give one example with a lambda expression.**

**Model Answer:**

A **functional interface** is an interface that contains **exactly one abstract method**. It may contain any number of default or static methods. The annotation `@FunctionalInterface` is used to enforce this contract at compile time.

Functional interfaces serve as the **target type for lambda expressions** and method references introduced in Java 8.

**Example:**
```java
@FunctionalInterface
interface Square {
    int calculate(int x);
}

public class Demo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Square s = (x) -> x * x;
        System.out.println(s.calculate(5));   // Output: 25
    }
}
```

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for annotation/lambda link, 1 for working example.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the concept of interfaces in Java with a suitable example. Discuss how a class implements an interface. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Write a Java program to demonstrate multiple inheritance using interfaces. The program should have two interfaces `Sports` and `Exam` with appropriate methods, and a class `Student` implementing both. Include method overriding and demonstrate dynamic polymorphism. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Solution to (a):**

**Definition:** An interface is a **pure abstract type** in Java declared using the `interface` keyword. It declares a set of method signatures (and constants) that implementing classes must provide.

**Syntax of declaration:**
```java
access interface InterfaceName {
    // public static final constants
    // public abstract methods
    // default methods (Java 8+)
    // static methods (Java 8+)
}
```

**Example:**
```java
interface Animal {
    void sound();         // abstract
    default void sleep() {
        System.out.println("Animal sleeps.");
    }
}

class Dog implements Animal {
    public void sound() {
        System.out.println("Dog barks.");
    }
}

public class Test {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Animal a = new Dog();   // polymorphic reference
        a.sound();
        a.sleep();
    }
}
```

**How a class implements an interface:**

1. The class uses the `implements` keyword: `class Dog implements Animal`.
2. It must provide a **public** implementation of **every abstract method**, else the compiler raises an error.
3. The class can **inherit default methods as-is** without overriding.
4. An interface reference can hold the implementing class's object, enabling **runtime polymorphism**.

**Output:**
```
Dog barks.
Animal sleeps.
```

*(7 marks distributed: 1 definition, 2 syntax/example, 2 implementation steps, 2 polymorphism explanation.)*

---

#### **Solution to (b):**

```java
// File: MultipleInheritanceDemo.java

interface Sports {
    int mark1 = 85;                      // public static final
    void sportsScore();                  // abstract
}

interface Exam {
    int mark2 = 92;
    void examScore();
}

class Student implements Sports, Exam {
    private String name;
    public Student(String n) { this.name = n; }
    
    @Override
    public void sportsScore() {
        System.out.println(name + " sports mark: " + mark1);
    }
    
    @Override
    public void examScore() {
        System.out.println(name + " exam mark: " + mark2);
    }
    
    public void total() {
        System.out.println("Total: " + (mark1 + mark2));
    }
}

public class MultipleInheritanceDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Sports s = new Student("Arjun");
        Exam   e = new Student("Arjun");
        Student stu = new Student("Arjun");
        
        s.sportsScore();      // dynamic dispatch via Sports
        e.examScore();        // dynamic dispatch via Exam
        stu.total();          // direct call
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Arjun sports mark: 85
Arjun exam mark: 92
Total: 177
```

**Dynamic polymorphism demonstrated:** The same `Student` object is referenced through two different interface types (`Sports` and `Exam`). At runtime, the JVM dispatches `sportsScore()` and `examScore()` correctly based on the actual object's class. This proves that **multiple inheritance of type** is achieved safely via interfaces.

*(7 marks: 2 interface design, 2 class implementation, 1 override correctness, 2 polymorphism demonstration.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Differentiate between abstract class and interface in Java with a detailed comparison table. When would you prefer one over the other? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain default methods and multiple interface inheritance conflicts in Java 8. Write a program that resolves a diamond problem using the `InterfaceName.super.method()` syntax. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Solution to (a):**

**Abstract Class vs Interface — Detailed Comparison:**

| Feature | Abstract Class | Interface |
|---|---|---|
| Keyword | `abstract class` | `interface` |
| Variables | Instance, static, final (any) | Only `public static final` constants |
| Methods | Abstract + concrete | Abstract, default, static, private |
| Constructors | Allowed (used by subclasses) | **Not allowed** |
| Access Modifiers | public, protected, private, default | Methods are implicitly `public` |
| Inheritance | Single (`extends`) | Multiple (`implements`) |
| Instantiation | No (if abstract) | No |
| State | Can hold object state | No state |
| When to use | Shared code + state for **closely related** classes | Shared **contract** for **unrelated** classes |

**When to prefer which:**

- Use an **abstract class** when subclasses share **common code (concrete methods)** and **common state (instance variables)**, and there is a clear **"is-a"** relationship (e.g., `abstract class Shape` with `int sides` field shared by `Circle`, `Square`).
- Use an **interface** when unrelated classes must adhere to a **common behavioural contract**, especially when multiple inheritance of type is needed (e.g., `Comparable`, `Serializable`, `Runnable`).

*(7 marks: 3 for table, 2 for justification of each, 2 for "when to use".)*

---

#### **Solution to (b):**

**Default Methods (Java 8):** A `default` method in an interface has a body and provides a **default implementation** that implementing classes inherit automatically. It enables **backward-compatible API evolution** — adding new methods to existing interfaces without breaking implementations.

**Diamond Problem in Interfaces:** When a class implements two interfaces that both provide a **default method with the same signature**, a conflict arises. The implementing class **must override** the conflicting method and use `InterfaceName.super.method()` to disambiguate which parent's implementation to invoke.

**Program to Resolve the Diamond Problem:**

```java
interface Printer {
    default void connect() {
        System.out.println("Printer connected via USB");
    }
    void print(String doc);
}

interface Scanner {
    default void connect() {
        System.out.println("Scanner connected via WiFi");
    }
    void scan(String doc);
}

class AllInOne implements Printer, Scanner {
    @Override
    public void connect() {
        System.out.println("--- Resolving Diamond Conflict ---");
        Printer.super.connect();   // explicit call to Printer's default
        Scanner.super.connect();   // explicit call to Scanner's default
        System.out.println("AllInOne device is fully connected.");
    }
    
    @Override
    public void print(String doc) {
        System.out.println("Printing: " + doc);
    }
    
    @Override
    public void scan(String doc) {
        System.out.println("Scanning: " + doc);
    }
}

public class DiamondResolve {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        AllInOne device = new AllInOne();
        device.connect();
        device.print("Report.pdf");
        device.scan("Photo.jpg");
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
--- Resolving Diamond Conflict ---
Printer connected via USB
Scanner connected via WiFi
AllInOne device is fully connected.
Printing: Report.pdf
Scanning: Photo.jpg
```

**Key points covered:**
1. Without the `connect()` override, the compiler raises: *"incompatible types: ... unrelated defaults for connect()"*.
2. `Printer.super.connect()` and `Scanner.super.connect()` explicitly call each parent interface's default implementation.
3. The class is therefore **not** forced to choose one; it can compose behaviour from both.

*(7 marks: 2 default methods theory, 2 diamond problem theory, 3 working program.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting `public` on overrides** — Interface methods are *implicitly* `public`; reducing visibility to package-private is a compile error worth **−2 marks** in KTU valuation.
> 2. **Not overriding default methods in diamond conflict** — Leads to *"unrelated defaults"* compile error; examiner deducts **−1 to −2 marks** if you describe the conflict but don't override.
> 3. **Confusing `extends` with `implements`** — Interfaces are *extended* by other interfaces (`interface B extends A`), but *implemented* by classes (`class C implements A`). Mixing these costs marks.
> 4. **Omitting `@Override` annotation** — Not a compile error, but examiners expect it for clarity. Missing it may cost **−0.5 mark** for "code quality".
> 5. **Trying to instantiate an interface** — `new Payment()` is illegal. KTU trick question; students who write this lose the whole 7-mark sub-part.
> 6. **Forgetting that interface variables are `final`** — You cannot write `Payment.PI = 3.14;`. Common mistake.
> 7. **Writing a constructor inside an interface** — Illegal; lose marks if you include it in a class diagram or code snippet.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Implementing Interfaces**

- **Interface = Pure Contract:** no state, no constructors, no instantiation. ✓
- **Implicit Modifiers:** all methods are `public abstract` (pre-Java 8); all variables are `public static final`. ✓
- **Java 8 Additions:** `default` methods (with body, inheritable), `static` methods (interface-level utility). ✓
- **Java 9 Additions:** `private` methods in interfaces (helper utilities, not inherited). ✓
- **Implementation Keyword:** `class Foo implements InterfaceName`. ✓
- **Multiple Implementation:** comma-separated list — `implements I1, I2, I3`. ✓
- **Interface Inheritance:** `interface I2 extends I1` — multiple interface inheritance is allowed. ✓
- **Override Visibility Rule:** must be `public` — cannot reduce access. ✓
- **Diamond Problem Resolution:** use `InterfaceName.super.method()` to disambiguate default methods. ✓
- **Functional Interface:** exactly **one** abstract method; use `@FunctionalInterface` annotation. ✓
- **Marker Interface:** zero methods — e.g., `Serializable`, `Cloneable`, `Remote`. ✓
- **Polymorphism:** interface reference can hold any implementing class's object; JVM performs **dynamic dispatch** at runtime. ✓
- **Naming Convention:** suffix `-able` for capability interfaces (`Comparable`, `Runnable`, `Drawable`). ✓
- **Abstract Class vs Interface:** use abstract for shared *state + code* (is-a); use interface for shared *contract* (can-do). ✓
- **Real-World Examples:** `List`, `Set`, `Map`, `Runnable`, `Comparable`, `ActionListener`, `Connection`, `Statement`. ✓
- **Compile Error if Unimplemented:** *"X is not abstract and does not override abstract method Y()"* — unless the class itself is declared `abstract`. ✓
- **Final Mantra:** *Interfaces decouple **what** from **how** — they define a contract; classes define the implementation.* ✓
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Accessing Implementations through Interface References
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT633 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Accessing Implementations through Interface References

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the Java programming language (and in object-oriented design generally), an **Interface Reference** is a variable whose declared (compile-time) type is an interface, even though the object it points to at runtime belongs to a concrete class that *implements* that interface.

The official Java Language Specification (JLS, §4.3) and KTU 2024 PECIT633 syllabus define this concept as:

> *"An interface type can be used as the type of a reference variable. Such a reference variable can hold a reference to any instance of any class that implements the declared interface."*

Formally, if `I` is an interface and `C` is a class such that `C implements I`, then the assignment:

$$I \; ref \; = \; new \; C();$$

is a **legal, type-safe upcast** in Java. The variable `ref` is statically typed as `I`, but dynamically typed as `C`. The compiler enforces the interface contract; the JVM dispatches methods based on the actual object's runtime class.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> The ability to declare a reference of an interface type and bind it to any implementation is the **cornerstone of polymorphism** in Java. It is the practical mechanism behind the principle *"Program to an interface, not an implementation"* — a tenet of the **SOLID** design principles (specifically, the **Dependency Inversion Principle**).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Universal Remote Control"

Imagine you walk into an electronics shop and buy a **Universal Remote Control** (the *Interface Reference*). The remote itself is *not* a TV, *not* an air conditioner, *not* a set-top box. It is simply a remote with buttons: `power()`, `volumeUp()`, `volumeDown()`, `channelUp()`, `channelDown()` (these are the *abstract methods* of the interface).

Now you point this universal remote at:

- A **Samsung TV** (one implementation class)
- A **Sony TV** (another implementation class)
- An **LG TV** (a third implementation class)

The remote does not change. The buttons are the same. But pressing "Power" on the remote *delegates* the actual power-on command to whichever device is currently pointed at.

In this analogy:
- The **Universal Remote** = the *Interface Reference Variable*
- The **Button Layout** = the *Interface Contract (methods)*
- The **TVs** = the *Concrete Implementation Classes*
- **Pointing the remote at a TV** = `InterfaceType ref = new ConcreteClass();`
- **Pressing a button** = calling `ref.methodName()` — the actual response depends on the TV

This is precisely how Java achieves **dynamic (runtime) polymorphism** through interface references.

---

## 1.3 Why It Matters — Engineering Motivation

| Benefit | Real-World Software Engineering Value |
|---|---|
| **Loose Coupling** | Client code depends on an abstraction (interface), not a concrete class. Easy to swap implementations without rewriting client. |
| **Testability** | Unit tests can substitute mock implementations of an interface (e.g., for database or network mocking). |
| **Multiple Implementations** | Different algorithms/strategies can be selected at runtime (Strategy Pattern, Factory Pattern). |
| **API Stability** | A library can publish an interface as a stable contract and change internal implementations without breaking clients. |
| **Dependency Injection** | Frameworks like Spring leverage interface references to inject dependencies at runtime. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Java Metrics / Constants to remember:**
> - A class can implement **any number of interfaces** in Java (multiple interface inheritance is allowed).
> - A class can **extend only one class** (single class inheritance).
> - Since **Java 8**, interfaces can have `default` and `static` methods with bodies.
> - Since **Java 9**, interfaces can have `private` methods for code reuse among `default` methods.

---

## 1.4 Formal Grammar of Interface Reference Declaration

The general syntactic form sanctioned by the Java compiler is:

```
InterfaceName   referenceVariable;
referenceVariable = new ImplementingClassName(constructorArgs);
```

or in a single statement:

```
InterfaceName   referenceVariable = new ImplementingClassName(constructorArgs);
```

The compiler permits this assignment **if and only if** the class on the right-hand side has `implements` (or transitively implements) the interface on the left-hand side. If the type relationship does not exist, a **compile-time error** is raised:

```
error: incompatible types: ImplementingClassName cannot be converted to InterfaceName
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Rules — The "Laws" of Interface References

When a reference is declared with an interface type, the following rules are enforced by the **Java Compiler (javac)** at compile time and the **Java Virtual Machine (JVM)** at runtime.

### Rule 1 — Assignment Compatibility (Upcast)

A reference of an interface type may refer to **any instance** of any class that implements that interface, *directly or indirectly*.

```java
interface Drawable { void draw(); }
class Circle implements Drawable { public void draw() { /* ... */ } }
class RedCircle extends Circle { /* inherits draw() */ }

Drawable d1 = new Circle();      // ✅ LEGAL
Drawable d2 = new RedCircle();   // ✅ LEGAL (transitive implementation)
```

### Rule 2 — Restriction on Direct Instantiation

An interface is **incomplete** (in pre-Java 8 form, it had only abstract methods). Therefore, the compiler **forbids** the direct instantiation of an interface:

```java
Drawable d = new Drawable();   // ❌ COMPILE ERROR: Drawable is abstract; cannot be instantiated
```

This rule was **partially relaxed** in Java 8 with the introduction of `default` methods, but even then, an interface with *any* abstract method still cannot be instantiated.

### Rule 3 — Method Invocation Set is Determined by the Static Type

When you call a method on an interface reference, **only methods declared in the interface (or its super-interfaces) are accessible** through that reference, even if the actual object has more methods.

```java
interface Animal { void speak(); }
class Dog implements Animal {
    public void speak() { System.out.println("Woof"); }
    public void fetch() { System.out.println("Fetching..."); }
}

Animal a = new Dog();
a.speak();    // ✅ Allowed — speak() is in Animal
a.fetch();    // ❌ COMPILE ERROR — fetch() is not in Animal
((Dog) a).fetch();   // ✅ Allowed after explicit downcast
```

### Rule 4 — Method Body Selection is Determined by the Dynamic Type (Dynamic Dispatch)

The JVM uses the **actual runtime class** of the object to select which method body to execute. This is called **Dynamic Method Dispatch** and is implemented in the JVM via the **virtual method table (vtable)**.

### Rule 5 — Interface Inheritance Chain

Interfaces can extend other interfaces using `extends` (note: interfaces use `extends`, *not* `implements`, for parent interfaces):

```java
interface A { void methodA(); }
interface B extends A { void methodB(); }
```

A class implementing `B` must provide implementations for both `methodA()` and `methodB()`. A reference of type `A` can still hold a class implementing `B` (transitive assignment).

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula / Rules Cheat Sheet

| # | Rule / Formula | Description | Enforcement Stage |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `I ref = new C();` ⟺ `C implements I` | Assignment valid iff `C` is a subtype of `I` | **Compile time** |
| 2 | `new I()` | Direct instantiation of an interface is **forbidden** | **Compile time** |
| 3 | `ref.methodX()` | Only methods in the *static* type are callable | **Compile time** |
| 4 | `ref.methodX()` body | Method body comes from the *dynamic* (actual) type | **Runtime** (vtable lookup) |
| 5 | `I ref = new C();` then `C c = (C) ref;` | Explicit **downcast** required to access `C`-specific members | **Compile time** check; **runtime** `ClassCastException` if actual type is incompatible |
| 6 | `I ref = null;` `ref.methodX();` | Causes `NullPointerException` at runtime | **Runtime** |
| 7 | `instanceof` operator | `ref instanceof C` returns `true` iff actual object is `C` or subclass | **Runtime** |
| 8 | `final` interface variables | All interface fields are implicitly `public static final` (constants) | **Compile time** |

---

## 2.3 The Polymorphism Equation

The polymorphic behavior of interface references can be summarized as:

$$
\text{Method Executed} = f(\text{Dynamic Type of Object at Runtime})
$$

$$
\text{Methods Callable} = g(\text{Static Type of Reference at Compile Time})
$$

These are **two independent dimensions**:

- **Compile-time dimension** (rules 1, 2, 3, 5, 8) — controlled by the *reference* type.
- **Runtime dimension** (rules 4, 6, 7) — controlled by the *object* type.

This separation is what gives Java its **strong static typing** *and* **dynamic dispatch** — a combination unique to languages like Java and C#.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility in the Real World

| Domain | Use of Interface References |
|---|---|
| **JDBC** | `Connection conn = DriverManager.getConnection(...)` — `Connection` is an interface; the actual class is the vendor-specific driver. |
| **Collections Framework** | `List<String> list = new ArrayList<>();` vs. `List<String> list = new LinkedList<>();` — one reference, swappable implementations. |
| **GUI Frameworks** | `ActionListener listener = new MyButtonHandler();` — `ActionListener` is the interface; `MyButtonHandler` is the concrete class. |
| **Spring / Dependency Injection** | Beans are wired by interface type, allowing runtime substitution. |
| **Strategy Pattern** | `SortStrategy s = new QuickSort();` then `s.sort(arr);` — the same code uses different sorting algorithms. |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU lab exams and viva questions, you will frequently be asked: *"Why do we declare `List list = new ArrayList();` instead of `ArrayList list = new ArrayList();`?"* The expected answer: to **decouple the abstraction (`List`) from the implementation (`ArrayList`)**, allowing the implementation to be changed without altering the client code.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Basic Interface Reference Assignment

### Problem Statement
Given the following interface and classes, trace the output produced by each statement and explain which rules apply.

```java
interface Shape {
    void draw();
    double area();
}

class Circle implements Shape {
    private double r;
    public Circle(double r) { this.r = r; }
    public void draw()   { System.out.println("Drawing Circle"); }
    public double area() { return Math.PI * r * r; }
    public double circumference() { return 2 * Math.PI * r; }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Execution Trace

**Step 1: Declare interface reference, assign `Circle` object**

```java
Shape s = new Circle(5.0);
```

- **Rule 1** applies. `Circle` implements `Shape`, so the assignment is legal.
- `s` has **static type** = `Shape`, **dynamic type** = `Circle`.
- Memory: A `Circle` object is allocated on the heap. The reference `s` (on the stack) holds its address.

**Step 2: Call `draw()` through interface reference**

```java
s.draw();        // Output: "Drawing Circle"
```

- **Rule 3** — `draw()` is declared in `Shape`, so the call compiles.
- **Rule 4** — The JVM looks at the vtable of the actual object (`Circle`), finds `Circle.draw()`, and invokes it.

**Step 3: Call `area()` and assign to a `double`**

```java
double a = s.area();      // a ≈ 78.5398...
```

- `area()` is in `Shape`. Compile-time check passes. Runtime resolves to `Circle.area()`, which uses `r = 5.0`.

**Step 4: Attempt to call `circumference()` directly**

```java
s.circumference();   // ❌ COMPILE ERROR
```

- **Rule 3** — `circumference()` is **not** declared in `Shape`. The compiler rejects the call, even though the actual object is a `Circle` and has the method.

**Step 5: Downcast and call `circumference()`**

```java
Circle c = (Circle) s;
c.circumference();   // ✅ LEGAL → returns ≈ 31.4159
```

- **Rule 5** — Explicit downcast restores access to `Circle`-specific methods.
- The runtime check (`Checkcast` JVM instruction) verifies the actual type matches. Since `s` does point to a `Circle`, the cast succeeds.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Interface Reference in an Array

### Problem Statement

Demonstrate polymorphic array processing where the array's element type is an interface.

```java
interface Payable {
    double getPayment();
}

class Employee implements Payable {
    private double salary;
    public Employee(double s) { this.salary = s; }
    public double getPayment() { return salary; }
}

class Contractor implements Payable {
    private double hours, rate;
    public Contractor(double h, double r) { this.hours = h; this.rate = r; }
    public double getPayment() { return hours * rate; }
}

class Invoice implements Payable {
    private double amount;
    public Invoice(double a) { this.amount = a; }
    public double getPayment() { return amount; }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Trace

**Step 1: Create a polymorphic array**

```java
Payable[] payments = new Payable[3];
payments[0] = new Employee(50000);
payments[1] = new Contractor(160, 750);
payments[2] = new Invoice(250000);
```

- All three objects are subtypes of `Payable`, so all three assignments are legal.
- The array is **statically typed** to hold `Payable` references; each slot is **dynamically** a different concrete type.

**Step 2: Process uniformly using a loop**

```java
double total = 0;
for (Payable p : payments) {
    total += p.getPayment();
}
```

- For each iteration, the JVM performs a vtable lookup on the actual object:
  - `payments[0]` is `Employee` → calls `Employee.getPayment()` → returns `50000.0`
  - `payments[1]` is `Contractor` → calls `Contractor.getPayment()` → returns `160 × 750 = 120000.0`
  - `payments[2]` is `Invoice` → calls `Invoice.getPayment()` → returns `250000.0`

**Step 3: Final value of `total`**

$$
\text{total} = 50000.0 + 120000.0 + 250000.0 = 420000.0
$$

This is the **engineering power** of interface references: *one piece of client code, many possible behaviors, all selected dynamically.*

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Method Parameter as Interface Type

### Problem Statement

Show how a method signature can declare an interface parameter, accepting any implementation.

```java
interface Logger {
    void log(String message);
}

class ConsoleLogger implements Logger {
    public void log(String m) { System.out.println("CONSOLE: " + m); }
}

class FileLogger implements Logger {
    public void log(String m) { System.out.println("FILE: " + m); }
}

class Application {
    public void performAction(Logger logger) {
        logger.log("Action started");
        // ... business logic ...
        logger.log("Action completed");
    }
}
```

### Analytical Trace

**Call 1:**

```java
Application app = new Application();
app.performAction(new ConsoleLogger());
```

- Inside `performAction`, the parameter `logger` has static type `Logger` and dynamic type `ConsoleLogger`. Each `logger.log(...)` call dispatches to `ConsoleLogger.log()`.
- Output: `CONSOLE: Action started` and `CONSOLE: Action completed`.

**Call 2:**

```java
app.performAction(new FileLogger());
```

- No change to `Application` code. The same method now dispatches to `FileLogger.log()`.
- Output: `FILE: Action started` and `FILE: Action completed`.

**Mathematical abstraction of this pattern:**

$$
\text{Application.performAction}: \text{Logger} \rightarrow \text{Void}
$$

where the actual mapping (semantics) is determined by whichever `Logger` implementation is supplied. This is the **Strategy Pattern** in its purest form.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Static vs Dynamic Type Decisions

| Statement | Static Type of `ref` | Dynamic Type of Object | Methods Compilable | Method Body Executed | Rule Applied |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `I r = new C();` | `I` | `C` | All of `I` | — | Rule 1 |
| `r.m1();` (m1 in I) | `I` | `C` | `m1` | `C.m1` | Rules 3, 4 |
| `r.m2();` (m2 not in I) | `I` | `C` | None | Compile error | Rule 3 |
| `((C) r).m2();` | `I` then `C` | `C` | `m2` | `C.m2` | Rule 5 |
| `r = null; r.m1();` | `I` | (none) | `m1` | `NullPointerException` | Rule 6 |
| `r instanceof D` | `I` | `C` | — | `true` if `C` is `D` or subclass | Rule 7 |
| `I r2 = new D();` where `D implements I` | `I` | `D` | All of `I` | — | Rule 1 |

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 4 — `instanceof` Guarded Downcast

### Problem Statement

Safely access implementation-specific methods when the static type is the interface.

```java
interface Vehicle { void start(); }
class Car implements Vehicle {
    public void start() { System.out.println("Car starting"); }
    public void playRadio() { System.out.println("Radio on"); }
}
class Bike implements Vehicle {
    public void start() { System.out.println("Bike starting"); }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Trace

```java
Vehicle v = factoryProduce();   // may return Car or Bike

v.start();                      // Always safe — in interface

if (v instanceof Car) {         // Runtime type check
    Car c = (Car) v;            // Safe downcast — instanceof guarantees compatibility
    c.playRadio();              // Implementation-specific method
}
```

- The `instanceof` operator returns `true` only if the actual object is `Car` or a subclass of `Car`.
- After the `if`, the compiler *and* the JVM trust the downcast.
- **Best practice (KTU viva)**: Always use `instanceof` before downcasting from an interface to a concrete type, to avoid `ClassCastException`.

---

## 3.6 The Dynamic Method Dispatch Equation

When a method is called on an interface reference `r` pointing to an object of class `C`, the JVM performs:

$$
\text{address of method to invoke} = \text{vtable}_{C}[\text{index of method}]
$$

where `vtable_C` is the **virtual method table** of class `C` (built at class loading time). This is a constant-time operation, making polymorphic dispatch in Java very efficient — no linear search at call time.

If class `C` does not override a method `m` from interface `I`, the vtable entry for `m` points to the **superclass's** or **default** implementation, recursively up the hierarchy until a concrete implementation is found.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Class Hierarchy Diagram — Interface Implementation Map

```mermaid
classDiagram
    direction LR
    class Payable {
        <<interface>>
        +getPayment() double
    }
    class Employee {
        -salary double
        +Employee(double)
        +getPayment() double
    }
    class Contractor {
        -hours double
        -rate double
        +Contractor(double, double)
        +getPayment() double
    }
    class Invoice {
        -amount double
        +Invoice(double)
        +getPayment() double
    }
    Payable <|.. Employee : implements
    Payable <|.. Contractor : implements
    Payable <|.. Invoice : implements
```

### Diagram Interpretation (for exam answers)

- The `<<interface>>` stereotype in UML identifies `Payable` as an interface (Java keyword `interface`).
- Dashed arrows with open triangles (`<|..`) denote **realization/implementation**.
- All three concrete classes *realize* the `Payable` contract, each providing its own `getPayment()` body.
- A reference of type `Payable` may legally point to an instance of **any** of the three concrete classes.

---

## 4.2 Memory Reference Model — Interface Reference to Object

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph StackFrame["Call Stack (main method)"]
        refVar["ref : Drawable\n[address: 0x4F2A]"]
    end
    subgraph HeapArea["Heap Memory"]
        circleObj["Object: Circle\n------------\nr = 5.0\n------------\nvtable ptr ---->"]
        vtable["Virtual Method Table (Circle)\n--------------------------\ndraw()   --> Circle.draw()\narea()   --> Circle.area()\nclone()  --> Object.clone()\n..."]
    end
    refVar -. "points to" .-> circleObj
    circleObj -- "has reference to" --> vtable
```

### Diagram Interpretation

- The **stack frame** holds the local variable `ref` of static type `Drawable`, containing the heap address of the actual `Circle` object.
- The **heap object** contains the instance field `r = 5.0` and a **pointer to the vtable** for `Circle`.
- When `ref.draw()` is called:
  1. Compiler verifies `draw()` is in `Drawable` (static check).
  2. JVM follows the reference to the `Circle` object.
  3. JVM consults the vtable, finds `Circle.draw()`, and invokes it.

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Runtime Method Dispatch

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Source Code:\nDrawable ref = new Circle(5.0);\nref.draw();"] --> B["javac Compiler"]
    B --> C{"Static Type Check\nIs draw() in Drawable?"}
    C -- "Yes" --> D["Generate Bytecode:\nALOAD ref\nINVOKEINTERFACE Drawable.draw"]
    C -- "No" --> E["Compile Error:\ncannot find symbol"]
    D --> F["JVM Execution"]
    F --> G["Pop reference from stack"]
    G --> H["Follow pointer to Circle object"]
    H --> I["Load Circle vtable"]
    I --> J{"Lookup draw()\nin vtable"}
    J --> K["Invoke Circle.draw()\nOutput: Drawing Circle"]
```

### Diagram Interpretation

This flow chart captures the **two-stage process** of a polymorphic method call through an interface reference:

1. **Static stage** (compile time): the compiler checks that the method exists in the interface.
2. **Dynamic stage** (runtime): the JVM uses the vtable to select the actual method body.

---

## 4.4 Use-Case Diagram — Pattern of Interface Reference Usage

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ClientCode["Client Application Code"]
        clientApp["Application\n---------\nperformAction(Logger l)\n{ l.log(msg); }"]
    end
    subgraph InterfaceContract["Abstraction Layer"]
        loggerIface["Logger (interface)\n+ log(String)"]
    end
    subgraph Implementations["Concrete Implementations"]
        consoleImpl["ConsoleLogger\n+ log(String)"]
        fileImpl["FileLogger\n+ log(String)"]
        dbImpl["DatabaseLogger\n+ log(String)"]
    end
    clientApp -->|"depends on (loose coupling)"| loggerIface
    consoleImpl -.->|"implements"| loggerIface
    fileImpl -.->|"implements"| loggerIface
    dbImpl -.->|"implements"| loggerIface
```

### Diagram Interpretation

- The `Application` class **depends only on the `Logger` interface**, not on any concrete class.
- Each concrete logger (`ConsoleLogger`, `FileLogger`, `DatabaseLogger`) realizes the same interface.
- The client code is **independent of the implementation**, satisfying the **Dependency Inversion Principle (DIP)** of SOLID.
