# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example 1 — Basic Multi-Catch with Arithmetic and Array Operations

Let us derive, step-by-step, the complete execution of the following Java program:

```java
public class MultiCatchDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            int[] arr = new int[5];
            arr[0] = 10;
            arr[4] = 40;
            int x = arr[4] / arr[0];      // Line A: 40 / 10 = 4
            System.out.println("Result: " + x);
            String s = null;
            System.out.println(s.length()); // Line B: NullPointerException
        } catch (ArithmeticException ae) {
            System.out.println("Caught Arithmetic: " + ae.getMessage());
        } catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException aioobe) {
            System.out.println("Caught Array Index: " + aioobe.getMessage());
        } catch (NullPointerException npe) {
            System.out.println("Caught Null Pointer: " + npe.getMessage());
        } catch (Exception e) {
            System.out.println("Generic catch-all: " + e.getMessage());
        } finally {
            System.out.println("Finally block executed.");
        }
        System.out.println("Program continues normally.");
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Trace (Line-by-Line Derivation):

**Step 1 — Program Entry**
The JVM invokes `main()`. Execution enters the `try` block.

**Step 2 — Array Creation**
`int[] arr = new int[5];` creates an integer array of length 5, with default values `{0,0,0,0,0}`. No exception is thrown.

**Step 3 — Assignment Operations**
`arr[0] = 10;` and `arr[4] = 40;` modify the array to `{10, 0, 0, 0, 40}`. No exception.

**Step 4 — Division at Line A**
The expression `arr[4] / arr[0]` evaluates to $40 \div 10 = 4$. Stored in `x`. No exception.

**Step 5 — Println Execution**
`System.out.println("Result: " + x);` prints `Result: 4`. No exception.

**Step 6 — String Initialization**
`String s = null;` initializes the reference variable `s` to `null`. No exception yet — a `null` reference is a valid Java state.

**Step 7 — Exception Trigger at Line B**
`System.out.println(s.length());` attempts to invoke the `length()` method on a `null` reference. The JVM detects this and **throws a `NullPointerException`** object.

**Step 8 — Termination of try Block**
Execution of the remaining statements inside the `try` block is **abruptly aborted**. The JVM now begins searching for a matching catch block.

**Step 9 — Sequential Catch Matching**

The thrown exception type is `NullPointerException`. The JVM checks catch blocks in order:

$$\text{Catch Block 1: } \texttt{ArithmeticException}$$

Is `NullPointerException instanceof ArithmeticException`? **No** (they are siblings under `RuntimeException`). Skip.

$$\text{Catch Block 2: } \texttt{ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException}$$

Is `NullPointerException instanceof ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`? **No**. Skip.

$$\text{Catch Block 3: } \texttt{NullPointerException}$$

Is `NullPointerException instanceof NullPointerException`? **YES** (exact type match). The parameter `npe` is bound to the thrown object.

**Step 10 — Handler Execution**
`System.out.println("Caught Null Pointer: " + npe.getMessage());` executes, printing:
`Caught Null Pointer: null`

**Step 11 — Finally Block Execution**
Control transfers to the `finally` block, executing:
`Finally block executed.`

**Step 12 — Post-Construct Continuation**
`System.out.println("Program continues normally.");` is executed.

**Final Output:**
```
Result: 4
Caught Null Pointer: null
Finally block executed.
Program continues normally.
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Observation:** Note how the `catch (Exception e)` block at the bottom was **NEVER reached**, even though `NullPointerException` IS a subclass of `Exception`. This proves the first-match-wins rule.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example 2 — Compile-Time Error: Unreachable Catch

Let us derive why the following code **fails to compile**:

```java
try {
    int[] a = new int[5];
    a[10] = 50;
} catch (Exception e) {
    System.out.println("General exception");
} catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e) {
    System.out.println("This will NEVER be reached");
}
```

### Step-by-Step Compilation Analysis:

**Step 1 — Compiler Type Analysis**
The compiler performs a **reachability analysis** on the catch blocks. It recognizes that `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` is a **subclass** of `Exception`.

**Step 2 — Reachability Check**
Since `catch (Exception e)` would match **any** `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` thrown in the try block (because subclass instances are valid for supertype handlers), the JVM is guaranteed to select the first catch block.

**Step 3 — Compile-Time Error Generation**
The `catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e)` block is therefore **provably unreachable code**. The Java compiler raises:

```
error: exception java.lang.ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException has already been caught
```

**Conclusion / Derivation Result:**

$$\text{Compiler Error} \iff \exists \text{ catch block } C_i \text{ whose parameter type } T_i \text{ is a strict subclass of some } T_j \text{ where } j < i$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Exception Class Hierarchy in Java

The following table (excerpted from the standard `java.lang` package) clarifies the catch ordering requirement:

| Exception Class | Extends | Extends | Type | Checked/Unchecked |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `ArithmeticException` | `RuntimeException` | `Exception` | Subclass | Unchecked |
| `NullPointerException` | `RuntimeException` | `Exception` | Subclass | Unchecked |
| `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` | `IndexOutOfBoundsException` | `RuntimeException` → `Exception` | Subclass | Unchecked |
| `NumberFormatException` | `IllegalArgumentException` | `RuntimeException` → `Exception` | Subclass | Unchecked |
| `IOException` | `Exception` | `Throwable` | Subclass | **Checked** |
| `FileNotFoundException` | `IOException` | `Exception` | Sub-subclass | **Checked** |
| `SQLException` | `Exception` | `Throwable` | Subclass | **Checked** |
| `RuntimeException` | `Exception` | `Throwable` | Mid-level | Unchecked |
| `Exception` | `Throwable` | `Object` | Top-level | Mixed |
| `Error` | `Throwable` | `Object` | Sibling to `Exception` | Unchecked (FATAL) |
| `Throwable` | `Object` | — | Root | Mixed |

**Correct catch order (derived from the table):**

```java
try { ... }
catch (FileNotFoundException e)   { /* most specific */ }
catch (IOException e)             { /* intermediate */ }
catch (Exception e)               { /* most general */ }
```

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Exception Class Hierarchy — Tree Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Object] --> B[Throwable]
    B --> C[Exception]
    B --> D[Error]
    C --> E[IOException]
    C --> F[RuntimeException]
    C --> G[SQLException]
    E --> H[FileNotFoundException]
    E --> I[EOFException]
    F --> J[ArithmeticException]
    F --> K[NullPointerException]
    F --> L[ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException]
    F --> M[NumberFormatException]
    F --> N[ClassCastException]
    D --> O[OutOfMemoryError]
    D --> P[StackOverflowError]
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**
- `Throwable` is the **root** of the exception hierarchy in Java.
- The two main branches are `Exception` (recoverable) and `Error` (fatal, typically not caught).
- `RuntimeException` and its subclasses are **unchecked** (compiler does not force handling).
- `IOException` and its subclasses are **checked** (compiler enforces handling).

## 4.2 Execution Flow Diagram — Try with Multiple Catch Clauses

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Enter try block] --> B{Execute try body}
    B -->|No exception thrown| C[Skip all catch blocks]
    B -->|Exception thrown| D[JVM creates exception object]
    D --> E[Search catch blocks in order]
    E --> F{catch 1 matches?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Execute catch 1 handler]
    F -->|No| H{catch 2 matches?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Execute catch 2 handler]
    H -->|No| J{catch N matches?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Execute catch N handler]
    J -->|No| L[No match found]
    G --> M[finally block executes]
    I --> M
    K --> M
    C --> M
    L --> N[Exception propagates up the call stack]
    M --> O[Continue program execution]
    N --> O
    O --> P[End]
```

**Interpretation:**
- The diagram visualizes the **sequential top-to-bottom matching** of catch blocks.
- Notice that **only one** catch block is ever executed per exception event.
- The `finally` block executes **regardless** of whether a catch matched, making it ideal for resource cleanup.
- If no catch matches, the exception **propagates** up the call stack for the caller to handle.

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Multi-Catch Handler in a Banking System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User Initiates Transaction] --> B[Try Block: Process Transaction]
    B --> C{Exception Type}
    C -->|InsufficientFundsException| D[Handler 1: Notify user, log, suggest top-up]
    C -->|InvalidPINException| E[Handler 2: Prompt re-entry, lock after 3 attempts]
    C -->|DatabaseException| F[Handler 3: Retry 3 times, alert admin if persistent]
    C -->|NetworkException| G[Handler 4: Queue transaction for offline processing]
    C -->|Generic Exception| H[Handler 5: Rollback, log critical error, alert user]
    D --> I[Finally: Close DB connection, release lock]
    E --> I
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Return to Main Flow]
```

**Interpretation:**
- This real-world block diagram shows how a banking system uses Multiple catch Clauses to provide **type-specific recovery** strategies.
- The `finally` block consistently ensures that **resources (DB connections, locks) are released** regardless of which catch handler ran.
- This is the **canonical enterprise pattern** for exception handling.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Define Multiple catch Clauses. Why is the order of catch blocks important in Java?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Multiple catch Clauses refer to the use of two or more `catch` blocks following a single `try` block, with each catch block designed to handle a **specific type of exception**. When an exception occurs in the try block, the JVM matches it against the catch blocks **sequentially in the order they are written** and executes the **first matching handler**. **(1.5 marks)**

The order is critical because if a more general (superclass) catch block is placed before a more specific (subclass) catch block, the specific catch block becomes **unreachable code**, which results in a **compilation error** in Java. Therefore, catch blocks must always be ordered from **most specific (subclass) to most general (superclass)**. **(1.5 marks)**

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**What is the difference between a single catch and multiple catch clauses? When is the multi-catch syntax (Java 7+) preferred?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A single catch clause can handle only one type of exception, while multiple catch clauses allow handling of **multiple different exception types** with **distinct, specialized recovery logic** for each. **(1 mark)**

The Java 7+ multi-catch syntax, written as `catch (IOException | SQLException e)`, is preferred when:
- The handling logic for multiple unrelated exception types is **identical**, eliminating code duplication. **(1 mark)**
- It improves code **readability and maintainability** by combining handlers that would otherwise be duplicated. **(1 mark)**

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the concept of Multiple catch Clauses in Java with a suitable example. Discuss the rules that must be followed when using multiple catch blocks. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program that demonstrates the use of multiple catch clauses to handle `ArithmeticException`, `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`, and a generic `Exception`. Show what happens when the order of catch blocks is changed. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Explanation (7 marks):**

Multiple catch Clauses in Java allow a single `try` block to be followed by **multiple catch blocks**, each specialized for a particular exception type. When an exception is thrown within the `try` block, the JVM performs a **sequential type-check** of the catch blocks in their order of appearance and executes the body of the **first catch block whose parameter type matches** the thrown exception's class (or one of its superclasses). **(2 marks)**

**Syntax:**
```java
try {
    // protected code
} catch (ExceptionType1 e1) {
    // handler 1
} catch (ExceptionType2 e2) {
    // handler 2
} catch (ExceptionTypeN eN) {
    // handler N
}
```

**Rules to follow: (5 marks — 1 mark each)**

1. **Only one catch block executes per exception event.** The matching process terminates once a compatible handler is found.

2. **Catch blocks must be ordered from most specific (subclass) to most general (superclass).** Placing a superclass catch before a subclass catch results in a **compilation error** of "unreachable code."

3. **The catch parameter must be a reference type that is a subclass of `Throwable`** (or `Throwable` itself). Primitive types are not permitted.

4. **Sibling catch blocks (those with no inheritance relationship) can be placed in any order** without affecting correctness, as the matching depends on the runtime type of the exception.

5. **A multi-catch block (Java 7+) cannot include exception types that share an inheritance relationship.** For example, `catch (IOException | FileNotFoundException e)` is illegal because `FileNotFoundException` IS-A `IOException`.

---

**(b) Java Program Demonstration (7 marks):**

```java
import java.util.Scanner;

public class MultiCatchOrderDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
        try {
            System.out.print("Enter numerator: ");
            int a = sc.nextInt();
            System.out.print("Enter denominator: ");
            int b = sc.nextInt();
            int result = a / b;
            System.out.println("Result: " + result);
            
            int[] arr = {1, 2, 3};
            System.out.println("Element at index 5: " + arr[5]);
            
        } catch (ArithmeticException e) {
            System.out.println("Arithmetic error: Cannot divide by zero.");
        } catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e) {
            System.out.println("Array error: Index out of range.");
        } catch (Exception e) {
            System.out.println("Generic error: " + e.getMessage());
        } finally {
            System.out.println("Cleanup in finally block.");
            sc.close();
        }
    }
}
```

**Sample Run 1 (a=10, b=2):**
```
Enter numerator: 10
Enter denominator: 2
Result: 5
Array error: Index out of range.
Cleanup in finally block.
```
The `ArithmeticException` catch is skipped (no exception), the `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` catch matches and executes. **(1 mark for trace)**

**Sample Run 2 (a=10, b=0):**
```
Enter numerator: 10
Enter denominator: 0
Arithmetic error: Cannot divide by zero.
Cleanup in finally block.
```
The `ArithmeticException` catch is the first match. **(1 mark for trace)**

**Changing the Order — Compilation Error:**
If we reorder the catches to place `catch (Exception e)` first, followed by `catch (ArithmeticException e)`, the compiler raises an **"unreachable code" error** because `ArithmeticException` is a subclass of `Exception` and would never be reached. **(2 marks for explanation)**

**Key takeaway:** The order **subclass → superclass** is mandatory for compilation success and ensures the most specific handler gets the first opportunity to process the exception. **(1 mark for conclusion)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** What is the multi-catch feature introduced in Java 7? Explain with an example. What are the constraints of multi-catch? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between checked and unchecked exceptions. Write a Java program that uses multiple catch blocks to handle both types and explain the output. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Multi-catch Feature (7 marks):**

The **multi-catch feature**, introduced in **Java 7 (JSR 334 — Project Coin)**, allows a single `catch` block to handle **multiple unrelated exception types** in a compact form, using the **pipe symbol** `|` as a separator. **(2 marks)**

**Syntax:**
```java
try {
    // code that may throw multiple exception types
} catch (IOException | SQLException | ClassNotFoundException ex) {
    // common handler for all three
    System.out.println("Error: " + ex.getMessage());
}
```

**Example with Explanation: (3 marks)**
```java
import java.io.*;
import java.sql.*;

public class MultiCatchExample {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            FileReader fr = new FileReader("data.txt");
            Connection conn = DriverManager.getConnection("jdbc:mysql://localhost/test");
        } catch (FileNotFoundException | SQLException ex) {
            System.out.println("Resource access failed: " + ex.getMessage());
        }
    }
}
```
Here, both `FileNotFoundException` (a checked exception) and `SQLException` (also a checked exception) are handled by a **single catch block** with identical recovery logic, reducing code duplication.

**Constraints of Multi-catch: (2 marks)**

1. The exception types in a multi-catch **cannot be in the same inheritance hierarchy**. For example, `catch (IOException | FileNotFoundException e)` is **illegal** because `FileNotFoundException` extends `IOException`.

2. The exception parameter in a multi-catch is **implicitly `final`**. You **cannot reassign** it inside the catch block (e.g., `e = new IOException();` is a compile-time error).

---

**(b) Checked vs Unchecked Exceptions with Program (7 marks):**

**Differences: (3 marks — 1 mark each for three points)**

| Feature | Checked Exceptions | Unchecked Exceptions |
|---|---|---|
| **Compiler enforcement** | Compiler forces handling via `try-catch` or `throws` | Compiler does NOT enforce handling |
| **Inheritance** | Subclass of `Exception` (but NOT `RuntimeException`) | Subclass of `RuntimeException` or `Error` |
| **Examples** | `IOException`, `SQLException`, `ClassNotFoundException` | `ArithmeticException`, `NullPointerException`, `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` |
| **Use case** | External/resource-related failures (recoverable) | Programming bugs / logic errors |

**Java Program Demonstrating Both: (4 marks)**

```java
import java.io.*;

public class CheckedUncheckedDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            // Unchecked: division by zero
            int a = 10 / 0;
            
            // Checked: file operation
            FileReader fr = new FileReader("missing.txt");
            
        } catch (ArithmeticException e) {
            System.out.println("Unchecked (Arithmetic): " + e.getMessage());
        } catch (FileNotFoundException e) {
            System.out.println("Checked (File): " + e.getMessage());
        } catch (RuntimeException e) {
            System.out.println("Generic runtime: " + e.getMessage());
        } catch (Exception e) {
            System.out.println("Generic exception: " + e.getMessage());
        }
    }
}
```

**Output Analysis:**
For the code above, the division by zero on the first line throws `ArithmeticException` (an unchecked exception). The JVM matches it with the first catch block (`catch (ArithmeticException e)`), executes it, and **skips the remaining catches**. The `FileNotFoundException` line is **never reached** because the exception occurred before it.

**Output:**
```
Unchecked (Arithmetic): / by zero
```

This demonstrates the **first-match-wins** principle and the importance of catch block ordering. **(1 mark for conclusion)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Catch Order Trap:** Examiners frequently test whether you know that placing a superclass catch before a subclass catch causes a **compilation error**. Always state and demonstrate the correct order.
> 2. **Single Execution Myth:** Students write answers claiming "all matching catch blocks execute." This is factually wrong and costs full marks. State clearly: **only the first matching catch executes.**
> 3. **Multi-catch Hierarchy Mistake:** Never write `catch (IOException | FileNotFoundException e)` in answers — it is **invalid syntax** and will be penalized.
> 4. **Confusion between `throw` and `throws`:** Examiners look for precise keyword usage. `throw` is used with an object; `throws` is used in method signatures.
> 5. **Checked vs Unchecked Confusion:** Be precise — `RuntimeException` and its subclasses are unchecked, **not** `Exception` and its subclasses in general.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Multiple catch Clauses** = one `try` + many `catch` blocks, each handling a different exception type.
- ✅ **Only ONE catch block executes** per exception event — the first match in the order written.
- ✅ **Mandatory order:** Most specific (subclass) **→** Most general (superclass).
- ✅ **Compile-time error** arises if a superclass catch is placed before a subclass catch (unreachable code).
- ✅ **Multi-catch (Java 7+):** Uses `|` to combine unrelated exception types in a single catch. The parameter is implicitly `final`.
- ✅ **Multi-catch constraint:** Combined types must NOT be in the same inheritance hierarchy.
- ✅ **Sibling catches** (no inheritance relation) can be written in any order.
- ✅ **The `finally` block** always executes, regardless of whether an exception was caught, making it ideal for cleanup.
- ✅ **No match = exception propagates** up the call stack to the calling method.
- ✅ **Match criterion:** `thrownException instanceof catchParamType` evaluated top-to-bottom.
- ✅ **Best practice:** Use narrow, specific catch blocks; avoid catching `Throwable` or generic `Exception` unless absolutely necessary.
- ✅ **Difference between `throw` and `throws`:** `throw` raises an exception object; `throws` declares exceptions a method may propagate.
- ✅ **Real-world analogy:** Like a hospital triage desk routing patients to specialist doctors — specific first, generalist last.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nested try Statements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT633 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Nested try Statements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)**
> A **Nested try Statement** in Java is an exception-handling construct in which a `try` block is placed (nested) inside another `try` block, inside a `catch` block, inside a `finally` block, or inside any method invoked from within a `try` block. The inner `try` block operates on its own dedicated segment of the *Exception Monitor* (the runtime mechanism that watches for thrown exceptions), enabling granular, localized exception resolution. This is a direct application of the **Divide and Conquer** principle to error handling — a core tenet taught in the Object Oriented Programming (OOP) module of the KTU 2024 B.Tech CSE/IT curriculum.

Nested `try` statements allow programmers to handle different categories of exceptions at different levels of granularity. When an exception is thrown within an inner `try` block, the Java Virtual Machine (JVM) searches for a matching `catch` clause **starting from the innermost block** and progressively moves outward until a match is found. This search is governed by the **Method Call Stack unwinding mechanism**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Hospital Triage System"**
> Imagine you walk into a large hospital (the **outer try** block). The receptionist (the **outer catch**) handles general complaints. But suppose you have a specific cardiac issue — you are then routed to the **Cardiology Department** (the **inner try** block), where a specialist cardiologist (the **inner catch**) handles it precisely. If the specialist cannot resolve it, you are sent back to a higher-level consultant (the **outer catch**). This layered approach allows the system to handle problems at the **most specific level first**, then escalate gracefully.
>
> In Java terms: **Inner catch** = specialist; **Outer catch** = generalist. The most specific handler catches the exception first.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Terms You Must Memorize**
> - **Inner `try` block** — the `try` block that is physically enclosed within another `try` (or its associated `catch`/`finally`).
> - **Outer `try` block** — the enclosing `try` block.
> - **Exception Monitor** — the JVM component that watches the statements inside a `try` block for thrown exceptions.
> - **Stack Unwinding** — the process by which the JVM propagates an uncaught exception up the call stack to find a matching `catch` handler.
> - **Most-Recent Handler Rule** — Java always searches the **innermost** matching `catch` block first.
> - **Rethrowing an Exception** — a `catch` block can re-throw the caught exception to the outer `try` using the `throw` keyword.

### 1.4 Why Nested try? (The "Why" Question)

> [!NOTE]
> **Justification for Nested try in OOP Design**
> 1. **Localized Recovery** — handle a specific fault in a tight code region without aborting the whole method.
> 2. **Different Exception Categories** — handle arithmetic errors in the inner block, while the outer block handles I/O or network faults.
> 3. **Resource Hierarchies** — outer `try` opens a database connection; inner `try` performs a query. A query failure should not leave the connection unreleased, so the outer `finally` cleans up.
> 4. **Method Nesting** — methods called from within a `try` block can themselves contain their own `try-catch`, which is logically a *nested* scenario.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 General Syntax of Nested try

```java
try {
    // OUTER try block
    statement_1;
    try {
        // INNER try block
        statement_2;
        statement_3;
    }
    catch (ExceptionType1 e1) {
        // INNER catch
        handler_1;
    }
    catch (ExceptionType2 e2) {
        // INNER catch
        handler_2;
    }
    finally {
        // INNER finally (optional)
    }
    statement_4;
}
catch (ExceptionType3 e3) {
    // OUTER catch
    handler_3;
}
finally {
    // OUTER finally (optional)
}
```

### 2.2 Five Legal Nesting Configurations (KTU Frequently Asked)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Valid Places to Nest a `try` Block (Java Language Specification, JLS §14.20)**
> 1. `try` inside `try` — most common.
> 2. `try` inside `catch` — for re-throwing and re-handling.
> 3. `try` inside `finally` — for cleanup-time exception handling.
> 4. `try` inside a method that is *called from* a `try` block — method-level nesting.
> 5. `try` inside a constructor's `try` block — initialization fault isolation.

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Operational Logic (The "How")

The control-flow engine of nested `try` operates as follows:

1. **Step 1 — Enter Outer `try`**: JVM marks the outer block as the active exception-monitoring region.
2. **Step 2 — Enter Inner `try`**: A *new, more specific* monitoring region is pushed onto the internal exception stack.
3. **Step 3 — Statement Execution**: Statements execute sequentially. If no exception occurs, control flows normally past all `catch` blocks.
4. **Step 4 — Exception Thrown in Inner Block**: The JVM searches for a matching `catch` **within the inner block first**.
   - **Match found** → inner `catch` executes; control passes to the outer `try`'s post-block code (or its `finally`).
   - **No match in inner block** → the exception **propagates outward** to the outer `try`'s `catch` clauses.
5. **Step 5 — Exception Thrown in Outer Block (Outside Inner try)**: Only outer `catch` blocks are eligible.
6. **Step 6 — Finally Execution**: `finally` blocks execute **regardless of whether an exception occurred** (only `System.exit()` or JVM crash can bypass).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Rule / Formula** | **Example / Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| Search Order for `catch` | `Innermost → Outermost` | Always the inner block first |
| Inner exception, inner catch | `Handled locally` | Outer block never sees the exception |
| Inner exception, no inner catch | `Propagates to outer catch` | Stack unwinding occurs |
| Outer exception, no outer catch | `Propagates to caller` | Method-level propagation |
| `finally` execution | `Always` (except `System.exit()`) | Cleanup is guaranteed |
| Re-throw syntax | `throw e;` inside `catch` | Passes to outer handler |
| Method-call nesting | `Inner try` is in the called method | Same rules apply |
| Lost exception pitfall | Inner `try`'s exception **shadows** outer | Use specific catch types first |
| `try` with resources in nested | `try (...) { try (...) { ... } }` | Available since Java 7 |
| Multi-catch (Java 7+) | `catch (E1 \| E2 e)` | Single parameter for many types |

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Mnemonic**
> **"I-SO-LATE"** — *Inner Searched first, Outer Last, Any Finally Terminates Exit* (rule for `finally` override).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Database Programming** — outer `try` opens connection; inner `try` runs queries. If a query fails, only the query's `catch` runs, but the connection still closes via outer `finally`.
- **Network Programming** — outer `try` opens socket; inner `try` performs I/O on the socket.
- **File Processing** — outer `try` opens file; inner `try` parses records. A bad record triggers inner `catch`, file still closes.
- **Embedded Systems / IoT** — outer `try` initializes sensor; inner `try` reads value. A faulty read is handled locally without restarting the whole acquisition loop.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Since *Object Oriented Programming* is a CS core subject, **Java code listings and exhaustive line-by-line analysis** are the appropriate analytical tools here. The KTU 2024 syllabus uses Java as the reference language.

### 3.1 Case Study 1 — Inner Exception Caught Locally (Most Common Scenario)

**Problem Statement:** Demonstrate a nested `try` where the inner block throws an `ArithmeticException` that is caught **within the inner block** itself. The outer `try` block contains code that is *not* affected.

```java
// File: NestedTryDemo1.java
class NestedTryDemo1 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // OUTER try block begins
        try {
            System.out.println("OUTER try: Beginning outer block.");
            int a = 10, b = 2;

            // INNER try block begins
            try {
                System.out.println("INNER try: Performing risky division...");
                int result = a / b;          // Line A — no exception if b != 0
                System.out.println("INNER try: Result = " + result);
                int[] arr = {1, 2, 3};
                System.out.println("INNER try: arr[5] = " + arr[5]); // Line B — ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
            }
            catch (ArithmeticException ae) {
                System.out.println("INNER catch: Arithmetic fault → " + ae);
            }
            catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException aiobe) {
                System.out.println("INNER catch: Array fault → " + aiobe);
            }
            // INNER try block ends

            System.out.println("OUTER try: Inner block finished normally.");
        }
        catch (Exception e) {
            System.out.println("OUTER catch: Generic → " + e);
        }
        finally {
            System.out.println("OUTER finally: Cleanup complete.");
        }
        System.out.println("main(): Program ended gracefully.");
    }
}
```

#### Line-by-Line Exhaustive Analysis

1. **Line 1-2**: Class declaration and `main` method entry — JVM starts execution.
2. **Line 4**: Outer `try` block opened. JVM pushes an "outer monitor" frame.
3. **Line 5**: Prints `"OUTER try: Beginning outer block."` — confirms entry.
4. **Line 6**: Local variables `a=10, b=2` declared. **No exception possible** here.
5. **Line 9**: Inner `try` block opened. JVM pushes an "inner monitor" frame **on top of the outer**.
6. **Line 10**: Print statement — entry confirmation.
7. **Line 11**: `result = 10 / 2 = 5`. **No exception** (denominator ≠ 0). Stored in `result`.
8. **Line 12**: Prints `"INNER try: Result = 5"`.
9. **Line 13**: Array `arr` of length **3** is created and initialized.
10. **Line 14**: `arr[5]` — **index out of bounds** (valid indices: 0, 1, 2). The JVM throws `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` at this exact instruction.
11. **Line 14 (cont.)**: Control immediately jumps to the inner `catch` clauses. The first inner `catch` is for `ArithmeticException` — does **not** match. The second is for `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` — **MATCHES!**
12. **Line 19**: Inner `catch` executes. Prints `"INNER catch: Array fault → java.lang.ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException: Index 5 out of bounds for length 3"`.
13. **Line 20-21**: After the matched `catch` finishes, control jumps to the **first statement after the entire inner try-catch** (line 24 in source numbering, i.e., `System.out.println("OUTER try: Inner block finished normally.")`).
14. **Line 24**: Prints `"OUTER try: Inner block finished normally."` — **proves the outer try continues normally**.
15. **Line 26**: Outer `catch` is **never reached** because no exception escaped the inner block.
16. **Line 28**: `finally` always executes. Prints `"OUTER finally: Cleanup complete."`.
17. **Line 30**: Prints `"main(): Program ended gracefully."`.

#### Program Output (Verbatim)

```
OUTER try: Beginning outer block.
INNER try: Performing risky division...
INNER try: Result = 5
INNER catch: Array fault → java.lang.ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException: Index 5 out of bounds for length 3
OUTER try: Inner block finished normally.
OUTER finally: Cleanup complete.
main(): Program ended gracefully.
```

### 3.2 Case Study 2 — Inner Exception NOT Caught → Propagates to Outer

**Problem Statement:** The inner block throws an exception for which **no inner `catch` matches**, forcing the JVM to propagate it to the outer `try`.

```java
class NestedTryDemo2 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            System.out.println("OUTER try: Start");
            try {
                int[] arr = new int[-5];   // Line C — NegativeArraySizeException
                System.out.println("INNER try: This line never executes");
            }
            catch (ArithmeticException ae) {
                // Does NOT match NegativeArraySizeException
                System.out.println("INNER catch (arithmetic): " + ae);
            }
            // No catch for NegativeArraySizeException here
            System.out.println("OUTER try: After inner block (unreachable)");
        }
        catch (NegativeArraySizeException nase) {
            System.out.println("OUTER catch: Negative array → " + nase);
        }
        catch (Exception e) {
            System.out.println("OUTER catch: Generic → " + e);
        }
    }
}
```

#### Exhaustive Analysis

1. **Outer `try`**: Entered normally.
2. **Inner `try`**: Entered.
3. **Line C**: `new int[-5]` — array size **cannot be negative**. JVM throws `NegativeArraySizeException`.
4. **Inner `catch` clauses**: Only `ArithmeticException` is present. **No match.**
5. **Stack Unwinding Initiated**: The JVM exits the inner `try-catch` structure **immediately** and propagates the exception outward to the enclosing `try`.
6. **Inner block is abandoned**: The line `System.out.println("INNER try: This line never executes")` is **skipped**.
7. **Outer `try`'s `catch` clauses searched**: The first outer `catch` is for `NegativeArraySizeException` — **MATCHES!**
8. **Outer catch executes**: Prints the error message.
9. **`System.out.println("OUTER try: After inner block (unreachable)")`** is **never reached** because the exception escaped the inner block, terminating the outer's normal flow.

#### Program Output (Verbatim)

```
OUTER try: Start
OUTER catch: Negative array → java.lang.NegativeArraySizeException
```

### 3.3 Case Study 3 — `try` Inside `catch` (Rethrow Pattern)

```java
class NestedTryDemo3 {
    static void demoproc() {
        try {
            throw new ArithmeticException("original");   // Thrown here
        }
        catch (ArithmeticException e) {
            System.out.println("Caught inside demoproc(): " + e);
            try {
                throw new NullPointerException("re-thrown chain");  // Rethrow different exception
            }
            catch (NullPointerException npe) {
                System.out.println("Inner catch: " + npe);
            }
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        demoproc();
        System.out.println("main(): After demoproc()");
    }
}
```

#### Analytical Walk-Through

1. `demoproc()` enters outer `try`.
2. `throw new ArithmeticException("original")` — exception thrown.
3. Caught by `catch (ArithmeticException e)` — handled.
4. **Inner `try` begins inside the `catch` block** — this is **legal nested configuration #2**.
5. `throw new NullPointerException(...)` — second exception thrown inside the inner try.
6. Caught by inner `catch (NullPointerException npe)`.
7. `demoproc()` returns normally.
8. `main()` resumes and prints the final message.

#### Program Output

```
Caught inside demoproc(): java.lang.ArithmeticException: original
Inner catch: java.lang.NullPointerException: re-thrown chain
main(): After demoproc()
```

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Summary of All Cases

| **Case** | **Exception Origin** | **Inner Catch Matches?** | **Outer Catch Matches?** | **Result** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Inner block | ✅ Yes | — | Inner handler runs; outer continues |
| 2 | Inner block | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | Outer handler runs; inner block abandoned |
| 3 | Inner block | ❌ No | ❌ No | JVM throws to caller; program may crash |
| 4 | Outer block | — | ✅ Yes | Outer handler runs |
| 5 | Outer block | — | ❌ No | JVM throws to caller; `finally` still runs |
| 6 | `finally` block | — | — | Original exception is **lost** (see pitfall) |
| 7 | Method call from `try` | Inner catch in called method | Outer catch in caller | Method-level nesting |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Control-Flow Diagram — Nested try (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Program Start] --> B[Enter OUTER try]
    B --> C{Execute Outer Statements}
    C -->|No Exception| D[Enter INNER try]
    C -->|Exception in Outer| E[Search OUTER catch]
    D --> F{Execute Inner Statements}
    F -->|No Exception| G[Exit INNER try normally]
    F -->|Exception Thrown| H{Inner catch matches?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Inner catch handler executes]
    H -->|No| J[Propagate to OUTER]
    I --> K[Continue after inner block]
    K --> L[Continue outer try]
    J --> E
    E -->|Match Found| M[Outer catch handler executes]
    E -->|No Match| N[Propagate to caller]
    G --> L
    L --> O[Exit OUTER try]
    M --> P[OUTER finally runs]
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Program continues / Ends]
    N --> P
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Layered Exception Handling

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OUTER_REGION["OUTER try-catch-finally REGION"]
        direction TB
        OT["OUTER try Block"]
        OC1["OUTER catch: ExceptionType A"]
        OC2["OUTER catch: ExceptionType B"]
        OF["OUTER finally (always runs)"]
    end

    subgraph INNER_REGION["INNER try-catch-finally REGION"]
        direction TB
        IT["INNER try Block"]
        IC1["INNER catch: ExceptionType 1"]
        IC2["INNER catch: ExceptionType 2"]
        IF["INNER finally (always runs)"]
    end

    CALLER["Calling Method / JVM Default Handler"]

    OT --> IT
    IT -->|Exception| IC1
    IT -->|Exception| IC2
    IT -->|Uncaught| OC1
    IT -->|Uncaught| OC2
    IT -->|Uncaught| CALLER
    OC1 --> OF
    OC2 --> OF
    IT --> OF

    style OUTER_REGION fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px
    style INNER_REGION fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px
    style CALLER fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Method-Call Nesting Schematic (Logical Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M["main() — OUTER try"] --> M1["caller() method"]
    M1 --> M2["callee() method with INNER try"]
    M2 -->|Exception thrown| M3["Inner catch in callee()"]
    M3 -->|Rethrow if needed| M1
    M1 -->|Exception in caller scope| OC["Outer catch in main()"]
    OC --> OF["Outer finally in main()"]

    style M fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003399
    style M1 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
    style M2 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404
    style M3 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24
    style OC fill:#f5c6cb,stroke:#721c24
    style OF fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460
```

### 4.4 Decision Tree — Which Handler Catches?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{"Where was the exception thrown?"}
    Q1 -->|Inside INNER try| Q2{"Is there a matching INNER catch?"}
    Q1 -->|Outside INNER try but inside OUTER try| Q3{"Is there a matching OUTER catch?"}
    Q1 -->|In a method called from within try| Q4{"Did the called method catch it?"}

    Q2 -->|Yes| R1["INNER handler catches"]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3

    Q3 -->|Yes| R2["OUTER handler catches"]
    Q3 -->|No| R3["Propagates to caller / JVM"]

    Q4 -->|Yes| R4["Method's catch handled it"]
    Q4 -->|No| Q3
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]**
>
> **Q1.** What is a *nested try* statement in Java? Mention **two** valid placements of a `try` block inside another `try-catch` construct. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer (Examiner's Key Style):**

A *nested try* statement is a `try-catch` construct placed within the body of another `try`, `catch`, or `finally` block. It enables localized exception handling at multiple levels of granularity.

**Two valid placements:**
1. A `try` block can be placed **directly inside** the `try` block of an enclosing `try-catch`.
2. A `try` block can be placed **inside a `catch`** block of an outer `try-catch` to perform recovery actions or rethrow.

*(Note: Other valid placements — inside `finally`, inside a method called from within a `try` — also earn full credit.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**
>
> **Q2.** Explain the **search order** of `catch` blocks in a nested `try` statement. What happens when the inner `catch` does **not** match the thrown exception? **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

When a nested `try` construct encounters an exception in the **inner** block, the JVM searches for a matching `catch` clause **starting from the innermost block** (Most-Recent Handler Rule). If a match is found in the inner block, the outer `try` continues execution as if nothing went wrong within its scope.

If **no inner `catch` matches**, the exception undergoes **stack unwinding** — the JVM exits the inner `try-catch` and propagates the exception to the **outer** `try` block's `catch` clauses, where the search is repeated.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Note (KTU ESE Rule):** *Answer ANY ONE full question from the choice provided. Each Part B carries 14 marks split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.*

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Predicted Pattern]**

**(a)** With a suitable Java program, explain how **nested try** statements are used to handle **different categories of exceptions at different levels**. Your program should contain at least one inner `try` that throws an exception **caught locally**, and another inner `try` whose exception **propagates to the outer** `catch`. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Discuss the role of the `finally` block in nested `try` constructs. When does a `finally` block **not** execute? Provide a Java code snippet to demonstrate. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part A (a)

```java
class NestedTryQ_A_a {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // OUTER try — handles broad I/O / generic errors
        try {
            System.out.println("--- OUTER try begins ---");

            // INNER try #1 — local recovery
            try {
                int x = 50, y = 0;
                int z = x / y;   // ArithmeticException
                System.out.println("Result = " + z);
            }
            catch (ArithmeticException ae) {
                System.out.println("INNER catch 1: Division error → " + ae);
            }

            // INNER try #2 — exception escapes to outer
            try {
                int[] numbers = new int[3];
                numbers[10] = 99;   // ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
                System.out.println("This line is never reached");
            }
            // NOTE: No catch for ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException inside
            // → exception will propagate to OUTER catch below

            System.out.println("Outer try: This line is also skipped");
        }
        catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException aiobe) {
            System.out.println("OUTER catch: Handled array error → " + aiobe);
        }
        catch (Exception e) {
            System.out.println("OUTER catch: Generic handler → " + e);
        }
        finally {
            System.out.println("OUTER finally: Always executed.");
        }
        System.out.println("main(): Program normal termination.");
    }
}
```

**Expected Output:**
```
--- OUTER try begins ---
INNER catch 1: Division error → java.lang.ArithmeticException: / by zero
OUTER catch: Handled array error → java.lang.ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException: Index 10 out of length 3
OUTER finally: Always executed.
main(): Program normal termination.
```

**Step-by-step justification (7 marks distribution):**
- Definition + syntax: **1 mark**
- Inner #1 with local catch: **2 marks**
- Inner #2 with propagation: **2 marks**
- Output reasoning: **1 mark**
- Conclusion about layered handling: **1 mark**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part A (b)

**Explanation:**

The `finally` block in a nested `try` construct is used to write code that **must execute regardless of whether an exception occurred or was caught**. Common uses include closing files, releasing database connections, or releasing locks.

**Execution Rules of `finally`:**
1. Executes after the `try` (or `catch`) block finishes — even if no exception occurred.
2. Executes even if the exception was **caught by an inner `catch`**.
3. Executes even if the exception **propagates outward** unhandled.
4. Executes even on a `return` statement inside `try`/`catch`.
5. **Does NOT execute only when**:
   - `System.exit()` is called.
   - The JVM crashes (e.g., `Runtime.getRuntime().halt()`).
   - The thread executing the `try` is killed (deprecated `Thread.stop()`).
   - An infinite loop or power failure occurs.

**Demonstration Code:**

```java
class FinallyDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            System.out.println("Inside try");
            System.exit(0);           // Forces JVM termination
        }
        catch (Exception e) {
            System.out.println("Inside catch");
        }
        finally {
            System.out.println("Inside finally");   // NEVER prints
        }
    }
}
```

**Expected Output:**
```
Inside try
```
*(Note: The `finally` block was bypassed because `System.exit(0)` was invoked.)*

**7 marks distribution:**
- Role of `finally` (2)
- Execution rules (2)
- Code demonstration (2)
- Cases where it does NOT execute (1)

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain with a Java program the concept of placing a `try` block **inside a `catch` block**. How does this pattern help in **recovering from one exception and handling another**? **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Differentiate between **method-call nesting** and **syntactic nesting** of `try` blocks. Provide illustrative code for both. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part B (a)

**Concept:** A `try` block can legally be placed inside the body of a `catch` block. This is useful when you want to perform a **recovery operation** that itself might throw an exception.

```java
class TryInsideCatch {
    static void recover() {
        try {
            int[] data = {1, 2, 3};
            System.out.println(data[5]);   // Throws ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
        }
        catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e) {
            System.out.println("Outer catch: array error caught. Initiating recovery...");

            // try block INSIDE the catch block
            try {
                String s = null;
                System.out.println(s.length());  // Throws NullPointerException
            }
            catch (NullPointerException npe) {
                System.out.println("Inner catch (inside outer catch): recovery itself failed → " + npe);
            }
            finally {
                System.out.println("Inner finally: cleanup after recovery attempt.");
            }
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        recover();
        System.out.println("main(): Completed");
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Outer catch: array error caught. Initiating recovery...
Inner catch (inside outer catch): recovery itself failed → java.lang.NullPointerException
Inner finally: cleanup after recovery attempt.
main(): Completed
```

**7 marks distribution:**
- Legal placement explanation (1)
- Code structure (3)
- Recovery logic reasoning (2)
- Output justification (1)

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part B (a) Continued — Syntactic vs Method-Call Nesting

**Syntactic Nesting:** A `try` block is *physically enclosed* within another `try`/`catch`/`finally` in the **same method**.

```java
void syntacticNesting() {
    try {                          // outer
        try {                      // inner — physically inside
            int x = 10 / 0;
        }
        catch (ArithmeticException e) {
            System.out.println("Inner catch: " + e);
        }
    }
    catch (Exception e) {
        System.out.println("Outer catch: " + e);
    }
}
```

**Method-Call Nesting:** The `try` block is in a **different method** that is *called* from within an outer `try`.

```java
class MethodCallNesting {
    static void innerMethod() {
        try {
            int[] a = new int[-2];   // NegativeArraySizeException
        }
        catch (NegativeArraySizeException e) {
            System.out.println("Caught in innerMethod(): " + e);
            throw e;                 // re-throw to caller
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            innerMethod();           // call from outer try
        }
        catch (NegativeArraySizeException e) {
            System.out.println("Caught in main()'s outer catch: " + e);
        }
    }
}
```

**Difference Table (to be drawn in the answer sheet):**

| **Aspect** | **Syntactic Nesting** | **Method-Call Nesting** |
|---|---|---|
| Location | Same method body | Different method |
| Visibility | Inline reading | Requires call-stack knowledge |
| Maintenance | Easier to follow | More modular, but harder to trace |
| Reusability | Low | High (the inner method can be reused) |
| Debugging | Simple | Needs stack-trace analysis |

**7 marks distribution:**
- Syntactic nesting definition + code (2)
- Method-call nesting definition + code (2)
- Difference explanation (2)
- Comparison table (1)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Valuation**
>
> 1. **Forgetting that inner `catch` is searched first** — many students wrongly assume the outer `catch` runs. Examiner's check: your explanation **must clearly state** the *innermost-first* rule.
> 2. **Confusing the inner block's `finally` with the outer block's `finally`** — both can exist, and the inner one runs **first**, then the outer. Marks lost if order is wrong.
> 3. **Using `throw e;` without a matching `throws` clause in the method signature** (for checked exceptions) — causes compile-time error. If the inner block throws a checked exception that the outer `catch` must handle, the method must declare `throws`.
> 4. **Placing an unreachable `catch` after a more general one** — e.g., `catch (Exception e)` followed by `catch (IOException ioe)`. **Compilation fails.** KTU examiners are strict about this.
> 5. **Assuming `finally` always runs** — it does NOT if `System.exit(0)` is called. State this explicitly if asked.
> 6. **Using checked exceptions incorrectly** — KTU questions often test whether you know that `ArithmeticException`, `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`, and `NullPointerException` are **unchecked** (subclasses of `RuntimeException`) and need NOT be declared in a `throws` clause.
> 7. **Confusing "throw" and "throws"** — `throw` is a statement inside a method; `throws` is a clause in a method signature.
> 8. **Forgetting the `finally` block's `finally` of inner try still runs** even when the exception escapes outward — losing this point costs 1 mark easily.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**
>
> ✅ **Definition**: A `try` block placed inside another `try`, `catch`, `finally`, or method body.
>
> ✅ **Innermost-First Rule**: Inner `catch` blocks are searched **before** outer `catch` blocks.
>
> ✅ **Stack Unwinding**: If inner `catch` does not match, the exception propagates outward to the enclosing `try`.
>
> ✅ **Five Legal Nesting Positions**: `try`-in-`try`, `try`-in-`catch`, `try`-in-`finally`, `try`-in-method-called-from-`try`, `try`-in-constructor-`try`.
>
> ✅ **`finally` Execution**: Runs **always** except when `System.exit()` is invoked or the JVM crashes.
>
> ✅ **Re-throw Pattern**: A `catch` block can `throw e;` to forward the exception to the outer handler.
>
> ✅ **Unchecked Exceptions in Common Examples**: `ArithmeticException`, `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`, `NullPointerException`, `NegativeArraySizeException` — all subclass `RuntimeException`, so **no `throws` declaration required**.
>
> ✅ **Method-Call vs Syntactic Nesting**: Two distinct forms; both follow the innermost-first search rule.
>
> ✅ **`try`-with-resources** (Java 7+) can be nested — same propagation rules apply.
>
> ✅ **Common Pitfall**: An exception thrown inside a `finally` block **shadows** any pending exception — be careful with cleanup code that itself can fail.
>
> ✅ **Module 3 Context**: This topic is foundational for *Custom Exception Handling*, *Exception Chaining*, and *Logging Frameworks* (later modules of PECIT633).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: throw
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT633 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** throw

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the Java Object-Oriented Programming paradigm, **`throw`** is a **unary keyword** that explicitly triggers (invokes) an exception at runtime by transferring a **Throwable** instance from the point of occurrence to the nearest enclosing exception handler. It is a fundamental component of Java's **decentralized exception handling model** under the `java.lang` package hierarchy.

According to the **JLS (Java Language Specification, §14.18)**, a `throw` statement evaluates an expression yielding a reference to a `Throwable` object, and the **Java Virtual Machine (JVM)** abruptly aborts the current method's execution frame, performing an **exception table lookup** to locate a matching `catch` clause.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board-Exam Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> *"The `throw` keyword is used to explicitly raise (generate) an exception in a Java program. It transfers control from the current execution point to the nearest matching `catch` block, signalling the JVM that an abnormal condition has occurred."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **factory assembly line** where a worker (your code) is processing items. Suddenly, a **defective product** arrives. The worker does not panic, break, or silently pass the product. Instead, the worker **lifts a red flag (throw)** to alert the floor supervisor (exception handler), and **immediately stops the current task**. The supervisor then decides what to do (handle the error) — either fix it, re-route it, or shut down the line.

- **Worker** → Method body
- **Defective product** → Error condition
- **Red flag (`throw`)** → The throw statement
- **Supervisor (`catch`)** → Exception handler
- **Stopping the current task** → Abrupt completion of method frame

> [!NOTE]
> **`throw` is an *action* (verb)** — it *causes* an exception.
> **`throws` is a *declaration* (adverb)** — it *announces* that an exception may occur.
> Mixing these two is one of the **most common KTU board-exam mistakes** (see Section 5 warnings).

### 1.3 Syntactic Specification

The general syntax of the `throw` statement is:

```java
throw <ThrowableInstance>;
```

Where `<ThrowableInstance>` must be a **non-null reference** to an object of class `Throwable` or any of its subclasses (`Exception`, `RuntimeException`, `Error`, user-defined exceptions, etc.).

> [!WARNING]
> Attempting to `throw null` results in a **`NullPointerException`** being thrown implicitly by the JVM at the throw site.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Mechanics of `throw` — Step-by-Step Logic

The execution of a `throw` statement is governed by a precise **5-stage pipeline**:

1. **Expression Evaluation Phase:**
   The expression operand of `throw` is evaluated. This typically creates a new exception object via the `new` operator (e.g., `new ArithmeticException("Div by zero")`).

2. **Type-Compatibility Check (Compile-Time):**
   The compiler verifies (statically) that the operand's **static type** is assignable to `Throwable`. If not, a **compile-time error** is issued.

3. **Null-Check & Throw (Runtime):**
   At runtime, the JVM checks whether the reference is `null`. If null → `NullPointerException` is auto-thrown. Otherwise, the actual object is thrown.

4. **Stack Unwinding (Abrupt Completion):**
   The current method's **execution frame** is marked as *abruptly completed*. The JVM begins **unwinding the call stack**, discarding all currently active stack frames until it finds an enclosing `try-catch` block whose `catch` parameter type matches the thrown exception (assignable from it).

5. **Handler Invocation or Propagation:**
   - If a **matching handler** is found → the `catch` block executes.
   - If **no handler** is found in the call stack → the exception is handled by the **default uncaught exception handler** (which prints the stack trace and terminates the thread).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Code below a `throw` statement inside the same block is UNREACHABLE** unless guarded by an `if-else`. The compiler issues the error: *"unreachable statement"*.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Reference Table)

| **Aspect** | **Specification** | **Engineering Significance** |
|---|---|---|
| Keyword Category | Unary operator / statement-level | Atomic control-transfer primitive |
| Operand Type | Any `Throwable` subclass (reference) | Compile-time safety guarantee |
| Exceptions Per Statement | **Exactly 1** | Cannot `throw` two exceptions in one statement |
| Post-Statement Code | **Unreachable** | Compiler-enforced constraint |
| Scope | **Inside method body or instance initializer** | Cannot appear in class scope (use `throws` instead) |
| Throwable Hierarchy Root | `java.lang.Throwable` | Parent of `Exception` and `Error` |
| Checked Exception Rule | Must be declared in `throws` clause if checked | Compile-time enforcement |
| Runtime Cost | Stack-trace capture (lazy in modern JVMs) | Performance-sensitive loops should avoid `throw` |
| Null Operand Behaviour | Implicit `NullPointerException` | Defensive programming pitfall |
| `final` Block Interaction | `final` block always executes before re-throw | Resource cleanup guarantee |

### 2.3 The `Throwable` Class Hierarchy (Engineering Foundation)

$$
\text{java.lang.Object} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{java.lang.Throwable} \;\longrightarrow\;
\begin{cases}
\text{java.lang.Error} \;(\text{unchecked, fatal}) \\
\text{java.lang.Exception} \;\longrightarrow\;
\begin{cases}
\text{Checked Exceptions (e.g., IOException, SQLException)} \\
\text{java.lang.RuntimeException} \;(\text{unchecked, e.g., ArithmeticException, NullPointerException})
\end{cases}
\end{cases}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **`throw` can dispatch ANY subclass of `Throwable`** — but for application-level logic, we virtually always throw `Exception` subclasses (specifically `RuntimeException` for unchecked scenarios).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The `throw` mechanism is the backbone of **Defensive Programming** and **Design-by-Contract** methodologies:

- **API Validation:** Methods like `Objects.requireNonNull(x)` internally use `throw new NullPointerException(...)`.
- **Precondition Enforcement:** Custom validators throw `IllegalArgumentException` when input contracts are violated.
- **Banking Systems:** Insufficient-funds checks throw custom `InsufficientBalanceException`.
- **IoT / Embedded:** Sensor read-failures throw `IOException` to upper-level recovery routines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Canonical Code Trace — Division with Explicit `throw`

Below is the **fully annotated, step-by-step execution trace** of a `throw` statement in action.

```java
public class ThrowDemo {
    public static double divide(int a, int b) {
        if (b == 0) {
            throw new ArithmeticException("Denominator cannot be zero");
        }
        return (double) a / b;
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            double result = divide(10, 0);
            System.out.println("Result = " + result);
        } catch (ArithmeticException e) {
            System.out.println("Caught: " + e.getMessage());
        }
    }
}
```

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Execution Trace (Exhaustive)

| **Step** | **Action** | **Internal JVM State** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `main()` invokes `divide(10, 0)` | New stack frame pushed for `divide` |
| 2 | Parameter binding: `a=10`, `b=0` | Local variable table populated |
| 3 | `if (b == 0)` evaluates to `true` | Branch taken |
| 4 | `new ArithmeticException("...")` allocates object | Heap allocation of `Throwable` instance; constructor stores message |
| 5 | `throw` statement executes | **Stack trace captured** (lazy in modern JVMs via `fillInStackTrace`) |
| 6 | `divide` frame marked *abruptly completed* | Normal return path skipped |
| 7 | JVM searches enclosing `try` blocks | `main()` has a matching `try` |
| 8 | JVM compares thrown type `ArithmeticException` with `catch` parameter | **Match found** (`ArithmeticException` assignable to `ArithmeticException`) |
| 9 | `catch` block executes | Prints *"Caught: Denominator cannot be zero"* |
| 10 | After `catch`, `main` continues normally | Program terminates cleanly |

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — `throw` vs `throws` vs `Throwable`

> This is the **highest-weightage comparison** in KTU Module 3 board exams.

| **Feature** | **`throw`** | **`throws`** | **`Throwable`** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Type | Keyword (statement) | Keyword (clause) | Class (superclass) |
| Purpose | Explicitly raise exception | Declare possible exceptions | Root class of all throwables |
| Location | Inside method body | Method signature | Class declaration |
| Number of exceptions | **One** at a time | **Multiple** (comma-separated) | N/A (parent type) |
| Checked exception support | Yes (requires `throws` declaration if checked) | Yes | Yes (any subclass) |
| After this keyword | Code becomes **unreachable** | Method body follows normally | Inherits `getMessage()`, `printStackTrace()` |
| KTU Board Question Type | *"Differentiate between..."* (5 marks) | Same | Same |

### 3.4 Mathematical / Logical Proof of Unreachability

Consider the following snippet:

```java
throw new RuntimeException("Error");
// System.out.println("Unreachable");   // ← Compile error here
```

**Logical proof:**

$$
\text{Let } S = \text{throw statement at line } n
$$

$$
\forall\, T \in \{\text{statements at line } n+k \;\vert\; k \geq 1\} : T \text{ is unreachable}
$$

**Proof by contradiction:** Assume a statement $T$ at line $n+1$ is reachable. Then control must transfer from $S$ to $T$. But $S$ causes *abrupt completion* of the method frame, which guarantees that $T$ cannot be the immediate successor. Hence contradiction. $\blacksquare$

### 3.5 Re-throwing Pattern (Advanced — Frequently Asked in 14-mark Questions)

```java
public void processFile(String path) throws IOException {
    try {
        FileReader fr = new FileReader(path);
        // ... operations ...
    } catch (IOException e) {
        System.out.println("Logging: " + e.getMessage());
        throw e;   // ← Re-throw preserves the original stack trace
    }
}
```

**Why re-throw?** It allows **partial handling** (e.g., logging, cleanup) at the current level while still propagating the exception to higher-level handlers. This pattern is critical in **layered architectures** (DAO → Service → Controller).

> [!NOTE]
> The re-thrown exception retains its **original cause chain** if `e.initCause(cause)` was set, enabling forensic debugging in production systems.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Control-Flow Diagram of `throw` Execution (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Method Invocation"] --> B{"Condition Check if b == 0"}
    B -- "False: Normal path" --> C["Execute Business Logic"]
    C --> D["Return Result to Caller"]
    B -- "True: Error path" --> E["new ArithmeticException message"]
    E --> F["throw statement executes"]
    F --> G["Stack Unwinding Initiated"]
    G --> H{"Search enclosing try-catch"}
    H -- "Match Found" --> I["catch block executes"]
    I --> J["Continue after catch block"]
    H -- "No Match Found" --> K["Default Uncaught Handler"]
    K --> L["Print Stack Trace and Terminate"]
```

### 4.2 Throwable Class Hierarchy (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    OBJ["java.lang.Object"] --> THR["java.lang.Throwable"]
    THR --> ERR["java.lang.Error"]
    THR --> EXC["java.lang.Exception"]
    ERR --> OOM["OutOfMemoryError"]
    ERR --> SOF["StackOverflowError"]
    EXC --> CE["Checked Exceptions"]
    EXC --> RTE["java.lang.RuntimeException"]
    CE --> IOE["IOException"]
    CE --> SQLE["SQLException"]
    RTE --> AE["ArithmeticException"]
    RTE --> NPE["NullPointerException"]
    RTE --> IAE["IllegalArgumentException"]
    RTE --> AIOBE["ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException"]
    USER["UserDefinedException extends Exception"] -.->|"custom throw"| EXC
```

### 4.3 `throw` vs `throws` — Visual Differentiation Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph THROW["throw - Action Verb"]
        T1["Located INSIDE method body"]
        T2["Throws ONE exception instance"]
        T3["Object created via new keyword"]
        T4["Causes abrupt completion"]
    end
    subgraph THROWS["throws - Declaration Adverb"]
        S1["Located in METHOD SIGNATURE"]
        S2["Declares MULTIPLE potential exceptions"]
        S3["Does NOT create objects"]
        S4["Compiles a warning for checked exceptions"]
    end
    THROW --> DECISION["Both work together for checked exceptions"]
    THROWS --> DECISION
```

### 4.4 Exception Propagation Stack (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    MAIN["main method"] -->|"calls"| M1["methodA"]
    M1 -->|"calls"| M2["methodB"]
    M2 -->|"calls"| M3["methodC"]
    M3 -->|"throw new IOException"| PROP["Exception propagates UP the call stack"]
    PROP --> M2
    M2 --> M1
    M1 --> MAIN
    MAIN --> CATCH["catch IOException handler in main"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
*What is the purpose of the `throw` keyword in Java? Give the general syntax.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The `throw` keyword in Java is used to **explicitly raise (generate) an exception** during program execution. It transfers control from the current point to the nearest matching `catch` block, signalling abnormal termination of the current code path.

**Syntax:**

```java
throw <ThrowableInstance>;
```

**Example:**

```java
throw new ArithmeticException("Division by zero not allowed");
```

The operand must be an object of type `Throwable` or its subclass. Only **one exception** can be thrown per `throw` statement. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`**
*Can we throw multiple exceptions using a single `throw` statement? Justify.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**No**, we cannot throw multiple exceptions using a single `throw` statement in Java. The `throw` keyword accepts **only one `Throwable` instance** as its operand. To handle multiple potential exceptions, we must use either:
1. **Sequential `throw` statements** inside separate `if-else` branches, or
2. The **`throws` clause** in the method signature to declare multiple possible exception types.

Attempting to write `throw e1, e2;` is a **compile-time syntax error**. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Explain the `throw` keyword in Java with suitable examples. Discuss the rules to be followed while using `throw`. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between `throw` and `throws` in Java with a comprehensive example demonstrating both. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) The `throw` keyword (7 marks):**

The `throw` keyword in Java is used to **explicitly invoke an exception** at runtime. It is typically used when a method detects an error condition that it cannot handle locally, and needs to delegate the handling to a calling method.

**Key Rules:**

1. The operand of `throw` must be an object of type `Throwable` or its subclass — otherwise a compile-time error occurs.
2. Only **one exception** can be thrown per `throw` statement.
3. The `throw` statement **abruptly terminates** the current method; no code after it (in the same block) will execute.
4. If a **checked exception** is thrown, the method must declare it in its `throws` clause.
5. The thrown object is usually instantiated using the `new` operator.

**Example:**

```java
public void setAge(int age) {
    if (age < 0 || age > 150) {
        throw new IllegalArgumentException("Invalid age: " + age);
    }
    this.age = age;
}
```

In the above code, when an invalid age is provided, an `IllegalArgumentException` is thrown. The `IllegalArgumentException` is a **RuntimeException** (unchecked), so no `throws` declaration is required. **(7 marks — full breakdown: definition 2, rules 3, example 2)**

---

**(b) `throw` vs `throws` (7 marks):**

| **S.No** | **`throw`** | **`throws`** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Used to **explicitly throw** an exception | Used to **declare** exceptions a method may throw |
| 2 | Used **inside** the method body | Used in the **method signature** |
| 3 | Can throw only **one** exception at a time | Can declare **multiple** exceptions (comma-separated) |
| 4 | The operand must be a `Throwable` instance | Lists exception **class names** only |
| 5 | Causes **abrupt completion** of the method | Does not affect normal flow |
| 6 | Compiles a *runtime* check | Compiles a *compile-time* enforcement for checked exceptions |

**Comprehensive Example Demonstrating Both:**

```java
import java.io.IOException;

public class ThrowVsThrowsDemo {

    // 'throws' declares that this method may throw IOException
    public static void readFile(String path) throws IOException {
        if (path == null) {
            // 'throw' explicitly raises a NullPointerException
            throw new NullPointerException("Path cannot be null");
        }
        if (!path.endsWith(".txt")) {
            // 'throw' raises a checked IOException
            throw new IOException("Only .txt files supported");
        }
        System.out.println("Reading: " + path);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            readFile("data.pdf");  // Will throw IOException
        } catch (IOException e) {
            System.out.println("Caught: " + e.getMessage());
        }
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Caught: Only .txt files supported
```

This example uses **`throws`** in the `readFile` method signature to declare that a checked `IOException` may be thrown, and uses **`throw`** twice inside the method body to actually raise specific exceptions. **(7 marks — full breakdown: comparison table 4, example 3)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** What is exception handling in Java? Explain the role of the `throw` keyword with an example of a user-defined exception class. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **re-throwing an exception** in Java. Write a Java program to demonstrate re-throwing with proper exception chaining. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Exception handling & `throw` with user-defined exception (7 marks):**

**Exception Handling:** It is a mechanism in Java to handle runtime errors (exceptions) gracefully, preventing abrupt termination of the program. Java uses 5 keywords: `try`, `catch`, `finally`, `throw`, and `throws`.

**User-Defined Exception Example with `throw`:**

```java
// Custom checked exception
class InsufficientBalanceException extends Exception {
    public InsufficientBalanceException(String message) {
        super(message);
    }
}

class BankAccount {
    private double balance = 1000.0;

    public void withdraw(double amount) throws InsufficientBalanceException {
        if (amount > balance) {
            throw new InsufficientBalanceException(
                "Insufficient balance. Available: " + balance
            );
        }
        balance -= amount;
        System.out.println("Withdrawal successful. New balance: " + balance);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        BankAccount acc = new BankAccount();
        try {
            acc.withdraw(5000.0);
        } catch (InsufficientBalanceException e) {
            System.out.println("Transaction failed: " + e.getMessage());
        }
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Transaction failed: Insufficient balance. Available: 1000.0
```

The `throw` statement inside `withdraw()` explicitly creates and dispatches the user-defined `InsufficientBalanceException`, which is caught by the `catch` block in `main()`. **(7 marks — exception handling concept 2, class definition 2, throw usage 3)**

---

**(b) Re-throwing exceptions with chaining (7 marks):**

**Re-throwing** is the technique of catching an exception, performing some local action (like logging or cleanup), and then **re-dispatching the same exception** to a higher-level handler using the `throw` statement.

**Program Demonstrating Re-throw:**

```java
public class RethrowDemo {

    public static void level1() throws Exception {
        try {
            int data = 10 / 0;  // Triggers ArithmeticException
        } catch (ArithmeticException e) {
            System.out.println("[Level 1] Logging error: " + e.getMessage());
            throw e;  // Re-throw to caller
        }
    }

    public static void level2() throws Exception {
        try {
            level1();
        } catch (ArithmeticException e) {
            System.out.println("[Level 2] Wrapping as Exception");
            Exception wrapped = new Exception("Wrapped from Level 2", e);
            throw wrapped;  // Re-throw with cause chain
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            level2();
        } catch (Exception e) {
            System.out.println("[Main] Caught: " + e.getMessage());
            System.out.println("[Main] Original cause: " + e.getCause());
        }
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
[Level 1] Logging error: / by zero
[Level 2] Wrapping as Exception
[Main] Caught: Wrapped from Level 2
[Main] Original cause: java.lang.ArithmeticException: / by zero
```

**Explanation:** `level1()` catches the `ArithmeticException`, logs it, and re-throws it. `level2()` catches it, wraps it in a new `Exception` with the original as the *cause* (preserving the chain via `Throwable(String, Throwable)` constructor), and re-throws. The `main()` method accesses the original cause via `e.getCause()`. This pattern is essential for **layered exception translation** in enterprise applications. **(7 marks — concept 2, code 3, explanation 2)**

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing `throw` with `throws`** — Costs **2 marks** instantly. Memorize: `throw` (verb) is action; `throws` (adverb) is declaration.
> 2. **Forgetting `throws` clause for checked exceptions** — Code won't compile; **0 marks** for the program.
> 3. **Writing code after `throw` in the same block** — Produces *"unreachable statement"* compile error; examiner deducts **1 mark**.
> 4. **Throwing `null`** — Throws implicit `NullPointerException`; not what students usually intend. Costs **1 mark** in trace questions.
> 5. **Missing the `new` keyword** — `throw ArithmeticException("msg")` is invalid; must be `throw new ArithmeticException("msg")`. Costs **1 mark**.
> 6. **Confusing `Error` vs `Exception`** — `Error` subclasses (e.g., `OutOfMemoryError`) should *not* be thrown by application code. Costs **1 mark** in design questions.
> 7. **Not preserving cause chain in re-throw** — Use `new Exception("msg", cause)` constructor; throwing a fresh exception without `cause` loses forensic data. Costs **2 marks** in 14-mark questions.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- 🔹 `throw` is a **statement**, not an expression; it cannot be used as the RHS of an assignment.
- 🔹 Operand must be a **non-null `Throwable` reference**; otherwise implicit `NullPointerException`.
- 🔹 **One exception per `throw` statement** — no comma-separated syntax.
- 🔹 Code **after `throw` is unreachable**; compiler-enforced.
- 🔹 For **checked exceptions**, the method must include them in its **`throws` clause**.
- 🔹 `throw` can throw **user-defined exceptions** that extend `Exception` or `RuntimeException`.
- 🔹 The thrown object's **stack trace is captured lazily** in modern JVMs (since Java 7+) for performance.
- 🔹 **Re-throwing** preserves the original exception object — useful for partial handling and cleanup.
