- 🔹 **Exception chaining** via `Throwable(cause)` or `initCause(cause)` maintains the original cause for debugging.
- 🔹 `throw` is **method-scoped**; it cannot appear at class level (use `throws` there).
- 🔹 The `final` block executes **even if `throw` is executed** — guaranteeing resource cleanup.
- 🔹 **Difference table (`throw` vs `throws`)** is a **5-mark favourite** — memorize verbatim for KTU.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: throws and finally
Java Built-in Exceptions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT633 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Java Built-in Exceptions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Java Built-in Exceptions** are pre-defined exception classes provided by the Java Standard Library (primarily under the `java.lang` package) that automatically represent common runtime anomalies, programming errors, and exceptional conditions. They are subclasses of `java.lang.Throwable` and form a well-organized class hierarchy allowing developers to handle predictable and unpredictable failures using the `try-catch-finally` mechanism.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PECIT633 – OOP, Module 3), built-in exceptions are categorized into three families:

1. **Checked Exceptions** — detected at compile-time (subclasses of `Exception`, excluding `RuntimeException`)
2. **Unchecked Exceptions** — detected at run-time (subclasses of `RuntimeException`)
3. **Errors** — serious, unrecoverable conditions (subclasses of `Error`)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Hospital Emergency Ward**
>
> Imagine a hospital. Patients arrive with different categories of illness:
> - **Minor ailments** (cold, headache) → outpatient treatment → *Unchecked Exceptions* (RuntimeException)
> - **Scheduled surgeries** (planned in advance, paperwork required) → *Checked Exceptions* (compile-time verified)
> - **Catastrophic failures** (building collapse, fire) → hospital cannot recover → *Errors* (e.g., OutOfMemoryError)
>
> Java's built-in exceptions are exactly like the hospital's **pre-defined triage protocols** — they are ready-made responses to known crisis patterns. You don't invent the response; you simply choose which protocol (`catch` block) to apply.

## 1.3 The Three Categories at a Glance

| Category | Parent Class | Compile-time Checked? | Recoverable? |
|----------|-------------|----------------------|--------------|
| Checked Exceptions | `java.lang.Exception` | ✅ Yes | Generally Yes |
| Unchecked Exceptions | `java.lang.RuntimeException` | ❌ No | Often Yes |
| Errors | `java.lang.Error` | ❌ No | ❌ No |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** All built-in exceptions reside in `java.lang`, so they are **automatically imported** — no `import` statement is required to use them in any Java program.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The `Throwable` Class Hierarchy — Root of All Exceptions

The `java.lang.Throwable` class is the **superclass of every exception and error** in Java. Any object that can be thrown and caught using `throw`/`catch` must be a `Throwable` (or one of its subclasses).

### 2.1.1 Major Branches Under `Throwable`

- **`java.lang.Exception`** — represents conditions that a reasonable application *should* anticipate and recover from.
- **`java.lang.Error`** — represents serious problems that an application *should not* try to handle (e.g., JVM crash, stack overflow).

## 2.2 Checked vs Unchecked — The Compile-Time vs Run-Time Distinction

### Why Two Types?

The Java compiler enforces checked exceptions to enforce the **"handle-or-declare" rule**: code that may throw a checked exception must either:
- Wrap it in a `try-catch` block, **OR**
- Declare it using the `throws` clause in the method signature.

This prevents catastrophic runtime surprises like unhandled file-not-found errors in production.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why are RuntimeExceptions unchecked?**
> They typically indicate **programming bugs** (logic errors) rather than external conditions. The compiler trusts the developer to write bug-free code; if a `NullPointerException` occurs, that's a bug to fix, not a runtime contingency to handle.

## 2.3 Commonly Tested Built-in Exceptions (KTU 2024 Priority List)

| Exception | Type | When Thrown |
|-----------|------|-------------|
| `ArithmeticException` | Unchecked | Integer division by zero |
| `NullPointerException` | Unchecked | Accessing method/field on a null reference |
| `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` | Unchecked | Array index ≥ length or < 0 |
| `StringIndexOutOfBoundsException` | Unchecked | String index out of range |
| `IndexOutOfBoundsException` | Unchecked | Parent of array/string index errors |
| `NumberFormatException` | Unchecked | Invalid string-to-number conversion |
| `ClassCastException` | Unchecked | Invalid type casting |
| `IllegalArgumentException` | Unchecked | Method given an invalid argument |
| `IOException` | Checked | General I/O failure |
| `FileNotFoundException` | Checked | Attempted file access when file missing |
| `EOFException` | Checked | Unexpected end-of-file during input |
| `InterruptedException` | Checked | Thread interrupted while waiting/sleeping |
| `ClassNotFoundException` | Checked | Class not found at runtime via reflection |
| `CloneNotSupportedException` | Checked | Object does not implement Cloneable |
| `SQLException` | Checked | Database access error |
| `StackOverflowError` | Error | Recursive method calls too deep |
| `OutOfMemoryError` | Error | JVM runs out of heap memory |
| `NoClassDefFoundError` | Error | Class definition missing at runtime |

## 2.4 Useful Methods of `Throwable` (and its subclasses)

Every built-in exception object exposes the following inherited methods:

- `String getMessage()` — returns the detail message string.
- `String toString()` — returns a short description (class name + message).
- `void printStackTrace()` — prints the stack trace to `System.err`.
- `Throwable getCause()` — returns the underlying cause (if chained).
- `StackTraceElement[] getStackTrace()` — programmatic stack-trace access.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Master Rule of Throwable Hierarchy**

$$
\boxed{\text{Throwable} \rightarrow
\begin{cases}
\text{Exception} \rightarrow
\begin{cases}
\text{IOException, SQLException, ClassNotFoundException} \quad (\text{Checked}) \\
\text{RuntimeException} \rightarrow
\begin{cases}
\text{ArithmeticException} \\
\text{NullPointerException} \\
\text{ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException} \\
\text{NumberFormatException} \\
\text{ClassCastException} \\
\text{IllegalArgumentException}
\end{cases} \quad (\text{Unchecked})
\end{cases} \\
\text{Error} \rightarrow \text{OutOfMemoryError, StackOverflowError, NoClassDefFoundError}
\end{cases}}
$$

### Quick-Reference Decision Table

$$
\text{Is the exception a subclass of RuntimeException?}
\begin{cases}
\text{Yes} \Rightarrow \textbf{Unchecked} \Rightarrow \text{No compile-time obligation} \\
\text{No (but subclass of Exception)} \Rightarrow \textbf{Checked} \Rightarrow \text{Handle or declare} \\
\text{Subclass of Error} \Rightarrow \textbf{Unrecoverable} \Rightarrow \text{Don't catch}
\end{cases}
$$

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

In **enterprise software engineering**, built-in exceptions power:
- **Banking systems** — `ArithmeticException` prevents division-by-zero in interest calculations.
- **Database apps** — `SQLException` triggers transaction rollback on integrity violations.
- **Network services** — `IOException` handles dropped socket connections.
- **Mobile/Android apps** — `OutOfMemoryError` triggers bitmap recycling to prevent crashes.

Understanding which exception is thrown by which operation is essential for writing **robust, fault-tolerant systems** — a direct Course Outcome (CO) of PECIT633.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Trace: How the Compiler Decides if an Exception is "Checked"

The compiler performs a deterministic traversal on the exception class hierarchy at compile time. Below is the step-by-step logical derivation used by `javac` (the Java compiler):

### Step 1 — Classify the exception object

$$
\text{Let } E \text{ be the thrown object. Identify its class } C.
$$

### Step 2 — Walk the inheritance chain

The compiler builds the chain by repeatedly invoking `getSuperclass()`:

$$
C_0 = C, \quad C_{n+1} = \text{getSuperclass}(C_n) \quad \text{until } C_n = \texttt{Object}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the decision predicate

$$
\text{Checked}(C) =
\begin{cases}
\text{true}, & \text{if } \exists \, i \text{ such that } C_i = \texttt{Exception} \text{ and } C_j \neq \texttt{RuntimeException} \, \forall j \leq i \\
\text{false}, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

### Step 4 — Enforce the handle-or-declare rule

$$
\text{If Checked}(C) = \text{true}:
\begin{cases}
\text{Method must contain } \texttt{try-catch}(C) \text{ block} \\
\text{OR method signature contains } \texttt{throws } C
\end{cases}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example — Classification of `IOException`:**
>
> 1. Class `C` = `IOException`
> 2. Chain: `IOException` → `Exception` → `Throwable` → `Object`
> 3. Check: `Exception` is present in chain, and `RuntimeException` is **not** in the chain → `Checked = true`
> 4. Therefore, the compiler **demands** a `try-catch` or `throws` declaration.
>
> **Worked Example — Classification of `ArithmeticException`:**
>
> 1. Class `C` = `ArithmeticException`
> 2. Chain: `ArithmeticException` → `RuntimeException` → `Exception` → `Throwable` → `Object`
> 3. Check: `RuntimeException` is present in chain → `Checked = false`
> 4. Therefore, the compiler **does not** demand any handling.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Exception Trigger Conditions

The following matrix maps each built-in exception to the exact triggering condition. This is a **high-frequency KTU question type**.

| Exception | Triggering Code Pattern | Triggering Condition |
|-----------|------------------------|----------------------|
| `ArithmeticException` | `int x = 10 / y;` where `y == 0` | Integer division by zero |
| `NullPointerException` | `String s = null; s.length();` | Method/field access on `null` |
| `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` | `arr[5]` where `arr.length == 3` | Index ≥ length OR < 0 |
| `NumberFormatException` | `Integer.parseInt("abc");` | Non-numeric input to parse methods |
| `ClassCastException` | `Object o = "hi"; Integer i = (Integer) o;` | Incompatible downcast |
| `StringIndexOutOfBoundsException` | `"Java".charAt(10);` | String index out of bounds |
| `NegativeArraySizeException` | `new int[-5];` | Array size < 0 |
| `IllegalArgumentException` | `Thread.sleep(-100);` | Invalid argument to method |
| `ArrayStoreException` | `Object[] o = new String[3]; o[0] = 10;` | Wrong-type element stored in array |
| `SecurityException` | `System.setSecurityManager(new SecurityManager());` | Security violation |

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Trace — Handling a Built-in Exception

Let us trace the execution flow for the following program (a classic KTU example):

```java
public class Demo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            int a = 10, b = 0;
            int c = a / b;     // Step 1: ArithmeticException thrown here
            System.out.println(c); // Step 2: Skipped
        } catch (ArithmeticException e) {
            System.out.println("Cannot divide by zero."); // Step 3: Caught
        } finally {
            System.out.println("Finally block always runs."); // Step 4: Always runs
        }
        System.out.println("Program continues."); // Step 5: Resumes
    }
}
```

### Execution Trace

$$
\begin{aligned}
& t_0: \quad \text{JVM enters try block, allocates } a=10,\ b=0 \\
& t_1: \quad \text{evaluates } a/b \Rightarrow 10/0 \Rightarrow \text{JVM throws ArithmeticException} \\
& t_2: \quad \text{JVM halts try block, looks for matching catch} \\
& t_3: \quad \text{Catch block matches } \Rightarrow \text{prints "Cannot divide by zero."} \\
& t_4: \quad \text{finally block executes unconditionally} \Rightarrow \text{prints "Finally block always runs."} \\
& t_5: \quad \text{Execution resumes after try-catch-finally} \Rightarrow \text{prints "Program continues."}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Output:**
> ```
> Cannot divide by zero.
> Finally block always runs.
> Program continues.
> ```
> The program does **not crash** — control is gracefully transferred to the catch block.

## 3.4 Analytical Derivation — The `throw` vs `throws` Distinction

These two keywords are **frequently confused** in KTU exams. Here is the rigorous differentiation:

| Aspect | `throw` | `throws` |
|--------|---------|----------|
| Purpose | Actually throws an exception object | Declares that a method may throw exceptions |
| Usage | Inside method body | In method signature |
| Syntax | `throw new ArithmeticException("...");` | `void m() throws IOException { ... }` |
| Number of exceptions | One exception object at a time | Multiple exceptions can be listed (comma-separated) |
| Effect | Transfers control immediately | Informs compiler / caller |

$$
\boxed{\texttt{throw} = \text{action} \quad ; \quad \texttt{throws} = \text{declaration}}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Complete Throwable Class Hierarchy (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1["java lang Object"] --> A2["java lang Throwable"]
    A2 --> A3["java lang Exception"]
    A2 --> A4["java lang Error"]

    A3 --> A5["java lang IOException"]
    A3 --> A6["java lang SQLException"]
    A3 --> A7["java lang ClassNotFoundException"]
    A3 --> A8["java lang RuntimeException"]

    A8 --> A9["java lang ArithmeticException"]
    A8 --> A10["java lang NullPointerException"]
    A8 --> A11["java lang ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException"]
    A8 --> A12["java lang NumberFormatException"]
    A8 --> A13["java lang ClassCastException"]
    A8 --> A14["java lang IllegalArgumentException"]
    A8 --> A15["java lang StringIndexOutOfBoundsException"]
    A8 --> A16["java lang IndexOutOfBoundsException"]

    A11 -.inherits.-> A16

    A4 --> A17["java lang OutOfMemoryError"]
    A4 --> A18["java lang StackOverflowError"]
    A4 --> A19["java lang NoClassDefFoundError"]
    A4 --> A20["java lang VirtualMachineError"]

    A17 -.inherits.-> A20
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - Solid arrows `→` indicate direct inheritance (`extends`).
> - Dotted arrows `.->` indicate transitive inheritance (multilevel).
> - All nodes in the right branch (Exception side) are split into **Checked** (yellow in typical render) and **Unchecked** (RuntimeException subtree).

## 4.2 Exception Handling Flow — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Program Execution"] --> P2["Try Block Entry"]
    P2 --> P3{"Exception Thrown?"}
    P3 -- "No" --> P4["Continue Normal Flow"]
    P3 -- "Yes" --> P5["JVM Creates Exception Object"]
    P5 --> P6{"Matching Catch Block?"}
    P6 -- "Yes" --> P7["Execute Catch Handler"]
    P6 -- "No" --> P8["Propagate Up Call Stack"]
    P8 --> P9{"Main Method Catches?"}
    P9 -- "Yes" --> P7
    P9 -- "No" --> P10["Default JVM Handler Prints Stack Trace"]
    P7 --> P11["Execute Finally Block"]
    P4 --> P11
    P10 --> P12["Program Terminates Abnormally"]
    P11 --> P13["Resume After Try-Catch-Finally"]
```

## 4.3 Checked vs Unchecked Decision Logic (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Exception Class C"] --> S2{"Is C a subclass of RuntimeException?"}
    S2 -- "Yes" --> S3["Unchecked Exception"]
    S3 --> S4["Compiler does NOT enforce handling"]
    S2 -- "No" --> S5{"Is C a subclass of Error?"}
    S5 -- "Yes" --> S6["Error Unrecoverable"]
    S6 --> S7["Do NOT catch in normal code"]
    S5 -- "No" --> S8["Checked Exception"]
    S8 --> S9["Compiler ENFORCES handle-or-declare"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three-Way Decision:** Every `Throwable` falls into exactly one of: **Checked Exception**, **Unchecked Exception**, or **Error**. This trichotomy is the single most-tested concept in KTU Module 3 viva and written exams.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between checked and unchecked exceptions in Java. Give one example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Checked Exception | Unchecked Exception |
|--------|------------------|---------------------|
| Detected at | Compile-time | Run-time |
| Parent class | `Exception` (not RuntimeException) | `RuntimeException` |
| Handling | Mandatory (handle or declare) | Optional |
| Example | `IOException`, `FileNotFoundException` | `ArithmeticException`, `NullPointerException` |

Checked exceptions are verified by the compiler and force the programmer to either wrap the risky code in `try-catch` or declare it with `throws`. Unchecked exceptions are typically caused by programming errors (logic bugs) and the compiler does not enforce handling — they reflect developer mistakes that should be fixed rather than caught.

*(1 mark: definition of each, 1 mark: examples, 1 mark: correct distinction)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Explain the difference between `throw` and `throws` keywords in Java with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **`throw`** is used to **explicitly throw** a single exception object from within a method body. Example: `throw new ArithmeticException("Divide by zero");`
- **`throws`** is used in a **method signature to declare** that the method may pass one or more exceptions to its caller. Example: `void readFile() throws IOException { ... }`
- `throw` causes an actual transfer of control; `throws` merely informs the compiler and caller.

*(1 mark each for definition and example, 1 mark for key distinction)*

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, every Part B question carries **14 marks** and offers an **internal choice** between two sub-questions. Each sub-question is typically worth 7 marks. Below are **Question A** and **Question B**.

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **hierarchy of built-in exceptions** in Java under `java.lang.Throwable`. List any **five unchecked exceptions** with their triggering conditions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the significance of the following built-in exceptions with suitable Java code snippets:
   (i) `ArithmeticException`
   (ii) `NullPointerException`
   (iii) `NumberFormatException` **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

The Java exception hierarchy begins at `java.lang.Throwable`, which has two principal subclasses: `Exception` and `Error`. The `Exception` branch is further divided into checked exceptions (e.g., `IOException`, `ClassNotFoundException`) and unchecked exceptions under `RuntimeException` (e.g., `ArithmeticException`, `NullPointerException`). The `Error` branch contains serious, unrecoverable problems such as `OutOfMemoryError` and `StackOverflowError`. *(2 marks — explanation)*

A clean hierarchical diagram (as shown in SECTION 4.1) is expected. *(2 marks — diagram)*

**Five unchecked exceptions and triggering conditions:** *(3 marks — ½ mark each, ½ mark per condition)*

| Unchecked Exception | Triggering Condition |
|---------------------|----------------------|
| `ArithmeticException` | Integer division by zero, e.g., `10 / 0` |
| `NullPointerException` | Calling a method on a `null` reference, e.g., `s.length()` where `s = null` |
| `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` | Accessing `arr[i]` where `i` is negative or `≥ arr.length` |
| `NumberFormatException` | Passing a non-numeric string to `Integer.parseInt()` |
| `StringIndexOutOfBoundsException` | Calling `charAt(i)` on a string where `i` is out of range |

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**(i) `ArithmeticException` — 2 marks**

Thrown when an exceptional arithmetic condition occurs, most commonly **integer division by zero**.

```java
public class ArithDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            int result = 10 / 0;
            System.out.println(result);
        } catch (ArithmeticException e) {
            System.out.println("Cannot divide by zero: " + e.getMessage());
        }
    }
}
```

**(ii) `NullPointerException` — 2 marks**

Thrown when an application attempts to use `null` in a case where an object is required (calling a method or accessing a field on a null reference).

```java
public class NullDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            String s = null;
            System.out.println(s.length());
        } catch (NullPointerException e) {
            System.out.println("Null reference detected: " + e);
        }
    }
}
```

**(iii) `NumberFormatException` — 2 marks**

Thrown to indicate that the application has attempted to convert a string to one of the numeric types, but the string does **not** have the appropriate format.

```java
public class NFDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            int n = Integer.parseInt("abc");
            System.out.println(n);
        } catch (NumberFormatException e) {
            System.out.println("Invalid number format: " + e.getMessage());
        }
    }
}
```

*(Remaining 1 mark awarded for overall presentation / correct use of `try-catch` block)*

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 1]**

**(a)** What are **Errors** in Java? Differentiate between **Exception** and **Error** with examples. List any **three built-in Errors**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program that demonstrates the handling of **`ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`** and **`ClassCastException`** using a single `try-catch` mechanism. Explain the output. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Errors** in Java are subclasses of `java.lang.Error` and represent **serious, unrecoverable** conditions that a reasonable application **should not attempt to catch or handle**. They are typically thrown by the JVM itself when something goes fundamentally wrong (e.g., the system has run out of resources). *(2 marks)*

**Difference between Exception and Error:** *(3 marks)*

| Aspect | Exception | Error |
|--------|-----------|-------|
| Recoverability | Usually recoverable | Unrecoverable |
| Parent class | `Throwable → Exception` | `Throwable → Error` |
| Handling | Recommended (try-catch) | Should NOT be handled |
| Cause | Application-level conditions | System / JVM-level failures |
| Example | `IOException`, `ArithmeticException` | `OutOfMemoryError`, `StackOverflowError` |

**Three built-in Errors:** *(2 marks)*
1. `OutOfMemoryError` — JVM heap memory exhausted.
2. `StackOverflowError` — Method call stack exceeded (typically due to unbounded recursion).
3. `NoClassDefFoundError` — A required class definition was missing at runtime.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

```java
public class MultiCatchDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            int[] arr = new int[3];
            arr[5] = 10;   // Triggers ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException

            Object o = "Hello";
            Integer i = (Integer) o;  // Triggers ClassCastException
        } catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e) {
            System.out.println("Array index out of range: " + e.getMessage());
        } catch (ClassCastException e) {
            System.out.println("Invalid cast: " + e.getMessage());
        } finally {
            System.out.println("Cleanup completed.");
        }
        System.out.println("Program ends normally.");
    }
}
```

**Explanation of Output:** *(3 marks)*

The first statement inside the `try` block (`arr[5] = 10;`) triggers an `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` because the array has only 3 elements (valid indices: 0, 1, 2). At this moment, control immediately transfers to the first matching catch block. The second statement (`Integer i = (Integer) o;`) is **never executed**. The `finally` block runs unconditionally, followed by the final `System.out.println`.

**Output:**
```
Array index out of range: Index 5 out of bounds for length 3
Cleanup completed.
Program ends normally.
```

*(1 mark for correct output, 1 mark for the explanation of control flow, 1 mark for proper `finally` usage)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing `throw` and `throws`** — These are two different keywords with two different purposes. Examiners specifically deduct 1–2 marks for this confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention the package** — Always state "`java.lang.Throwable`" (not just "Throwable").
> 3. **Missing `RuntimeException` in classification** — Many students list exceptions without explaining *why* they are checked or unchecked. Always explain the inheritance relationship.
> 4. **Incorrect diagram direction** — Arrow direction in hierarchy diagrams must flow from child → parent (subclass → superclass).
> 5. **Skipping the `finally` block in code snippets** — Examiners reward inclusion of `finally` for demonstrating complete understanding.
> 6. **Not including imports / `main` method signature** — Full code must be syntactically complete to earn full marks.
> 7. **Wrong output tracing** — When tracing programs with exceptions, students often forget that **only the first matching catch** executes and remaining statements in the `try` block are skipped.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Java Built-in Exceptions**

- ✅ All Java exceptions inherit from **`java.lang.Throwable`**.
- ✅ Two main branches: **`Exception`** (recoverable) and **`Error`** (unrecoverable).
- ✅ **Checked exceptions** = direct subclasses of `Exception` but **NOT** `RuntimeException`. Compiler enforces handling.
- ✅ **Unchecked exceptions** = subclasses of `RuntimeException`. Compiler does **not** enforce handling.
- ✅ **Errors** are unrecoverable and should not be caught in normal application code.
- ✅ `ArithmeticException` is thrown for **integer division by zero** (not floating-point — floating-point division yields `Infinity` or `NaN`).
- ✅ `NullPointerException` is the **most common** runtime exception in Java.
- ✅ `NumberFormatException` is a subclass of `IllegalArgumentException`, which is itself a subclass of `RuntimeException`.
- ✅ `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` extends `IndexOutOfBoundsException`, which extends `RuntimeException`.
- ✅ `throw` *throws* an object; `throws` *declares* the possibility.
- ✅ The `finally` block **always executes** (except when `System.exit()` is called).
- ✅ A `catch` block for a parent class (e.g., `Exception`) can catch a child class (e.g., `IOException`) due to polymorphism.
- ✅ Multiple catch blocks must be ordered **from most specific to most general** (compile error otherwise).
- ✅ Built-in exceptions are in `java.lang` → **no import statement** is needed.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Java Built-in Exceptions** ✅
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Custom Exception
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT633 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Custom Exception

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Custom Exception (User-Defined Exception):** A *custom exception* is a programmer-defined class that extends either the built-in `Exception` class (for **checked exceptions**) or the `RuntimeException` class (for **unchecked exceptions**) in Java. It is used to model application-specific error conditions that are not adequately represented by the standard exception hierarchy provided by the language.

In Object-Oriented Programming (OOP), exceptions are objects. Therefore, the act of creating a custom exception is simply the act of **defining a new class** that represents a unique, domain-specific error scenario within an application.

### The Two Architectural Choices in Java

| Choice | Parent Class | Type | Compiler Enforcement |
|---|---|---|---|
| `extends Exception` | `java.lang.Exception` | **Checked** | Yes (must be declared or caught) |
| `extends RuntimeException` | `java.lang.RuntimeException` | **Unchecked** | No (optional handling) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Specialized Alarm System"

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of exceptions as fire alarms in a building.**
> The building's standard alarm system can detect *smoke* or *heat* (these are built-in exceptions like `IOException`, `NullPointerException`). However, what if your specific office needs an alarm for **"Unauthorized Data Access"** or **"Insufficient Wallet Balance"**? You cannot ask the building's manufacturer to add this for you — you must **install your own custom alarm**. A custom exception is precisely this: your own specialized alarm, wired into your application's error-detection circuit.

## 1.3 Why Do We Need Custom Exceptions?

Standard library exceptions are **generic** (e.g., `ArithmeticException` for any divide-by-zero, regardless of context). In real-world business software, we need **semantic precision**:

- A banking application needs to distinguish between *"Insufficient Funds"*, *"Invalid PIN"*, and *"Daily Limit Exceeded"*.
- A university portal needs to distinguish between *"Invalid Roll Number"*, *"Duplicate Registration"*, and *"CGPA Out of Range"*.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners specifically award marks for explaining *why* custom exceptions are preferred over returning error codes or `boolean` flags. Always mention: **"Custom exceptions carry rich contextual information, integrate seamlessly with the try-catch mechanism, and obey OOP principles like inheritance and polymorphism."**

## 1.4 When Should You Create a Custom Exception?

Create one when:
1. The error is **domain-specific** (not a generic programming error).
2. The built-in exceptions **do not convey the precise meaning** of the failure.
3. You want the **caller to handle this specific error type differently** from other errors.
4. You need to **attach extra context data** (e.g., error code, timestamp, invalid value).

## 1.5 Anatomy of a Custom Exception Class

A custom exception class in Java is structurally identical to any other class, but it follows these conventions:
- It **extends** `Exception` or `RuntimeException`.
- It is **named with the suffix `Exception`** (e.g., `InvalidAgeException`, `InsufficientBalanceException`).
- It **provides constructors** — typically a no-arg constructor and a constructor that accepts a `String message`.
- The `String message` is stored in the parent class via `super(message)`.

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Pillar Architecture of Custom Exceptions

A complete custom exception system rests on three pillars:

### Pillar 1: **Definition (The Class)**
You declare a new class that inherits from `Exception` or `RuntimeException`.

### Pillar 2: **Throwing (The Trigger)**
The `throw` keyword instantiates your exception object and hands control to the JVM/runtime.

### Pillar 3: **Catching (The Response)**
The `try-catch` block intercepts the thrown object and executes recovery logic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Keyword Distinction (Frequently Asked in KTU Exams):**
> - `throw` (lowercase, single 'r') — **used to actually throw** an exception object. Example: `throw new MyException("Error");`
> - `throws` (lowercase, with 's') — **used in method signature** to declare that a method *may* propagate an exception. Example: `void read() throws IOException;`

## 2.2 KTU Formula / Syntax Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Syntax / Formula | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Class declaration | `class MyException extends Exception` | Create a checked custom exception |
| 2 | Unchecked variant | `class MyException extends RuntimeException` | Create an unchecked custom exception |
| 3 | Default constructor | `MyException() { super(); }` | Allows `throw new MyException();` |
| 4 | Message constructor | `MyException(String s) { super(s); }` | Allows `throw new MyException("msg");` |
| 5 | Cause constructor | `MyException(String s, Throwable c) { super(s, c); }` | Supports **exception chaining** |
| 6 | Throwing | `throw new MyException("Invalid input");` | Triggers the exception |
| 7 | Declaring | `void m() throws MyException` | Tells caller to handle it |
| 8 | Catching | `try { ... } catch(MyException e) { ... }` | Handles the custom exception |
| 9 | Get message | `e.getMessage()` | Retrieves the stored message |
| 10 | Print stack trace | `e.printStackTrace()` | Prints the full trace to console |

> [!NOTE]
> **Do not confuse:** A custom exception class itself is **just a blueprint** (the class). The actual exception object that flows through the `try-catch` pipeline is an **instance** of that class, created at the moment of `throw`.

## 2.3 Checked vs Unchecked — The Decision Matrix

| Criterion | Checked (extends Exception) | Unchecked (extends RuntimeException) |
|---|---|---|
| Compile-time check | **Yes** — compiler forces handling | No — optional |
| Use case | Recoverable conditions (e.g., file not found, invalid input) | Programming bugs (e.g., null pointer, divide by zero) |
| Declaration needed? | Yes (`throws` in method signature) | No |
| Inheritance chain | `Throwable → Exception → YourClass` | `Throwable → Exception → RuntimeException → YourClass` |
| Example | `IOException`, `SQLException` | `ArithmeticException`, `NullPointerException` |

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb for KTU Answers:** "If the caller can reasonably be expected to **recover** from the error, make it **checked**. If the error indicates a **programming bug** that should crash early, make it **unchecked**."

## 2.4 Constructor Chaining with `super()`

The parent `Exception` class stores the message string internally. The child class **must delegate** the storage via `super(message)`. This is constructor chaining — a direct application of OOP inheritance.

```
Child constructor  →  super(message)  →  Exception(String)  →  Throwable(String)
     (your code)        (delegation)        (stores the message)     (root class)
```

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Custom exceptions are foundational in:
- **Enterprise Java (Spring/Hibernate):** `org.springframework.dao.DataAccessException` is itself a custom exception hierarchy.
- **Banking Software:** `InsufficientFundsException`, `AccountFrozenException`.
- **Network Programming:** `ConnectionTimeoutException`, `ProtocolViolationException`.
- **Compiler Design:** `SyntaxErrorException`, `TypeMismatchException`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Insight:** In production codebases, custom exception hierarchies can grow to **50+ classes** organized by module. KTU tests whether you understand the *principle*, not the scale.

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Code Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Walkthrough 1 — Basic Checked Custom Exception

**Problem Statement:** Create a custom checked exception `InvalidAgeException`. Throw it from a `validateAge(int age)` method if `age < 18`. Handle it in `main`.

### Step 1: Define the Custom Exception Class

```java
// Step 1: Creating the blueprint
class InvalidAgeException extends Exception {
    // Constructor 1: No-argument constructor
    public InvalidAgeException() {
        super();  // Calls Exception()
    }
    
    // Constructor 2: Constructor that accepts an error message
    public InvalidAgeException(String message) {
        super(message);  // Delegates message storage to parent Exception class
    }
}
```

**Explanation of Step 1:**
- We declare `class InvalidAgeException extends Exception`. Since it extends `Exception`, it is a **checked exception** — the compiler will enforce that any method throwing it must either catch it or declare it with `throws`.
- The two constructors mirror the constructors of the parent `Exception` class, providing flexibility to the caller.
- `super(message)` is the **constructor chaining** mechanism that stores the descriptive string inside the parent class.

### Step 2: Create the Throwing Method

```java
class Voter {
    // 'throws' keyword declares that this method may propagate InvalidAgeException
    public void validateAge(int age) throws InvalidAgeException {
        if (age < 18) {
            // 'throw' keyword actually creates the exception object and triggers it
            throw new InvalidAgeException("Age " + age + " is not eligible for voting. Minimum age is 18.");
        } else {
            System.out.println("Welcome! You are eligible to vote.");
        }
    }
}
```

**Explanation of Step 2:**
- The method signature includes `throws InvalidAgeException` — this is a **declaration**, not a throw. It tells the compiler: "I am passing responsibility for this exception to my caller."
- Inside, `throw new InvalidAgeException(...)` is the **actual triggering** — it creates the object, stops normal execution, and hands it to the JVM.

### Step 3: Handle in the Caller (`main`)

```java
public class VotingApp {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Voter v = new Voter();
        try {
            v.validateAge(15);   // This will trigger the exception
            v.validateAge(25);   // This line will NOT execute (control has left try)
        } catch (InvalidAgeException e) {
            // Catch block is polymorphic — it matches the exact type or any supertype
            System.out.println("Caught Custom Exception: " + e.getMessage());
        }
        System.out.println("Program continues normally...");
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Caught Custom Exception: Age 15 is not eligible for voting. Minimum age is 18.
Program continues normally...
```

### Step 4: Trace the Execution Flow

```
main() called
    ↓
try block entered
    ↓
v.validateAge(15) called
    ↓
Inside validateAge: age < 18 is TRUE
    ↓
throw new InvalidAgeException("Age 15 ...") executed
    ↓
JVM unwinds the call stack from validateAge
    ↓
Looks for a matching catch block in main
    ↓
catch(InvalidAgeException e) matches → e refers to the thrown object
    ↓
e.getMessage() retrieves the stored "Age 15 ..." string
    ↓
catch block body executes → prints message
    ↓
Execution resumes after the try-catch block
```

---

## 3.2 Walkthrough 2 — Unchecked Custom Exception with Extra Fields

**Problem Statement:** Create an unchecked custom exception `InsufficientBalanceException` that stores both a message AND the deficit amount.

### Step 1: Define the Class with Custom Field

```java
// Unchecked because we extend RuntimeException
class InsufficientBalanceException extends RuntimeException {
    private double deficitAmount;  // Extra context data specific to our domain
    
    public InsufficientBalanceException(String message, double deficitAmount) {
        super(message);                  // Store message in parent
        this.deficitAmount = deficitAmount;  // Store our custom field
    }
    
    public double getDeficitAmount() {
        return deficitAmount;
    }
}
```

### Step 2: Use It in a Banking Context

```java
class BankAccount {
    private double balance;
    
    public BankAccount(double balance) {
        this.balance = balance;
    }
    
    public void withdraw(double amount) {
        if (amount > balance) {
            double deficit = amount - balance;
            throw new InsufficientBalanceException(
                "Cannot withdraw " + amount + ". Balance is " + balance,
                deficit
            );
        }
        balance -= amount;
        System.out.println("Withdrawal successful. New balance: " + balance);
    }
}
```

### Step 3: Handle with Rich Information

```java
public class BankingApp {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        BankAccount acc = new BankAccount(5000);
        try {
            acc.withdraw(7500);
        } catch (InsufficientBalanceException e) {
            System.out.println("Error: " + e.getMessage());
            System.out.println("You are short by: Rs. " + e.getDeficitAmount());
        }
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Error: Cannot withdraw 7500.0. Balance is 5000.0
You are short by: Rs. 2500.0
```

**Why this matters (KTU Insight):** This demonstrates that custom exceptions are not just message-carriers — they are **full-fledged objects** that can carry arbitrary fields and methods, embodying the OOP principle of **encapsulation of error data**.

---

## 3.3 Walkthrough 3 — Custom Exception Hierarchy (Inheritance Applied)

```java
// Root custom exception for a library system
class LibraryException extends Exception {
    public LibraryException(String message) {
        super(message);
    }
}

// Specific subclass
class BookNotFoundException extends LibraryException {
    public BookNotFoundException(String isbn) {
        super("Book with ISBN " + isbn + " not found in the library.");
    }
}

// Another specific subclass
class MaxBooksReachedException extends LibraryException {
    private int limit;
    public MaxBooksReachedException(int limit) {
        super("You have reached the maximum borrowing limit of " + limit + " books.");
        this.limit = limit;
    }
    public int getLimit() { return limit; }
}
```

**Polymorphic Catching:**
```java
try {
    library.borrowBook("978-0134685991");
} catch (BookNotFoundException e) {
    // Specific handling
} catch (LibraryException e) {
    // Catches any other LibraryException subclass (e.g., MaxBooksReachedException)
}
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Order matters in catch blocks!** Always catch the **most specific** (subclass) first, then the **more general** (parent class). Otherwise, the compiler will complain: *"Unreachable catch block."*

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Common Scenarios in KTU Questions

| Scenario | Checked or Unchecked? | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| `InvalidAgeException` for voting eligibility | **Checked** | Caller can prompt the user to re-enter a valid age — recoverable |
| `InsufficientBalanceException` in ATM | **Unchecked** | Treated as a programming/business rule violation that should halt transaction |
| `InvalidRollNumberException` in university | **Checked** | Recoverable — admin can correct the input |
| `NullStudentReferenceException` | **Unchecked** | Indicates a programmer bug, not a recoverable scenario |
| `DatabaseConnectionException` | **Checked** | Caller can retry the connection — recoverable |

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Exception Class Hierarchy (Java)

## 4.2 Lifecycle of a Custom Exception Object (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid

    D --> E{"Step 5: Is there a matching catch block in the call stack?"}

```

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture: Components of a Custom Exception System

```mermaid

    subgraph DEFINITION["1. EXCEPTION CLASS DEFINITION"]
        D1["class header: extends Exception"]
        D2["field: message via super"]

    end
    
    subgraph THROWING["2. THROWING MECHANISM"]
        T1["method signature: throws MyException"]
        T2["throw new MyException message"]
        T3["object created in heap memory"]
    end
    
    subgraph CATCHING["3. CATCHING MECHANISM"]
        C1["try block wraps risky code"]
        C2["catch MyException e matches by type"]
        C3["e.getMessage retrieves info"]
    end
    
    DEFINITION --> THROWING
    THROWING --> CATCHING
```

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**What is a custom exception in Java? Why is it preferred over returning error codes?**

**Model Answer:**
A custom exception is a user-defined class that inherits from `Exception` (checked) or `RuntimeException` (unchecked) to represent application-specific error conditions. It is preferred over error codes because:
1. Exceptions **carry rich contextual data** (message, stack trace, custom fields).
2. Exceptions **integrate with the try-catch mechanism**, separating error-handling from normal flow.
3. Exceptions **obey OOP principles** like inheritance and polymorphism, allowing specialized handling.
4. Error codes are easily ignored by the caller; exceptions are enforced by the compiler (for checked).

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**Differentiate between `throw` and `throws` keywords in Java.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | `throw` | `throws` |
|---|---|---|
| Usage | Used to **actually throw** an exception object | Used to **declare** exceptions a method may propagate |
| Position | Inside the method body | In the method signature |
| Syntax | `throw new MyException("msg");` | `void m() throws MyException` |
| Number | Throws **one** exception at a time | Can declare **multiple** exceptions separated by commas |
| Nature | Statement (executes at runtime) | Declaration (compile-time contract) |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of user-defined exceptions in Java with a suitable example. Discuss the difference between checked and unchecked exceptions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition of User-Defined Exceptions:**
User-defined (custom) exceptions are classes created by the programmer to handle specific error conditions in an application. They are created by extending the built-in `Exception` class (for checked) or `RuntimeException` class (for unchecked).

**Example Code:**
```java
// Custom checked exception
class InsufficientFundsException extends Exception {
    private double amount;
    public InsufficientFundsException(String message, double amount) {
        super(message);
        this.amount = amount;
    }
    public double getAmount() { return amount; }
}

class Account {
    private double balance = 1000;
    public void withdraw(double amt) throws InsufficientFundsException {
        if (amt > balance) {
            throw new InsufficientFundsException("Withdrawal exceeds balance", amt - balance);
        }
        balance -= amt;
    }
}

public class TestAccount {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Account a = new Account();
        try {
            a.withdraw(2000);
        } catch (InsufficientFundsException e) {
            System.out.println(e.getMessage() + " Short by: " + e.getAmount());
        }
    }
}
```

**Checked vs Unchecked Exceptions:**

| Feature | Checked (extends Exception) | Unchecked (extends RuntimeException) |
|---|---|---|
| Compile-time verification | Yes | No |
| Mandatory declaration with throws | Yes | No |
| Example | `IOException`, `SQLException` | `ArithmeticException`, `NullPointerException` |
| Use case | Recoverable conditions | Programming errors |
| Inheritance chain | `Throwable → Exception → Yours` | `Throwable → Exception → RuntimeException → Yours` |

**(b)** Write a Java program to create a custom exception `InvalidMarksException` that is thrown when a student enters marks greater than 100 or less than 0. Demonstrate its use in a `Student` class. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```java
// Custom unchecked exception (since input validation bugs are programmer errors)
class InvalidMarksException extends RuntimeException {
    public InvalidMarksException(String message) {
        super(message);
    }
}

class Student {
    private String name;
    private int marks;
    
    public Student(String name, int marks) {
        this.name = name;
        setMarks(marks);  // Use setter for validation
    }
    
    public void setMarks(int marks) {
        if (marks < 0 || marks > 100) {
            throw new InvalidMarksException(
                "Invalid marks: " + marks + ". Marks must be between 0 and 100."
            );
        }
        this.marks = marks;
    }
    
    public void display() {
        System.out.println("Student: " + name + ", Marks: " + marks);
    }
}

public class StudentTest {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            Student s1 = new Student("Anand", 85);
            s1.display();
            
            Student s2 = new Student("Priya", 150);  // This will throw
            s2.display();
        } catch (InvalidMarksException e) {
            System.out.println("Error: " + e.getMessage());
        }
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Student: Anand, Marks: 85
Error: Invalid marks: 150. Marks must be between 0 and 100.
```

**Explanation of Key Design Choices:**
- The exception is **unchecked** (extends `RuntimeException`) because entering marks > 100 is considered a **programming/input-validation bug** rather than a recoverable condition.
- The `setMarks` method is used from the constructor to **centralize validation** logic.
- The `try-catch` in `main` demonstrates **polymorphic exception handling** — the JVM routes the thrown object to the matching catch block.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam - June 2023, Module 3]**

**(a)** What are the different ways to create custom exceptions in Java? Explain with examples how you can create a custom exception that stores additional information beyond the message. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Two Ways to Create Custom Exceptions:**

**Way 1: Extending `Exception` (Checked)**
```java
class MyCheckedException extends Exception {
    public MyCheckedException(String message) {
        super(message);
    }
}
```
*Compiler forces the caller to either catch or declare this exception.*

**Way 2: Extending `RuntimeException` (Unchecked)**
```java
class MyUncheckedException extends RuntimeException {
    public MyUncheckedException(String message) {
        super(message);
    }
}
```
*Compiler does not force handling — optional.*

**Custom Exception with Additional Fields:**
```java
class TransactionException extends Exception {
    private String transactionId;  // Extra field 1
    private double amount;        // Extra field 2
    private String currency;      // Extra field 3
    
    public TransactionException(String message, String tid, double amt, String cur) {
        super(message);
        this.transactionId = tid;
        this.amount = amt;
        this.currency = cur;
    }
    
    // Getters to access the extra info
    public String getTransactionId() { return transactionId; }
    public double getAmount() { return amount; }
    public String getCurrency() { return currency; }
    
    // Overriding toString for custom display
    @Override
    public String toString() {
        return super.getMessage() + " [TxnID: " + transactionId + 
               ", Amount: " + amount + " " + currency + "]";
    }
}

class PaymentGateway {
    public void processPayment(String tid, double amt, String cur) throws TransactionException {
        if (amt <= 0) {
            throw new TransactionException("Invalid transaction amount", tid, amt, cur);
        }
        System.out.println("Processed: " + tid);
    }
}

public class PaymentTest {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            new PaymentGateway().processPayment("TXN001", -500, "INR");
        } catch (TransactionException e) {
            System.out.println("Error: " + e.getMessage());
            System.out.println("Failed Txn ID: " + e.getTransactionId());
            System.out.println("Amount: " + e.getAmount() + " " + e.getCurrency());
        }
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Error: Invalid transaction amount
Failed Txn ID: TXN001
Amount: -500.0 INR
```

**(b)** Explain exception chaining in Java with a custom exception example. Write a program where one custom exception wraps another. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Concept of Exception Chaining:**
Exception chaining (also called *wrapping* or *cause chaining*) is the technique where one exception is stored as the "cause" of another exception. This preserves the original stack trace while allowing a higher-level abstraction.

Java's `Throwable` class provides:
- `getCause()` — returns the wrapped exception
- `initCause(Throwable cause)` — sets the cause
- Constructor: `Throwable(String message, Throwable cause)`

**Program Demonstrating Chaining:**
```java
// Low-level custom exception
class DatabaseException extends Exception {
    public DatabaseException(String message) {
        super(message);
    }
}

// High-level custom exception that wraps the low-level one
class BusinessLogicException extends Exception {
    public BusinessLogicException(String message, Throwable cause) {
        super(message, cause);  // 'cause' is stored as the underlying reason
    }
}

class OrderProcessor {
    public void connectToDB() throws DatabaseException {
        // Simulate low-level failure
        throw new DatabaseException("Failed to connect to MySQL at port 3306");
    }
    
    public void processOrder(String orderId) throws BusinessLogicException {
        try {
            connectToDB();
        } catch (DatabaseException dbEx) {
            // Wrap the low-level exception in a high-level one
            throw new BusinessLogicException(
                "Unable to process order " + orderId + " due to system error",
                dbEx  // This is the cause
            );
        }
    }
}

public class ChainingDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            new OrderProcessor().processOrder("ORD-2024-7891");
        } catch (BusinessLogicException e) {
            System.out.println("High-level error: " + e.getMessage());
            System.out.println("Root cause: " + e.getCause().getMessage());
            
            // Print full chain
            System.out.println("\nFull stack trace:");
            e.printStackTrace();
        }
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
High-level error: Unable to process order ORD-2024-7891 due to system error
Root cause: Failed to connect to MySQL at port 3306

Full stack trace:
BusinessLogicException: Unable to process order ORD-2024-7891 due to system error
    at OrderProcessor.processOrder(ChainingDemo.java:23)
    at ChainingDemo.main(ChainingDemo.java:34)
Caused by: DatabaseException: Failed to connect to MySQL at port 3306
    at OrderProcessor.connectToDB(ChainingDemo.java:15)
    ... 1 more
```

**Why Exception Chaining is Important:**
1. **Layered Architecture:** In n-tier applications (presentation → business → data), each layer can throw its own exception while preserving the original cause.
2. **Debugging:** The `Caused by:` line in the stack trace points engineers directly to the root failure.
3. **Abstraction:** Higher layers need not expose low-level technical details to end users, but developers can still trace them.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting `super(message)`** in the custom exception constructor — message will be `null` and `e.getMessage()` returns nothing. **Loss: 1-2 marks.**
> 2. **Wrong catch-block order** — placing the parent class catch before the child class catch causes an *"Unreachable catch block"* compile error. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 3. **Confusing `throw` and `throws`** — using `throws` to actually trigger an exception, or using `throw` in the method signature. **Loss: 1-2 marks.**
> 4. **Forgetting to declare `throws`** for checked custom exceptions — causes compile-time error. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 5. **Not explaining WHY** checked vs unchecked was chosen — examiners want a justification based on recoverability, not just a code dump. **Loss: 1-2 marks.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Memorize These Before Entering the Exam Hall:**

- **Definition:** A custom exception is a user-defined class extending `Exception` (checked) or `RuntimeException` (unchecked).
- **Naming Convention:** Always suffix with the word `Exception` (e.g., `InvalidAgeException`).
- **Two Constructors Recommended:** No-arg constructor and `String message` constructor.
- **Constructor Chaining:** Always call `super(message)` to store the message in the parent `Throwable` class.
- **`throw` vs `throws`:** `throw` is action (inside method body); `throws` is declaration (in method signature).
- **Checked vs Unchecked Rule:** Checked = recoverable conditions (compiler enforces). Unchecked = programming bugs (optional handling).
- **Catch Block Order:** Most specific (child) first, then general (parent). Otherwise, compile error.
- **Inheritance Hierarchy:** All exceptions ultimately inherit from `java.lang.Throwable`.
- **Polymorphism in Catching:** A `catch (ParentException e)` block catches any subclass instance too.
- **Exception Chaining:** Use `super(message, cause)` or `initCause()` to wrap a low-level exception inside a high-level one.
- **Methods Available from Parent:** `getMessage()`, `toString()`, `printStackTrace()`, `getCause()`.
- **Don't Catch `Throwable` or `Error`:** These represent JVM-level fatal conditions; let them propagate.
- **Best Practice:** Catch the most specific exception type possible; avoid generic `catch (Exception e)` unless rethrowing.
- **Real-World Use:** Custom exceptions are used in **every enterprise application** — banking, e-commerce, healthcare, telecom.
- **KTU Favorite Question Pattern:** "Create a custom exception for [domain]. Demonstrate with try-catch-finally." — Practice this template.
- **Don't Forget:** The exception class itself is just a blueprint; the actual object is created by `new` at the `throw` site.
- **Key Insight for 14-mark Answers:** Always include a **diagram or text-based hierarchy**, **2-3 constructors**, **a real use-case justification** for checked vs unchecked, and a **complete working program**.
- **Examiner Loves:** When you mention that custom exceptions **encapsulate error data** and **obey OOP principles** (inheritance, polymorphism, encapsulation).
- **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Writing `class MyException implements Exception` — **WRONG.** It must be `extends`, not `implements`, because `Exception` is a class, not an interface.
- **Module Context:** This topic is part of Module 3 (Exception Handling) and is frequently clubbed with `try-catch-finally`, `throw/throws`, and `built-in exceptions` in 14-mark questions.

topics.title: Swings Fundamentals - Overview of AWT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT633 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Swings Fundamentals - Overview of AWT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of AWT

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Abstract Window Toolkit (AWT)** is the original platform-dependent, heavyweight GUI (Graphical User Interface) toolkit provided by Java (`java.awt` package) that forms the foundation upon which the lightweight, platform-independent Swing framework (`javax.swing` package) is built. AWT supplies the core set of GUI components, container classes, layout managers, and the underlying event-handling architecture used to build desktop graphical applications in Java.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECIT633 – Object Oriented Programming, Module 4)**, AWT is treated as the *ancestor* and *conceptual prerequisite* of Swing. The KTU syllabus explicitly requires students to understand:

- The **Component** and **Container** class hierarchy of AWT.
- The distinction between **heavyweight** (AWT) and **lightweight** (Swing) components.
- How Swing components are **pluggable look-and-feel** extensions of AWT concepts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU expects you to know that every top-level Swing container (e.g., `JFrame`, `JApplet`, `JDialog`) internally *contains* a hidden AWT peer (a heavy-weight native window). This is why understanding AWT is mandatory before diving into Swing.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Kitchen" Model

Imagine you are opening a restaurant:

| Real-World Element | Java AWT / Swing Equivalent |
|---|---|
| The **building structure, walls, doors** (made of bricks, concrete) | **AWT Components** — heavy, native, platform-built (uses OS resources) |
| The **interior decoration, furniture, lighting** (modular, swappable) | **Swing Components** — lightweight, painted by Java itself, look can be changed |
| The **chef (cook)** | The **Event Listener** — reacts when something happens |
| The **customer pressing a service bell** | The **Event Object** — carries info about what happened |
| The **waiter carrying the bell signal to the chef** | The **Event Dispatch Thread (EDT)** |

> [!TIP]
> AWT = the **raw building**. Swing = the **furnished interior**. You cannot have a furnished interior without a building, which is *exactly* why AWT is taught before Swing.

## 1.3 Why AWT Was Created (Historical Context for KTU 2-Mark Answers)

Before AWT, Java applets and applications had no standard way to draw on the screen. AWT was introduced in **JDK 1.0 (1996)** with the following goals:

1. **Platform Independence at the API level** — Java code uses `Button`, not Windows `BUTTON` or Mac `NSButton`.
2. **Common denominator approach** — AWT uses only components supported by *every* native platform, leading to the "least common denominator" criticism.
3. **Event Delegation Model** — AWT 1.1 (JDK 1.1, 1997) replaced the brittle inheritance-based event model with the modern **delegation event model**, which Swing also uses.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Many students think AWT is "deprecated and useless." This is **wrong**. AWT is still the *only* way to obtain top-level windows, the system tray, the clipboard, the robot class, and headless-mode operations. Swing literally cannot exist without AWT underneath.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The AWT Class Hierarchy — Root Philosophy

The entire AWT revolves around two abstract super-classes from `java.awt`:

- **`java.awt.Component`** — represents anything that can be **drawn on the screen** and can **receive events** (Button, TextField, Label, Choice, List, Checkbox, Canvas, etc.).
- **`java.awt.Container`** — a *special* kind of `Component` that can **hold other components**, including other containers. Sub-classes: `Panel`, `Window` → `Frame`, `Dialog`.

The canonical chain is:

```
java.lang.Object
   └── java.awt.Component
         └── java.awt.Container
               ├── java.awt.Panel  ──► java.applet.Applet (legacy)
               └── java.awt.Window
                     ├── java.awt.Frame
                     ├── java.awt.Dialog
                     └── java.awt.FileDialog
```

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of AWT Architecture

1. **Step 1 — Native Peer Creation.** When you instantiate an AWT component (e.g., `new Button("OK")`), the JVM creates a *peer object* — a small native (OS-level) component that mirrors the Java object. This is the source of the term **"heavyweight"**.
2. **Step 2 — Layout Negotiation.** A container's `LayoutManager` is invoked to compute (x, y, width, height) for every child component. AWT ships `FlowLayout`, `BorderLayout`, `GridLayout`, `GridBagLayout`, and `CardLayout`.
3. **Step 3 — Event Source Registration.** Each component can register `Listener` objects. When the user interacts (click, key press, mouse move), the JVM fires a corresponding `AWTEvent` subclass.
4. **Step 4 — Event Dispatch.** All AWT events are placed on the **AWT EventQueue** and processed by a single thread — the **Event Dispatch Thread (EDT)**. Swing *re-uses* this same thread.
5. **Step 5 — Painting Cycle.** The `Component.paint(Graphics g)` method is called by the system, which recursively paints all children. AWT's `Graphics` class is later subclassed by `Graphics2D` for richer 2D rendering.

## 2.3 AWT vs. Swing — The Core Trade-offs (High-Yield Comparison)

| Property | AWT (`java.awt.*`) | Swing (`javax.swing.*`) |
|---|---|---|
| Package | `java.awt` | `javax.swing` |
| Component Weight | **Heavyweight** (native peer) | **Lightweight** (drawn by Java) |
| Platform Dependency | Look depends on OS | **Pluggable Look-and-Feel** (Metal, Nimbus, System) |
| Inheritance Root | `Component` | `JComponent` (extends `Container`) |
| Top-level Containers | `Frame`, `Dialog`, `Applet` | `JFrame`, `JDialog`, `JApplet` |
| Drawing Capability | Limited (`Component.paint`) | Powerful (`JComponent.paintComponent`) |
| MVC Architecture | No | **Yes** (Model-View-Controller built-in) |
| Component Prefix | None (`Button`) | **`J` prefix** (`JButton`) |
| Threading | Single EDT | Single EDT (same) |
| Performance | Faster on simple UIs | Slightly slower but more flexible |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MVC in Swing:** Each Swing component (e.g., `JButton`) is split into three objects: the *Model* (`ButtonModel`), the *View* (the painted UI), and the *Controller* (UI delegate). AWT does *not* follow MVC.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Fact Sheet

| Concept | Definition / Formula | Key Term to Memorize |
|---|---|---|
| AWT Package | `java.awt` | **Heavyweight** |
| Swing Package | `javax.swing` | **Lightweight** |
| Root Class of AWT | `java.awt.Component` | Abstract class |
| Root Class of Swing | `javax.swing.JComponent` | Abstract class |
| AWT Top-level Window | `java.awt.Frame` | Title bar + border |
| Swing Top-level Window | `javax.swing.JFrame` | Extends `Frame` internally |
| Event Dispatch Thread | A single thread handling all UI events | **EDT** |
| Default Layout of `Frame` | `BorderLayout` | North, South, East, West, Center |
| Default Layout of `Panel` | `FlowLayout` | Left-to-right, top-to-bottom |
| `paint()` method signature | `public void paint(Graphics g)` | Called by EDT |
| MVC in Swing | Model-View-Controller | **Pluggable UI Delegate** |
| Look-and-Feel Setter | `UIManager.setLookAndFeel(...)` | Static call before UI creation |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

AWT/Swing are used to build:

- **Standalone Java desktop tools** (IDEs like older NetBeans, jEdit).
- **Internal enterprise dashboards** for banking & inventory (SWT + Swing hybrids).
- **Kiosk applications** where native look is required → AWT peers help.
- **Robotics/automation control panels** (using `java.awt.Robot` for simulating mouse/keyboard input).
- **Scientific visualization** (Swing's `Graphics2D` for plotting).

> [!TIP]
> Although JavaFX replaced Swing as the *recommended* modern toolkit (since JDK 8), the **KTU 2024 syllabus still mandates AWT + Swing** because they teach the *foundational* OOP event model that recurs in JavaFX, Android UI, and web frameworks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown & Concept Derivations

## 3.1 Derivation of the "Heavyweight vs Lightweight" Distinction

The KTU exam often asks: *"Why are AWT components called heavyweight and Swing components lightweight?"* Here is the rigorous conceptual derivation.

### Step 1 — Define the term "Component"

A **component** in Java is any class that inherits from `java.awt.Component`. It must be:

$$
\text{Component} \;\equiv\; \text{displayable} \;\land\; \text{event-aware}
$$

where **displayable** means the object can be rendered on a screen, and **event-aware** means it can register and respond to user input.

### Step 2 — Memory Footprint Analysis

When the JVM instantiates an AWT `Button`, it performs the following sequence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Java Heap:} \quad & \text{Button}_J \;\text{(small Java object)} \\
\text{Native Heap:} \quad & \text{Button}_{OS} \;\text{(OS-level widget, e.g., Win32 HWND)} \\
\text{Peer Object:} \quad & \text{ButtonPeer} \;\text{(Java-C bridge)}
\end{aligned}
$$

So one logical `Button` is backed by **three** memory objects. This justifies the term **heavyweight** = "carries native baggage."

### Step 3 — Swing's Optimization

A Swing `JButton` does **not** request a native widget. Instead:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Java Heap:} \quad & \text{JButton}_J \;\text{(single Java object)} \\
\text{Native Heap:} \quad & \varnothing \;\text{(nothing — purely painted by Java)} \\
\text{Peer Object:} \quad & \varnothing
\end{aligned}
$$

The drawing is performed by overriding `JComponent.paintComponent(Graphics g)` and using Java's own 2D pipeline. Hence **lightweight** = "Java-only footprint."

### Step 4 — Mathematical Cost Model (Conceptual)

Let $n$ be the number of components and $m$ be the number of native peers. Then:

$$
\text{Memory}(AWT) = n \cdot (\text{Java} + \text{Native} + \text{Peer}) = O(3n)
$$

$$
\text{Memory}(Swing) = n \cdot (\text{Java only}) = O(n)
$$

For large UIs (e.g., a table with 10 000 rows), the savings are substantial — this is *why* Swing exists.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Mapping KTU 2024 Sub-Topics to AWT Classes

| KTU Module-4 Sub-Topic | AWT Class | Swing Counterpart | Key Method to Remember |
|---|---|---|---|
| Button | `java.awt.Button` | `JButton` | `addActionListener(...)` |
| Label | `java.awt.Label` | `JLabel` | `setText(String)` |
| Text Input | `java.awt.TextField` | `JTextField` | `getText()` |
| Multi-line Text | `java.awt.TextArea` | `JTextArea` | `setEditable(boolean)` |
| Checkbox | `java.awt.Checkbox` | `JCheckBox` | `setState(boolean)` |
| Choice Dropdown | `java.awt.Choice` | `JComboBox` | `addItem(Object)` |
| List | `java.awt.List` | `JList` | `setListData(...)` |
| Top Window | `java.awt.Frame` | `JFrame` | `setSize(int,int)` |
| Modal Dialog | `java.awt.Dialog` | `JDialog` | `setModal(true)` |
| Drawing Surface | `java.awt.Canvas` | `JPanel` | `paint(Graphics g)` |
| Menu Bar | `java.awt.MenuBar` | `JMenuBar` | `setJMenuBar(...)` |

## 3.3 Exhaustive Walkthrough: AWT "Hello World" Program

Even though the question is about *overview*, KTU often expects 1–2 line code snippets. Here is the canonical AWT example, fully annotated.

```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class AWTHello extends Frame implements ActionListener {
    Label lbl;
    Button btn;

    public AWTHello() {
        // Step 1: Set layout manager
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());

        // Step 2: Instantiate AWT components
        lbl = new Label("Press the button");
        btn = new Button("Click Me");

        // Step 3: Register the listener (Event Delegation Model)
        btn.addActionListener(this);

        // Step 4: Add components to the container
        add(lbl);
        add(btn);

        // Step 5: Configure the top-level window
        setTitle("AWT Demo");
        setSize(300, 200);
        setVisible(true);   // triggers native peer creation
    }

    // Step 6: Define the event-handling method
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        lbl.setText("Hello, AWT World!");
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new AWTHello();
    }
}
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Line-by-line Pedagogical Notes (for KTU 14-mark answers):**
>
> 1. `extends Frame` — Inherits a top-level window with title bar & border.
> 2. `setLayout(new FlowLayout())` — Without a layout manager, components are placed at (0,0) and overlap. KTU loves asking *"Why do we need a LayoutManager?"*
> 3. `addActionListener(this)` — This is the **Event Delegation Model** in action. The event source (`btn`) *delegates* the event to the listener (`this`).
> 4. `setVisible(true)` — Until this call, no native peer exists. The peer is created **lazily**.
> 5. `actionPerformed` — The *only* method of the `ActionListener` interface that must be implemented.

## 3.4 Derivation of the Event Delegation Model Flow

The flow of an event in AWT can be expressed as a 4-tuple mapping:

$$
E : (S, T, M, L) \mapsto \text{Callback}
$$

where:

- $S$ = **Source** of the event (e.g., `Button` instance).
- $T$ = **Type** of event (e.g., `ActionEvent`).
- $M$ = **Method** invoked on the listener (e.g., `actionPerformed`).
- $L$ = **Listener** object that registered interest.

Step-by-step derivation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(1)} \quad & \text{User clicks Button} \;\Rightarrow\; OS sends a native event to the JVM. \\
\text{(2)} \quad & \text{JVM constructs an AWTEvent subclass} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{ActionEvent } e. \\
\text{(3)} \quad & \text{JVM iterates over } S.\text{listenerList}: \\
& \quad \forall\, \ell \in \text{listenerList}: \; \ell.\text{method}_M(e) \\
\text{(4)} \quad & \text{Listener's callback executes} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{UI updates.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Trick Question:** *"What is the difference between the AWT 1.0 event model and the AWT 1.1 event model?"* — Answer: AWT 1.0 used the **inheritance model** (override `action(Event, Object)` in a subclass). AWT 1.1 introduced the **delegation model** (register a `Listener` object). The delegation model is the one Swing uses.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 AWT Class Hierarchy (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    objA["Object"] --> compA["Component"]
    compA --> contA["Container"]
    compA --> btnA["Button"]
    compA --> lblA["Label"]
    compA --> tfA["TextField"]
    compA --> taA["TextArea"]
    compA --> chkA["Checkbox"]
    compA --> chA["Choice"]
    compA --> lstA["List"]
    compA --> canA["Canvas"]
    contA --> panA["Panel"]
    contA --> winA["Window"]
    contA --> scA["ScrollPane"]
    panA --> aplA["Applet"]
    winA --> frmA["Frame"]
    winA --> dlgA["Dialog"]
    dlgA --> fdA["FileDialog"]
    frmA --> jfA["JFrame (Swing top-level)"]
```

## 4.2 Swing Class Hierarchy — Built on Top of AWT

```mermaid
graph TD
    objS["Object"] --> compS["Component (AWT)"]
    compS --> contS["Container (AWT)"]
    contS --> jcompS["JComponent (Swing root)"]
    jcompS --> jpnlS["JPanel"]
    jcompS --> jbtnS["JButton"]
    jcompS --> jlblS["JLabel"]
    jcompS --> jtfS["JTextField"]
    jcompS --> jtaS["JTextArea"]
    jcompS --> jcbS["JCheckBox"]
    jcompS --> jcmbS["JComboBox"]
    jcompS --> jlstS["JList"]
    jcompS --> jtblS["JTable"]
    jcompS --> jtrS["JTree"]
    contS --> jfrmS["JFrame"]
    contS --> jdlgS["JDialog"]
    contS --> japlS["JApplet"]
```

## 4.3 Event Delegation Model — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    userA["User Interaction"] --> osA["Operating System"]
    osA --> jvmA["JVM Event Pump"]
    jvmA --> evtA["ActionEvent Object Created"]
    evtA --> qA["AWT EventQueue"]
    qA --> edtA["Event Dispatch Thread"]
    edtA --> srcA["Event Source (Button)"]
    srcA --> llA["ListenerList Iteration"]
    llA --> cbA["Listener Callback actionPerformed"]
    cbA --> updA["UI State Updated"]
    updA --> rdrA["paint Method Invoked"]
    rdrA --> userA
```

## 4.4 Heavyweight vs Lightweight — Block-Level Comparison

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph HEAVY["AWT HEAVYWEIGHT FLOW"]
        hJ["Java Button Object"] --> hP["ButtonPeer Bridge"]
        hP --> hN["Native OS Widget (HWND / NSView)"]
        hN --> hD["OS Draws Button"]
    end
    subgraph LIGHT["SWING LIGHTWEIGHT FLOW"]
        lJ["Java JButton Object"] --> lU["UI Delegate (View)"]
        lU --> lG["Java Graphics2D Engine"]
        lG --> lD["Java Draws Button Pixel by Pixel"]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** Notice how the heavyweight path has *three* layers (Java, Peer, Native) while the lightweight path stays *entirely* in Java. This visualizes the O(3n) vs O(n) memory model from Section 3.1.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**
**Q:** Define AWT. List any four AWT components with their corresponding Swing counterparts.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Abstract Window Toolkit (AWT)** is the platform-dependent GUI toolkit in `java.awt` that provides the core set of components, containers, layout managers, and event-handling classes for building Java graphical applications. *(1 mark)*

| AWT Component | Swing Counterpart | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `Button` | `JButton` | Clickable button |
| `Label` | `JLabel` | Non-editable text |
| `TextField` | `JTextField` | Single-line input |
| `Checkbox` | `JCheckBox` | Toggle option |

*(2 marks for the table with correct one-to-one mapping)*

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**
**Q:** What is the Event Delegation Model in AWT? Mention the three participants involved.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Event Delegation Model (EDM)** is an event-handling mechanism introduced in AWT 1.1 where an event source *delegates* the processing of an event to a registered listener object, decoupling event generation from event handling. *(1 mark)*

The three participants are:

1. **Event Source** — The component that generates the event (e.g., `Button`).
2. **Event Object** — The object that carries event info (e.g., `ActionEvent`).
3. **Event Listener** — The object that receives and handles the event (e.g., a class implementing `ActionListener`). *(2 marks — 1 for each participant named correctly, with an example)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks
**`[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4]`**
**Q:** *(a)* Explain the AWT class hierarchy starting from `java.lang.Object` up to `Frame` and `Dialog`. Draw a neat diagram. *(7 marks)*
**Q:** *(b)* Write a complete Java AWT program to create a `Frame` containing a `Label`, a `TextField`, and a `Button`. When the button is clicked, the label should display the text entered in the text field. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

The AWT class hierarchy begins at `java.lang.Object`, the root of the entire Java class system.

- **`java.awt.Component`** is an abstract class that represents any object that can be displayed on the screen and can receive input events. Examples: `Button`, `Label`, `TextField`, `Checkbox`, `Choice`, `List`, `Canvas`, `TextArea`. *(1 mark)*
- **`java.awt.Container`** is a subclass of `Component` that can hold other components, including other containers. Subclasses are `Panel`, `Window`, and `ScrollPane`. *(1 mark)*
- **`java.awt.Panel`** is a generic container with no title bar, usually embedded inside another container (e.g., inside a `Frame`). The default layout is `FlowLayout`. *(1 mark)*
- **`java.awt.Window`** is a top-level window with no title bar and no border. It is rarely used directly. *(1 mark)*
- **`java.awt.Frame`** is a subclass of `Window` that adds a **title bar, menu bar, and resizable border**. It is the most common AWT top-level window. Default layout is `BorderLayout`. *(1 mark)*
- **`java.awt.Dialog`** is another `Window` subclass used to create **modal or modeless** pop-up dialogs (e.g., file chooser, message box). *(1 mark)*
- **`java.awt.FileDialog`** is a special dialog for selecting files, displaying the OS-native file picker. *(1 mark)*

```mermaid
graph TD
    objX["java.lang.Object"] --> compX["java.awt.Component"]
    compX --> contX["java.awt.Container"]
    contX --> panX["java.awt.Panel"]
    contX --> winX["java.awt.Window"]
    contX --> spX["java.awt.ScrollPane"]
    winX --> frmX["java.awt.Frame"]
```
