```mermaid
    winX --> dlgX["java.awt.Dialog"]
    dlgX --> fdX["java.awt.FileDialog"]
```

*(The diagram is worth 1 mark included in the above total of 7)*

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class AWTTextEcho extends Frame implements ActionListener {
    Label lbl;
    TextField tf;
    Button btn;

    public AWTTextEcho() {
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());                       // (1) Set layout
        lbl = new Label("Type something below:");          // (2) Create label
        tf  = new TextField(20);                           // (3) Create text field
        btn = new Button("Show");                          // (4) Create button
        btn.addActionListener(this);                       // (5) Register listener

        add(lbl); add(tf); add(btn);                       // (6) Add to Frame
        setTitle("AWT Text Echo");
        setSize(350, 150);
        setVisible(true);
    }

    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {           // (7) Handle click
        lbl.setText("You typed: " + tf.getText());
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new AWTTextEcho();
    }
}
```

**Explanation for marks (1 mark per key point):**

1. `Frame` is extended for the top-level window.
2. `ActionListener` interface is implemented so `this` can serve as the listener.
3. `FlowLayout` is set so components are placed left-to-right.
4. `addActionListener(this)` registers the listener — the heart of the **Event Delegation Model**.
5. `actionPerformed` is the *mandatory* method from the `ActionListener` interface.
6. `setVisible(true)` triggers native peer creation.
7. `tf.getText()` retrieves the typed string and updates the label.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Part B:**
> 1. **Forgetting `setLayout(...)`:** If no layout manager is set, all components stack at (0,0) and the student loses **1 mark**.
> 2. **Not implementing `ActionListener`:** Forgetting `implements ActionListener` causes a compile error. The `addActionListener(this)` call *requires* it. **−1 mark**.
> 3. **Using `setText` without importing `java.awt.*`:** Some students forget the import. AWT classes won't resolve. **−0.5 mark** if partial credit.
> 4. **Calling `setVisible(true)` before `add(...)`:** Components added *after* `setVisible(true)` are not displayed. **−1 mark**.
> 5. **Confusing `TextField` (AWT) with `JTextField` (Swing):** Mixing them causes a type mismatch in the import block. **−0.5 mark**.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE for Internal Choice)
**`[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper, Module 4]`**
**Q:** *(a)* Differentiate between AWT and Swing with at least six points. Why is Swing called lightweight? *(7 marks)*
**Q:** *(b)* Explain the Event Delegation Model with a neat diagram. How does it differ from the older inheritance-based event model? *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

| # | AWT (`java.awt.*`) | Swing (`javax.swing.*`) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Heavyweight — each component has a native peer. | Lightweight — no native peer; drawn by Java. |
| 2 | Components are in `java.awt`. | Components are in `javax.swing`, prefixed with `J`. |
| 3 | Look-and-feel is fixed by the OS. | Pluggable look-and-feel (Metal, Nimbus, System). |
| 4 | Does **not** follow MVC. | Follows **Model-View-Controller**. |
| 5 | Limited drawing support (only `paint(Graphics)`). | Rich 2D drawing via `paintComponent(Graphics)` and `Graphics2D`. |
| 6 | Top-level: `Frame`, `Dialog`, `Applet`. | Top-level: `JFrame`, `JDialog`, `JApplet` (extend AWT peers). |

*(6 marks — 1 for each row)*

**Why is Swing called lightweight?** *(1 mark)*

Because Swing components do **not** allocate a corresponding native OS widget. They are rendered entirely by Java's 2D graphics engine using the *peer* of a *single* shared AWT top-level container. Hence they consume less memory and offer more flexibility (pluggable look-and-feel).

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

The **Event Delegation Model (EDM)** is the modern AWT/Swing event-handling architecture in which event-handling logic is *delegated* from the event source to a separate listener object.

**Participants:**

1. **Event Source** — The GUI component (e.g., `Button`) that fires events. It maintains a list of registered listeners.
2. **Event Class** — An `AWTEvent` subclass (e.g., `ActionEvent`, `MouseEvent`) that encapsulates the event's data.
3. **Listener Interface** — A Java interface (e.g., `ActionListener`) defining the callback method.
4. **Listener Object** — A class that implements the listener interface and provides the actual event-handling code.

**Flow Diagram:**

```mermaid
graph LR
    srcY["Event Source (Button)"] --> evtY["Event Object (ActionEvent)"]
    evtY --> lY["Listener Object"]
    srcY -. "addActionListener" .-> lY
    lY --> cbY["Callback Method (actionPerformed)"]
```

*(2 marks for diagram + 2 marks for participant explanation)*

**Difference from the older inheritance-based model:**

| Aspect | Inheritance Model (AWT 1.0) | Delegation Model (AWT 1.1+) |
|---|---|---|
| Where logic lives | In a subclass that overrides `handleEvent`. | In a separate listener class. |
| Coupling | Tightly coupled — one class per event type. | Loosely coupled — any class can listen. |
| Extensibility | Must subclass the component. | Can add listeners to any component. |
| Multiple events | Inefficient — single `handleEvent`. | Clean — separate interfaces per event type. |

*(3 marks for comparison table)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Question B:**
> 1. **Writing only 4 points in the AWT vs Swing table** instead of 6 → **−2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting to draw the diagram** for the Event Delegation Model → **−2 marks** (diagrams are mandatory in 7-mark sub-questions).
> 3. **Calling Swing "platform-dependent"** — this is the *opposite* of the truth. Swing is **platform-independent at the rendering level** (Java paints it). Lose **1 mark**.
> 4. **Confusing `AWTEvent` with `EventObject`** — `EventObject` is the JDK utility superclass; `AWTEvent` is the AWT-specific subclass. Mentioning both shows depth (+0.5 bonus mark per examiner's discretion).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4 / AWT Overview**

- **AWT** lives in `java.awt`; **Swing** lives in `javax.swing`. *(High-frequency 2-mark question.)*
- AWT components are **heavyweight** (native peers). Swing components are **lightweight** (Java-painted).
- The root AWT class is `java.awt.Component`; the root Swing class is `javax.swing.JComponent`.
- Top-level AWT containers: `Frame`, `Dialog`, `Window`, `Applet`, `Panel`.
- Swing adds a `J` prefix to AWT names: `JButton`, `JFrame`, `JDialog`, `JApplet`, `JPanel`.
- **Event Delegation Model** has three participants: **Source, Event, Listener**.
- All AWT/Swing events are dispatched on the **Event Dispatch Thread (EDT)** — never run long tasks on it.
- The **default layout** of `Frame` is `BorderLayout`; of `Panel` is `FlowLayout`.
- Swing supports **pluggable Look-and-Feel** via `UIManager.setLookAndFeel(...)`.
- Swing follows the **MVC architecture**; AWT does not.
- The `paint(Graphics g)` method is called by the EDT to render components; never call it directly.
- `setVisible(true)` triggers **lazy native peer creation** for AWT components.
- The Event Delegation Model **replaced** the older inheritance-based model in AWT 1.1 (JDK 1.1).
- AWT classes are still required in Swing to obtain top-level windows — Swing is built **on top of** AWT, not a replacement.
- `java.awt.Robot` is an AWT utility for simulating keyboard/mouse input — a common viva question.

> **End of Module 4 — AWT Overview Notes. Best of luck for your KTU 2024 ESE!**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Swing vs AWT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECIT633 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Swing vs AWT # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Abstract Window Toolkit (AWT)** is Java's original, platform-dependent GUI (Graphical User Interface) framework introduced in JDK 1.0 (1996). It provides a thin abstraction layer over the underlying Operating System's native windowing system, meaning every AWT component (e.g., `Button`, `TextField`) is a peer-based **heavyweight** component that directly maps to a native OS widget via the *Peer* class architecture. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Swing** is Java's second-generation, lightweight GUI toolkit introduced as part of the Java Foundation Classes (JFC) in JDK 1.2 (1998). Swing is built entirely in **pure Java** (package `javax.swing`) and is a direct extension of the AWT architecture. It replaces native peer components with **lightweight** components (prefixed with the letter `J`, e.g., `JButton`, `JTextField`) painted directly on the screen by Java code, making Swing **platform-independent** and offering **Pluggable Look and Feel (PLAF)**. ### Course Outcome (CO) Mapping As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme OBE Framework**, this topic maps to: - **CO4:** *Apply Object Oriented Programming concepts to develop GUI-based applications using AWT and Swing.* (K3 — Apply / K4 — Analyze) --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Building Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Imagine you are building a house (your GUI application):** > > - **AWT** is like hiring **local contractors** from every country (Windows, macOS, Linux). Each contractor uses their own local materials, tools, and styles. The house *looks native* to the country, but the **construction quality, materials, and behaviour are inconsistent** across platforms. If a contractor is unavailable, the house cannot be built. > > - **Swing** is like a **self-contained, prefabricated building kit** shipped from the Java factory. Every brick, window (`JButton`, `JLabel`), and door (`JTextField`) is built identically in Java. The house may not always look "native" to the country, but the Java team can **paint it any way they want** (Pluggable Look and Feel), making it look like Windows, macOS, or even a custom theme — all rendered by Java's own painters. The result is **predictable, consistent, and customizable**. --- ## 1.3 Foundational Class Hierarchy | Framework | Root Class (Component) | Top-Level Container | Event Package | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **AWT** | `java.awt.Component` | `java.awt.Frame` | `java.awt.event` | | **Swing** | `javax.swing.JComponent` (extends `java.awt.Container`) | `javax.swing.JFrame` | `javax.swing.event` | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Rule:** Swing is **NOT** a replacement for AWT — it is a **superset**. Every Swing application still uses AWT's `LayoutManager`, `Color`, `Font`, `Graphics`, and event-delegation model underneath. You cannot build a Swing application without AWT on the classpath. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Breakdown: The "Why" Behind the Two Frameworks ### 2.1.1 AWT — The Peer-Based Heavyweight Model 1. **Why was AWT created?** — To give Java an immediate, cross-language GUI before the industry standardized on a pure-Java solution. 2. **How does it work?** — When you instantiate `new Button("OK")`, the Java Virtual Machine (JVM) creates a **peer object** (a hidden native handle) via `Toolkit.getDefaultToolkit().createButton()`. The actual pixels, clicks, and rendering happen in **native C/C++ code** supplied by the OS. 3. **The Core Weakness** — Because every AWT component depends on an **OS-native equivalent**, only the **smallest common set** of widgets is exposed (the *least common denominator*). Features like trees, tables, tabbed panes, and styled text simply **do not exist** in AWT. 4. **Performance Reality** — Native rendering is fast on its own OS, but cross-platform testing becomes a nightmare. On a system missing the native peer, the component simply **throws a runtime error** (`java.awt.AWTError: Cannot load AWT toolkit: win32com.dll`). ### 2.1.2 Swing — The Lightweight Model 1. **Why was Swing created?** — To overcome AWT's limitations: provide a **rich, consistent, customizable** component set without OS dependencies. 2. **How does it work?** — Swing components inherit from `JComponent` (which extends `Container`). They have **no peer**. Drawing is performed by overriding the `paintComponent(Graphics g)` method, where `Graphics` is a Java abstraction that delegates to the native `Graphics2D` engine. 3. **The Core Strength** — A Swing `JButton` looks, behaves, and renders **identically** on Windows, macOS, and Linux — and can be re-skinned dynamically using `UIManager.setLookAndFeel()`. 4. **Trade-off** — Slight CPU overhead for painting, but vastly superior portability and feature set. --- ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Comparison Table (The "Cheat Sheet") > [!IMPORTANT] > This table is the **single most important deliverable** for KTU exams. Memorizing the contrasting properties earns you 3 to 5 marks instantly. | Property | AWT (Abstract Window Toolkit) | Swing | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Package** | `java.awt` | `javax.swing` | | **Introduced In** | JDK 1.0 (1996) | JDK 1.2 (1998), part of JFC | | **Component Prefix** | `Button`, `TextField`, `Label` | `JButton`, `JTextField`, `JLabel` | | **Component Weight** | **Heavyweight** (relies on OS peer) | **Lightweight** (no native peer) | | **Platform Dependency** | Platform-dependent (native rendering) | **Platform-independent** (pure Java) | | **Look and Feel** | Fixed — mimics host OS | **Pluggable** (Metal, Nimbus, Windows, Motif) | | **MVC Architecture** | Not strictly followed | **Strictly follows MVC** (`ButtonModel`) | | **Component Count** | Limited (~ 20 components) | **Rich** (~ 50+ components, e.g., `JTree`, `JTable`, `JTabbedPane`) | | **Pluggable Event Handlers** | Limited | **Pluggable** event model (`EventListenerList`) | | **Threading Model** | Not thread-safe by default | **Thread-safe** (uses `invokeLater`, `SwingUtilities`) | | **Performance** | Faster on host OS | Slightly slower (Java-rendered), but consistent | | **Z-Ordering Bug** | Affected (Heavy/Lightweight mix) | None (homogeneous lightweight) | | **Root Class** | `java.awt.Component` | `javax.swing.JComponent` (extends `Container`) | | **Top-Level Containers** | `Frame`, `Dialog`, `Applet` | `JFrame`, `JDialog`, `JApplet` | | **Painting Control** | `update()` and `paint()` (overlap risk) | **Double-buffered** + `paintComponent()` | | **Icon Support** | Basic | **Rich** — disabled icons, rollover, pressed icons | | **Accessibility** | Limited | **Built-in** (`Accessible` interface) | | **Dependency on AWT** | Independent (the foundation) | **Depends on AWT** (extends its architecture) | --- ## 2.3 The Pluggable Look and Feel (PLAF) — Swing's Crown Jewel > [!NOTE] > **What is PLAF?** It is Swing's ability to dynamically swap the entire visual skin of an application at runtime using a single static call: `UIManager.setLookAndFeel(className)`. Available built-in L&Fs include: > - `javax.swing.plaf.metal.MetalLookAndFeel` (default, cross-platform) > - `com.sun.java.swing.plaf.nimbus.NimbusLookAndFeel` (modern, recommended) > - `com.sun.java.swing.plaf.windows.WindowsLookAndFeel` (Windows native) > - `com.sun.java.swing.plaf.motif.MotifLookAndFeel` (legacy UNIX) > - `com.sun.java.swing.plaf.macOS.MacOSLookAndFeel` (macOS native) **Why this matters in real engineering:** Enterprise applications (banking, ERP) ship with their own **custom L&F** to enforce brand consistency across all client operating systems. This is **impossible with AWT**. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 The MVC Architecture Derivation in Swing > [!NOTE] > Swing is one of the **earliest large-scale Java implementations** of the **Model-View-Controller (MVC)** design pattern. The KTU examiner frequently asks you to *derive* how MVC is applied to a Swing component. Below is the exhaustive step-by-step breakdown using `JButton` as the canonical example. ### Step 1 — Identify the Three MVC Roles The pattern mandates that the **data (Model)**, the **display (View)**, and the **input logic (Controller)** must be three independent, loosely-coupled objects. ### Step 2 — Map the Roles to Swing's `JButton` Internals - **Model** → `javax.swing.DefaultButtonModel` (implements `ButtonModel`). Holds state: `isArmed()`, `isPressed()`, `isSelected()`, `isEnabled()`, label, mnemonic, icon, action command. - **View** → `javax.swing.plaf.basic.BasicButtonUI` (or platform-specific `ButtonUI` subclass). Handles the `paint(...)` cycle and the visual representation. - **Controller** → `javax.swing.plaf.basic.BasicButtonListener` (an `ActionListener`, `ChangeListener`, `ItemListener`). Translates mouse/keyboard events into Model state changes. ### Step 3 — Wire the Three Together When you write `JButton b = new JButton("OK")`, the constructor calls `init()` which: 1. Creates a `DefaultButtonModel`. 2. Installs the `BasicButtonUI` (View). 3. Attaches the `BasicButtonListener` (Controller) to the Model. The Controller updates the Model; the Model fires `ChangeEvent`s; the View repaints itself in response. **The developer code (`ActionListener` attached via `b.addActionListener(...)`) is a fourth, application-level Controller that only responds to semantic "click" events emitted by the built-in Controller.** ### Step 4 — The Benefit Derived Because Model and View are decoupled, you can **swap the UI** (the L&F) without touching the data or the application logic, and you can **query the model state** for unit tests without rendering the GUI. This is the *exact reason* Swing supports PLAF cleanly while AWT does not. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — When to Choose What (KTU Engineering Decision) | Scenario | Recommended Framework | Justification | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Building a new Java desktop application (2024+) | **Swing** | Rich components, PLAF, MVC, thread safety. | | Maintaining legacy code written pre-JDK 1.2 | **AWT** | Refactoring is expensive; behaviour is well-known. | | Targeting Applets (browser-based) | **Both** (mostly `JApplet`) | Note: Applets are deprecated in JDK 11+. | | Resource-constrained embedded systems | **AWT** | Lower memory footprint (no `JComponent` classes loaded). | | Cross-platform consistency is mandatory | **Swing** | AWT will render differently on different OSes. | | Needing advanced widgets (Trees, Tables, Tabbed Panes) | **Swing** | AWT simply does not provide them. | | Needing full native OS integration (e.g., system tray in older apps) | **AWT** | Some native features existed before Swing caught up. | --- ## 3.3 Event Delegation Model — The Common Foundation > [!IMPORTANT] > Both AWT and Swing use the **Event Delegation Model (EDM)**, but Swing **enhances** it with an `EventListenerList` for memory-efficient multi-listener support. The event flow is: 1. **Source** (e.g., `JButton`) generates an `ActionEvent` when clicked. 2. The source's `EventListenerList` is iterated. 3. Each registered `ActionListener.actionPerformed()` is invoked in the order of registration. 4. Exceptions thrown in one listener **do not break** the dispatch chain (added robustness in Swing). The formula for listener invocation can be expressed as: $$\text{Dispatch}(s, e) = \bigotimes_{i=1}^{n} L_i.\text{actionPerformed}(e)$$ where $s$ is the event source, $e$ is the event, and $L_i$ is the $i$-th registered listener from the `EventListenerList`. The $\bigotimes$ operator denotes *sequential, fault-isolated* invocation — a listener throwing an exception does not abort subsequent listeners. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 AWT vs Swing — Class Hierarchy Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[java.lang.Object] --> B[java.awt.Component] A --> C[java.awt.MenuComponent] B --> D[java.awt.Container] D --> E[java.awt.Window] D --> F[java.awt.Panel] E --> G[java.awt.Frame] E --> H[java.awt.Dialog] F --> I[java.applet.Applet] B --> J[java.awt.Button] B --> K[java.awt.TextField] B --> L[java.awt.Label] B --> M[java.awt.Checkbox] B --> N[java.awt.Choice] B --> O[java.awt.List] B --> P[java.awt.Canvas] G -.extends.-> Q[java.awt.MenuBar] D --> R[java.awt.Container Extended by Swing] R --> S[javax.swing.JComponent] S --> T[javax.swing.AbstractButton] T --> U[JButton] T --> V[JToggleButton] T --> W[JMenuItem] T --> X[JCheckBoxMenuItem] T --> Y[JRadioButtonMenuItem] S --> Z[JLabel] S --> AA[JTextField] S --> AB[JTextArea] S --> AC[JScrollPane] S --> AD[JPanel] S --> AE[JTable] S --> AF[JTree] S --> AG[JTabbedPane] S --> AH[JSlider] S --> AI[JProgressBar] E --> AJ[java.awt.Window Extended by Swing] AJ --> AK[javax.swing.JWindow] G --> AL[javax.swing.JFrame] H --> AM[javax.swing.JDialog] I --> AN[javax.swing.JApplet] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Read this diagram top-down:** The `JComponent` branch does **not** replace the AWT branch — it sits *parallel* and *on top of* it. `JComponent` ultimately extends `java.awt.Container`, proving that **Swing is built upon AWT, not independent of it.** --- ## 4.2 MVC Decomposition Inside a Swing `JButton` ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SwingJButton["JButton (Facade)"] direction LR M[DefaultButtonModel
STATE: armed, pressed, enabled,
label, icon, mnemonic] V[BasicButtonUI
RENDERING: paint, paintComponent,
icon composition, border] C[BasicButtonListener
INPUT MAP: mousePressed, mouseReleased,
keyPressed, focus changes] end User([User Mouse / Keyboard]) -- raw events --> C C -- mutates state --> M M -- fires ChangeEvent --> V V -- requests repaint --> SwingJButton M -- fires ActionEvent --> AppAL[Application ActionListener] AppAL -- business logic --> AppCode[Your Code: open dialog, save file, etc.] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Notice the separation:** Your application code never touches the View or the Model directly. It only listens for high-level `ActionEvent`s. This is the textbook MVC contract in action. --- ## 4.3 Heavyweight vs Lightweight Rendering — Peer Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph HWAWT["AWT Heavyweight Path"] direction TB A1[Java Code: new Button] A2[Toolkit.createButton] A3[Native Peer: win32 Button / Cocoa NSButton] A4[OS Window Manager Draws Pixels] A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 end subgraph HWSwing["Swing Lightweight Path"] direction TB S1[Java Code: new JButton] S2[JComponent.paintComponent] S3[Java Graphics2D / Java2D] S4[Swing paints directly into a JRootPane-owned
native heavyweight window] S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 end OS[Operating System Native Window] --> A4 OS --> S4 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key insight:** Even Swing's lightweight components need **one** native heavyweight window (the `JFrame` or `JApplet`) owned by the OS to receive paint and event messages from the window manager. That single root is the *only* place where Swing touches the native layer. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) > `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]` ### **Q1. Differentiate between Heavyweight and Lightweight components in Java AWT/Swing. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** A **heavyweight component** (e.g., `java.awt.Button`) relies on the underlying operating system to provide its visual representation through a *peer object* created by the native toolkit. Its rendering and event handling depend on the host platform, making it slower to render changes and causing *Z-ordering issues* when mixed with lightweight peers. In contrast, a **lightweight component** (e.g., `javax.swing.JButton`) has **no native peer**; it is drawn entirely by Java code inside its parent container's window. This makes lightweight components platform-independent, faster to repaint, and free from Z-ordering conflicts, but it requires the parent to be a heavyweight (a `JFrame`) to be visible on screen. *(3 marks)* --- ### **Q2. What is Pluggable Look and Feel (PLAF) in Swing? Name any two built-in L&Fs. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** Pluggable Look and Feel is a Swing architecture feature that allows the entire visual appearance of a Java GUI application to be **changed at runtime** without modifying the application code. It is implemented via the `javax.swing.UIManager.setLookAndFeel(String className)` static method and is possible because Swing components strictly separate their **Model** (data) from their **View** (rendering) — the View can be swapped out at will. Two built-in L&Fs are: 1. `javax.swing.plaf.metal.MetalLookAndFeel` (default cross-platform theme). 2. `com.sun.java.swing.plaf.windows.WindowsLookAndFeel` (mimics Microsoft Windows). *(3 marks)* --- ## 📗 PART B — Full-Question Choices (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > `[KTU University Exam - Model Question Bank, Module 4]` --- ### ✅ CHOICE A — Deep Comparison with Code-Architecture Analysis > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]** **Question A (a): Compare AWT and Swing in detail across at least 8 technical parameters. Explain why Swing is considered a strict superset of AWT. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** | # | Parameter | AWT | Swing | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | **Component Prefix** | `Button`, `Label`, `TextField` | `JButton`, `JLabel`, `JTextField` | | 2 | **Peer Architecture** | Heavyweight — every component has a native OS peer | Lightweight — no native peer, drawn by Java | | 3 | **Look and Feel** | Fixed, mimics host OS | Pluggable (Metal, Nimbus, Windows, Motif) | | 4 | **MVC Compliance** | Partial | Strict — `JButton` separates `ButtonModel` and `ButtonUI` | | 5 | **Component Variety** | ~ 20 basic components | 50+ components (trees, tables, tabbed panes) | | 6 | **Threading** | Not thread-safe by default | Thread-safe via `SwingUtilities.invokeLater()` | | 7 | **Z-Ordering Bug** | Affects mixed heavy/light components | None — all Swing components are lightweight | | 8 | **Dependency** | Independent | **Depends on AWT** (extends `java.awt.Container`) | **Why Swing is a Superset of AWT:** Swing **inherits** from AWT's class hierarchy — `JComponent` extends `java.awt.Container`, which extends `java.awt.Component`. Swing **reuses** AWT's `LayoutManager`, `Color`, `Font`, `Graphics`, `Image`, and the entire **Event Delegation Model**. Without `java.awt` on the classpath, **no Swing program will compile or run**. Therefore, every Swing application is also an AWT application (it has top-level `JFrame`s which are heavyweight AWT descendants), making Swing a *superset*, not a replacement. *(7 marks)* --- **Question A (b): With a neat block diagram, explain the MVC architecture of a Swing `JButton`. Show how the Model, View, and Controller interact during a click event. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** The `JButton` in Swing is a canonical implementation of the **Model-View-Controller (MVC)** pattern. 1. **Model — `DefaultButtonModel`:** Stores the state of the button — `armed`, `pressed`, `enabled`, `rollover`, label string, icon, and mnemonic. It implements the `ButtonModel` interface and fires `ChangeEvent`s and `ActionEvent`s when its state changes. 2. **View — `ButtonUI` (typically `BasicButtonUI`):** Responsible solely for rendering. It overrides `paintComponent(Graphics g)` to draw the icon, label, border, and focus indicator. The View never modifies state — it only reads from the Model. 3. **Controller — `BasicButtonListener`:** Installed automatically by `JButton.init()`. It listens to low-level `MouseEvent`s and `KeyEvent`s from the AWT event queue, then translates them into Model mutations (e.g., `setArmed(true)` on mouse press, `setPressed(true)` on release). **Click Event Interaction (Step-by-Step):** 1. The user clicks the mouse. A native `MouseEvent` arrives at the `JButton` from the OS. 2. The Controller (`BasicButtonListener`) catches the event and calls `model.setArmed(true)`, then `model.setPressed(true)`. 3. The Model fires a `ChangeEvent` to all registered `ChangeListener`s, including the View. 4. The View (`BasicButtonUI`) receives the change, invalidates its bounds, and `JComponent.paintComponent()` repaints the button to reflect the pressed state. 5. On mouse release, the Controller calls `model.setPressed(false)`, and the Model fires an `ActionEvent` to all `ActionListener`s. 6. The application's `ActionListener.actionPerformed(e)` method executes the business logic (e.g., opening a dialog). The decoupling ensures the button can be re-skinned (`UIManager.setLookAndFeel(...)`) without altering the application code. *(7 marks)* --- ### ✅ CHOICE B — Conceptual Deep-Dive with Pluggable L&F > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Retest Paper]** **Question B (a): Explain the event delegation model in Swing. How does Swing's `EventListenerList` improve upon AWT's listener model? (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Event Delegation Model (EDM):** Both AWT and Swing use EDM, where the **event source** (e.g., `JButton`) maintains a list of registered **listeners** (e.g., `ActionListener`) and delegates the event to them when the event occurs. The pattern has three participants: *Source* (the object generating events), *Event* (the object carrying data, e.g., `ActionEvent`), and *Listener* (the object implementing the callback interface, e.g., `ActionListener`). **AWT's Limitation:** AWT components store listeners in **typed arrays** (e.g., `ActionListener[] actionListenerList`). This has three drawbacks: 1. Each new event type requires a new typed array, consuming memory even if no listener is registered. 2. Reflection is required to add/remove listeners dynamically, hurting performance. 3. If a listener throws an exception, the dispatch chain for **subsequent listeners is broken** (in older JDKs). **Swing's `EventListenerList` Improvement:** Swing replaces typed arrays with a single generic `Object[]` of `EventListener` pairs (listener-class, listener-instance). The benefits are: 1. **Memory efficiency:** Only one array is allocated per component, regardless of how many listener types are supported. 2. **Type safety preserved:** A custom helper (`getListeners(Class type)`) casts entries at lookup time. 3. **Fault isolation:** A thrown exception in one listener is caught and logged, and the next listener is still invoked, ensuring robustness. 4. **Uniform API:** Adding/removing any listener type (`addActionListener`, `addKeyListener`, etc.) routes through the same internal method, simplifying Swing's source code. This makes Swing's event handling **more scalable** for complex GUIs that register dozens of listener types. *(7 marks)* --- **Question B (b): Write short notes on: (i) Threading in Swing, (ii) `JComponent` vs `Component`. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **(i) Threading in Swing (3.5 marks):** Swing components are **not thread-safe** for arbitrary thread access, but Swing enforces a single rule: **all interaction with the GUI must happen on the Event Dispatch Thread (EDT)**. The EDT is a single, dedicated thread that processes paint events, input events, and component-modification calls. Long-running background tasks (e.g., database queries, file I/O) must be offloaded to a separate worker thread (e.g., `SwingWorker`) to prevent the GUI from freezing. The standard idiom for safely updating the GUI from a background thread is: ```java SwingUtilities.invokeLater(new Runnable() { public void run() { statusLabel.setText("Data loaded"); } }); ``` `invokeLater` queues the Runnable on the EDT, which executes it after all pending events are processed. `invokeAndWait` is the blocking variant. AWT, by contrast, has no equivalent discipline, leading to subtle race conditions and repaint glitches. **(ii) `JComponent` vs `Component` (3.5 marks):** - `java.awt.Component` is the **root class of the AWT hierarchy**. It provides basic properties like position (`x`, `y`), size (`width`, `height`), foreground/background colors, font, and the abstract `paint(Graphics g)` method. It is the superclass of both AWT and Swing components. - `javax.swing.JComponent` extends `java.awt.Container` (which itself extends `Component`) and is the **root class of all Swing components**. It adds: 1. **Pluggable border architecture** (`setBorder(Border b)`). 2. **Tooltips** (`setToolTipText(String text)`). 3. **Double buffering** by default (via `setDoubleBuffered(true)`). 4. **The `paintComponent(Graphics g)` template method** — overriding this (instead of `paint`) is the recommended Swing painting pattern. 5. **Keyboard binding API** (`InputMap` and `ActionMap` for stroke-to-action mapping). 6. **Accessible** support (built-in screen-reader hooks). 7. **Client properties** — a `Map` for storing custom component data. In short, `JComponent` is a **superset** of `Component`, adding every feature needed for a modern, accessible, skinnable GUI that pure AWT could not deliver. *(7 marks)* --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common places where students lose marks on Swing vs AWT questions:** > > 1. **Stating Swing replaces AWT (WRONG).** It *extends* AWT. Saying "Swing replaces AWT" will cost you 1–2 marks instantly. Always say: *"Swing is a superset of AWT, built on top of the `java.awt` package."* > > 2. **Forgetting the "J" prefix when listing Swing components.** AWT has `Button`; Swing has **`JButton`**. Mixing them up (e.g., writing `Button` in a Swing context) shows incomplete understanding — penalised heavily. > > 3. **Confusing `paint()` with `paintComponent()`.** Overriding `paint()` in Swing is a classic mistake that breaks double-buffering and causes flicker. Always override **`paintComponent(Graphics g)`** in Swing, and call `super.paintComponent(g)` as the first line. > > 4. **Omitting the EDT rule in threading questions.** If asked about Swing threading and you don't mention the **Event Dispatch Thread** and `SwingUtilities.invokeLater()`, you will lose 2–3 marks. > > 5. **Writing "Swing is platform-dependent" or "AWT is pure Java" — these are the REVERSE of the truth.** Memorize the table above. > > 6. **Ignoring the `MVC` aspect in `JButton` questions.** Simply saying "JButton has a UI" without naming `DefaultButtonModel`, `ButtonUI`, and `BasicButtonListener` will earn you only 2 of 7 marks. --- ## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Read this 5 minutes before the exam.** - [x] **AWT** = `java.awt` package, **heavyweight** (native peers), platform-dependent, fixed L&F, ~ 20 components. - [x] **Swing** = `javax.swing` package, **lightweight** (no peers), platform-independent, **Pluggable L&F**, 50+ components. - [x] **Swing IS NOT a replacement** — it is a **superset** that **extends AWT** (`JComponent` → `Container` → `Component`). - [x] **All Swing components start with "J"** — `JButton`, `JLabel`, `JFrame`, `JPanel`, `JTextField`, etc. - [x] **MVC in `JButton`** — Model: `DefaultButtonModel`, View: `BasicButtonUI`, Controller: `BasicButtonListener`. - [x] **PLAF** — `UIManager.setLookAndFeel(className)` swaps the entire visual skin at runtime. - [x] **Threading** — All GUI updates must happen on the **Event Dispatch Thread (EDT)** using `SwingUtilities.invokeLater()`. - [x] **Painting** — Override `paintComponent(Graphics g)`, **never** `paint()` (breaks double buffering). - [x] **Swing depends on AWT** for layout managers, `Color`, `Font`, `Graphics`, and event delivery. - [x] **Event handling** — Swing uses `EventListenerList` for memory-efficient, fault-isolated dispatch. - [x] **Top-level containers in Swing** — `JFrame`, `JDialog`, `JApplet` (only these can be heavyweight roots). - [x] **Applets are deprecated** in JDK 11+ — KTU may still ask; answer based on historical context. - [x] **Common components unique to Swing** — `JTree`, `JTable`, `JTabbedPane`, `JMenuBar`, `JSlider`, `JProgressBar`.

topics.title: Swing Key Features
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT633 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Swing Key Features

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned):**
> **Swing** is the primary Java GUI (Graphical User Interface) widget toolkit that is part of Oracle's Java Foundation Classes (JFC). It provides a richer set of **lightweight, pluggable look-and-feel** components that extend the capabilities of the older AWT (Abstract Window Toolkit). Swing components are written entirely in Java (except for the top-level containers like `JWindow`, `JFrame`, `JDialog`, and `JApplet` which rely on native peers) and follow the **MVC (Model-View-Controller)** architecture.

The **Key Features of Swing** are a set of architectural and functional advantages that distinguish Swing from its predecessor, AWT. These features collectively make Swing a robust, portable, and highly customizable GUI framework. The KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to enumerate and explain at least **5 to 7 key features** in Part A questions and provide a comparative study with AWT in Part B questions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Modular Kitchen Cabinet" 🍳**
> Imagine you are building a kitchen.
> - **AWT** is like a **fixed, built-in kitchen** made of bricks and marble. It looks and behaves according to the OS on which it is built. If you change the house (Windows → Linux → Mac), the kitchen changes shape and behavior. You cannot move a stove from one slot to another.
> - **Swing** is like a **modular, flat-pack kitchen** (think IKEA). Every cabinet, drawer, and shelf is self-contained, portable, and identical regardless of the house (OS) it is placed in. You can **change the look** (oak finish, white gloss, metallic) using a single switch — this is the **Pluggable Look And Feel (PLAF)**. You can also easily rearrange the components.

**Core Takeaway:** Swing gives you **"Write Once, Run Anywhere with Any Look"** — a level of control and uniformity that AWT simply cannot provide.

## 1.3 Why Swing Was Introduced — The Limitations of AWT

| # | AWT Limitation | Swing Solution |
|---|----------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Heavyweight** components (one peer per OS) | **Lightweight** components (mostly no native peers) |
| 2 | **Fixed Look & Feel** tied to OS | **Pluggable Look & Feel (PLAF)** |
| 3 | **Limited component set** (~6 basic widgets) | **Rich component set** (~40+ widgets) |
| 4 | **No MVC separation** | **MVC architecture** cleanly separates data from UI |
| 5 | **No double buffering** by default (flickering) | **Built-in double buffering** eliminates flicker |
| 6 | Platform-dependent rendering | **100% Pure Java** rendering (except top-level containers) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric to Remember:**
> Swing contains approximately **40+ components** compared to AWT's mere **~6 peers**. This figure is commonly asked as a **2-mark short question** in KTU exams.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Sheet

## 2.1 The 7 Key Features of Swing — Structured Logic Breakdown

### **Feature 1: Lightweight Components** ⚖️

- **What:** Most Swing components (prefixed with the letter `J`, e.g., `JButton`, `JLabel`, `JTextField`) do **not** rely on the underlying Operating System's native peer objects for rendering. They are drawn directly onto a canvas using pure Java 2D API calls.
- **Why:** Eliminates platform-dependent bugs and inconsistencies. A `JButton` looks **identical** in shape and behavior on Windows, Linux, and macOS.
- **How:** They inherit from `javax.swing.JComponent`, which extends `java.awt.Container`. The drawing is handled by the `paintComponent(Graphics g)` method.
- **Exception:** Top-level containers (`JFrame`, `JDialog`, `JWindow`, `JApplet`) **must** be heavyweight because they are the root windows of the OS.

### **Feature 2: Pluggable Look And Feel (PLAF)** 🎨

- **What:** The same Swing component can dynamically change its visual appearance at runtime.
- **Why:** Allows applications to mimic native OS look (for familiarity) OR adopt a custom look (for branding).
- **How:** The `UIManager.setLookAndFeel()` static method swaps the rendering delegate.
- **Available L&F options:**
  - `Metal` (default, cross-platform — formerly known as *Ocean*)
  - `Nimbus` (modern default since Java SE 7)
  - `Windows` (mimics Windows OS)
  - `Motif` (mimics old UNIX)
  - `GTK` (mimics Linux GNOME)

```java
// Code pattern for KTU exams (read-only, no execution required)
UIManager.setLookAndFeel("javax.swing.plaf.nimbus.NimbusLookAndFeel");
```

### **Feature 3: MVC Architecture** 🏗️

- **What:** Swing separates the component into three logical parts:
  - **Model:** Stores the data / state (e.g., `ButtonModel` for a button's pressed/unpressed state).
  - **View:** Renders the visual representation (`BasicButtonUI`).
  - **Controller:** Handles user input events (`ButtonUI` listens to MouseEvents / KeyEvents).
- **Why:** A change in data automatically updates the view, and vice versa. Multiple views can share a single model.
- **How:** Each Swing component has a corresponding **UI Delegate** object (e.g., `ButtonUI`, `LabelUI`) that implements the view and controller responsibilities.

### **Feature 4: Built-in Double Buffering** 🖥️

- **What:** Swing automatically uses a **double-buffering** painting strategy.
- **Why:** Eliminates **flickering** during repaints (a notorious AWT problem). It also reduces CPU load.
- **How:** Drawing operations are first performed on an off-screen **buffer image**. Once the entire frame is complete, the buffer is rapidly copied to the screen in a single pass.
- **Bonus:** Triple buffering can be enabled via the system property `awt.image.bufferedimage.num`.

### **Feature 5: Rich Set of Components (Expanded Widget Library)** 📦

- **What:** Swing provides a vastly expanded set of UI components beyond AWT.
- **Why:** Modern applications require complex widgets — trees, tabbed panes, tables, sliders, spinners, etc.
- **Key new components:**
  - `JTable` — display data in rows and columns
  - `JTree` — display hierarchical data
  - `JTabbedPane` — notebook-style tabs
  - `JSlider`, `JSpinner` — bounded input
  - `JMenuBar`, `JPopupMenu`, `JToolBar` — advanced menus
  - `JProgressBar` — visual progress indicator
  - `JSplitPane` — resizable split view

### **Feature 6: Customizable and Extensible** 🔧

- **What:** Developers can subclass existing Swing components to override `paintComponent()`, install custom UI delegates, or use the `Component.setUI()` method.
- **Why:** Off-the-shelf components never satisfy all UI needs. Extensibility is critical for branded enterprise apps.
- **How:** Override the `paintComponent(Graphics g)` method, call `super.paintComponent(g)` first, then draw custom graphics using `Graphics2D`.

### **Feature 7: Support for Accessibility (Java Accessibility API)** ♿

- **What:** Swing integrates with the **Java Accessibility API** (`javax.accessibility`), enabling screen readers, Braille displays, and other assistive technologies.
- **Why:** Legal compliance (e.g., Section 508) and inclusive design.
- **How:** Each component provides `AccessibleContext` automatically. Developers can set `setAccessibleName()` and `setAccessibleDescription()` for screen readers.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Comparison Sheet

| # | Property | AWT | Swing |
|---|----------|-----|-------|
| 1 | Package | `java.awt` | `javax.swing` |
| 2 | Component count | ~6 peers | **40+ components** |
| 3 | Weight | **Heavyweight** (uses OS peers) | **Lightweight** (pure Java, mostly) |
| 4 | Look & Feel | Fixed (OS dependent) | **Pluggable (PLAF)** |
| 5 | Architecture | No MVC | **MVC based** |
| 6 | Flicker | Yes (no double buffering) | **No (double buffering built-in)** |
| 7 | Portability | Limited | **High** (uniform across OS) |
| 8 | Component prefix | `Button`, `TextField` | `JButton`, `JTextField` |
| 9 | Top-level containers | `Frame`, `Dialog` | `JFrame`, `JDialog` |
| 10 | Rendering | Native OS calls | Java 2D API |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Swing is the foundational GUI layer for many **enterprise-grade applications**:
- **IntelliJ IDEA** (early versions) — JetBrains' IDE was originally built on Swing.
- **NetBeans IDE** — still uses Swing heavily in its RCP platform.
- **Moodle** (LMS used by universities) — uses Swing for desktop client.
- **Financial trading terminals** — where `JTable` renders thousands of rows in real-time.
- **Medical imaging software** — relies on Swing's custom painting for displaying X-rays and MRIs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance:** Swing's MVC architecture directly influenced modern UI frameworks like **JavaFX**, **React (with Flux/Redux)**, and **Angular**. Understanding Swing-MVC is therefore **prerequisite knowledge** for any front-end engineering role.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analysis & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Why Swing Components Are Lightweight — A Step-by-Step Logic Walkthrough

This section breaks down the *how* of Swing's lightweight nature, which is the most commonly asked **7-mark sub-question** in KTU exams.

### **Step 1 — Component Instantiation**

When you write `new JButton("Submit")`, the `JButton` constructor is invoked. Unlike `java.awt.Button` (which would now request the OS to create a native peer widget), `JButton` simply allocates a pure Java object.

```java
JButton btn = new JButton("Submit");
```

**Logic:** No native code is invoked. No OS-specific DLL or `.so` library is loaded. The button exists entirely in JVM heap memory.

### **Step 2 — Inheritance Chain Verification**

Every Swing component extends `javax.swing.JComponent`. The hierarchy is:

```
java.lang.Object
   └── java.awt.Component
        └── java.awt.Container
             └── javax.swing.JComponent
                  └── javax.swing.AbstractButton
                       └── javax.swing.JButton
```

**Logic:** The `Component` class declares a `paint(Graphics g)` method. `JComponent` **overrides** this to call `paintComponent()`, `paintBorder()`, and `paintChildren()` in a specific, deterministic order — independent of the OS.

### **Step 3 — Custom Painting via paintComponent()**

```java
class FancyButton extends JButton {
    @Override
    protected void paintComponent(Graphics g) {
        Graphics2D g2 = (Graphics2D) g;
        g2.setRenderingHint(RenderingHints.KEY_ANTIALIASING,
                            RenderingHints.VALUE_ANTIALIAS_ON);
        g2.setColor(new Color(60, 120, 200));
        g2.fillRoundRect(0, 0, getWidth(), getHeight(), 20, 20);
        super.paintComponent(g);
    }
}
```

**Logic:** The drawing is performed by Java 2D APIs (`fillRoundRect`, `drawString`). These are **platform-independent** because Java 2D has its own internal rasterizer that handles the final pixel blitting.

### **Step 4 — Double Buffering Mechanism**

When `JComponent.paint()` is called, Swing checks a private flag `_doubleBuffered` (default **true**). If true:

1. An off-screen `BufferedImage` matching the component's dimensions is created.
2. All drawing is performed on this buffer's `Graphics2D` context.
3. Once complete, the buffer's pixels are copied to the screen in **one** atomic operation via `VolatileImage`.

**Logic:** This atomic copy prevents the user from seeing a half-drawn frame, eliminating **flicker**.

### **Step 5 — UI Delegate Invocation (MVC Step)**

When painting, Swing looks up the component's UI delegate:

```java
ComponentUI ui = UIManager.getUI(this);  // returns MetalButtonUI, NimbusButtonUI, etc.
ui.update(swingGraphics, this);
```

**Logic:** The UI delegate is the **View** of MVC. It contains all rendering logic. By swapping the delegate (via `setUI()` or `UIManager.setLookAndFeel()`), the **same component object** can render entirely differently — this is the foundation of PLAF.

## 3.2 Feature-to-Benefit Analytical Table

| Key Feature | Implementation Class | Concrete Benefit (Why a Student Should Care) |
|-------------|----------------------|---------------------------------------------|
| Lightweight | `JComponent` | Uniform appearance across platforms — fewer debugging surprises. |
| PLAF | `UIManager`, `LookAndFeel` | One-line code change to re-skin entire application. |
| MVC | `ComponentUI`, Model interfaces | Cleaner code, easier maintenance. |
| Double Buffering | `JComponent._doubleBuffered` field | No flicker → professional-looking UI. |
| Rich Components | `JTree`, `JTable`, `JTabbedPane` | Save 100s of lines of custom code. |
| Extensibility | `paintComponent()` override | Total visual control. |
| Accessibility | `AccessibleContext`, `Accessible` interface | Inclusive design / legal compliance. |

## 3.3 Derivation-Style Logic: From AWT Limitation to Swing Solution

$$
\text{AWT Limitation} \xrightarrow{\text{Java 2D + JComponent}} \text{Swing Solution}
$$

$$
\text{OS-dependent rendering} \xrightarrow{\text{lightweight components}} \text{Platform-independent rendering}
$$

$$
\text{Fixed L\&F} \xrightarrow{\text{UIManager.setLookAndFeel()}} \text{Swappable L\&F (PLAF)}
$$

$$
\text{Single drawing pass (flicker)} \xrightarrow{\text{Double buffer (off-screen image)}} \text{Atomic frame copy (no flicker)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked to "explain how Swing overcomes AWT's limitations," follow this **Problem → Solution** pattern. It guarantees a **7/7** score on any KTU comparison question.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Swing Class Hierarchy (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["java lang Object"] --> B["java awt Component"]
    B --> C["java awt Container"]
    C --> D["javax swing JComponent"]
    D --> E["javax swing AbstractButton"]
    E --> F1["JButton"]
    E --> F2["JToggleButton"]
    E --> F3["JCheckBox"]
    E --> F4["JRadioButton"]
    E --> F5["JMenuItem"]
    D --> G1["JLabel"]
    D --> G2["JTextField"]
    D --> G3["JTextArea"]
    D --> G4["JList"]
    D --> G5["JTable"]
    D --> G6["JTree"]
    D --> G7["JSlider"]
    D --> G8["JProgressBar"]
    D --> G9["JPanel"]
    H1["java awt Window"] --> I1["JWindow"]
    H1 --> I2["JFrame"]
    H1 --> I3["JDialog"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Swing MVC + PLAF

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Model["MODEL — Data and State"]
        M1["ButtonModel stores pressed boolean"]
        M2["ListModel stores items"]
        M3["TableModel stores rows"]
    end

    subgraph View["VIEW — Visual Rendering UI Delegate"]
        V1["ButtonUI paints the button"]
        V2["ListUI paints the list"]
        V3["TableUI paints the table"]
    end

    subgraph Controller["CONTROLLER — Event Handling"]
        C1["MouseListener triggers click"]
        C2["KeyListener triggers key events"]
        C3["ActionListener fires action"]
    end

    subgraph PLAF["PLUGGABLE LOOK AND FEEL"]
        P1["MetalLookAndFeel"]
        P2["NimbusLookAndFeel"]
        P3["WindowsLookAndFeel"]
        P4["GTKLookAndFeel"]
    end

    M1 -- state change --> V1
    M1 -- state change --> V3
    C1 -- click event --> M1
    C3 -- action --> M1
    PLAF -- provides UI class --> V1
    PLAF -- provides UI class --> V2
    PLAF -- provides UI class --> V3
```

## 4.3 Feature-to-Implementation Mapping (Schematic)

```mermaid
graph TD
    SW["Swing Framework"]
    SW --> F1["Feature Lightweight"]
    SW --> F2["Feature PLAF"]
    SW --> F3["Feature MVC"]
    SW --> F4["Feature Double Buffering"]
    SW --> F5["Feature Rich Components"]

    F1 --> F1A["JComponent overrides paint"]
    F2 --> F2A["UIManager setLookAndFeel"]
    F3 --> F3A["ComponentUI is the delegate"]
    F4 --> F4A["RepaintManager handles buffer"]
    F5 --> F5A["JTable JTree JTabbedPane"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip:** When KTU asks "Draw the Swing class hierarchy," the **root** is always `java.lang.Object`, and the most important branch is the one ending in **`JComponent`**. Remember: **anything ending in `J` is a Swing component**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**"What is Pluggable Look And Feel (PLAF) in Swing? Why is it considered an advantage over AWT?"**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Pluggable Look And Feel (PLAF) is a Swing feature that allows the **entire appearance** of a Swing-based GUI application to be changed at runtime without modifying the source code of the components.

It is achieved through the static method **`UIManager.setLookAndFeel(String className)`**, which swaps the **UI Delegate** classes responsible for painting each component.

**Advantages over AWT:**
1. In AWT, the look and feel is fixed by the underlying OS (e.g., buttons look different on Windows vs. Linux). Swing provides **uniformity** while still allowing native mimicry if desired (e.g., `WindowsLookAndFeel`).
2. A single line of code can re-skin the entire application — useful for branding, accessibility, or user preference.
3. Default L&F options: **Metal, Nimbus, Windows, Motif, GTK**.

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**"List any four key features of Java Swing. Briefly explain the concept of lightweight components."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Four key features of Java Swing:**
1. Lightweight Components
2. Pluggable Look And Feel (PLAF)
3. MVC Architecture
4. Double Buffering (Flicker-free rendering)
*(Plus any of: Rich component set, Extensibility, Accessibility support)*

**Lightweight components:** Most Swing components (those prefixed with `J` such as `JButton`, `JLabel`, `JTextField`) do **not depend on the underlying Operating System's native peer** for rendering. They are drawn directly on the screen using the **Java 2D API**, ensuring consistent behavior and appearance across all platforms. Only top-level containers (`JFrame`, `JDialog`, `JWindow`, `JApplet`) are heavyweight because they represent the OS's root window.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — June 2023 / Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the MVC architecture used by Swing components. How does it differ from AWT's approach?**

**Model Answer:**

**Model-View-Controller (MVC)** is a design pattern that Swing uses to separate concerns within a component:

- **Model:** Stores the **state/data** of the component. For example, `ButtonModel` (an interface in `javax.swing`) holds whether a button is pressed, armed, enabled, etc. `ListModel` and `TableModel` store list items and table rows respectively.
- **View:** Responsible for **rendering** the component visually. In Swing, the View is implemented by a **UI Delegate** class (e.g., `BasicButtonUI` for Metal L&F).
- **Controller:** Handles **user input** events (mouse clicks, key presses) and translates them into state changes in the Model. The UI Delegate also acts as the Controller.

**Difference from AWT:**
AWT does **not** follow MVC. In AWT, the component class itself combines data, rendering, and event handling into a single class that delegates to the native peer. This makes AWT components tightly coupled to the OS and difficult to customize. Swing's MVC separation enables **independent swapping** of data, appearance, and behavior — which is the foundation of features like PLAF and rich data models like `DefaultTableModel`.

**Example code structure:**
```java
DefaultTableModel model = new DefaultTableModel(data, columns);
JTable table = new JTable(model);   // Model attached to View
```

**(b) [7 Marks] Explain the concept of double buffering in Swing. How does it eliminate flickering?**

**Model Answer:**

**Double buffering** is a painting technique where drawing operations are performed on an **off-screen image buffer** before being copied to the screen in a single, atomic operation.

**Process:**
1. When `JComponent.paint(Graphics g)` is called, Swing checks the `_doubleBuffered` flag (default: **true**).
2. An off-screen `BufferedImage` matching the component's size is created in memory.
3. All drawing operations (background, border, children, text) are performed on this off-screen buffer's `Graphics2D` object.
4. Once the entire frame is complete, the buffer's pixel data is **atomically copied** to the screen via `Graphics.drawImage()`.

**Why it eliminates flickering:**
Without double buffering (as in older AWT), the screen is updated **incrementally** — the user sees background drawn first, then border, then text. This partial-frame rendering causes the eye to perceive **flicker**, especially during animations or rapid repaints.

With double buffering, the user sees **only the final, complete frame**, because the screen update is instantaneous and atomic. This results in smooth, professional-looking animations.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice): [KTU University Exam — Sample Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks] Compare AWT and Swing across any six parameters. Why did Sun Microsystems introduce Swing despite AWT being available?**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Parameter | AWT | Swing |
|---|-----------|-----|-------|
| 1 | Package | `java.awt` | `javax.swing` |
| 2 | Weight | Heavyweight (native peers) | Lightweight (pure Java, mostly) |
| 3 | Look & Feel | OS-dependent, fixed | **Pluggable (PLAF)** |
| 4 | Components | ~6 basic widgets | **40+ advanced widgets** |
| 5 | Architecture | Monolithic | **MVC based** |
| 6 | Flicker | Yes | **No (double buffering)** |
| 7 | Top-level | `Frame`, `Dialog` | `JFrame`, `JDialog` |

**Why Swing was introduced:**
Sun Microsystems introduced Swing because AWT's dependence on native peers caused **"the lowest common denominator" problem** — AWT components could only use features supported by every OS, severely limiting functionality. Additionally, AWT had no PLAF, no MVC, suffered from flicker, and had only ~6 components. Swing solved all of these by being **100% pure Java** (except top-level containers), providing a **rich component library**, and offering **customizable rendering** through UI delegates.

**(b) [7 Marks] Explain the role of `UIManager` and `LookAndFeel` classes in implementing PLAF. Write the steps to change the L&F of an application at runtime.**

**Model Answer:**

**Role of `UIManager`:**
`javax.swing.UIManager` is a central repository that maintains a mapping between Swing component classes (e.g., `JButton.class`) and their corresponding **UI Delegate** classes (e.g., `MetalButtonUI`, `NimbusButtonUI`). It also stores default property values (fonts, colors) used by the delegates.

**Role of `LookAndFeel`:**
`javax.swing.LookAndFeel` is an **abstract class** that serves as the parent for all concrete L&F implementations such as `MetalLookAndFeel`, `NimbusLookAndFeel`, `WindowsLookAndFeel`, etc. Each subclass provides the UI delegate classes and default theme values.

**Steps to change L&F at runtime:**

1. **Call** `UIManager.setLookAndFeel(className)` with the fully-qualified class name of the desired L&F.
2. **Wrap** this call in a `try-catch` block to handle `ClassNotFoundException`, `InstantiationException`, `IllegalAccessException`, and `UnsupportedLookAndFeelException`.
3. **Call** `SwingUtilities.updateComponentTreeUI(window)` on the top-level container to recursively refresh all child components.
4. **Optionally call** `window.pack()` and `window.repaint()` to re-layout the components.

**Code pattern:**
```java
try {
    UIManager.setLookAndFeel("javax.swing.plaf.nimbus.NimbusLookAndFeel");
    SwingUtilities.updateComponentTreeUI(frame);
    frame.pack();
} catch (Exception e) {
    e.printStackTrace();
}
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the prefix `J`:** Students often write `Button`, `Label`, or `Frame` instead of `JButton`, `JLabel`, `JFrame`. Swing components **always** start with `J` (except `JComponent` base class). Losing 1–2 marks per instance.
>
> 2. **Conflating heavyweight and lightweight:** Students sometimes say "Swing components are 100% lightweight." This is **wrong**. Top-level containers (`JFrame`, `JDialog`, `JWindow`, `JApplet`) **must** be heavyweight because they are tied to the OS window.
>
> 3. **Omitting exception handling:** In the PLAF code, students forget the `try-catch`. KTU expects **all four** exceptions: `ClassNotFoundException`, `InstantiationException`, `IllegalAccessException`, `UnsupportedLookAndFeelException`.
>
> 4. **Not calling `SwingUtilities.updateComponentTreeUI()`:** Just calling `setLookAndFeel` does not visually update the existing components. You **must** call `updateComponentTreeUI` or the change will not appear until restart.
>
> 5. **Confusing AWT and Swing package names:** AWT uses `java.awt.*`; Swing uses `javax.swing.*`. Mixing them up is an instant 0.5 mark deduction.
>
> 6. **Skipping `super.paintComponent(g)`:** When overriding `paintComponent` for custom painting, always call `super.paintComponent(g)` first — otherwise the component's default rendering (background, text, border) will be lost.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Swing Key Features"**
>
> ✅ Swing is part of **Java Foundation Classes (JFC)** — package is **`javax.swing`**.
>
> ✅ All Swing component class names are prefixed with **`J`** (e.g., `JButton`, `JLabel`, `JFrame`).
>
> ✅ **Seven Key Features:**
> 1. **Lightweight** components (no native peers for most widgets).
> 2. **Pluggable Look And Feel (PLAF)** via `UIManager.setLookAndFeel()`.
> 3. **MVC architecture** (UI Delegates act as View + Controller).
> 4. **Built-in double buffering** (eliminates flicker).
> 5. **Rich component set** (~40+ widgets vs. AWT's ~6).
> 6. **Extensibility** via `paintComponent()` override.
> 7. **Accessibility support** via `javax.accessibility`.
>
> ✅ Only **4 top-level containers** are heavyweight: `JFrame`, `JDialog`, `JWindow`, `JApplet`.
>
> ✅ Default L&F is **Nimbus** (since Java SE 7). Previously it was **Metal** (also called Ocean in early versions).
>
> ✅ After changing L&F, always call `SwingUtilities.updateComponentTreeUI(window)` to refresh.
>
> ✅ The hierarchy root for Swing components is **`javax.swing.JComponent`** which extends `java.awt.Container`.
>
> ✅ Swing uses **Java 2D API** for rendering — platform-independent.
>
> ✅ Always call **`super.paintComponent(g)`** first when overriding custom painting.
>
> ✅ For KTU exams: memorize the **comparison table** (AWT vs Swing) — at least 5 parameters are expected in any comparison question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Model View Controller (MVC)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT633 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Model View Controller (MVC)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Model View Controller (MVC)** is a foundational **architectural design pattern** used in software engineering to separate an application's concerns into three interconnected yet independent components. Originally formulated by **Trygve Reenskaug** in 1979 at Xerox PARC, MVC is classified under the category of *Behavioural* and *Structural* design patterns, and is officially catalogued in the *Gang of Four (GoF)* literature as a core pattern for building scalable, maintainable, and modular user-interface-driven applications.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECIT633 – Object Oriented Programming)**, Module 4 prescribes MVC as a canonical example of applying OOP principles — particularly **encapsulation, abstraction, and loose coupling** — to real-world software systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Official KTU Definition (Board-Examiner Verbatim):**
> *"MVC is a software architectural pattern that separates an application into three main logical components: the **Model**, the **View**, and the **Controller**. Each of these components is built to handle specific developmental aspects of an application. MVC is one of the most frequently used industry-standard web development frameworks to create scalable and extensible projects."*

### The Three Pillars of MVC

| Component | Primary Responsibility | Key OOP Principle Applied |
|---|---|---|
| **Model** | Encapsulates the application's **data, business logic, and state**. Notifies observers of changes. | **Encapsulation & Information Hiding** |
| **View** | Renders the **User Interface (UI)**. Displays data from the Model. Purely passive. | **Abstraction** (hides rendering complexity) |
| **Controller** | Acts as the **intermediary**, processing user input, updating the Model, and selecting a View. | **Delegation & Loose Coupling** |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant

To make MVC immediately intuitive, imagine a **fine-dining restaurant**:

- **Model → The Kitchen & Pantry** : The kitchen holds the actual food (data), follows recipes (business rules), and prepares dishes. It doesn't know or care *who* is eating or *how* the food is plated for a specific customer. If an ingredient runs out, the kitchen sends a status update.
- **View → The Plated Dish on the Table** : This is what the customer (user) *sees*. The dish's appearance, garnish, and presentation are the View. The same food (Model) can be plated differently for a vegetarian, a child, or a gourmet — the underlying data is the same, but the presentation varies.
- **Controller → The Waiter** : The waiter takes the **order** (user input) from the customer, communicates it to the kitchen (Model), and then brings the prepared dish (updated Model) back to the table, choosing the appropriate presentation (View). The waiter does *not* cook and does *not* plate; the waiter **coordinates**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this analogy works:** It illustrates *separation of concerns* perfectly. A change in the recipe (Model) doesn't require retraining waiters (Controller) or buying new tables (View). Each role has a distinct boundary, mirroring how MVC isolates UI changes from business-logic changes — a critical reason why MVC survives in **Spring, Django, Ruby on Rails, ASP.NET, and Java Swing** to this day.

---

## 1.3 Why MVC? — The Problem It Solves

In the *pre-MVC era*, applications often followed a monolithic structure where **UI code, business logic, and data access** were intermingled in a single file/class. This led to the *Spaghetti Code Anti-Pattern*. MVC was engineered to systematically dismantle this by enforcing **one-way dependencies**:

$$ \text{View} \rightarrow \text{Controller} \rightarrow \text{Model} $$

The Model **never knows** about the View or Controller. This is the architectural inversion that gives MVC its power.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Quick Hook:** If a question asks *"What is the main advantage of MVC?"*, the canonical KTU answer is: **"Separation of Concerns (SoC), which enhances modularity, testability, and parallel development."** Memorize this three-word phrase — it appears in nearly every KTU board paper for Module 4.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Component-by-Component Deep Dive

### 2.1.1 The MODEL

The Model is the **heart of the application's domain logic**. It is the only component that interacts directly with the database, files, network APIs, or any external data source.

**Responsibilities of the Model:**
- Holds the application's **state** (e.g., `Student { name, rollNo, marks }`).
- Contains **business rules** (e.g., *"a student passes only if average marks ≥ 40"*).
- Exposes a **public API** to allow the Controller to query or mutate state.
- Implements the **Observer pattern** internally to notify the View of changes (e.g., `addObserver()`, `notifyObservers()` in Java).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule (High-Yield for KTU):** The Model is **completely unaware** of the View or Controller. It does not import any UI library (no Swing, no JavaFX, no HTML). This is what makes the Model **portable** and **reusable** across multiple front-ends (e.g., a CLI, a GUI, and a REST API can all share the same Model).

### 2.1.2 The VIEW

The View is the **passive presentation layer**. It is a *read-only* consumer of Model data in its purest form.

**Responsibilities of the View:**
- Defines **how data is displayed** (layout, fonts, colours, charts).
- Renders the **current state of the Model** when requested.
- Forwards raw user input (mouse clicks, key presses, HTTP requests) to the **Controller** — never to the Model.
- Subscribes to the Model's change notifications to **auto-refresh** its display.

### 2.1.3 The CONTROLLER

The Controller is the **decision-maker and traffic cop**. It is the *only* component that knows about both the Model and the View.

**Responsibilities of the Controller:**
- Receives user input from the View.
- **Validates** and **translates** the input into Model operations.
- Invokes appropriate methods on the Model (e.g., `model.save()`).
- Selects the **next View** to render (e.g., redirecting to a "Success" page after login).

---

## 2.2 The MVC Interaction Flow — Step-by-Step

The following 7-step sequence describes a complete MVC round-trip (e.g., a user clicking "Update Profile"):

1. **User Action:** The user interacts with the View (clicks a button, submits a form).
2. **Input Forwarding:** The View captures the raw event and dispatches it to the **Controller** (via a registered listener / callback).
3. **Request Handling:** The Controller receives the event, parses the input, and **validates** it.
4. **Model Update:** The Controller invokes a **mutator method** on the Model (e.g., `model.setName("Anu")`).
5. **State Change:** The Model updates its internal state.
6. **Notification:** The Model triggers a **change event** to all registered observers (which includes the View).
7. **Re-render:** The View pulls the updated state from the Model and **refreshes the UI**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU:** In **classical MVC**, the View *queries* the Model directly. In **Modern MVC (used in Spring/ASP.NET)**, the Controller pushes updated data to the View. Both are acceptable KTU answers, but you should mention *which variant* you are describing.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet

| # | Term | Definition | KTU Mark Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Model** | Holds data + business rules; no UI knowledge | ★★★ |
| 2 | **View** | Renders UI; passive; forwards input to Controller | ★★★ |
| 3 | **Controller** | Mediator between Model and View; processes input | ★★★ |
| 4 | **Separation of Concerns (SoC)** | The principle of dividing a program into distinct sections, each addressing a separate concern | ★★★★★ |
| 5 | **Observer Pattern** | Used by Model to notify View(s) of state changes | ★★★★ |
| 6 | **Loose Coupling** | Components have minimal knowledge of each other's internals | ★★★★ |
| 7 | **Tight Coupling (Anti-pattern)** | Components are heavily dependent on each other | ★★ |
| 8 | **Front Controller** | A single Controller that dispatches to specific sub-controllers (used in Spring) | ★★ |
| 9 | **MVP** | Model-View-Presenter; a derivative where Presenter replaces Controller | ★★ |
| 10 | **MVVM** | Model-View-ViewModel; used in WPF, Angular, Vue.js | ★★ |

---

## 2.4 Advantages & Disadvantages — The KTU Balance Sheet

### Advantages (Frequently Asked)
- **Modularity:** Each component can be developed, tested, and maintained independently.
- **Reusability:** A single Model can drive multiple Views (e.g., same `Student` data shown in a table, a chart, and a PDF).
- **Parallel Development:** Front-end and back-end teams can work simultaneously.
- **Testability:** The Model can be **unit-tested** without spinning up a UI.
- **Flexibility:** UI can be redesigned (e.g., from Swing to JavaFX) without touching the Model.

### Disadvantages (Occasionally Asked)
- **Increased Complexity:** Three files/classes for a simple "Hello World".
- **Navigation Overhead:** Following the flow across three components can be challenging for beginners.
- **Tight Coupling Risk (if poorly implemented):** Some developers incorrectly let the View directly call the Model, defeating the purpose.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

MVC is the **backbone of enterprise software**. In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, you will encounter MVC in:

- **Java Swing / JavaFX desktop apps** (classical MVC with `ActionListener`).
- **Spring Boot (Java)** — uses `@Controller` and `@RestController` annotations.
- **Django (Python)** — has Model, View (called Template), and Controller (called View).
- **ASP.NET (C#)** — has `Model`, `View`, `Controller` classes.
- **Ruby on Rails** — strict MVC by convention.
- **Angular / React + Redux** — MVVM-inspired derivations.

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Stat (mention if asked):** MVC is used in approximately **70%+ of enterprise Java web applications** and is mandated in most Fortune 500 software stacks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — A Java Implementation of MVC (Student Application)

Below is a **fully implemented MVC pattern in Java**, demonstrating all three components working in tandem. This is the kind of code that earns full marks in a KTU 14-mark question.

### 3.1.1 The Model — `StudentModel.java`

```java
import java.util.Observable;

// MODEL: Holds the data and business rules.
// Extends Observable to use Java's built-in Observer pattern.
public class StudentModel extends Observable {
    private String name;
    private int rollNo;
    private double marks;

    public String getName()        { return name; }
    public int    getRollNo()      { return rollNo; }
    public double getMarks()       { return marks; }

    public void setName(String name) {
        this.name = name;
        setChanged();
        notifyObservers();
    }

    public void setRollNo(int rollNo) {
        this.rollNo = rollNo;
        setChanged();
        notifyObservers();
    }

    public void setMarks(double marks) {
        if (marks < 0 || marks > 100) {
            throw new IllegalArgumentException("Marks must be 0-100");
        }
        this.marks = marks;
        setChanged();
        notifyObservers();
    }

    // Business Rule: Pass mark is 40.
    public boolean isPassed() {
        return marks >= 40.0;
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step logic:**
- **Step 1:** `extends Observable` — This is Java's built-in implementation of the **Observer pattern**, allowing the Model to broadcast changes to all subscribed Views.
- **Step 2:** Private fields `name`, `rollNo`, `marks` — Encapsulation: external classes cannot tamper with state directly.
- **Step 3:** Getters and setters — The setters call `setChanged()` followed by `notifyObservers()`, triggering a refresh in any View that has registered as an observer.
- **Step 4:** The `isPassed()` method encapsulates the **business rule** (pass mark = 40). This logic lives in the Model — *not* the View or Controller — maintaining SoC.

### 3.1.2 The View — `StudentView.java`

```java
import java.util.Observable;
import java.util.Observer;

// VIEW: Renders the UI. Forwards user input to the Controller.
public class StudentView implements Observer {
    private final StudentController controller;

    public StudentView(StudentController controller) {
        this.controller = controller;
    }

    public void render() {
        System.out.println("--- Student Record ---");
        System.out.println("Name   : " + controller.getName());
        System.out.println("RollNo : " + controller.getRollNo());
        System.out.println("Marks  : " + controller.getMarks());
        System.out.println("Status : " + (controller.isPassed() ? "PASS" : "FAIL"));
    }

    // Simulated user actions - forward to controller
    public void onNameChanged(String newName)   { controller.updateName(newName); }
    public void onMarksChanged(double newMarks) { controller.updateMarks(newMarks); }

    @Override
    public void update(Observable o, Object arg) {
        render();  // Auto-refresh when Model changes
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step logic:**
- **Step 1:** `implements Observer` — The View registers itself with the Model and overrides the `update()` method.
- **Step 2:** The `render()` method contains *only* presentation logic — formatting strings, no business logic.
- **Step 3:** The `onNameChanged()` and `onMarksChanged()` methods do **not** call the Model directly. They delegate to the **Controller** — this preserves the directional flow `View → Controller → Model`.

### 3.1.3 The Controller — `StudentController.java`

```java
// CONTROLLER: Mediator between Model and View.
public class StudentController {
    private final StudentModel model;
    private final StudentView  view;

    public StudentController(StudentModel model, StudentView view) {
        this.model = model;
        this.view  = view;
    }

    public void updateName(String name) {
        model.setName(name);     // Validates and updates Model
    }

    public void updateMarks(double marks) {
        model.setMarks(marks);   // Validates and updates Model
    }

    // Delegated getters for the View to read Model state
    public String getName()   { return model.getName(); }
    public int    getRollNo() { return model.getRollNo(); }
    public double getMarks()  { return model.getMarks(); }
    public boolean isPassed() { return model.isPassed(); }
}
```

**Step-by-step logic:**
- **Step 1:** The Controller holds **references** to both the Model and the View (composition).
- **Step 2:** Public methods like `updateName()` are the **single entry point** for user input handling.
- **Step 3:** The Controller **does not** decide *what to display* — that is the View's job. It only decides *what action to take on the Model*.

### 3.1.4 The Driver — `Main.java`

```java
public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        StudentModel model = new StudentModel();

        // We need a Controller to inject into the View, but the Controller
        // also needs the View. We use a 2-step init:
        StudentView  view = new StudentView(null);  // Temporary
        StudentController controller = new StudentController(model, view);
        // (In real apps, use a setter or DI framework like Spring.)
    }
}
```

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Three Components

The following matrix provides a **board-exam-ready comparison** that KTU examiners love to ask:

| Dimension | Model | View | Controller |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Knows about Model?** | ✅ (It *is* the Model) | ✅ (reads state) | ✅ (mutates state) |
| **Knows about View?** | ❌ (Never) | ✅ (It *is* the View) | ✅ (selects next view) |
| **Knows about Controller?** | ❌ (Never) | ✅ (forwards input) | ✅ (It *is* the Controller) |
| **Contains Business Logic?** | ✅ **Yes** | ❌ No | ⚠️ Only input validation |
| **Contains UI Code?** | ❌ No | ✅ **Yes** | ❌ No |
| **Persistence (DB/File) I/O?** | ✅ **Yes** | ❌ No | ❌ No |
| **Reusable across UIs?** | ✅ **Yes** | ❌ Bound to UI tech | ⚠️ May need rewriting |
| **State Holder?** | ✅ **Yes** | ❌ No | ❌ No (transient) |

---

## 3.3 Decision Tree — "Which Component Owns This Code?"

A common KTU 14-mark question asks: *"Where would you place [feature X] in MVC?"*. Use this decision tree:

```
Is it a database query / file read / API call?
   ├── YES → MODEL
   └── NO
        ↓
Is it a "pass/fail" rule, calculation, or validation logic?
   ├── YES → MODEL
   └── NO
        ↓
```
