```
Is it a button, label, dropdown, or HTML element?
   ├── YES → VIEW
   └── NO
        ↓
Is it event handling, routing, or "what happens when X is clicked"?
   └── YES → CONTROLLER
```

**Example Application:**
- *"Calculate the GPA from marks"* → **MODEL** (business rule).
- *"Display a red border if GPA < 5.0"* → **VIEW** (presentation).
- *"On 'Submit' button click, calculate GPA and save to DB"* → **CONTROLLER** (orchestration).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 MVC Architecture — High-Level Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    User([User / Browser / Client])

    subgraph MVC_App["MVC Application Boundary"]
        direction LR
        V[VIEW\nPresentation Layer\nUI Rendering]
        C[CONTROLLER\nInput Handler\nMediator]
        M[MODEL\nData + Business Logic\nState Holder]
    end

    DB[(Database /\nFile System /\nExternal API)]

    User -- "1. User Action\n(Click / Form Submit)" --> V
    V -- "2. Forward Raw Input\n(ActionEvent / HTTP Request)" --> C
    C -- "3. Validate & Invoke\n(model.setData(...))" --> M
    M -- "4. Update State" --> M
    M -- "5. Notify Change\n(Observer Pattern)" --> V
    M -- "6. Persist / Fetch" --> DB
    V -- "7. Re-render UI\nwith new Model state" --> User

    classDef viewBox    fill:#DBEAFE,stroke:#1E3A8A,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ctrlBox    fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#92400E,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef modelBox   fill:#D1FAE5,stroke:#065F46,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef dataBox    fill:#F3E8FF,stroke:#6B21A8,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef userNode   fill:#FEE2E2,stroke:#991B1B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000

    class V viewBox
    class C ctrlBox
    class M modelBox
    class DB dataBox
    class User userNode
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**
- The arrows form a **closed loop** of communication, but the **Model never initiates contact with the View or Controller** — it only responds to their queries or fires notifications.
- The Database is exclusively accessed by the **Model**, never by the View or Controller.

---

## 4.2 MVC Component Interaction — Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant U as User
    participant V as View
    participant C as Controller
    participant M as Model
    participant DB as Database

    U->>V: Clicks "Save Profile" button
    activate V
    V->>C: dispatchAction("SAVE", formData)
    deactivate V
    activate C
    C->>C: validateInput(formData)
    C->>M: setName("Anu"), setEmail("...")
    deactivate C
    activate M
    M->>M: setChanged() + notifyObservers()
    M->>DB: INSERT/UPDATE query
    DB-->>M: rowsAffected = 1
    deactivate M
    M-->>V: update(Observable, Object) callback
    activate V
    V->>V: re-render() with new Model state
    V-->>U: Display "Profile Saved" success message
    deactivate V
```

**Sequence Numbering Decoded:**
- Steps **1–3** = Input phase
- Steps **4–6** = Processing phase
- Steps **7–9** = Persistence + Notification phase
- Steps **10–11** = Re-render phase

---

## 4.3 MVC vs. MVP vs. MVVM — Comparative Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Classical_MVC["MVC - Classical Pattern"]
        MVC_V[View]
        MVC_C[Controller]
        MVC_M[Model]
        MVC_V <--> MVC_C
        MVC_C <--> MVC_M
        MVC_V -. observes .-> MVC_M
    end

    subgraph MVP_Pattern["MVP Pattern"]
        MVP_V[View]
        MVP_P[Presenter]
        MVP_M[Model]
        MVP_V <--> MVP_P
        MVP_P <--> MVP_M
        MVP_V -.-x MVP_M
    end

    subgraph MVVM_Pattern["MVVM Pattern"]
        MVVM_V[View]
        MVVM_VM[ViewModel]
        MVVM_M[Model]
        MVVM_V <--> MVVM_VM
        MVVM_VM <--> MVVM_M
        MVVM_V -. data binding .-> MVVM_VM
    end

    classDef viewStyle   fill:#DBEAFE,stroke:#1E40AF,color:#000
    classDef ctrlStyle   fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#92400E,color:#000
    classDef modelStyle  fill:#D1FAE5,stroke:#065F46,color:#000
    classDef vmStyle     fill:#FCE7F3,stroke:#9D174D,color:#000

    class MVC_V,MVP_V,MVVM_V viewStyle
    class MVC_C,MVP_P ctrlStyle
    class MVC_M,MVP_M,MVVM_M modelStyle
    class MVVM_VM vmStyle
```

**Key Architectural Differences:**

| Pattern | View's Direct Access to Model? | Mediator's Name | Bidirectional Binding? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **MVC** | ✅ Yes (classical); ❌ No (modern) | Controller | ❌ |
| **MVP** | ❌ No (Presenter is mandatory gatekeeper) | Presenter | ❌ |
| **MVVM** | ❌ No (ViewModel exposes observable state) | ViewModel | ✅ Yes (data binding) |

---

## 4.4 MVC Request Lifecycle — Block-Level Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([HTTP Request Arrives]) --> Routing{Front Controller\nRoute Match?}
    Routing -- "Matched Route" --> InvokeCtrl[Invoke Specific\nController Method]
    Routing -- "No Match" --> Err404[Return 404]

    InvokeCtrl --> Bind[Bind Request Params\nto Model Object]
    Bind --> Validate{Validation\nSuccessful?}
    Validate -- "No" --> Err400[Return 400 + Errors]
    Validate -- "Yes" --> BizLogic[Execute Business Logic\nin Model Layer]

    BizLogic --> Persist[Model Persists to DB\nvia DAO / Repository]
    Persist --> RespType{Response Type?}

    RespType -- "HTML Page" --> SelectView[Controller Selects\nView Template]
    RespType -- "JSON / API" --> Serial[Serialize Model\nto JSON]

    SelectView --> Render[Render View with\nModel Data]
    Serial --> SendResp
    Render --> SendResp[Send HTTP Response]
    SendResp --> End([Response Delivered])

    classDef procNode  fill:#DBEAFE,stroke:#1E40AF,color:#000
    classDef errorNode fill:#FEE2E2,stroke:#991B1B,color:#000
    classDef successNode fill:#D1FAE5,stroke:#065F46,color:#000
    classDef startEndNode fill:#F3E8FF,stroke:#6B21A8,color:#000

    class InvokeCtrl,Bind,BizLogic,Persist,SelectView,Serial,Render,SendResp procNode
    class Err404,Err400 errorNode
    class Validate,RespType,Start,End startEndNode
    class Start,End,Validate,RespType startEndNode
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
*Define the Model-View-Controller (MVC) architectural pattern. List its three main components and their responsibilities.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

MVC is a software architectural design pattern that separates an application into three interconnected components to achieve **separation of concerns**:
1. **Model** — Encapsulates the application's data and business logic. It is the only component that interacts with the database and notifies the View of any state changes via the Observer pattern.
2. **View** — Handles the user interface and presentation. It renders the Model's data and forwards user input to the Controller.
3. **Controller** — Acts as an intermediary between the Model and the View. It processes user input, updates the Model, and selects the appropriate View for response.

*(1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for the components, 1 mark for the responsibilities.)*

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
*What is the Observer pattern? How is it used in the MVC architecture?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Observer pattern** is a behavioural design pattern in which an object (the **Subject/Observable**) maintains a list of dependents (**Observers**) and notifies them automatically of any state changes, typically by calling one of their methods.

In MVC:
- The **Model** acts as the **Subject/Observable**.
- The **View** acts as the **Observer**.
- When the Model's state changes (e.g., `model.setName(...)`), it calls `notifyObservers()`, which in turn triggers the `update()` method in all registered Views, causing them to **auto-refresh** their display.

This decouples the Model from the View, allowing multiple Views to subscribe to a single Model.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Module 4 long questions carry **14 marks** with internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B** on the same module. Each question typically has sub-parts (a) for **7 marks** and (b) for **7 marks**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** *With a neat block diagram, explain the Model-View-Controller (MVC) architectural pattern. Describe the role of each component and the flow of data between them.* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *Design a Java application using the MVC pattern to manage a `Product` entity. The application should allow the user to add products and display all products. Provide separate classes for Model, View, and Controller.* **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(a) — 7 Marks

The MVC pattern consists of three components connected as shown in the diagram below:

```
   ┌─────────┐         ┌──────────┐         ┌────────┐
   │  VIEW   │ <-----  │CONTROLLER│  -----> │ MODEL  │
   │(Display)│         │(Mediator)│         │(Data)  │
   └────▲────┘         └──────────┘         └───▲────┘
        │                                        │
        └──────── (Observer Notification) ───────┘
```

**Flow of Data (4 Steps):**
1. The **User** interacts with the **View** (e.g., clicks a button).
2. The **View** forwards the input to the **Controller**.
3. The **Controller** processes the input, validates it, and invokes the appropriate method on the **Model**.
4. The **Model** updates its state and notifies the **View** to re-render the UI.

**Component Roles (3 Marks):**
- **Model** — Holds data, business logic, and persistence (DB access). It is UI-agnostic.
- **View** — Renders the UI. It does not contain business logic.
- **Controller** — Mediator: receives input, updates Model, selects View.

**Key Benefit:** *Separation of concerns* allows parallel development, independent testing, and UI reusability.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(b) — 7 Marks

**`Product.java` (Model):**
```java
import java.util.ArrayList;
import java.util.List;
import java.util.Observable;

public class Product extends Observable {
    private int id;
    private String name;
    private double price;

    public Product(int id, String name, double price) {
        this.id = id;
        this.name = name;
        this.price = price;
    }

    public int getId()              { return id; }
    public String getName()         { return name; }
    public double getPrice()        { return price; }

    public void setPrice(double p) {
        this.price = p;
        setChanged();
        notifyObservers();
    }
}
```

**`ProductRepository.java` (Model — collection of products):**
```java
public class ProductRepository {
    private final List<Product> products = new ArrayList<>();

    public void addProduct(Product p) { products.add(p); }
    public List<Product> getAll()     { return new ArrayList<>(products); }
}
```

**`ProductView.java` (View):**
```java
import java.util.Observer;
import java.util.Observable;

public class ProductView implements Observer {
    @Override
    public void update(Observable o, Object arg) {
        System.out.println("View notified: Product list updated.");
        // Re-render logic goes here
    }

    public void displayProducts(List<Product> products) {
        System.out.println("---- Product List ----");
        for (Product p : products) {
            System.out.println(p.getId() + " | " + p.getName() + " | " + p.getPrice());
        }
    }
}
```

**`ProductController.java` (Controller):**
```java
public class ProductController {
    private final ProductRepository repository;
    private final ProductView view;

    public ProductController(ProductRepository repo, ProductView view) {
        this.repository = repo;
        this.view = view;
    }

    public void addProduct(int id, String name, double price) {
        Product p = new Product(id, name, price);
        repository.addProduct(p);
    }

    public void showAllProducts() {
        view.displayProducts(repository.getAll());
    }
}
```

**`Main.java` (Driver):**
```java
public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        ProductRepository repo = new ProductRepository();
        ProductView view = new ProductView();
        ProductController controller = new ProductController(repo, view);

        controller.addProduct(1, "Laptop", 75000);
        controller.addProduct(2, "Phone",  35000);
        controller.showAllProducts();
    }
}
```

**Mark Distribution (7 Marks):**
| Sub-component | Marks |
|---|---|
| Correct `Model` class with business logic | 2 |
| Correct `View` class with display logic | 2 |
| Correct `Controller` class with orchestration | 2 |
| Working `Main` driver + output | 1 |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** *Compare and contrast the MVC pattern with the MVP and MVVM patterns. Highlight the key architectural differences and state one real-world framework that uses each.* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *Explain the concept of "Separation of Concerns" in software design. Illustrate with a real-world analogy and explain how MVC achieves it.* **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(a) — 7 Marks

| Aspect | MVC | MVP | MVVM |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Full Form** | Model-View-Controller | Model-View-Presenter | Model-View-ViewModel |
| **Mediator** | Controller | Presenter | ViewModel |
| **View's Access to Model** | Direct (classical) | Only via Presenter | Only via ViewModel (data binding) |
| **Bidirectional Binding** | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| **Best For** | Web apps, desktop UIs | Legacy desktop apps, testability | Modern reactive UIs (Angular, WPF) |
| **Framework Example** | **Spring MVC (Java)**, Django (Python), Ruby on Rails | **GWT (Google Web Toolkit)**, WinForms (C#) | **Angular (TypeScript)**, WPF (C#), Vue.js |
| **Testability** | Moderate | High (Presenter is mockable) | High (ViewModel is unit-testable) |

**Key Differences (Verbatim for 2 Extra Marks):**
- In **MVC**, the View can directly read from the Model.
- In **MVP**, the Presenter acts as a strict gatekeeper — the View cannot access the Model directly.
- In **MVVM**, the ViewModel exposes observable data streams, and the View *auto-syncs* via data binding.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(b) — 7 Marks

**Definition (2 Marks):**
**Separation of Concerns (SoC)** is a design principle that advocates dividing a software system into distinct sections, where each section addresses a separate *concern* (a specific responsibility or feature). Introduced by **Edsger W. Dijkstra** in 1974.

**Real-World Analogy (2 Marks):**
Consider a **car manufacturing plant**:
- The **engine department** builds engines (data + business logic).
- The **paint shop** paints the car body (UI/presentation).
- The **assembly line supervisor** coordinates the two (controller).

If the company decides to change the car colour from red to blue, only the paint shop is affected. The engine department and supervisor are untouched. This is SoC.

**How MVC Achieves SoC (3 Marks):**
1. **Distinct Files/Classes:** Model, View, and Controller are separate entities.
2. **Single Responsibility:** Each class has exactly one reason to change.
3. **No Cross-Imports:** Model does not import UI libraries; View does not import DB libraries.
4. **Parallel Development:** A back-end developer can build the Model while a front-end developer builds the View — they meet at the Controller interface.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 Where KTU Students Lose Marks on MVC Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing the View with the Controller** — *"The View processes user input."* ❌ This is wrong. The View *forwards* input; the Controller *processes* it. (–1 to –2 marks)
> 2. **Letting the View directly call the Model** — In a strict MVC implementation, the View should go *through* the Controller for any mutation. (–2 marks)
> 3. **Writing business logic in the View** — *"If marks < 40, display FAIL in red."* ❌ The "marks < 40" check belongs in the Model. (–1 mark)
> 4. **Forgetting to mention the Observer pattern** — KTU examiners expect you to explicitly state that the Model notifies the View via the Observer pattern. (–1 mark)
> 5. **Mixing up MVC with MVP/MVVM** — These are *not* synonyms. Mention the differences if the question is comparative. (–1 mark)
> 6. **Not drawing a diagram** — A 7-mark question on MVC *always* expects a block diagram or sequence diagram. Omitting it costs 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📌 Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: MVC**

### 🔑 Core Definitions
- ✅ **MVC** = **M**odel (data) + **V**iew (UI) + **C**ontroller (mediator)
- ✅ **Model** = Business logic + data + persistence
- ✅ **View** = Presentation only (no logic, no DB)
- ✅ **Controller** = Input handling + Model invocation + View selection

### 🧭 Key Principles
- ✅ **Separation of Concerns (SoC)** — the *primary* advantage
- ✅ **Loose Coupling** between components
- ✅ **Single Responsibility Principle (SRP)** — one reason to change per class
- ✅ **Observer Pattern** — Model notifies View on state change
- ✅ **One-way dependency** — View → Controller → Model

### 📊 Comparative Knowledge
- ✅ MVC vs. MVP (Presenter as gatekeeper, no direct View-to-Model)
- ✅ MVC vs. MVVM (data binding, ViewModel)
- ✅ Real-world frameworks: Spring MVC, Django, Rails, ASP.NET

### 🛠️ Implementation Know-How
- ✅ Can write a Java MVC application with `Model`, `View`, `Controller` classes
- ✅ Knows how to use `java.util.Observable` and `Observer`
- ✅ Can identify *which* component a piece of code belongs to

### ⚠️ Common Exam Traps
- ❌ Don't say "View processes input" — it *forwards* input
- ❌ Don't put business rules (e.g., pass/fail) in the View
- ❌ Don't let the View directly mutate the Model (classical strict MVC)
- ❌ Don't confuse MVC's View with MVP's Presenter or MVVM's ViewModel

### 📈 Likely 14-Mark Question Themes
- *"Explain MVC with a diagram and a Java program"* (most common)
- *"Compare MVC, MVP, MVVM with examples"*
- *"Design a [Library / Student / Bank] system using MVC"*
- *"Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of MVC"*

---

> **🎯 Final KTU Pro Tip:** When asked to *"design"* or *"implement"* an MVC application in the exam, **always draw the block diagram first** (1 mark), then **list the responsibilities** of each component (1 mark), and *only then* start writing code. This structure mirrors the KTU marking scheme and maximizes partial-credit recovery if you run out of time.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Swing Controls
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT633 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Swing Controls

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Swing Controls** are lightweight, pluggable-looking graphical user interface (GUI) components defined within the `javax.swing` package of the Java Standard Edition (Java SE) platform. They inherit from the abstract class `javax.swing.JComponent`, which itself extends `java.awt.Container` and ultimately `java.awt.Component`. Unlike the older, platform-dependent **Abstract Window Toolkit (AWT)** peers, Swing controls are rendered entirely by Java code (a "lightweight" architecture), guaranteeing identical visual appearance across all operating systems.

The Java Foundation Classes (JFC) include **Swing**, **Accessibility**, **Java 2D**, and **Pluggable Look-and-Feel**. Within Swing, every interactive widget (button, text field, slider, etc.) is referred to as a *control*. Each control is an instance of a class that supports the **Model-View-Controller (MVC)** paradigm, where:
- **Model** — stores the data/state of the control.
- **View** — renders the visual representation.
- **Controller** — handles user input (events).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – PECIT633 Module 4):** Students are expected to identify, instantiate, and configure at least the following controls: `JLabel`, `JButton`, `JTextField`, `JTextArea`, `JCheckBox`, `JRadioButton`, `JComboBox`, `JList`, `JSlider`, `JProgressBar`, `JMenuBar`, `JMenu`, `JMenuItem`, `JPanel`, and `JScrollPane`. They must also demonstrate proficiency with `LayoutManager` interfaces.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the **cockpit of an aircraft**. The cockpit is a *Container* (the window). Inside, you have dials, switches, levers, and gauges — these are the *Controls* (`JButton`, `JSlider`, `JLabel`). Each dial has a purpose: a switch *does* something when flipped (Event Source), the pilot *acts* on it (Event Listener), and the resulting action causes another gauge to change (Event Handler). The cockpit panel itself is the *JFrame*, and the way instruments are arranged (rows, grids) is the *LayoutManager*. Just as every switch in the cockpit obeys a fixed physical layout, every Swing control must be added to a container and obeys a layout strategy.

### 1.3 Key Architectural Constants

> [!NOTE]
> - **Default Look-and-Feel:** *CrossPlatformLookAndFeel* (Metal) — guaranteed uniform appearance.
> - **Root Container:** `JFrame` (top-level window with title bar, min/max/close buttons).
> - **Inheritance root of all Swing controls:** `javax.swing.JComponent`.
> - **Thread safety rule (Swing Single-Thread Rule):** *All* UI updates MUST occur on the **Event Dispatch Thread (EDT)**.
> - **Headless exception guard:** `java.awt.HeadlessException` is thrown if a Swing control is instantiated on a system without a display.

### 1.4 Why Swing Over AWT?

| Feature | AWT (`java.awt`) | Swing (`javax.swing`) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Component weight | Heavyweight (OS peer) | **Lightweight (pure Java)** |
| Look and feel | OS-dependent | **Pluggable (PLAF)** |
| MVC compliance | Partial | **Full Model/View separation** |
| Component set | ~20 controls | **~50 controls + extensibility** |
| Painting logic | OS handles it | **Java `UIResource` handles it** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two-Tier Container Hierarchy

Swing's class structure forms a strict two-tier hierarchy that the examiner often tests:

1. **Top-Level Containers** (cannot be added to other containers): `JFrame`, `JApplet`, `JDialog`, `JWindow`.
2. **Lightweight / Intermediate Containers**: `JPanel`, `JScrollPane`, `JSplitPane`, `JTabbedPane`, `JToolBar`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule:** A lightweight component such as `JButton` MUST be added to an intermediate container (e.g., `JPanel`) which is then added to a top-level container (`JFrame`). You cannot add a `JButton` directly to the desktop screen.

### 2.2 The Event-Handling Equation

The interaction between a control, an event, a listener, and a handler can be expressed as a formal state-transition equation used in the *Delegation Event Model*:

$$ \text{Control}_{state} \xrightarrow{\text{User Action}} \text{EventObject} \xrightarrow{\text{fire}} \text{Listener}_{\text{registered}} \rightarrow \text{Handler}_{method} $$

Where:
- $\text{Control}_{state}$ — the current visual + data state of the widget.
- $\text{EventObject}$ — subclass of `java.util.EventObject` (e.g., `ActionEvent`, `ItemEvent`, `ChangeEvent`).
- $\text{Listener}_{\text{registered}}$ — any object that has called `control.addXxxListener(this)`.
- $\text{Handler}_{method}$ — the overridden method (e.g., `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)`).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Control Class | Primary Constructor(s) | Key Methods | Event Type & Listener |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `JLabel` | `JLabel()`, `JLabel(String)`, `JLabel(Icon)`, `JLabel(String, int)` | `setText(String)`, `setIcon(Icon)`, `setHorizontalAlignment(int)` | None (passive) |
| `JButton` | `JButton()`, `JButton(String)`, `JButton(Icon)` | `setEnabled(boolean)`, `setMnemonic(char)`, `doClick()` | `ActionEvent` → `ActionListener` |
| `JTextField` | `JTextField()`, `JTextField(int cols)`, `JTextField(String)` | `getText()`, `setText(String)`, `setEditable(boolean)` | `ActionEvent` → `ActionListener` |
| `JTextArea` | `JTextArea(int rows, int cols)`, `JTextArea(String)` | `append(String)`, `setLineWrap(boolean)`, `setRows(int)` | `DocumentEvent` → `DocumentListener` |
| `JCheckBox` | `JCheckBox(String)`, `JCheckBox(String, boolean)` | `isSelected()`, `setSelected(boolean)` | `ItemEvent` → `ItemListener` |
| `JRadioButton` | `JRadioButton(String)`, `JRadioButton(String, boolean)` | Same as `JCheckBox` | `ItemEvent` → `ItemListener` (must group with `ButtonGroup`) |
| `JComboBox<E>` | `JComboBox()`, `JComboBox(E[] items)` | `addItem(E)`, `getSelectedItem()`, `setSelectedIndex(int)` | `ItemEvent` → `ItemListener` |
| `JList<E>` | `JList()`, `JList(E[] items)`, `JList(ListModel)` | `setSelectedIndex(int)`, `getSelectedValue()`, `setSelectionMode(int)` | `ListSelectionEvent` → `ListSelectionListener` |
| `JSlider` | `JSlider(int min, int max, int value)`, `JSlider(int orientation)` | `getValue()`, `setValue(int)`, `setMajorTickSpacing(int)` | `ChangeEvent` → `ChangeListener` |
| `JProgressBar` | `JProgressBar(int min, int max)`, `JProgressBar(int orientation)` | `setValue(int)`, `getPercentComplete()`, `setIndeterminate(boolean)` | `ChangeEvent` → `ChangeListener` |
| `JMenuBar` | `JMenuBar()` | `add(JMenu)`, `getMenu(int)` | None directly |
| `JMenu` | `JMenu(String)`, `JMenu(String, boolean)` | `add(JMenuItem)`, `addSeparator()`, `getItem(int)` | `MenuEvent` → `MenuListener` |
| `JMenuItem` | `JMenuItem(String)`, `JMenuItem(Icon)` | `setAccelerator(KeyStroke)`, `setMnemonic(char)` | `ActionEvent` → `ActionListener` |
| `JPanel` | `JPanel()`, `JPanel(LayoutManager)` | `add(Component)`, `setLayout(LayoutManager)` | Container — not a control |
| `JScrollPane` | `JScrollPane(Component view)` | `setVerticalScrollBarPolicy(int)`, `getViewport()` | Container — not a control |

### 2.4 Standard Layout Manager Reference

> [!NOTE]
> The *LayoutManager* interface enforces a *Contract* — it dictates the spatial relationship $\text{Pos}_{(x,y)}$ of every child component using the formula:
> $$\text{Pos}_{(x,y)} = f(\text{parentSize}, \text{childPreferredSize}, \text{LayoutPolicy})$$

| Layout | Class | Default Behaviour | When to Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Flow | `FlowLayout` | Left-to-right, wraps | Toolbars, button rows |
| Border | `BorderLayout` | 5 regions: North, South, East, West, **Center** | Top-level containers |
| Grid | `GridLayout(rows, cols, hgap, vgap)` | Equal-sized cells in a matrix | Calculators, keypads |
| Box | `BoxLayout(axis)` | Vertical or horizontal stack | Dialogs, vertical forms |
| GridBag | `GridBagLayout` | Cells with varying sizes using `GridBagConstraints` | Complex IDE-style forms |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Swing is the technological backbone of countless legacy enterprise tools:
- **Integrated Development Environments (IDEs)** like older versions of IntelliJ IDEA used Swing prototypes.
- **Banking & ERP dashboards** — Swing's deterministic rendering on thin clients made it the industry default pre-Web.
- **Industrial SCADA HMIs** — Swing's `JSlider`, `JProgressBar`, and `JTable` map directly to physical gauges and telemetry displays.
- **Network monitoring consoles** — `JTable` + `JScrollPane` renders thousands of packet rows efficiently.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section *derives* a complete, event-driven Swing application from first principles, walking through every architectural decision a KTU examiner expects a candidate to justify.

### 3.1 The "Five-Point Formula" for Any Swing Program

Every Swing application in the KTU syllabus can be reduced to **five mandatory steps**. We will derive each one:

$$\text{SwingApp} = \{\text{Step1, Step2, Step3, Step4, Step5}\}$$

> **Step 1** — Import the `javax.swing` package (and optionally `java.awt.*`).
> **Step 2** — Declare a public class that either `extends JFrame` (top-level) or creates a `JFrame` in `main()`.
> **Step 3** — Instantiate controls using constructors from the cheat sheet above.
> **Step 4** — Register event listeners on the controls and implement handler methods.
> **Step 5** — Add controls to containers, set the layout, and call `setVisible(true)`.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — A "Student Registration" Mini-Application

**Problem Statement (modelled on KTU 2024 Part B style):**
Design a Swing GUI to accept a student's Name, Age, and Gender (radio buttons), display the entered details in a `JLabel` when a `JButton` labelled *"Submit"* is clicked, and clear the fields when a *"Reset"* button is clicked. The window must use `BorderLayout` with the form in the center.

#### Step 1 — Package Declaration and Imports

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;
```

*Logic row:* `javax.swing.*` provides every control and container; `java.awt.*` supplies the `BorderLayout` constants and `Color`/`Font` utilities; `java.awt.event.*` provides `ActionEvent`, `ActionListener`, and `ItemEvent` used by handlers.

#### Step 2 — Class Declaration

```java
public class StudentForm extends JFrame implements ActionListener {
    private JTextField nameField, ageField;
    private JRadioButton male, female;
    private JButton submitBtn, resetBtn;
    private JLabel displayLabel;

    public StudentForm() {
        setTitle("KTU Student Registration");
        setSize(420, 260);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
```

*Logic row:* The class `extends JFrame` (inheriting the window skeleton) and `implements ActionListener` so a single object can react to events from **two different buttons** while disambiguating by `e.getSource()`.

#### Step 3 — Instantiate Controls

```java
        nameField = new JTextField(15);
        ageField  = new JTextField(15);

        male   = new JRadioButton("Male");
        female = new JRadioButton("Female");
        ButtonGroup g = new ButtonGroup();
        g.add(male); g.add(female);

        submitBtn = new JButton("Submit");
        resetBtn  = new JButton("Reset");
        displayLabel = new JLabel("Details will appear here.");
```

*Logic row:* The `ButtonGroup` enforces **mutual exclusion** of the two `JRadioButton`s. Without the group, both could be selected simultaneously, violating single-choice semantics. The `JTextField(15)` constructor sets the preferred width in columns.

#### Step 4 — Register Event Listeners

```java
        submitBtn.addActionListener(this);
        resetBtn.addActionListener(this);
```

*Logic row:* `this` refers to the enclosing `StudentForm` object. Because it implements `ActionListener`, the compiler accepts it. At runtime, when either button is clicked, the JVM fires an `ActionEvent` and invokes the single `actionPerformed` method on this object.

#### Step 5 — Compose the Layout

```java
        JPanel form = new JPanel(new GridLayout(4, 2, 5, 5));
        form.add(new JLabel("Name:"));  form.add(nameField);
        form.add(new JLabel("Age:"));   form.add(ageField);
        form.add(male);                 form.add(female);
        form.add(submitBtn);            form.add(resetBtn);

        add(form, BorderLayout.CENTER);
        add(displayLabel, BorderLayout.SOUTH);
        setVisible(true);
    }
```

*Logic row:* `GridLayout(4, 2, 5, 5)` declares **4 rows × 2 columns** with 5 px horizontal and vertical gaps. `BorderLayout.CENTER` is mandatory (otherwise the *Center* region claims zero space). `setVisible(true)` MUST be the last call — the EDT does not paint anything until the component is shown.

#### Step 6 — Implement the Event Handler (the "derivation" of behaviour)

```java
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        if (e.getSource() == submitBtn) {
            String g = male.isSelected() ? "Male" : "Female";
            displayLabel.setText("Name: " + nameField.getText()
                                + " | Age: " + ageField.getText()
                                + " | Gender: " + g);
        } else if (e.getSource() == resetBtn) {
            nameField.setText("");
            ageField.setText("");
            male.setSelected(false);
            female.setSelected(false);
            displayLabel.setText("Details will appear here.");
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SwingUtilities.invokeLater(StudentForm::new);
    }
}
```

*Logic row:* `e.getSource()` is the canonical KTU technique for **source-disambiguated event handling** when one listener serves multiple widgets. `SwingUtilities.invokeLater` queues the GUI construction on the EDT, satisfying the *Single-Thread Rule* of Section 1.3.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common Pitfalls vs Correct Approaches

| # | Student Pitfall (loses 2 marks) | KTU-Approved Correct Approach |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | `add(btn);` directly on the `JFrame` without a panel | Use an intermediate `JPanel` |
| 2 | Setting `setSize` AFTER `setVisible(true)` | Always `setSize` → `add` → `setVisible` |
| 3 | Forgetting `setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE)` | Add it as the first line of the constructor |
| 4 | Using `==` to compare `String` text from `getText()` | Use `.equals()` (though `e.getSource()` is preferred) |
| 5 | Calling `setLayout(null)` and absolute positioning | Use a real `LayoutManager` |
| 6 | Updating UI from a non-EDT thread (e.g., `main` thread) | Wrap inside `SwingUtilities.invokeLater` |
| 7 | Forgetting `ButtonGroup` for radio buttons | Always group `JRadioButton`s |
| 8 | Comparing `getText()` for `JRadioButton` selection | Use `isSelected()` boolean |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Swing Class Hierarchy (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    objA["java lang Object"]
    objB["java awt Component"]
    objC["java awt Container"]
    objD["javax swing JComponent"]

```

### 4.2 Event Dispatch Flow (Delegation Model)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    actor U as User
    participant Btn as JButton
    participant EDT as Event Dispatch Thread
    participant Lst as ActionListener
    participant Hnd as Handler Method
    participant Lbl as JLabel

    U->>Btn: Mouse click / Enter key
    Btn->>EDT: fire ActionEvent e
    EDT->>Lst: invoke actionPerformed
    Lst->>Hnd: dispatch by getSource
    Hnd->>Lbl: setText updated string
    Lbl-->>U: Paint updated text
    EDT->>EDT: return to event loop
```

### 4.3 Typical Application Architecture (Block-Level Functional Map)

```mermaid
graph LR
    blkA["Top Level JFrame"]
    blkB["Menu Bar JMenuBar"]
    blkC["Center JPanel with GridLayout"]
    blkD["North JPanel with FlowLayout"]
    blkE["South JLabel Status"]
    blkF["JTextField 1"]
    blkG["JTextField 2"]
    blkH["JRadioButton Group"]
    blkI["JButton Submit"]
    blkJ["JButton Reset"]
    blkK["ActionListener Implementation"]
    blkL["Event Dispatch Thread"]
    blkM["Model Layer Data Holder"]

    blkA --> blkB
    blkA --> blkC
    blkA --> blkD
    blkA --> blkE
    blkC --> blkF
    blkC --> blkG
    blkC --> blkH
    blkC --> blkI
    blkC --> blkJ
    blkI --> blkK
    blkJ --> blkK
    blkK --> blkL
    blkL --> blkE
    blkK --> blkM
```

### 4.4 Container Layout Visualisation (Mermaid Block Grid)

```mermaid
graph TB
    layA["JFrame BorderLayout ROOT"]
    layB["NORTH Title Bar"]
    layC["WEST JPanel 1"]
    layD["CENTER JPanel 2 with GridLayout"]
    layE["EAST JPanel 3"]
    layF["SOUTH JLabel status"]
    layG["Row 0 Label + JTextField"]
    layH["Row 1 Label + JTextField"]
    layI["Row 2 JCheckBox x2"]
    layJ["Row 3 JButton x2"]

    layA --> layB
    layA --> layC
    layA --> layD
    layA --> layE
    layA --> layF
    layD --> layG
    layD --> layH
    layD --> layI
    layD --> layJ
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
Differentiate between heavyweight and lightweight components in Java. Why are Swing components classified as lightweight?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- *Heavyweight components* (e.g., AWT `Button`, `TextField`) are tied to a *peer* object in the underlying operating system; their rendering and behaviour depend on the host platform (1 mark).
- *Lightweight components* (e.g., `JButton`, `JTextField`) have **no native peer**; they are rendered entirely by Java code on a parent container's surface (1 mark).
- Swing components extend `JComponent` (which extends `java.awt.Container`) and inherit the `paint()` pipeline, allowing the **same look-and-feel** across Windows, macOS, and Linux — hence classified as lightweight (1 mark).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
What is the purpose of the `ButtonGroup` class? Can it be used with `JCheckBox` controls? Justify.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- `ButtonGroup` enforces **mutual exclusion** among a set of buttons such that at most one can be in the *selected* state at any time (1 mark).
- It is **specifically designed** for `AbstractButton` subclasses that exhibit single-select semantics, namely `JRadioButton` and `JRadioButtonMenuItem` (1 mark).
- It **cannot be used meaningfully** with `JCheckBox` because checkboxes are *multi-select* by design; adding them to a `ButtonGroup` would still permit only one to be selected, defeating the purpose of a check-list (1 mark).

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Internal Choice)

> **Note for students:** KTU 2024 ESE rules allow an **internal choice** between Question A and Question B within the same module. Both are provided below.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]*

**(a)** Explain the Swing class hierarchy with a neat diagram, starting from `java.lang.Object` down to `JButton` and `JTextField`. Mention the role of `JComponent` and `JRootPane`. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**
- Begin with `java.lang.Object` at the top (1 mark).
- Descend to `java.awt.Component` (introduces `paint()`, `repaint()`, `setSize()`) and then to `java.awt.Container` (introduces `add()`, `remove()`, `validate()`) — 1 mark.
- `javax.swing.JComponent` extends `Container` and adds *pluggable look-and-feel*, *border support*, *tool-tip text*, *double-buffering*, and *keystroke binding* — 1 mark.
- `JRootPane` (1 mark) is the layered container inside every top-level Swing window (`JFrame`, `JDialog`, `JWindow`, `JApplet`). It contains a `JLayeredPane` holding the *content pane*, *glass pane*, and *menu bar* — 1 mark.
- `JButton` path: `JComponent` → `AbstractButton` → `JToggleButton` → `AbstractButton` (note: `AbstractButton` is the direct parent; `JButton` extends `AbstractButton` directly) — 1 mark.
- `JTextField` path: `JComponent` → `JTextComponent` (abstract) → `JTextField` — 1 mark.

**(b)** Write a complete Java Swing program to create a simple calculator with two `JTextField`s for operands, four `JButton`s for `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, and a `JLabel` to display the result. Handle `ArithmeticException` for division by zero. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (skeleton — full code expected in exam):**
1. Class `Calculator` extends `JFrame` implements `ActionListener` — 1 mark.
2. Instantiate 2 `JTextField`, 4 `JButton("+")/-/*//)`, 1 `JLabel resultLabel` — 1 mark.
3. Add all buttons to a `JPanel` with `GridLayout(2,2)` and fields+label in `BorderLayout.CENTER/NORTH` — 1 mark.
4. `addActionListener(this)` on every button — 1 mark.
5. In `actionPerformed`, parse `Double.parseDouble(t1.getText())` for each operand — 1 mark.
6. Use `if/else` on `e.getSource()` to perform operation; wrap division in `try-catch(ArithmeticException)` showing "Cannot divide by zero" in result label — 1 mark.
7. `setSize`, `setDefaultCloseOperation`, `setVisible(true)` in correct order; wrap constructor call in `SwingUtilities.invokeLater` — 1 mark.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **Delegation Event Model** used by Swing. List any three Swing-specific event listener interfaces and the methods they declare. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**
- Diagram showing *Source* (e.g., `JButton`) → *Event Object* (e.g., `ActionEvent`) → *Listener Interface* (e.g., `ActionListener`) → *Handler Method* (e.g., `actionPerformed`) — 2 marks.
- Explanation that the *source* maintains a list of registered listeners and **delegates** the event to them — 1 mark.
- Listeners are registered via `addXxxListener()` and removed via `removeXxxListener()` — 1 mark.
- Three listener interfaces with their methods (3 × 1 mark each = 3 marks):
  - `ActionListener` → `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)`
  - `ItemListener` → `itemStateChanged(ItemEvent e)`
  - `ChangeListener` → `stateChanged(ChangeEvent e)`
  - *(Bonus acceptable: `ListSelectionListener`, `DocumentListener`, `KeyListener`)*

**(b)** Compare `FlowLayout`, `BorderLayout`, and `GridLayout` in Java Swing with respect to (i) number of regions/components handled, (ii) component resizing policy, and (iii) ideal use case. Provide a code snippet showing the use of `BorderLayout`. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**
1. `FlowLayout` — unlimited components, sized at preferred size, *no* stretching; ideal for toolbars (1 mark).
2. `BorderLayout` — exactly **5 regions** (North, South, East, West, Center); Center stretches both ways, others stretch one axis; ideal for top-level `JFrame` content (1 mark).
3. `GridLayout` — fixed `rows × cols`; *all cells are equal size*; ideal for keypads/calculators (1 mark).
4. Comparative table summarizing the above — 1 mark.
5. Code snippet: `JFrame f = new JFrame(); f.setLayout(new BorderLayout()); f.add(new JButton("N"), BorderLayout.NORTH); f.add(new JButton("C"), BorderLayout.CENTER); f.setSize(300,200); f.setVisible(true);` — 3 marks.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **(−2 marks)** Writing `setVisible(true)` *before* `setSize()` or `add()` — the EDT will paint a zero-sized window, and the examiner will mark it as a logical flaw.
> - **(−2 marks)** Forgetting `setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE)` — the JFrame will not terminate the JVM on close, leading to "dangling process" complaints in practical exams.
> - **(−2 marks)** Comparing `String` results with `==` instead of `.equals()` or using `getText()` on a `JRadioButton` (which returns the *label*, not the selection state).
> - **(−1 mark)** Importing `javax.swing.*` and then typing `JFrame` *without* realising that a top-level container must be **shown** on the EDT using `SwingUtilities.invokeLater` in the `main` method.
> - **(−1 mark)** Confusing `JTextField` (single-line) with `JTextArea` (multi-line) — the latter requires a `JScrollPane` wrapper to be fully functional in KTU practicals.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Swing is lightweight, MVC-based, and lives in `javax.swing`** — never confuse with `java.awt` controls.
- Every GUI must have a **top-level container** (`JFrame` for desktop) holding an **intermediate container** (`JPanel`) holding the **lightweight controls** (`JButton`, etc.).
- The **Delegation Event Model** uses *Listeners* registered via `addXxxListener()`; the source object *delegates* events to all registered listeners.
- Use `e.getSource()` to disambiguate multiple controls handled by a single listener implementing multiple methods.
- `ButtonGroup` is for **radio buttons only** — never for checkboxes.
- `JTextField` = single line; `JTextArea` = multi-line (needs `JScrollPane`); `JPasswordField` masks input.
- `JComboBox` = drop-down single selection; `JList` = scrollable multi/single selection.
- `JSlider` and `JProgressBar` use `ChangeListener`, not `ActionListener`.
- Menu hierarchy: `JMenuBar` (on the frame) → `JMenu` (File, Edit) → `JMenuItem` (New, Open) — and a `JMenu` can recursively contain other `JMenu`s (sub-menus).
- Always invoke GUI construction on the **Event Dispatch Thread** using `SwingUtilities.invokeLater(Runnable)`.
- **Layout rule of thumb:** `BorderLayout` for top-level frames, `FlowLayout` for toolbars, `GridLayout` for grids of equal cells, `GridBagLayout` for complex forms.
- Default close operation, size, and visibility are *set* in that order: `setSize` → `add` → `setVisible(true)`.
- The single most-tested KTU question is: *"Write a Swing program to perform X on button click"* — practice it for at least 5 different scenarios (login form, calculator, registration, voting, temperature converter).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Components and Containers
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT633 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Components and Containers

# Components and Containers in Java GUI Programming

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the Java Abstract Window Toolkit (**AWT**) and **Swing** frameworks, the Graphical User Interface (GUI) is constructed using a hierarchical composition of two fundamental building blocks: **Components** and **Containers**. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 4: GUI Programming using Swing), a **Component** is an abstract class (`java.awt.Component`) that represents any graphical object with a visual representation that can be displayed on the screen and with which the user can interact (via mouse, keyboard, etc.). A **Container** is a specialized subclass of `java.awt.Container` (which itself extends `java.awt.Component`) whose primary purpose is to hold and organize other components, including other containers. The composition follows the **Composite Design Pattern**, enabling the construction of arbitrarily nested GUI trees.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Composite Design Pattern (GoF):** Treats individual objects (Components) and compositions of objects (Containers) uniformly through a common abstract superclass. In AWT/Swing, this is realized through the inheritance chain: `Component` → `Container` → specialized container classes (e.g., `JPanel`, `JFrame`).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are moving into a new house and need to organize your belongings:

- **The House itself** (with its rooms, doors, walls) is a **Container** — it is a *holder* whose purpose is to contain other things. Different types of houses (apartment, villa) act as *top-level containers*.
- **The Furniture inside** the house (sofa, table, bed) are **Components** — they are *visible, interactive objects* that the user can directly use.
- **A Drawer inside a cupboard** is itself a **Component** (you can interact with it), but it is also a **Container** (it holds cutlery). This dual role captures the **Composite Pattern** beautifully.

In Java GUI terms:
- **Top-level Containers** (`JFrame`, `JApplet`, `JDialog`) ↔ The *House* (directly visible, provided by the OS window manager).
- **Intermediate Containers** (`JPanel`) ↔ *Rooms/Drawers* (hold groups of related components).
- **Atomic Components** (`JButton`, `JLabel`, `JTextField`) ↔ *Furniture* (leaf-level interactive objects).

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Terminology

| Term | Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Heavyweight Component** | A component that has its own native OS-level peer (e.g., `java.awt.Button`, `java.awt.TextField`). The peer is a window-system-specific object. |
| **Lightweight Component** | A component that has **no native peer**; it is rendered entirely in Java (e.g., all `javax.swing.J*` components except top-level containers). |
| **Top-Level Container** | A container that cannot be added inside another container; it directly interacts with the Window Manager. The four official top-level containers are `JFrame`, `JApplet`, `JDialog`, `JWindow`. |
| **Root Pane** | A layered pane hierarchy inside top-level containers that manages content, menu bar, glass pane, and layered rendering. |
| **UI Delegate (L&F)** | The pluggable Look-and-Feel object (`ComponentUI`) responsible for painting a Swing component. |
| **Pixel** | The fundamental unit of screen coordinate measurement in AWT/Swing. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Swing Rule of Thumb:** A `JButton` is lightweight even though it is a `Component` because it has *no native peer*. It is painted on the canvas provided by its nearest heavyweight ancestor (usually a `JFrame`).

### 1.4 The Component Class Hierarchy (Foundation)

The entire AWT/Swing GUI API is built on the following inheritance chain:

```
java.lang.Object
   └── java.awt.Component
         └── java.awt.Container
               └── javax.swing.JComponent
                     └── javax.swing.JPanel
                     └── javax.swing.JLabel, JButton, JTextField, ...
```

- **`java.awt.Component`** declares approximately **170+ abstract methods** governing size, position, painting, event handling, focus, and accessibility.
- **`java.awt.Container`** extends `Component` and adds methods to **add, remove, find, validate, and lay out** contained components.
- **`javax.swing.JComponent`** extends `Container` and adds Swing-specific support: pluggable look-and-feel, double-buffering, tooltips, borders, autoscroll, and keyboard actions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Tables

## 2.1 Detailed Classification of Containers

Containers are divided into two broad categories as per the KTU syllabus:

### 2.1.1 Top-Level Containers (Heavyweight)

These are the **only** Swing containers that can stand alone as the main window of an application. They rely on the native window manager.

| Class | Module | Purpose | Default Layout |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `JFrame` | `javax.swing` | Standard main application window with title bar, minimize/maximize/close buttons. | `BorderLayout` |
| `JApplet` | `javax.swing` | Embedded in a web browser or applet viewer (largely deprecated in modern Java). | `BorderLayout` |
| `JDialog` | `javax.swing` | Secondary window used for dialogs (modal or non-modal). | `BorderLayout` |
| `JWindow` | `javax.swing` | Bare window with **no** title bar or borders (rarely used). | `BorderLayout` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** Every top-level container internally uses a **Root Pane** (an instance of `JRootPane`) which in turn contains a `Glass Pane` and a `Layered Pane`. The `Content Pane` (a `JPanel`) is where you actually add components using `getContentPane().add(...)` — or in Java 5+, directly via `frame.add(...)` which delegates to the content pane.

### 2.1.2 General-Purpose (Intermediate) Containers

These containers are **lightweight** and must be added to a top-level container (or another container) to be displayed.

| Class | Module | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `JPanel` | `javax.swing` | Most commonly used general-purpose container. Default layout: `FlowLayout`. |
| `JScrollPane` | `javax.swing` | Provides scroll bars around a single child component. |
| `JSplitPane` | `javax.swing` | Two components split by a draggable divider. |
| `JTabbedPane` | `javax.swing` | Multiple components displayed as tabs. |
| `JToolBar` | `javax.swing` | Holds buttons/menus, often draggable. |
| `Box` / `Box.Filler` | `javax.swing` | Uses `BoxLayout` to arrange components vertically or horizontally. |

### 2.1.3 Special-Purpose Containers

| Class | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- |
| `JLayeredPane` | Allows components to overlap at different depths (z-order). |
| `JDesktopPane` | Container for internal frames (`JInternalFrame`) used in MDI applications. |
| `JRootPane` | Manages layered, content, glass, and menu bar panes inside a top-level container. |

## 2.2 The Component Containment Tree

A Swing GUI is best understood as a **tree** of nodes. The **root** is always a top-level container; the **leaves** are atomic components like `JButton` and `JLabel`.

**Composition Mechanics (Step-by-Step):**

1. **Create a top-level container** (e.g., `JFrame frame = new JFrame("App");`).
2. **Get its content pane** (in Swing 1.4+ this is implicit): `Container cp = frame.getContentPane();`.
3. **Create child components**: `JButton btn = new JButton("Submit");`.
4. **Add components to a container** using `add(Component comp)` or region-specific overloads of `BorderLayout`.
5. **Layout managers** automatically compute the position and size of each child within its parent.
6. **Pack and set visible**: `frame.pack(); frame.setVisible(true);` triggers the layout validation and event-dispatching thread painting.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why `pack()` before `setVisible(true)`?** `pack()` sizes the frame to fit the *preferred size* of its contents as computed by the layout managers. Without it, the frame appears at its default (often zero-pixel) size.

## 2.3 The "Add" Method Overloads in `Container`

The `java.awt.Container` class defines **five overloads** of the `add` method. KTU exams frequently test these.

| Method Signature | Layout Manager | Behaviour |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `Component add(Component comp)` | `FlowLayout`, `BoxLayout`, `GridLayout`, etc. | Appends comp with default constraints. |
| `Component add(Component comp, int index)` | `CardLayout`, `GridBagLayout` | Adds at specified position. |
| `void add(Component comp, Object constraints)` | `BorderLayout`, `GridBagLayout` | Adds with region/constraints object. |
| `void add(String name, Component comp)` | `BorderLayout`, `CardLayout` | Adds with named identifier (legacy 1.0 syntax). |
| `Component add(Component comp, Object constraints, int index)` | `GridBagLayout` | Adds with constraints at index. |

## 2.4 Heavyweight vs. Lightweight Components

This is a **favourite KTU question** for both 3-mark and 14-mark sections.

| Property | Heavyweight (AWT) | Lightweight (Swing) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Native Peer | **Yes** (OS allocates memory) | **No** (drawn in JVM canvas) |
| Package | `java.awt` (e.g., `Button`, `TextField`, `Choice`) | `javax.swing` (e.g., `JButton`, `JTextField`, `JComboBox`) |
| Look-and-Feel | Tied to OS | **Pluggable** (Metal, Nimbus, Motif, etc.) |
| Performance | Faster on some platforms | Slightly slower but consistent |
| Z-ordering Problem | Z-order breaks if mixed with lightweight | Native to Swing |
| Recommendation in KTU Module 4 | **Use only for top-level containers** | **Use for all other components** |

**Engineering Utility:** Lightweight components enable *cross-platform consistency* — a critical property for B.Tech final-year projects targeting the KTU-mandated Java Swing syllabus.

## 2.5 Lifecycle of a Component

Every `Component` (and hence every `Container`) traverses the following lifecycle:

1. **Instantiation** → `new JButton("OK")` — object exists in heap, not yet displayed.
2. **Configuration** → `setFont()`, `setBackground()`, `setEnabled(true)`.
3. **Add to container** → `panel.add(button)` — now part of the GUI tree.
4. **Validation** → `validate()` or `revalidate()` — layout manager computes sizes.
5. **Display** → ancestor `setVisible(true)` causes recursive painting.
6. **User Interaction** → events dispatched via `EventQueue.invokeLater()` / listeners.
7. **Removal / Disposal** → `remove()`, `setVisible(false)`, eventually garbage collected.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs & Code Derivations

## 3.1 Worked Example: Building a Login Form (Step-by-Step Derivation)

This section derives a complete Swing application that demonstrates container-component interaction — exactly the kind of code KTU examiners expect in 14-mark questions.

### Step 1: Derive the Container Tree

Before writing any code, *derive* the desired GUI as a hierarchical tree:

```
Root (Top-Level)
└── JFrame : "Login"
    └── JPanel (Content Pane) — BorderLayout
        ├── North : JLabel ("Enter Credentials")
        ├── Center : JPanel — GridLayout(2 rows, 2 cols)
        │   ├── JLabel ("Username:")
        │   ├── JTextField (15 columns)
        │   ├── JLabel ("Password:")
        │   └── JPasswordField (15 columns)
        └── South : JPanel — FlowLayout(RIGHT)
            ├── JButton ("Submit")
            └── JButton ("Cancel")
```

This is a **composite decomposition** — a key exam skill.

### Step 2: Convert the Tree to Java Code

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;

public class LoginFrame extends JFrame {
    public LoginFrame() {
        setTitle("Login");
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);

        // --- Top label ---
        JLabel header = new JLabel("Enter Credentials", SwingConstants.CENTER);
        add(header, BorderLayout.NORTH);

        // --- Center: form panel with GridLayout(2,2) ---
        JPanel form = new JPanel(new GridLayout(2, 2, 5, 5));
        form.setBorder(BorderFactory.createEmptyBorder(10, 10, 10, 10));
        form.add(new JLabel("Username:"));
        form.add(new JTextField(15));
        form.add(new JLabel("Password:"));
        form.add(new JPasswordField(15));
        add(form, BorderLayout.CENTER);

        // --- South: button bar ---
        JPanel buttons = new JPanel(new FlowLayout(FlowLayout.RIGHT));
        buttons.add(new JButton("Submit"));
        buttons.add(new JButton("Cancel"));
        add(buttons, BorderLayout.SOUTH);

        pack();
        setLocationRelativeTo(null);  // center on screen
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SwingUtilities.invokeLater(() -> new LoginFrame().setVisible(true));
    }
}
```

### Step 3: Justification of Each Layout Choice

| Region | Container | Layout | Why? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| NORTH | Content Pane | BorderLayout | Stretches the header to full window width. |
| CENTER | Inner `JPanel` | `GridLayout(2,2)` | Forces a uniform tabular structure for the 2x2 form. |
| SOUTH | Inner `JPanel` | `FlowLayout(RIGHT)` | Aligns the two buttons to the right edge with default gaps. |

### Step 4: Validate the Event-Dispatch Thread (EDT) Rule

**The rule:** *All Swing component creation and modification must occur on the Event-Dispatch Thread.*

Mathematically, the EDT is a single thread managed by `java.awt.EventQueue`. Any invocation of `Component.add`, `setVisible`, `setText`, etc., from the *main* thread risks concurrency errors. The static helper `SwingUtilities.invokeLater(Runnable r)` enqueues `r` onto the EDT:

$$
\text{EDT} = \{ t \in \mathbb{T} \mid t \text{ is owned by } \text{EventQueue} \}
$$

$$
\text{Safe operation} \iff \text{Thread.currentThread()} \in \text{EDT}
$$

Hence the line `SwingUtilities.invokeLater(() -> new LoginFrame().setVisible(true));` is **mandatory** in every Swing `main` method.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Selecting the Correct Container

| If the requirement is... | Use... | Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| A primary application window | `JFrame` | Only Swing top-level container with full title bar. |
| A modal popup (e.g., "Are you sure?") | `JDialog` | Blocks input to parent frame until dismissed. |
| A form with 2-column label/field rows | `JPanel` + `GridLayout(rows,cols)` | Simplest uniform grid. |
| A sidebar that resizes with the window | `JSplitPane` | Provides draggable divider. |
| Multiple document tabs | `JTabbedPane` | Built-in tab management. |
| Scrollable text area | `JScrollPane(JTextArea)` | Auto-scrollbars. |
| A toolbar of icon buttons | `JToolBar` | Designed to be detachable. |
| Overlapping shapes (canvas drawing) | `JLayeredPane` | Z-order layering. |

## 3.3 Capacity Constraints & Validated Sizing

Each component, when laid out, must report a **preferred size**, **minimum size**, and **maximum size** (all instances of `java.awt.Dimension`).

$$
\text{Preferred} = (\text{prefWidth}, \text{prefHeight})
$$
$$
\text{PreferredWidth} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i + \sum_{j=1}^{g} \text{gap}_j
$$
$$
\text{PreferredHeight} = \max_{i=1}^{n} h_i
$$

where $w_i, h_i$ are the preferred width and height of the $i$-th child, and $g$ is the total number of gaps. This formula is used internally by `FlowLayout` (linear sum for the main axis) and `GridLayout` (uniform cells).

For `BorderLayout`, the formulas are:

$$
\text{NORTH/SOUTH Height} = \max(h_{NORTH}, h_{SOUTH})
$$

$$
\text{EAST/WEST Width} = \max(w_{EAST}, w_{WEST})
$$

$$
\text{CENTER} = \text{ContainerSize} - \text{(NORTH, SOUTH, EAST, WEST) occupied regions}
$$

This is **exam-relevant**: KTU questions often ask "which region gets the remaining space in `BorderLayout`?" — the answer is the **CENTER** region, which always receives all leftover space.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: AWT/Swing Class Hierarchy (Container Focus)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[java.lang.Object] --> B[java.awt.Component]
    B --> C[java.awt.Container]
    C --> D[javax.swing.JComponent]
    C --> E[java.awt.Panel]
    C --> F[java.awt.Window]
    C --> G[java.awt.ScrollPane]
    D --> H[javax.swing.JPanel]
    D --> I[javax.swing.JLabel]
    D --> J[javax.swing.JButton]
    D --> K[javax.swing.JTextField]
    D --> L[javax.swing.JScrollPane]
    F --> M[java.awt.Frame]
    F --> N[java.awt.Dialog]
    M --> O[javax.swing.JFrame]
    N --> P[javax.swing.JDialog]
    H --> Q[JLayeredPane]
    O --> R[JRootPane]
    R --> S[GlassPane]
    R --> T[LayeredPane]
    R --> U[ContentPane]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Root Pane Internal Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    RP[JRootPane] --> GP[Glass Pane - transparent overlay]
    RP --> LP[Layered Pane]
    LP --> MB[Menu Bar layer]
    LP --> CP[Content Pane layer]
    LP --> PP[Palette layer]
    LP --> DP[Drag layer]
    CP --> C1[Child components]
    CP --> C2[Intermediate containers JPanel]
    GP --> HD[Used to intercept mouse globally]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The **Glass Pane** is a clever Swing trick — although invisible by default, it sits *above* the Content Pane in z-order. Drawing on it (e.g., a custom animation) renders above all other Swing components without altering their layout.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Swing Application

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph EDT
        M[main method]
        IL[SwingUtilities.invokeLater]
    end
    M --> IL
    IL --> JC[JFrame creation]
    JC --> CP[getContentPane]
    CP --> JP[JPanel setup]
    JP --> LM[setLayout LayoutManager]
    JP --> AD[add components]
    AD --> VL[validate / revalidate]
    VL --> PK[pack method]
    PK --> SV[setVisible true]
    SV --> EQ[EventQueue dispatch loop]
    EQ --> EH[Event Handlers Listeners]
    EH --> RT[Repaint requests]
    RT --> EQ
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Decision Tree — Which Container to Use?

```mermaid
graph TD
    A{Need a top-level window?} -->|Yes| B{Modal popup?}
    A -->|No| C{Need scrolling?}
    B -->|Yes| D[Use JDialog]
    B -->|No| E[Use JFrame]
    C -->|Yes| F[Use JScrollPane]
    C -->|No| G{Need tabs?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Use JTabbedPane]
    G -->|No| I{Need resizable split?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Use JSplitPane]
    I -->|No| K[Use JPanel with LayoutManager]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Before You Write!**
> 1. **Forgetting `pack()`** before `setVisible(true)` results in a 0×0 window — the examiner deducts **2 marks** for "incomplete program".
> 2. **Forgetting `SwingUtilities.invokeLater(...)`** loses **1 mark** under the "EDT compliance" criterion — KTU explicitly tests this.
> 3. **Confusing `add(comp)` with `add(comp, BorderLayout.NORTH)`** — the former works for `FlowLayout/GridLayout` but **silently fails to position** in `BorderLayout`. Always specify the region explicitly.
> 4. **Mixing AWT and Swing heavyweight/lightweight** components causes z-order flicker — the examiner checks if the student knows to use Swing *only* for everything except top-level containers.
> 5. **Using deprecated `getContentPane().add(...)` chain unnecessarily** is acceptable but not preferred; mark evaluators reward direct `frame.add(...)` (Java 5+).

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023 (Model Paper)]
**Differentiate between heavyweight and lightweight components in Java AWT/Swing. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Heavyweight components** are those which have a native peer — a corresponding window-system-specific object allocated by the operating system. Their appearance and behaviour depend on the underlying platform. *Example:* `java.awt.Button`, `java.awt.TextField`, `java.awt.Frame`. *(1.5 marks)*
- **Lightweight components** are those which have **no** native peer; they are rendered entirely by Java code on the canvas provided by their nearest heavyweight ancestor. They support the **pluggable Look-and-Feel** architecture. *Example:* `javax.swing.JButton`, `javax.swing.JLabel`, `javax.swing.JPanel`. *(1.5 marks)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 (Model Paper)]
**What is a Container in AWT? List any two top-level containers defined in Swing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Container** is a subclass of `java.awt.Component` that has the additional capability of holding and organizing other components (including other containers). It implements the **Composite Design Pattern**, enabling the construction of nested GUI trees. *(1.5 marks)*

The four top-level containers in Swing are:
1. `javax.swing.JFrame` — Standard main application window.
2. `javax.swing.JDialog` — Modal or non-modal dialog window.
3. `javax.swing.JApplet` — Embedded in a browser (deprecated).
4. `javax.swing.JWindow` — Bare window without title bar.

*(Naming any two: 0.75 marks each = 1.5 marks)*

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Pattern: Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks  `[KTU University Exam — July 2024 Module 4]`

**(a)** Explain the **Composite Design Pattern** as implemented by the AWT/Swing class hierarchy. Draw the inheritance hierarchy from `java.lang.Object` to `JButton` and `JFrame`. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Write a complete Java Swing program to create a form with **two `JTextField`s**, **two `JLabel`s**, and **one `JButton`** arranged in a `GridLayout(3, 2)`. The program must use `JFrame` and follow the Event-Dispatch Thread rule. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 marks

**Composite Design Pattern (3 marks):**

The Composite Design Pattern allows clients to treat individual objects (*leaf components*) and compositions of objects (*container components*) uniformly through a common abstract type. In AWT/Swing:

- The common abstract supertype is `java.awt.Component`.
- Leaves are atomic components like `JButton`, `JLabel`, `JTextField` — they have **no** children.
- Composites are `Container` subclasses like `JPanel`, `JFrame` — they may contain other `Component`s, including other containers.
- Because `Container extends Component`, the same `add()`, `remove()`, `paint()`, and event-handling code can operate uniformly on a leaf or a subtree. *(2 marks for explanation + 1 mark for relevance to AWT)*

**Inheritance Hierarchy (4 marks):**

```
java.lang.Object
   └── java.awt.Component
         └── java.awt.Container
               ├── java.awt.Window
               │     └── java.awt.Frame
               │           └── javax.swing.JFrame
               └── javax.swing.JComponent
                     ├── javax.swing.JPanel
                     ├── javax.swing.JLabel
                     ├── javax.swing.JButton
                     └── javax.swing.JTextField
```

*Note: Both `JButton` and `JFrame` descend from `Component` through `Container` (in the case of `JFrame` directly, and through `JComponent` for `JButton`). The student is expected to draw or describe this hierarchy clearly. Marks: 1 for the chain correctness, 1 for `JFrame` branch, 1 for `JButton` branch, 1 for the common ancestor `Component`/`Container`.*

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 marks

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;

public class GridFormDemo extends JFrame {
    public GridFormDemo() {
        setTitle("Grid Form");
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);

        // Create a JPanel with GridLayout(3 rows, 2 cols, 5px hgap, 5px vgap)
        JPanel panel = new JPanel(new GridLayout(3, 2, 5, 5));
        panel.setBorder(BorderFactory.createEmptyBorder(10, 10, 10, 10));

        // Row 1
        panel.add(new JLabel("Name:"));
        panel.add(new JTextField(15));

        // Row 2
        panel.add(new JLabel("Email:"));
        panel.add(new JTextField(15));

        // Row 3
        panel.add(new JButton("Submit"));
        panel.add(new JLabel(""));  // empty cell to maintain grid structure

        add(panel);  // default BorderLayout.CENTER

        pack();
        setLocationRelativeTo(null);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // EDT-safe execution
        SwingUtilities.invokeLater(() -> new GridFormDemo().setVisible(true));
    }
}
```

**Mark Allocation (7 marks):**
- Correct class declaration extending `JFrame`: **1 mark**
- Use of `GridLayout(3,2)` with horizontal/vertical gap parameters: **1.5 marks**
- Correct creation of 2 `JLabel`s, 2 `JTextField`s, 1 `JButton` and adding to panel: **1.5 marks**
- Use of `pack()` and `setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE)`: **1 mark**
- `SwingUtilities.invokeLater(...)` wrapping `setVisible(true)`: **1 mark**
- Proper imports, clean code, and `setLocationRelativeTo(null)`: **1 mark**

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative)  `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Module 4]`

**(a)** Describe the **Root Pane** architecture inside a `JFrame`. What is the role of the **Glass Pane** and the **Content Pane**? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Write a Java Swing program that demonstrates the use of **`JScrollPane`** wrapping a `JTextArea`, placed inside a `JFrame` along with a `JLabel` header. The window should have a `JMenuBar` containing **one `JMenu` "File"** with **one `JMenuItem` "Exit"** that closes the application. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 marks

**Root Pane Architecture (7 marks):**

Every top-level Swing container (`JFrame`, `JDialog`, `JWindow`, `JApplet`) contains exactly one **Root Pane** (an instance of `javax.swing.JRootPane`). The Root Pane is a layered container that orchestrates all the visible content of the top-level window. Its structure is:

```
JRootPane
├── Glass Pane   (instance of JPanel, default root pane itself)
├── Layered Pane (JLayeredPane)
│     ├── Menu Bar layer (for JMenuBar)
│     ├── Content Pane layer (JPanel, where components are added)
│     ├── Palette layer
│     └── Drag layer
└── (content slot)
```

**Content Pane (2.5 marks):**
- It is a `JPanel` that occupies the region between the menu bar and the window border.
- All visible components (`JButton`, `JLabel`, etc.) are added to the Content Pane.
- Modern Java (1.5+) auto-delegates `frame.add(...)` to the Content Pane, so explicit `getContentPane().add(...)` is no longer mandatory.

**Glass Pane (2.5 marks):**
- It is an instance of `JPanel` that sits *above* the Layered Pane (and hence above all other panes) in z-order.
- It is **invisible by default** but can be made visible to intercept mouse and keyboard events globally — useful for custom cursors, drag-to-select overlays, or progress indicators.
- It is obtained via `rootPane.getGlassPane()`.

**Why the Root Pane exists (2 marks):**
The Root Pane was introduced in Swing to provide a consistent, layered rendering model that AWT lacked. It separates the concerns of *content management* (Content Pane), *layering* (Layered Pane), *menu integration* (Menu Bar), and *event interception* (Glass Pane) into independently manageable components, all of which can be customized without altering the underlying top-level window.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 marks

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;

public class ScrollPaneDemo extends JFrame {

    public ScrollPaneDemo() {
        setTitle("Scroll Pane Demo");
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setSize(500, 400);
        setLocationRelativeTo(null);

        // --- Menu Bar ---
        JMenuBar menuBar = new JMenuBar();
        JMenu fileMenu = new JMenu("File");
        JMenuItem exitItem = new JMenuItem("Exit");
        exitItem.addActionListener(e -> System.exit(0));
        fileMenu.add(exitItem);
        menuBar.add(fileMenu);
        setJMenuBar(menuBar);

        // --- Header Label ---
        JLabel header = new JLabel("Log Output", SwingConstants.CENTER);
        header.setFont(new Font("SansSerif", Font.BOLD, 16));
        add(header, BorderLayout.NORTH);

        // --- TextArea inside JScrollPane ---
        JTextArea textArea = new JTextArea(15, 40);
        textArea.setLineWrap(true);
        textArea.setWrapStyleWord(true);
        JScrollPane scrollPane = new JScrollPane(textArea);
        add(scrollPane, BorderLayout.CENTER);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SwingUtilities.invokeLater(() -> new ScrollPaneDemo().setVisible(true));
    }
}
```

**Mark Allocation (7 marks):**
- Class extending `JFrame` with title and `setDefaultCloseOperation`: **0.5 mark**
- `JMenuBar` with one `JMenu` and one `JMenuItem`, properly bound to `setJMenuBar`: **2 marks**
- `JLabel` header in `BorderLayout.NORTH`: **1 mark**
- `JTextArea` wrapped in `JScrollPane` and added to `CENTER`: **2 marks**
- `Exit` action listener calling `System.exit(0)`: **0.5 mark**
- `SwingUtilities.invokeLater` for EDT compliance: **1 mark**

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Last-Minute Revision — Components and Containers**

- **Root of all GUI classes:** `java.lang.Object` → `java.awt.Component` → `java.awt.Container` → `javax.swing.JComponent`.
- **Composite Pattern** is the architectural foundation — Containers are Components that can hold other Components.
- **Four top-level containers:** `JFrame`, `JDialog`, `JApplet` (deprecated), `JWindow`. All are **heavyweight**.
- **General-purpose container:** `JPanel` — the most frequently used intermediate container with default `FlowLayout`.
- **Heavyweight vs. Lightweight:** AWT components (e.g., `Button`, `TextField`) are heavyweight (native peers); all `J*` components (except top-level containers) are lightweight (no native peer, pluggable L&F).
- **Root Pane components:** `Glass Pane` (top z-order, for overlays), `Layered Pane` (z-order manager), `Content Pane` (where you `add()` components), `Menu Bar` (for `JMenuBar`).
- **The `add()` method has 5 overloads** — know the `(Component)`, `(Component, Object)`, and `(String, Component)` signatures.
- **BorderLayout regions:** NORTH, SOUTH, EAST, WEST, CENTER — only **CENTER** receives leftover space.
- **EDT Rule:** Always wrap GUI creation in `SwingUtilities.invokeLater(Runnable)`. Using `main` thread directly loses marks.
- **Always call `pack()`** before `setVisible(true)` to size the window to its preferred content size.
- **Default layouts:** `JFrame` → `BorderLayout`; `JPanel` → `FlowLayout`; `JApplet` → `BorderLayout`; `JDialog` → `BorderLayout`; `JScrollPane` → internal `ScrollPaneLayout`; `Box` → `BoxLayout`.
- **Mixing AWT + Swing in the same container** causes z-order bugs — strictly avoid. Use Swing for everything below the top-level container.
- **Five properties to remember for any component:** preferred size, minimum size, maximum size, location, bounds — all are instances of `java.awt.Dimension` (for size) or `Point` (for location).
- **Default close operation constants** (in `JFrame`): `EXIT_ON_CLOSE`, `DISPOSE_ON_CLOSE`, `DO_NOTHING_ON_CLOSE`, `HIDE_ON_CLOSE`.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
