<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 4 — Swing Packages: Foundation Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Swing Packages** in Java refer to the comprehensive set of classes, interfaces, and APIs provided under the **`javax.swing`** package hierarchy, which form the **Standard Widget Toolkit (SWT-alternative)** for building **Graphical User Interfaces (GUIs)** in Java. Swing is part of the **Java Foundation Classes (JFC)** — a suite of APIs designed to provide a rich, platform-independent, and highly customizable UI framework that operates entirely in **pure Java** without relying on the underlying Operating System's native peer components.

The **`javax.swing`** package extends the capabilities of the older **AWT (Abstract Window Toolkit)** by offering **lightweight components** that are rendered using Java's own 2D drawing API rather than relying on host OS controls.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "javax" and not "java"?** The "x" in `javax` historically denoted "extension" packages. Although Swing was promoted to a core package in Java SE, the original naming convention was preserved for backward compatibility — a classic KTU 2-mark favorite question.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🏠

Imagine you are an architect designing a modern house interior:

| Concept | Swing Analogy | AWT Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| **Lightweight components** (JButton, JLabel) | Custom-designed furniture you can repaint, re-shape, and restyle infinitely | Readymade wooden chairs bought from a local carpenter |
| **Heavyweight components** (AWT Button, TextField) | None — Swing is purely lightweight | OS-native chairs that look different in every country |
| **Pluggable Look & Feel** | Changing the entire interior decor theme (Modern, Classic, Minimalist) with one switch | Stuck with the carpenter's style — cannot change fabric |
| **MVC Architecture** | A restaurant with separate Chefs (Model), Waiters (View), and Manager (Controller) | One person doing everything — messy and inflexible |

In essence, **Swing = Decoupled, Customizable, Platform-Independent UI Toolkit**.

## 1.3 Key Characteristics of Swing

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Properties of Swing (Must remember for 2-mark questions):**
> 1. **Lightweight Architecture** — Components are drawn by Java, not the OS.
> 2. **Pluggable Look and Feel (PLAF)** — UI appearance can be dynamically switched.
> 3. **MVC-based Design** — Clean separation of data, display, and logic.
> 4. **Platform Independent** — Same appearance across Windows, Linux, macOS.
> 5. **Rich Component Set** — Tables, trees, tabbed panes, sliders, etc.
> 6. **Double Buffering** — Built-in to eliminate flicker during repaint.
> 7. **Event Delegation Model** — Clean event handling via listeners.

## 1.4 Swing Packages Hierarchy — The Major Sub-Packages

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **javax.swing** is the root package. Below it, several sub-packages organize related classes:

| Sub-Package | Purpose | Key Classes |
|---|---|---|
| `javax.swing` | Core components, top-level containers | `JFrame`, `JPanel`, `JButton`, `JLabel` |
| `javax.swing.event` | Event handling interfaces and classes | `ChangeEvent`, `ListSelectionEvent` |
| `javax.swing.border` | Border styles for components | `LineBorder`, `TitledBorder`, `EtchedBorder` |
| `javax.swing.plaf` | Pluggable Look and Feel (abstract + basic) | `ComponentUI`, `BasicLookAndFeel` |
| `javax.swing.table` | Table component support | `TableModel`, `JTable`, `TableColumn` |
| `javax.swing.tree` | Tree component support | `TreeNode`, `MutableTreeNode`, `JTree` |
| `javax.swing.text` | Text component support | `JTextField`, `JTextArea`, `Document` |
| `javax.swing.undo` | Undo/Redo support | `UndoManager`, `UndoableEdit` |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 Module 4 — Deep Theoretical Analysis of Swing Architecture

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Swing Design

> [!NOTE]
> Swing's superiority over AWT stems from **three architectural pillars** that examiners love to test. Memorize the model below thoroughly.

### Pillar 1: Lightweight vs. Heavyweight Components

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heavyweight Components (AWT):** Each AWT component has a **peer** — a native OS counterpart (Win32, Cocoa, X11). The JVM delegates drawing and event handling to the OS. Hence, appearance = OS-dependent.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lightweight Components (Swing):** Swing components **do NOT have OS peers**. They are drawn entirely by Java's 2D API using the `paintComponent()` method inherited from `JComponent`. They rely on a **single heavyweight ancestor** (usually `JFrame`) for OS interaction.

$$
\text{Lightweight Component} = \text{Java-rendered} + \text{No OS Peer} + \text{Inherits Native Window from Ancestor}
$$

**Key Advantage:** A Swing `JButton` on Windows looks identical to one on Linux, and crucially, can be **re-skinned dynamically** using PLAF.

### Pillar 2: The MVC Architecture in Swing

> [!NOTE]
> Swing strictly follows the **Model-View-Controller (MVC)** pattern, although Java calls it a slight variation — **"Separable Model Architecture"** because the View and Controller are sometimes bundled (e.g., `UIResource`).

| MVC Layer | Swing Equivalent | Responsibility | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Model** | Interface like `ButtonModel`, `ListModel` | Stores data and state | Stores whether button is pressed |
| **View** | `ComponentUI` subclasses | Renders the visual representation | Paints the button on screen |
| **Controller** | `ComponentUI` + Event Listeners | Handles user input, updates Model | Mouse click triggers `ActionEvent` |

**Flow Logic:**
1. User clicks the button → Controller receives event.
2. Controller mutates the **Model** (e.g., toggles `isSelected()`).
3. Model fires a **ChangeEvent** to all registered listeners.
4. **View** re-renders itself based on updated Model state.

### Pillar 3: Pluggable Look and Feel (PLAF)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> PLAF is a **strategy pattern** implementation that allows the entire UI to swap its rendering logic at runtime.

**Available Built-in Look & Feels:**

| Look & Feel | Class Identifier | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Metal (Ocean) | `"javax.swing.plaf.metal.MetalLookAndFeel"` | Default cross-platform Java look |
| Nimbus | `"javax.swing.plaf.nimbus.NimbusLookAndFeel"` | Modern polished appearance (recommended) |
| System | `UIManager.getSystemLookAndFeelClassName()` | Matches host OS |
| Motif | `"com.sun.java.swing.plaf.motif.MotifLookAndFeel"` | Unix-style |

**Code Pattern:**
```java
try {
    UIManager.setLookAndFeel("javax.swing.plaf.nimbus.NimbusLookAndFeel");
    SwingUtilities.updateComponentTreeUI(frame);
} catch (Exception e) { e.printStackTrace(); }
```

## 2.2 Container Hierarchy (The Top-Level → Content Tree)

> [!NOTE]
> A Swing GUI is built as a **tree of containers**. Understanding this hierarchy is critical for KTU lab viva and theory.

$$
\text{JFrame} \to \text{Content Pane (JRootPane)} \to \text{Glass Pane + Layered Pane} \to \text{JPanel} \to \text{JButton, JLabel, ...}
$$

| Container | Type | Role |
|---|---|---|
| `JFrame` | Top-Level (Heavyweight) | The main window with title bar, minimize/maximize/close |
| `JDialog` | Top-Level | Secondary windows (e.g., message dialogs) |
| `JApplet` | Top-Level (Deprecated in Java 17+) | Browser-embedded (replaced by Java Web Start) |
| `JPanel` | Intermediate | Generic container for grouping components |
| `JScrollPane` | Intermediate | Adds scrollbars to a single child component |
| `JTabbedPane` | Intermediate | Multiple tabs sharing same space |
| `JSplitPane` | Intermediate | Resizable two-pane layout |
| `JRootPane` | Internal | Manages glass pane, layered pane, content pane |

## 2.3 Component Inheritance Chain

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every Swing component inherits from `JComponent` (which itself extends `java.awt.Container`). This means every Swing component supports:
> - **Tooltips** via `setToolTipText(String)`
> - **Double buffering** (automatic)
> - **Borders** via `setBorder(Border)`
> - **Custom painting** via overriding `paintComponent(Graphics g)`

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates the **must-memorize** facts for KTU theory exams.

| Property | AWT (Heavyweight) | Swing (Lightweight) |
|---|---|---|
| Package | `java.awt` | `javax.swing` |
| Class prefix | None (Button, TextField) | `J` prefix (JButton, JTextField) |
| Peer dependency | Yes — native OS peer | No — pure Java rendering |
| Platform dependent | Yes | No (with PLAF) |
| MVC support | No | Yes (full) |
| Look & Feel | Fixed by OS | Pluggable, switchable at runtime |
| Double buffering | Manual | Automatic |
| Component count | Limited (~14) | Rich (~40+) |
| Painting method | `paint(Graphics g)` | `paintComponent(Graphics g)` (override this!) |
| Top container | `Frame` | `JFrame` |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Enterprise Applications:** IDEs like **NetBeans** and **IntelliJ IDEA** use Swing-based UI components (intensively) for their desktop clients.
- **Banking & POS Systems:** Swing powers ATM simulation software and Point-of-Sale terminals requiring **cross-platform** consistency.
- **Scientific Visualization:** Tools like **ImageJ** (used in biomedical research) leverage Swing for image processing UIs.
- **Rapid Prototyping:** Swing's RAD-friendly API allows quick mockup of engineering dashboards.

# 📘 Module 4 — Step-by-Step Code Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Anatomy of the Simplest Swing Program

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every Swing application follows a **4-step ritual**. KTU lab exams and 7-mark theory questions will require you to write this skeleton from memory.

**Step-by-Step Construction (with rationale):**

### Step 1: Import the Required Packages
```java
import javax.swing.*;          // Core Swing components
import java.awt.*;             // Layout managers, Color, Font
import java.awt.event.*;       // Event handling interfaces
```

### Step 2: Extend `JFrame` (or instantiate one)
```java
public class SwingDemo extends JFrame {
    JLabel lbl;
    JButton btn;
    JTextField txt;
```
**Rationale:** Extending `JFrame` allows the class itself to act as the window. The three declared components are class-level so that event handlers can access them (closures across inner classes).

### Step 3: Constructor — Initialize Components and Add Listeners
```java
    SwingDemo() {
        setTitle("KTU Swing Demo");
        setSize(400, 300);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());

        lbl = new JLabel("Enter your name:");
        txt = new JTextField(15);
        btn = new JButton("Submit");

        btn.addActionListener(new ActionListener() {
            public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
                String name = txt.getText();
                lbl.setText("Hello, " + name + "!");
            }
        });

        add(lbl); add(txt); add(btn);
        setVisible(true);
    }
```
**Rationale Walkthrough:**
- `setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE)` — terminates JVM on window close.
- `FlowLayout` — left-to-right horizontal placement.
- `addActionListener(...)` — registers the **Controller** of the MVC pattern.
- The anonymous inner class implements the **ActionListener** interface, overriding `actionPerformed()` — the **callback method** invoked on user interaction.
- `setVisible(true)` — must be called **last**, after all components are added (otherwise initial render is empty).

### Step 4: Main Method
```java
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SwingUtilities.invokeLater(new Runnable() {
            public void run() {
                new SwingDemo();
            }
        });
    }
}
```
**Rationale:** `SwingUtilities.invokeLater()` ensures that **all Swing component creation and UI updates occur on the Event Dispatch Thread (EDT)**. This is a **thread-safety best practice** in Swing.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake (KTU Lab Exam):** Calling `new SwingDemo()` directly inside `main()` without `invokeLater()` is a **frequently-deducted-mark** issue. Although it works for trivial programs, the official Java Swing tutorial mandates EDT usage.

| Component | Purpose | Key Constructor(s) | Critical Methods | Test Frequency |

| `JButton` | Clickable button | `JButton()`, `JButton(String)`, `JButton(Icon)` | `addActionListener(...)`, `setEnabled(bool)`, `doClick()` | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| `JTextField` | Single-line text input | `JTextField(int cols)`, `JTextField(String text, int cols)` | `getText()`, `setText(String)`, `addActionListener(...)` | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| `JTextArea` | Multi-line text input | `JTextArea(int rows, int cols)`, `JTextArea(String text, int r, int c)` | `append(String)`, `setLineWrap(bool)`, `setWrapStyleWord(bool)` | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| `JCheckBox` | Toggle on/off | `JCheckBox(String)`, `JCheckBox(String, boolean selected)` | `isSelected()`, `addItemListener(...)`, `setSelected(bool)` | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

| `JList<T>` | Scrollable list of items | `JList()`, `JList(Object[] items)`, `JList(ListModel)` | `setListData(...)`, `getSelectedValue()`, `addListSelectionListener(...)` | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| `JTable` | Tabular data display | `JTable()`, `JTable(Object[][] data, Object[] colNames)`, `JTable(TableModel)` | `setModel(...)`, `getValueAt(r,c)`, `setValueAt(...)` | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| `JSlider` | Continuous/discrete value | `JSlider(int min, int max, int value)`, `JSlider(int orientation, min, max, val)` | `getValue()`, `setValue(int)`, `addChangeListener(...)` | ⭐⭐ |
| `JProgressBar` | Visual progress indicator | `JProgressBar()`, `JProgressBar(int orient, int min, int max)` | `setValue(int)`, `setIndeterminate(bool)`, `setStringPainted(bool)` | ⭐⭐ |
| `JMenuBar/JMenu/JMenuItem` | Menu system | `JMenu(String)`, `JMenuItem(String)` | `addSeparator()`, `add(JMenuItem)`, `setMnemonic(char)` | ⭐⭐⭐ |

## 3.3 Layout Managers — The Geometric Arrangers

> [!NOTE]
> A **LayoutManager** is a `java.awt.LayoutManager` interface implementation that controls the size and position of components inside a container. KTU often asks to **write the code snippet using a specific layout**.

| Layout Manager | Behavior | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| `FlowLayout` | Left-to-right, wraps to next line | Toolbars, button rows |
| `BorderLayout` | 5 regions: North, South, East, West, Center | Main window skeletons |
| `GridLayout(rows, cols)` | Equal-sized grid cells | Calculator-style UIs |
| `GridBagLayout` | Most flexible — cells of varying size, anchor, fill | Complex forms |
| `BoxLayout` | Single-axis (X or Y) stacking | Vertical menus, toolbars |
| `CardLayout` | Stack of panels, only one visible at a time | Wizards, tab simulations |
| `SpringLayout` | Constraint-based positioning | Used by GUI builders |
| `GroupLayout` | Hierarchical groups (parallel/sequential) | NetBeans Matisse designer |

## 3.4 Event Handling — The Delegation Event Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Swing uses the **Event Delegation Model (EDM)**: events generated by a source component are **delegated** to a registered **listener** object, which then processes them.

$$
\text{Source Component} \xrightarrow{\text{fires event}} \text{Event Object} \xrightarrow{\text{passed to}} \text{Listener Method}
$$

**The Three Pillars of EDM:**

1. **Event Source** — the component generating the event (e.g., `JButton`).
2. **Event Object** — encapsulates event data (e.g., `ActionEvent`).
3. **Event Listener** — interface implemented by the handler, contains the callback method.

**Event-Listener Mapping Table (Exam-Critical):**

| Event Class | Listener Interface | Key Method | Triggered By |
|---|---|---|---|
| `ActionEvent` | `ActionListener` | `actionPerformed(ActionEvent)` | Button click, menu item, Enter in text field |
| `ItemEvent` | `ItemListener` | `itemStateChanged(ItemEvent)` | Checkbox, radio button toggle |
| `ChangeEvent` | `ChangeListener` | `stateChanged(ChangeEvent)` | Slider, spinner, progress bar |
| `MouseEvent` | `MouseListener` / `MouseMotionListener` | `mouseClicked/Pressed/Released/Entered/Exited` | All mouse interactions |
| `KeyEvent` | `KeyListener` | `keyTyped/Pressed/Released(KeyEvent)` | Keyboard input |
| `WindowEvent` | `WindowListener` | `windowOpened/Closing/Closed/Activated/...` | Window lifecycle |
| `ListSelectionEvent` | `ListSelectionListener` | `valueChanged(ListSelectionEvent)` | `JList` row selection |
| `FocusEvent` | `FocusListener` | `focusGained/Lost(FocusEvent)` | Component focus change |

## 3.5 Code Walkthrough — Adapter Classes

> [!NOTE]
> Many listener interfaces have **multiple methods** (e.g., `MouseListener` has 5). Implementing all of them is tedious. Java provides **Adapter classes** that implement the interface with **empty method bodies**, so you override only what you need.

```java
btn.addMouseListener(new MouseAdapter() {
    @Override
    public void mouseClicked(MouseEvent e) {
        System.out.println("Clicked at: " + e.getPoint());
    }
});
```
**Available Adapter Classes:** `MouseAdapter`, `KeyAdapter`, `WindowAdapter`, `FocusAdapter`, `ComponentAdapter`.

## 3.6 Lambda Expression Equivalent (Java 8+)

> [!NOTE]
> Since Swing listener interfaces are **functional interfaces** (single abstract method), they can be replaced with **lambda expressions** for cleaner code.

```java
// Traditional (verbose):
btn.addActionListener(new ActionListener() {
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) { 
        System.out.println("Clicked!"); 
    }
});

// Lambda (concise):
btn.addActionListener(e -> System.out.println("Clicked!"));
```

This transformation is **highly relevant** for KTU's Java 8+ syllabus focus.

## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any three features of Java Swing. Differentiate between AWT and Swing.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Three features of Swing:**
> 1. **Lightweight Components** — Swing components have no native OS peer; they are drawn entirely in Java using the 2D API.
> 2. **Pluggable Look and Feel (PLAF)** — The UI's appearance can be switched at runtime using `UIManager.setLookAndFeel()`.
> 3. **MVC Architecture** — Each component separates its data (Model), appearance (View), and input handling (Controller).

| Feature | AWT | Swing |
|---|---|---|
| Package | `java.awt` | `javax.swing` |
| Component type | Heavyweight (OS peer) | Lightweight (pure Java) |
| Look and Feel | Fixed by OS | Pluggable |
| Class prefix | None | `J` prefix |
| Component variety | Limited | Rich and extensive |

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the Delegation Event Model used in Swing with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

> The **Event Delegation Model (EDM)** delegates event processing to **listener objects** rather than the component handling the event itself. The three participants are:
> 1. **Event Source** — the component generating the event (e.g., `JButton`).
> 2. **Event Object** — encapsulates event data (e.g., `ActionEvent`).
> 3. **Event Listener** — interface with a callback method (e.g., `ActionListener.actionPerformed`).

**Example:**
```java
JButton b = new JButton("Click Me");
b.addActionListener(e -> System.out.println("Button Clicked!"));
```
Here, `JButton` is the **source**, `ActionEvent` is the **event object**, and the lambda is the **listener** whose `actionPerformed` method is invoked when the user clicks the button.

## 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, you must answer **ONE full question (a + b)**. Both sub-parts carry **7 marks each**.

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | 14 Marks]`

**(a)** Explain the MVC architecture used in Swing with a neat diagram. List any two advantages of MVC. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Write a Java Swing program to create a simple calculator GUI with two text fields for input, four buttons (+, −, ×, ÷), and a label to display the result. Use appropriate Layout Manager. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### 📌 Model Solution for Part (a) — MVC Architecture

> The **Model-View-Controller (MVC)** pattern in Swing divides each component into three logical units:

| Layer | Role | Swing Representation |
|---|---|---|
| **Model** | Stores the data and internal state of the component | `ButtonModel`, `ListModel`, `TableModel`, `Document` |
| **View** | Renders the visual representation of the component | `ComponentUI` subclasses (e.g., `ButtonUI`, `LabelUI`) |
| **Controller** | Handles user input and updates the Model accordingly | Event Listeners (`ActionListener`, `MouseListener`, etc.) |

**Workflow:**

1. User interacts with the **View** (e.g., clicks a `JButton`).
2. The **Controller** (Listener) receives the event and mutates the **Model** (e.g., toggles `isPressed`).
3. The **Model** fires a `ChangeEvent` to all registered listeners.
4. The **View** queries the updated Model and re-renders itself.

**Diagram:**
```mermaid
graph LR
    U["User"] -->|clicks| V["View ComponentUI"]
    V -->|triggers| C["Controller Listener"]
    C -->|mutates| M["Model ButtonModel"]
    M -->|fires ChangeEvent| C
    C -->|repaint| V
```

**Two Advantages of MVC:**
1. **Separation of Concerns** — Data, presentation, and logic are isolated, simplifying maintenance and debugging.
2. **Multiple Views from One Model** — A single Model can drive multiple Views (e.g., a bar chart and pie chart from the same dataset), enabling flexible UI design.
3. **Reusability** — Models and Views can be reused across applications independently.

---

#### 📌 Model Solution for Part (b) — Calculator Program

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class CalculatorGUI extends JFrame implements ActionListener {
    JTextField tf1, tf2;
    JLabel result;
    JButton add, sub, mul, div;

    CalculatorGUI() {
        setTitle("Simple Calculator");
        setSize(350, 200);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());

        tf1 = new JTextField(8);
        tf2 = new JTextField(8);
        add = new JButton("+");
        sub = new JButton("-");
        mul = new JButton("*");
        div = new JButton("/");
        result = new JLabel("Result: ");

        add(tf1); add(tf2);
        add(add); add(sub); add(mul); add(div);
        add(result);

        add.addActionListener(this);
        sub.addActionListener(this);
        mul.addActionListener(this);
        div.addActionListener(this);

        setVisible(true);
    }

    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        try {
            double a = Double.parseDouble(tf1.getText());
            double b = Double.parseDouble(tf2.getText());
            double r = 0;
            if (e.getSource() == add) r = a + b;
            else if (e.getSource() == sub) r = a - b;
            else if (e.getSource() == mul) r = a * b;
            else if (e.getSource() == div) r = a / b;
            result.setText("Result: " + r);
        } catch (NumberFormatException ex) {
            result.setText("Invalid input!");
        } catch (ArithmeticException ex) {
            result.setText("Divide by zero!");
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SwingUtilities.invokeLater(() -> new CalculatorGUI());
    }
}
```

**Explanation of Key Code Lines:**
- `implements ActionListener` — the frame itself acts as the listener, avoiding multiple inner classes.
- `FlowLayout` — components flow left-to-right; appropriate for this horizontal calculator layout.
- `Double.parseDouble(...)` — converts text input to numeric for arithmetic.
- `e.getSource()` — identifies which button fired the event (since one listener handles all four).
- `SwingUtilities.invokeLater(...)` — ensures EDT-safe initialization.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting `setLayout(...)` to use `GridLayout(2,2)`** for the button group — examiners specifically test layout choice. For a more polished UI, use `GridLayout(2, 2)` for the buttons inside a sub-panel.
> 2. **Not handling `NumberFormatException`** when text fields are empty — 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Using `paint(Graphics g)` instead of `paintComponent(Graphics g)`** when overriding custom painting — 2-mark deduction. Always override `paintComponent` in Swing.
> 4. **Missing `SwingUtilities.invokeLater()`** in `main()` — 1-mark deduction for thread-safety.
> 5. **Forgetting `setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE)`** — the program will not terminate on close.

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | 14 Marks]` — **Alternative**

**(a)** Explain the Pluggable Look and Feel (PLAF) feature of Swing. Write the code to change the L&F to Nimbus at runtime. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Write a Java Swing program that uses `JCheckBox` and `JRadioButton` to allow a user to select their hobbies and gender, and displays the selected options in a `JTextArea` when a "Show" button is pressed. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### 📌 Model Solution for Part (a) — PLAF

> **Pluggable Look and Feel (PLAF)** is a Swing feature that decouples the **rendering logic** of components from the component's **functional behavior**, allowing the entire UI's appearance to be swapped dynamically at runtime.

**Why PLAF Exists:**
- AWT components inherit their look from the host OS — a `Button` looks different on Windows, macOS, and Linux.
- Swing separates **HOW a component looks** (its `ComponentUI` delegate) from **WHAT a component does** (its data and events).
- This enables applications to provide multiple skins (e.g., "Light Mode" / "Dark Mode") or match the host OS without code changes.

**Built-in L&F Classes:**

| L&F | Class Name |
|---|---|
| Metal | `javax.swing.plaf.metal.MetalLookAndFeel` |
| Nimbus | `javax.swing.plaf.nimbus.NimbusLookAndFeel` |
| Motif | `com.sun.java.swing.plaf.motif.MotifLookAndFeel` |
| System | `UIManager.getSystemLookAndFeelClassName()` |

**Code to Set Nimbus L&F:**

```java
import javax.swing.*;

public class PLAFDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            UIManager.setLookAndFeel("javax.swing.plaf.nimbus.NimbusLookAndFeel");
        } catch (ClassNotFoundException | InstantiationException |
                 IllegalAccessException | UnsupportedLookAndFeelException e) {
            System.out.println("Nimbus not available: " + e.getMessage());
        }
        SwingUtilities.invokeLater(() -> {
            JFrame f = new JFrame("Nimbus Demo");
            f.setSize(300, 200);
            f.setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
            f.add(new JButton("Click Me"));
            f.setVisible(true);
        });
    }
}
```

**Note:** `SwingUtilities.updateComponentTreeUI(frame)` must be called on an already-visible frame to apply L&F changes **after** the UI is built.

---

#### 📌 Model Solution for Part (b) — Checkbox, RadioButton, TextArea Program

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class HobbySelector extends JFrame implements ActionListener {
    JCheckBox chkReading, chkSports, chkMusic;
    JRadioButton rdbMale, rdbFemale, rdbOther;
    JButton btnShow;
    JTextArea taResult;

    HobbySelector() {
        setTitle("Hobby and Gender Selector");
        setSize(400, 300);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());

        JLabel lblHobby = new JLabel("Select Hobbies:");
        chkReading = new JCheckBox("Reading");
        chkSports  = new JCheckBox("Sports");
        chkMusic   = new JCheckBox("Music");

        JLabel lblGender = new JLabel("Select Gender:");
        rdbMale   = new JRadioButton("Male");
        rdbFemale = new JRadioButton("Female");
        rdbOther  = new JRadioButton("Other");

        // Grouping radio buttons ensures mutual exclusivity
        ButtonGroup grpGender = new ButtonGroup();
        grpGender.add(rdbMale);
        grpGender.add(rdbFemale);
        grpGender.add(rdbOther);

        btnShow = new JButton("Show");
        taResult = new JTextArea(5, 30);
        taResult.setEditable(false);

        add(lblHobby);
        add(chkReading); add(chkSports); add(chkMusic);
        add(lblGender);
        add(rdbMale); add(rdbFemale); add(rdbOther);
        add(btnShow);
        add(new JScrollPane(taResult));   // scroll for long text

        btnShow.addActionListener(this);
        setVisible(true);
    }

    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder("Hobbies: ");
        if (chkReading.isSelected()) sb.append("Reading ");
        if (chkSports.isSelected())  sb.append("Sports ");
        if (chkMusic.isSelected())   sb.append("Music ");

        sb.append("\nGender: ");
        if (rdbMale.isSelected())      sb.append("Male");
        else if (rdbFemale.isSelected()) sb.append("Female");
        else if (rdbOther.isSelected())  sb.append("Other");
        else sb.append("Not selected");

        taResult.setText(sb.toString());
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SwingUtilities.invokeLater(() -> new HobbySelector());
    }
}
```

**Code Explanation:**
- `ButtonGroup` ensures only **one** `JRadioButton` can be selected at a time (mutual exclusivity). Without grouping, all three would behave like independent checkboxes.
- `isSelected()` returns `true` if the user has ticked the box.
- `JScrollPane(taResult)` — wraps the text area to provide scrollbars when output exceeds the visible area.
- `setEditable(false)` — prevents the user from typing into the result area.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **Forgetting to wrap radio buttons in a `ButtonGroup`** — 2-mark penalty. Radio buttons **do not** auto-mutually-exclude; the group is mandatory.
> 2. **Overriding `paint()` instead of `paintComponent()`** for custom graphics — instant 2-mark loss.
> 3. **Using `getText()` on checkboxes/radios** — they have no text content; use `isSelected()`. Many students confuse this with labels.
> 4. **Forgetting to add the JTextArea inside a JScrollPane** — long results overflow visually. 1-mark deduction.
> 5. **Not using `StringBuilder` for concatenated strings** in loops — performance deduction, though logically correct.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Print This Before Exam!**

- ✅ **Swing is in `javax.swing`** — note the "x". All classes use the **`J` prefix** (`JButton`, `JFrame`, `JPanel`).
- ✅ **Swing is lightweight** — drawn by Java, no native OS peer. AWT is heavyweight.
- ✅ **`paintComponent(Graphics g)` is the correct override** for custom painting — not `paint()`.
- ✅ **MVC** — Model (`ButtonModel`), View (`ComponentUI`), Controller (Listeners).
- ✅ **PLAF** — `UIManager.setLookAndFeel(...)` + `SwingUtilities.updateComponentTreeUI(frame)`.
- ✅ **EDM** — Event Source → Event Object → Event Listener.
- ✅ **Adapter classes** (`MouseAdapter`, `WindowAdapter`, `KeyAdapter`) provide empty method bodies — override only what you need.
- ✅ **`SwingUtilities.invokeLater()`** — always wrap GUI creation in EDT-safe thread.
- ✅ **`ButtonGroup`** — required for mutually exclusive `JRadioButton`s.
- ✅ **`JCheckBox.isSelected()`** — not `getText()`.
- ✅ **Common Event-Listener pairs**: `ActionEvent→ActionListener`, `ItemEvent→ItemListener`, `MouseEvent→MouseListener`, `KeyEvent→KeyListener`, `WindowEvent→WindowListener`, `ChangeEvent→ChangeListener`.
- ✅ **Layout Managers** — `FlowLayout`, `BorderLayout`, `GridLayout`, `BoxLayout`, `CardLayout`, `GridBagLayout`.
- ✅ **JFrame structure** — JFrame → JRootPane → LayeredPane → ContentPane + GlassPane + MenuBar.
- ✅ **All Swing components extend `JComponent`** → supports tooltips, borders, double buffering.
- ✅ **Lambda expressions** can replace functional listener interfaces (Java 8+).
- ✅ **`JTable` requires a `TableModel`**, **`JList` uses a `ListModel`** — MVC in action.
- ✅ **SwingUtilities methods to remember**: `invokeLater()`, `invokeAndWait()`, `updateComponentTreeUI()`, `isEventDispatchThread()`.

---

> **End of Module 4 — Swing Packages Notes**

topics.title: Event Handling in Swings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT633 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Event Handling in Swings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Event Handling in Swings** is a mechanism provided by the Java AWT (Abstract Window Toolkit) and Swing frameworks that allows a Graphical User Interface (GUI) component to react to user interactions such as mouse clicks, key presses, window resizing, button clicks, and other user-driven actions. The JDK 1.1 (and later) introduced the **Delegation Event Model**, which forms the backbone of Swing event handling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Delegation Event Model (KTU Syllabus Highlight):**
> A source object generates (fires) events, and these events are *delegated* (handed over) to one or more listener objects. The source does **not** process the event itself — it simply notifies registered listeners.

The three principal actors in this model are:

| Actor | Role |
|---|---|
| **Event Source** | The GUI component (e.g., `JButton`, `JTextField`) that generates an event. |
| **Event Object** | An instance of a subclass of `java.util.EventObject` that encapsulates the state change. |
| **Event Listener** | An object that implements a listener interface and is registered with the source to receive and process events. |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Newspaper Subscription System:**
> Think of a **JButton** as a *newspaper publisher*. When something noteworthy happens (a *new edition* is published), the publisher does not personally deliver it to every house. Instead, **subscribers** (the *listeners*) have registered their addresses with the publisher. The moment an event (a *new edition*) is published, the publisher fires off copies to every registered subscriber, and each subscriber *reacts* in their own way — one reads the sports page first, another the business section.
>
> In this analogy:
> - **Publisher** = Event Source (`JButton`)
> - **New Edition** = Event Object (`ActionEvent`)
> - **Subscribers** = Event Listeners (`ActionListener`)
> - **Subscription Form** = The `addActionListener()` registration call

### 1.3 The Hierarchy of Event Classes

All Swing/AWT event classes ultimately extend `java.util.EventObject` (the root), and the AWT events further extend `java.awt.AWTEvent`.

```
java.util.EventObject
        └── java.awt.AWTEvent
                ├── java.awt.event.ActionEvent
                ├── java.awt.event.AdjustmentEvent
                ├── java.awt.event.ItemEvent
                ├── java.awt.event.TextEvent
                ├── java.awt.event.ComponentEvent
                │       ├── java.awt.event.FocusEvent
                │       ├── java.awt.event.WindowEvent
                │       └── java.awt.event.PaintEvent
                └── java.awt.event.InputEvent
                        ├── java.awt.event.KeyEvent
                        └── java.awt.event.MouseEvent
                                └── java.awt.event.MouseWheelEvent
```

### 1.4 Physical / Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constants & Metrics in Event Handling:**
> - **Maximum listeners per source**: Practically unlimited; the source stores them in an `EventListener` vector.
> - **Default event dispatch thread (EDT)**: A single dedicated thread (`AWT-EventQueue-0`) that processes all GUI events — ensuring *thread safety* in Swing.
> - **Event ID range**: Each AWT event has an `int id`; the first 1500 IDs are reserved for `AWTEvent` subclasses.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Delegation Event Model — Operational Logic

The Delegation Event Model works in **three structured steps**:

1. **Event Source Generation** — The user interacts with a GUI component (e.g., clicks a `JButton`). The source component builds an *event object* containing relevant data (source, ID, timestamp, modifiers).
2. **Listener Invocation** — The source iterates through its internal list of registered listeners. For each listener whose interface matches the event type, the source invokes the corresponding *callback method* (e.g., `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)`).
3. **Handler Execution** — The listener's callback method executes the custom business logic, accessing event data via the `EventObject` parameter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the Delegation Model?**
> Before JDK 1.1, Java used the *inheritance-based* model where the container had to subclass AWT components and override event-handling methods (`handleEvent`). This violated encapsulation and was inflexible. Delegation decouples **event generation** from **event handling**, allowing a single event source to broadcast to many listeners and a single listener to receive events from many sources.

### 2.2 Listener Interfaces and Their Callback Methods

| Listener Interface | Event Class | Callback Methods |
|---|---|---|
| `ActionListener` | `ActionEvent` | `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)` |
| `MouseListener` | `MouseEvent` | `mouseClicked`, `mousePressed`, `mouseReleased`, `mouseEntered`, `mouseExited` |
| `MouseMotionListener` | `MouseEvent` | `mouseDragged`, `mouseMoved` |
| `KeyListener` | `KeyEvent` | `keyTyped`, `keyPressed`, `keyReleased` |
| `WindowListener` | `WindowEvent` | `windowOpened`, `windowClosing`, `windowClosed`, `windowIconified`, `windowDeiconified`, `windowActivated`, `windowDeactivated` |
| `FocusListener` | `FocusEvent` | `focusGained`, `focusLost` |
| `ItemListener` | `ItemEvent` | `itemStateChanged(ItemEvent e)` |
| `AdjustmentListener` | `AdjustmentEvent` | `adjustmentValueChanged(AdjustmentEvent e)` |
| `ComponentListener` | `ComponentEvent` | `componentShown`, `componentHidden`, `componentResized`, `componentMoved` |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Notation / Method Signature | Purpose / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Event Source registration | `source.addXxxListener(listener)` | Registers listener to receive events. |
| Event Source deregistration | `source.removeXxxListener(listener)` | Unregisters listener. |
| Callback invocation | `event.getSource()` | Returns the object that fired the event. |
| Event ID retrieval | `event.getID()` | Returns the integer event type ID. |
| Modifier key check | `event.getModifiersEx()` | Returns extended modifier mask (e.g., `SHIFT_DOWN_MASK`). |
| Key character retrieval | `e.getKeyChar()` | Returns the char associated with the key press. |
| Key code retrieval | `e.getKeyCode()` | Returns the virtual key code (e.g., `VK_ENTER`). |
| Mouse coordinates | `e.getX()`, `e.getY()`, `e.getPoint()` | Returns location relative to source. |
| Click count | `e.getClickCount()` | Returns 1 for single click, 2 for double click. |
| Window close operation | `frame.setDefaultCloseOperation(int op)` | Options: `EXIT_ON_CLOSE`, `DISPOSE_ON_CLOSE`, `HIDE_ON_CLOSE`, `DO_NOTHING_ON_CLOSE`. |

### 2.4 Adapter Classes — The "Empty Implementation" Shortcut

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Adapter Classes Exist:**
> Listener interfaces such as `MouseListener` declare **multiple** methods. If you need to handle only `mouseClicked`, you must still provide empty bodies for the other four methods. To eliminate this boilerplate, the AWT provides **Adapter classes** — abstract classes that provide empty default implementations of all methods in the corresponding listener interface.

| Listener Interface | Adapter Class |
|---|---|
| `MouseListener` | `MouseAdapter` |
| `MouseMotionListener` | `MouseMotionAdapter` |
| `KeyListener` | `KeyAdapter` |
| `WindowListener` | `WindowAdapter` |
| `FocusListener` | `FocusAdapter` |
| `ComponentListener` | `ComponentAdapter` |

**Real-world utility in engineering:** Event handling is the foundation of every interactive software system — from embedded device HMIs (Human-Machine Interfaces) in automotive dashboards, to medical device touchscreens, to ATM UIs. The Delegation Model's decoupled design is what makes the **Observer Design Pattern** (a Gang-of-Four pattern) possible.

### 2.5 Inner Classes and Anonymous Inner Classes for Listeners

In practical Swing programming, listeners are typically declared as:
- **Named inner classes** (for clarity and reusability).
- **Anonymous inner classes** (for one-off handlers, reducing class proliferation).
- **Lambda expressions** (Java 8+, for functional interfaces like `ActionListener`).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Walk-Through: Building a Simple Swing Event-Driven Application

We will build a `JFrame` containing a `JButton` and a `JLabel`. When the button is clicked, the label text changes. Each line is annotated to make the event-handling flow explicit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Java Code (as expected for a CS/OOP course):**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class EventDemo extends JFrame implements ActionListener {
    // Step 1: Declare GUI components (these are potential event sources)
    JButton clickButton;
    JLabel messageLabel;

    public EventDemo() {
        // Step 2: Set up the JFrame (window)
        setTitle("KTU Event Handling Demo");
        setSize(400, 150);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);

        // Step 3: Instantiate the components
        clickButton = new JButton("Click Me!");
        messageLabel = new JLabel("No event yet.");

        // Step 4: Register the listener with the event source
        // The 'this' object is the ActionListener
        clickButton.addActionListener(this);

        // Step 5: Add components to the frame
        add(clickButton);
        add(messageLabel);

        setVisible(true);
    }

    // Step 6: Implement the callback method defined in ActionListener
    @Override
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        // Step 7: React to the event
        if (e.getSource() == clickButton) {
            messageLabel.setText("Button clicked at " + e.getWhen());
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Step 8: Launch the GUI on the Event Dispatch Thread
        SwingUtilities.invokeLater(() -> new EventDemo());
    }
}
```

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

| Step | Action | Event-Handling Concept Demonstrated |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Declare `JButton clickButton` | This is a potential **event source**. |
| 2 | `setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE)` | Registers an internal **WindowListener** for the close button. |
| 3 | `new JButton("Click Me!")` | Creates a concrete source capable of firing `ActionEvent`. |
| 4 | `clickButton.addActionListener(this)` | The **registration** — the source now holds a reference to the listener. |
| 5 | `add(clickButton)` | Adds component to container's component hierarchy. |
| 6 | `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)` | The **callback method** — invoked when the event occurs. |
| 7 | `e.getSource() == clickButton` | Identifies *which* source fired the event. |
| 8 | `SwingUtilities.invokeLater(...)` | Ensures thread-safe GUI creation on the **EDT**. |

### 3.3 Derivative Example: Anonymous Inner Class Handler

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class AnonymousDemo extends JFrame {
    public AnonymousDemo() {
        JButton btn = new JButton("Press");
        btn.addActionListener(new ActionListener() {
            @Override
            public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
                System.out.println("Anonymous handler triggered.");
            }
        });
        add(btn);
        setSize(200, 100);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setVisible(true);
    }
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new AnonymousDemo();
    }
}
```

### 3.4 Derivative Example: Adapter Class for Window Events

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class AdapterDemo extends JFrame {
    public AdapterDemo() {
        setSize(300, 200);
        setTitle("Adapter Demo");

        // Using WindowAdapter to override only the needed method
        addWindowListener(new WindowAdapter() {
            @Override
            public void windowClosing(WindowEvent e) {
                System.out.println("Window is closing...");
                System.exit(0);
            }
        });

        setVisible(true);
    }
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new AdapterDemo();
    }
}
```

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Approach | Pros | Cons | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Class implements Listener** | Reusable across multiple sources | Couples the class to listener type | When many components share the same handler |
| **Named Inner Class** | Clear naming, reusability within outer class | Slight verbosity | When handler logic is complex |
| **Anonymous Inner Class** | Concise, inline definition | Not reusable, can be hard to debug | When handler is used only once |
| **Lambda Expression** (Java 8+) | Most concise, functional style | Only for *functional interfaces* (single-method) | Quick, single-method handlers like `ActionListener` |
| **Adapter Class** | No need to implement unused methods | Cannot extend another useful class (Java has no multiple class inheritance) | Multi-method listeners (`MouseListener`, `KeyListener`) |

### 3.6 Mathematical Representation of Event Processing

Although event handling is event-driven (not numerical), we can symbolically represent the event-dispatch relationship:

$$L = \{ l_1, l_2, l_3, \dots, l_n \}$$

where $L$ is the *listener set* registered with a source. When an event $E$ is generated:

$$\text{Dispatch}(E) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{callback}(l_i, E) \quad \text{where } l_i \in L_{compatible}$$

$L_{compatible}$ denotes the subset of listeners whose interface type matches the event class of $E$. The order of invocation follows the **registration order** (FIFO — First In, First Out).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Delegation Event Model — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    userA[User Interaction] --> sourceA[JButton Event Source]
    sourceA --> eventObjA[ActionEvent Object]
    eventObjA --> dispatchA{Event Dispatcher}
    dispatchA --> listener1[ActionListener 1]
    dispatchA --> listener2[ActionListener 2]
    dispatchA --> listener3[ActionListener 3]
    listener1 --> action1[actionPerformed Method 1]
    listener2 --> action2[actionPerformed Method 2]
    listener3 --> action3[actionPerformed Method 3]
    action1 --> result1[Update GUI Component A]
    action2 --> result2[Log to File]
    action3 --> result3[Trigger Network Call]
```

### 4.2 Event Class Hierarchy and Listener Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA[java.util.EventObject]
    rootA --> awtA[java.awt.AWTEvent]
    awtA --> actA[ActionEvent]
    awtA --> mouseA[MouseEvent]
    awtA --> keyA[KeyEvent]
    awtA --> winA[WindowEvent]
    awtA --> focA[FocusEvent]
    awtA --> itemA[ItemEvent]
    actA -.registered to.-> actL[ActionListener]
    mouseA -.registered to.-> mouseL[MouseListener]
    mouseA -.registered to.-> mouseML[MouseMotionListener]
    keyA -.registered to.-> keyL[KeyListener]
    winA -.registered to.-> winL[WindowListener]
    focA -.registered to.-> focL[FocusListener]
    itemA -.registered to.-> itemL[ItemListener]
    mouseL -.has adapter.-> mouseAd[MouseAdapter]
    keyL -.has adapter.-> keyAd[KeyAdapter]
    winL -.has adapter.-> winAd[WindowAdapter]
```

### 4.3 Event Handling Workflow — Step-by-Step Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant S as Event Source
    participant E as Event Object
    participant D as Dispatcher
    participant L as Listener
    participant H as Handler Method

    U->>S: Click / Key Press / Action
    S->>E: Construct EventObject
    S->>D: fireEvent
    D->>L: Identify registered listener
    L->>H: Invoke callback method
    H->>S: Update GUI / State
    H-->>U: Visual feedback
```

### 4.4 Adapter Class Pattern Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ifaceA[MouseListener Interface]
    ifaceA --> method1[mouseClicked]
    ifaceA --> method2[mousePressed]
    ifaceA --> method3[mouseReleased]
    ifaceA --> method4[mouseEntered]
    ifaceA --> method5[mouseExited]
    ifaceA -.implemented by.-> adapterA[MouseAdapter Abstract Class]
    adapterA -.empty defaults.-> emptyM1[empty mouseClicked]
    adapterA -.empty defaults.-> emptyM2[empty mousePressed]
    adapterA -.empty defaults.-> emptyM3[empty mouseReleased]
    adapterA -.empty defaults.-> emptyM4[empty mouseEntered]
    adapterA -.empty defaults.-> emptyM5[empty mouseExited]
    adapterA -.extended by.-> userA[User-Defined Class]
    userA -.overrides only.-> override1[custom mouseClicked]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`  
**What is the Delegation Event Model in Java? Name its three key components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Delegation Event Model** is a JDK 1.1 event-handling architecture in which an event source does not process events itself but *delegates* (hands over) the responsibility to one or more registered listener objects.
The three key components are:
1. **Event Source** — the GUI component that generates the event (e.g., `JButton`).
2. **Event Object** — encapsulates the event data (e.g., `ActionEvent`).
3. **Event Listener** — the object that implements a listener interface and contains the handler method to process the event.

*(Award 1 mark each for the model name, the three components.)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`  
**Differentiate between a Listener Interface and an Adapter Class with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Listener Interface | Adapter Class |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | A pure abstract type declaring event-handling methods. | An abstract class providing empty implementations of all listener methods. |
| Method count | Declares all methods abstract. | Implements all methods with empty bodies. |
| Usage | Must implement *all* declared methods. | Override *only* the methods of interest. |
| Example | `MouseListener` (5 methods) | `MouseAdapter` (extends `MouseAdapter`, override only `mouseClicked`) |

*(Award 1 mark for definition of each, 1 mark for example differentiation.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Many students **forget to register the listener** with `addXxxListener()` — the button will do nothing. Always verify registration.
> - When asked to write a program, students often omit `setDefaultCloseOperation()` or `setVisible(true)`. Both are **mandatory** for the code to run.
> - Mistaking the **event class** (`ActionEvent`) for the **listener interface** (`ActionListener`) is a common error. Examiners **deduct 1 mark** for this confusion.
> - For adapter-based questions, ensure the class `extends` the adapter and `overrides` *only* the necessary method.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain the Delegation Event Model in Java with a suitable diagram. List any **four** commonly used listener interfaces with their corresponding event classes. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **Delegation Event Model** is the standard event-handling framework introduced in JDK 1.1 to replace the older inheritance-based model. Its key features are:

- **Source–Listener Decoupling:** The event source (a GUI component) does not know *how* the event will be handled. It maintains a list of registered listeners and notifies them when an event occurs.
- **One-to-Many Relationship:** A single source can fire an event to multiple listeners; a single listener can receive events from multiple sources.
- **Multicast Capability:** Events are multicast — the source broadcasts to all registered listeners.

**Diagram (textual representation):**

```
[User] --clicks--> [JButton Source] --fires--> [ActionEvent Object]
                                            |
                                            v
                          [ActionListener 1] --> actionPerformed()
                          [ActionListener 2] --> actionPerformed()
                          [ActionListener 3] --> actionPerformed()
```

**Four commonly used listener interfaces:**

| Listener Interface | Event Class | Callback Method |
|---|---|---|
| `ActionListener` | `ActionEvent` | `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)` |
| `MouseListener` | `MouseEvent` | `mouseClicked`, `mousePressed`, `mouseReleased`, `mouseEntered`, `mouseExited` |
| `KeyListener` | `KeyEvent` | `keyTyped`, `keyPressed`, `keyReleased` |
| `WindowListener` | `WindowEvent` | `windowClosing`, `windowOpened`, `windowClosed`, etc. |

*(Award 3 marks for explanation, 2 marks for diagram, 2 marks for the listener table.)*

---

**(b)** Write a Java Swing program to create a window with a `JTextField` and a `JButton` labeled "Submit". When the button is clicked, the text from the text field should be displayed in a `JLabel` below. Use an **anonymous inner class** to handle the event. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class SubmitForm extends JFrame {
    JTextField nameField;
    JLabel outputLabel;

    public SubmitForm() {
        setTitle("Submit Form");
        setSize(350, 150);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);

        nameField = new JTextField(15);
        JButton submitBtn = new JButton("Submit");
        outputLabel = new JLabel("Result will appear here.");

        // Anonymous inner class as ActionListener
        submitBtn.addActionListener(new ActionListener() {
            @Override
            public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
                String input = nameField.getText();
                outputLabel.setText("Hello, " + input + "!");
            }
        });

        add(nameField);
        add(submitBtn);
        add(outputLabel);
        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new SubmitForm();
    }
}
```

**Explanation of key steps (1 mark each for clarity):**
1. `JTextField` and `JButton` are instantiated as event sources.
2. The anonymous inner class implements `ActionListener` and overrides `actionPerformed`.
3. `e.getSource()` is implicitly the button; `nameField.getText()` retrieves the user input.
4. The label is updated with the processed text.
5. `setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE)` ensures the JVM exits on close.
6. The `main` method triggers GUI creation directly (acceptable in this small example).
7. The output displays the personalized greeting upon each click.

*(Award 2 marks for the anonymous inner class, 2 marks for the handler logic, 1 mark for layout/structure, 2 marks for execution and imports.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** What are Adapter Classes in Java event handling? Why are they needed? Write a program using `WindowAdapter` to handle the `windowClosing` event of a `JFrame`. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Adapter Classes** are abstract classes in `java.awt.event` package that provide **empty (no-op) implementations** of all methods declared in their corresponding listener interface. They exist because most listener interfaces declare **multiple methods**, and developers often want to handle only **one or two** of them. Without adapters, the developer would be forced to write empty bodies for the unwanted methods.

**Common Adapters:** `MouseAdapter`, `KeyAdapter`, `WindowAdapter`, `FocusAdapter`, `ComponentAdapter`.

**Program using `WindowAdapter`:**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class WindowAdapterDemo extends JFrame {
    public WindowAdapterDemo() {
        setTitle("Window Adapter Demo");
        setSize(300, 200);

        addWindowListener(new WindowAdapter() {
            @Override
            public void windowClosing(WindowEvent e) {
                System.out.println("Window is closing. Exiting application...");
                System.exit(0);
            }

            @Override
            public void windowOpened(WindowEvent e) {
                System.out.println("Window opened successfully.");
            }
        });

        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new WindowAdapterDemo();
    }
}
```

*(Award 2 marks for adapter concept, 2 marks for "why needed", 3 marks for working code.)*

---

**(b)** Explain the `MouseListener` and `MouseMotionListener` interfaces. Differentiate between `mouseClicked` and `mousePressed`. Write a program that changes the background color of a `JPanel` to **red** when the mouse is **clicked** and to **blue** when the mouse is **moved over** the panel. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**`MouseListener`** handles mouse button events and enter/exit events. It declares five methods:
- `mouseClicked(MouseEvent e)` — invoked when a mouse button is pressed *and* released on the component *without* significant movement.
- `mousePressed(MouseEvent e)` — invoked when a mouse button is *pressed down* on the component.
- `mouseReleased(MouseEvent e)` — invoked when a mouse button is *released*.
- `mouseEntered(MouseEvent e)` — invoked when the mouse cursor *enters* the component's bounds.
- `mouseExited(MouseEvent e)` — invoked when the mouse cursor *leaves* the component's bounds.

**`MouseMotionListener`** handles mouse movement events:
- `mouseDragged(MouseEvent e)` — mouse moved while a button is held down.
- `mouseMoved(MouseEvent e)` — mouse moved *without* any button pressed.

**`mouseClicked` vs `mousePressed`:**

| Aspect | `mouseClicked` | `mousePressed` |
|---|---|---|
| Trigger | Full click (press + release on same component) | Button is pressed down |
| Timing | After release | Immediately on press |
| Use case | "User chose this item" | "User is starting a drag operation" |

**Program:**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class MouseColorDemo extends JFrame {
    JPanel panel;

    public MouseColorDemo() {
        setTitle("Mouse Color Demo");
        setSize(400, 300);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE);

        panel = new JPanel();
        panel.setBackground(Color.WHITE);

        // MouseListener for click event
        panel.addMouseListener(new MouseAdapter() {
            @Override
            public void mouseClicked(MouseEvent e) {
                panel.setBackground(Color.RED);
                System.out.println("Mouse clicked — color set to RED.");
            }
        });

        // MouseMotionListener for mouse move event
        panel.addMouseMotionListener(new MouseMotionAdapter() {
            @Override
            public void mouseMoved(MouseEvent e) {
                panel.setBackground(Color.BLUE);
                System.out.println("Mouse moved — color set to BLUE.");
            }
        });

        add(panel);
        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new MouseColorDemo();
    }
}
```

*(Award 2 marks for interface explanations, 1 mark for the difference table, 4 marks for working code with both listeners.)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Event Handling in Swings:**
>
> - **Delegation Event Model** is the *only* event model used in modern Java (post JDK 1.1).
> - **Three key actors:** Event Source, Event Object, Event Listener.
> - **Listener interfaces** are pure abstract types; **Adapter classes** provide empty implementations.
> - All AWT events extend `java.util.EventObject` via `java.awt.AWTEvent`.
> - The most-used interfaces: `ActionListener`, `MouseListener`, `KeyListener`, `WindowListener`.
> - **Callback method** names are predictable: `actionPerformed`, `mouseClicked`, `keyPressed`, `windowClosing`, etc.
> - Registration is done via `source.addXxxListener(listenerObj)`.
> - **Adapter classes** save you from implementing unused methods; use them for multi-method interfaces.
> - The **`Event Dispatch Thread (EDT)`** is responsible for processing all GUI events — Swing components are *not* thread-safe by default.
> - **Lambda expressions** (Java 8+) can replace anonymous inner classes *only* for *functional interfaces* (single-method listeners like `ActionListener`).
> - **Common mistake:** Forgetting `setVisible(true)` or `addXxxListener()` — the GUI appears but does nothing.
> - **`e.getSource()`** returns the object that fired the event — essential when one listener handles multiple sources.
> - **`setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE)`** is the quickest way to ensure the JVM exits on window close.
> - **Event firing order** follows registration order (FIFO).
> - **Best practice for KTU exams:** Always provide *complete, compilable* code with imports, `main` method, and proper indentation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Swing Layout Managers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT633 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Swing Layout Managers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Layout Manager** in Java Swing is an object that implements the `java.awt.LayoutManager` (or `LayoutManager2`) interface and is responsible for arranging the components (such as `JButton`, `JLabel`, `JTextField`, etc.) inside a `Container` (like `JFrame`, `JPanel`, `JDialog`) according to a predefined **layout policy**. It dictates how components are positioned, sized, and spaced, and dynamically adjusts them when the container is resized, ensuring a **platform-independent, responsive, and consistent GUI** across different operating systems.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus, layout managers are categorized under **Module 4 – Event-Driven Programming and Swing**, and the focus is on understanding the philosophy, the five most-used managers, and their behavioral rules.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Syllabus Takeaway:** KTU specifically emphasizes the following five layout managers: **`BorderLayout`**, **`FlowLayout`**, **`GridLayout`**, **`CardLayout`**, and **`GridBagLayout`**. The `BoxLayout` is often covered as a bonus topic.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The House Architect"

Imagine you are moving into a new house. You have a **living room** (the `Container`) and several pieces of **furniture** (the Swing `Components` — buttons, labels, text fields).

* A **Layout Manager** is like an **interior architect** who decides *where* each piece of furniture should go.
* If you resize the room (e.g., a user maximizes the window), the architect intelligently **re-positions** and **re-sizes** the furniture so the room still looks balanced.
* Different architects follow different **rules**:
   * `BorderLayout` = A strict architect who says "sofa goes North, TV goes South, lamps go East and West" — only 5 fixed zones.
   * `FlowLayout` = A casual architect who lines up furniture left-to-right, like words in a paragraph, and wraps to the next "row" when full.
   * `GridLayout` = A grid-obsessed architect who splits the room into an **N x M matrix of equal-sized cells** (like a checkerboard).
   * `CardLayout` = A magician architect who shows **only one piece of furniture at a time**, like flipping through a deck of cards.
   * `GridBagLayout` = The most flexible (and complex) architect, who places furniture with **constraints** (size, weight, padding, span).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why can't we use absolute positioning?**
> In AWT/Swing, you *can* set `container.setLayout(null)` and use `setBounds(x, y, width, height)` to fix components. However, this is **discouraged** because the layout breaks on different screen resolutions, fonts, and Look & Feels. KTU examiners heavily penalize manual positioning in GUI questions.

---

### 1.3 The `LayoutManager` Interface (Conceptual Foundation)

Every layout manager implements the following core contract:

| Method Signature | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `void addLayoutComponent(String name, Component comp)` | Adds a component to the layout (used by `BorderLayout` with region names). |
| `void removeLayoutComponent(Component comp)` | Removes a component from the layout. |
| `Dimension preferredLayoutSize(Container parent)` | Returns the **ideal** size of the container. |
| `Dimension minimumLayoutSize(Container parent)` | Returns the smallest size the layout can shrink to. |
| `void layoutContainer(Container parent)` | The actual logic that **places and sizes** components. |

> [!TIP]
> **`LayoutManager2` extends `LayoutManager`** and adds support for constraints (e.g., `GridBagConstraints`), which is what `GridBagLayout` uses.

---

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following **standard pixel gaps** are used by layout managers:

* `HORIZONTAL_GAP` / `VERTICAL_GAP` = **0** by default (FlowLayout).
* `BorderLayout` gaps default to **0** pixels horizontally and vertically.
* Standard Look & Feel insets are typically **11 pixels** on macOS and **2 pixels** on Windows/Linux.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU questions may ask: *"What is the default gap in `FlowLayout`?"* — Answer: **5 pixels horizontal and vertical** (actually 5+5 with vgap/hgap constructor, but default constructor sets both to **5**). BorderLayout default is **0**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Pipeline (How a Layout Manager Works)

When a Swing container is displayed, the layout engine follows this exact **4-step pipeline**:

1. **Trigger** → A layout request is fired (initial display, `pack()`, `validate()`, `revalidate()`, or resizing).
2. **Query** → The container asks each child for its `getPreferredSize()`, `getMinimumSize()`, and `getMaximumSize()`.
3. **Calculate** → The layout manager's `layoutContainer()` method computes the position (`x`, `y`) and size (`width`, `height`) of each component based on the available space and its own rules.
4. **Apply** → `component.setBounds(x, y, width, height)` is called on every child, and the components are painted.

> [!NOTE]
> **Order matters in `BorderLayout`!** When you add components without specifying a region, they all stack in the **CENTER**. Only **one component per region** is allowed.

---

### 2.2 The "Big Five" Layout Managers — Detailed Breakdown

#### A. `BorderLayout`

* **Structure**: Divides the container into **5 regions**: `NORTH`, `SOUTH`, `EAST`, `WEST`, `CENTER`.
* **Behavior**:
   * `NORTH` and `SOUTH` components stretch **horizontally** and get their **preferred height**.
   * `EAST` and `WEST` components stretch **vertically** (between NORTH/SOUTH) and get their **preferred width**.
   * `CENTER` gets **all remaining space**.
* **Default for**: `JFrame`, `JDialog`, `JApplet` content panes.
* **Constructor**: `BorderLayout()`, `BorderLayout(int hgap, int vgap)`.
* **Add method**: `add(Component comp, Object constraints)` where constraints is a `String` like `"North"`.

```java
setLayout(new BorderLayout(10, 20));  // 10px hgap, 20px vgap
add(new JButton("Top"),    BorderLayout.NORTH);
add(new JButton("Center"), BorderLayout.CENTER);
```

#### B. `FlowLayout`

* **Structure**: Components are placed **left-to-right** in the order added, then **wrapped** to the next line when the row is full.
* **Default for**: `JPanel`.
* **Alignment**: `LEFT`, `CENTER` (default), `RIGHT`.
* **Constructor**: `FlowLayout()`, `FlowLayout(int align)`, `FlowLayout(int align, int hgap, int vgap)`.
* **Best for**: Toolbars, button rows, dialog boxes.

```java
setLayout(new FlowLayout(FlowLayout.RIGHT, 5, 10));
add(new JButton("OK"));
add(new JButton("Cancel"));
```

#### C. `GridLayout`

* **Structure**: Divides the container into a **grid of equal-sized cells** (like a spreadsheet). Each component fills **one cell** completely.
* **Behavior**: All cells are **exactly the same size** — calculated as `container_width / cols` and `container_height / rows`.
* **Constructor**: `GridLayout()`, `GridLayout(int rows, int cols)`, `GridLayout(int rows, int cols, int hgap, int vgap)`.
* **Note**: Setting `rows=0` means "any number of rows" (unlimited).
* **Best for**: Calculator buttons, image galleries, login forms.

```java
setLayout(new GridLayout(3, 2, 5, 5));  // 3 rows, 2 cols, 5px gaps
add(new JButton("1"));
add(new JButton("2"));
// ... etc.
```

#### D. `CardLayout`

* **Structure**: Manages components as a **stack of "cards"** (like a tabbed pane without tabs). Only **one card is visible at a time**.
* **Methods**:
   * `add(String name, Component comp)` — name is the card's identifier.
   * `show(Container parent, String name)` — display a specific card.
   * `first(Container parent)`, `next(Container parent)`, `previous(Container parent)`, `last(Container parent)`.
* **Best for**: Wizards (Next → Next → Finish), tab-like interfaces, slide shows.

```java
CardLayout cards = new CardLayout();
setLayout(cards);
add("login",    new LoginPanel());
add("welcome",  new WelcomePanel());
cards.show(this, "login");
```

#### E. `GridBagLayout`

* **Structure**: The **most powerful and complex** layout. It places components in a **grid**, but each cell can be:
   * **Spanned** across multiple rows/columns.
   * **Stretched** to fill space (controlled by `weightx` and `weighty`).
   * **Aligned** within its cell (top, bottom, left, right, center, etc.).
   * **Padded** with insets.
* **Companion class**: `GridBagConstraints` — holds 11+ properties (gridx, gridy, gridwidth, gridheight, weightx, weighty, anchor, fill, insets, ipadx, ipady).
* **Helper method**: `GridBagLayoutHelper` (or manual `gbc` pattern) is typically used.

```java
setLayout(new GridBagLayout());
GridBagConstraints gbc = new GridBagConstraints();
gbc.gridx = 0; gbc.gridy = 0;
gbc.weightx = 1; gbc.weighty = 1;
gbc.fill = GridBagConstraints.BOTH;
add(new JButton("Fill"), gbc);
```

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Layout Manager | Default Container | Key Property | Stretches? | Components per Region | Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `BorderLayout` | `JFrame` content pane | 5 regions (N/S/E/W/C) | Yes (CENTER fills) | 1 per region | Low |
| `FlowLayout` | `JPanel` | L-to-R, wraps | No (preferred size) | Unlimited | Lowest |
| `GridLayout` | None (explicit) | Equal-sized cells | Yes (all cells) | 1 per cell | Low |
| `CardLayout` | None (explicit) | Stacked cards | Depends on card | 1 visible | Medium |
| `GridBagLayout` | None (explicit) | Constraint-based | Yes (with weight) | Unlimited | **Highest** |

| Important Constant / Method | Value / Signature |
|---|---|
| `BorderLayout.NORTH` | `"North"` (top edge) |
| `BorderLayout.SOUTH` | `"South"` (bottom edge) |
| `BorderLayout.EAST` | `"East"` (right edge) |
| `BorderLayout.WEST` | `"West"` (left edge) |
| `BorderLayout.CENTER` | `"Center"` (middle, fills) |
| `FlowLayout.LEFT \vert CENTER \vert RIGHT` | Alignment constants |
| `GridBagConstraints.BOTH \vert HORIZONTAL \vert VERTICAL \vert NONE` | Fill constants |
| `Container.setLayout(LayoutManager mgr)` | Sets the manager |
| `Container.validate() \vert revalidate()` | Triggers re-layout |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** Layout managers are used in every Java desktop app — from NetBeans IDE's toolbox to IntelliJ's plugin panels. In enterprise software (Swing-based dashboards), `GridBagLayout` is the de-facto choice for forms.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Code Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: A Login Form using `BorderLayout` + `FlowLayout` (Composite Pattern)

**Problem:** Build a login window with:
* A title label at the top.
* A form panel (center) with username and password fields.
* A button row at the bottom (OK, Cancel, Help).

**Step 1 — Conceptual decomposition:**

$$
\text{JFrame} = \text{NORTH: Title} \;\cup\; \text{CENTER: FormPanel(GridLayout)} \;\cup\; \text{SOUTH: ButtonRow(FlowLayout)}
$$

**Step 2 — Complete Java Code:**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;

public class LoginDemo extends JFrame {
    public LoginDemo() {
        setTitle("Login");
        setSize(400, 200);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        
        // NORTH: Title label
        JLabel title = new JLabel("Please Login", SwingConstants.CENTER);
        add(title, BorderLayout.NORTH);
        
        // CENTER: Form panel with GridLayout (2 rows, 2 cols)
        JPanel formPanel = new JPanel(new GridLayout(2, 2, 5, 5));
        formPanel.add(new JLabel("Username:"));
        formPanel.add(new JTextField(15));
        formPanel.add(new JLabel("Password:"));
        formPanel.add(new JPasswordField(15));
        add(formPanel, BorderLayout.CENTER);
        
        // SOUTH: Button row with FlowLayout
        JPanel buttonPanel = new JPanel(new FlowLayout(FlowLayout.RIGHT));
        buttonPanel.add(new JButton("OK"));
        buttonPanel.add(new JButton("Cancel"));
        buttonPanel.add(new JButton("Help"));
        add(buttonPanel, BorderLayout.SOUTH);
        
        setLocationRelativeTo(null);
        setVisible(true);
    }
    
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new LoginDemo();
    }
}
```

**Step 3 — Layout derivation:**

* `BorderLayout` first assigns the **NORTH** label its preferred height (`title.getPreferredSize().height`) and full width.
* The **SOUTH** button panel is then sized to its preferred height, and the **CENTER** gets the remaining space.
* The `formPanel` (CENTER) uses `GridLayout(2, 2)` to split itself into 4 equal cells, each cell filled completely by a label or text field.
* The `buttonPanel` (SOUTH) uses `FlowLayout(RIGHT)` to right-align the 3 buttons in a single row.

**Step 4 — Resize behavior:**

| Region | Horizontal Stretch | Vertical Stretch |
|---|---|---|
| NORTH (Title) | ✅ Yes (full width) | ❌ No (preferred height) |
| CENTER (Form) | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes (fills remaining) |
| SOUTH (Buttons) | ✅ Yes (full width) | ❌ No (preferred height) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Tip:** When the user resizes the window, only the **CENTER** component grows. NORTH and SOUTH stay at their preferred heights. This is a common viva question.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: A Wizard using `CardLayout`

**Problem:** Build a 3-step wizard: "Step 1: Personal Info" → "Step 2: Address" → "Step 3: Confirm".

**Step 1 — Derive the navigation logic:**

$$
\text{CardState}_{n+1} = \text{CardLayout.next(parent)}
$$

$$
\text{CardState}_{n-1} = \text{CardLayout.previous(parent)}
$$

**Step 2 — Complete Java Code:**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class WizardDemo extends JFrame implements ActionListener {
    private CardLayout cards;
    private JPanel cardPanel;
    private JButton nextBtn, prevBtn;
    
    public WizardDemo() {
        setTitle("Wizard");
        setSize(400, 250);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        
        // Card panel in the CENTER
        cards = new CardLayout();
        cardPanel = new JPanel(cards);
        
        // Card 1
        JPanel p1 = new JPanel(new GridLayout(2, 2));
        p1.add(new JLabel("Name:"));  p1.add(new JTextField());
        p1.add(new JLabel("Age:"));   p1.add(new JTextField());
        cardPanel.add(p1, "Step1");
        
        // Card 2
        JPanel p2 = new JPanel(new GridLayout(3, 2));
        p2.add(new JLabel("City:"));  p2.add(new JTextField());
        p2.add(new JLabel("State:")); p2.add(new JTextField());
        p2.add(new JLabel("Zip:"));   p2.add(new JTextField());
        cardPanel.add(p2, "Step2");
        
        // Card 3
        JPanel p3 = new JPanel();
        p3.add(new JLabel("Click Finish to confirm."));
        cardPanel.add(p3, "Step3");
        
        add(cardPanel, BorderLayout.CENTER);
        
        // Navigation buttons in the SOUTH
        JPanel nav = new JPanel(new FlowLayout());
        prevBtn = new JButton("<< Previous");
        nextBtn = new JButton("Next >>");
        prevBtn.addActionListener(this);
        nextBtn.addActionListener(this);
        nav.add(prevBtn);
        nav.add(nextBtn);
        add(nav, BorderLayout.SOUTH);
        
        setLocationRelativeTo(null);
        setVisible(true);
    }
    
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        if (e.getSource() == nextBtn) cards.next(cardPanel);
        if (e.getSource() == prevBtn) cards.previous(cardPanel);
    }
    
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new WizardDemo();
    }
}
```

**Step 3 — Step-by-step analysis:**

1. `cards = new CardLayout()` creates the manager.
2. `cardPanel = new JPanel(cards)` creates a panel that uses CardLayout.
3. `cardPanel.add(p1, "Step1")` registers each card with a **unique string identifier**.
4. `cards.next(cardPanel)` advances to the next card in the **order they were added**.
5. `cards.show(cardPanel, "Step2")` can jump directly to a named card.
6. The outer `JFrame` uses `BorderLayout` so the card panel fills the **CENTER**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget to pass the `cardPanel` (the parent container) to `cards.next()`. The signature is `next(Container parent)`, not just `next()`.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3: A Calculator Grid using `GridLayout`

**Problem:** A numeric keypad with 4 rows × 3 cols (digits 7-8-9, 4-5-6, 1-2-3, 0-.-=).

**Code:**

```java
JPanel keypad = new JPanel(new GridLayout(4, 3, 5, 5));
String[] labels = {"7","8","9","4","5","6","1","2","3","0",".","="};
for (String s : labels) {
    keypad.add(new JButton(s));
}
```

**Analysis:** The container is divided into a **4 × 3 matrix of equal cells**. Each button is placed in the next cell in row-major order. The 5-pixel gaps (hgap, vgap) add visual breathing room.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **GridLayout quirk:** If you add 13 components to a `GridLayout(4, 3)`, the grid **adjusts** to 5 × 3 (or stays 4 × 3 with the last cell empty, depending on the constructor's `rows` vs `cols` precedence).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Layout

| Scenario | Recommended Layout | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Top-level window (title, menu, status) | `BorderLayout` | Standard Swing convention |
| Toolbar with buttons | `FlowLayout` | Buttons line up left-to-right |
| Calculator / Phone keypad | `GridLayout` | Equal cells, row-major order |
| Installation wizard | `CardLayout` | One screen at a time |
| Complex form (label + field, spanning) | `GridBagLayout` | Most flexible, alignment control |
| Vertical/horizontal list of items | `BoxLayout` | Simple axis-based stacking |
| Tabbed dialog | `JTabbedPane` (not a layout) | Built-in tab UI |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Rule of Thumb:** Always start with `BorderLayout` for the **outer container** and nest `JPanel`s with other layouts **inside the regions**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Map: The Layout Manager Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[java.awt.LayoutManager] --> B[java.awt.LayoutManager2]
    A --> C[BorderLayout]
    A --> D[FlowLayout]
    A --> E[GridLayout]
    A --> F[CardLayout]
    B --> G[GridBagLayout]
    B --> H[BoxLayout]
    C --> I[JFrame default]
    C --> J[JDialog default]
    D --> K[JPanel default]
```
