```mermaid
    E --> L[Calculator style]
    F --> M[Wizard style]
    G --> N[Form style]
    H --> O[Axis style]
```

---

### 4.2 BorderLayout Zone Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    root[Container using BorderLayout]
    n[NORTH region]
    s[SOUTH region]
    e[EAST region]
    w[WEST region]
    c[CENTER region - fills all remaining]
    root --- n
    root --- s
    root --- e
    root --- w
    root --- c
    n --> n1[Stretches full width]
    n --> n2[Preferred height]
    s --> s1[Stretches full width]
    s --> s2[Preferred height]
    e --> e1[Preferred width]
    e --> e2[Stretches vertically]
    w --> w1[Preferred width]
    w --> w2[Stretches vertically]
    c --> c1[Stretches in both directions]
```

---

### 4.3 FlowLayout Wrapping Logic

```mermaid
graph LR
    a[Component 1] --> b[Component 2] --> c[Component 3] --> d[Component 4]
    d -.row full.-> e[Wrap to next row]
    e --> f[Component 5] --> g[Component 6]
    g -.end.-> h[Alignment: LEFT CENTER or RIGHT]
```

---

### 4.4 GridLayout Cell Matrix

```mermaid
graph TD
    g[GridLayout 3x2]
    c1[Cell 0,0] --> c2[Cell 0,1]
    c2 --> c3[Cell 1,0]
    c3 --> c4[Cell 1,1]
    c4 --> c5[Cell 2,0]
    c5 --> c6[Cell 2,1]
    c6 --> note[All cells are EQUAL SIZE]
    style note fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#333
```

---

### 4.5 CardLayout State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Card1
    Card1 --> Card2 : next
    Card2 --> Card3 : next
    Card3 --> Card2 : previous
    Card2 --> Card1 : previous
    Card1 --> Card3 : show
    Card2 --> Card1 : show
    Card3 --> Card1 : show
```

---

### 4.6 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Login Window

```mermaid
graph TD
    jf[JFrame - BorderLayout]
    jf --> north[NORTH: JLabel Title]
    jf --> center[CENTER: JPanel formPanel GridLayout 2x2]
    jf --> south[SOUTH: JPanel buttonPanel FlowLayout RIGHT]
    center --> r1c1[Row 0 Col 0: JLabel Username]
    center --> r1c2[Row 0 Col 1: JTextField]
    center --> r2c1[Row 1 Col 0: JLabel Password]
    center --> r2c2[Row 1 Col 1: JPasswordField]
    south --> b1[JButton OK]
    south --> b2[JButton Cancel]
    south --> b3[JButton Help]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This nested-layout pattern (BorderLayout outer + FlowLayout/GridLayout inner) is the **most frequently asked GUI question in KTU exams**. Memorize it!

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between `BorderLayout` and `FlowLayout` in Java Swing. List their default alignment properties.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | `BorderLayout` | `FlowLayout` |
|---|---|---|
| Structure | 5 regions (N, S, E, W, C) | Linear, left-to-right with wrapping |
| Default container | `JFrame` content pane | `JPanel` |
| Stretches on resize | Yes, only CENTER fills all remaining | No, components keep preferred size |
| Component per region | 1 per region | Unlimited (wraps to next line) |
| Default alignment | Center | `FlowLayout.CENTER` |

**Code snippet (1 mark):**
```java
setLayout(new BorderLayout());     // BorderLayout
setLayout(new FlowLayout());       // FlowLayout
```

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
**What is the role of `CardLayout` in Swing? Write the methods to navigate between cards.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

`CardLayout` is a layout manager that treats each component as a **card** in a deck. Only **one card is visible at a time**, and the others are hidden. It is used to build **wizards, tab-like interfaces, and step-by-step forms**.

**Navigation methods (2 marks):**
* `cards.next(Container parent)` — show the next card.
* `cards.previous(Container parent)` — show the previous card.
* `cards.first(Container parent)` — show the first card.
* `cards.last(Container parent)` — show the last card.
* `cards.show(Container parent, String name)` — show a specific card by name.

**Code snippet (1 mark):**
```java
CardLayout cl = new CardLayout();
panel.setLayout(cl);
panel.add(card1, "first");
cl.show(panel, "first");
```

---

### 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**(a)** Explain the concept of a Layout Manager in Java Swing. Describe the `GridLayout` manager with a suitable code example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java Swing program to create a window with three buttons labeled "Red", "Green", and "Blue" arranged using `FlowLayout` in a single row, centered. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Concept of Layout Manager + GridLayout (7 marks):**

* **Definition (2 marks):** A Layout Manager is an object of a class that implements the `LayoutManager` interface. It controls the **position, size, and arrangement** of components inside a `Container`. It ensures the GUI is **platform-independent** and **responsive** to resizing.

* **GridLayout explanation (3 marks):**
   * Divides the container into a **grid of rows × columns of equal-sized cells**.
   * Each component added fills **exactly one cell**.
   * Components are placed in **row-major order** (left-to-right, top-to-bottom).
   * Constructors: `GridLayout()`, `GridLayout(int rows, int cols)`, `GridLayout(int rows, int cols, int hgap, int vgap)`.
   * **Best use case:** Calculator buttons, numeric keypads.

* **Code example (2 marks):**
```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
public class GridDemo extends JFrame {
    GridDemo() {
        setLayout(new GridLayout(3, 2, 10, 10));  // 3 rows, 2 cols
        add(new JButton("A"));
        add(new JButton("B"));
        add(new JButton("C"));
        add(new JButton("D"));
        add(new JButton("E"));
        add(new JButton("F"));
        setSize(300, 200);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setVisible(true);
    }
    public static void main(String[] args) { new GridDemo(); }
}
```

**(b) FlowLayout with three colored buttons (7 marks):**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;

public class ColorButtons extends JFrame {
    public ColorButtons() {
        setTitle("Color Buttons");
        setSize(400, 100);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        
        // FlowLayout with CENTER alignment, 10px hgap, 10px vgap
        setLayout(new FlowLayout(FlowLayout.CENTER, 10, 10));
        
        JButton red   = new JButton("Red");
        JButton green = new JButton("Green");
        JButton blue  = new JButton("Blue");
        
        add(red);
        add(green);
        add(blue);
        
        setLocationRelativeTo(null);
        setVisible(true);
    }
    
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new ColorButtons();
    }
}
```

**Explanation (2 marks):** The `FlowLayout(FlowLayout.CENTER, 10, 10)` constructor sets the alignment to center and adds 10px gaps. Buttons are added left-to-right. If the window is too narrow, they will wrap to the next line.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

#### **[KTU University Exam – June 2022]**
**(a)** Explain the `BorderLayout` manager in detail. Write a Java program to display a label at the top, a text area in the center, and a button at the bottom of a `JFrame`. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss `GridBagLayout` and `CardLayout` with suitable examples. Compare their use cases. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) BorderLayout explanation + code (7 marks):**

* **Explanation (3 marks):** `BorderLayout` is the **default layout** for `JFrame`, `JDialog`, and `JApplet` content panes. It divides the container into **5 regions**:
   * `NORTH` (top) and `SOUTH` (bottom) — stretch horizontally, get preferred height.
   * `EAST` (right) and `WEST` (left) — get preferred width, stretch vertically (between N and S).
   * `CENTER` — fills all **remaining space**.
   * At most **one component per region** can be added. If you add multiple to the same region, only the last one is shown.

* **Code (4 marks):**
```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;

public class BorderDemo extends JFrame {
    public BorderDemo() {
        setTitle("BorderLayout Demo");
        setSize(400, 300);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new BorderLayout(5, 5));  // 5px gaps
        
        JLabel header = new JLabel("Header", SwingConstants.CENTER);
        add(header, BorderLayout.NORTH);
        
        JTextArea text = new JTextArea("Type here...");
        add(text, BorderLayout.CENTER);
        
        JButton submit = new JButton("Submit");
        add(submit, BorderLayout.SOUTH);
        
        setLocationRelativeTo(null);
        setVisible(true);
    }
    
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new BorderDemo();
    }
}
```

**(b) GridBagLayout + CardLayout comparison (7 marks):**

* **GridBagLayout (3 marks):**
   * The **most flexible** layout manager.
   * Uses a helper class `GridBagConstraints` with properties: `gridx`, `gridy`, `gridwidth`, `gridheight`, `weightx`, `weighty`, `anchor`, `fill`, `insets`, `ipadx`, `ipady`.
   * Allows components to **span multiple cells**, **stretch** based on weight, and be **anchored** (top-left, center, etc.).
   * **Use case:** Complex forms, IDE tool windows, professional dashboards.

```java
GridBagLayout gbl = new GridBagLayout();
setLayout(gbl);
GridBagConstraints gbc = new GridBagConstraints();
gbc.gridx = 0; gbc.gridy = 0; gbc.weightx = 1; gbc.fill = GridBagConstraints.HORIZONTAL;
add(new JTextField(), gbc);
```

* **CardLayout (2 marks):**
   * Shows **one component at a time** from a stack of "cards".
   * Methods: `next()`, `previous()`, `first()`, `last()`, `show(parent, name)`.
   * **Use case:** Wizards, step-by-step installers, tabbed interfaces.

* **Comparison (2 marks):**

| Aspect | GridBagLayout | CardLayout |
|---|---|---|
| Visibility | All components visible at once | Only one visible at a time |
| Complexity | High (needs constraints) | Medium |
| Use case | Forms, dashboards | Wizards, installers |
| Switching | Automatic (layout-driven) | Manual (programmatic) |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting `setVisible(true)`** at the end of the constructor — the window will not appear (−2 marks).
> 2. **Confusing `validate()` and `revalidate()`** — use `revalidate()` after dynamically adding components at runtime.
> 3. **Adding multiple components to the same `BorderLayout` region** without realizing only the last one shows (−1 to −2 marks).
> 4. **Forgetting to import `java.awt.*`** for `BorderLayout`, `FlowLayout`, `GridLayout` (−1 mark).
> 5. **Confusing `GridLayout(rows, cols)` with `rows = 0`** — `rows=0` means unlimited rows; the grid will keep growing.
> 6. **Not using `setLocationRelativeTo(null)`** to center the window — not a mark-loser, but shows polish.
> 7. **Mixing up `FlowLayout.LEFT` with `FlowLayout.LEADING`** — they are different constants.
> 8. **Forgetting to set the layout BEFORE adding components** — `add()` captures the layout at the time of addition.

---

### 🚀 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Swing Layout Managers**

* ⭐ A **Layout Manager** automatically positions and sizes components in a container.
* ⭐ Every Swing container has a **default layout**:
   * `JFrame`, `JDialog`, `JApplet` → `BorderLayout`.
   * `JPanel` → `FlowLayout`.
* ⭐ **`BorderLayout` has 5 regions:** NORTH, SOUTH, EAST, WEST, CENTER. Only **CENTER** stretches in both directions.
* ⭐ **`FlowLayout`** arranges components left-to-right and wraps to the next line. Alignment: `LEFT`, `CENTER` (default), `RIGHT`.
* ⭐ **`GridLayout`** creates equal-sized cells in a row × column matrix. Components fill one cell each in row-major order.
* ⭐ **`CardLayout`** shows one component at a time. Navigate with `next()`, `previous()`, `first()`, `last()`, `show()`.
* ⭐ **`GridBagLayout`** is the most powerful — uses `GridBagConstraints` for fine-grained control (weights, spans, anchors, insets).
* ⭐ The method `setLayout(LayoutManager)` assigns a manager to a container.
* ⭐ Calling `container.validate()` (or `revalidate()`) forces a re-layout.
* ⭐ The `LayoutManager` interface methods: `addLayoutComponent`, `removeLayoutComponent`, `preferredLayoutSize`, `minimumLayoutSize`, `layoutContainer`.
* ⭐ For KTU exams, the **most common pattern** is: outer `BorderLayout` (JFrame) + nested `JPanel` with `FlowLayout`/`GridLayout` (for toolbars/forms).
* ⭐ **Never** use `setLayout(null)` with absolute positioning in KTU answers — it is a mark-loser.
* ⭐ Standard gap values: `FlowLayout` default = **5 px**, `BorderLayout` default = **0 px**.
* ⭐ To center a window, use `setLocationRelativeTo(null)` after `setSize()`.
* ⭐ Always call `setVisible(true)` as the **last** statement in the constructor.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exploring Swings - JFrame
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT633 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Exploring Swings - JFrame

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of JFrame

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **Java Swing API** (part of **Java Foundation Classes – JFC**), a **`JFrame`** is a top-level container defined in the `javax.swing` package. It is a direct subclass of `java.awt.Frame` and is used to create the **primary application window** on a Graphical User Interface (GUI). A `JFrame` serves as the **root container** of a Swing-based application — every other Swing component (buttons, labels, panels, text fields) must ultimately be hosted inside it (directly or through nested containers like `JPanel`).

The official KTU 2024 syllabus defines it as:

> **JFrame** is a Swing top-level container that provides a window with a title bar, border, optional menu bar, and content pane where other Swing components can be placed. It is the foundational class for building any standalone Swing GUI application in Java.

- **Class Name:** `javax.swing.JFrame`
- **Direct Superclass:** `java.awt.Frame`
- **Type:** Top-level container (`java.awt.Window` → `java.awt.Frame` → `javax.swing.JFrame`)
- **Package:** `javax.swing`
- **Implements:** `Accessible`, `RootPaneContainer`, `WindowConstants`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Never confuse **`JFrame`** with **`Frame`**. `Frame` is a heavyweight AWT component, while `JFrame` is a lightweight Swing component with rich pluggable look-and-feel support. In all KTU 2024 scheme code, you will use **`JFrame`** and not `Frame`.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are building a **house**:
- The **JFrame** is the **outer structure of the house** — the walls, roof, and front door. Without it, nothing else has a place to exist.
- The **title bar** of the JFrame is like the **nameplate** on the front door.
- The **content pane** inside the JFrame is the **rooms of the house** where you place your **furniture** (Swing components like buttons, labels, text fields).
- The **Layout Manager** is the **interior designer** who decides where each piece of furniture goes.
- The **Event Listeners** are the **doorbell and switches** — they respond when the user "rings" (clicks) or "flips" (types) something.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic to remember:** **JFrame = Foundation of every Swing application.** No matter how complex a Java GUI gets, it always starts with a `JFrame`.

---

## 1.3 Position in the Class Hierarchy

The hierarchy is critical for KTU exams. You should memorize the chain:

$$
\text{java.lang.Object} \;\rightarrow\; \text{java.awt.Component} \;\rightarrow\; \text{java.awt.Container} \;\rightarrow\; \text{java.awt.Window} \;\rightarrow\; \text{java.awt.Frame} \;\rightarrow\; \text{javax.swing.JFrame}
$$

This explains why a `JFrame` inherits methods like `setSize()`, `setLocation()`, `setVisible()`, `setBackground()`, and `add()` from its ancestors.

---

## 1.4 Why JFrame Exists — The Problem It Solves

Before Swing (i.e., in pure AWT), the `Frame` class had several limitations:
- **Fixed look-and-feel** dependent on the operating system.
- **Heavyweight** components (each component was a peer of the OS native widget) → poor performance and bugs.
- **No pluggable look-and-feel** support.
- **No double-buffering** by default → flicker during repaints.

`JFrame` solves these by:
- Being **lightweight** (most inner components are pure Java).
- Supporting **pluggable look-and-feel** (Metal, Nimbus, System, etc.).
- Providing a **Root Pane architecture** (layered pane, glass pane, content pane, menu bar slot).
- Enabling **programmatic UI customization** without OS dependency.

---

## 1.5 Markable Highlights for KTU 2024

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Top 5 KTU Board Favourites on JFrame:**
> 1. Default constructor vs parameterized constructor.
> 2. `setDefaultCloseOperation(int operation)` and its 4 constants.
> 3. Difference between `getContentPane().add()` and `add()` (Swing 1.1+ behavior).
> 4. `setLayout()` interaction with `JFrame`.
> 5. `pack()` vs `setSize()` for window sizing.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Method Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Constructors of JFrame

A constructor is used to **instantiate** a `JFrame` object. JFrame provides two main constructors:

| **Constructor** | **Description** | **When to Use** |
|---|---|---|
| `JFrame()` | Creates a new, initially **invisible** frame with no title. | When you want to set the title later using `setTitle()`. |
| `JFrame(String title)` | Creates a new, initially **invisible** frame with the given title. | When you already know the title at construction time. |

```java
// Example 1: Default constructor
JFrame f1 = new JFrame();

// Example 2: Parameterized constructor
JFrame f2 = new JFrame("KTU Student Portal");
```

> [!NOTE]
> A common KTU question asks: *"Why is the JFrame not visible by default?"*
> **Answer:** Because a newly created `JFrame` is **invisible** to give the programmer a chance to add components and configure it before displaying. You must explicitly call `setVisible(true)`.

---

## 2.2 Core Operational Concept — "The Root Pane Architecture"

Every `JFrame` internally contains a **`JRootPane`**, which in turn manages four sub-components:

$$
\text{JFrame} \;\supset\; \text{JRootPane} \;\supset\;
\begin{cases}
\text{JLayeredPane} \\
\quad\;\;\supset \text{JContentPane (where you add components)} \\
\quad\;\;\supset \text{JGlassPane (transparent overlay)} \\
\text{JMenuBar (optional, attached to root pane)}
\end{cases}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Point:** Components are NOT added directly to the `JFrame` — they are added to the **Content Pane**. From **Swing 1.1+**, the `add()` method on `JFrame` is overridden to forward calls to the content pane. So `f.add(button)` is equivalent to `f.getContentPane().add(button)`. Both styles appear in KTU question papers.

---

## 2.3 KTU Method Cheat Sheet — All Essential JFrame Methods

The following table covers the methods that appear most frequently in KTU 2024 scheme exams. Memorize these.

| **Method** | **Signature** | **Purpose** | **Default Behavior** |
|---|---|---|---|
| `setTitle(String)` | `void setTitle(String title)` | Sets the title bar text. | Empty string |
| `setSize(int, int)` | `void setSize(int width, int height)` | Sets window dimensions in pixels. | `(0, 0)` |
| `setLocation(int, int)` | `void setLocation(int x, int y)` | Sets top-left corner position on screen. | OS-dependent |
| `setBounds(int, int, int, int)` | `void setBounds(int x, int y, int w, int h)` | Combines location and size in one call. | N/A |
| `setVisible(boolean)` | `void setVisible(boolean b)` | Shows (`true`) or hides (`false`) the frame. | `false` (hidden) |
| `setResizable(boolean)` | `void setResizable(boolean b)` | Allows (`true`) or disallows user resizing. | `true` |
| `setDefaultCloseOperation(int)` | `void setDefaultCloseOperation(int op)` | Defines what happens on close (X) button. | `HIDE_ON_CLOSE` (Swing), but AWT Frame was `EXIT_ON_CLOSE`-like; the **Swing default for JFrame is `HIDE_ON_CLOSE`** only when set explicitly — actually default is `WindowConstants.HIDE_ON_CLOSE` in JFrame? **Correction:** Default is `DISPOSE_ON_CLOSE` for `JFrame` since Swing 1.3 — verify with constants below. |
| `getContentPane()` | `Container getContentPane()` | Returns the content pane where components are added. | Always present |
| `setContentPane(Container)` | `void setContentPane(Container c)` | Replaces the default content pane. | Internal JPanel |
| `setJMenuBar(JMenuBar)` | `void setJMenuBar(JMenuBar mb)` | Attaches a menu bar to the frame. | None |
| `setLayout(LayoutManager)` | `void setLayout(LayoutManager mgr)` | Sets the layout manager. | `BorderLayout` |
| `pack()` | `void pack()` | Sizes the frame to fit preferred sizes of its components. | Not auto-called |
| `setLocationRelativeTo(Component)` | `void setLocationRelativeTo(Component c)` | Centers the window relative to a component (or screen if `null`). | N/A |
| `setIconImage(Image)` | `void setIconImage(Image img)` | Sets the frame's icon (taskbar/window). | Java default |
| `setBackground(Color)` | `void setBackground(Color bg)` | Sets background color of content pane. | OS-dependent |

### 2.3.1 The 4 Constants of `setDefaultCloseOperation`

This is one of the most asked sub-topics. The parameter is an `int` constant from `WindowConstants`:

$$
\text{WindowConstants} = \begin{cases}
\text{EXIT\_ON\_CLOSE} = 3 \\[-2pt]
\text{DISPOSE\_ON\_CLOSE} = 2 \\[-2pt]
\text{HIDE\_ON\_CLOSE} = 1 \\[-2pt]
\text{DO\_NOTHING\_ON\_CLOSE} = 0
\end{cases}
$$

| **Constant** | **Value** | **Behavior on Close Button Click** |
|---|---|---|
| `EXIT_ON_CLOSE` | **3** | Exits the entire application (terminates JVM via `System.exit(0)`). |
| `DISPOSE_ON_CLOSE` | **2** | Closes & disposes the window. JVM continues if other windows exist. |
| `HIDE_ON_CLOSE` | **1** | Just hides the window. The object still exists in memory. |
| `DO_NOTHING_ON_CLOSE` | **0** | Ignores the close event — window stays open. Programmer must handle manually. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Using `EXIT_ON_CLOSE` in a Java Applet or multi-window app is incorrect. Use `DISPOSE_ON_CLOSE` for child windows, `EXIT_ON_CLOSE` only for the **main/primary** window. **Common 1-mark trap.**

---

## 2.4 Deep Dive — `pack()` vs `setSize()`

| **Aspect** | `setSize(w, h)` | `pack()` |
|---|---|---|
| Dimension source | **Hardcoded** by programmer. | **Computed** from preferred sizes of inner components. |
| When to use | Fixed-size UIs (calculators, splash screens). | Dynamic UIs with auto-scaling. |
| Order of call | Any time, but typically before `setVisible()`. | MUST be called after all components are added. |
| Respects `setPreferredSize()`? | No, overrides it. | Yes, uses it. |
| Respects Layout Manager? | No, overrides layout. | Yes, re-runs layout. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Best practice (KTU-recommended):** If you use a `LayoutManager`, prefer `pack()` over `setSize()`. If you use `setSize()`, you may want to call `setLayout(null)` to use absolute positioning.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

In engineering applications, `JFrame` is the foundation of:
- **SCADA/HMI panels** in Industrial Automation (factory control rooms).
- **Data acquisition GUIs** for IoT dashboards.
- **Medical imaging tools** (DICOM viewers, ECG monitors).
- **CAD/CAM frontends** and simulation tools.
- **Educational software** for circuit simulators and physics labs.

In the KTU 2024 B.Tech context, you will build mini-projects in **OOP labs** using JFrame + JButton + JTextField + ActionListener.

---

## 2.6 Lifecycle of a JFrame Application

The standard sequence of method calls is critical and is a frequent KTU question:

```java
JFrame f = new JFrame("Title");    // 1. Construct
f.setSize(400, 300);                 // 2. Size (or use pack())
f.setLayout(new FlowLayout());       // 3. Layout (optional)
f.add(new JButton("Click"));         // 4. Add components
f.setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE); // 5. Close behavior
f.setVisible(true);                  // 6. Display (ALWAYS LAST)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic:** **"C-S-L-A-D-V"** → **C**onstruct, **S**ize, **L**ayout, **A**dd, **D**efault-close, **V**isible.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Code Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Method-by-Method Tracing

This section walks through the **internal call chain** of common JFrame operations, as a KTU examiner expects when they ask "explain the working of `setVisible(true)`".

---

### 3.1.1 Trace: `f.setVisible(true)`

When `setVisible(true)` is invoked, the following internal sequence occurs on the Event Dispatch Thread (EDT):

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{setVisible(true)} \\
&\;\;\downarrow \;\text{overridden in JFrame to handle root pane} \\
&\text{Window.show()} \\
&\;\;\downarrow \\
&\text{addNotify()} \rightarrow \text{peer created (native window)} \\
&\;\;\downarrow \\
&\text{EventQueue.invokeLater(postEvent)} \\
&\;\;\downarrow \\
&\text{WindowEvent.WINDOW\_OPENED dispatched to listeners} \\
&\;\;\downarrow \\
&\text{paint() called recursively} \rightarrow \text{children painted}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The first call to `setVisible(true)` is what **triggers peer creation** and **layout calculation**. If you change component properties after `setVisible(true)`, you must call `revalidate()` and `repaint()` manually.

---

### 3.1.2 Trace: `f.add(component)`

Because `JFrame` extends `RootPaneContainer`, the overridden `add()` is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{f.add(Component c)} \;&\equiv\; \text{f.getContentPane().add(c)} \\
&\equiv \text{contentPane.add(c)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This delegation is why both code styles compile and work identically in Swing.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Minimum Viable JFrame

**Problem (3 marks KTU style):** Write a Java program to create a JFrame titled "Hello KTU" of size 300x200, with a JLabel "Welcome", and ensure the program exits when the window is closed.

### Solution with full derivation:

**Step 1:** Import required packages.
```java
import javax.swing.JFrame;
import javax.swing.JLabel;
import java.awt.FlowLayout;
```

**Step 2:** Decide on the close behavior.
- Since this is the only window, **`EXIT_ON_CLOSE`** is appropriate (JVM should terminate when user closes it).

**Step 3:** Choose a layout.
- `FlowLayout` is the simplest, suitable for a single label.

**Step 4:** Apply the **C-S-L-A-D-V** lifecycle.

```java
import javax.swing.JFrame;
import javax.swing.JLabel;
import java.awt.FlowLayout;

public class HelloKTUFrame {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // 1. Construct
        JFrame f = new JFrame("Hello KTU");
        
        // 2. Size
        f.setSize(300, 200);
        
        // 3. Layout
        f.setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        
        // 4. Add
        f.add(new JLabel("Welcome to Object Oriented Programming"));
        
        // 5. Default close operation
        f.setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        
        // 6. Visible
        f.setVisible(true);
    }
}
```

### Output (conceptual):
A 300x200 pixel window appears with title **"Hello KTU"**, containing the text "Welcome to Object Oriented Programming", left-aligned. Clicking the **X** button terminates the program.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Centering a Frame on Screen

**Problem:** Modify the previous program to center the window on the user's screen.

### Derivation:
- Method: `setLocationRelativeTo(null)` → passing `null` centers the window **relative to the screen**.
- This method should be called **after `setSize()`** and **before `setVisible(true)`** so the centering is computed on the final dimensions.

### Solution:
```java
import javax.swing.JFrame;
import javax.swing.JLabel;
import java.awt.FlowLayout;

public class CenteredFrame {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        JFrame f = new JFrame("Centered KTU Window");
        f.setSize(400, 250);
        f.setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        f.add(new JLabel("This window is centered."));
        f.setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        
        // Center the frame on the screen
        f.setLocationRelativeTo(null);
        
        f.setVisible(true);
    }
}
```

### Step-by-step logical breakdown:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Screen width} = W_s, \quad \text{Screen height} = H_s \\
&\text{Frame width} = W_f = 400, \quad \text{Frame height} = H_f = 250 \\
&\text{Computed } x = \frac{W_s - W_f}{2} \\
&\text{Computed } y = \frac{H_s - H_f}{2} \\
&\text{Frame top-left} = (x,\; y)
\end{aligned}
$$

The JVM uses the screen dimensions reported by `GraphicsEnvironment` and applies the above formula internally.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Using `pack()` with Preferred Sizes

**Problem (7 marks KTU style):** Demonstrate the use of `pack()` and explain why it is preferred over `setSize()` when a LayoutManager is used.

### Solution derivation:

**Concept:** `pack()` queries every component's `getPreferredSize()` and resizes the frame to exactly fit them, with the layout manager determining the arrangement.

```java
import javax.swing.JFrame;
import javax.swing.JButton;
import javax.swing.JPanel;
import java.awt.GridLayout;

public class PackExample {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        JFrame f = new JFrame("Pack Demo");
        f.setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        f.setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        
        JButton b1 = new JButton("Submit");
        JButton b2 = new JButton("Cancel");
        JButton b3 = new JButton("Reset");
        
        f.add(b1);
        f.add(b2);
        f.add(b3);
        
        f.pack();  // Sizes the frame to fit preferred sizes
        
        f.setLocationRelativeTo(null);
        f.setVisible(true);
    }
}
```

### Why `pack()` works:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Frame width} &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\text{component } i \text{ preferred width}) + \text{horizontal gaps} \\
\text{Frame height} &= \max_{i}(\text{component } i \text{ preferred height}) + \text{vertical gaps}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparative behavior with `setSize(300, 300)`:**
> - `setSize(300,300)` would force the window to 300x300 regardless of button sizes — possible clipping or wasted space.
> - `pack()` adapts to the actual content — robust across platforms and fonts.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 4 — Handling Window Events with WindowAdapter

**Problem (7 marks):** Write a program that confirms before closing — show a JOptionPane dialog on the close button.

### Full derivation:

**Step 1:** Identify needed classes:
- `WindowAdapter` → convenience class implementing `WindowListener`.
- `JOptionPane.showConfirmDialog()` → standard confirmation dialog.

**Step 2:** Override only `windowClosing()` since `WindowAdapter` provides empty defaults for other methods.

```java
import javax.swing.JFrame;
import javax.swing.JOptionPane;
import java.awt.event.WindowAdapter;
import java.awt.event.WindowEvent;

public class ConfirmCloseFrame {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        JFrame f = new JFrame("Confirm Close Demo");
        f.setSize(350, 150);
        f.setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.DO_NOTHING_ON_CLOSE);
        
        f.addWindowListener(new WindowAdapter() {
            @Override
            public void windowClosing(WindowEvent e) {
                int choice = JOptionPane.showConfirmDialog(
                    f,
                    "Are you sure you want to close?",
                    "Confirm Exit",
                    JOptionPane.YES_NO_OPTION
                );
                if (choice == JOptionPane.YES_OPTION) {
                    f.dispose();
                    System.exit(0);
                }
            }
        });
        
        f.setLocationRelativeTo(null);
        f.setVisible(true);
    }
}
```

### Logical chain:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{User clicks X} \rightarrow \text{DO\_NOTHING\_ON\_CLOSE intercepts} \\
&\;\;\downarrow \\
&\text{WindowAdapter.windowClosing() fires} \\
&\;\;\downarrow \\
&\text{JOptionPane appears} \\
&\;\;\downarrow \\
&\text{If YES} \rightarrow f.dispose() \rightarrow System.exit(0) \\
&\text{If NO} \rightarrow \text{Window stays open (no action)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why `WindowAdapter` and not `WindowListener` directly?**
> - `WindowListener` has 7 abstract methods. If you implement it, you must override all 7 (even with empty bodies).
> - `WindowAdapter` provides **empty default implementations** for all 7 methods. You override only what you need — cleaner code, fewer KTU mark deductions.

The 7 methods of `WindowListener` are: `windowOpened`, `windowClosing`, `windowClosed`, `windowIconified`, `windowDeiconified`, `windowActivated`, `windowDeactivated`.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 x 3 = 6 Marks)

---

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Explain the role of `JFrame` in Java Swing. List any four important methods of `JFrame` with their purpose.** (3 Marks)

### Model Answer:

**Role of JFrame:**
`JFrame` is a top-level container in the `javax.swing` package used to create the main application window of a Swing-based GUI. It provides a window with a title bar, border, optional menu bar, and a content pane where other Swing components such as buttons, labels, and text fields are placed. Every Swing GUI application must start with a `JFrame`.

**Four Important Methods:**

| **Method** | **Purpose** |
|---|---|
| `setTitle(String t)` | Sets the title displayed in the window's title bar. |
| `setSize(int w, int h)` | Defines the width and height of the frame in pixels. |
| `setVisible(boolean b)` | Makes the window visible (`true`) or hides it (`false`). |
| `setDefaultCloseOperation(int op)` | Defines what happens when the user clicks the close (X) button — e.g., `EXIT_ON_CLOSE`, `DISPOSE_ON_CLOSE`. |

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Differentiate between `pack()` and `setSize()` methods of `JFrame`. When would you prefer one over the other?** (3 Marks)

### Model Answer:

| **Aspect** | `setSize(w, h)` | `pack()` |
|---|---|---|
| Dimension source | Hardcoded by the programmer. | Computed from the preferred sizes of components. |
| Flexibility | Fixed regardless of content. | Adaptive — frame resizes to fit content. |
| Layout manager interaction | May override layout decisions. | Respects layout manager. |

**Preference:**
- Use **`setSize()`** when the window must have a **fixed dimension** (e.g., a calculator, splash screen).
- Use **`pack()`** when components have variable or natural sizes, and you want the frame to **auto-size** to fit them — this is the **KTU-recommended best practice** when a layout manager is in use.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the JFrame class hierarchy. Discuss the significance of the **Root Pane architecture** in `JFrame`. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Write a Java program to create a JFrame titled **"KTU Student Registration"** with:
- A `JLabel` displaying "Enter Name:".
- A `JTextField` for input.
- A `JButton` labeled "Submit".
- A `FlowLayout` layout manager.
- The frame should be **400x150 pixels**, **centered on screen**, and the program should **exit** when the close button is clicked. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution — (a)

**JFrame Class Hierarchy:**

$$
\text{java.lang.Object} \rightarrow \text{java.awt.Component} \rightarrow \text{java.awt.Container} \rightarrow \text{java.awt.Window} \rightarrow \text{java.awt.Frame} \rightarrow \text{javax.swing.JFrame}
$$

- `JFrame` inherits all window-related functionality from `Frame`.
- It overrides `add()` and `setLayout()` to delegate to the content pane (since Swing 1.1).
- It implements `RootPaneContainer` and `WindowConstants` interfaces.

**Significance of Root Pane Architecture:**

Every `JFrame` has a single `JRootPane` that organises its child components into:
- **Glass Pane** — transparent overlay (used for tooltips, mouse-drag painting).
- **Layered Pane** — holds the content pane and menu bar in distinct layers.
- **Content Pane** — the actual area where user-interactive components are added.
- **Menu Bar** — optional, attached separately to the root pane.

**Why it matters:**
- Decouples the developer (who adds to content pane) from the internal Swing rendering machinery.
- Enables features like **JInternalFrame**, **desktop panes**, and **layered popups**.
- Simplifies painting, event-dispatching, and look-and-feel switching.

---

#### Model Solution — (b)

```java
import javax.swing.JFrame;
import javax.swing.JLabel;
import javax.swing.JTextField;
import javax.swing.JButton;
import java.awt.FlowLayout;

public class KTURegistration {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Step 1: Construct the JFrame with title
        JFrame frame = new JFrame("KTU Student Registration");
        
        // Step 2: Set size
        frame.setSize(400, 150);
        
        // Step 3: Set layout
        frame.setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        
        // Step 4: Add components
        frame.add(new JLabel("Enter Name:"));
        frame.add(new JTextField(20));
        frame.add(new JButton("Submit"));
        
        // Step 5: Default close operation
        frame.setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        
        // Center the frame
        frame.setLocationRelativeTo(null);
        
        // Step 6: Make visible
        frame.setVisible(true);
    }
}
```

**Explanation of key lines:**
- `new JFrame("KTU Student Registration")` — uses the **parameterized constructor** to set the title at creation time.
- `setSize(400, 150)` — sets the window to exactly 400 pixels wide, 150 pixels tall.
- `setLayout(new FlowLayout())` — arranges components left-to-right, top-to-bottom.
- `new JTextField(20)` — creates a text field 20 columns wide.
- `setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE)` — clicking X terminates the program (suitable for the only/main window).
- `setLocationRelativeTo(null)` — centers the frame on the screen.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** With suitable examples, explain the **four constants** used in `setDefaultCloseOperation()`. Discuss when each is appropriate. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Write a Java Swing program that demonstrates the use of **`WindowAdapter`** to handle the window-closing event. The program should display a **confirmation dialog** asking "Do you want to exit?" before actually closing the JFrame. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution — (a)

The `setDefaultCloseOperation(int operation)` method takes one of four integer constants defined in the `WindowConstants` interface (inherited by `JFrame`):

| **Constant** | **Integer Value** | **Behavior** | **When Appropriate** |
|---|---|---|---|
| `DO_NOTHING_ON_CLOSE` | **0** | Ignores the close event. Window remains open. Programmer must handle the close manually. | When unsaved data must be confirmed before exit; for security-sensitive GUIs. |
| `HIDE_ON_CLOSE` | **1** | Hides the window but keeps the object in memory. | For child/dialog-style windows of a parent app that must remain alive in the background. |
| `DISPOSE_ON_CLOSE` | **2** | Closes the window and releases its resources. JVM continues if other windows are open. | For secondary windows of a multi-window application (e.g., a sub-form). |
| `EXIT_ON_CLOSE` | **3** | Terminates the JVM via `System.exit(0)`. | For the **main/primary** window of a single-window application. |

**Example code:**
```java
JFrame main = new JFrame("Main");
main.setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);

JFrame child = new JFrame("Child");
child.setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.DISPOSE_ON_CLOSE);
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do not** use `EXIT_ON_CLOSE` in applets (deprecated, may throw `SecurityException`).
> - **Do not** use `EXIT_ON_CLOSE` for child windows of a multi-document app — only the main window should exit the JVM.
> - Forgetting to set this method results in the **default behavior being to do nothing visible** (the window hides but program continues, often surprising students).

---

#### Model Solution — (b)

```java
import javax.swing.JFrame;
import javax.swing.JOptionPane;
import javax.swing.JLabel;
import java.awt.FlowLayout;
import java.awt.event.WindowAdapter;
import java.awt.event.WindowEvent;

public class ConfirmExitFrame extends JFrame {
    
    public ConfirmExitFrame() {
        setTitle("Confirm Close Demo");
        setSize(350, 150);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        add(new JLabel("Try closing this window."));
        
        // Override default close to do nothing — we handle it manually
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.DO_NOTHING_ON_CLOSE);
        
        // Attach a WindowAdapter
        addWindowListener(new WindowAdapter() {
            @Override
            public void windowClosing(WindowEvent e) {
                int response = JOptionPane.showConfirmDialog(
                    ConfirmExitFrame.this,
                    "Do you want to exit?",
                    "Exit Confirmation",
                    JOptionPane.YES_NO_OPTION,
                    JOptionPane.WARNING_MESSAGE
                );
                
                if (response == JOptionPane.YES_OPTION) {
                    dispose();      // release window resources
                    System.exit(0); // terminate JVM
                }
                // If NO, do nothing — window stays open
            }
        });
        
        setLocationRelativeTo(null);
        setVisible(true);
    }
    
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new ConfirmExitFrame();
    }
}
```

**Key Concept Explanation:**

1. `WindowAdapter` is a **convenience class** that provides empty implementations of all 7 methods of `WindowListener`. By extending it and overriding only `windowClosing()`, we avoid boilerplate code.
2. `setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.DO_NOTHING_ON_CLOSE)` is **essential** — without it, the window would still hide automatically. With this constant, the click event is dispatched to our listener for custom handling.
3. The lambda/inner class captures the outer `JFrame` reference (`ConfirmExitFrame.this`) for the dialog's parent component.
4. Only if the user clicks "Yes" do we call `dispose()` followed by `System.exit(0)`. Otherwise, control returns and the window remains open.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Forgetting** `setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.DO_NOTHING_ON_CLOSE)` → the window hides automatically, your confirmation dialog becomes **redundant** → **2 marks lost**.
> - **Implementing `WindowListener` directly** instead of `WindowAdapter` → must override all 7 methods (most students leave them empty or miss one) → **1 mark lost** for "best practice".
> - **Calling `System.exit(0)` before `dispose()`** is acceptable, but calling `dispose()` without `System.exit(0)` won't end the program (JVM keeps running) → may be acceptable for multi-window apps but a **partial deduction** if the question says "exit the program".
> - **Missing `setLocationRelativeTo(null)` or `pack()`** when not strictly required → not penalized if the question didn't specify.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for "Exploring Swings – JFrame"**

- **`JFrame` is in `javax.swing`** (not `java.awt` — that is `Frame`, an AWT class).
- **Class hierarchy:** `Object → Component → Container → Window → Frame → JFrame`.
- A new `JFrame` is **invisible by default** — must call `setVisible(true)`.
- Two constructors: `JFrame()` and `JFrame(String title)`.
- Default **layout manager** of `JFrame` is `BorderLayout`.
- Components are added to the **content pane**, not directly to the JFrame (though `JFrame.add()` auto-delegates to the content pane).
- The four close-operation constants and their integer values:
   - `DO_NOTHING_ON_CLOSE` = **0**
   - `HIDE_ON_CLOSE` = **1**
   - `DISPOSE_ON_CLOSE` = **2**
   - `EXIT_ON_CLOSE` = **3**
- Use **`EXIT_ON_CLOSE`** only for the main window; use **`DISPOSE_ON_CLOSE`** for child windows.
- Use **`pack()`** instead of `setSize()` for auto-sizing with layout managers.
- Use **`setLocationRelativeTo(null)`** to center on screen.
- Use **`WindowAdapter`** (not `WindowListener`) to handle window events cleanly.
- The 7 methods of `WindowListener`: `windowOpened`, `windowClosing`, `windowClosed`, `windowIconified`, `windowDeiconified`, `windowActivated`, `windowDeactivated`.
- **Root Pane** components: Glass Pane, Layered Pane, Content Pane, optional Menu Bar.
- **C-S-L-A-D-V** lifecycle mnemonic: **C**onstruct → **S**ize → **L**ayout → **A**dd → **D**efault-close → **V**isible.
- **JFrame is lightweight** (Swing); **Frame is heavyweight** (AWT) — do not mix in KTU code.
- `JFrame` implements `WindowConstants`, `RootPaneContainer`, and `Accessible` interfaces.
- Always call `setDefaultCloseOperation()` explicitly — do not rely on defaults.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

In the **Java Swing** graphical user interface (GUI) framework, a **`JLabel`** (declared in the package **`javax.swing`**, class file `JLabel.class`) is a **lightweight, non-interactive display area** used to render a single line of read-only text, an image, or both. As a subclass of **`javax.swing.JComponent`**, the `JLabel` is the most fundamental passive visual element in Swing and serves as the primary mechanism for **describing the purpose of interactive components** (such as `JButton`, `JTextField`) to the end user, thereby satisfying accessibility (screen-reader) requirements under the Java Accessibility API.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim style):**
> *"A `JLabel` is an object of class `javax.swing.JLabel` that can display a short text string, an image, or both. It does not generate any `ActionEvent` when clicked because it inherits the passive behavior of `JComponent`."*

### Class Hierarchy Declaration

```java
java.lang.Object
  └── java.awt.Component
        └── java.awt.Container
              └── javax.swing.JComponent
                        └── javax.swing.JLabel
```

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Museum Wall Placard" 🏛️

Imagine walking through a museum. Next to every painting (the *interactive components* like buttons and text fields) is a small **placard on the wall** that says "Mona Lisa — c.1503" or "Please do not touch." That placard:

- **Tells you what something is** (it labels the painting).
- **Cannot be clicked or modified by visitors** (it is passive).
- **May contain text only, a picture only, or both** (text + icon).

A `JLabel` is exactly that **passive placard** in a Java Swing window. It does not "do" anything; it merely **describes, identifies, or decorates** the interactive widgets beside it.

## 1.3 Key Properties (Highlight Callouts)

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Properties of `JLabel`:**
> - **Package:** `javax.swing`
> - **Direct Superclass:** `javax.swing.JComponent`
> - **Direct Subclasses:** `BasicOptionPaneUI.ButtonAreaLayout` (rare), and it is commonly extended to build custom label-like widgets.
> - **Event Generation:** **None** (no `ActionEvent`, no `MouseEvent` handling by default).
> - **Default Layout Manager Behavior:** Uses the `LayoutManager` of the parent container; the label itself does not lay out children.
> - **Default Visual Style:** Transparent background, uses the system "Label" UI defaults (`LabelUI`).

## 1.4 Standard Metric — Mnemonic Key `&` for Keyboard Shortcuts

In Swing, the **`&` (ampersand) character** inside a `JLabel` text string is treated as a **mnemonic key** indicator. When followed by a letter (e.g., `"&Name"`), that letter becomes an **underlined shortcut**. If the label is paired with a component via **`setLabelFor(Component)`**, pressing **Alt + that letter** transfers focus to the target component. This is a **standard WCAG accessibility metric** used in Java GUIs.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Constructors of `JLabel`

A `JLabel` can be instantiated using **one of three primary constructors**. Selecting the right constructor is a frequent **3-mark KTU question**.

| # | Constructor Signature | Resulting Content | Typical Use Case |
|---|----------------------|-------------------|------------------|
| **C1** | `JLabel()` | Empty label (no text, no icon) | Placeholder; assigned dynamically later. |
| **C2** | `JLabel(String text)` | Text-only label | Form field descriptions (e.g., "Username:"). |
| **C3** | `JLabel(Icon image)` | Icon-only label | Decorative graphics, status indicators. |
| **C4** | `JLabel(String text, Icon image, int horizontalAlignment)` | Text + Icon combined | Unified display (icon beside name). |

## 2.2 The Horizontal Alignment Constant Set

`JLabel` exposes a static integer constant set used to govern the placement of its contents **within the label's painted area**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The four alignment constants are:**
> - `SwingConstants.LEFT` (value = **2**)
> - `SwingConstants.CENTER` (value = **0**)
> - `SwingConstants.RIGHT` (value = **4**)
> - `SwingConstants.LEADING` (value = **10**) — *default; respects locale orientation (LTR/RTL)*
> - `SwingConstants.TRAILING` (value = **11**)

The default alignment is **`LEADING`**, which means the content is positioned at the start of the text direction in the current locale. This is the only alignment that handles right-to-left (RTL) languages such as Arabic correctly without code changes.

## 2.3 Core API Methods — The High-Yield Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates the **exam-essential methods** of `JLabel`. Note the explicit use of `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

| Method | Return Type | Purpose / Why it Matters |
|--------|-------------|--------------------------|
| `setText(String text)` | `void` | Dynamically updates the label's text string at runtime. |
| `getText()` | `String` | Retrieves the current text — used in form validation logic. |
| `setIcon(Icon icon)` | `void` | Attaches an `ImageIcon` or any `Icon` implementation to the label. |
| `getIcon()` | `Icon` | Returns the current icon; `null` if none. |
| `setHorizontalAlignment(int)` | `void` | Sets text/icon placement (`LEFT`, `CENTER`, `RIGHT`, `LEADING`, `TRAILING`). |
| `setVerticalAlignment(int)` | `void` | Sets vertical positioning (`TOP`, `CENTER`, `BOTTOM`). |
| `setHorizontalTextPosition(int)` | `void` | Position of the **text relative to the icon** (e.g., text on right of icon). |
| `setVerticalTextPosition(int)` | `void` | Position of text **above/below** the icon. |
| `setIconTextGap(int)` | `void` | Pixel gap between icon and text (default = **4 pixels**). |
| `setLabelFor(Component c)` | `void` | Associates a focusable component with the label (mnemonic target). |
| `setDisplayedMnemonic(char)` | `void` | Sets the underlined mnemonic character (e.g., `'N'` for **N**ame). |
| `setDisplayedMnemonicIndex(int)` | `void` | Index of the mnemonic character within the text (for Unicode safety). |
| `setDisabledIcon(Icon)` | `void` | Icon shown when label is **disabled** (when `setEnabled(false)` is invoked). |

## 2.4 The `Why` Behind Passive Behavior — Inheritance Logic

A `JLabel` **does not** fire `ActionEvent`s because it inherits from `JComponent`, which extends `java.awt.Container`, which extends `java.awt.Component`. The event-firing capability of interactive widgets like `AbstractButton` is **not** in this lineage. This is a deliberate **separation-of-concerns** design pattern in Swing: *display components display; control components control.*

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

`JLabel` is heavily used in:

1. **Enterprise Java Desktop Apps (legacy Swing GUIs in banking/ERP software)** — labelling every input field.
2. **Java IDE plugins and developer tools** — NetBeans, IntelliJ, and Eclipse use Swing extensively for dialogs.
3. **Accessibility-compliant form design** — the `setLabelFor()` + mnemonic pattern is **mandatory** under Java SE Accessibility guidelines.
4. **Embedded system UI dashboards** running JavaFX/Swing on kiosks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — How a `JLabel` is Constructed and Configured

Below is the **exhaustive, step-by-step logical derivation** of how a typical KTU-style "Student Login" label is built. Every transition is explicitly justified.

### Step 1 — Import Statements

```java
import javax.swing.JFrame;
import javax.swing.JLabel;
import javax.swing.JTextField;
import javax.swing.SwingConstants;
import java.awt.FlowLayout;
```

**Reasoning:** `JLabel` lives in `javax.swing` (not `java.awt.Label`, which is the older heavyweight AWT version). The `SwingConstants` interface provides the alignment integers.

### Step 2 — Instantiate the Container

```java
JFrame frame = new JFrame("Student Login Portal");
frame.setSize(400, 200);
frame.setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
frame.setLayout(new FlowLayout());
```

**Reasoning:** A `JLabel` cannot stand alone — it must be hosted by a top-level container (`JFrame`, `JDialog`, or `JApplet`). The `FlowLayout` arranges components left-to-right in their preferred order.

### Step 3 — Construct the Label

```java
JLabel nameLabel = new JLabel("Enter your full name:");
```

**Reasoning:** This invokes **Constructor C2** from the cheat sheet. The string is stored internally in the protected field `text` of `JLabel`.

### Step 4 — Apply the Mnemonic Pattern

```java
JTextField nameField = new JTextField(20);
nameLabel.setDisplayedMnemonic('N');
nameLabel.setLabelFor(nameField);
```

**Reasoning:** The character `'N'` is the mnemonic for the underlined letter in "**N**ame" (well, here the label is "Enter your full name:", and `'N'` will be the first 'n' encountered by default — but we can override the index using `setDisplayedMnemonicIndex(6)` to target the capital **N** in "Name"). The `setLabelFor` call binds the focus target.

### Step 5 — Configure Alignment

```java
nameLabel.setHorizontalAlignment(SwingConstants.LEFT);
nameLabel.setVerticalAlignment(SwingConstants.CENTER);
```

**Reasoning:** These calls are necessary only when the label's painted area is **larger** than its content (e.g., inside a `GridLayout` cell). For `FlowLayout`, alignment has no visible effect.

### Step 6 — Attach an Icon (Text + Icon Variant)

```java
import javax.swing.ImageIcon;

ImageIcon userIcon = new ImageIcon("user.png");
JLabel iconLabel = new JLabel("Profile", userIcon, SwingConstants.LEFT);
iconLabel.setHorizontalTextPosition(SwingConstants.RIGHT);
iconLabel.setVerticalTextPosition(SwingConstants.CENTER);
iconLabel.setIconTextGap(8);
```

**Reasoning:** This invokes **Constructor C4**. The `setIconTextGap(8)` overrides the **4-pixel default** to provide more whitespace.

### Step 7 — Add to the Container

```java
frame.add(nameLabel);
frame.add(nameField);
frame.add(iconLabel);
frame.setVisible(true);
```

**Reasoning:** Until `add()` is called, the label exists only in memory. `setVisible(true)` triggers Swing's painting pipeline, which calls `paintComponent(Graphics g)` on each component.

## 3.2 Full Standalone Runnable Demonstration Program

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;

public class JLabelDemo extends JFrame {

    public JLabelDemo() {
        setTitle("KTU JLabel Demonstration");
        setSize(450, 250);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout(FlowLayout.LEFT, 10, 15));

        // Text-only label
        JLabel lbl1 = new JLabel("Roll Number:");
        add(lbl1);

        JTextField tf1 = new JTextField(15);
        add(tf1);

        // Mnemonic binding
        lbl1.setDisplayedMnemonic('R');
        lbl1.setLabelFor(tf1);

        // Icon + text label
        ImageIcon icon = new ImageIcon("logo.png");
        JLabel lbl2 = new JLabel("Verified", icon, SwingConstants.LEFT);
        lbl2.setHorizontalTextPosition(SwingConstants.RIGHT);
        lbl2.setIconTextGap(10);
        lbl2.setEnabled(true);
        add(lbl2);

        // Disabled label
        JLabel lbl3 = new JLabel("Disabled State");
        lbl3.setEnabled(false);
        add(lbl3);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SwingUtilities.invokeLater(() -> {
            new JLabelDemo().setVisible(true);
        });
    }
}
```

## 3.3 The "Mnemonic Index" Mathematical Mapping

The relationship between the displayed text string and the underlined mnemonic character is given by:

$$M = \text{setDisplayedMnemonicIndex}(i)$$

where $M$ is the character at index $i$ in the underlying `String text` (0-based, Java index).

$$i \in [0, \text{text.length()}-1] \quad \text{and} \quad i \in \mathbb{Z}^+ \cup \{0\}$$

If the index $i$ exceeds the string length, the call is silently ignored. This is the **standard Java bounds-checked assignment** behavior seen throughout Swing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter?** Unicode strings (e.g., Hindi, Malayalam) may have compound characters where the "N" character begins at byte offset that does not match the visual position. `setDisplayedMnemonicIndex` allows the developer to specify the **visual** position independent of the byte index.

## 3.4 Icon-Text Gap — Pixel-Based Offset Formula

Let $G$ be the gap (in pixels) between the icon and the text. The total painted width of a `JLabel` holding both an icon and text is:

$$W_{\text{label}} = W_{\text{icon}} + G + W_{\text{text}}$$

where $W_{\text{icon}}$ is the icon's intrinsic width, $G$ is the gap set via `setIconTextGap()`, and $W_{\text{text}}$ is the text's font-metric width. Default values: $G_{\text{default}} = 4$ pixels.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Class Inheritance Hierarchy (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    objA[java lang Object]
    compA[java awt Component]
    contA[java awt Container]
    jcA[javax swing JComponent]

    style jbA fill:#bfb,stroke:#333
    style jtfA fill:#bfb,stroke:#333
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** `JLabel` and `JTextField` share a common ancestor at `JComponent`, **not** at `AbstractButton`. This is precisely why `JLabel` is passive (no `ActionEvent`) while `JTextField` (which extends `AbstractButton`... actually `JTextComponent` from `JComponent`) handles user input.

## 4.2 JLabel Object Lifecycle — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid

    addA[Step 5 Add to Container JFrame]
    paintA[Step 6 setVisible true triggers paint]

    eventA -.-> displayA

```

## 4.3 Mnemonic Binding Flow — Accessibility Architecture

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User Keyboard
    participant JL as JLabel
    participant KMD as KeyEventDispatcher
    participant TC as Target Component

    U->>JL: Press Alt + N
    JL->>KMD: Fire Mnemonic Action
    KMD->>TC: requestFocusInWindow
    TC-->>U: Caret visible in text field
    Note over JL,TC: LabelFor binding has transferred focus
```


# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define `JLabel` in Java Swing. List any four of its commonly used methods.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A `JLabel` is a class in the `javax.swing` package used to display a **short text string, an image, or both** on a GUI. It is a **passive, non-interactive** component (inherits from `JComponent`) and does not generate any `ActionEvent`.

Four commonly used methods:
1. `setText(String)` — sets the displayed text.
2. `setIcon(Icon)` — sets the displayed icon.
3. `setHorizontalAlignment(int)` — aligns the content (`LEFT`, `CENTER`, `RIGHT`, `LEADING`, `TRAILING`).
4. `setLabelFor(Component)` — binds a focusable target component to the label for mnemonic shortcuts.

*(1 mark definition + ½ mark × 4 = 2 marks methods; rounding per KTU valuation key = 3 marks total.)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between `java.awt.Label` and `javax.swing.JLabel`. Mention any two points.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Sl. No. | `java.awt.Label` | `javax.swing.JLabel` |
|---------|-------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | Belongs to the **AWT** package (heavyweight; uses peer components). | Belongs to the **Swing** package (lightweight; 100% Java painted). |
| 2 | Supports **text only**. | Supports **text, icon, or both** in a single label. |
| 3 | Limited accessibility support. | Supports **mnemonics** and `setLabelFor()` for screen-reader / keyboard accessibility. |
| 4 | Extends `java.awt.Component` directly. | Extends `javax.swing.JComponent` (inherits pluggable Look-and-Feel). |

*(1 mark per valid point + ½ mark for any additional correct point; capped at 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Explain the class hierarchy of `JLabel` in the Java Swing framework. Discuss the role of `JComponent` and `JLabel` with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete Java Swing program to create a `JFrame` containing **two `JLabel`s** — one displaying the text **"Username:"** with a mnemonic key `'U'` linked to a `JTextField`, and another displaying **"Login Status: OK"** along with an `ImageIcon` loaded from a file `"tick.png"`. Configure the icon-text gap to **10 pixels**, place the text to the **right** of the icon, and disable the second label using `setEnabled(false)`. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Hierarchy explanation (3 marks):**

The class `javax.swing.JLabel` inherits from the following chain:
- `java.lang.Object` (root of all Java classes)
- `java.awt.Component` (provides positioning, painting, events)
- `java.awt.Container` (allows children, although `JLabel` does not use this capability)
- `javax.swing.JComponent` (adds pluggable look-and-feel, borders, tooltips, double-buffering, and the `paintComponent` method)
- `javax.swing.JLabel` (the concrete, passive display class)

**Role of `JComponent` (2 marks):**
`JComponent` is the **base class for all Swing components except the top-level containers** (`JFrame`, `JDialog`, `JApplet`). It supplies:
- The `paintComponent(Graphics g)` method that subclasses override.
- Support for **pluggable Look-and-Feel (PLAF)** — the same label can look like a Windows label, a Mac label, or a Motif label without code changes.
- **ToolTip support** via `setToolTipText(String)`.
- **Border support** via `setBorder(Border)`.
- **Double buffering** for flicker-free repaints.

**Role of `JLabel` with example (2 marks):**
`JLabel` specializes `JComponent` by adding **read-only text/icon display logic** plus the **mnemonic API**. Example:

```java
JLabel lbl = new JLabel("Hello, KTU Student!");
lbl.setToolTipText("Greeting message");
lbl.setBorder(BorderFactory.createEtchedBorder());
```

This single line creates a Swing label that benefits from every feature of `JComponent` while displaying static text.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;

public class LoginFrame extends JFrame {

    public LoginFrame() {
        setTitle("Login Window - KTU Demo");
        setSize(400, 200);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout(FlowLayout.LEFT, 10, 20));

        // --- Label 1: text + mnemonic + LabelFor binding ---
        JLabel userLabel = new JLabel("Username:");
        JTextField userField = new JTextField(15);
        userLabel.setDisplayedMnemonic('U');     // underline U
        userLabel.setLabelFor(userField);        // focus target

        // --- Label 2: text + icon, then disabled ---
        ImageIcon tickIcon = new ImageIcon("tick.png");
        JLabel statusLabel = new JLabel("Login Status: OK", tickIcon, SwingConstants.LEFT);
        statusLabel.setHorizontalTextPosition(SwingConstants.RIGHT);
        statusLabel.setIconTextGap(10);
        statusLabel.setEnabled(false);

        // Add components to frame
        add(userLabel);
        add(userField);
        add(statusLabel);

        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SwingUtilities.invokeLater(() -> new LoginFrame());
    }
}
```

**Mark Distribution (per KTU 2024 valuation key):**

| Step | Marks |
|------|-------|
| Correct imports and class declaration | 1 |
| Frame setup (size, close operation, layout) | 1 |
| Label 1 creation with mnemonic + `LabelFor` | 2 |
| Label 2 creation with icon and `setHorizontalTextPosition(RIGHT)` | 1 |
| `setIconTextGap(10)` applied | 1 |
| `setEnabled(false)` on second label | 0.5 |
| `main()` method with `SwingUtilities.invokeLater` (EDT thread) | 0.5 |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Discuss the three primary constructors of `JLabel` with their method signatures. Explain the meaning of the `horizontalAlignment` parameter in the combined constructor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a Swing application that demonstrates a `JLabel` displaying both text and an icon. Configure the label so that the **text appears below the icon** and the **icon-text gap is 15 pixels**. Briefly explain what happens if you set the label's `setEnabled(false)`. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Constructor 1 — `JLabel()` (1.5 marks):**
Creates an **empty label** with no text and no icon. Alignment defaults to `SwingConstants.LEADING`. Used when the label content is determined at runtime (e.g., populated from a database).

**Constructor 2 — `JLabel(String text)` (1.5 marks):**
Creates a label initialized with the supplied text string. Icon is `null`. Signature: `public JLabel(String text)`.

**Constructor 3 — `JLabel(Icon image)` (1.5 marks):**
Creates an icon-only label. Text is `null`. Signature: `public JLabel(Icon image)`.

**Constructor 4 — Combined `JLabel(String text, Icon image, int horizontalAlignment)` (2.5 marks):**
The most powerful constructor. The third argument is a constant from the `SwingConstants` interface that specifies how the content (text+icon combination) is aligned **within the label's painted bounds**. The legal values are:
- `SwingConstants.LEFT` (2)
- `SwingConstants.CENTER` (0)
- `SwingConstants.RIGHT` (4)
- `SwingConstants.LEADING` (10) — *default; locale-aware*
- `SwingConstants.TRAILING` (11)

**Note:** The constructor argument only governs **horizontal** placement. The vertical alignment is set separately via `setVerticalAlignment(int)`.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;

public class IconBelowTextDemo extends JFrame {

    public IconBelowTextDemo() {
        setTitle("Icon Below Text - KTU Swing Demo");
        setSize(300, 250);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());

        ImageIcon logo = new ImageIcon("ktu_logo.png");
        JLabel caption = new JLabel("KTU 2024 Scheme", logo, SwingConstants.CENTER);

        // Text BELOW icon
        caption.setHorizontalTextPosition(SwingConstants.CENTER);
        caption.setVerticalTextPosition(SwingConstants.BOTTOM);

        // 15 pixel gap
        caption.setIconTextGap(15);

        // Disable demonstration
        caption.setEnabled(false);

        add(caption);
        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SwingUtilities.invokeLater(() -> new IconBelowTextDemo());
    }
}
```

**Explanation of `setEnabled(false)` (2 marks):**
When a `JLabel` is **disabled**:
- The label's **text and icon are rendered in a "grayed-out" color** (usually a faded gray) by the current Look-and-Feel.
- The label does **not receive focus** when the user tabs through components.
- Any **mnemonic shortcut** bound via `setLabelFor()` becomes **inactive** — pressing **Alt + U** will not transfer focus.
- The label's `isEnabled()` method returns `false`.

**Mark Distribution:**

| Step | Marks |
|------|-------|
| Imports and class declaration | 1 |
| Icon creation | 1 |
| `JLabel` with combined constructor | 1 |
| `setHorizontalTextPosition(CENTER)` and `setVerticalTextPosition(BOTTOM)` | 1.5 |
| `setIconTextGap(15)` | 1 |
| `setEnabled(false)` and its explanation | 1.5 |

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost students marks:**
> 1. **Writing `java.awt.Label` instead of `javax.swing.JLabel`** — instant 0.5 mark penalty on definition. KTU 2024 syllabus is explicitly **Swing-based**.
> 2. **Forgetting the `SwingUtilities.invokeLater()` wrapper** in the `main()` method — valuators often deduct ½ mark because running GUI code outside the **Event Dispatch Thread (EDT)** is unsafe.
> 3. **Confusing `setHorizontalAlignment` with `setHorizontalTextPosition`**:
>    - `setHorizontalAlignment` → places the **whole label content** within the label's bounds.
>    - `setHorizontalTextPosition` → places the **text relative to the icon** when both are present.
> 4. **Using a numeric literal (e.g., `2`) instead of the named constant** (`SwingConstants.LEFT`). KTU expects the symbolic constant for readability marks.
> 5. **Adding a `JLabel` to a `JFrame` without calling `setVisible(true)`** — the program compiles but the label is never painted, leading to "program not working" complaints during lab evaluations.
> 6. **Loading `ImageIcon("file.png")` from a relative path that fails** — the label silently shows empty space. Best practice: use `getClass().getResource("/file.png")`.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Fire Revision:**
> - 📦 **Package:** `javax.swing`
> - 🧬 **Superclass:** `javax.swing.JComponent`
> - 🎯 **Purpose:** **Passive** display of text, icon, or both.
> - 🚫 **No ActionEvents** — `JLabel` is **not** an interactive widget.
> - 🛠️ **4 Constructors:** no-arg, text-only, icon-only, text+icon+alignment.
> - 🧭 **Default Horizontal Alignment:** `SwingConstants.LEADING` (locale-aware).
> - ⌨️ **Mnemonic API:** `setDisplayedMnemonic(char)`, `setDisplayedMnemonicIndex(int)`, `setLabelFor(Component)`.
> - 📏 **Default Icon-Text Gap:** **4 pixels** (overridable via `setIconTextGap(int)`).
> - 🧩 **Alignment Constants** are in the **`SwingConstants`** interface (implemented by `JLabel`).
> - 🔘 **Disabled state** (via `setEnabled(false)`) grays out text and icon; disables mnemonics.
> - 🧵 **Always launch Swing GUIs** inside `SwingUtilities.invokeLater(Runnable)` to honor the **Event Dispatch Thread**.
> - 🖼️ **Icon loading:** `new ImageIcon(String filename)` — fails silently if file missing.
> - 🪟 **AWT vs Swing:** `java.awt.Label` is **heavyweight** (peer-based); `javax.swing.JLabel` is **lightweight** (100% Java painted, PLAF-enabled).
> - 🎓 **KTU exam hot topics:** constructor differences, mnemonic binding, `LabelFor`, alignment constants, `setEnabled(false)` behavior.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
