topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECIT633 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Sources of Events ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition An **Event Source** is any Graphical User Interface (GUI) component object that is capable of generating (or "firing") an **Event** in response to a user interaction or a system-triggered action. In the Java Abstract Window Toolkit (AWT) and Swing frameworks, an event source is an object of a class that **inherits** from either `java.awt.Component` (for visual components) or `java.awt.MenuComponent` (for menu-based components). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Event Delegation Model (EDM) consists of three pillars: **Event Source → Event Object → Event Listener**. The "Source" is the producer, the "Object" is the data carrier, and the "Listener" is the consumer. Without a valid source, no event can be generated. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **doorbell system** in a house: - The **Doorbell Button** = **Event Source** (the physical thing the user pushes). - The **Electric Pulse** sent through the wire = **Event Object** (carries information about *what* happened). - The **Bell (Chime)** that rings inside the house = **Event Listener** (the object that reacts). When you press the doorbell (interaction with the **source**), a pulse is sent (**event object**), and the bell rings (**listener** processes the event). Crucially, if no bell is connected (no listener registered), pressing the button still sends the pulse — but nothing useful happens. This separation of "who generates" from "who handles" is the essence of the **Delegation Model**. > [!NOTE] > **Definition – Event Source:** An event source is the originating GUI component that detects an interaction (such as a mouse click, keystroke, or window state change) and instantiates an appropriate subclass of `java.awt.AWTEvent` to notify all its registered listeners. ### 1.3 Classification of Event Sources in Java AWT Event sources in Java are classified into two superclass branches: | Superclass | Type | Examples | |------------|------|----------| | `java.awt.Component` | Visual/Widget-based | Button, TextField, Checkbox, Choice, List, Label, Scrollbar | | `java.awt.MenuComponent` | Menu-based | MenuItem, Menu, CheckboxMenuItem, MenuBar | > [!TIP] > **Examiner's Tip:** If asked "Is `String` an event source?", the answer is **NO**. Strings, `Thread`, `Object`, and `Integer` are *not* event sources because they do not extend `Component` or `MenuComponent`. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Anatomy of an Event Source Every event source object in Java AWT/Swing internally follows a three-step lifecycle when an interaction occurs: 1. **Detection Phase:** The JVM's underlying OS thread (e.g., AWT-EventQueue) detects a low-level native event (e.g., WM_LBUTTONDOWN on Windows) on the component. 2. **Event Instantiation:** The source's overridden `processEvent()` constructs a specific `AWTEvent` subclass (e.g., `ActionEvent`, `MouseEvent`). 3. **Dispatching Phase:** The source invokes the appropriate listener method on every object previously registered via `addXXXListener()`. ### 2.2 Detailed Mapping of Event Sources to Event Types (High-Yield Table) | Event Source (Class) | Event Fired | Triggering Action | Default Listener Interface | |----------------------|-------------|-------------------|----------------------------| | `Button` | `ActionEvent` | Click / Enter key press | `ActionListener` | | `TextField` | `ActionEvent` | Enter key pressed | `ActionListener` | | `Checkbox` | `ItemEvent` | Checked / Unchecked | `ItemListener` | | `Choice` (Dropdown) | `ItemEvent` | Item selected | `ItemListener` | | `List` | `ActionEvent` + `ItemEvent` | Double-click / Single-click | `ActionListener` + `ItemListener` | | `MenuItem` | `ActionEvent` | Menu item selected | `ActionListener` | | `Scrollbar` | `AdjustmentEvent` | Scroll / Drag | `AdjustmentListener` | | `Window` / `Frame` / `Dialog` | `WindowEvent` | Open / Close / Iconify | `WindowListener` | | `TextField` / `TextArea` | `TextEvent` | Content modified | `TextListener` | | Any `Component` | `MouseEvent` | Click / Press / Release / Enter / Exit | `MouseListener` | | Any `Component` | `KeyEvent` | Key typed / pressed / released | `KeyListener` | | Any `Component` | `FocusEvent` | Gained / Lost keyboard focus | `FocusListener` | | Any `Component` | `ComponentEvent` | Moved / Resized / Shown / Hidden | `ComponentListener` | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Key:** Notice that `MouseEvent`, `KeyEvent`, `FocusEvent`, and `ComponentEvent` can be fired by **ANY** `java.awt.Component`. So even a `Label` (which normally does nothing) is technically an event source if a mouse listener is attached to it. ### 2.3 The Registration Protocol (Why it Matters) A source does *nothing* meaningful with an event unless at least one listener is registered. The registration contract uses these paired methods: $$\text{source.addXXXListener(ListenerObject)} \longleftrightarrow \text{source.removeXXXListener(ListenerObject)}$$ The `XXX` is replaced by the event type name. For example: - `Button b = new Button("OK");` is paired with `b.addActionListener(handler);` - `Frame f = new Frame("Demo");` is paired with `f.addWindowListener(handler);` > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfall:** If a student writes `b.addActionListener(handler);` for a `Checkbox` (which fires `ItemEvent`), the code compiles but the program **silently does nothing** at runtime. Always match the listener type to the source's event category. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Event sources form the backbone of every interactive software: ATM machines (button + card-reader sources), mobile apps (touch-event sources), IoT dashboards (sensor-event sources), and web UIs (DOM-event sources). In Java-based enterprise GUIs (Swing/JavaFX), the same source-listener pattern scales to millions of concurrent users via the Event Dispatch Thread (EDT). ## 3. Step-by-Step Logical Derivation & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > Since this is a CS/OOP topic, "derivation" here refers to the **step-by-step logical trace** of the Event Delegation Model — how a source, event, and listener collaborate. Every transition is shown explicitly. ### 3.1 Worked Example: A Button Click Event — Full Logical Trace **Scenario:** A user clicks a `Button` labeled "Submit" inside a `Frame`. We trace the entire event flow. **Step 1 — Object Construction (Programmer Side)** $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{Frame } f = \text{new Frame("Login");} \\ &\text{Button } b = \text{new Button("Submit");} \\ &f.\text{add}(b); \quad \text{// Button is added to the container} \end{aligned} $$ At this point, the button `b` is an **event source** capable of firing `ActionEvent`, but it has no listener — so any click would be silently discarded by the JVM. **Step 2 — Listener Implementation (Programmer Side)** The programmer creates a class that implements `ActionListener` and overrides the `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)` method: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{class Handler implements ActionListener } \{ \\ \qquad \text{public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)} \{ \\ \qquad\qquad \text{System.out.println("Source: " + e.getSource());} \\ \qquad\qquad \text{System.out.println("Command: " + e.getActionCommand());} \\ \qquad \}\} \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Registration (Bridging Source to Listener)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Handler } h = \text{new Handler();} \\ b.\text{addActionListener}(h); \quad \text{// Source now knows its listener} \end{aligned} $$ Internally, Java maintains a `EventListener` list inside the `Button` object. After this call, the list contains a reference to `h`. **Step 4 — User Interaction (Runtime Side)** The user clicks the button. The OS sends a native event to the JVM's AWT-EventQueue thread. **Step 5 — Event Object Creation** The `Button` class's overridden `processEvent(AWTEvent e)` method detects that the event is a mouse release matching its bounds. It then constructs: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{ActionEvent } ae = \text{new ActionEvent(} \\ \qquad \text{this,} \quad \text{// source: the Button itself} \\ \qquad \text{ActionEvent.ACTION\_PERFORMED,} \\ \qquad \text{"Submit"} \quad \text{// action command = label} \\ \text{);} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Dispatching** The source iterates over its listener list and invokes `actionPerformed(ae)` on each. The `e.getSource()` returns the `Button` object; `e.getActionCommand()` returns `"Submit"`. **Step 7 — Cleanup (Optional)** If the button is destroyed or `removeActionListener(h)` is called, the source drops the reference. Garbage collection can then reclaim the listener if no other references exist. ### 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — "Which Source Fires Which Event?" | User Action | Probable Source | Event Generated | Listener to Register | |-------------|-----------------|------------------|------------------------| | Clicks a button | `Button` | `ActionEvent` | `ActionListener` | | Ticks a checkbox | `Checkbox` | `ItemEvent` | `ItemListener` | | Selects dropdown item | `Choice` | `ItemEvent` | `ItemListener` | | Scrolls a bar | `Scrollbar` | `AdjustmentEvent` | `AdjustmentListener` | | Types in a text box | `TextField` | `TextEvent` | `TextListener` | | Presses Enter in text box | `TextField` | `ActionEvent` | `ActionListener` | | Closes a window | `Window` | `WindowEvent` | `WindowListener` | | Hovers mouse on label | `Label` | `MouseEvent` | `MouseListener` | | Tabs between fields | Any `Component` | `FocusEvent` | `FocusListener` | > [!IMPORTANT] > **DERIVATION RULE (KTU Pattern):** A single event source can register **multiple listeners** of the *same* type, and they will all be invoked in the order they were added. However, a source cannot fire a *type* of event for which no matching listener interface exists in the AWT hierarchy. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Event Delegation Model — Source → Event → Listener Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR userA[User Interaction] --> sourceA[GUI Component
Event Source] sourceA --> detectA{Component detects
raw OS event} detectA --> createA[Construct AWTEvent
subclass object] createA --> dispatchA[Invoke processEvent
on source] dispatchA --> lookupA{Look up registered
listener list} lookupA --> invokeA[Call listener method
e.g., actionPerformed] invokeA --> resultA[Listener executes
business logic] resultA --> returnA[Control returns to
Event Dispatch Thread] regA[Programmer registers
addXXXListener] -.-> sourceA style userA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style sourceA fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style detectA fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style createA fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style dispatchA fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style lookupA fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style invokeA fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style resultA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style returnA fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style regA fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 ``` ### 4.2 Class Hierarchy of AWT Event Sources ```mermaid flowchart TD objA[java.lang.Object] --> compA[java.awt.Component] objA --> menuA[java.awt.MenuComponent] compA --> btnA[Button] compA --> lblA[Label] compA --> chkA[Checkbox] compA --> choA[Choice] compA --> lstA[List] compA --> sbA[Scrollbar] compA --> txtA[TextComponent] compA --> canA[Canvas] compA --> ctnA[Container] txtA --> tfA[TextField] txtA --> taA[TextArea] ctnA --> panA[Panel] ctnA --> winA[Window] panA --> aplA[Applet] winA --> dlgA[Dialog] winA --> frmA[Frame] menuA --> mbA[MenuBar] menuA --> miA[MenuItem] miA --> muA[Menu] miA --> cmiA[CheckboxMenuItem] style objA fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style compA fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style menuA fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style btnA fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style lblA fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style chkA fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style choA fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style lstA fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style sbA fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style txtA fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style canA fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style ctnA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style tfA fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style taA fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style panA fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style winA fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style aplA fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style dlgA fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style frmA fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style mbA fill:#20B2AA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style miA fill:#20B2AA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style muA fill:#3CB371,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style cmiA fill:#3CB371,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 ``` ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Typical Event Source ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph srcBlock[Event Source Object e.g. Button] stateA[Internal State
label, bounds, enabled] listenerA[Listener Registry
List of ActionListener] methodA[processEvent Method
overridden from Component] methodB[addActionListener
removeActionListener] end subgraph eventBlock[Event Object] evtTypeA[Event Type ID] evtSrcA[Source Reference] evtCmdA[Action Command String] evtTimeA[Timestamp] end subgraph listenerBlock[Registered Listener] handlerA[actionPerformed Method
business logic] end stateA -.-> methodA listenerA -.-> methodA methodB -.-> listenerA methodA --> eventBlock eventBlock --> handlerA style srcBlock fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style eventBlock fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style listenerBlock fill:#F0FFF0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style stateA fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style listenerA fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style methodA fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style methodB fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style evtTypeA fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style evtSrcA fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style evtCmdA fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style evtTimeA fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style handlerA fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define an event source. List any four AWT components that can act as event sources.** **Model Answer:** An *event source* is a GUI component object that generates (fires) an event in response to user interaction. In Java AWT, any class extending `java.awt.Component` or `java.awt.MenuComponent` can serve as an event source. Four examples are: **(1) Button** (fires `ActionEvent`), **(2) TextField** (fires `ActionEvent`/`TextEvent`), **(3) Checkbox** (fires `ItemEvent`), and **(4) Scrollbar** (fires `AdjustmentEvent`). *(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for listing four valid sources with their events)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] Distinguish between `ActionEvent` and `ItemEvent` in terms of their source components and triggering actions.** **Model Answer:** `ActionEvent` is fired by sources like `Button`, `TextField`, `MenuItem`, and `List` when the user **activates** the component (e.g., clicks a button, presses Enter in a text field). `ItemEvent` is fired by sources like `Checkbox`, `Choice`, and `List` when the user **selects or deselects** an item (state toggle). The corresponding listeners are `ActionListener` and `ItemListener` respectively. *(3 marks: 1.5 for `ActionEvent`, 1.5 for `ItemEvent`)* --- ### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **INTERNAL CHOICE:** Answer **ONE** of the following two alternatives. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]** **(a)** Explain the Event Delegation Model in Java AWT with a neat block diagram. Describe the roles of event source, event object, and event listener. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write a complete Java AWT program that creates a `Frame` containing a `TextField` and a `Button` labeled "Reverse". When the button is clicked, the text in the TextField must be reversed and displayed back in the same TextField. Use proper event-source-to-listener registration. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution for (a):** The **Event Delegation Model (EDM)** is Java's modern mechanism (since JDK 1.1) for handling GUI events. It separates the responsibility of event generation from event handling. - **Event Source:** The GUI component (e.g., `Button`) that detects a user action and creates an event object. - **Event Object:** A subclass of `java.awt.AWTEvent` (e.g., `ActionEvent`) that encapsulates details such as the source reference, event type, and timestamp. - **Event Listener:** An object of a class that implements a listener interface (e.g., `ActionListener`). The source maintains a list of registered listeners and invokes the appropriate callback method (`actionPerformed`). **Flow Diagram (in words):** User → interacts with → **Source** → generates → **Event Object** → passed to → **Listener** → executes → handler method → returns to **EDT**. **Advantages of EDM over the legacy `handleEvent()` approach:** (i) cleaner separation of concerns, (ii) multiple listeners can be registered on a single source, (iii) easier maintenance, (iv) scalable to complex GUIs. *(7 marks: 2 for roles, 3 for flow explanation, 2 for advantages)* **Model Solution for (b):** ```java import java.awt.*; import java.awt.event.*; public class ReverseTextDemo extends Frame implements ActionListener { TextField tf; public ReverseTextDemo() { setLayout(new FlowLayout()); setTitle("Reverse Text - Event Source Demo"); setSize(400, 100); tf = new TextField(20); Button btn = new Button("Reverse"); add(tf); add(btn); // Register the listener with the source btn.addActionListener(this); // The TextField is also an event source for TextEvent tf.addActionListener(this); addWindowListener(new WindowAdapter() { public void windowClosing(WindowEvent e) { dispose(); } }); setVisible(true); } public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) { // e.getSource() returns the component that fired the event if (e.getSource() instanceof TextField) { // When user presses Enter, move focus to button ((TextField) e.getSource()).transferFocus(); } else { // Reverse logic when button is clicked String original = tf.getText(); String reversed = new StringBuilder(original).reverse().toString(); tf.setText(reversed); } } public static void main(String[] args) { new ReverseTextDemo(); } } ``` **Explanation of sources in this program:** - `Frame f` → source for `WindowEvent`. - `TextField tf` → source for `ActionEvent` (Enter) and `TextEvent` (typing). - `Button btn` → source for `ActionEvent` (click). - The current class `this` (implementing `ActionListener`) is the **listener**, registered onto both `btn` and `tf` via `addActionListener()`. *(7 marks: 2 for source identification, 3 for correct code, 2 for listener registration logic)* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]** **(a)** List and explain **any five** common AWT event sources along with the event each fires and the listener interface to be implemented. Present your answer in a tabular form. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the significance of the `addXXXListener()` and `removeXXXListener()` methods in the event source mechanism. What happens if a programmer forgets to register a listener? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution for (a):** | Sl. | Event Source | Event Fired | Trigger | Listener Interface | |-----|--------------|-------------|---------|---------------------| | 1 | `Button` | `ActionEvent` | Mouse click / Enter key | `ActionListener` | | 2 | `TextField` | `ActionEvent` | Enter key pressed | `ActionListener` | | 3 | `Checkbox` | `ItemEvent` | Checked or unchecked | `ItemListener` | | 4 | `Scrollbar` | `AdjustmentEvent` | Scroll / drag | `AdjustmentListener` | | 5 | `Choice` | `ItemEvent` | Dropdown item selected | `ItemListener` | | 6 (Bonus) | `Window` | `WindowEvent` | Open / close / iconify | `WindowListener` | **Brief explanation:** 1. A `Button` is the most common action source — a click generates an `ActionEvent` delivered to `actionPerformed()`. 2. A `TextField` fires `ActionEvent` specifically when the user presses the Enter key (not on every keystroke; for that, `TextEvent` is used). 3. A `Checkbox` toggling state produces an `ItemEvent`, delivered to `itemStateChanged()`. 4. A `Scrollbar` triggers an `AdjustmentEvent` whenever its thumb moves; handled via `adjustmentValueChanged()`. 5. A `Choice` (dropdown) generates an `ItemEvent` when the user selects a new entry; delivered to `itemStateChanged()`. *(7 marks: 5 × 1 mark for each row, 2 marks for the explanation paragraph)* **Model Solution for (b):** **Significance of `addXXXListener()`:** The method `addXXXListener()` (where `XXX` is the event category, e.g., `addActionListener`, `addWindowListener`) is the **registration hook** that binds a listener object to an event source. Internally, the source maintains an `EventListener` vector; on user interaction, the source iterates this list and calls the appropriate callback method on each listener. **Significance of `removeXXXListener()`:** This is the symmetric deregistration method. It removes the listener object from the source's internal list, preventing further callbacks. It is essential for **memory management** — if a listener is no longer needed (e.g., a dialog is closed), failing to deregister can cause memory leaks because the source holds a strong reference to the listener. **What if a programmer forgets to register a listener?** The event source will still detect the user interaction and create the `AWTEvent` object internally, but since its listener list is **empty**, the `dispatchEvent()` call will find no targets. The event is silently discarded. The program **does not crash** and **does not throw an exception** — it simply produces no observable reaction. This is one of the most common silent-failure scenarios in beginner Java GUI programming. *(7 marks: 2 for add, 2 for remove, 3 for the silent-failure explanation with the term "silently discarded")* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing source with listener:** Writing "the Button listens to the click" is *wrong* — the Button is the *source*, and the listener *hears* the click. > 2. **Omitting the listener interface name:** A 14-mark answer that lists sources without naming the matching `XXXListener` interface will lose 2–3 marks. > 3. **Forgetting `import java.awt.event.*;`:** Code that references `ActionEvent`, `ActionListener`, etc., without the import statement is marked down. > 4. **Using Swing (`javax.swing.JButton`) when AWT is asked:** KTU's PECIT633 module specifically tests `java.awt` — mixing Swing components in an AWT context shows a conceptual gap. > 5. **Claiming events are fired even without listeners "with no effect":** While technically true, the more precise answer is that the event is **silently discarded by the Event Dispatch Thread** — examiners reward the precise terminology. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Event Source** = GUI component object that generates events; must extend `java.awt.Component` or `java.awt.MenuComponent`. - **Three pillars of EDM:** Source → Event Object → Listener. Memorize this order; KTU questions often test it directly. - **Common AWT sources:** `Button`, `TextField`, `Checkbox`, `Choice`, `List`, `Label`, `Scrollbar`, `MenuItem`, `Window`. - **Event-to-Source matching (must memorize):** - `ActionEvent` ← `Button`, `TextField`, `MenuItem`, `List` - `ItemEvent` ← `Checkbox`, `Choice`, `List` - `TextEvent` ← `TextField`, `TextArea` - `AdjustmentEvent` ← `Scrollbar` - `WindowEvent` ← `Window`, `Frame`, `Dialog` - `MouseEvent` / `KeyEvent` / `FocusEvent` / `ComponentEvent` ← any `Component` - **Registration methods** follow the pattern `addXXXListener()` / `removeXXXListener()`. - **Silent failure:** Unregistered listeners cause the program to compile and run, but the GUI does nothing on interaction — no exception is thrown. - **Multiple listeners allowed:** A single source can register many listeners of the same type; they fire in registration order. - **Event Object class hierarchy root:** `java.awt.AWTEvent` → further subclassed by `ActionEvent`, `MouseEvent`, etc. - **Legacy alternative:** Before JDK 1.1, Java used the inheritance-based `handleEvent()` method in the `Component` class. The modern EDM is preferred because it uses composition (listener objects) over inheritance. - **Examiner buzzwords to use in answers:** "Event Dispatch Thread", "callback method", "listener registry", "silent discarding", "delegation", "callback".

topics.title: Event Listener Interfaces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT633 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Event Listener Interfaces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the Java AWT and Swing event-handling framework, an **Event Listener Interface** is a `java.util.EventListener`-tagged interface that declares one or more callback methods. These methods are invoked by the JVM (via the **Delegation Event Model**) when a specific type of event occurs on a GUI component (the *event source*). Listener interfaces are the cornerstone of the *Observer design pattern* as applied to graphical user interfaces.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (verbatim-grade):**
> *An Event Listener Interface is a Java interface (extending `java.util.EventListener`) that defines the methods an object must implement in order to receive and process specific types of events generated by AWT/Swing components.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Doorbell" Model

Imagine a house (the **GUI Component**). Installed on the door is a **Doorbell Button** (the **Event Source**). When a visitor presses the button, an electrical pulse travels through the wiring (the **Event Object**) and reaches a **Bell inside the house** (the **Event Listener**), which then rings. The visitor (the user) does not need to know who is inside; they just press the button.

| Real-World Element | Java AWT/Swing Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Doorbell Button | `JButton`, `JTextField`, `JMenuItem` (Event Source) |
| Electrical Pulse | `ActionEvent`, `MouseEvent`, `KeyEvent` (Event Object) |
| Bell Mechanism | `ActionListener`, `MouseListener`, `KeyListener` (Listener) |
| Wiring | Event Dispatch Thread (EDT) |
| Person inside the house | Object implementing the Listener (Callback) |

> [!NOTE]
> The wiring analogy is important: nothing happens *automatically*. The listener object must be **registered** with the source using `source.addXxxListener(listenerObj)`. Until that registration, pressing the doorbell is "silently ignored" by the house.

## 1.3 Why Event Listeners Exist — The Problem They Solve

Early Java GUI prototypes used a **inheritance-based** model (the `java.awt.Component` `handleEvent()` method) where every component subclass had to override an enormous switch statement. This produced the "fat class" anti-pattern, coupled 100 % of event logic to the component itself, and made code un-reusable.

The Listener Interface pattern **decouples** the *view* (button) from the *behaviour* (what happens on click). One button can be wired to any number of independent listener objects, and the same listener can be reused across many components.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line Board Answer:** Event Listener Interfaces are the *contract* that converts GUI user actions into Java method calls, enabling loosely coupled, reusable event-driven programs.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Architectural Pillars of the Delegation Event Model

The Delegation Event Model (DEM), introduced in JDK 1.1 and still used in 2024, is built on three interacting actors. Master these and you have mastered 70 % of the module.

### Pillar 1 — Event Source (The Producer)
- A GUI object that **fires** events.
- Examples: `JButton`, `JTextField`, `JFrame`, `JMenu`, `JList`.
- Maintains an **internal listener list** (a `EventListener` array).
- Exposes overloaded `addXxxListener()` and `removeXxxListener()` mutators.

### Pillar 2 — Event Object (The Data Packet)
- A subclass of `java.util.EventObject` (root) or `java.awt.AWTEvent`.
- Encapsulates information about *what happened*:
  - Source reference (`getSource()`).
  - Coordinates (e.g., `getX()`, `getY()` for mouse events).
  - Modifiers (e.g., `isShiftDown()`).
  - Character / key code (for key events).

### Pillar 3 — Event Listener Interface (The Contract)
- A Java interface that **extends** `java.util.EventListener` (marker interface, no methods).
- Declares one or more abstract methods that the implementing class *must* override.
- Each listener type corresponds to a *category* of user interaction.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Event Delivery

1. User performs a physical action (click, key press, window resize).
2. The AWT/Swing toolkit on the OS level converts this into a low-level event.
3. The event is **queued** onto the **Event Dispatch Thread (EDT)**.
4. The source object's `processXxxEvent()` walks its internal listener list.
5. For every registered listener, the matching `listener.method(eventObj)` is invoked.
6. Control returns to the EDT, which paints the next frame.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Listener Interface (lives in `java.awt.event` or `javax.swing.event`) | Abstract Methods Declared | Triggering Event Object |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `ActionListener` | `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)` | Button click, menu select, Enter in text field |
| 2 | `MouseListener` | `mouseClicked`, `mousePressed`, `mouseReleased`, `mouseEntered`, `mouseExited` | All mouse-button & hover transitions |
| 3 | `MouseMotionListener` | `mouseDragged`, `mouseMoved` | Mouse movement while / not while pressed |
| 4 | `KeyListener` | `keyTyped`, `keyPressed`, `keyReleased` | Keyboard activity |
| 5 | `WindowListener` | 7 methods: `windowOpened`, `windowClosing`, `windowClosed`, `windowIconified`, `windowDeiconified`, `windowActivated`, `windowDeactivated` | Frame/dialog lifecycle |
| 6 | `ItemListener` | `itemStateChanged(ItemEvent e)` | Checkbox, radio button, list, combo-box |
| 7 | `TextListener` | `textValueChanged(TextEvent e)` | Text change in `TextField`/`TextArea` |
| 8 | `FocusListener` | `focusGained`, `focusLost` | Focus traversal |
| 9 | `AdjustmentListener` | `adjustmentValueChanged` | Scrollbar movement |
| 10 | `ComponentListener` | `componentResized`, `componentMoved`, `componentShown`, `componentHidden` | Component geometry/visibility |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Heuristic:** If the question mentions a "single-click" scenario, the answer is **`ActionListener` with one method**. If it mentions "right-click" or "mouse entered/exited", it is **`MouseListener` with five methods**. Memorise this mapping — it appears in 80 % of board questions.

## 2.4 Adapter Classes — The Convenience Wrappers

Implementing a listener with N methods forces you to override *all* of them, even if you only need one. Java provides **Adapter Classes** — skeletal no-op implementations of multi-method listener interfaces.

| Listener | Adapter | Methods Overridden to No-Op |
|---|---|---|
| `MouseListener` | `MouseAdapter` | 5 |
| `MouseMotionListener` | `MouseMotionAdapter` | 2 |
| `KeyListener` | `KeyAdapter` | 3 |
| `WindowListener` | `WindowAdapter` | 7 |
| `ComponentListener` | `ComponentAdapter` | 4 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why no `ActionAdapter`?** Because `ActionListener` has only **one** method — there is nothing to "default". Adapter classes are provided *only* when the listener has more than one abstract method.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Event Listener Interfaces are not academic curiosities. They are the substrate of every modern Java desktop application (IntelliJ IDEA, NetBeans, Eclipse RCP), every Android UI (which uses a near-identical pattern with `View.OnClickListener`), and the foundation of *reactive* frameworks like RxJava and JavaFX's `EventHandler<T>`. Mastering this module unlocks your ability to build any interactive software — from ATM screens to cockpit GUIs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walk-Through — Event Lifecycle & Code Derivation

## 3.1 Exhaustive Trace of an Event: From User Click to Method Call

This derivation walks the complete lifecycle of a single `ActionEvent` triggered by a `JButton` click, mapping every internal Java step.

### Step 1 — Component Creation & Listener Registration

The application code (in the constructor of the GUI class) creates a button and a listener object, then calls the registration method.

```java
JButton btn = new JButton("Submit");
btn.addActionListener(this);  // "this" implements ActionListener
```

Logical state after this line:

- Inside the `JButton` object, the `listenerList` field (of type `javax.swing.event.EventListenerList`) now contains one entry: a reference to the enclosing GUI object.
- The button's `actionMask` is unchanged — it is the *JVM* that decides when to call `fireActionPerformed()`.

### Step 2 — User Clicks the Button (OS-Level)

The user moves the mouse, hovers over the button, and presses/releases. The OS dispatches a `WM_LBUTTONDOWN` (Windows) or `ButtonPress` (X11) event to the AWT robot peer.

### Step 3 — AWT Peer Translates to `MouseEvent`

The peer (`WButtonPeer`) creates a `java.awt.event.MouseEvent` object and forwards it up the component chain. The AWT event dispatcher catches the event and determines it is a *button-click gesture*.

### Step 4 — Conversion to `ActionEvent`

The button's `processMouseEvent()` recognises the press+release pair as a "click", and internally calls `fireActionPerformed(new ActionEvent(this, ActionEvent.ACTION_PERFORMED, "Submit"))`.

### Step 5 — Firing the Event

The `fireActionPerformed()` method (inherited from `AbstractButton`) does the following:

$$
\text{listenerList.getListenerList()} \rightarrow \text{array of [ListenerClass, ListenerInstance] pairs}
$$

For each pair, the JVM performs:

$$
\text{listener.actionPerformed}(\text{eventObj})
$$

### Step 6 — Callback Executes

Control jumps into the user's overridden `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)` method. The method reads the event label (`e.getActionCommand()` returns `"Submit"`), updates the model, and returns.

### Step 7 — EDT Paints Next Frame

After the callback completes, the EDT calls `repaint()` on affected components. The screen is re-rendered, completing one event-handling cycle.

## 3.2 Algebraic Model of Event Distribution

Let:

$$
S = \{s_1, s_2, \dots, s_m\} \quad \text{(set of event sources)}
$$

$$
L = \{l_1, l_2, \dots, l_n\} \quad \text{(set of listener objects)}
$$

$$
R \subseteq S \times L \quad \text{(registration relation: source } s \text{ listens via } l \text{)}
$$

When event $e$ of type $T$ is fired on source $s_i$:

$$
\text{Dispatch}(e) = \forall\, (s_i, l_j) \in R, \; \text{invoke } l_j.\text{handler}_T(e)
$$

A component can register **multiple** listeners, and **one** listener can be registered to **multiple** sources. This is the cardinality property that gives the pattern its flexibility.

## 3.3 Worked Derivation — Building a Two-Button Counter GUI

**Problem:** Build a frame with two buttons ("+" and "−") and a label. Each click on "+" increments the label, each click on "−" decrements it.

### Step 1 — Design the listener

Since both buttons perform the same kind of event (a click), one `ActionListener` is enough. The listener must distinguish which button was pressed by inspecting `e.getSource()`.

```java
class CounterListener implements ActionListener {
    int count = 0;
    JLabel label;
    CounterListener(JLabel l) { this.label = l; }
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        if (e.getActionCommand().equals("+")) count++;
        else count--;
        label.setText("Count: " + count);
    }
}
```

### Step 2 — Wire the listener to both buttons

```java
CounterListener cl = new CounterListener(lbl);
btnPlus.addActionListener(cl);
btnMinus.addActionListener(cl);
```

### Step 3 — Logical Analysis of the Registration Set

After Step 2, the relation $R$ is:

$$
R = \{(\text{btnPlus}, cl),\; (\text{btnMinus}, cl)\}
$$

When the user clicks `btnPlus`:

$$
\text{Dispatch}(e) \rightarrow cl.\text{actionPerformed}(e) \rightarrow \text{count} = \text{count} + 1
$$

When the user clicks `btnMinus`:

$$
\text{Dispatch}(e) \rightarrow cl.\text{actionPerformed}(e) \rightarrow \text{count} = \text{count} - 1
$$

### Step 4 — Verify the State

After 3 "+" clicks and 1 "−" click, count = 2. The label shows `"Count: 2"`. The `EventListenerList` of `btnPlus` and `btnMinus` both contain the same `cl` reference — proving the **many-to-one** mapping property.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Three-Pillar Delegation Event Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    user[USER Action Click or Key] --> src[EVENT SOURCE JButton JTextField JFrame]
    src -->|fires| evt[EVENT OBJECT ActionEvent MouseEvent KeyEvent]
    evt -->|dispatched by EDT| lst[EVENT LISTENER INTERFACE ActionListener MouseListener]
    lst -->|callback| impl[IMPLEMENTING CLASS User Code]
    impl -->|update| model[MODEL or UI State]
    model -->|repaint| screen[SCREEN Refresh]
    classDef pillar fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class src,evt,lst pillar
```

## 4.2 Listener Interface Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    utilEL[java.util.EventListener Marker] --> awtEL[java.awt.event package listeners]
    utilEL --> swingEL[javax.swing.event package listeners]
    awtEL --> actL[ActionListener 1 method]
    awtEL --> mouL[MouseListener 5 methods]
    awtEL --> keyL[KeyListener 3 methods]
    awtEL --> winL[WindowListener 7 methods]
    awtEL --> itmL[ItemListener 1 method]
    awtEL --> focL[FocusListener 2 methods]
    swingEL --> chgL[ChangeListener]
    swingEL --> docL[DocumentListener]
    classDef iface fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064,color:#000
    class utilEL,awtEL,swingEL iface
    classDef leaf fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#827717,color:#000
    class actL,mouL,keyL,winL,itmL,focL,chgL,docL leaf
```

## 4.3 Adapter Class Bridge Pattern

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    lst[MouseListener 5 abstract methods] --> adp[MouseAdapter no-op defaults]
    adp --> dev[Developer extends MouseAdapter]
    dev -->|override only mouseClicked| usr[Clean Code only 1 method written]
    classDef adpClass fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
    class adp adpClass
    classDef usrClass fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    class dev,usr usrClass
```

## 4.4 Internal Architecture of `EventListenerList`

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    btn[JButton source] --> ell[EventListenerList internal]
    ell --> pair1[Pair 0: ActionListener.class]
    ell --> obj1[Pair 1: CounterListener instance]
    ell --> pair2[Pair 2: ActionListener.class]
    ell --> obj2[Pair 3: AuditLogger instance]
    ell -.->|getListenerList returns array| fire[fireActionPerformed loop]
    fire --> call1[counterListener.actionPerformed e]
    fire --> call2[auditLogger.actionPerformed e]
    classDef internal fill:#D1C4E9,stroke:#311B92,color:#000
    class ell,fire internal
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip:** The `EventListenerList` stores listeners in **class-object pairs** (two slots per listener). This is why `getListenerList()` returns `Object[]` rather than `Listener[]` — the array contains both `Class` tokens and instance references so the dispatcher can type-check via reflection.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]`
**Define an event listener interface. Explain its role in the Delegation Event Model with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (Board Standard):**
An **event listener interface** is a Java interface that extends `java.util.EventListener` and declares one or more abstract methods which are invoked by the JVM when a corresponding event occurs on a GUI component.
**Role:** It implements the *Observer* design pattern — the listener is the *observer*, the GUI component is the *subject*. For example, a class implementing `ActionListener` must override `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)`. When the user clicks a `JButton` registered with this listener via `addActionListener()`, the JVM automatically calls `actionPerformed()` on the EDT. This **decouples** event-generation (in the component) from event-handling (in the listener class), making GUI code modular and reusable. **(3 marks)**

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between `MouseListener` and `MouseMotionListener` with example events handled by each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | `MouseListener` | `MouseMotionListener` |
|---|---|---|
| Package | `java.awt.event` | `java.awt.event` |
| Methods | 5 (`mouseClicked`, `mousePressed`, `mouseReleased`, `mouseEntered`, `mouseExited`) | 2 (`mouseDragged`, `mouseMoved`) |
| Adapter | `MouseAdapter` | `MouseMotionAdapter` |
| Trigger | Click / hover / press / release | Movement with or without button held |
| Frequency | Low (per click) | High (per pixel of movement) |

**Example:** `MouseListener` handles the user pressing a button to start a drag. `MouseMotionListener` handles the continuous movement of the mouse while the button is held. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE Part B for Module 4 is internally chosen. **Attempt ANY ONE** of the two sub-questions below. Each has sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each.

---

### ✦ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024]`

**Q.A (a) [7 Marks]** Explain the Delegation Event Model in Java AWT. List and briefly describe any **five** commonly used event listener interfaces along with the events they handle.

**Model Solution:**

The **Delegation Event Model (DEM)** is Java's standard mechanism (since JDK 1.1) for handling GUI events. It is based on three actors: the **Event Source** (the component that fires events), the **Event Object** (encapsulates event data), and the **Event Listener** (an object that receives and processes the event). The source delegates the responsibility of handling to a registered listener — hence the name *Delegation*.

Key characteristics:
1. Event sources maintain a **listener list** internally.
2. Listeners are registered via `addXxxListener()` and removed via `removeXxxListener()`.
3. Each event is delivered on the **Event Dispatch Thread (EDT)**.
4. A single source can have **multiple** listeners; a single listener can serve **multiple** sources.

**Five Listener Interfaces:**

| # | Interface | Method(s) | Event Handled |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `ActionListener` | `actionPerformed(ActionEvent)` | Button click, menu selection |
| 2 | `MouseListener` | 5 methods | Mouse click, press, release, enter, exit |
| 3 | `KeyListener` | 3 methods | Key press, release, type |
| 4 | `WindowListener` | 7 methods | Window open, close, iconify, activate |
| 5 | `ItemListener` | `itemStateChanged(ItemEvent)` | Checkbox toggle, list selection |

**(7 marks — 2 for DEM explanation, 5 for the table)**

---

**Q.A (b) [7 Marks]** Write a complete Java program using AWT that creates a frame with a text field and a button. When the button is clicked, the text typed in the text field should be displayed in a label below. Use the `ActionListener` interface.

**Model Solution:**

```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class TextDisplayDemo extends Frame implements ActionListener {
    TextField tf;
    Label result;
    Button showBtn;

    public TextDisplayDemo() {
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        setTitle("Text Display Demo");
        setSize(400, 150);

        tf = new TextField(20);
        showBtn = new Button("Show");
        result = new Label("Result will appear here");

        add(tf);
        add(showBtn);
        add(result);

        showBtn.addActionListener(this);   // registration

        addWindowListener(new WindowAdapter() {
            public void windowClosing(WindowEvent e) {
                System.exit(0);
            }
        });
    }

    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        String text = tf.getText();
        result.setText("You typed: " + text);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        TextDisplayDemo d = new TextDisplayDemo();
        d.setVisible(true);
    }
}
```

**Explanation of Code Flow (for board marks):**
1. Class `TextDisplayDemo` **implements** `ActionListener` — so `this` is the listener. **(1 mark)**
2. In the constructor, components are created and added to the frame using `FlowLayout`. **(1 mark)**
3. `showBtn.addActionListener(this)` **registers** the listener with the source. This is the *heart* of the Delegation Event Model. **(2 marks)**
4. The overridden `actionPerformed()` reads the text from `tf` and updates the `result` label. **(1 mark)**
5. `WindowAdapter` is used to close the window cleanly (no need to override all 7 `WindowListener` methods). **(1 mark)**
6. `main()` creates the object and calls `setVisible(true)` to display. **(1 mark)**

**(Total: 7 marks)**

---

### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**Q.B (a) [7 Marks]** What are adapter classes in Java event handling? Explain with a suitable example. Why is there no `ActionAdapter`?

**Model Solution:**

An **Adapter Class** is a class in the `java.awt.event` package that provides **default no-op implementations** for all the methods of a multi-method listener interface. By **extending** an adapter instead of **implementing** the listener, the developer needs to override only the methods of interest, leaving the rest as empty bodies.

**Why they exist:** Many listener interfaces (e.g., `MouseListener`) have 5 methods. If a developer only cares about `mouseClicked`, they would otherwise be forced to write four empty methods. Adapters eliminate this boilerplate.

**Example using `MouseAdapter`:**

```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class ClickLogger extends MouseAdapter {
    Frame f;
    ClickLogger() {
        f = new Frame("Click Logger");
        f.setSize(300, 200);
        f.addMouseListener(this);  // this extends MouseAdapter
        f.setVisible(true);
    }
    public void mouseClicked(MouseEvent e) {
        System.out.println("Clicked at (" + e.getX() + ", " + e.getY() + ")");
    }
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new ClickLogger();
    }
}
```

Here, `MouseAdapter` provides empty bodies for `mousePressed`, `mouseReleased`, `mouseEntered`, and `mouseExited`. We override only `mouseClicked`.

**Why no `ActionAdapter`:** `ActionListener` declares only **one** method (`actionPerformed`). Since the developer *must* override the only method anyway, an adapter would provide no convenience. Adapters are provided by the JDK **only when a listener has more than one abstract method**. **(7 marks — 2 for theory, 3 for code, 2 for the ActionAdapter reasoning)**

---

**Q.B (b) [7 Marks]** Write a Java AWT program that uses `KeyListener` (via `KeyAdapter`) to display every key typed by the user in a text area, along with its key code and character value.

**Model Solution:**

```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class KeyLogger extends KeyAdapter {
    Frame f;
    TextArea ta;

    KeyLogger() {
        f = new Frame("Key Logger");
        f.setLayout(new BorderLayout());
        f.setSize(400, 300);

        ta = new TextArea();
        ta.setEditable(false);
        f.add(ta, BorderLayout.CENTER);

        f.addKeyListener(this);   // register KeyAdapter instance
        f.setFocusable(true);

        f.addWindowListener(new WindowAdapter() {
            public void windowClosing(WindowEvent e) { System.exit(0); }
        });

        f.setVisible(true);
    }

    public void keyTyped(KeyEvent e) {
        ta.append("Typed: '" + e.getKeyChar() +
                  "'  Code: " + e.getKeyCode() + "\n");
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new KeyLogger();
    }
}
```

**Mark Distribution:**
1. Correct class declaration extending `KeyAdapter`. **(1 mark)**
2. Proper `Frame` + `TextArea` setup with `BorderLayout`. **(1 mark)**
3. `addKeyListener(this)` registration. **(1 mark)**
4. Overriding `keyTyped` to display the character and key code. **(2 marks)**
5. `WindowAdapter` for clean close. **(1 mark)**
6. `main()` method. **(1 mark)**

**(Total: 7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks — read this twice.**
>
> 1. **Confusing listener interfaces with event classes.** `ActionListener` is a *listener* (interface you implement). `ActionEvent` is the *event* (object passed to your method). Mixing them up is an automatic 1-mark deduction.
> 2. **Forgetting to register the listener.** Writing `implements ActionListener` and the `actionPerformed` method is only *half* the answer. The line `btn.addActionListener(this);` is worth **2 marks on its own** in KTU papers. Do not omit it.
> 3. **Overriding all methods when extending an adapter** — and *not* overriding the one you need. Use adapters only when you want to handle a subset of events.
> 4. **Using `WindowListener` directly with empty methods** instead of `WindowAdapter`. The board examiner will deduct 1 mark for the verbosity.
> 5. **Performing heavy computation inside `actionPerformed`** — this blocks the EDT and freezes the GUI. KTU questions may include a "what happens if…" part; the answer is "the GUI becomes unresponsive" — mention this to grab the extra mark.
> 6. **Case-sensitive method names.** `actionPerformed` is not `ActionPerformed`. Java is case-sensitive; one typo = zero output = zero marks for the program.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Event Listener Interfaces extend `java.util.EventListener` (a marker interface).
- ✅ The **Delegation Event Model** has three actors: Source, Event Object, Listener.
- ✅ Registration is mandatory: `source.addXxxListener(listenerObj)`.
- ✅ The Event Dispatch Thread (EDT) is the single thread that delivers all GUI events.
- ✅ `ActionListener` → 1 method. `MouseListener` → 5 methods. `KeyListener` → 3 methods. `WindowListener` → 7 methods.
- ✅ Adapter classes exist for listeners with **more than one** method — they provide no-op defaults.
- ✅ No `ActionAdapter` because `ActionListener` has only one method.
- ✅ One source can have **many** listeners; one listener can serve **many** sources.
- ✅ `e.getSource()` distinguishes which component fired the event inside a shared listener.
- ✅ `WindowAdapter` is the most-used adapter in KTU practical exams — memorise its usage.
- ✅ Lambda expressions can replace listener classes for **single-method** listeners (e.g., `ActionListener`) since Java 8 — but the KTU 2024 syllabus still emphasises the class-based approach.
- ✅ Always close AWT frames via `WindowAdapter.windowClosing` → `System.exit(0)`.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Using the Delegation Event Model, Developing Database Applications using JDBC - JDBC Overview
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT633 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Using the Delegation Event Model, Developing Database Applications using JDBC - JDBC Overview

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Delegation Event Model (DEM)

### Formal Academic Definition

The **Delegation Event Model (DEM)** is the event-handling architecture adopted by Java AWT and Swing in which an **Event Source** registers a separate **Event Listener** object, and upon the occurrence of an event, the source *delegates* (hands over) the responsibility of processing the event to that registered listener. The model is built upon three cooperating entities: the **Event Source**, the **Event Object**, and the **Event Listener**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4)**
> The Delegation Event Model is the *only* event-handling model in modern Java. The earlier Java 1.0 inheritance-based model is **obsolete** and is **not** part of the KTU 2024 syllabus.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Newspaper Subscription"

Imagine you are a **House Owner** (Event Source). Every morning, a fire alarm may ring, the doorbell may buzz, or a window may break — these are **Events**. Now, instead of handling them yourself, you **subscribe** (register) a **Security Guard** (Event Listener) to receive alerts. When the alarm rings, *you don't handle it* — you **delegate** the job to the security guard, who decides what to do (call the police, sound siren, etc.).

**Mapping to Java terms:**

| Real-World Entity | Java Equivalent |
|---|---|
| House Owner | Component (e.g., JButton) |
| Fire / Doorbell | Event (ActionEvent, MouseEvent) |
| Subscription Form | `addXxxListener()` method |
| Security Guard | Listener Object (implements interface) |
| Guard's Action Plan | Method overridden in the listener (e.g., `actionPerformed()`) |

---

## 1.2 Java Database Connectivity (JDBC) — Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

**JDBC (Java Database Connectivity)** is a **Java API** (defined in the `java.sql` and `javax.sql` packages) that enables Java applications to interact with relational databases in a **database-independent**, vendor-neutral manner. It provides a standardized set of classes and interfaces that abstract the underlying database protocol, allowing the same Java code to query MySQL, Oracle, PostgreSQL, or any other **RDBMS** that supplies a compliant JDBC driver.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition to Memorize**
> *"JDBC is a specification (API) — not an implementation. The actual database communication is performed by a vendor-supplied JDBC Driver."*

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Universal Travel Adapter"

Think of JDBC as a **Universal Travel Adapter** that fits any socket worldwide. Your laptop (Java Program) has a single power plug. When you travel to Germany (MySQL) or the UK (Oracle), you don't buy a new laptop — you simply **swap the adapter tip** (JDBC Driver). The laptop's charging logic remains *unchanged*, just as your Java code remains the same regardless of the database.

**Why JDBC matters in industry:** It enforces the **Open-Closed Principle** — code is *open* to extension (new drivers) but *closed* for modification (same Java code).

---

## 1.3 Key Metrics & Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Standard Metric / Property |
|---|---|
| **Event Package** | `java.awt.event` and `javax.swing.event` |
| **JDBC Core Package** | `java.sql` |
| **JDBC Extension Package** | `javax.sql` |
| **Default Listener Interface Count** | **11+** standard listener interfaces in `java.awt.event` |
| **Standard JDBC URL Format** | `jdbc:<subprotocol>:<subname>` |
| **Mandatory JDBC Step Count** | **6** canonical steps (Load → Connect → Create → Execute → Process → Close) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of the Delegation Event Model

### Pillar 1 — Event Source
- The **object that generates** an event (e.g., `JButton`, `JTextField`, `JFrame`).
- Maintains an **internal list** of registered listeners (using `EventListener` polymorphic vector).
- Provides a pair of methods for every event category:
  * `addXxxListener(Listener l)` — to *register* a listener.
  * `removeXxxListener(Listener l)` — to *deregister* a listener.
- Example: A `JButton` has the method pair `addActionListener(...)` and `removeActionListener(...)`.

### Pillar 2 — Event Object (Event Class)
- An **object that encapsulates** all information about the event.
- Always a **subclass of `java.util.EventObject`**.
- Examples: `ActionEvent`, `MouseEvent`, `KeyEvent`, `WindowEvent`, `ItemEvent`, `TextEvent`.
- The source can be retrieved via the inherited method `getSource()`.
- Coordinates (for mouse events) are obtained via `getX()` and `getY()`.

### Pillar 3 — Event Listener
- An **object that waits** for events and reacts to them.
- Must implement a **listener interface** (e.g., `ActionListener`, `MouseListener`).
- All listener interfaces extend the marker interface `java.util.EventListener`.
- The **callback method** inside the listener interface is invoked automatically by the JVM when the event occurs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule of DEM**
> A source may have **multiple** listeners. A listener may be **shared** across multiple sources. This **1-to-many, many-to-1** fan-out is the core strength of the delegation model over inheritance.

---

## 2.2 Classification of Listeners (High-Yield Table)

| Listener Interface | Source Components | Key Callback Method |
|---|---|---|
| `ActionListener` | `JButton`, `JMenuItem`, `JTextField` | `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)` |
| `MouseListener` | Any component | `mouseClicked`, `mousePressed`, `mouseReleased`, `mouseEntered`, `mouseExited` |
| `MouseMotionListener` | Any component | `mouseDragged`, `mouseMoved` |
| `KeyListener` | Any component | `keyTyped`, `keyPressed`, `keyReleased` |
| `WindowListener` | `JFrame`, `JDialog` | 7 methods incl. `windowClosing`, `windowOpened` |
| `ItemListener` | `JCheckBox`, `JRadioButton`, `JComboBox` | `itemStateChanged(ItemEvent e)` |
| `TextListener` | `TextField`, `TextArea` | `textValueChanged(TextEvent e)` |
| `FocusListener` | Any component | `focusGained`, `focusLost` |
| `AdjustmentListener` | `Scrollbar` | `adjustmentValueChanged` |

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Syntax |
|---|---|
| Event Registration | `sourceObj.addXxxListener(listenerObj);` |
| Event Deregistration | `sourceObj.removeXxxListener(listenerObj);` |
| Source Retrieval | `Object src = event.getSource();` |
| Generic Event Class | `public class XxxEvent extends java.util.EventObject` |
| Generic Listener | `public interface XxxListener extends EventListener` |
| Inner-Class Listener Pattern | Listener class declared **inside** the GUI class for tight coupling |
| Anonymous Listener Pattern | `new ActionListener() { public void actionPerformed(...) {} }` |
| Adapter Class Trick | Extend `XxxAdapter` to override **only** the methods you need |
| JDBC URL General | `jdbc:<subprotocol>:<subname>` |
| JDBC Connection String | `jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/dbName` |
| Driver Load (Modern) | `Class.forName("com.mysql.cj.jdbc.Driver");` |
| Driver Auto-Load (JDBC 4.0+) | Drop the JAR in classpath — DriverManager auto-detects |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

The Delegation Event Model is the architectural blueprint for **Observer Pattern** in OOP. It underpins every modern GUI framework — Java Swing, JavaFX, Android's `OnClickListener`, and even web frameworks like React's event system. Understanding DEM is therefore a **prerequisite** for any event-driven system.

---

## 2.3 JDBC Architecture — The Layered Model

JDBC follows a **two-tier / three-tier** architectural model with the following layers:

| Layer | Responsibility |
|---|---|
| **Java Application Layer** | Invokes JDBC API methods (`DriverManager`, `Connection`, `Statement`, `ResultSet`) |
| **JDBC API Layer** | Standard interface contracts (`java.sql`) — vendor-neutral |
| **JDBC Driver Manager Layer** | `DriverManager` class — selects the correct driver for the JDBC URL |
| **JDBC Driver Layer** | Vendor-specific translation of JDBC calls to DB protocol (MySQL, Oracle, etc.) |
| **Database Layer** | The actual RDBMS storing the data |

---

## 2.4 The Four Types of JDBC Drivers

| Type | Name | Pure Java? | Speed | Maintainability | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Type 1** | JDBC-ODBC Bridge Driver | No (uses ODBC) | Slow | Obsolete (removed in JDK 8) | **High (Theory)** |
| **Type 2** | Native-API Driver | Partly (uses native client) | Medium | Requires native lib on client | **High (Theory)** |
| **Type 3** | Network Protocol (Middleware) Driver | Yes | Medium | Needs middleware server | **Medium** |
| **Type 4** | Thin Driver (Pure Java) | **Yes** | **Fastest** | **Most portable** | **Highest (Practical)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Type 4 Driver** is the **industry standard**. MySQL Connector/J, Oracle Thin Driver, and PostgreSQL JDBC are all **Type 4**.

---

## 2.5 Core JDBC Classes & Interfaces

| Component | Type | Package | Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| `DriverManager` | Class | `java.sql` | Manages DB drivers, returns `Connection` |
| `Connection` | Interface | `java.sql` | Represents a session with the DB |
| `Statement` | Interface | `java.sql` | Used for static SQL queries |
| `PreparedStatement` | Interface | `java.sql` | Pre-compiled, parameterized SQL (prevents SQL injection) |
| `CallableStatement` | Interface | `java.sql` | Used to invoke stored procedures |
| `ResultSet` | Interface | `java.sql` | Holds the result of a SELECT query |
| `ResultSetMetaData` | Interface | `java.sql` | Describes columns in a `ResultSet` |
| `DatabaseMetaData` | Interface | `java.sql` | Describes the DB (version, tables, etc.) |
| `SQLException` | Class | `java.sql` | Checked exception for all DB errors |
| `DataSource` | Interface | `javax.sql` | Preferred over `DriverManager` in enterprise apps (JNDI) |

### Utility in Engineering

JDBC is the **backbone** of enterprise Java (J2EE / Jakarta EE). Spring Boot's `JdbcTemplate`, Hibernate ORM, and JPA all sit **on top of** JDBC. Mastering JDBC is therefore a non-negotiable skill for any backend engineer.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Implementation Blueprints

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Implementation — Delegation Event Model

The complete lifecycle of a DEM-based application follows **5 canonical steps**:

### Step 1 — Create the Event Source
A GUI component is instantiated (Button, TextField, Frame).

```java
JButton submitBtn = new JButton("Submit");
```

### Step 2 — Implement the Listener Interface
The listener is typically a class that **implements** a listener interface. Either:
- **Separate class** (reusable across sources)
- **Inner class** (encapsulated in the GUI class)
- **Anonymous inner class** (one-off, ad-hoc handler)
- **Lambda expression** (Java 8+, only for *functional* listener interfaces like `ActionListener`)

```java
class SubmitHandler implements ActionListener {
    @Override
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        System.out.println("Submit clicked at " + e.getWhen());
    }
}
```

### Step 3 — Register the Listener with the Source
The source is told which listener will handle its events.

```java
submitBtn.addActionListener(new SubmitHandler());
```

### Step 4 — User Triggers the Event
When the user clicks the button, the JVM:
- Creates an `ActionEvent` object.
- Iterates through the internal listener list of the button.
- Invokes `actionPerformed(eventObj)` on **each** registered listener.

### Step 5 — Deregister (Optional)
To remove the listener:

```java
submitBtn.removeActionListener(handlerObj);
```

---

## 3.2 Complete DEM Program — ActionListener with Inner Class

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class CounterApp extends JFrame {
    private int count = 0;
    private JLabel label;
    private JButton incrementBtn;

    public CounterApp() {
        setTitle("DEM Counter Demo");
        setSize(300, 150);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);

        label = new JLabel("Count: 0");
        incrementBtn = new JButton("Increment");

        // STEP 3: Register the listener (inner-class form)
        incrementBtn.addActionListener(new ActionListener() {
            @Override
            public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
                count++;
                label.setText("Count: " + count);
            }
        });

        add(label);
        add(incrementBtn);
        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new CounterApp();
    }
}
```

### Code-Walkthrough (Line-by-Line Logic)

| Line | Logic |
|---|---|
| `class CounterApp extends JFrame` | Inherits a window container. |
| `private int count = 0` | Application state to be mutated by event. |
| `incrementBtn.addActionListener(new ActionListener() { ... })` | Anonymous inner class — instantiates the listener inline. |
| `count++` | The *callback* logic; runs **only** when the button is clicked. |
| `label.setText(...)` | Reflects the new state back to the UI. |

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Implementation — JDBC Application

The canonical JDBC workflow has **6 mandatory steps**:

### Step 1 — Load / Register the JDBC Driver

```java
// Explicit (old style, JDBC < 4.0)
Class.forName("com.mysql.cj.jdbc.Driver");

// Implicit (modern JDBC 4.0+) — JAR on classpath is enough
// The DriverManager auto-discovers via META-INF/services
```

**Logic:** The driver class is loaded into the JVM, and its static initializer registers itself with `DriverManager`.

### Step 2 — Establish a Connection

```java
String url  = "jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/studentdb";
String user = "root";
String pass = "password";

Connection con = DriverManager.getConnection(url, user, pass);
```

**Logic:** `DriverManager` inspects the URL's `subprotocol`, picks the matching driver, opens a TCP socket session with the DB, and returns a `Connection` object.

### Step 3 — Create a Statement

```java
Statement stmt = con.createStatement();
```

**Logic:** The `Statement` object acts as a *carrier* for the SQL string. The `Connection` factory-creates it.

### Step 4 — Execute the Query

```java
ResultSet rs = stmt.executeQuery("SELECT * FROM students");
```

**Logic:** The SQL string is shipped to the DB engine, which parses, optimizes, executes, and returns rows. `ResultSet` is a **cursor** pointing at the first row.

### Step 5 — Process the ResultSet

```java
while (rs.next()) {
    int id       = rs.getInt("id");
    String name  = rs.getString("name");
    double marks = rs.getDouble("marks");
    System.out.println(id + " | " + name + " | " + marks);
}
```

**Logic:** `rs.next()` advances the cursor. `rs.getXxx(colName)` retrieves column values. After the last row, `next()` returns `false`, exiting the loop.

### Step 6 — Close the Connection

```java
rs.close();
stmt.close();
con.close();
```

**Logic:** Resources must be released in **reverse order of creation** to avoid leaks. The `close()` method of each resource invalidates the JDBC cursor / prepared statement / socket.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Best Practice (KTU 2024 — Try-with-Resources)**
> Since Java 7, the recommended idiom is *try-with-resources*, which auto-closes everything:
> ```java
> try (Connection con = DriverManager.getConnection(url, user, pass);
>      Statement stmt = con.createStatement();
>      ResultSet rs   = stmt.executeQuery("SELECT * FROM students")) {
>     while (rs.next()) {
>         System.out.println(rs.getString(1));
>     }
> } // All three auto-closed here
> ```

---

## 3.4 Complete JDBC Program — Insert & Retrieve

```java
import java.sql.*;

public class StudentDAO {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        String url  = "jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/school";
        String user = "root";
        String pass = "admin123";

        try (Connection con = DriverManager.getConnection(url, user, pass)) {

            // ---- INSERT via PreparedStatement ----
            String insertSQL = "INSERT INTO students(name, marks) VALUES(?, ?)";
            try (PreparedStatement pst = con.prepareStatement(insertSQL)) {
                pst.setString(1, "Ananya");
                pst.setDouble(2, 92.5);
                int rows = pst.executeUpdate();
                System.out.println("Rows inserted: " + rows);
            }

            // ---- RETRIEVE ----
            String selectSQL = "SELECT id, name, marks FROM students";
            try (Statement st = con.createStatement();
                 ResultSet rs = st.executeQuery(selectSQL)) {
                while (rs.next()) {
                    System.out.println(
                        rs.getInt("id") + "\t" +
                        rs.getString("name") + "\t" +
                        rs.getDouble("marks")
                    );
                }
            }

        } catch (SQLException e) {
            e.printStackTrace();
        }
    }
}
```

### Analytical Matrix — Statement vs PreparedStatement

| Criterion | `Statement` | `PreparedStatement` |
|---|---|---|
| SQL Parameterization | No | Yes — uses `?` placeholders |
| Performance on Repeated Queries | Slow (re-parsed each time) | **Fast** (precompiled in DB) |
| SQL Injection Risk | **High** | **Low** (parameters are escaped) |
| Readability | Lower | Higher |
| KTU Recommendation | Use only for DDL or one-shot queries | **Default choice for DML** |

### Why PreparedStatement is Faster — Algebraic Insight

For `n` identical SELECT queries:

$$
T_{\text{Statement}} = n \times (T_{\text{parse}} + T_{\text{optimize}} + T_{\text{execute}})
$$

$$
T_{\text{PreparedStmt}} = 1 \times T_{\text{compile}} + n \times T_{\text{execute}}
$$

**Inference:** The compile overhead is paid **once** for `PreparedStatement`, making the per-query cost drop as $n$ grows. This is the reason enterprise systems **always** prefer `PreparedStatement`.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Delegation Event Model — Interaction Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant Src as Event Source (JButton)
    participant Lst as Event Listener (ActionListener)
    participant EObj as Event Object (ActionEvent)

    U->>Src: Click the button
    Src->>Src: Create ActionEvent object
    Src->>EObj: Populate event details (source, when, modifiers)
    Src->>Lst: Lookup registered listener in internal list
    Src->>Lst: Invoke actionPerformed(EObj)
    Lst->>Lst: Execute callback logic
    Lst-->>Src: Return (control back to source)
    Src-->>U: UI repaints / output updated
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The source **never** contains business logic. It only knows *how* to find listeners and *what* to hand them. This separation of concerns is the soul of DEM.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📘 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Explain the Delegation Event Model with its components.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Delegation Event Model (DEM)** is Java's mechanism for handling GUI events by delegating event processing from the *source* to a *registered listener*.

It has **three components**:

1. **Event Source** — The GUI component (e.g., `JButton`) that generates the event. It maintains a list of listeners and exposes `addXxxListener()` and `removeXxxListener()` methods.
2. **Event Object** — A subclass of `java.util.EventObject` (e.g., `ActionEvent`) that carries event information like source, timestamp, and modifiers.
3. **Event Listener** — An object implementing a listener interface (e.g., `ActionListener`) and providing the callback method (`actionPerformed`) that is automatically invoked when the event occurs.

When the user interacts with the source, the source creates an event object and **delegates** it to all registered listeners by calling their callback methods. This decouples event generation from event handling.

*(1 mark each for source, event, listener + 0 mark extra for connectivity — total 3 marks)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**List the four types of JDBC drivers. Which one is preferred and why?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Type | Name |
|---|---|
| **Type 1** | JDBC-ODBC Bridge Driver |
| **Type 2** | Native-API (Partly Java) Driver |
| **Type 3** | Network Protocol Driver |
| **Type 4** | Thin Driver (Pure Java) |

**Type 4 — Thin Driver** is the most preferred because:
- It is **written entirely in Java**, so it is platform-independent and requires no native libraries.
- It converts JDBC calls **directly** to the database-specific protocol — no middleware or ODBC overhead.
- It offers the **best performance** and is the industry standard (e.g., MySQL Connector/J).

*(2 marks for the list + 1 mark for the justification of Type 4)*

---

## 📗 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Rule (KTU ESE):** *Answer ANY ONE full question from each module. Each carries 14 marks split into two 7-mark sub-parts.*

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the architecture of the Delegation Event Model. Describe the role of `Event Source`, `Event Listener`, and `Event Object` with a suitable Java code example using a `JButton` and `ActionListener`. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between `ActionListener` and `MouseListener` interfaces. Write a Java program to handle a mouse-click event on a `JFrame` and display the coordinates of the click. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — (a)

**Architecture (3 Marks):**
The Delegation Event Model is built on **three pillars**:
- **Event Source** — The component generating the event (e.g., `JButton`). It exposes methods to register/deregister listeners.
- **Event Object** — A subclass of `java.util.EventObject` (e.g., `ActionEvent`) carrying event data such as source and timestamp.
- **Event Listener** — An object implementing a listener interface (e.g., `ActionListener`) that defines callback methods to be invoked when the event occurs.

The flow: *User triggers event → Source creates Event object → Source delegates to all registered listeners → Listener callback runs.*

**Java Code (4 Marks):**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class LoginDemo extends JFrame {
    public LoginDemo() {
        setTitle("DEM Login");
        setSize(300, 150);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);

        JButton loginBtn = new JButton("Login");
        JLabel statusLbl = new JLabel("Status: Awaiting login...");

        // Inner-class listener
        loginBtn.addActionListener(new ActionListener() {
            @Override
            public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
                statusLbl.setText("Status: Login Successful!");
            }
        });

        add(loginBtn);
        add(statusLbl);
        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new LoginDemo();
    }
}
```

**Code Walkthrough (0 marks — for explanation):**
- Line `loginBtn.addActionListener(...)` — *registers* the listener with the source.
- The anonymous inner class overrides `actionPerformed` — the **callback method**.
- When the button is clicked, the source fires the event and the listener updates the label.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — (b)

**Difference Table (3 Marks):**

| Criterion | `ActionListener` | `MouseListener` |
|---|---|---|
| Package | `java.awt.event` | `java.awt.event` |
| Source | Buttons, MenuItems, TextFields | Any component |
| No. of Callback Methods | **1** (`actionPerformed`) | **5** (`mouseClicked`, `mousePressed`, `mouseReleased`, `mouseEntered`, `mouseExited`) |
| Event Class | `ActionEvent` | `MouseEvent` |
| Adapter | No adapter (only 1 method) | `MouseAdapter` (empty impl of all 5) |
| Coordinates Available | No | Yes — `e.getX()`, `e.getY()` |

**Java Program using `MouseAdapter` (4 Marks):**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class MouseTracker extends JFrame {
    private JLabel coordLabel;

    public MouseTracker() {
        setTitle("Mouse Click Tracker");
        setSize(400, 300);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);

        coordLabel = new JLabel("Click anywhere in the window");
        add(coordLabel);

        // Using MouseAdapter to override only the methods we need
        addMouseListener(new MouseAdapter() {
            @Override
            public void mouseClicked(MouseEvent e) {
                int x = e.getX();
                int y = e.getY();
                coordLabel.setText("Clicked at X=" + x + ", Y=" + y);
            }
        });

        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new MouseTracker();
    }
}
```

**Explanation (0 marks — supporting):**
- `MouseAdapter` is a **convenience class** that provides empty implementations of all 5 `MouseListener` methods. We override only `mouseClicked` to keep code clean.
- `e.getX()` and `e.getY()` return the click coordinates relative to the component.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** What is JDBC? Explain the JDBC architecture with a neat diagram. List the different types of JDBC drivers. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program to connect to a MySQL database using JDBC and retrieve all records from a `students` table having columns `id`, `name`, and `marks`. Use `PreparedStatement` to insert a new record. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — (a)

**JDBC Definition (1 Mark):**
JDBC (Java Database Connectivity) is a Java API in the `java.sql` and `javax.sql` packages that provides a standard, vendor-neutral way for Java applications to communicate with relational databases.

**JDBC Architecture (4 Marks):**
JDBC follows a layered architecture:

1. **Java Application Layer** — Calls JDBC API methods.
2. **JDBC API Layer** — Set of standard interfaces (`Connection`, `Statement`, `ResultSet`).
3. **DriverManager Layer** — Selects the appropriate driver for the JDBC URL.
4. **JDBC Driver Layer** — Vendor-specific translator (e.g., MySQL Connector/J).
5. **Database Layer** — The actual RDBMS (MySQL, Oracle, etc.).

**Diagram (already covered in Section 4.2 above)** — (1 mark for the diagram).

**Types of JDBC Drivers (2 Marks):**
- **Type 1** — JDBC-ODBC Bridge (obsolete since JDK 8)
- **Type 2** — Native-API / Partly Java Driver
- **Type 3** — Network Protocol (Middleware) Driver
- **Type 4** — Thin Driver (Pure Java) — *industry standard*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — (b)

**Java Program (7 Marks):**

```java
import java.sql.*;

public class StudentApp {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        String url  = "jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/schooldb";
        String user = "root";
        String pass = "admin123";

        // INSERT using PreparedStatement
        try (Connection con = DriverManager.getConnection(url, user, pass)) {
```
