```java
            String insertSQL = "INSERT INTO students(name, marks) VALUES(?, ?)";
            try (PreparedStatement pst = con.prepareStatement(insertSQL)) {
                pst.setString(1, "Rahul");
                pst.setDouble(2, 88.0);
                int n = pst.executeUpdate();
                System.out.println(n + " record(s) inserted.");
            }

            // RETRIEVE all records
            String selectSQL = "SELECT id, name, marks FROM students";
            try (Statement st  = con.createStatement();
                 ResultSet rs   = st.executeQuery(selectSQL)) {

                System.out.println("ID\tName\tMarks");
                System.out.println("----------------------");
                while (rs.next()) {
                    System.out.println(
                        rs.getInt("id") + "\t" +
                        rs.getString("name") + "\t" +
                        rs.getDouble("marks")
                    );
                }
            }

        } catch (SQLException e) {
            System.out.println("DB Error: " + e.getMessage());
        }
    }
}
```

**Code Walkthrough (0 marks — explanatory):**
- `Class.forName()` is **not** required in modern JDBC 4.0+ — the driver is auto-loaded from the JAR.
- `PreparedStatement` uses `?` placeholders and `setXxx(index, value)` to bind parameters — this **prevents SQL injection** and improves performance.
- The **try-with-resources** block ensures all three resources (`Connection`, `Statement`, `ResultSet`) are closed automatically.
- `rs.next()` returns `true` while rows remain and moves the cursor forward by one.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to close `ResultSet` / `Statement` / `Connection`** — Results in **resource-leak** and **2-mark penalty** even if logic is correct.
> 2. **Confusing `executeQuery()` (returns `ResultSet`) with `executeUpdate()` (returns `int`)** — A wrong choice in the answer paper costs the entire 7 marks of that sub-question.
> 3. **Writing `Class.forName("...")` as mandatory in modern JDBC** — Examiner may deduct a mark. State: *"Optional since JDBC 4.0; auto-loaded via ServiceLoader."*
> 4. **Using `Statement` instead of `PreparedStatement` for parameterized queries** — The examiner *expects* `PreparedStatement` with `?` placeholders for full marks on insertion programs.
> 5. **Forgetting to register the listener** (`addActionListener(...)`) — In DEM questions, students often write the listener class but forget the `addXxxListener()` call on the source. This is the **#1 reason** for losing 2 marks.
> 6. **Mixing up `WindowListener` (7 methods) with `WindowAdapter`** — In KTU papers, mentioning the `WindowAdapter` convenience class earns a **bonus 1 mark**.
> 7. **Wrong JDBC URL format** — Writing `jdbc:mysql//localhost...` (missing `:`) instead of `jdbc:mysql://localhost...` — common typo, results in `SQLException`.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### Delegation Event Model
- ⭐ **Three components**: Event Source, Event Listener, Event Object.
- ⭐ All listeners extend the marker interface `java.util.EventListener`.
- ⭐ All event classes extend `java.util.EventObject`.
- ⭐ Registration via `addXxxListener()`; deregistration via `removeXxxListener()`.
- ⭐ **Adapter classes** (e.g., `MouseAdapter`, `WindowAdapter`, `KeyAdapter`) provide empty implementations — override only the methods you need.
- ⭐ A single source can have **multiple** listeners; a single listener can serve **multiple** sources.
- ⭐ Lambda expressions work for **functional** listener interfaces (those with a single abstract method) — e.g., `ActionListener`.
- ⭐ `getSource()` returns the originating component; `getX()`, `getY()` give coordinates for mouse events.

### JDBC Overview
- ⭐ JDBC = **Java Database Connectivity** — vendor-neutral API in `java.sql` and `javax.sql`.
- ⭐ **6 canonical steps**: Load Driver → Connect → Create Statement → Execute → Process → Close.
- ⭐ **4 driver types** — Type 4 is pure Java, fastest, most portable (industry standard).
- ⭐ **Core interfaces**: `Connection`, `Statement`, `PreparedStatement`, `CallableStatement`, `ResultSet`, `ResultSetMetaData`, `DatabaseMetaData`.
- ⭐ **Core class**: `DriverManager` (deprecated in favor of `DataSource` for enterprise).
- ⭐ **`Statement`** = static SQL; **`PreparedStatement`** = precompiled, parameterized, faster, safer.
- ⭐ **`executeQuery()`** returns `ResultSet`; **`executeUpdate()`** returns `int` (rows affected).
- ⭐ **Try-with-resources** is the modern way to auto-close JDBC resources.
- ⭐ **JDBC URL format** = `jdbc:<subprotocol>:<subname>` (e.g., `jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/dbname`).
- ⭐ **`SQLException`** is a *checked* exception — must be caught or declared.

### Quick-Fire Memory Aid
- **DEM = Source + Event + Listener** → "**S.E.L.**" — *Sel*egate the event.
- **JDBC 6 Steps** → "**L**ord **C**ommon **S**ense **E**xpects **P**atients **C**lose" → **L**oad, **C**onnect, **S**tatement, **E**xecute, **P**rocess, **C**lose.
- **Driver Type Hierarchy** → 1 = Bridge (worst) → 4 = Thin (best).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECIT633 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Types # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In **Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)**, a **Type** is a classification of data that specifies: 1. The **set of values** a variable can hold. 2. The **operations** that can be performed on those values. 3. The **memory representation** of the data. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PECIT633, **Types** in OOP refer to the systematic categorization of data and the mechanisms by which objects and primitives interact through type systems. They form the foundation of **type safety**, **polymorphism**, and **memory management** in OOP languages. ### Classification of Types in OOP | Category | Description | Examples (Java/C#) | |----------|-------------|---------------------| | **Primitive Types** | Built-in, basic data types | `int`, `float`, `char`, `boolean` | | **Reference Types** | Types that refer to objects | Classes, Interfaces, Arrays | | **User-Defined Types** | Types created by the programmer | Custom Classes, Structs, Enums | | **Generic Types** | Parameterized types for type safety | `List`, `Map` | | **Value Types** (C#) | Store data directly in memory | `struct`, `int`, `double` | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Understanding the distinction between **Value Types** and **Reference Types** is a high-weightage topic. It directly influences memory allocation, parameter passing semantics, and assignment behavior — all of which are recurring questions in KTU End Semester Examinations (ESE). --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you have a **warehouse with different storage containers**: - **Primitive/Value Types** are like **small labeled boxes** kept on your workbench. The box *is* the item — you can directly access the value inside, and when you copy it, you get a brand-new identical box. - **Reference Types** are like **paper slips with addresses** that point to items stored in a distant warehouse. The slip itself isn't the item; it just tells you *where* to find it. If you photocopy the slip, both slips point to the **same warehouse item**. ### The "Hotel Key Card" Analogy > [!NOTE] > Think of a **Hotel Key Card** as a Reference Type. The card isn't your room — it just points to it. If you give a copy of the card to your friend, both of you can access the *same room* and modify its contents (e.g., move furniture). But a **Value Type** is like a **physical room key** — giving your friend a copy doesn't affect your key, and changes to the room you make don't reflect on the copy. --- ## 1.3 Why Types Matter in OOP - **Type Safety:** Prevents invalid operations (e.g., adding a `String` to an `int`). - **Memory Efficiency:** Primitive types avoid heap allocation overhead. - **Polymorphism Support:** Enables method overriding and dynamic dispatch. - **Code Clarity:** Makes APIs self-documenting. - **Compile-Time Checking:** Many type errors are caught before runtime. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Metric (Java):** In Java, the **8 primitive types** have fixed sizes: > - `byte`: **8 bits**, `short`: **16 bits**, `int`: **32 bits**, `long`: **64 bits** > - `float`: **32 bits**, `double`: **64 bits** > - `char`: **16 bits** (Unicode), `boolean`: **1 bit** (logical) # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet ## 2.1 Value Types vs Reference Types — The Core Distinction ### 2.1.1 Value Types **Definition:** Value types **directly contain their data**. They are stored either on the **stack** (in Java/C#) or **inline** within containing structures. Assignment creates a **copy** of the value. **Characteristics:** - Stored on the **stack** (in most implementations). - Each variable has its **own copy** of the data. - Cannot be `null` (in Java for primitives). - Default initialization is automatic. - Faster access (stack allocation/deallocation). ### 2.1.2 Reference Types **Definition:** Reference types **store a reference (memory address)** to the actual data, which lives on the **heap**. Assignment copies the *reference*, not the object. **Characteristics:** - Stored on the **heap**; the reference variable is on the **stack**. - Multiple variables can refer to the **same object**. - Can be assigned `null`. - Garbage collected when no references remain. - Slower allocation (heap + GC overhead). --- ## 2.2 Type Conversion (Type Casting) ### 2.2.1 Implicit (Widening) Conversion - **Automatic** by the compiler. - Occurs when converting a **smaller type to a larger type** (no data loss). - Safe conversion: `byte → short → int → long → float → double` ### 2.2.2 Explicit (Narrowing) Conversion - **Manual** using cast operator `(type)`. - Required when converting a **larger type to a smaller type** (potential data loss). - Example: `double d = 9.7; int i = (int) d;` // i = 9 --- ## 2.3 Boxing and Unboxing > [!NOTE] > **Boxing:** Converting a value type (primitive) to a reference type (wrapper object) by wrapping it in an object — happens on the **heap**. > [!NOTE] > **Unboxing:** Extracting the value type from the wrapper object — value is copied from the **heap** to the **stack**. ```java // Boxing int x = 10; Integer obj = Integer.valueOf(x); // Explicit boxing (Java 5+) // or: Integer obj = x; // Auto-boxing // Unboxing int y = obj.intValue(); // Explicit unboxing // or: int y = obj; // Auto-unboxing ``` --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet (Cheat Table) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** This table is the most frequently tested comparison in Module 4. Memorize the **memory location**, **default value**, and **assignment behavior** columns. | Property | Value Type | Reference Type | |----------|------------|----------------| | **Storage Location** | Stack (typically) | Heap (object), Stack (reference) | | **Contains** | Actual data | Reference (memory address) | | **Default Value** | 0, 0.0, false, '\u0000' | `null` | | **Assignment** | Creates a copy | Copies the reference | | **`null` Allowed?** | No (Java primitives) | Yes | | **Garbage Collected?** | No (auto-cleaned) | Yes (when unreachable) | | **Examples** | `int`, `double`, `char`, `struct` (C#) | Objects, Arrays, Strings, Classes | | **Memory Size** | Fixed (e.g., 4 bytes for int) | Variable | | **Access Speed** | Faster | Slower (pointer indirection) | | **Destructor/Finalizer** | Not applicable | Yes (via GC) | --- ## 2.5 Parameter Passing Semantics | Mechanism | Description | Affects Original? | |-----------|-------------|-------------------| | **Pass by Value** (Java primitives) | Copy of value passed | No | | **Pass by Reference (Object)** (Java) | Copy of reference passed | Yes (object state can change) | | **`ref` (C#)** | Reference to variable passed | Yes | | **`out` (C#)** | Reference for output, must assign | Yes | | **Pointer** (C/C++) | Direct memory address | Yes | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Java-Specific KTU Note:** Java is **strictly pass-by-value**. However, for objects, the *reference* (address) is passed by value, so the object's internal state *can* be modified through the parameter. --- ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Type Systems in Compilers:** Enable optimization (e.g., just-in-time compilation). - **Database Systems:** Type mapping between application objects and SQL types (ORM frameworks like Hibernate). - **Embedded Systems:** Choosing appropriate types (e.g., `int8_t` vs `int32_t`) optimizes memory. - **API Design:** Strong typing prevents misuse of libraries. - **Type Inference (var/auto):** Modern languages (C#, Java 10+, C++) use type inference for cleaner code. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analysis & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Walkthrough: Type Conversion Rules ### Problem Setup Given the following Java code segment, determine the output and explain the type conversions: ```java int a = 5; double b = a; // Line 1 double c = 7.8; int d = (int) c; // Line 2 Integer obj = a; // Line 3 int e = obj; // Line 4 ``` ### Step-by-Step Execution **Line 1: `double b = a;`** - Initial value: $a = 5$ (int, 32-bit) - Conversion: `int` → `double` (widening, implicit) - Result: $b = 5.0$ - Memory: $a$ stays in stack as 4 bytes; $b$ stored as 8-byte IEEE-754 double. **Line 2: `int d = (int) c;`** - Initial value: $c = 7.8$ (double) - Conversion: `double` → `int` (narrowing, explicit cast required) - Truncation rule: $d = \lfloor 7.8 \rfloor = 7$ (fractional part discarded, not rounded) - Result: $d = 7$ **Line 3: `Integer obj = a;` (Auto-boxing)** - Initial value: $a = 5$ (primitive int) - Conversion: `int` → `java.lang.Integer` (boxing) - The JVM allocates an Integer object on the **heap** with value 5. - `obj` (on stack) holds a reference to this heap object. **Line 4: `int e = obj;` (Auto-unboxing)** - `obj` is dereferenced, and its int value is copied to `e`. - Result: $e = 5$ ### Final Output Trace $$ \begin{aligned} a &= 5 \quad (\text{int, stack}) \\ b &= 5.0 \quad (\text{double, stack}) \\ d &= 7 \quad (\text{int, stack — truncated from } 7.8) \\ obj &\rightarrow \text{Integer}(5) \quad (\text{heap}) \\ e &= 5 \quad (\text{int, stack — unboxed from obj}) \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Implicit Type Promotion Hierarchy In Java, numeric operators apply **binary numeric promotion** following this strict hierarchy: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{byte} \rightarrow \text{short} \rightarrow \text{int} \rightarrow \text{long} \rightarrow \text{float} \rightarrow \text{double} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Rule:** When two operands of different types meet in an expression, the *smaller* type is promoted to the *larger* type **before** the operation. The result type is the larger of the two. ### Worked Example ```java byte b = 10; int i = 20; long l = 30000L; float f = 1.5f; double d = 2.5; // Expression: b + i * l - f / d ``` **Step 1:** `i * l` → `int * long` → promoted to `long` → result: $20 \times 30000 = 600000L$ **Step 2:** `f / d` → `float / double` → promoted to `double` → result: $1.5 / 2.5 = 0.6$ **Step 3:** `b + (result of step 1)` → `byte + long` → promoted to `long` → result: $10 + 600000 = 600010L$ **Step 4:** Final subtraction: `600010L - 0.6` → promoted to `double` → result: $600009.4$ --- ## 3.3 Reference Type Behavior: The "Two Variables, One Object" Problem ### Code Segment ```java class Box { int value; } Box b1 = new Box(); b1.value = 100; Box b2 = b1; // Reference copy b2.value = 200; System.out.println(b1.value); ``` ### Step-by-Step Memory Analysis $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{Heap: } \text{Object}_{\text{Box}} \text{ at address } 0x1000, \text{ value} = 100 \\ &\text{Stack: } b1 \rightarrow 0x1000 \\ &\text{After } b2 = b1\text{: Stack: } b2 \rightarrow 0x1000 \quad (\text{same address}) \\ &\text{After } b2.value = 200\text{: Heap object value field updated to } 200 \\ &\text{Output: } b1.value = 200 \quad (\text{both refer to same object}) \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Common Pitfall:** Students often assume `b2 = b1` creates a copy of the object. It does **not** — it only copies the *reference*. This is a guaranteed 7-mark question in ESE. ### Comparison with Value Type Behavior ```java int a = 100; int b = a; // Value copy b = 200; System.out.println(a); // Output: 100 (a is unchanged) ``` --- ## 3.4 Type Casting Rules — Decision Matrix | Source Type | Target Type | Casting Required? | Data Loss Possible? | Example | |-------------|-------------|-------------------|---------------------|---------| | `byte` | `short` | No (implicit) | No | `byte b = 10; short s = b;` | | `int` | `long` | No (implicit) | No | `int i = 5; long l = i;` | | `long` | `int` | Yes (explicit) | Yes | `long l = 5L; int i = (int) l;` | | `float` | `int` | Yes (explicit) | Yes (truncation) | `float f = 9.7f; int i = (int) f;` | | `int` | `Integer` | No (auto-boxing) | No | `int i = 5; Integer obj = i;` | | `Integer` | `int` | No (auto-unboxing) | No (NPE risk) | `Integer obj = 5; int i = obj;` | | `double` | `String` | No (using wrapper) | No | `String s = String.valueOf(3.14);` | | `String` | `int` | Yes (parse) | Yes (NumberFormatException) | `int i = Integer.parseInt("123");` | --- ## 3.5 Key Formulas and Conversion Rules ### 3.5.1 Truncation Formula (double → int) $$ \text{int\_value} = \text{sign}(\text{double\_value}) \times \lfloor \vert \text{double\_value} \vert \rfloor $$ **Example:** `(int) -7.8` → $-1 \times \lfloor 7.8 \rfloor = -7$ (truncation towards zero) ### 3.5.2 Overflow Formula (for int) $$ \text{overflow} = (2^{31} - 1) + 1 \rightarrow -2^{31} $$ **Example:** `int x = Integer.MAX_VALUE; x++;` → $x = -2147483648$ (wraps around) ### 3.5.3 Memory Size of Reference $$ \text{Size}_{\text{reference}} = \text{constant} \quad (\text{typically 4 or 8 bytes depending on JVM/architecture}) $$ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Type Hierarchy in OOP The following diagram shows the **Type System Hierarchy** in OOP, illustrating how primitive types, reference types, and user-defined types relate. ```mermaid graph TD A[OOP Type System] --> B[Primitive Types] A --> C[Reference Types] A --> D[User Defined Types] B --> B1[Integer Family] B --> B2[Floating Family] B --> B3[Character] B --> B4[Boolean] B1 --> B11[byte: 8 bits] B1 --> B12[short: 16 bits] B1 --> B13[int: 32 bits] B1 --> B14[long: 64 bits] B2 --> B21[float: 32 bits] B2 --> B22[double: 64 bits] C --> C1[Predefined Classes] C --> C2[Arrays] C --> C3[Interfaces] C --> C4[Enumerations] D --> D1[Custom Classes] D --> D2[Abstract Classes] D --> D3[Generic Classes] ``` --- ## 4.2 Memory Layout: Value Type vs Reference Type This diagram visualizes how **value types** and **reference types** are stored in memory (stack vs heap). ```mermaid graph LR subgraph STK[STACK MEMORY] A1[Variable a: 5
Value Type int] A2[Reference b1: 0x1000
Pointer to Heap] A3[Reference b2: 0x1000
Pointer to Heap] end subgraph HEP[HEAP MEMORY] B1[Object at 0x1000
Class Box
value field: 200] end A2 -.-> B1 A3 -.-> B1 style A1 fill:#bbf,stroke:#333 style A2 fill:#fbb,stroke:#333 style A3 fill:#fbb,stroke:#333 style B1 fill:#bfb,stroke:#333 ``` --- ## 4.3 Type Conversion Flow (Boxing/Unboxing) ```mermaid graph TD P[Primitive int x = 5] -->|Auto-Boxing| B[Wrapper Integer obj] B -->|Stored on Heap| H[Heap: Integer object value 5] B -->|Auto-Unboxing| U[Primitive int y = obj] U --> Y[Result: y = 5] style P fill:#bbf style B fill:#fbf style H fill:#bfb style U fill:#ffb style Y fill:#bff ``` --- ## 4.4 Type Casting Decision Block ```mermaid graph TD START[Source Type] --> Q1{Target Larger?} Q1 -->|Yes| IMP[Implicit Widening Conversion] Q1 -->|No| Q2{Target Smaller?} Q2 -->|Yes| EXP[Explicit Narrowing Cast] Q2 -->|Same| NOOP[No Conversion Needed] IMP --> SAFE[No Data Loss] EXP --> WARN[Possible Data Loss or Overflow] SAFE --> DONE1[Compile Time: Auto] WARN --> DONE2[Programmer Must Write Cast] style START fill:#bbf style IMP fill:#bfb style EXP fill:#fbb style WARN fill:#f99 ``` --- ## 4.5 Parameter Passing Mechanism Block Diagram ```mermaid graph LR subgraph CALLER[Caller Method] X[Original Variable] end subgraph CALLEE[Callee Method] P[Parameter] end X -->|Pass by Value: copy of value| PV[P receives copy] X -->|Pass by Reference: address| PR[P receives address] X -->|Pass by Reference of Variable ref in C#| PRR[P receives reference] PV --> NO[Original Unchanged] PR --> YES[Object State Mutable] PRR --> YES2[Original Variable Mutable] style X fill:#bbf style PV fill:#fbb style PR fill:#ffb style PRR fill:#bfb ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]** **Differentiate between value types and reference types in Java with suitable examples.** **Model Answer (3 Marks — Allocation: Definition 1M, Differences 1M, Example 1M):** | Aspect | Value Type | Reference Type | |--------|------------|----------------| | **Storage** | Stored on stack | Object on heap, reference on stack | | **Contains** | Actual data | Memory address of object | | **Assignment** | Creates independent copy | Copies reference, shares object | | **Default** | 0, 0.0, false | `null` | | **Example** | `int a = 5;` | `Box b = new Box();` | **Example Code:** ```java int x = 10; // value type int y = x; // y = 10, independent copy Box b1 = new Box(); // reference type Box b2 = b1; // b1 and b2 refer to same object ``` --- ### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sessional]** **What is auto-boxing and auto-unboxing in Java? Give an example.** **Model Answer (3 Marks — Allocation: Definition 1.5M, Example 1.5M):** - **Auto-Boxing:** The automatic conversion of a primitive type to its corresponding wrapper class object by the compiler (introduced in Java 5). - **Auto-Unboxing:** The automatic conversion of a wrapper class object back to its corresponding primitive type. **Example:** ```java Integer obj = 50; // auto-boxing: int → Integer int x = obj; // auto-unboxing: Integer → int System.out.println(x); // Output: 50 ``` The compiler internally generates `Integer.valueOf(50)` and `obj.intValue()`. --- ## PART B — Full ESE-Style Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Note:** As per KTU ESE pattern, students answer ONE full question from a choice of TWO. Each question has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. --- ### **Question A: 14 Marks** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]** **(a)** Explain the concept of **value types and reference types** in OOP with a neat diagram. Discuss how they differ in terms of **memory allocation, assignment, and default values**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** With a suitable Java program, demonstrate **pass-by-value semantics for primitive types and pass-by-reference-value for objects**. Explain why Java is strictly pass-by-value even for objects. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution: **(a) Value Types vs Reference Types — 7 Marks** **Definition (1 Mark):** - **Value Type:** A type whose variable directly contains the data. - **Reference Type:** A type whose variable contains a reference (address) to the data stored on the heap. **Memory Allocation (2 Marks):** - Value types → stack memory. - Reference types → object on heap, reference variable on stack. **Assignment (2 Marks):** - Value type: `int a = 5; int b = a;` → `b` is an independent copy. Modifying `b` does not affect `a`. - Reference type: `Box b1 = new Box(); Box b2 = b1;` → `b2` and `b1` point to the **same** object. Modifying object via `b2` reflects in `b1`. **Default Values (1 Mark):** - Value types: `0`, `0.0`, `false`, `'\u0000'`. - Reference types: `null`. **Diagram (1 Mark):** Refer to Section 4.2 memory layout diagram. --- **(b) Pass-by-Value Demonstration — 7 Marks** **Java Code (4 Marks):** ```java class Number { int value; } public class PassDemo { static void modifyPrimitive(int x) { x = 100; } static void modifyObject(Number n) { n.value = 100; } public static void main(String[] args) { int a = 5; modifyPrimitive(a); System.out.println("After primitive: " + a); // 5 Number obj = new Number(); obj.value = 10; modifyObject(obj); System.out.println("After object: " + obj.value); // 100 } } ``` **Explanation (3 Marks):** - In `modifyPrimitive(int x)`, the parameter `x` is a **copy** of `a`. Modifying `x` does not affect `a`. - In `modifyObject(Number n)`, the parameter `n` is a **copy of the reference** held by `obj`. Both `n` and `obj` point to the **same object** on the heap, so `n.value = 100` modifies the original object. - **Why Java is strictly pass-by-value:** Even for objects, Java passes a *copy of the reference* (the address value), not the original reference variable itself. Hence, if we reassign `n = new Number()` inside the method, the caller's `obj` remains unchanged. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Question A):** A common student error is stating "Java is pass-by-reference for objects." This is **wrong** and will cost 2–3 marks. The correct statement is: **"Java is always pass-by-value, but for objects, the value passed is a copy of the reference."** --- ### **Question B: 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]** **(a)** What is **type casting**? Explain **implicit (widening) and explicit (narrowing) conversion** in Java with examples. List the order of type promotion in numeric expressions. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain **boxing and unboxing** with a code example. Discuss why boxing is considered an expensive operation. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution: **(a) Type Casting — 7 Marks** **Definition (1 Mark):** Type casting is the process of converting a value from one data type to another. **Implicit/Widening Conversion (2 Marks):** - Compiler performs automatically. - Converts a smaller type to a larger type. - **No data loss.** - Example: ```java int i = 100; double d = i; // d = 100.0 ``` **Explicit/Narrowing Conversion (2 Marks):** - Programmer must use cast operator `(type)`. - Converts a larger type to a smaller type. - **Possible data loss or overflow.** - Example: ```java double d = 9.78; int i = (int) d; // i = 9 (truncation, not rounding) ``` **Type Promotion Order (2 Marks):** $$ \text{byte} \rightarrow \text{short} \rightarrow \text{int} \rightarrow \text{long} \rightarrow \text{float} \rightarrow \text{double} $$ In expressions, the *smaller* operand is promoted to the *larger* type. `char` is also promoted to `int` in expressions. --- **(b) Boxing and Unboxing — 7 Marks** **Definition (1 Mark):** - **Boxing:** Converting a primitive type to its corresponding wrapper class object. - **Unboxing:** Extracting the primitive value from a wrapper object. **Code Example (3 Marks):** ```java public class BoxingDemo { public static void main(String[] args) { int primitive = 42; Integer boxed = Integer.valueOf(primitive); // explicit boxing Integer autoBoxed = primitive; // auto-boxing (Java 5+) int unboxed = boxed.intValue(); // explicit unboxing int autoUnboxed = autoBoxed; // auto-unboxing System.out.println(autoUnboxed); // 42 } } ``` **Why Boxing is Expensive (3 Marks):** 1. **Heap Allocation:** Boxing requires creating a new object on the heap, which is slower than stack allocation. 2. **Garbage Collection Overhead:** Wrapper objects become eligible for GC, adding CPU cost. 3. **Memory Footprint:** A single `Integer` object consumes ~16 bytes (header + value) vs. 4 bytes for an `int`. 4. **Caching:** Java caches small integers (–128 to 127) in `IntegerCache`, but values outside this range create new objects. 5. **NullPointerException Risk:** Unboxing a `null` wrapper throws NPE. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Question B):** Students often forget that **auto-unboxing a `null` wrapper throws `NullPointerException`**. A common interview/ESE question is: *"What happens when you auto-unbox a null Integer?"* Correct answer: `NullPointerException` is thrown at runtime. --- ## KTU Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Types** ### Core Concepts - ✅ **Value Types** are stored on the **stack** and contain data directly (Java primitives: `byte`, `short`, `int`, `long`, `float`, `double`, `char`, `boolean`). - ✅ **Reference Types** are stored on the **heap**; variables hold a **reference (address)** to the object. - ✅ **Assignment** of value types creates an **independent copy**; assignment of reference types copies the **reference** (aliasing). - ✅ **Default value** of reference types is `null`; primitive types default to `0`/`0.0`/`false`. ### Type Conversion - ✅ **Implicit (widening):** smaller → larger type, automatic, **no data loss**. - ✅ **Explicit (narrowing):** larger → smaller type, requires cast, **possible data loss/truncation**. - ✅ **Type Promotion Order:** `byte → short → int → long → float → double`. ### Boxing/Unboxing - ✅ **Boxing:** primitive → wrapper object (heap allocation). - ✅ **Unboxing:** wrapper object → primitive. - ✅ Auto-boxing/unboxing introduced in **Java 5**. - ✅ Integer cache range: **-128 to 127**. - ✅ Auto-unboxing `null` → **NullPointerException**. ### Parameter Passing - ✅ Java is **always pass-by-value** (even for objects — it passes a copy of the reference). - ✅ C# has explicit `ref`, `out`, and `in` keywords for reference parameter passing. ### Common Pitfalls - ❌ Don't confuse "pass by reference" with "passing object reference by value." - ❌ Don't assume `b2 = b1` creates an object copy — it copies the reference. - ❌ Don't forget that `(int) 7.8` truncates to `7` (not rounds to `8`). - ❌ Don't auto-unbox a `null` wrapper. ### Memory Sizes (Java) - `byte`: **1 byte**, `short`: **2 bytes**, `int`: **4 bytes**, `long`: **8 bytes** - `float`: **4 bytes**, `double`: **8 bytes**, `char`: **2 bytes** > [!NOTE] > **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When answering value-vs-reference type questions, always include a **diagram of stack and heap memory**. Examiners award 1–2 marks specifically for a correct diagram. The 3 most repeated ESE questions in this module are: > 1. Value vs Reference Type comparison (7–14 marks) > 2. Boxing/Unboxing with example (7 marks) > 3. Type casting (implicit/explicit) with truncation behavior (3–7 marks)

topics.title: Steps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT633 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Steps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal KTU 2024 Definition

In the context of **Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)** under the KTU 2024 Scheme, the term **"Steps"** refers to the **systematic, sequential methodology** followed in **Object-Oriented Analysis and Design (OOAD)** — the disciplined procedure used to model a real-world problem domain into a set of collaborating objects, classes, and relationships that can be implemented as software.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECIT633 - Module 4):**
> The "Steps" in OOAD constitute the structured engineering pipeline: **Problem Understanding → Object Identification → Class Modeling → Relationship Establishment → Implementation → Testing**. Mastering this pipeline is a **high-weightage** item in KTU University Examinations.

The core steps in OOP methodology are formally categorized into three major phases:

| Phase | Full Form | Core Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **OOA** | Object-Oriented Analysis | *What* the system must do |
| **OOD** | Object-Oriented Design | *How* the system will be built |
| **OOP** | Object-Oriented Programming | Actual *coding* in a language |

### Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it like building a custom car from scratch:**
>
> - **Step 1 (Analysis):** You interview the customer to understand their needs (speed? family use? off-road?).
> - **Step 2 (Object Identification):** You list the parts — Engine, Wheel, Seat, Steering, Body.
> - **Step 3 (Class Definition):** You group similar parts — all four Wheels belong to the *Wheel* class.
> - **Step 4 (Attributes):** Each Wheel has a `radius`, `material`, and `pressure`.
> - **Step 5 (Methods/Behaviors):** A Wheel can `rotate()`, `inflate()`, `deflate()`.
> - **Step 6 (Relationships):** A Car *has-a* Engine, *has-four* Wheels (Composition + Multiplicity).
> - **Step 7 (Implementation):** You manufacture the parts and assemble them.
> - **Step 8 (Testing):** You test-drive the car on different terrains.

Just as an engineer cannot randomly weld parts, an OOP developer cannot directly write code without following these structured **steps** to ensure the software is **modular, reusable, maintainable, and scalable**.

### Key Terminology (KTU Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Vocabulary for KTU 2024 Scheme Examinations:**
> - **Object:** A real-world entity with state and behavior.
> - **Class:** A blueprint/template for creating objects.
> - **Attribute:** A data member that represents the state of an object.
> - **Method:** A member function that represents the behavior.
> - **Encapsulation:** Binding data and methods within a single unit.
> - **Message Passing:** Objects communicating by calling each other's methods.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### The 8 Foundational Steps of Object-Oriented Methodology

The complete OOAD pipeline is divided into **8 sequential steps**. Each step has a specific input, output, and validation gate. The KTU examiner frequently tests the student's ability to *map* a problem statement to the correct step.

#### **Step 1: Problem Domain Analysis**
- **Goal:** Understand the *what* of the system.
- **Activity:** Read the problem statement. Identify nouns (potential objects), verbs (potential methods), and adjectives (potential attributes).
- **Output:** A textual summary and a use-case list.
- **Why it matters:** Prevents *feature creep* and *scope misalignment*.

#### **Step 2: Candidate Object Identification**
- **Goal:** Extract all possible objects from the problem.
- **Activity:** Underline all nouns in the problem statement.
- **Output:** A long, unfiltered list of candidate objects.
- **Why it matters:** Provides raw material for class design.

#### **Step 3: Object Filtering & Class Identification**
- **Goal:** Remove redundant/irrelevant objects and group similar ones.
- **Activity:** Eliminate objects outside the system boundary. Merge similar objects (e.g., "Manager" and "Employee" → single `Employee` class with a `role` attribute).
- **Output:** A refined list of *candidate classes*.
- **Why it matters:** Ensures *high cohesion* and prevents *class explosion*.

#### **Step 4: Attribute Identification**
- **Goal:** Define the *state* of each class.
- **Activity:** For each class, list all relevant data fields.
- **Output:** A data dictionary per class.
- **Why it matters:** Defines the memory footprint of objects.

#### **Step 5: Method (Behavior) Identification**
- **Goal:** Define the *behavior* of each class.
- **Activity:** For each class, list all operations. Identify which class is *responsible* for each operation (CRC cards: Class-Responsibility-Collaboration).
- **Output:** A method signature list per class.
- **Why it matters:** Implements *encapsulation* and *information hiding*.

#### **Step 6: Relationship Establishment**
- **Goal:** Connect classes logically.
- **Activity:** Identify *IS-A* (Generalization/Inheritance), *HAS-A* (Aggregation/Composition), and *USES* (Association/Dependency).
- **Output:** A class relationship matrix.
- **Why it matters:** Forms the *structural backbone* of the system.

#### **Step 7: UML Class Diagram Construction**
- **Goal:** Visualize the design.
- **Activity:** Draw the Unified Modeling Language (UML) class diagram with multiplicities, stereotypes, and visibility.
- **Output:** A graphical class diagram.
- **Why it matters:** Serves as the *blueprint* for the programmer.

#### **Step 8: Implementation, Testing & Refinement**
- **Goal:** Convert design into working code.
- **Activity:** Write classes, instantiate objects, perform unit testing, integrate, and refactor.
- **Output:** Executable, tested software.
- **Why it matters:** Validates the entire OOAD pipeline.

### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Step" Decision Matrix — When to use which OOP feature:**

| Design Requirement | Correct OOP Step to Apply | Resulting Concept |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Two classes share common code | **Step 6**: Identify *IS-A* | **Inheritance** |
| One class contains another as data member | **Step 6**: Identify *HAS-A* | **Composition** |
| Same method name, different behavior | **Step 5**: Method overriding | **Polymorphism** |
| Hide internal data from outside world | **Step 4-5**: Private attributes + Public getters/setters | **Encapsulation** |
| Single interface, multiple implementations | **Step 7**: Abstract class in UML | **Abstraction** |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

The 8-step OOAD pipeline is the **backbone of enterprise software engineering**:
- **Banking Software (e.g., SBI Core Banking):** `Customer`, `Account`, `Transaction` classes with *HAS-A* and *IS-A* relationships.
- **Automotive Embedded Systems (e.g., Tesla Autopilot):** `Sensor`, `ECU`, `Actuator` objects communicating via *message passing*.
- **Healthcare Information Systems:** `Patient`, `Doctor`, `Prescription` classes with strict *encapsulation* for HIPAA compliance.
- **Aerospace (ISRO Satellite Control):** Real-time OOP models for `Telemetry`, `Thruster`, `OrbitCalculator` modules.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Worked Example: Applying the 8 Steps to a Problem

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU 2024 Style):**
> *"Design an object-oriented model for a **Library Management System**. The library issues books to members. Each book has a title, author, and ISBN. Each member has a name, member ID, and can borrow up to 3 books. The system must track which member has which book and the due date."*

---

### **Step 1: Problem Domain Analysis**

**Action:** Parse the textual description.

- **Nouns (potential objects):** Library, Book, Member, Title, Author, ISBN, Name, Member ID, Due Date.
- **Verbs (potential methods):** Issue, Borrow, Track, Return.
- **Adjectives (potential attributes/constraints):** "up to 3 books" (multiplicity constraint).

**Output:** Two primary entities emerge — **Book** and **Member**.

---

### **Step 2: Candidate Object Identification**

Raw object list:

$$\text{Objects} = \{\text{Library}, \text{Book}, \text{Member}, \text{Title}, \text{Author}, \text{ISBN}, \text{Name}, \text{MemberID}, \text{DueDate}\}$$

**KTU Insight:** Every noun in the problem is a *candidate*, but not every candidate survives to Step 3.

---

### **Step 3: Object Filtering & Class Identification**

Apply the **"Outside System Boundary"** filter and the **"Group Similar"** rule:

- `Title`, `Author`, `ISBN` → **Attributes of Book** (not separate objects).
- `Name`, `MemberID` → **Attributes of Member**.
- `DueDate` → **Attribute of a transaction/loan**.
- `Library` → **System class** (the main controller).

$$\text{Final Classes} = \{\text{Library}, \text{Book}, \text{Member}\}$$

---

### **Step 4: Attribute Identification**

For each class, list the data members:

$$\text{Book} = \{\text{title} : \text{String}, \text{author} : \text{String}, \text{ISBN} : \text{String}, \text{isIssued} : \text{Boolean}\}$$

$$\text{Member} = \{\text{name} : \text{String}, \text{memberID} : \text{String}, \text{borrowedBooks} : \text{List of Book}\}$$

$$\text{Library} = \{\text{books} : \text{List of Book}, \text{members} : \text{List of Member}\}$$

---

### **Step 5: Method (Behavior) Identification**

Use **CRC (Class-Responsibility-Collaboration)** technique:

$$\text{Book.issue()} \rightarrow \text{collaborates with } \text{Member}$$

$$\text{Member.borrowBook(book : Book)} \rightarrow \text{checks limit} \leq 3$$

$$\text{Library.searchBook(title : String)} \rightarrow \text{returns Book}$$

$$\text{Library.addMember(m : Member)}, \quad \text{Library.addBook(b : Book)}$$

---

### **Step 6: Relationship Establishment**

Analyze the verbs between classes:

| Phrase in Problem | Type of Relationship | OOP Concept |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| "Member *has* borrowed books" | *HAS-MANY* | **Aggregation** (Member holds reference to Books) |
| "Library *contains* books and members" | *WHOLE-PART* (lifecycle dependent) | **Composition** |
| "Book *is issued to* Member" | *USES* | **Association** |
| No two classes share common traits here | *IS-A* absent | No Inheritance |

$$\text{Composition : Library} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Book}$$

$$\text{Association : Member} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Book} \quad (\text{multiplicity } 0..3)$$

---

### **Step 7: UML Class Diagram Construction**

(See SECTION 4 for the rendered Mermaid diagram.)

The diagram should show:
- Three boxes for `Library`, `Book`, `Member`.
- Lines connecting them with multiplicity markers.
- Visibility symbols: `-` for private, `+` for public.

---

### **Step 8: Implementation, Testing & Refinement**

Convert the UML diagram to code (conceptual pseudocode):

```cpp
class Book {
private:
    string title, author, ISBN;
    bool isIssued;
public:
    void issue() { isIssued = true; }
    void returnBook() { isIssued = false; }
};

class Member {
private:
    string name, memberID;
    vector<Book*> borrowedBooks;
public:
    bool borrowBook(Book* b) {
        if (borrowedBooks.size() < 3) {
            borrowedBooks.push_back(b);
            b->issue();
            return true;
        }
        return false;
    }
};

class Library {
private:
    vector<Book> books;
    vector<Member> members;
public:
    void addBook(Book b) { books.push_back(b); }
    void addMember(Member m) { members.push_back(m); }
};
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Validation:** Test by creating 1 Library, 5 Books, 2 Members. Member 1 borrows 3 books (success). Member 1 attempts 4th book (fails — constraint enforced). This is the **refinement** step in action.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1: The 8-Step OOAD Pipeline (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step1["STEP 1: Problem Domain Analysis"]:::stepStyle
    step2["STEP 2: Candidate Object Identification"]:::stepStyle
    step3["STEP 3: Object Filtering and Class Identification"]:::stepStyle
    step4["STEP 4: Attribute Identification"]:::stepStyle
    step5["STEP 5: Method and Behavior Identification"]:::stepStyle
    step6["STEP 6: Relationship Establishment"]:::stepStyle
    step7["STEP 7: UML Class Diagram Construction"]:::stepStyle
    step8["STEP 8: Implementation Testing and Refinement"]:::stepStyle

    step1 --> step2
    step2 --> step3
    step3 --> step4
    step4 --> step5
    step5 --> step6
    step6 --> step7
    step7 --> step8
    step8 --> step7

    classDef stepStyle fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### Diagram 2: UML Class Diagram for the Library Management System

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Library {
        -books: List of Book
        -members: List of Member
        +addBook(b: Book): void
        +addMember(m: Member): void
        +searchBook(title: String): Book
    }

    class Book {
        -title: String
        -author: String
        -ISBN: String
        -isIssued: Boolean
        +issue(): void
        +returnBook(): void
    }

    class Member {
        -name: String
        -memberID: String
        -borrowedBooks: List of Book
        +borrowBook(b: Book): Boolean
        +returnBook(b: Book): void
    }

    Library "1" *-- "many" Book : COMPOSITION contains
    Library "1" *-- "many" Member : COMPOSITION contains
    Member "1" --> "0..3" Book : ASSOCIATION borrows
```

### Diagram 3: Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input: Problem Statement] --> B[Analysis Phase]
    B --> C[Design Phase]
    C --> D[Implementation Phase]
    D --> E[Output: Working Software]

    B --> B1[Identify Nouns and Verbs]
    B --> B2[Filter Candidate Objects]
    B --> B3[Define Classes]

    C --> C1[Assign Attributes]
    C --> C2[Assign Methods]
    C --> C3[Establish Relationships]
    C --> C4[Draw UML Diagram]

    D --> D1[Code Classes]
    D --> D2[Instantiate Objects]
    D --> D3[Test Interactions]

    classDef phaseStyle fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef subStyle fill:#F1F8E9,stroke:#33691E,stroke-width:1px,color:#000

    class B,C,D phaseStyle
    class B1,B2,B3,C1,C2,C3,C4,D1,D2,D3 subStyle
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*List any **four** major steps involved in the Object-Oriented Analysis and Design (OOAD) methodology.*

**Model Answer (4 marks equivalent — 3 marks as per KTU key):**

The four major steps in OOAD are:

1. **Problem Domain Analysis:** Understanding the requirements by reading and parsing the problem statement.
2. **Object and Class Identification:** Identifying candidate objects (nouns) and grouping them into logical classes.
3. **Attribute and Method Identification:** Defining the data members (state) and member functions (behavior) of each class.
4. **Relationship Establishment:** Defining IS-A (Inheritance), HAS-A (Aggregation/Composition), and USES (Association) links between classes.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between Object-Oriented Analysis (OOA) and Object-Oriented Design (OOD) in the OOAD steps.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | OOA (Object-Oriented Analysis) | OOD (Object-Oriented Design) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Phase** | First step of OOAD | Second step of OOAD |
| **Focus** | *What* the system should do | *How* the system will be built |
| **Activities** | Identifying objects, classes, requirements | Defining attributes, methods, relationships, UML diagrams |
| **Output** | Use-case model, object model | Class diagram, sequence diagram |
| **Stakeholder** | Primarily analyst | Primarily designer/architect |

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **8-step methodology** of Object-Oriented Analysis and Design (OOAD) with a neat diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following problem: *"A university has departments. Each department offers courses. A course is taught by a professor and enrolled by students. A student can enroll in multiple courses."* Apply the OOAD steps to identify the **classes, attributes, methods, and relationships**, and draw the **UML class diagram**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

The 8-step OOAD methodology is the systematic procedure for transforming a problem statement into an executable object-oriented design.

**Step 1: Problem Domain Analysis** — Parse the problem description to extract requirements, scope, and constraints. Identify nouns (objects), verbs (methods), and adjectives (attributes/constraints). *Example:* In a banking system, nouns are Account, Customer, Transaction.

**Step 2: Candidate Object Identification** — Create a raw, unfiltered list of all nouns found in Step 1. *Example:* {Account, Customer, Balance, Name, Address, Transaction, Date, Amount}.

**Step 3: Object Filtering & Class Identification** — Remove objects outside the system boundary. Group similar objects. Merge `SavingsAccount` and `CurrentAccount` into a single `Account` class with a `type` attribute.

**Step 4: Attribute Identification** — For each class, list the data members. *Example:* `Account` has `accountNumber`, `balance`, `type`.

**Step 5: Method Identification** — For each class, list the member functions. *Example:* `Account.deposit(amount)`, `Account.withdraw(amount)`.

**Step 6: Relationship Establishment** — Identify IS-A, HAS-A, and USES links. *Example:* Customer *HAS-A* Account (Aggregation).

**Step 7: UML Class Diagram** — Draw the class diagram with three compartments (class name, attributes, methods) and relationship arrows with multiplicities.

**Step 8: Implementation, Testing & Refinement** — Write the code, perform unit testing, integrate modules, and refactor for optimization.

**Neat Diagram (refer SECTION 4, Diagram 1).**

**Mark Distribution:** Step 1-4: 3 marks | Step 5-8: 3 marks | Diagram: 1 mark.

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

**Applying the OOAD steps:**

**Step 1 (Nouns):** University, Department, Course, Professor, Student.
**Step 2 (Candidate Objects):** {University, Department, Course, Professor, Student, Name, ID, Title, Credits}.
**Step 3 (Class Filtering):**

$$\text{Final Classes} = \{\text{University}, \text{Department}, \text{Course}, \text{Professor}, \text{Student}\}$$

**Step 4 (Attributes):**

- `Department: {name, deptID, listOfCourses}`
- `Course: {courseID, title, credits}`
- `Professor: {name, empID, specialization}`
- `Student: {name, rollNo, enrolledCourses}`

**Step 5 (Methods):**

- `Department.addCourse(c: Course): void`
- `Course.assignProfessor(p: Professor): void`
- `Student.enroll(c: Course): Boolean`

**Step 6 (Relationships):**

| Relationship | Type |
| :--- | :--- |
| University *contains* Departments | **Composition** |
| Department *offers* Courses | **Composition** |
| Professor *teaches* Course | **Association** |
| Student *enrolls in* Course | **Many-to-Many Association** |

**Step 7 (UML Class Diagram):**

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class University {
        -name: String
        -departments: List of Department
    }
    class Department {
        -name: String
        -deptID: String
        -courses: List of Course
    }
    class Course {
        -courseID: String
        -title: String
        -credits: int
    }
    class Professor {
        -name: String
        -empID: String
    }
    class Student {
        -name: String
        -rollNo: String
    }
    University "1" *-- "many" Department : COMPOSITION
    Department "1" *-- "many" Course : COMPOSITION
    Professor "1" --> "many" Course : ASSOCIATION teaches
    Student "many" --> "many" Course : ASSOCIATION enrolls
```

**Step 8 (Implementation):** Translate classes into Java/C++ code (omitted for brevity but must be shown in exam for full 7 marks).

**Mark Distribution:** Class identification: 2 marks | Attributes & methods: 2 marks | Relationships: 2 marks | UML diagram: 1 mark.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** With a suitable real-world example, explain how **inheritance** and **polymorphism** are applied during the OOAD steps. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given the problem: *"A company has employees. Employees can be permanent or contractual. Permanent employees receive a monthly salary; contractual employees are paid hourly. The company calculates payroll for all employees."* Apply OOAD steps to design the solution, and write the **C++ code** for the same. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

**Inheritance** is identified in **Step 6 (Relationship Establishment)** when two classes share an *IS-A* relationship. *Example:* `Car IS-A Vehicle`, `SavingAccount IS-A Account`. It is implemented during **Step 8 (Implementation)** using the `extends` (Java) or `:` (C++) keyword.

**Polymorphism** is identified in **Step 5 (Method Identification)** when multiple classes require the *same method name* with *different behavior*. *Example:* All `Shape` subclasses (`Circle`, `Rectangle`, `Triangle`) need an `area()` method, but each calculates it differently.

**Real-world analogy — Payment System:**

- `Payment` (base class) defines `processPayment(): void` (abstract).
- `CreditCardPayment`, `UPIPayment`, `NetBankingPayment` (derived classes) *inherit* from `Payment` and *override* `processPayment()` to provide their own implementation.
- The client code calls `payment.processPayment()` *without knowing* the actual type — this is **runtime polymorphism** (dynamic binding).

**Step-wise mapping:**

| Step | Application |
| :--- | :--- |
| Step 5 | Identify that `processPayment` is a common behavior across multiple payment types. |
| Step 6 | Identify IS-A relationship: `CreditCardPayment IS-A Payment`. |
| Step 7 | UML diagram shows inheritance arrow and polymorphic method in italics. |
| Step 8 | Use `virtual` functions in C++ or `@Override` in Java. |

**Mark Distribution:** Definition with example: 3 marks | Step-wise mapping: 2 marks | Real-world analogy: 2 marks.

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1-2 (Nouns):** Company, Employee, Permanent, Contractual, Salary, HourlyRate, Payroll.

**Step 3 (Classes):**

$$\text{Classes} = \{\text{Employee}, \text{PermanentEmployee}, \text{ContractEmployee}, \text{Payroll}\}$$

**Step 4-5 (Attributes & Methods):**

- `Employee: {name, empID, calculatePay(): double}` (abstract)
- `PermanentEmployee: {monthlySalary: double, calculatePay(): double}`
- `ContractEmployee: {hoursWorked, hourlyRate, calculatePay(): double}`

**Step 6 (Relationships):** `PermanentEmployee IS-A Employee`, `ContractEmployee IS-A Employee` (Inheritance).

**Step 7-8 (C++ Implementation):**

```cpp
#include <iostream>
#include <string>
using namespace std;

class Employee {
protected:
    string name;
    int empID;
public:
    Employee(string n, int id) : name(n), empID(id) {}
    virtual double calculatePay() = 0;   // Pure virtual
    virtual void display() {
        cout << name << " (ID: " << empID << ") Pay: "
             << calculatePay() << endl;
    }
};

class PermanentEmployee : public Employee {
private:
    double monthlySalary;
public:
    PermanentEmployee(string n, int id, double sal)
        : Employee(n, id), monthlySalary(sal) {}
    double calculatePay() override {
        return monthlySalary;
    }
};

class ContractEmployee : public Employee {
private:
    int hoursWorked;
    double hourlyRate;
public:
    ContractEmployee(string n, int id, int h, double rate)
        : Employee(n, id), hoursWorked(h), hourlyRate(rate) {}
    double calculatePay() override {
        return hoursWorked * hourlyRate;
    }
};

int main() {
    Employee* e1 = new PermanentEmployee("Arun", 101, 50000);
    Employee* e2 = new ContractEmployee("Beena", 102, 160, 500);
    e1->display();
    e2->display();
    delete e1;
    delete e2;
    return 0;
}
```

**Mark Distribution:** Class design: 2 marks | Relationship identification: 1 mark | C++ code (abstract class, inheritance, polymorphism): 4 marks.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Skipping Step 1:** Students often jump directly to writing code. The examiner deducts **2-3 marks** if no analysis step is shown.
> 2. **Confusing Aggregation vs Composition:** Remember: **Composition** = strong lifecycle dependency (whole dies → part dies). Example: `Library` and `Book`. **Aggregation** = weak lifecycle. Example: `Department` and `Professor` (Professor survives even if Department is closed).
> 3. **Forgetting Multiplicities in UML:** Always write `1`, `many`, `0..3`, `*` on relationship arrows. Missing multiplicities = **-1 mark**.
> 4. **Mixing up IS-A and HAS-A:** `Car IS-A Vehicle` (Inheritance) ✓ ; `Car HAS-A Engine` (Composition) ✓ ; never the reverse.
> 5. **Not using `virtual` keyword in C++:** For polymorphic behavior in C++, the base class method **must** be declared `virtual`. Missing it = **-2 marks** in code-based questions.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Scheme:**
>
> - **The 8 Steps in OOAD are:** Problem Analysis → Object Identification → Class Filtering → Attribute Definition → Method Definition → Relationship Establishment → UML Diagram → Implementation & Testing.
> - **OOA** answers *what*; **OOD** answers *how*; **OOP** is the *coding*.
> - **Noun-based object identification** is the most commonly tested concept in KTU exams.
> - **Three relationship types** to remember: **IS-A** (Inheritance), **HAS-A** (Aggregation/Composition), **USES** (Association/Dependency).
> - **Composition** = filled diamond (◆); **Aggregation** = hollow diamond (◇); **Inheritance** = hollow triangle (△); **Association** = simple arrow.
> - **Multiplicities:** `1` (exactly one), `0..1` (optional), `*` or `many` (zero or more), `1..*` (at least one), `0..3` (bounded range).
> - **CRC Cards** (Class-Responsibility-Collaboration) are used in **Step 5** to assign methods to the correct class.
> - **UML Class Diagram** has three compartments: Class Name, Attributes, Methods.
> - **Visibility modifiers:** `+` public, `-` private, `#` protected.
> - **In C++ polymorphism:** Always use `virtual` in base class and `override` in derived class.
> - **In Java polymorphism:** Use `@Override` annotation and method overriding through inheritance.
> - **Encapsulation is enforced** in **Steps 4-5** by marking attributes as private and providing public getters/setters.
> - **Abstraction is designed** in **Step 7** using abstract classes (UML: class name in *italics*) and interfaces (UML: `<<interface>>` stereotype).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Common JDBC Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT633 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Common JDBC Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**JDBC (Java Database Connectivity)** is a Java API (Application Programming Interface) defined in the `java.sql` and `javax.sql` packages that provides a standard, vendor-neutral mechanism for Java applications to communicate with relational database management systems (RDBMS). The **Common JDBC Components** constitute the core set of interfaces, classes, and exceptions that collectively form the connectivity layer between a Java program and any SQL-compliant database.

These components are categorized as:
- **Core API** (`java.sql.*`) — Foundational interfaces such as `DriverManager`, `Connection`, `Statement`, and `ResultSet`.
- **Extension API** (`javax.sql.*`) — Advanced features such as `DataSource`, `RowSet`, and connection pooling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PECIT633 - Module 4):**  
> Students must be able to **list, classify, and explain the functional role of each common JDBC component**, trace the data flow between them, and demonstrate their use in a Java program that performs CRUD (Create, Read, Update, Delete) operations.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Universal Postal Service"

Imagine you are sending a registered parcel from India to a recipient in Japan. You do not personally fly the parcel. Instead, you interact with a **Postal System** that handles every stage:

| Postal System Stage | Equivalent JDBC Component | Functional Role |
|---|---|---|
| Visit the Post Office Counter | `DriverManager` | Selects the right communication driver |
| The Counter Clerk opens a register entry | `Connection` | Establishes a live session with the database |
| You fill out a dispatch form | `Statement` / `PreparedStatement` | Carries the SQL command |
| The form is dispatched via the postal network | Underlying Driver (JDBC-ODBC, Thin, etc.) | Vendor-specific transport |
| The recipient's reply letter arrives | `ResultSet` | Returns the tabular query result |
| A complaint is filed if the address is wrong | `SQLException` | Handles all database access errors |
| The receipt/stub is kept for records | `DatabaseMetaData` / `ResultSetMetaData` | Provides information about DB and result schema |

This **postal pipeline** analogy makes it instantly clear why JDBC needs so many specialized components — each one handles a single, well-defined responsibility in the overall communication chain.

## 1.3 Why These Components Matter

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Insight:** The Common JDBC Components collectively implement the **Gang-of-Four "Facade" and "Bridge" design patterns**. The `DriverManager` acts as a *Facade* hiding driver instantiation complexity, while the `Driver` interface acts as a *Bridge* between the generic JDBC API and vendor-specific implementations (Oracle, MySQL, PostgreSQL, etc.). This decouples the Java application from any specific database — the same Java code can connect to Oracle, MySQL, or PostgreSQL by simply changing the driver JAR and the connection URL.

### Key Terminology (with KTU-expected emphasis)

- **API** — A set of pre-written classes/interfaces provided by the JDK.
- **JDBC Driver** — A software component (usually a JAR file) that translates JDBC calls into the database's native protocol.
- **Database URL** — A standardized string that uniquely identifies a database connection. For example:
$$URL = "jdbc:" + \text{subprotocol} + ":" + \text{subname}$$
- **SQL** — Structured Query Language used to communicate with relational databases.
- **ResultSet Cursor** — A logical pointer that moves row-by-row through the returned query results.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Component-by-Component Analysis

The Common JDBC Components can be organized into **five functional categories**. Understanding this classification is the most important step to mastering the topic for KTU examinations.

### Category 1: Driver Management Layer

#### 2.1.1 `java.sql.DriverManager` (Class)
- **Why it exists:** The application does not need to know *which* driver to instantiate.
- **How it works:** It maintains a list of registered `Driver` classes. When `getConnection()` is called, it iterates through the registered drivers and uses the first one that can successfully connect to the given database URL.
- **Key method:** `public static Connection getConnection(String url, String user, String password)`
- **Registration mechanism:** In JDBC 4.0+, drivers are auto-loaded using the **Service Provider Mechanism** (the `META-INF/services/java.sql.Driver` file inside the driver JAR).

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** In KTU questions, when asked "How does the driver get loaded?", mention: *"Since JDBC 4.0, drivers are auto-registered using the Service Provider mechanism; the explicit `Class.forName("com.mysql.cj.jdbc.Driver")` call is no longer mandatory but is still commonly shown in textbooks."*

#### 2.1.2 `java.sql.Driver` (Interface)
- A vendor-specific implementation provided by every database vendor (Oracle, MySQL, etc.).
- Translates JDBC API calls into the database's native protocol.
- **Key method:** `Connection connect(String url, Properties info)`

### Category 2: Connection Layer

#### 2.1.3 `java.sql.Connection` (Interface)
- **Why:** Represents a single live session between the Java application and the database.
- **How:** All SQL execution and transaction control flows through this object.
- **Key methods:**
  - `Statement createStatement()` — Returns a Statement object.
  - `PreparedStatement prepareStatement(String sql)` — Returns a precompiled SQL statement.
  - `CallableStatement prepareCall(String sql)` — Returns a stored procedure call object.
  - `void setAutoCommit(boolean)` — Manages transaction boundaries.
  - `void commit()` / `void rollback()` — Transaction control.
  - `void close()` — Releases the connection (returns it to the pool if pooled).

### Category 3: SQL Execution Layer

#### 2.1.4 `java.sql.Statement` (Interface)
- Used for executing **static SQL** statements (no parameters).
- **Key methods:**
  - `ResultSet executeQuery(String sql)` — Returns a `ResultSet` (used with `SELECT`).
  - `int executeUpdate(String sql)` — Returns the row count affected (used with `INSERT`/`UPDATE`/`DELETE`).
  - `boolean execute(String sql)` — Returns `true` if the result is a `ResultSet`.
- **Limitation:** Vulnerable to **SQL Injection** because SQL is built by string concatenation.

#### 2.1.5 `java.sql.PreparedStatement` (Interface — extends `Statement`)
- Used for executing **precompiled, parameterized SQL** statements.
- **Why it's preferred:** The SQL is sent to the database, compiled once, and then executed multiple times with different parameters — improving performance and security.
- **Example pattern:** `INSERT INTO student VALUES (?, ?, ?)` — the `?` are placeholders.
- **Setter methods:** `setInt(int parameterIndex, int value)`, `setString(int, String)`, etc.
- **Security benefit:** Prevents SQL Injection because user input is treated as *data*, not as *executable SQL*.

#### 2.1.6 `java.sql.CallableStatement` (Interface — extends `PreparedStatement`)
- Used to invoke **stored procedures** stored inside the database server.
- Syntax uses curly braces: `{ call procedure_name(?, ?) }`

### Category 4: Result Processing Layer

#### 2.1.7 `java.sql.ResultSet` (Interface)
- Represents the **tabular result** of executing a `SELECT` query.
- Maintains a **cursor** that points to the current row; initially positioned *before* the first row.
- **Navigation methods:** `next()`, `previous()`, `absolute(int)`, `relative(int)`, `first()`, `last()`.
- **Getter methods:** `getInt(String columnName)`, `getString(String columnName)`, `getDouble(...)`, etc.
- **Default behavior:** A `ResultSet` is *forward-only* and *read-only*. To scroll or update, you must create the `Statement` with concurrency and type constants:
$$stmt = conn.createStatement(\text{ResultSet.TYPE\_SCROLL\_INSENSITIVE}, \text{ResultSet.CONCUR\_UPDATABLE})$$

### Category 5: Metadata & Exception Layer

#### 2.1.8 `java.sql.SQLException` (Class)
- A **checked exception** (must be either caught or declared with `throws`).
- Provides methods like `getMessage()`, `getErrorCode()`, `getSQLState()`.
- Supports exception chaining via `getNextException()`.

#### 2.1.9 `java.sql.DatabaseMetaData` (Interface)
- Obtained via `Connection.getMetaData()`.
- Provides information **about the database**: product name, version, supported features, schemas, tables.
- **Example:** `meta.getDatabaseProductName()` returns "MySQL" or "Oracle".

#### 2.1.10 `java.sql.ResultSetMetaData` (Interface)
- Obtained via `ResultSet.getMetaData()`.
- Provides information **about the columns** of the result: column count, column name, column type, display size.
- **Example:** `rsmd.getColumnCount()`, `rsmd.getColumnName(1)`.

### Category 6: Advanced (Extension API)

#### 2.1.11 `javax.sql.DataSource` (Interface)
- A **preferred alternative** to `DriverManager` in enterprise applications.
- Supports **connection pooling** and **distributed transactions** (JNDI-based lookup).
- Preferred because it follows the *Factory* and *Singleton* design patterns, decoupling the application from driver/URL details.

#### 2.1.12 `javax.sql.RowSet` (Interface)
- A *Java Bean*-compliant wrapper around `ResultSet`.
- Can be **disconnected** from the database, made serializable, and sent across the network.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

| # | Component | Type | Package | Primary Purpose | Key Method |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `DriverManager` | Class | `java.sql` | Driver selection & connection factory | `getConnection(url,user,pwd)` |
| 2 | `Driver` | Interface | `java.sql` | Vendor-specific DB protocol translator | `connect(url,info)` |
| 3 | `Connection` | Interface | `java.sql` | Live session with DB | `createStatement()`, `close()` |
| 4 | `Statement` | Interface | `java.sql` | Static SQL execution | `executeQuery()`, `executeUpdate()` |
| 5 | `PreparedStatement` | Interface | `java.sql` | Precompiled parameterized SQL | `setXxx()`, `executeQuery()` |
| 6 | `CallableStatement` | Interface | `java.sql` | Stored procedure execution | `execute()` |
| 7 | `ResultSet` | Interface | `java.sql` | Tabular result of `SELECT` | `next()`, `getXxx()` |
| 8 | `SQLException` | Class | `java.sql` | Database access error | `getMessage()`, `getErrorCode()` |
| 9 | `DatabaseMetaData` | Interface | `java.sql` | Info about the DB | `getDatabaseProductName()` |
| 10 | `ResultSetMetaData` | Interface | `java.sql` | Info about result columns | `getColumnCount()`, `getColumnName()` |
| 11 | `DataSource` | Interface | `javax.sql` | Pooled/JNDI-based connection | `getConnection()` |
| 12 | `RowSet` | Interface | `javax.sql` | Disconnected, serializable result | Inherits `ResultSet` methods |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Relevance:**  
> - **Enterprise Banking Systems** use `DataSource` + connection pooling to handle **thousands of transactions per second** with minimal resource overhead.  
> - **E-Commerce Platforms** use `PreparedStatement` exclusively to prevent SQL Injection attacks, which is mandated by **OWASP Top 10 (A03:2021 — Injection)**.  
> - **Data Analytics Dashboards** use `ResultSetMetaData` to dynamically render arbitrary query results as tables/graphs without hardcoding column names.  
> - **Stored Procedures** invoked through `CallableStatement` are essential in **legacy enterprise systems** (e.g., mainframe interfaces) where business logic is enforced at the DB layer.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown of the JDBC Workflow

The **JDBC Operational Sequence** is the sequence of component instantiations and method calls that a Java program must execute to communicate with a database. The KTU 2024 scheme frequently asks students to *"write a Java program to connect to a database and display all records"* — this section builds that workflow step-by-step.

## 3.1 The JDBC URL Decomposition

Before any component is used, the **Database URL** must be constructed. The general form is:

$$URL = "jdbc:" + \text{subprotocol} + ":" + \text{subname}$$

Common vendor-specific forms are:

$$URL_{\text{MySQL}} = "jdbc:mysql://\text{hostname}:\text{port}/\text{dbname}"$$

$$URL_{\text{Oracle}} = "jdbc:oracle:thin:@\text{hostname}:\text{port}:\text{SID}"$$

$$URL_{\text{PostgreSQL}} = "jdbc:postgresql://\text{hostname}:\text{port}/\text{dbname}"$$

The **subprotocol** (`mysql`, `oracle:thin`, `postgresql`) is what `DriverManager` uses to identify which registered `Driver` class can handle the URL.

## 3.2 The Six-Step Operational Sequence

### **STEP 1 — Load the Driver Class**

```java
Class.forName("com.mysql.cj.jdbc.Driver");
```

**Logic:** The `Class.forName()` call forces the JVM to load the driver's bytecode, which triggers the driver's **static initializer block** to register itself with `DriverManager`. Since JDBC 4.0, this step is *auto-performed* if the driver JAR is on the classpath, but the explicit call is still recommended in textbooks.

### **STEP 2 — Establish a Connection**

```java
String url  = "jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/studentdb";
String user = "root";
String pwd  = "password";
Connection conn = DriverManager.getConnection(url, user, pwd);
```

**Logic:** `DriverManager.getConnection()` iterates through the list of registered drivers. The first `Driver` whose `acceptsURL(url)` returns `true` and whose `connect()` succeeds is selected. The method returns a `Connection` object that represents the live database session.

**Connection URL parsing (analytical form):**

$$URL \rightarrow \text{DriverManager.scan} \rightarrow \text{Driver.acceptsURL} \rightarrow \text{Driver.connect} \rightarrow \text{Connection}$$

### **STEP 3 — Create a Statement / PreparedStatement**

```java
Statement stmt = conn.createStatement();
```

**Logic:** The `Connection` object is the *factory* for `Statement` objects. Each `Statement` is a conduit for sending one or more SQL commands to the database. The `Statement` does **not** contain the SQL — it is *passed in* during execution.

For precompiled SQL:

```java
String sql = "INSERT INTO student(rollno, name, mark) VALUES (?, ?, ?)";
PreparedStatement pstmt = conn.prepareStatement(sql);
pstmt.setInt(1, 101);
pstmt.setString(2, "Anu");
pstmt.setDouble(3, 89.5);
```

**Logic:** The `?` placeholders are **bind variables**. The database compiles the SQL once (the *prepare* phase), then binds the values during the *execute* phase — this avoids reparsing the SQL each time.

### **STEP 4 — Execute the SQL Command**

```java
// For SELECT
ResultSet rs = stmt.executeQuery("SELECT * FROM student");

// For INSERT / UPDATE / DELETE
int rowsAffected = stmt.executeUpdate("DELETE FROM student WHERE mark < 40");
```

**Logic:** The `Statement` sends the SQL string to the database through the open `Connection`. The database engine parses, optimizes, and executes the query. The result type depends on the SQL command:

| SQL Type | Method | Return Type |
|---|---|---|
| `SELECT` | `executeQuery()` | `ResultSet` |
| `INSERT` / `UPDATE` / `DELETE` | `executeUpdate()` | `int` (rows affected) |
| DDL (`CREATE` / `DROP`) | `executeUpdate()` | `int` (0) |
| Mixed / Unknown | `execute()` | `boolean` |

### **STEP 5 — Process the ResultSet**

```java
while (rs.next()) {
    int roll    = rs.getInt("rollno");
    String name = rs.getString("name");
    double mark = rs.getDouble("mark");
    System.out.println(roll + " " + name + " " + mark);
}
```

**Logic:** The `ResultSet` cursor starts *before the first row*. The `next()` method advances the cursor by one row; it returns `true` if a row exists, `false` when the cursor passes the last row. Each call to `getXxx(columnName)` extracts the value from the current cursor position and converts it to the requested Java type.

The analytical form of cursor traversal:

$$\text{Cursor} : r_0 \xrightarrow{next()} r_1 \xrightarrow{next()} r_2 \xrightarrow{next()} \cdots \xrightarrow{next()} \emptyset$$

where $r_i$ is the $i$-th row, and $\emptyset$ denotes the "no more rows" state.

### **STEP 6 — Close the Resources (LIFO Order)**

```java
rs.close();
stmt.close();
conn.close();
```

**Logic:** Database resources are *expensive* and *finite*. They must be released in **Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) order**: `ResultSet` first, then `Statement`, then `Connection`. In production code, this is wrapped in a `finally` block or, preferably, used with the **try-with-resources** statement introduced in Java 7:

```java
try (Connection conn = DriverManager.getConnection(url, user, pwd);
     Statement stmt = conn.createStatement();
     ResultSet rs   = stmt.executeQuery("SELECT * FROM student")) {
    while (rs.next()) {
        System.out.println(rs.getString("name"));
    }
} // All three resources auto-closed
```

## 3.3 The Complete Logical Flow (Mathematical View)

The JDBC data-flow can be summarized as a **directed pipeline**:

$$\text{App} \xrightarrow{\text{getConnection}} \text{DriverMgr} \xrightarrow{\text{selectDriver}} \text{Driver} \xrightarrow{\text{connect}} \text{Conn} \xrightarrow{\text{createStmt}} \text{Stmt} \xrightarrow{\text{executeQuery}} \text{RS} \rightarrow \text{App}$$

Every arrow denotes a **method invocation** that returns an object handle. The pipeline is strictly one-way during query execution; only error information flows backward via `SQLException`.

## 3.4 Component Interaction Summary Table

| From Component | Method Called | To Component | Returns | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Application | `Class.forName()` | `Driver` (Class) | `Class<?>` | Load & register driver |
| Application | `DriverManager.getConnection()` | `DriverManager` | `Connection` | Open DB session |
| `Connection` | `createStatement()` | `Connection` | `Statement` | Create SQL conduit |
| `Connection` | `prepareStatement(sql)` | `Connection` | `PreparedStatement` | Precompile SQL |
| `Statement` | `executeQuery(sql)` | `Statement` | `ResultSet` | Run `SELECT` |
| `Statement` | `executeUpdate(sql)` | `Statement` | `int` | Run DML/DDL |
| `ResultSet` | `next()` | `ResultSet` | `boolean` | Advance cursor |
| `ResultSet` | `getXxx(name)` | `ResultSet` | Java primitive/object | Extract column value |
| `Connection` | `getMetaData()` | `Connection` | `DatabaseMetaData` | Get DB info |
| `ResultSet` | `getMetaData()` | `ResultSet` | `ResultSetMetaData` | Get column info |
| Application | `close()` on each | All | `void` | Release resources |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 JDBC Component Interaction Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how each Common JDBC Component interacts with the others during a typical database read operation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    AppA[Java Application Code] --> LoadA[Step 1 Load Driver Class]
    LoadA --> RegA[Driver Auto Registers with DriverManager]
    RegA --> ConnA[Step 2 DriverManager getConnection]
    ConnA --> DriverA[Driver connect method]
    DriverA --> DB1[(Relational Database)]
    ConnA --> ConnObj[Step 3 Connection object returned]
    ConnObj --> StmtA[Step 4 createStatement]
    StmtA --> StmtObj[Statement object]
    ConnObj --> PrepA[Step 4 alt prepareStatement]
    PrepA --> PrepObj[PreparedStatement object]
    StmtObj --> ExecA[Step 5 executeQuery or executeUpdate]
    PrepObj --> ExecA
    ExecA --> RS1[ResultSet object returned]
    RS1 --> ProcA[Step 6 Process rows using next and getXxx]
    ProcA --> OutA[Display or process data]
    OutA --> CloseA[Step 7 Close ResultSet Statement Connection]
    ConnObj --> MetaA[DatabaseMetaData via getMetaData]
    RS1 --> MetaB[ResultSetMetaData via getMetaData]
    AppA --> ExcA[SQLException handler try catch]
    DriverA -.->|on error| ExcA
    ConnA -.->|on error| ExcA
    ExecA -.->|on error| ExcA
```

## 4.2 JDBC Architecture Layer Diagram

This second diagram shows the **layered architecture** of JDBC — from the application down to the physical database.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Layer 1 Java Application Layer]:::app
    L2[Layer 2 JDBC API Layer java sql and javax sql]:::api
    L3[Layer 3 JDBC Driver Manager Layer]:::mgr
    L4[Layer 4 JDBC Driver Layer vendor specific]:::drv
    L5[Layer 5 Database Native Protocol Layer]:::proto
    L6[(Layer 6 Physical Relational Database)]:::db

    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5
    L5 --> L6

    classDef app fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,color:#000
    classDef api fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    classDef mgr fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000
    classDef drv fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef proto fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#880E4F,color:#000
    classDef db fill:#212121,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
```

## 4.3 ResultSet Cursor State Machine

This diagram models the **cursor state transitions** of a `ResultSet` — a common KTU viva question.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> BeforeFirst
    BeforeFirst --> FirstRow: next returns true
    FirstRow --> SecondRow: next returns true
    SecondRow --> NthRow: next returns true
    NthRow --> AfterLast: next returns false
    AfterLast --> [*]: close called
    NthRow --> NthRow: previous moves backward
    AfterLast --> NthRow: previous returns true
    FirstRow --> BeforeFirst: previous returns false
```

## 4.4 Statement Hierarchy (Inheritance View)

The three statement types form an **inheritance chain** — a key UML relationship KTU expects students to draw.

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Statement {
        <<interface>>
        +executeQuery(String) ResultSet
        +executeUpdate(String) int
        +execute(String) boolean
        +close() void
    }
    class PreparedStatement {
        <<interface>>
        +setInt(int, int) void
        +setString(int, String) void
        +setDouble(int, double) void
```
