```mermaid
        +executeQuery() ResultSet
        +executeUpdate() int
    }
    class CallableStatement {
        <<interface>>
        +registerOutParameter(int, int) void
        +getXxx(int) primitive
    }

    Statement <|-- PreparedStatement : extends
    PreparedStatement <|-- CallableStatement : extends
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define JDBC. List any four common JDBC components and state their purpose.**
`[KTU University Exam — May 2024 | Model Question Paper]`

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**
JDBC (Java Database Connectivity) is a Java API in the `java.sql` package that enables Java applications to communicate with relational databases in a vendor-independent manner.

Four common JDBC components:
1. **`DriverManager`** — Selects the appropriate database driver and establishes a `Connection`. (0.75 mark)
2. **`Connection`** — Represents a live session with the database. (0.75 mark)
3. **`Statement`** — Carries static SQL statements to the database for execution. (0.75 mark)
4. **`ResultSet`** — Holds the tabular data returned by a `SELECT` query and provides cursor-based row access. (0.75 mark)

*(3 marks total)*

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between `Statement` and `PreparedStatement` in JDBC.**
`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 | Repeat Pattern]`

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

| Feature | `Statement` | `PreparedStatement` |
|---|---|---|
| SQL Type | Static SQL | Precompiled, parameterized SQL |
| Compilation | Compiled **every** time it is executed | Compiled **once**, executed many times |
| Parameters | Does **not** support `?` placeholders | Supports `?` placeholders bound via `setXxx()` |
| SQL Injection | Vulnerable | Protected (input treated as data) |
| Performance | Slower for repeated queries | Faster for repeated queries |
| Inheritance | Implements `Statement` directly | Extends `Statement` interface |

*(1 mark per row × 3 most relevant rows = 3 marks; alternatively 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for any 4 differences)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Every Part B question carries **14 marks**, has an **internal choice** (either OR), and is split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Full programs, diagrams, and justifications are required.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam — May 2024 | Module 4 Full Question]`

**(a)** Explain the JDBC architecture with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of `DriverManager` and `Connection` interfaces. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain any five common JDBC components other than `DriverManager` and `Connection` in detail. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**JDBC Architecture (4 marks for diagram + explanation):**

The JDBC architecture is a **two-tier** model consisting of:
- **JDBC API** — Provides the application-to-JDBC-Manager connection (`java.sql`).
- **JDBC Driver API** — Provides the JDBC-Manager-to-Driver connection.

The architecture has the following layers:
1. **Java Application** — The user code that issues JDBC calls.
2. **JDBC API** — `java.sql` and `javax.sql` packages.
3. **DriverManager** — Selects the correct driver from the registered list.
4. **JDBC Driver** — Vendor-specific implementation (Type-1, Type-2, Type-3, or Type-4).
5. **Database** — The actual RDBMS (Oracle, MySQL, etc.).

**Role of `DriverManager` (1.5 marks):**
- It is a **class** in `java.sql` that acts as a service for managing the set of JDBC drivers.
- It maintains a list of available drivers and chooses the most appropriate one to handle a given database URL.
- The key method `getConnection(url, user, pwd)` returns a `Connection` object.
- Since JDBC 4.0, drivers are auto-registered via the **Service Provider mechanism** using the file `META-INF/services/java.sql.Driver`.

**Role of `Connection` (1.5 marks):**
- It is an **interface** in `java.sql` that represents a single live session between the Java application and the database.
- It is the **factory** for `Statement`, `PreparedStatement`, and `CallableStatement` objects.
- It manages **transaction boundaries** via `setAutoCommit()`, `commit()`, and `rollback()`.
- It must be explicitly closed using `close()` to release database resources.

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

Any five of the following (1.4 marks each):

1. **`Statement`** — Interface used to execute static SQL. Methods: `executeQuery()`, `executeUpdate()`. Vulnerable to SQL Injection.

2. **`PreparedStatement`** — Sub-interface of `Statement`. Supports precompiled SQL with `?` placeholders. Provides security (prevents SQL Injection) and performance (compiled once).

3. **`ResultSet`** — Interface holding tabular query results. Uses a cursor (`next()`) and getter methods (`getInt()`, `getString()`). Default type is forward-only, read-only.

4. **`SQLException`** — Checked exception class for all DB access errors. Provides `getMessage()`, `getErrorCode()`, `getSQLState()`, and supports chaining via `getNextException()`.

5. **`DatabaseMetaData`** — Interface obtained from `Connection.getMetaData()`. Provides DB-level info: product name, version, supported features, available schemas.

6. **`ResultSetMetaData`** — Interface obtained from `ResultSet.getMetaData()`. Provides column-level info: count, name, type, display size — essential for building dynamic queries.

7. **`DataSource`** — Interface in `javax.sql`. Preferred over `DriverManager` in enterprise apps because it supports **connection pooling** and JNDI lookup.

8. **`RowSet`** — Interface in `javax.sql`. A JavaBean wrapper around `ResultSet` that can be disconnected, serialized, and sent over the network.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 | Module 4 Full Question]`

**(a)** Write a complete Java program to connect to a MySQL database `studentdb`, retrieve all rows from the `student` table (columns: `rollno`, `name`, `mark`), and display them. Use `PreparedStatement` for the query. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the differences among `Statement`, `PreparedStatement`, and `CallableStatement`. When would you prefer each one? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

```java
import java.sql.*;

public class StudentFetchDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        String url  = "jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/studentdb";
        String user = "root";
        String pwd  = "password";

        // try-with-resources auto-closes Connection, PreparedStatement, ResultSet
        try (Connection conn = DriverManager.getConnection(url, user, pwd)) {

            String sql = "SELECT rollno, name, mark FROM student WHERE mark > ?";
            PreparedStatement pstmt = conn.prepareStatement(sql);
            pstmt.setDouble(1, 50.0);

            try (ResultSet rs = pstmt.executeQuery()) {
                while (rs.next()) {
                    int r     = rs.getInt("rollno");
                    String n  = rs.getString("name");
                    double m  = rs.getDouble("mark");
                    System.out.println(r + "  " + n + "  " + m);
                }
            }
        } catch (ClassNotFoundException e) {
            System.out.println("Driver not found: " + e.getMessage());
        } catch (SQLException e) {
            System.out.println("SQL Error: " + e.getMessage());
        }
    }
}
```

**Mark Allocation:**
- Imports + Class declaration — 1 mark
- `getConnection()` call + URL — 1.5 marks
- `prepareStatement()` + `setDouble()` — 1.5 marks
- `executeQuery()` + `while(rs.next())` + getters — 2 marks
- Exception handling + `try-with-resources` — 1 mark

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

| Feature | `Statement` | `PreparedStatement` | `CallableStatement` |
|---|---|---|---|
| Inheritance | Base interface | Extends `Statement` | Extends `PreparedStatement` |
| SQL Type | Static SQL | Precompiled parameterized SQL | Stored procedure call |
| Compilation | Every execution | Once, then reused | Once (on the DB server) |
| Placeholders | Not supported | `?` placeholders | `?` positional and `?=` registered OUT params |
| SQL Injection | Vulnerable | Safe | Safe |
| Use Case | One-time, simple queries | Repeated queries with parameters | Complex business logic in DB stored procs |
| Performance | Slowest | Fastest for repeated SQL | Best for heavy DB-side processing |
| Method to call | `executeQuery(sql)` | `executeQuery()` (no args) | `execute()` and `registerOutParameter()` |

**When to use each (1 mark total — 0.5 mark for two correct scenarios):**
- Use **`Statement`** for simple, one-time DDL or ad-hoc queries where SQL injection is not a concern.
- Use **`PreparedStatement`** for any production application that runs **repeated queries with different parameters** — virtually all CRUD operations.
- Use **`CallableStatement`** when invoking **stored procedures/functions** for performance-critical or DB-enforced business logic (e.g., banking transactions, audit logging).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks (Read Carefully):**
> 1. **Confusing `DriverManager` (class) with `Driver` (interface).** Examiners deduct **1 mark** if students write "`DriverManager` is an interface" — it is a **class**.
> 2. **Forgetting to close `ResultSet` / `Statement` / `Connection`.** A common 1-mark deduction in the 7-mark program questions. Always use **try-with-resources** (Java 7+).
> 3. **Wrong import statements.** Use `java.sql.*`, not `javax.sql.*`, for the core components. The advanced components like `DataSource` and `RowSet` are in `javax.sql`.
> 4. **Writing `executeQuery()` for `INSERT` / `UPDATE` / `DELETE`.** Use `executeUpdate()` for DML. This is a **favourite trap question**.
> 5. **Claiming `PreparedStatement` "prevents all SQL Injection."** The technically correct phrasing is: *"It prevents SQL Injection **when used correctly with bind variables**, because user input is sent to the database as data, not as executable SQL."*
> 6. **Drawing a wrong JDBC architecture diagram.** The correct order is **Application → JDBC API → DriverManager → Driver → Database**. Reversing "Driver" and "DriverManager" loses 1 mark.
> 7. **Confusing `DatabaseMetaData` with `ResultSetMetaData`.** Remember: `DatabaseMetaData` describes the **database**; `ResultSetMetaData` describes the **columns of a query result**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Memorize These Points**

- **JDBC** = Java Database Connectivity, an API in `java.sql` and `javax.sql`.
- **12 Core Components:** `DriverManager`, `Driver`, `Connection`, `Statement`, `PreparedStatement`, `CallableStatement`, `ResultSet`, `SQLException`, `DatabaseMetaData`, `ResultSetMetaData`, `DataSource`, `RowSet`.
- **`DriverManager` is a CLASS** (not interface); all others are interfaces.
- **6-Step Workflow:** Load Driver → `getConnection()` → `createStatement()`/`prepareStatement()` → `executeQuery()`/`executeUpdate()` → Process `ResultSet` → `close()`.
- **Use `PreparedStatement` over `Statement`** in all production code — it prevents SQL Injection and improves performance.
- **`executeQuery()` → returns `ResultSet` (for `SELECT`).**
- **`executeUpdate()` → returns `int` rows affected (for `INSERT`/`UPDATE`/`DELETE`/DDL).**
- **`execute()` → returns `boolean` (used for mixed/unknown SQL).**
- **Cursor starts *before* the first row**; first call to `next()` positions it on row 1.
- **`getXxx(columnIndex)`** is faster than **`getXxx(columnName)`** (count starts at 1).
- **Always close in LIFO order:** ResultSet → Statement → Connection.
- **Use try-with-resources** (Java 7+) to avoid resource leaks.
- **Auto-loading since JDBC 4.0:** `Class.forName()` is no longer strictly required, but textbooks still show it.
- **JDBC URL format:** `jdbc:<subprotocol>:<subname>` (e.g., `jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/studentdb`).
- **Two architectures:** Two-Tier (App ↔ DB) and Three-Tier (App ↔ Middleware ↔ DB).
- **Inheritance chain:** `Statement` ← `PreparedStatement` ← `CallableStatement`.
- **`DataSource` (in `javax.sql`)** is preferred over `DriverManager` for enterprise apps — supports connection pooling.
- **`RowSet`** = disconnected, serializable `ResultSet`; useful for sending data over the network.
- **`SQLException` is a checked exception** — must be caught or declared with `throws`.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Common JDBC Components**
> *Prepared in alignment with KTU 2024 Scheme, Revised Bloom's Taxonomy, and ESE valuation patterns.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Connection Establishment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT633 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Connection Establishment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Connection Establishment** in Java Networking refers to the systematic, protocol-driven procedure of creating a reliable, bi-directional communication channel between a **Client process** and a **Server process** using the **TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)** over an **IP (Internet Protocol)** network. It is implemented in Java primarily through the `java.net.ServerSocket` and `java.net.Socket` classes located in the standard library package.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Connection Establishment is the **Module 4** foundation topic that bridges *Object Oriented Programming* with *Distributed Computing*. The Java API encapsulates the entire underlying **OSI Transport Layer (Layer 4)** handshake (the TCP three-way handshake) inside high-level class constructors, allowing the programmer to instantiate a connection with a single line of code.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Telephone Call"

Imagine two people, **Alice (Server)** and **Bob (Client)**, who want to talk:

1. Alice sets up a telephone at her desk and **waits for calls** → This is `new ServerSocket(port)`.
2. Alice announces her **phone number (port)** publicly → This is binding to a well-known port.
3. Bob **picks up his phone and dials** Alice's number → This is `new Socket(serverIP, serverPort)`.
4. Alice's phone **rings**; she **picks it up** (accepts the call) → This is `serverSocket.accept()`.
5. Now both have an **open duplex line** → This is the established `Socket` object.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why TCP and not UDP for connection establishment?**
> TCP is **connection-oriented** (reliable, ordered, error-checked). UDP is **connectionless** (fire-and-forget). The phrase "Connection Establishment" strictly applies to TCP-style socket programming.

## 1.3 Key Terminology

- **Port Number**: A **16-bit unsigned integer** (range **0 – 65535**) used to identify a specific process on a host. Ports **0–1023** are *well-known* (reserved for system services like HTTP on port **80**, HTTPS on **443**).
- **IP Address**: A **32-bit (IPv4)** or **128-bit (IPv6)** logical address identifying a network interface.
- **Socket**: An endpoint of a two-way communication link. A Java `Socket` object binds a local IP+Port to a remote IP+Port.
- **ServerSocket**: A *listening* socket that waits for incoming client connection requests.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Connection Establishment

Connection establishment in Java is a **three-step cooperation** between the application layer, transport layer, and network layer.

### Pillar 1 — The Server Side (Listener)

The server must first **bind** to a port and **listen**. In Java, this is achieved via:

| Step | Java Code | Underlying OS Action |
|------|-----------|----------------------|
| 1 | `ServerSocket ss = new ServerSocket(8080);` | Kernel binds the server process to TCP port **8080**. |
| 2 | `Socket s = ss.accept();` | Kernel blocks and waits for an incoming SYN packet. |
| 3 | `ss.close();` | Releases the port binding. |

### Pillar 2 — The Client Side (Initiator)

The client actively **opens** a connection by attempting to connect to the server's IP+Port.

| Step | Java Code | Underlying OS Action |
|------|-----------|----------------------|
| 1 | `Socket s = new Socket("127.0.0.1", 8080);` | Kernel sends a SYN packet to the server. |
| 2 | (Constructor returns) | SYN-ACK received; ACK sent → Connection established. |

### Pillar 3 — The TCP Three-Way Handshake (Transport Layer Magic)

This is the **core hidden mechanism** that makes connection establishment reliable. Even though Java hides it, KTU examiners frequently test it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three-Way Handshake Sequence:**
> 1. **SYN** → Client sends Synchronize packet (SEQ = x).
> 2. **SYN-ACK** → Server acknowledges (ACK = x+1) and sends its own SYN (SEQ = y).
> 3. **ACK** → Client acknowledges server's SYN (ACK = y+1).
> 
> After this, both sides are in **ESTABLISHED** state.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula / Method Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Java networking has no physics formulas, but the KTU examiner rewards the **Constructor + Method signatures** as "formulas". Memorize these.

| Class | Constructor / Method | Signature | Purpose |
|-------|----------------------|-----------|---------|
| `ServerSocket` | Constructor | `ServerSocket(int port)` | Creates server socket bound to `port`. |
| `ServerSocket` | Constructor | `ServerSocket(int port, int backlog)` | Specifies max queue length. |
| `ServerSocket` | `accept()` | `Socket accept()` | **Blocks** until a client connects. |
| `ServerSocket` | `close()` | `void close()` | Closes the listening socket. |
| `ServerSocket` | `getInetAddress()` | `InetAddress getInetAddress()` | Returns local address. |
| `Socket` | Constructor | `Socket(String host, int port)` | **Connects** to remote server. |
| `Socket` | `getInputStream()` | `InputStream getInputStream()` | Returns stream to **read** from socket. |
| `Socket` | `getOutputStream()` | `OutputStream getOutputStream()` | Returns stream to **write** to socket. |
| `Socket` | `getInetAddress()` | `InetAddress getInetAddress()` | Returns **remote** IP. |
| `Socket` | `getPort()` | `int getPort()` | Returns **remote** port. |
| `Socket` | `getLocalPort()` | `int getLocalPort()` | Returns local assigned port. |
| `InetAddress` | Static | `InetAddress.getByName(String host)` | Resolves a hostname to an IP. |
| `InetAddress` | Static | `InetAddress.getLocalHost()` | Returns local host's address. |

## 2.3 Engineering Utility

This mechanism powers:
- **Web Servers** (Apache Tomcat, Jetty) — accept HTTP connections on port **80/443**.
- **Database Servers** (MySQL on **3306**, PostgreSQL on **5432**).
- **Chat Applications** (WhatsApp backend, Slack).
- **Microservices Communication** (gRPC, REST APIs over TCP).
- **IoT Device Management** (MQTT over TCP port **1883**).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Walkthrough — How `new Socket(...)` Establishes a Connection

When a programmer writes `Socket s = new Socket("localhost", 8080);`, the JVM executes the following exhaustive internal sequence:

### Step 1 — DNS Resolution
The JVM calls the system's resolver to convert `"localhost"` to its IP address.

$$
\text{localhost} \xrightarrow{\text{getByName()}} \texttt{127.0.0.1}
$$

This is the *loopback* address used for testing on the same machine.

### Step 2 — Local Socket Allocation
The OS kernel allocates an **ephemeral port** (a free port from the dynamic range **49152 – 65535**) for the client side.

$$
\text{Local Endpoint} = (\texttt{127.0.0.1}, \; 49152 \leq p_c \leq 65535)
$$

### Step 3 — Transmission Control Block (TCB) Creation
The kernel creates a **TCB** for both client and server sides. The client's TCB state transitions:
$$
\text{CLOSED} \rightarrow \text{SYN\_SENT}
$$

### Step 4 — SYN Packet Transmission
The client kernel sends a TCP segment with:
- **SYN flag = 1**
- Initial sequence number **SEQ = x** (randomly chosen for security)

$$
\text{Packet}: \quad \text{SYN}, \; \text{SEQ} = x, \; \text{src} = (\text{local}, p_c), \; \text{dst} = (\texttt{127.0.0.1}, 8080)
$$

### Step 5 — Server's accept() Unblocks
On the server, the kernel (which was blocking the thread inside `accept()`) receives the SYN, creates a TCB in `SYN_RCVD` state, and responds.

### Step 6 — SYN-ACK Packet
The server sends back:
- **SYN flag = 1**, **ACK flag = 1**
- **SEQ = y** (server's own initial sequence)
- **ACK = x + 1** (acknowledging client's SYN)

$$
\text{Packet}: \quad \text{SYN-ACK}, \; \text{SEQ} = y, \; \text{ACK} = x+1
$$

### Step 7 — Client's Final ACK
The client receives the SYN-ACK. Its TCB moves to **ESTABLISHED** and sends a final ACK:

$$
\text{Packet}: \quad \text{ACK}, \; \text{SEQ} = x+1, \; \text{ACK} = y+1
$$

### Step 8 — Server's TCB Transitions to ESTABLISHED
The server's `accept()` method finally returns a **fully constructed `Socket` object** to the Java application.

$$
\text{CLOSED} \rightarrow \text{SYN\_SENT} \rightarrow \text{ESTABLISHED} \quad \text{(Client)}
$$

$$
\text{LISTEN} \rightarrow \text{SYN\_RCVD} \rightarrow \text{ESTABLISHED} \quad \text{(Server)}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Complete Server-Client Lifecycle

| Phase | Server Action | Client Action | State After |
|-------|---------------|---------------|-------------|
| **Bind** | `new ServerSocket(8080)` → binds port 8080 | — | Server: `LISTEN` |
| **Initiate** | — | `new Socket("server", 8080)` | Client: `SYN_SENT` |
| **SYN** | Receives SYN | Sends SYN | Server: `SYN_RCVD` |
| **SYN-ACK** | Sends SYN-ACK | Receives SYN-ACK | Client: `ESTABLISHED` |
| **ACK** | Receives ACK → `accept()` returns | Sends final ACK | Server: `ESTABLISHED` |
| **Data Transfer** | `s.getInputStream().read()` | `s.getOutputStream().write()` | Both: `ESTABLISHED` |
| **Termination** | `s.close()` (initiates FIN) | `s.close()` (sends FIN-ACK) | Both: `CLOSED` |

## 3.3 Mathematical View of Port Allocation

The total number of possible connections between a client and server on a single host is bounded by the **ephemeral port range**:

$$
N_{\text{concurrent}} = 65535 - 1024 = 64511 \text{ possible simultaneous client sockets per server port}
$$

For multi-server scenarios, the total possible concurrent sockets per host (considering all ports):
$$
N_{\text{total}} = 2^{16} = 65536 \text{ ports}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In practice, the OS imposes a lower limit (e.g., Linux's `net.ipv4.ip_local_port_range`) to reserve ports for other services.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Connection Establishment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Application Layer: Java Code] --> B[ServerSocket ss = new ServerSocket(8080)]
    B --> C[Kernel Binds to Port 8080]
    C --> D[State: LISTEN]
    D --> E[serverSocket.accept() Called - Thread Blocks]
    E --> F[Client Application Calls new Socket host port]
    F --> G[Kernel Allocates Ephemeral Port for Client]
    G --> H[Send SYN Packet]
    H --> I[Server Kernel Receives SYN]
    I --> J[Server Sends SYN-ACK]
    J --> K[Client Sends Final ACK]
    K --> L[Server TCB Enters ESTABLISHED State]
    L --> M[accept() Returns Socket Object]
    M --> N[Data Transfer via getInputStream and getOutputStream]
    N --> O[Either Side Closes Socket]
    O --> P[4-Way FIN Handshake for Termination]
    P --> Q[State: CLOSED]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style B fill:#fff4e1
    style F fill:#e1f5ff
    style H fill:#ffe1e1
    style J fill:#ffe1e1
    style K fill:#ffe1e1
    style N fill:#e1ffe1
    style Q fill:#f0f0f0
```

## 4.2 TCP Three-Way Handshake Timing Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client
    participant S as Server

    Note over S: ServerSocket created, State = LISTEN
    Note over C: Socket constructor invoked
    C->>S: SYN SEQ=x
    Note over S: accept() thread unblocks, State = SYN_RCVD
    S-->>C: SYN SEQ=y ACK=x+1
    Note over C: State transitions to ESTABLISHED
    C->>S: ACK SEQ=x+1 ACK=y+1
    Note over S: State transitions to ESTABLISHED
    Note over C,S: accept() returns Socket object on server side
    C->>S: DATA (via getOutputStream)
    S->>C: DATA (via getOutputStream)
    C->>S: FIN
    S->>C: FIN-ACK
    C->>S: ACK
```

## 4.3 Class Relationship Diagram

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class ServerSocket {
        -int port
        -InetAddress bindAddr
        +ServerSocket(int port)
        +ServerSocket(int port, int backlog)
        +Socket accept()
        +void close()
        +InetAddress getInetAddress()
        +int getLocalPort()
    }
    class Socket {
        -InetAddress address
        -int port
        -InputStream inputStream
        -OutputStream outputStream
        +Socket(String host, int port)
        +InputStream getInputStream()
        +OutputStream getOutputStream()
        +void close()
        +InetAddress getInetAddress()
        +int getPort()
    }
    class InetAddress {
        +static InetAddress getByName(String host)
        +static InetAddress getLocalHost()
        +String getHostAddress()
        +String getHostName()
    }
    ServerSocket ..> Socket : creates via accept()
    Socket ..> InetAddress : uses for addressing
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`**
**Q: Define Connection Establishment in Java. List the two main classes used for TCP-based connection establishment.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Connection Establishment** is the process of creating a reliable, bi-directional communication channel between a client and a server using the TCP protocol in Java.

The two main classes from the `java.net` package used for TCP-based connection establishment are:

1. **`java.net.ServerSocket`** — Used on the **server side** to listen for incoming client connection requests on a specified port.
2. **`java.net.Socket`** — Used on the **client side** to actively initiate a connection to a server. The same class is also used on the server side to represent the established connection returned by `accept()`.

These classes abstract the underlying TCP three-way handshake (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK) and provide simple constructors and methods for network communication. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]`**
**Q: What is the role of the `accept()` method in `ServerSocket`? Explain its blocking nature.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The `accept()` method plays a critical role in the **server-side connection establishment** process:

1. **Role**: It is the method that actually **accepts an incoming client connection request** and returns a new `Socket` object representing the established connection between the server and that specific client.

2. **Signature**: `public Socket accept() throws IOException`

3. **Blocking Nature**: The `accept()` method **blocks the calling thread** indefinitely until a client attempts to connect. The server thread remains suspended in a waiting state at the OS kernel level. Once a client's SYN packet arrives and the three-way handshake completes, `accept()` returns the connected `Socket` object, and the thread resumes execution. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE pattern: Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Both Question A and Question B are **alternatives** — answer ANY ONE.

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the TCP three-way handshake mechanism used during connection establishment between a Java client and server. Illustrate with a neat diagram and describe the state transitions on both sides.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write a complete Java program to establish a TCP connection between a client and server. The client should send a message *"Hello Server"* and the server should respond with *"Hello Client"*. Use `ServerSocket` and `Socket` classes.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A (a) — 7 Marks

The **TCP three-way handshake** is the underlying protocol mechanism that ensures a reliable connection is established between a Java client and server. It consists of three steps:

**Step 1: SYN (Synchronize)**
The client's kernel, upon executing `new Socket(serverIP, serverPort)`, sends a TCP segment with the **SYN flag set to 1** and an initial sequence number **SEQ = x**. The client's Transmission Control Block (TCB) transitions from `CLOSED` to `SYN_SENT` state.

**Step 2: SYN-ACK (Synchronize-Acknowledge)**
The server's kernel receives the SYN. Its TCB moves to `SYN_RCVD` state. The server responds with a segment having both **SYN and ACK flags set**, with **SEQ = y** (its own random initial sequence) and **ACK = x + 1**.

**Step 3: ACK (Acknowledge)**
The client receives the SYN-ACK. Its TCB transitions to `ESTABLISHED`. The client sends a final segment with **ACK flag set** and **ACK = y + 1**. The server's TCB also transitions to `ESTABLISHED`, and `accept()` returns the `Socket` object to the Java application.

**State Transition Summary:**

| Side | State Sequence |
|------|----------------|
| Client | `CLOSED` → `SYN_SENT` → `ESTABLISHED` |
| Server | `LISTEN` → `SYN_RCVD` → `ESTABLISHED` |

**Diagram:**

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client
    participant S as Server
    C->>S: SYN SEQ=x
    S-->>C: SYN SEQ=y ACK=x+1
    C->>S: ACK SEQ=x+1 ACK=y+1
    Note over C,S: Both sides now ESTABLISHED
```

This handshake ensures both sides agree on initial sequence numbers and that the connection is reliable. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A (b) — 7 Marks

```java
// Server.java
import java.net.*;
import java.io.*;

public class Server {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            // Step 1: Create ServerSocket bound to port 6789
            ServerSocket serverSocket = new ServerSocket(6789);
            System.out.println("Server started. Waiting for client...");
            
            // Step 2: Accept incoming client connection (blocks until client connects)
            Socket socket = serverSocket.accept();
            System.out.println("Client connected!");
            
            // Step 3: Set up input stream to read from client
            BufferedReader in = new BufferedReader(
                new InputStreamReader(socket.getInputStream()));
            
            // Step 4: Set up output stream to send to client
            PrintWriter out = new PrintWriter(
                socket.getOutputStream(), true);
            
            // Step 5: Read message from client
            String clientMessage = in.readLine();
            System.out.println("Client says: " + clientMessage);
            
            // Step 6: Send response to client
            out.println("Hello Client");
            
            // Step 7: Close connections
            socket.close();
            serverSocket.close();
        } catch (IOException e) {
            System.out.println("Server error: " + e.getMessage());
        }
    }
}
```

```java
// Client.java
import java.net.*;
import java.io.*;

public class Client {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            // Step 1: Create Socket - this initiates the connection
            Socket socket = new Socket("localhost", 6789);
            System.out.println("Connected to server!");
            
            // Step 2: Set up output stream to send to server
            PrintWriter out = new PrintWriter(
                socket.getOutputStream(), true);
            
            // Step 3: Set up input stream to read from server
            BufferedReader in = new BufferedReader(
                new InputStreamReader(socket.getInputStream()));
            
            // Step 4: Send message to server
            out.println("Hello Server");
            
            // Step 5: Read response from server
            String serverResponse = in.readLine();
            System.out.println("Server says: " + serverResponse);
            
            // Step 6: Close connection
            socket.close();
        } catch (IOException e) {
            System.out.println("Client error: " + e.getMessage());
        }
    }
}
```

**Execution Steps:**
1. Compile both files: `javac Server.java` and `javac Client.java`.
2. Run the server first: `java Server` (it blocks at `accept()`).
3. Run the client in another terminal: `java Client`.
4. The TCP three-way handshake occurs transparently during `new Socket("localhost", 6789)`.
5. Output: Server prints "Client says: Hello Server"; Client prints "Server says: Hello Client". **(7 marks)**

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative to Question A*

**`[KTU University Exam – Expected 2024 Pattern]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between `ServerSocket` and `Socket` classes in Java. Explain their constructors and important methods with signatures.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write a Java program where the server accepts a number from the client, computes its factorial, and sends the result back to the client. Use proper connection establishment and closing.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B (a) — 7 Marks

| Feature | `ServerSocket` | `Socket` |
|---------|----------------|----------|
| **Purpose** | Waits for incoming client requests | Initiates connection to server / represents established connection |
| **Side** | Server-side only | Both client and server side |
| **Key Constructor** | `ServerSocket(int port)` | `Socket(String host, int port)` |
| **Key Method** | `accept()` — blocks until client connects | `getInputStream()` / `getOutputStream()` |
| **Lifecycle** | Lives as long as the server is running | Created per client connection |
| **Port Binding** | Binds to a specific port (e.g., 8080) | Uses an ephemeral port automatically |

**Important Constructors and Methods:**

**`ServerSocket`:**
- `ServerSocket(int port)` — Creates a server socket bound to the specified port.
- `ServerSocket(int port, int backlog)` — Specifies maximum queue length for incoming connections.
- `Socket accept()` — Listens for and accepts a connection; blocks until a client connects.
- `void close()` — Closes the server socket.
- `InetAddress getInetAddress()` — Returns the local address.
- `int getLocalPort()` — Returns the port number on which the socket is listening.

**`Socket`:**
- `Socket(String host, int port)` — Creates a socket and connects to the specified host and port (triggers three-way handshake).
- `Socket(InetAddress address, int port)` — Same as above but with `InetAddress`.
- `InputStream getInputStream()` — Returns input stream for reading data.
- `OutputStream getOutputStream()` — Returns output stream for writing data.
- `void close()` — Closes the socket connection.
- `InetAddress getInetAddress()` — Returns the remote IP address.
- `int getPort()` — Returns the remote port number.
- `int getLocalPort()` — Returns the local port number. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B (b) — 7 Marks

```java
// FactorialServer.java
import java.net.*;
import java.io.*;

public class FactorialServer {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            ServerSocket serverSocket = new ServerSocket(5000);
            System.out.println("Factorial Server started on port 5000");
            
            Socket socket = serverSocket.accept();
            System.out.println("Client connected");
            
            BufferedReader in = new BufferedReader(
                new InputStreamReader(socket.getInputStream()));
            PrintWriter out = new PrintWriter(
                socket.getOutputStream(), true);
            
            // Receive number from client
            int number = Integer.parseInt(in.readLine());
            System.out.println("Received number: " + number);
            
            // Compute factorial
            long factorial = 1;
            for (int i = 1; i <= number; i++) {
                factorial *= i;
            }
            
            // Send result back
            out.println("Factorial of " + number + " is: " + factorial);
            
            socket.close();
            serverSocket.close();
        } catch (IOException e) {
            e.printStackTrace();
        }
    }
}
```

```java
// FactorialClient.java
import java.net.*;
import java.io.*;

public class FactorialClient {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            Socket socket = new Socket("localhost", 5000);
            System.out.println("Connected to server");
            
            PrintWriter out = new PrintWriter(
                socket.getOutputStream(), true);
            BufferedReader in = new BufferedReader(
                new InputStreamReader(socket.getInputStream()));
            
            // Send number to server
            out.println("5");
            
            // Receive factorial result
            String result = in.readLine();
            System.out.println("Server response: " + result);
            
            socket.close();
        } catch (IOException e) {
            e.printStackTrace();
        }
    }
}
```

**Output (Client side):**
```
Connected to server
Server response: Factorial of 5 is: 120
```

**Explanation of Connection Flow:**
1. **Server** creates `ServerSocket(5000)` and calls `accept()` (blocks).
2. **Client** calls `new Socket("localhost", 5000)` which triggers the TCP three-way handshake.
3. **Server's** `accept()` returns a connected `Socket` object.
4. Both sides exchange data via `getInputStream()` and `getOutputStream()`.
5. **Both** call `close()` to terminate the connection (four-way FIN handshake). **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to start the server first** — The `Socket` constructor on the client will throw a `ConnectException` if the server is not listening. Always mention execution order: **Server → Client**.
> 
> 2. **Not handling `IOException`** — All networking code **must** be inside a `try-catch` block or declare `throws IOException`. Examiners deduct **2 marks** for missing exception handling.
> 
> 3. **Confusing `getPort()` and `getLocalPort()`** — On a `Socket` object, `getPort()` returns the **remote** port, while `getLocalPort()` returns the **local** (client's ephemeral) port. This is a frequent trick question.
> 
> 4. **Confusing `ServerSocket.accept()` with `Socket.accept()`** — `accept()` exists **only on `ServerSocket`**, not on `Socket`. Writing `socket.accept()` will cause compilation failure.
> 
> 5. **Skipping the three-way handshake in theory questions** — Even in code-based questions, the KTU 2024 rubric awards **1–2 marks** for explaining the underlying TCP handshake.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Connection Establishment:**

- **Connection Establishment** uses **TCP** (reliable, connection-oriented) — not UDP.
- **Server side**: Create `ServerSocket(port)` → call `accept()` (blocks) → obtain `Socket`.
- **Client side**: Create `Socket(host, port)` → constructor triggers three-way handshake.
- **Three-Way Handshake**: `SYN` → `SYN-ACK` → `ACK`; both sides reach `ESTABLISHED` state.
- **Port range**: **0–65535**; well-known ports are **0–1023**; ephemeral ports are **49152–65535**.
- **Key methods**: `accept()`, `getInputStream()`, `getOutputStream()`, `close()`, `getInetAddress()`, `getPort()`, `getLocalPort()`.
- **Class location**: All networking classes are in `java.net` package.
- **InetAddress**: Use `InetAddress.getByName("hostname")` to resolve DNS; `getLocalHost()` for local.
- **Execution order**: Always start the **server first**, then the client.
- **Exception**: Always wrap networking code in `try-catch (IOException)`.
- **Blocking nature**: `accept()`, `read()`, and `accept()`-based loops block the calling thread.
- **Termination**: Closing the `Socket` initiates a 4-way FIN handshake, transitioning both sides to `CLOSED` state.
- **Difference table**: `ServerSocket` listens; `Socket` communicates — remember this distinction.
- **Real-world examples**: HTTP (port 80), HTTPS (443), MySQL (3306), PostgreSQL (5432), custom apps (any port > 1024).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SQL Fundamentals [For Projects Only] - Creating and Executing Basic SQL Queries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECIT633 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** SQL Fundamentals [For Projects Only] - Creating and Executing Basic SQL Queries # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **SQL (Structured Query Language)** is a standardized, declarative, domain-specific language used to define, manipulate, retrieve, and control data held in a **Relational Database Management System (RDBMS)**. As per the KTU 2024 OOP syllabus, SQL is treated as the *persistence-layer companion* to object-oriented programming — the bridge that allows Java/C++/Python objects to be stored, retrieved, and managed in relational stores. **A Basic SQL Query** is a syntactically valid statement (typically beginning with verbs such as `SELECT`, `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE`, or `CREATE`) that the database engine parses, optimizes, and executes against one or more tables. > [!NOTE] > **Key Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 4):** > Students are expected to be able to *(a)* create database tables using DDL, *(b)* insert and modify records using DML, and *(c)* write retrieval queries using DQL — primarily for **mini-project / final-year project back-end work**. ## 1.2 The Five Sub-Languages of SQL | Sub-Language | Full Form | Core Verbs | Purpose | |--------------|-----------|------------|---------| | **DDL** | Data Definition Language | `CREATE`, `ALTER`, `DROP`, `TRUNCATE` | Defines *schema/structure* | | **DML** | Data Manipulation Language | `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE` | Manipulates *rows* in existing tables | | **DQL** | Data Query Language | `SELECT` | Retrieves *data* | | **DCL** | Data Control Language | `GRANT`, `REVOKE` | Manages *permissions* | | **TCL** | Transaction Control Language | `COMMIT`, `ROLLBACK`, `SAVEPOINT` | Manages *transactions* | > [!IMPORTANT] > Some textbooks fold DQL inside DML. KTU examiners generally treat `SELECT` as **DQL**, distinct from DML. ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Excel Workbook is a Database" Model Imagine an entire **municipal library system**: - The **library building** is the *Database Server* (MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle). - Each **section rack** (Fiction, Science, History) is a *Table*. - Every **book** on a rack is a *Row (Record/Tuple)*. - The **fields printed on the book's catalog card** (Title, Author, ISBN, Year) are *Columns (Attributes)*. - The **librarian's rulebook** — *"New books must have a Title, ISBN cannot be repeated, deleted books cannot be recovered without a backup"* — is the *Schema + Constraints*. Now, when a student walks up and says *"Give me all Science books published after 2020, sorted by author name"*, the librarian translates that English sentence into a structured request. **SQL is exactly that translation grammar** — it is the formal, unambiguous language the database engine understands. ```sql SELECT title, author FROM books WHERE section = 'Science' AND year > 2020 ORDER BY author ASC; ``` Every keyword above is one of the librarian's standard phrases; every value is the data the student provides. > [!TIP] > **Why "Declarative" matters:** You tell SQL *what* you want, not *how* to fetch it. The database's *query optimizer* decides the execution strategy — index scan, full table scan, hash join, etc. As an OOP developer, you focus on **correctness**, not on low-level access paths. ## 1.4 Quick-Glance Boundary Definitions - **Database** → A collection of related tables, views, indexes, and stored procedures (e.g., `university_db`). - **Schema** → The *blueprint* describing the structure of tables, columns, datatypes, and constraints. - **Table** → A 2-D grid of rows × columns, modelling one *entity* (e.g., `Student`). - **Row / Record / Tuple** → A single instance of the entity (e.g., one student's data). - **Column / Field / Attribute** → A named property with a fixed datatype (e.g., `roll_no INT`). - **Primary Key (PK)** → A column (or set of columns) whose values **uniquely** identify each row. **Not NULL allowed.** - **Foreign Key (FK)** → A column whose values reference the PK of another table — enforces **referential integrity**. > [!WARNING] > KTU examiners frequently deduct marks if a student confuses **DDL** with **DML**, or treats `SELECT` as DML. Memorize the table in §1.2 cold. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 The SQL Command Lifecycle When a `SELECT` query is submitted, the RDBMS processes it in a strict pipeline: 1. **Parsing** → The engine checks SQL syntax against the grammar. Invalid SQL is rejected with a *parse error*. 2. **Binding / Resolution** → Table and column names are resolved against the catalog (data dictionary). 3. **Optimization** → The **Query Optimizer** (cost-based) chooses the best execution plan. 4. **Execution** → The engine physically reads pages, applies filters, joins, and returns a *result set*. 5. **Return** → The result set is forwarded to the client (your JDBC/ODBC/Python driver). > [!NOTE] > **Logical vs. Physical Order of `SELECT` Evaluation** > Although the SQL text is written in one order, the engine conceptually executes the clauses in a different order — this is the #1 source of confusion in KTU exam answers. | Written Order | Keyword | Logical Execution Order | |---------------|---------|--------------------------| | 1 | `SELECT` | 5 (almost last) | | 2 | `FROM` | 1 (first) | | 3 | `WHERE` | 2 | | 4 | `GROUP BY` | 3 | | 5 | `HAVING` | 4 | | 6 | `ORDER BY` | 6 | | 7 | `LIMIT` | 7 | ## 2.2 Standard ANSI SQL Data Types (Cheat Sheet) | Category | Common Types | Typical Use | |----------|--------------|-------------| | Exact Numeric | `INT`, `SMALLINT`, `BIGINT`, `DECIMAL(p,s)`, `NUMERIC` | IDs, counts, money | | Approximate Numeric | `FLOAT`, `REAL`, `DOUBLE PRECISION` | Scientific data | | Character | `CHAR(n)`, `VARCHAR(n)`, `TEXT` | Names, descriptions | | Date/Time | `DATE`, `TIME`, `DATETIME`, `TIMESTAMP` | Audit columns | | Boolean | `BOOLEAN` (true/false) | Flags | | Binary | `BLOB`, `VARBINARY` | Images, files | ## 2.3 Integrity Constraints (High-Yield for KTU) A **constraint** is a rule enforced by the DBMS to guarantee data validity. | Constraint | Syntax Example | What It Does | |------------|----------------|--------------| | `PRIMARY KEY` | `id INT PRIMARY KEY` | Unique + NOT NULL identifier | | `FOREIGN KEY` | `FOREIGN KEY(dept_id) REFERENCES Department(id)` | Enforces parent-child link | | `UNIQUE` | `email VARCHAR(100) UNIQUE` | No duplicate values allowed | | `NOT NULL` | `name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL` | Disallows missing values | | `CHECK` | `CHECK (age >= 18)` | Custom boolean rule | | `DEFAULT` | `status VARCHAR(10) DEFAULT 'Active'` | Auto-fills value if omitted | | `AUTO_INCREMENT` (MySQL) / `SERIAL` (PostgreSQL) / `IDENTITY` (SQL Server) | `id INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY` | Generates sequential values | ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Quick Reference | Operation | SQL Template | Notes | |-----------|--------------|-------| | Create DB | `CREATE DATABASE dbname;` | One-time setup | | Use DB | `USE dbname;` (MySQL) | Selects active schema | | Create Table | `CREATE TABLE t (col datatype CONSTRAINT, ...);` | DDL | | Insert Row | `INSERT INTO t (cols) VALUES (vals);` | DML | | Read All | `SELECT * FROM t;` | DQL | | Conditional Read | `SELECT cols FROM t WHERE condition;` | Operators: `=,<>,<,>,<=,>=,BETWEEN,IN,LIKE,IS NULL` | | Update | `UPDATE t SET col=val WHERE condition;` | Always include `WHERE`! | | Delete | `DELETE FROM t WHERE condition;` | Always include `WHERE`! | | Sort | `ORDER BY col ASC\vert DESC` | | | Limit Rows | `LIMIT n OFFSET m;` (MySQL) / `FETCH FIRST n ROWS ONLY` (ANSI) | | | Aggregate | `SELECT COUNT(*), SUM(col), AVG(col), MIN(col), MAX(col) FROM t;` | Often used with `GROUP BY` | | Group Filter | `GROUP BY col HAVING aggregate_condition;` | `WHERE` filters rows, `HAVING` filters groups | | Join | `SELECT ... FROM A [INNER/LEFT/RIGHT/FULL] JOIN B ON A.x=B.y;` | Combines rows from 2+ tables | | Alias | `SELECT col AS nickname FROM t;` | Cosmetic rename | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** In OOP projects, each `class` typically maps to one SQL `TABLE`; each object instance maps to a `ROW`; the `getters/setters` map to `SELECT`/`UPDATE` queries. JDBC's `PreparedStatement` is the standard bridge. ## 2.5 Why This Matters in Real Engineering - **Banking** systems use SQL transactions (`COMMIT`/`ROLLBACK`) to ensure atomic money transfers. - **E-commerce** sites use complex `JOIN` queries across `Customers`, `Orders`, `Products`, `Payments` tables. - **OOP Mini-Projects** at KTU (e.g., Library Management, Student Portal, Inventory System) invariably use a `MySQL`/`PostgreSQL` backend — **strong SQL is the single most common reason projects get full internal marks.** # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Query Construction & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > Although SQL is not a *derivation-heavy* topic, the logical construction of correct queries follows strict step-by-step rules. We illustrate the methodology with a worked **Student–Course–Enrollment** schema — the same example KTU examiners often use. ## 3.1 The Reference Schema We will use a three-table university schema throughout. **Table 1 — `Student`** | Column | Datatype | Constraints | |--------|----------|-------------| | `roll_no` | INT | **PRIMARY KEY**, AUTO_INCREMENT | | `name` | VARCHAR(50) | NOT NULL | | `dob` | DATE | — | | `cgpa` | DECIMAL(3,2) | CHECK (cgpa BETWEEN 0 AND 10) | | `dept_id` | INT | FOREIGN KEY → `Department(dept_id)` | **Table 2 — `Course`** | Column | Datatype | Constraints | |--------|----------|-------------| | `course_id` | VARCHAR(10) | **PRIMARY KEY** | | `title` | VARCHAR(100) | NOT NULL | | `credits` | INT | CHECK (credits BETWEEN 1 AND 6) | **Table 3 — `Enrollment` (Junction Table)** | Column | Datatype | Constraints | |--------|----------|-------------| | `roll_no` | INT | FK → `Student(roll_no)` | | `course_id` | VARCHAR(10) | FK → `Course(course_id)` | | `grade` | CHAR(2) | — | | **PRIMARY KEY (roll_no, course_id)** | | Composite PK | ## 3.2 Step-by-Step: DDL — `CREATE TABLE` **Step 1 — Create the database first.** ```sql CREATE DATABASE university_db; USE university_db; ``` *Logic:* Before defining any object, we must tell the server "this is the namespace we are working in." **Step 2 — Create the parent table `Department` (referenced by others).** ```sql CREATE TABLE Department ( dept_id INT PRIMARY KEY AUTO_INCREMENT, dept_name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL UNIQUE, hod VARCHAR(50) ); ``` *Logic:* Foreign keys require the referenced table to exist, so parent tables are always created first. **Step 3 — Create `Student`, referencing `Department`.** ```sql CREATE TABLE Student ( roll_no INT PRIMARY KEY AUTO_INCREMENT, name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL, dob DATE, cgpa DECIMAL(3,2) CHECK (cgpa >= 0 AND cgpa <= 10), dept_id INT, FOREIGN KEY (dept_id) REFERENCES Department(dept_id) ON DELETE SET NULL ON UPDATE CASCADE ); ``` *Logic:* - `AUTO_INCREMENT` lets the DB auto-generate sequential roll numbers. - `CHECK` enforces CGPA validity. - `FOREIGN KEY ... ON DELETE SET NULL` — if a department is deleted, students are not removed; their `dept_id` is set to `NULL`. - `ON UPDATE CASCADE` — if `dept_id` changes in the parent, it propagates here. **Step 4 — Create `Course`.** ```sql CREATE TABLE Course ( course_id VARCHAR(10) PRIMARY KEY, title VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL, credits INT CHECK (credits BETWEEN 1 AND 6) ); ``` **Step 5 — Create the junction table `Enrollment`.** ```sql CREATE TABLE Enrollment ( roll_no INT, course_id VARCHAR(10), grade CHAR(2), PRIMARY KEY (roll_no, course_id), FOREIGN KEY (roll_no) REFERENCES Student(roll_no) ON DELETE CASCADE, FOREIGN KEY (course_id) REFERENCES Course(course_id) ON DELETE CASCADE ); ``` *Logic:* A junction (associative) table resolves a *many-to-many* relationship — one student takes many courses; one course has many students. ## 3.3 Step-by-Step: DML — Insert, Update, Delete **Step 1 — Insert into `Department`.** ```sql INSERT INTO Department (dept_name, hod) VALUES ('Computer Science', 'Dr. Iyer'), ('Electrical', 'Dr. Menon'), ('Mechanical', 'Dr. Pillai'); ``` *Logic:* Column list explicit → safer than `INSERT ... VALUES (...)` without column names. **Step 2 — Insert into `Student` (multi-row insert).** ```sql INSERT INTO Student (name, dob, cgpa, dept_id) VALUES ('Anjali Raj', '2003-05-12', 8.74, 1), ('Rahul Dev', '2002-11-30', 7.10, 1), ('Sneha Pillai','2003-02-20', 9.25, 2), ('Arjun Nair', '2002-08-08', 6.50, 3); ``` *Logic:* `roll_no` is omitted → DB fills via `AUTO_INCREMENT`. **Step 3 — Update — raise all CS students' CGPA by 0.5 (capped at 10).** ```sql UPDATE Student SET cgpa = LEAST(cgpa + 0.5, 10) WHERE dept_id = 1; ``` *Logic:* Always `WHERE` an `UPDATE`. `LEAST()` (MySQL) ensures the cap. **Step 4 — Delete — remove students with CGPA below 6.0.** ```sql DELETE FROM Student WHERE cgpa < 6.0; ``` *Logic:* The `Enrollment` rows for these students are auto-removed due to `ON DELETE CASCADE`. ## 3.4 Step-by-Step: DQL — Building a Complex `SELECT` **Problem:** *List the names of all Computer Science students who scored grade 'A' in any course, sorted alphabetically.* **Construction methodology — KTU-recommended 5-step build:** **Step 1 — Identify the columns needed:** `Student.name`, `Department.dept_name`, `Course.title`, `Enrollment.grade`. **Step 2 — Identify the tables needed:** `Student`, `Department`, `Course`, `Enrollment`. **Step 3 — Identify the joins:** - `Student.dept_id = Department.dept_id` - `Student.roll_no = Enrollment.roll_no` - `Enrollment.course_id = Course.course_id` **Step 4 — Identify the filters:** `dept_name = 'Computer Science' AND grade = 'A'`. **Step 5 — Identify the ordering:** `ORDER BY Student.name ASC`. **Final Query:** ```sql SELECT S.name AS student_name, C.title AS course_title, E.grade FROM Student S JOIN Department D ON S.dept_id = D.dept_id JOIN Enrollment E ON S.roll_no = E.roll_no JOIN Course C ON E.course_id = C.course_id WHERE D.dept_name = 'Computer Science' AND E.grade = 'A' ORDER BY S.name ASC; ``` *Logic of execution (logical order — refer to §2.1 table):* 1. `FROM` → Cartesian product of 4 tables (conceptually). 2. `JOIN ON` → Restrict to matching rows. 3. `WHERE` → Apply dept + grade filters. 4. *(No `GROUP BY` here.)* 5. `SELECT` → Project only `name`, `title`, `grade`. 6. `ORDER BY` → Sort by name. ## 3.5 Step-by-Step: Aggregate + `GROUP BY` + `HAVING` **Problem:** *Find the course titles in which more than 2 students have scored grade 'A'.* ```sql SELECT C.title, COUNT(*) AS total_A_students FROM Course C JOIN Enrollment E ON C.course_id = E.course_id WHERE E.grade = 'A' GROUP BY C.course_id, C.title HAVING COUNT(*) > 2 ORDER BY total_A_students DESC; ``` *Logic:* - `WHERE` filters **rows** *before* grouping (eliminates non-'A' rows). - `GROUP BY` collapses remaining rows per course. - `HAVING` filters **groups** *after* aggregation. > [!WARNING] > A classic KTU mistake: writing `WHERE COUNT(*) > 2`. **`WHERE` cannot reference aggregate functions** — that is precisely what `HAVING` is for. ## 3.6 Step-by-Step: Subquery Example **Problem:** *Find the names of students who are not enrolled in any course.* ```sql SELECT name FROM Student WHERE roll_no NOT IN (SELECT roll_no FROM Enrollment); ``` *Logic:* Inner query returns the set of *enrolled* roll numbers; outer query picks the complement. ## 3.7 The Complete Analytical Construction Matrix | User Intent | Clause Order to Write | Execution Order | |-------------|----------------------|-----------------| | "Get me data" | `SELECT` | 5 | | "From where?" | `FROM` | 1 | | "Which rows?" | `WHERE` | 2 | | "Group similar" | `GROUP BY` | 3 | | "Filter groups" | `HAVING` | 4 | | "Sort output" | `ORDER BY` | 6 | | "Show only first N" | `LIMIT` | 7 | > [!TIP] > **KTU Valuation Tip:** When explaining a complex query in an exam, **always list the logical execution order** in your answer. Examiners reward clarity of *process* (Application level, Bloom's L3–L4). # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — SQL Sub-Language Taxonomy ```mermaid flowchart TD A0["SQL Structured Query Language"] --> B1["DDL Data Definition Language"] A0 --> B2["DML Data Manipulation Language"] A0 --> B3["DQL Data Query Language"] A0 --> B4["DCL Data Control Language"] A0 --> B5["TCL Transaction Control Language"] B1 --> C1["CREATE TABLE"] B1 --> C2["ALTER TABLE"] B1 --> C3["DROP TABLE"] B1 --> C4["TRUNCATE TABLE"] B2 --> D1["INSERT"] B2 --> D2["UPDATE"] B2 --> D3["DELETE"] B3 --> E1["SELECT with WHERE"] B3 --> E2["SELECT with JOIN"] B3 --> E3["SELECT with GROUP BY"] B4 --> F1["GRANT"] B4 --> F2["REVOKE"] B5 --> G1["COMMIT"] B5 --> G2["ROLLBACK"] B5 --> G3["SAVEPOINT"] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Logical Query Execution Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR Q1["User writes SELECT statement"] --> Q2["Parser checks syntax"] Q2 --> Q3["Binder resolves tables and columns"] Q3 --> Q4["Optimizer generates execution plan"] Q4 --> Q5["Executor reads pages from disk or buffer pool"] Q5 --> Q6["FROM stage builds initial row source"] Q6 --> Q7["WHERE filters rows"] Q7 --> Q8["GROUP BY partitions rows into groups"] Q8 --> Q9["HAVING filters groups"] Q9 --> Q10["SELECT projects columns and computes expressions"] Q10 --> Q11["ORDER BY sorts the result set"] Q11 --> Q12["LIMIT and OFFSET trims rows"] Q12 --> Q13["Result set returned to client application"] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Schema (Block-Level Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph SCHEMA["University Database Schema university_db"] T1["Table Department
PK dept_id
dept_name UNIQUE
hod"] T2["Table Student
PK roll_no AUTO_INCREMENT
name NOT NULL
dob cgpa
FK dept_id to Department"] T3["Table Course
PK course_id
title NOT NULL
credits CHECK 1 to 6"] T4["Table Enrollment
PK composite roll_no course_id
grade
FK roll_no to Student
FK course_id to Course"] end T1 -->|dept_id 1 to N| T2 T2 -->|roll_no 1 to N| T4 T3 -->|course_id 1 to N| T4 ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — CRUD Operations Data Flow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant App as OOP Application Java or Python participant JDBC as Driver PreparedStatement participant DB as MySQL or PostgreSQL Engine participant FS as Disk Storage InnoDB App->>JDBC: INSERT VALUES JDBC->>DB: Send parameterized SQL DB->>DB: Parse and bind DB->>DB: Optimize DB->>FS: Write row to B+ tree page FS-->>DB: Acknowledge write DB-->>JDBC: Rows affected = 1 JDBC-->>App: Return int status App->>JDBC: SELECT FROM JDBC->>DB: Send query DB->>FS: Read matching pages FS-->>DB: Return data blocks DB-->>JDBC: ResultSet JDBC-->>App: Iterate rows ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]` **Differentiate between DDL and DML. Give one example statement for each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | DDL — Data Definition Language | DML — Data Manipulation Language | |--------|-------------------------------|----------------------------------| | Purpose | Defines / alters **schema** (structure) | Manipulates **data** (rows) | | Effect | Auto-committed in most RDBMS | Transactional, can be rolled back | | Examples | `CREATE`, `ALTER`, `DROP`, `TRUNCATE` | `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE` | | Sample | `CREATE TABLE Dept (id INT PRIMARY KEY, name VARCHAR(50));` | `INSERT INTO Dept (id,name) VALUES (1,'CS');` | | Operates on | Table / database objects | Rows inside existing tables | *Marking scheme (typical KTU):* 1 mark definition each + 1 mark example → **3/3**. --- ### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]` **Explain the purpose of the `PRIMARY KEY` and `FOREIGN KEY` constraints. How do they differ?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - A **PRIMARY KEY** uniquely identifies each row in a table. It is implicitly `NOT NULL` and `UNIQUE`. Each table can have **only one** primary key (which may be composite across multiple columns). - A **FOREIGN KEY** is a column (or set of columns) whose values must match the primary key of another (or the same) table. It enforces **referential integrity** — you cannot insert a child row pointing to a non-existent parent. - **Key difference:** PK identifies a row *within* its own table; FK creates a *relationship* between two tables. *Example:* ```sql CREATE TABLE Course ( course_id VARCHAR(10) PRIMARY KEY, -- PK inside Course title VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL ); CREATE TABLE Enrollment ( roll_no INT, course_id VARCHAR(10), FOREIGN KEY (course_id) REFERENCES Course(course_id) -- FK ); ``` *Marking scheme:* 1 mark each for PK and FK definitions + 1 mark for difference → **3/3**. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question B-A. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model, 14 Marks]` **Consider the following scenario for a college library management system.** Design the database schema with the necessary tables (`Book`, `Member`, `Issue`) including **all relevant constraints**. Write SQL `CREATE TABLE` statements for each, then perform the following operations: **(a) [7 marks]** Insert at least 3 sample rows into each table. Write a `SELECT` query using `JOIN`, `WHERE`, and `ORDER BY` to display the **book title, member name, and issue date** for all books currently issued, sorted by issue date in **descending** order. **(b) [7 marks]** Write an `UPDATE` query to mark a fine of ₹10 per book for any issue whose return date is `NULL` and whose issue date is **older than 30 days** from today. Also, write a query using `GROUP BY` and `HAVING` to list **member names who have issued more than 2 books**. **Model Solution:** **(a) Schema + Inserts + JOIN query [7 marks]** ```sql CREATE TABLE Book ( book_id INT PRIMARY KEY AUTO_INCREMENT, title VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL, author VARCHAR(50), price DECIMAL(7,2) CHECK (price > 0) ); CREATE TABLE Member ( member_id INT PRIMARY KEY AUTO_INCREMENT, name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL, email VARCHAR(100) UNIQUE, join_date DATE DEFAULT (CURRENT_DATE) ); CREATE TABLE Issue ( issue_id INT PRIMARY KEY AUTO_INCREMENT, book_id INT, member_id INT, issue_date DATE NOT NULL, return_date DATE, fine DECIMAL(6,2) DEFAULT 0, FOREIGN KEY (book_id) REFERENCES Book(book_id) ON DELETE CASCADE, FOREIGN KEY (member_id) REFERENCES Member(member_id) ON DELETE CASCADE ); ``` *Sample Inserts:* ```sql INSERT INTO Book (title, author, price) VALUES ('Database System Concepts','Korth',650.00), ('Clean Code','Robert Martin',450.00), ('The Pragmatic Programmer','Hunt & Thomas',500.00); INSERT INTO Member (name, email) VALUES ('Anjali Raj','anjali@ktu.in'), ('Rahul Dev','rahul@ktu.in'), ('Sneha P.','sneha@ktu.in'); INSERT INTO Issue (book_id, member_id, issue_date) VALUES (1, 1, CURDATE() - INTERVAL 40 DAY), (2, 2, CURDATE() - INTERVAL 10 DAY), (3, 3, CURDATE() - INTERVAL 5 DAY); ``` *JOIN query:* ```sql SELECT B.title, M.name, I.issue_date FROM Issue I JOIN Book B ON I.book_id = B.book_id JOIN Member M ON I.member_id = M.member_id WHERE I.return_date IS NULL ORDER BY I.issue_date DESC; ``` *Step-by-step logic for marks:* 1. Correct schema with PK, FK, datatypes — **2 marks** 2. At least 3 valid `INSERT` rows per table — **2 marks** 3. Correct `JOIN` with `ON` conditions — **1 mark** 4. Correct `WHERE` filter for current issues — **1 mark** 5. Correct `ORDER BY ... DESC` — **1 mark** **(b) UPDATE + GROUP BY/HAVING query [7 marks]** ```sql UPDATE Issue SET fine = 10 WHERE return_date IS NULL AND issue_date < (CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL 30 DAY); ``` *Step-by-step logic:* 1. Correct `UPDATE ... SET` — **1 mark** 2. Correct `WHERE` with `IS NULL` — **1 mark** 3. Correct date arithmetic (`CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL 30 DAY`) — **1 mark** *GROUP BY / HAVING query:* ```sql SELECT M.name, COUNT(*) AS books_issued FROM Member M JOIN Issue I ON M.member_id = I.member_id GROUP BY M.member_id, M.name HAVING COUNT(*) > 2 ORDER BY books_issued DESC; ``` *Step-by-step logic:* 4. Correct `JOIN` to Issue — **1 mark** 5. Correct `GROUP BY` on member identifier — **1 mark** 6. Correct `HAVING COUNT(*) > 2` (not `WHERE`!) — **1 mark** 7. Output formatting / ordering — **1 mark** **Total: 14 / 14.** --- ### Question B-B. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model, 14 Marks — INTERNAL CHOICE]` **For a university examination system** with tables `Student(roll_no, name, dept_id)`, `Department(dept_id, dept_name)`, and `Marks(roll_no, subject, marks)`: **(a) [7 marks]** Write the `CREATE TABLE` statements with **all primary-key, foreign-key, NOT NULL, and CHECK constraints**. Insert at least 3 rows into `Marks`. Write a `SELECT` query to find the **topper (highest marks) in each subject**, showing subject name and marks. **(b) [7 marks]** Write a query using a **subquery** to display names of students who have scored **more than the average marks** in their department. Also, explain the **logical order of execution** of a `SELECT` statement with `WHERE`, `GROUP BY`, `HAVING`, and `ORDER BY`. **Model Solution:** **(a) Schema + Topper Query [7 marks]** ```sql CREATE TABLE Department ( dept_id INT PRIMARY KEY, dept_name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL UNIQUE ); CREATE TABLE Student ( roll_no INT PRIMARY KEY, name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL, dept_id INT NOT NULL, FOREIGN KEY (dept_id) REFERENCES Department(dept_id) ); CREATE TABLE Marks ( roll_no INT, subject VARCHAR(50), marks INT CHECK (marks BETWEEN 0 AND 100), PRIMARY KEY (roll_no, subject), FOREIGN KEY (roll_no) REFERENCES Student(roll_no) ON DELETE CASCADE ); ``` *Inserts:* ```sql INSERT INTO Department VALUES (1,'CS'),(2,'EC'),(3,'ME'); INSERT INTO Student VALUES (101,'Anjali',1),(102,'Rahul',1),(103,'Sneha',2); INSERT INTO Marks VALUES (101,'DBMS',92),(101,'OS',85), (102,'DBMS',88),(102,'OS',90), (103,'DBMS',75); ``` *Topper-per-subject query:* ```sql SELECT subject, marks FROM Marks WHERE (subject, marks) IN ( SELECT subject, MAX(marks) FROM Marks GROUP BY subject ) ORDER BY subject; ``` *Mark split:* 1. PK + FK + NOT NULL definitions — **2 marks** 2. CHECK constraints — **1 mark** 3. Valid inserts — **1 mark** 4. Subquery (or alternative correlated subquery) for topper — **2 marks** 5. Correct projection + ordering — **1 mark** **(b) Subquery + Logical Order Explanation [7 marks]** *Above-average query:* ```sql SELECT DISTINCT S.name FROM Student S JOIN Marks M ON S.roll_no = M.roll_no WHERE M.marks > ( SELECT AVG(M2.marks) FROM Marks M2 JOIN Student S2 ON M2.roll_no = S2.roll_no WHERE S2.dept_id = S.dept_id ); ``` *Logical Order Explanation (must include all four clauses):* > [!IMPORTANT] > **Logical Execution Order of SELECT:** > > 1. **`FROM`** — Identify source tables (`Student`, `Marks`). > 2. **`WHERE`** — Filter **individual rows** before any aggregation. > 3. **`GROUP BY`** — Collapse remaining rows into groups (one per category). > 4. **`HAVING`** — Filter **groups** based on aggregate conditions. > 5. **`SELECT`** — Compute expressions and project columns. > 6. **`ORDER BY`** — Sort the final result set. > 7. **`LIMIT / OFFSET`** — Trim to required number of rows. *Mark split:* 1. Correct subquery construction (correlated / scalar) — **3 marks** 2. Correct join + `DISTINCT` — **1 mark** 3. Logical order explanation listing all 4+ stages — **3 marks** **Total: 14 / 14.** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > > 1. **`WHERE` vs `HAVING` confusion** — biggest single reason for losing 2-3 marks per answer. Filter rows with `WHERE`, filter groups with `HAVING`. > 2. **Forgetting `WHERE` in `UPDATE` / `DELETE`** — will mass-update or wipe the entire table. Examiners *will* deduct a full mark. > 3. **Foreign-key tables out of order** — referencing a non-existent table throws a runtime error; always create parent (`Department`) before child (`Student`). > 4. **String literals** — use **single quotes** (`'CS'`), not double quotes (`"CS"`). Double quotes denote identifiers in standard SQL. > 5. **`NULL` comparisons** — never write `= NULL`; always use `IS NULL` / `IS NOT NULL`. > 6. **Misspelling keywords** — `SELET` / `FORM` will lose 1 mark each in a 14-mark answer. > 7. **Skipping the logical execution order in explanations** — examiners reward methodology, not just final code. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ SQL is **declarative** — you specify *what*, not *how*. - ✅ Five sub-languages: **DDL, DML, DQL, DCL, TCL** — memorize the verbs of each. - ✅ Logical execution order: **FROM → WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → SELECT → ORDER BY → LIMIT**. - ✅ **DDL** auto-commits; **DML** is transactional (can rollback). - ✅ **PRIMARY KEY** = unique + NOT NULL, one per table (can be composite). - ✅ **FOREIGN KEY** enforces referential integrity — parent table must exist first. - ✅ **CHECK** constraint enforces domain rules (e.g., `CGPA BETWEEN 0 AND 10`). - ✅ `AUTO_INCREMENT` (MySQL) / `SERIAL` (PostgreSQL) / `IDENTITY` (SQL Server) for surrogate keys. - ✅ `WHERE` filters **rows**; `HAVING` filters **groups** — never mix them. - ✅ Always include **`WHERE` clause** in `UPDATE` / `DELETE` to avoid disasters. - ✅ **Aggregate functions**: `COUNT`, `SUM`, `AVG`, `MIN`, `MAX` work only with `GROUP BY` (or across the whole table). - ✅ **JOIN types**: `INNER` (only matching), `LEFT` (all left + matching right), `RIGHT`, `FULL OUTER`. - ✅ String comparison uses single quotes `'value'`. - ✅ Use `IS NULL` / `IS NOT NULL` — never `= NULL`. - ✅ `LIKE 'A%'` matches strings starting with A; `_` matches any single character. - ✅ Subqueries can be **scalar** (single value), **row** (single row), or **table** (multiple rows/columns). - ✅ For OOP project back-ends, prefer `PreparedStatement` over `Statement` (prevents SQL injection). - ✅ Map each **class** to a **table**, each **object** to a **row**, each **field** to a **column** — this is the canonical ORM mental model. - ✅ `CHAR(n)` is fixed-length (pads with blanks); `VARCHAR(n)` is variable-length — prefer `VARCHAR` for unpredictable text. - ✅ For KTU project viva: always be ready to explain *why* you chose `INNER JOIN` over `LEFT JOIN` in any given query.

topics.title: Working with Result Set
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT633 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Working with Result Set

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ResultSet (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition)**
> The `ResultSet` is an interface declared in the `java.sql` package that represents a **tabular data structure** generated when a SQL `SELECT` query is executed through a `Statement` (or `PreparedStatement` / `CallableStatement`) object. It maintains a **cursor** that points to one row of data at a time and provides a rich API for **navigating, reading, and updating** the underlying database rows.

A `ResultSet` object is produced automatically by the JDBC driver when:

```java
ResultSet rs = stmt.executeQuery("SELECT * FROM student");
```

The object `rs` is conceptually a **window** that scrolls across rows returned by the SQL engine. By default, it is **forward-only**, **read-only**, and its cursor is positioned **before the first row**, requiring `rs.next()` to advance it.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Excel Spreadsheet on a Track"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy for ResultSet**
> Imagine you have a long printed spreadsheet on a long ribbon of paper sliding through a small viewing window on your desk.
> - The **window** is your `ResultSet` object.
> - The **ribbon (spreadsheet)** is the entire result table returned by the database.
> - Your **finger** that points to the currently visible row is the **cursor**.
> - You can only see and operate on **one row at a time** — the one your finger is touching.

| Real-world Element | JDBC Equivalent |
|---|---|
| The ribbon of paper | Full query result set |
| Viewing window | `ResultSet` object |
| Pointing finger | Cursor (logical pointer) |
| Moving the finger forward | `rs.next()` |
| Erasing/rewriting a cell on the ribbon | `rs.updateXXX()` + `rs.updateRow()` |
| Pulling a fresh sheet from the printer | `rs.refreshRow()` |

### 1.3 Why `ResultSet` Matters in JDBC

- It is the **only sanctioned channel** through which a Java program reads rows produced by a SQL `SELECT`.
- It supports **scrollable navigation** (Type 2 / Type 3) for back-and-forth traversal.
- It supports **updatable concurrency** allowing in-place row modification without writing raw `UPDATE` SQL.
- It exposes **`ResultSetMetaData`** for introspecting the schema at runtime (column count, types, names).

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Architectural Properties of Every ResultSet**
> 1. **Type** — controls directionality & sensitivity of the cursor.
> 2. **Concurrency** — controls whether the rows can be modified.
> 3. **Holdability** — controls cursor behavior across transaction commits.

These three properties are fixed at the moment the `Statement` is **created**, not after the `ResultSet` is obtained.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three ResultSet Properties

JDBC defines three independent property constants for every `ResultSet`. They are passed to `Connection.prepareStatement(...)` or `Connection.createStatement(...)` overloads.

### 2.1.1 ResultSet Type (Directionality & Sensitivity)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Type Constants (Declared in `java.sql.ResultSet`)**

| Constant | Cursor Behavior | Sensitivity | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| `TYPE_FORWARD_ONLY` | Forward only | N/A | Default; fastest; memory-efficient |
| `TYPE_SCROLL_INSENSITIVE` | Bi-directional & random | Insensitive to committed changes after result is generated | Reporting tools, cached browsing |
| `TYPE_SCROLL_SENSITIVE` | Bi-directional & random | **Sensitive** — reflects changes committed by other transactions | Real-time monitoring dashboards |

- **Default value**: `TYPE_FORWARD_ONLY`.
- **Sensitivity** means: when another transaction commits a change to a row visible in the result, an *insensitive* cursor still shows the **old** snapshot, while a *sensitive* cursor reflects the **new** committed value.

### 2.1.2 Concurrency Mode

| Constant | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `CONCUR_READ_ONLY` | You cannot update the result set via JDBC. (Default) |
| `CONCUR_UPDATABLE` | You can update, insert, and delete rows through the ResultSet. |

### 2.1.3 Holdability

| Constant | Behavior on `commit()` |
|---|---|
| `HOLD_CURSORS_OVER_COMMIT` | ResultSet **remains open** after commit |
| `CLOSE_CURSORS_AT_COMMIT` | ResultSet is **closed** when commit occurs (default) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Holdability is set on the `Connection` (`conn.setHoldability(...)`) and is honoured only when the underlying driver and database support it.

## 2.2 KTU Formula / Property Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Cheat Sheet — ResultSet Property Declaration Pattern**

| Syntax Form | Returns / Behavior |
|---|---|
| `Statement.createStatement()` | Forward-only + Read-only |
| `Statement.createStatement(int type, int concur)` | Custom ResultSet |
| `PreparedStatement ps = conn.prepareStatement(sql, type, concur)` | Custom scrollable/updatable PS |
| `ResultSet.TYPE_FORWARD_ONLY` | Value = **1004** |
| `ResultSet.TYPE_SCROLL_INSENSITIVE` | Value = **1004** (note: same integer but different interface constant — driver interprets by context) |
| `ResultSet.TYPE_SCROLL_SENSITIVE` | Value = **1005** |
| `ResultSet.CONCUR_READ_ONLY` | Value = **1007** |
| `ResultSet.CONCUR_UPDATABLE` | Value = **1008** |
| `ResultSet.HOLD_CURSORS_OVER_COMMIT` | Value = **1** |
| `ResultSet.CLOSE_CURSORS_AT_COMMIT` | Value = **2** |

> **Mnemonic for KTU Board Exams:** "**T-C-H** = **T**ype, **C**oncurrency, **H**oldability — fixed at Statement creation, never changeable afterwards."

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking ledgers** use `CONCUR_UPDATABLE` to edit account balances directly through the `ResultSet` without crafting raw `UPDATE` SQL.
- **Reporting dashboards** use `TYPE_SCROLL_INSENSITIVE` so that paginated UIs can jump to absolute page numbers using `absolute()`.
- **Trading terminal GUIs** use `TYPE_SCROLL_SENSITIVE` to display live price ticks that other transactions are mutating.
- **DAO layers** in enterprise Java (Spring + JDBC) standardize on `TYPE_FORWARD_ONLY` + `CONCUR_READ_ONLY` for performance; they use explicit `UPDATE` SQL instead.

## 2.4 Lifecycle of a ResultSet — Five Logical Steps

1. **Creation** — `executeQuery()` or `getResultSet()` returns a `ResultSet`.
2. **Positioning** — Cursor is logically positioned *before row 1*; first call to `next()` fetches row 1.
3. **Navigation** — Methods `next()`, `previous()`, `absolute()`, `relative()` move the cursor.
4. **Data Access** — `getXXX(int colIndex)` or `getXXX(String colLabel)` retrieves the value.
5. **Termination** — Either `close()` is called explicitly OR the underlying `Statement` is closed OR the `Connection` is closed — all implicitly close the `ResultSet`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Failing to close a `ResultSet` causes a **JDBC resource leak** (the cursor and DB resources stay locked). Always use `try-with-resources` in KTU lab exams.

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Reference — Methods of ResultSet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decomposition Mandate:** Every navigation, getter, and update method is now broken down step-by-step with code-level reasoning. No method is summarized; each is fully explained.

## 3.1 Cursor Navigation Methods

### Method 1 — `boolean next()`

- **Step 1:** Move the cursor **forward by one row** from its current position.
- **Step 2:** Return `true` if the new position is a valid row, `false` if cursor has moved past the last row.
- **Step 3:** On `false`, the cursor is positioned **after the last row**; further `getXXX()` calls throw `SQLException`.

```java
while (rs.next()) {
    int id = rs.getInt("id");
    System.out.println(id);
}
```

### Method 2 — `boolean previous()`

- **Step 1:** Move the cursor **backward by one row**.
- **Step 2:** Returns `true` if the new position is valid, `false` if cursor moves before the first row.
- **Step 3:** Throws `SQLException` on forward-only result sets.

### Method 3 — `void beforeFirst()` / `void afterLast()`

- **Step 1:** Position the cursor **just before the first row** / **just after the last row**.
- **Step 2:** No value is returned; subsequent `next()` (or `previous()`) call moves into valid territory.

### Method 4 — `boolean first()` / `boolean last()`

- **Step 1:** Position cursor on the **first / last row**.
- **Step 2:** Return `true` if the result set is non-empty.

### Method 5 — `boolean absolute(int row)`

- **Step 1:** Compute target position $P$ where $P$ may be **positive** or **negative**.
- **Step 2:** Apply the rule:

$$
\text{Target row} =
\begin{cases}
