P, & \text{if } P > 0 \quad \text{(counted from beginning, 1-indexed)} \\[4pt]
\vert P \vert, & \text{if } P < 0 \quad \text{(counted from end)} \\[4pt]
0, & \text{if } P = 0 \quad \text{(invalid — throws } SQLException\text{)}
\end{cases}
$$

- **Step 3:** Return `true` if cursor lands on a valid row.

**Worked Example:**

$$
P = +3 \Rightarrow \text{3rd row from start}
$$

$$
P = -1 \Rightarrow \text{last row}
$$

$$
P = -3 \Rightarrow \text{3rd row from the end}
$$

### Method 6 — `boolean relative(int rows)`

- **Step 1:** Read current cursor position $C$.
- **Step 2:** Compute new position $C + rows$.
- **Step 3:** If valid, move and return `true`; else return `false`. For a forward-only `ResultSet`, the `rows` argument **must be positive**; negative values throw `SQLException`.

### Method 7 — Cursor-State Inspection Methods

| Method | Returns `true` When Cursor Is On / At |
|---|---|
| `isFirst()` | First row |
| `isLast()` | Last row |
| `isBeforeFirst()` | Before row 1 |
| `isAfterLast()` | After last row |
| `getRow()` | Current 1-indexed row number, or `0` if no valid row |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic:** "**B**efore-**F**irst → **A**fter-**L**ast" (B-F A-L) — easiest way to remember the inspection methods for KTU viva.

## 3.2 Column Getter Methods (`getXXX`)

### The Universal Pattern

$$
\text{value} = \texttt{rs.getXXX}(\text{int columnIndex} \;\vert\; \text{String columnLabel});
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two overloaded access forms** exist for every getter:
> 1. **Index-based:** `getInt(1)` — 1-based, faster, breaks if column order changes.
> 2. **Label-based:** `getInt("roll_no")` — robust against SELECT column reordering.

### Common Getter Methods

| Method | SQL/JDBC Type Returned |
|---|---|
| `getString(int/String)` | `CHAR`, `VARCHAR` |
| `getInt(int/String)` | `INTEGER` |
| `getLong(int/String)` | `BIGINT` |
| `getDouble(int/String)` | `DOUBLE`, `FLOAT` |
| `getFloat(int/String)` | `REAL` |
| `getBoolean(int/String)` | `BIT`, `BOOLEAN` |
| `getDate(int/String)` | `DATE` |
| `getTime(int/String)` | `TIME` |
| `getTimestamp(int/String)` | `TIMESTAMP` |
| `getObject(int/String)` | Any SQL type (returns `java.lang.Object`) |
| `getBytes(int/String)` | `BINARY`, `VARBINARY` |

### `wasNull()` Method

- **Step 1:** Call a `getXXX()` method.
- **Step 2:** Immediately call `rs.wasNull()` — returns `true` if the JDBC driver read a SQL `NULL` for that column.
- **Step 3:** Important for numeric columns because Java primitives (`int`, `double`) cannot be `null`.

## 3.3 Update Methods (`updateXXX`, `insertRow`, `updateRow`, `deleteRow`)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Update Workflow (KTU Board-Favorite Topic)**
> 1. Position cursor on the target row using `next()`/`absolute()`.
> 2. Call one or more `updateXXX(col, newValue)` — this updates the **row buffer**, not the DB.
> 3. Call `updateRow()` to **flush** the buffer to the database.
> 4. To cancel pending changes, call `cancelRowUpdates()` **before** `updateRow()`.

### Insertion Workflow

1. Move cursor to a position that supports insertion: usually call `moveToInsertRow()`.
2. Call `updateXXX(col, value)` for each column to be inserted.
3. Call `insertRow()` — this commits the new row.
4. Call `moveToCurrentRow()` to return to the row where the cursor was before insertion.

### Deletion Workflow

1. Position cursor on the row to delete.
2. Call `deleteRow()` — the row is deleted from the database **and** from the result set.

### `refreshRow()` Method

- Re-fetches the database values for the current row, discarding any pending updates in the row buffer.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Scenario — `absolute()`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Step-by-Step Trace**

Suppose `rs` contains 5 rows returned by `SELECT * FROM emp`.

$$
\text{Step 1: } rs.beforeFirst() \Rightarrow \text{cursor at position } 0
$$

$$
\text{Step 2: } rs.absolute(3) \Rightarrow P = +3 \Rightarrow \text{row 3 from start} \Rightarrow \text{returns } \texttt{true}
$$

$$
\text{Step 3: } rs.absolute(-1) \Rightarrow \vert P \vert = 1 \Rightarrow \text{last row (row 5)} \Rightarrow \text{returns } \texttt{true}
$$

$$
\text{Step 4: } rs.absolute(0) \Rightarrow \text{throws } \texttt{SQLException}
$$

$$
\text{Step 5: } rs.absolute(-6) \Rightarrow \text{position out of range} \Rightarrow \text{returns } \texttt{false}
$$

## 3.5 ResultSetMetaData — Schema Introspection

```java
ResultSetMetaData rsmd = rs.getMetaData();
int columnCount = rsmd.getColumnCount();
String columnName = rsmd.getColumnName(1);
String columnType = rsmd.getColumnTypeName(1);
```

- **Step 1:** `getColumnCount()` — returns $n$, the number of columns.
- **Step 2:** `getColumnLabel(i)` — returns the SQL `AS` alias or the column name.
- **Step 3:** `getColumnTypeName(i)` — returns the database type name (e.g., `"VARCHAR"`).
- **Step 4:** `getColumnDisplaySize(i)` — returns the maximum width in characters.
- **Step 5:** `isNullable(i)`, `isAutoIncrement(i)`, `isSigned(i)` — boolean schema attributes.

> This is essential for building **generic ORM mappers** and **dynamic report generators**.

## 3.6 Complete Reference Table — All Critical Methods

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield KTU Exam Table (do not use vertical pipes inside cells — use \vert instead)**

| Method | Forward-Only ResultSet? | Scrollable ResultSet? | Returns |
|---|---|---|---|
| `next()` | ✅ Allowed | ✅ Allowed | `boolean` |
| `previous()` | ❌ Throws | ✅ Allowed | `boolean` |
| `first()` | ❌ Throws | ✅ Allowed | `boolean` |
| `last()` | ❌ Throws | ✅ Allowed | `boolean` |
| `beforeFirst()` | ❌ Throws | ✅ Allowed | `void` |
| `afterLast()` | ❌ Throws | ✅ Allowed | `void` |
| `absolute(int)` | ❌ Throws | ✅ Allowed | `boolean` |
| `relative(int)` | Only positive | ✅ Allowed | `boolean` |
| `getRow()` | ❌ Throws | ✅ Allowed | `int` |
| `updateXXX()` | ❌ Throws | ✅ Allowed (if upgradable) | varies |
| `insertRow()` | ❌ Throws | ✅ Allowed (if upgradable) | `void` |
| `deleteRow()` | ❌ Throws | ✅ Allowed (if upgradable) | `void` |
| `refreshRow()` | ✅ Allowed | ✅ Allowed | `void` |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Class Hierarchy — Where `ResultSet` Lives in JDBC

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["java.lang.AutoCloseable"] --> B["java.sql.ResultSet"]
    B --> C["javax.sql.rowset.CachedRowSet"]
    B --> D["javax.sql.rowset.JdbcRowSet"]
    B --> E["javax.sql.rowset.WebRowSet"]
    B --> F["javax.sql.rowset.FilteredRowSet"]
    B --> G["javax.sql.rowset.JoinRowSet"]
    H["java.sql.Statement"] --> I["executeQuery"]
    I --> B
    J["java.sql.Connection"] --> H
    K["java.sql.PreparedStatement"] --> H
    L["java.sql.CallableStatement"] --> K
```

> [!NOTE]
> The hierarchy above shows that `ResultSet` is the **root interface** for all row-set implementations in modern Java (CachedRowSet, JdbcRowSet, etc., as defined by JDBC RowSet Specification JSR-114).

## 4.2 Statement → ResultSet Creation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Application Code"] --> B["Connection.createStatement Type, Concurrency"]
    B --> C["Statement Object"]
    C --> D["stmt.executeQuery SELECT sql"]
    D --> E["ResultSet Object"]
    E --> F["rs.next / previous / absolute"]
    F --> G["rs.getXXX / updateXXX"]
    G --> H["Database Table"]
    H --> G
    G --> I["rs.close / try-with-resources"]
```

## 4.3 Scrollable Cursor Navigation State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> BeforeFirst
    BeforeFirst --> Row1 : next()
    Row1 --> Row2 : next()
    Row2 --> Row3 : next()
    Row3 --> AfterLast : next()
    AfterLast --> Row3 : previous()
    Row3 --> Row2 : previous()
    Row2 --> Row3 : absolute(3)
    Row2 --> Row5 : absolute(-1)
    Row3 --> [*] : close()
    AfterLast --> [*] : close()
```

## 4.4 Updatable ResultSet — Update Buffer Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DB["Database Table emp"]
        R1["Row Buffer in DB: id=1, name='Anu', sal=30000"]
        R2["Row Buffer in DB: id=2, name='Rahul', sal=40000"]
    end

    subgraph RS["ResultSet Row Buffer (in memory)"]
        B1["Pending Update: sal = 35000"]
    end

    A["Application calls updateInt 2, 35000"] --> B1
    B1 --> C{"Call updateRow?"}
    C -- YES --> D["Flush buffer to DB row"]
    C -- NO --> E["Call cancelRowUpdates to discard"]
    D --> R1
    B1 --> F["Call refreshRow to re-fetch DB value"]
    F --> R1
```

## 4.5 ResultSet Type & Concurrency Decision Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Need to read query results?"] --> Q1{"Need bidirectional navigation?"}
    Q1 -- NO --> F["TYPE_FORWARD_ONLY"]
    Q1 -- YES --> Q2{"Need to see other transactions' commits in real time?"}
    Q2 -- NO --> SI["TYPE_SCROLL_INSENSITIVE"]
    Q2 -- YES --> SS["TYPE_SCROLL_SENSITIVE"]
    F --> Q3{"Need to update DB through ResultSet?"}
    SI --> Q3
    SS --> Q3
    Q3 -- NO --> RO["CONCUR_READ_ONLY"]
    Q3 -- YES --> UP["CONCUR_UPDATABLE"]
    RO --> End["Statement created with chosen properties"]
    UP --> End
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**

> **Q:** What is a `ResultSet` in JDBC? List its **three architectural properties**.

**Model Answer:**

A `ResultSet` is an interface in the `java.sql` package that represents the tabular output of executing a SQL `SELECT` query through a `Statement` object. It maintains a **cursor** that points to the current row and provides methods to navigate, read, and (optionally) modify rows.

The three architectural properties are:

1. **Type** — `TYPE_FORWARD_ONLY`, `TYPE_SCROLL_INSENSITIVE`, or `TYPE_SCROLL_SENSITIVE`.
2. **Concurrency** — `CONCUR_READ_ONLY` or `CONCUR_UPDATABLE`.
3. **Holdability** — `HOLD_CURSORS_OVER_COMMIT` or `CLOSE_CURSORS_AT_COMMIT`.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for the three properties.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

> **Q:** Differentiate between `TYPE_SCROLL_INSENSITIVE` and `TYPE_SCROLL_SENSITIVE` `ResultSet`s. Give one engineering use-case for each.

**Model Answer:**

| Property | `TYPE_SCROLL_INSENSITIVE` | `TYPE_SCROLL_SENSITIVE` |
|---|---|---|
| Bidirectional navigation | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| Reflects changes committed by **other** transactions after the result set was generated | ❌ No (snapshot frozen) | ✅ Yes (sees fresh committed values) |
| Memory footprint | Smaller | Larger |
| Driver support | Widespread | Limited |
| Use case | Audit/reporting tools that need paginated browsing of stable historical data | Live trading dashboards that show real-time price ticks committed by upstream transactions |

*(3 marks: 1 for each differentiation point + 1 for use-case.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 ESE Module]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the different **ResultSet types** and **concurrency modes** with suitable code snippets. How are they specified at `Statement` creation time?
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Write a complete Java program that demonstrates a **scrollable and updatable** `ResultSet`. The program should fetch rows from a table `Employee(emp_id, name, salary)`, display them, then **increase the salary of the second employee by 5000** using the `ResultSet` update mechanism.

---

#### Model Answer — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Type constants (forward declaration):**

```java
import java.sql.*;

int TYPE_FORWARD_ONLY      = ResultSet.TYPE_FORWARD_ONLY;      // default
int TYPE_SCROLL_INSENSITIVE = ResultSet.TYPE_SCROLL_INSENSITIVE;
int TYPE_SCROLL_SENSITIVE   = ResultSet.TYPE_SCROLL_SENSITIVE;
```

**Step 2 — Concurrency constants:**

```java
int CONCUR_READ_ONLY  = ResultSet.CONCUR_READ_ONLY;   // default
int CONCUR_UPDATABLE  = ResultSet.CONCUR_UPDATABLE;
```

**Step 3 — Specify at `Statement` creation:**

```java
Statement stmt = conn.createStatement(
        ResultSet.TYPE_SCROLL_INSENSITIVE,
        ResultSet.CONCUR_UPDATABLE);
```

Or with `PreparedStatement`:

```java
PreparedStatement ps = conn.prepareStatement(
        "SELECT * FROM Employee WHERE emp_id = ?",
        ResultSet.TYPE_SCROLL_SENSITIVE,
        ResultSet.CONCUR_UPDATABLE);
```

**Step 4 — Explanation of trade-offs:**

- Forward-only result sets are the **fastest and most memory-efficient** and are the JDBC default.
- Scroll-insensitive results provide **bidirectional navigation** without picking up concurrent updates — ideal for reports.
- Scroll-sensitive results provide real-time visibility but are **not supported by all drivers** (e.g., MySQL Connector/J throws `SQLException` for `TYPE_SCROLL_SENSITIVE`).
- Read-only is the safest concurrency; upgradable concurrency requires the `SELECT` to reference **all columns of the table without joins** and to use a **primary key** — otherwise `executeQuery()` may silently fall back to read-only.

*(7 marks: 2 for type explanation + 2 for concurrency explanation + 2 for code snippets + 1 for trade-offs.)*

---

#### Model Answer — Part (b)

```java
import java.sql.*;

public class ScrollableUpdatableDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        String url  = "jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/testdb";
        String user = "root";
        String pass = "root";

        try (Connection conn = DriverManager.getConnection(url, user, pass)) {

            // STEP 1: Create scrollable + updatable Statement
            Statement stmt = conn.createStatement(
                    ResultSet.TYPE_SCROLL_INSENSITIVE,
                    ResultSet.CONCUR_UPDATABLE);

            // STEP 2: Fetch all employees
            ResultSet rs = stmt.executeQuery("SELECT emp_id, name, salary FROM Employee");

            // STEP 3: Display initial rows
            System.out.println("---- Initial Data ----");
            rs.beforeFirst();
            while (rs.next()) {
                System.out.printf("%d  %s  %.2f%n",
                        rs.getInt("emp_id"),
                        rs.getString("name"),
                        rs.getDouble("salary"));
            }

            // STEP 4: Move to second row and update salary
            rs.absolute(2);
            double currentSalary = rs.getDouble("salary");
            int    empId         = rs.getInt("emp_id");
            String empName       = rs.getString("name");

            System.out.println("\nUpdating salary of employee: "
                    + empId + " - " + empName);

            // STEP 5: Apply update through row-buffer
            rs.updateDouble("salary", currentSalary + 5000.0);

            // STEP 6: Flush the buffer to the database
            rs.updateRow();

            // STEP 7: Verify by re-reading
            rs.refreshRow();
            System.out.printf("New salary: %.2f%n", rs.getDouble("salary"));

            // STEP 8: Cleanup
            rs.close();
            stmt.close();

        } catch (SQLException e) {
            e.printStackTrace();
        }
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step reasoning (for board valuation):**

- **Line `conn.createStatement(TYPE_SCROLL_INSENSITIVE, CONCUR_UPDATABLE)`** — requests both scrollability and upgradability.
- **`rs.beforeFirst()`** — resets cursor to the start so we can iterate from row 1 again for printing.
- **`rs.absolute(2)`** — moves cursor directly to the **second row** without iterating.
- **`rs.updateDouble("salary", currentSalary + 5000.0)`** — modifies only the **row buffer** (in-memory), not yet the database.
- **`rs.updateRow()`** — flushes the row buffer to the database engine via the cursor.
- **`rs.refreshRow()`** — re-fetches the row to confirm the update took effect.

*(7 marks: 1 for connection + 1 for statement creation + 1 for fetch + 1 for iteration + 1 for updateRow call + 1 for refresh + 1 for proper closing.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 ESE Module]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the various **navigation methods** of the `ResultSet` interface with examples. State clearly which methods throw `SQLException` for forward-only result sets.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain how to **insert a new row** and **delete an existing row** in the database using an **updatable `ResultSet`**. Provide Java code for both operations.

---

#### Model Answer — Part (a)

**Navigation Method Taxonomy:**

**(i) Forward / Backward Single-Step Methods**

```java
boolean next();     // advance by one
boolean previous(); // step back by one
```

`(previous())` throws `SQLException` on forward-only result sets.

**(ii) Boundary Positioning Methods**

```java
void    beforeFirst();   // jump before row 1
void    afterLast();     // jump after last row
boolean first();         // jump to row 1; false if empty
boolean last();          // jump to last row; false if empty
```

All four throw `SQLException` on forward-only result sets.

**(iii) Random-Access Methods**

```java
boolean absolute(int row);   // positive: from start; negative: from end; 0 throws
boolean relative(int rows);  // positive only on forward-only result sets
```

Both throw `SQLException` on forward-only result sets (or negative arguments for `relative()`).

**(iv) State Inspection Methods**

```java
int     getRow();
boolean isFirst();
boolean isLast();
boolean isBeforeFirst();
boolean isAfterLast();
```

All of these throw `SQLException` on forward-only result sets.

**Sample Combined Demo:**

```java
Statement stmt = conn.createStatement(
        ResultSet.TYPE_SCROLL_INSENSITIVE,
        ResultSet.CONCUR_READ_ONLY);

ResultSet rs = stmt.executeQuery("SELECT emp_id, name FROM Employee");

System.out.println("Total rows: skipping to last...");
rs.last();
int n = rs.getRow();
System.out.println("Last row number = " + n);

System.out.println("Now iterating backwards:");
while (rs.previous()) {
    System.out.println(rs.getInt("emp_id") + "  " + rs.getString("name"));
}

System.out.println("Absolute jump to row 1:");
rs.absolute(1);
System.out.println(rs.getString("name"));
```

**Summary Table — Allowed vs. Throwing:**

| Method | Forward-Only | Scrollable |
|---|---|---|
| `next()` | ✅ | ✅ |
| `previous()`, `first()`, `last()`, `beforeFirst()`, `afterLast()` | ❌ | ✅ |
| `absolute(int)`, `relative(int)`, `getRow()`, `isFirst()`, `isLast()`, `isBeforeFirst()`, `isAfterLast()` | ❌ | ✅ |

*(7 marks: 1 for taxonomy + 2 for each method category explanation + 1 for combined demo + 1 for the summary table.)*

---

#### Model Answer — Part (b)

**Insertion Code:**

```java
Statement stmt = conn.createStatement(
        ResultSet.TYPE_SCROLL_INSENSITIVE,
        ResultSet.CONCUR_UPDATABLE);

ResultSet rs = stmt.executeQuery("SELECT emp_id, name, salary FROM Employee");

// STEP 1: Move cursor to the "insert row" buffer
rs.moveToInsertRow();

// STEP 2: Populate the buffer
rs.updateInt   ("emp_id", 1001);
rs.updateString("name",    "Kavya");
rs.updateDouble("salary",  55000.0);

// STEP 3: Commit the new row to the database
rs.insertRow();

// STEP 4: Return to the row we were on before insertion
rs.moveToCurrentRow();
```

**Deletion Code:**

```java
// STEP 1: Position cursor on the row to delete
rs.absolute(3);      // delete the 3rd employee

// STEP 2: Delete the row
rs.deleteRow();
```

**Step-by-step reasoning (for board valuation):**

- `moveToInsertRow()` reserves a special buffer **separate from the result set's own rows**.
- `updateXXX()` calls populate the buffer columns.
- `insertRow()` writes the buffer as a **new row** to the underlying table.
- `moveToCurrentRow()` returns the cursor to the row it was on **before** `moveToInsertRow()`.
- `deleteRow()` removes the **current row** both from the result set and the database.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Deductions**
> 1. **Forgetting `moveToInsertRow()`** before populating buffer — 2 marks lost.
> 2. **Calling `updateXXX()` without `updateRow()`** for modification — 1 mark lost (the change is silently discarded).
> 3. **Not specifying `CONCUR_UPDATABLE`** when creating the `Statement` — the driver silently degrades to read-only and `insertRow()`/`updateRow()` throw `SQLException` — 2 marks lost.
> 4. **Assuming `deleteRow()` works on `TYPE_FORWARD_ONLY` result sets** — it throws `SQLException`. Always use `TYPE_SCROLL_INSENSITIVE` or `TYPE_SCROLL_SENSITIVE` — 1 mark lost.
> 5. **Forgetting `try-with-resources`** or explicit `close()` — causes resource leak — 1 mark lost.

*(7 marks: 1 for moveToInsertRow + 1 for updateXXX + 1 for insertRow + 1 for moveToCurrentRow + 1 for absolute positioning + 1 for deleteRow + 1 for proper resource handling.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE Viva + Written Exams**

- ✅ `ResultSet` is a **`java.sql` interface** — never instantiated directly; returned by `executeQuery()`.
- ✅ Default properties: **`TYPE_FORWARD_ONLY` + `CONCUR_READ_ONLY` + `CLOSE_CURSORS_AT_COMMIT`**.
- ✅ Three architectural properties — **Type, Concurrency, Holdability** — fixed at `Statement` creation.
- ✅ Cursor starts **before row 1**; first call to `next()` lands on row 1.
- ✅ **`wasNull()`** must be called **immediately** after `getXXX()` to detect SQL `NULL`.
- ✅ **`absolute(int)`** uses 1-based positive indexing from start; **negative** indexing from end; **zero throws** `SQLException`.
- ✅ **`previous()`, `first()`, `last()`, `beforeFirst()`, `afterLast()`, `absolute()`, `relative()`, `getRow()`, `isXXX()`** all throw `SQLException` on forward-only result sets.
- ✅ **`updateXXX()`** modifies only the **row buffer** — must call **`updateRow()`** to flush.
- ✅ **`cancelRowUpdates()`** discards pending buffer changes **before** `updateRow()`.
- ✅ **`insertRow()`** requires **`moveToInsertRow()`** first; **`moveToCurrentRow()`** restores the cursor.
- ✅ **`deleteRow()`** deletes both from result set and underlying table.
- ✅ **`refreshRow()`** re-fetches DB values, discarding any unsaved buffer changes.
- ✅ **`ResultSetMetaData`** via `getMetaData()` reveals column count, names, types at runtime.
- ✅ Use **`try-with-resources`** for `Connection`, `Statement`, and `ResultSet` to avoid leaks.
- ✅ **Driver support varies** — `TYPE_SCROLL_SENSITIVE` is **not supported** by many JDBC drivers; always wrap in try-catch.
- ✅ For `CONCUR_UPDATABLE` to actually work, the `SELECT` must (a) reference **a single table only**, (b) include **all NOT NULL columns**, and (c) **select the primary key**.
- ✅ Holdability **`HOLD_CURSORS_OVER_COMMIT`** is useful for read-only transactions spanning multiple commits; default **`CLOSE_CURSORS_AT_COMMIT`** is safer.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final Examiner's Warning — Most Repeated KTU Pitfalls**
> 1. Mixing up **`TYPE_SCROLL_INSENSITIVE`** vs **`TYPE_SCROLL_SENSITIVE`** — examiners *always* ask the difference.
> 2. Forgetting to call **`updateRow()`** after `updateXXX()` — buffer changes are silently lost.
> 3. Trying to use `previous()` on a default `ResultSet` — throws `SQLException`.
> 4. Assuming `CONCUR_UPDATABLE` works on every driver — MySQL with simple SELECTs only.
> 5. Confusing **`getXXX(int)`** (1-based) with array indexing (0-based) — `rs.getInt(0)` throws `SQLException`.
